辍耕录 - Records Written After Laying Aside the Plough

Translation created with model: together-qwen3-14b-3step-fewshot / step-by-step-3step-fewshot

    Home
    1

    命馆臣録存杨维桢正统辨谕
    　　元杨维桢着宋辽金正统辨大防以元承宋统而排斥辽金其文不见本集惟陶宗仪辍耕録载之今馆臣编辑四库全书谓其持论纰缪并辍耕録内所载者亦与删除且言隋先代周继乃平陈未闻唐宋诸儒谓隋承陈不承周也此语似是而非葢尝论之春秋大一统之义尊王黜霸所以立万世之纲常使名正言顺出于天命人心之正紫阳纲目义在正统是以始皇之廿六年秦始继周汉始于髙帝之五年而不始于秦降之嵗三国不以魏吴之强夺汉统之正春秋之义然也杨维

    Explanation

    1

    The Imperial Library Scholars Were Ordered to Record and Preserve Yang Weizhen's Treatise on the Legitimacy of the Yuan Dynasty's Succession

    During the Yuan dynasty, Yang Weizhen wrote a treatise titled "Cóng Liáo Jīn Zhèngtǒng Biàn" (A Treatise on the Legitimacy of the Yuan Dynasty's Succession from the Song Dynasty), which argued that the Yuan dynasty should be recognized as the legitimate successor of the Song dynasty, while excluding the Liao and Jin dynasties. This treatise, however, was not included in Yang Weizhen's own collection but was instead preserved in Tao Zongyi's "Chuò Gēng Lù" (Records of Pausing the Plow). Today, the scholars editing the "Sì Kù Quán Shū" (Complete Library in Four Branches) criticized Yang Weizhen's argument as erroneous and also removed the relevant section from the "Chuò Gēng Lù."

    Furthermore, they argued that the Sui dynasty, which succeeded the Zhou dynasty after conquering the Chen dynasty, was traditionally seen as the legitimate successor of the Zhou dynasty, not the Chen dynasty. This statement, while seemingly correct, is actually flawed. Let us consider the principles of "da yitong" (great unity) and "zūn wáng chù bà" (uphold the king and reject the hegemon) from the "Chūnqiū" (Spring and Autumn Annals). These principles emphasize the importance of a unified political order and the moral and political legitimacy of a ruler. According to the "Zizhi Tongjian" (Comprehensive Mirror to Aid in Government) by Sima Guang, the legitimacy of a dynasty's succession is a crucial aspect of governance. The Qin dynasty began in the 26th year of Qin Shi Huang's reign, and the Han dynasty began in the fifth year of Emperor Gaozu's reign, not from the fall of the Qin dynasty. Similarly, the Three Kingdoms did not usurp the legitimate succession of the Han dynasty despite the strength of Wei and Wu. This is in accordance with the principles of the "Chūnqiū." Therefore, Yang Weizhen's argument is indeed flawed.

    2

    桢撰三史正统辨凡二千六百余言义本春秋法宗纲目其欲以元继南宋为正统而不及辽金持论颇正不得谓之纰缪夫维桢身为元臣入明虽不仕而应明太祖之召且上铙歌鼓吹曲颂美新朝非刺故国几于剧秦美新其进退无据较之钱谦益托言不忘故君者鄙倍尤甚向屡于诗文中斥之而维桢正统之辨则不可以人废言也夫正统者继前统受新命也东晋以后宋齐梁陈虽江左偏安而所承者晋之正统其时若拓跋魏氏地大势强北齐北周继之亦较南朝为盛而中华正
    统不得不属之宋齐梁陈者其所承之统正也至隋则平陈以后混一区宇始得为大一统即唐之末季藩镇扰乱自朱温以讫郭威等或起自冦窃或身为叛臣五十余年之间更易数姓甚且称臣称侄于契丹然中国统绪相承宋以前亦不得不以正统属之梁唐晋汉周也至于宋南渡后偏处临安其时辽金元相继起于北邉奄有河北宋虽称侄于金而其所承者究仍北宋之正统辽金不得攘而有之也至元世祖平宋始有宋统当絶我统当续之语则统绪之正元世祖已知之稔矣我

    Explanation

    2

    Zhen wrote the Three Histories' Debate on Legitimate Succession, totaling over two thousand six hundred words, based on the principles of the Spring and Autumn Annals and following the methods of the Comprehensive Mirror to Aid in Government. His argument is that the Yuan Dynasty should be considered the legitimate successor to the Southern Song Dynasty, excluding the Liao and Jin Dynasties. The argument is quite correct and cannot be considered mistaken. As for Zhen, who served as an official of the Yuan Dynasty and entered the Ming Dynasty without taking office, he was invited by the founding emperor of the Ming Dynasty and composed music and poetry to praise the new dynasty without criticizing the old one. His actions are almost as if he were praising the new dynasty like Jia Yi did during the Han Dynasty, who wrote Lament for the South to praise the Han and criticize the Qin. His lack of consistency in his actions is even more despicable than that of Qian Qianyi, who claimed to remember his former ruler. I have often criticized him in previous writings, but his argument on legitimate succession cannot be dismissed because of the person. The concept of a legitimate dynasty is one that inherits the previous dynasty and receives a new mandate. After the Eastern Jin Dynasty, the Southern Dynasties (Song, Qi, Liang, and Chen) were confined to the southern part of China but still inherited the legitimate mandate of the Eastern Jin. At that time, if the Tuoba (Northern Wei) Dynasty was large and powerful, followed by the Northern Qi and Northern Zhou Dynasties, which were also more powerful than the Southern Dynasties, the legitimate succession of China had to be attributed to the Southern Dynasties because they inherited the legitimate mandate. The Sui Dynasty, after conquering the Chen Dynasty, unified all of China and thus achieved a great unification. During the late Tang Dynasty, the regional military governors (fanzhen) caused chaos, and from Zhu Wen to Guo Wei, some rose from banditry and others were traitors. Over fifty years, several dynasties succeeded each other, and even some paid homage to the Khitan (Liao Dynasty). Despite this, the legitimate succession before the Song Dynasty had to be attributed to the Later Liang, Later Tang, Later Jin, Later Han, and Later Zhou. After the Southern Song Dynasty retreated south to Lin'an (modern-day Hangzhou), the Liao, Jin, and Yuan Dynasties rose in the north. Although the Song Dynasty paid homage to the Jin Dynasty, it still inherited the legitimate mandate of the Northern Song Dynasty, and the Liao and Jin could not claim it. When Kublai Khan (Yuan Shizu) conquered the Song Dynasty, he recognized that the Song Dynasty's mandate had ended and the Yuan Dynasty's mandate had begun, and he was well aware of the legitimacy of the Yuan Dynasty's succession.

    3

    皇祖御批通鉴及朕向所批通鉴辑览俱以此论定葢春秋大义纲目大法实万世不易之凖我朝为明复讐讨贼定鼎中原合一海宇为自古得天下最正然朕犹于通鉴辑览内存福王建国之号一年使其能保守南都未尝不可如南宋之承统绵延不絶而奈其当阳九之运
    　　天弗与人弗归自覆其宗社也此实大公至正天下万世可以共见共守之论然馆臣之删杨维桢正统辨者其意葢以金为满洲欲令承辽之统故曲为之説耳不知辽金皆自起北方本无所承继非若宋元之相承逓及为中华之主也若以此立论转觉狭小天下万世必有起而议之者是不可以不辨朕以为不但辍耕録中所载杨维桢之正统辨不必删除即杨维桢文集内亦当补録是编并将此谕各载卷首以昭天命人心之正以存春秋纲目之义特谕

    Explanation

    3

    In all my annotations of the Zizhi Tongjian and the Tongjian Jiluan, both by my ancestors and myself, we have reached a consensus on this interpretation. The principles of the Spring and Autumn Annals and the Gangmu are truly standards that will never change through the ages. Our dynasty avenged the Ming and punished the rebels, established its capital in the Central Plains, and unified the entire world, thus obtaining the world in the most legitimate way from ancient times. Nevertheless, I still include the period of the Southern Ming in my annotations of the Tongjian Jiluan. If the Southern Ming had been able to maintain control of the southern capital, they could have continued the Ming lineage like the Southern Song, but unfortunately, they faced a period of misfortune and lost divine and human support, leading to their downfall. This is indeed a great and righteous standard that can be seen and followed by all people throughout history. However, the scholars in the imperial library who edited out Yang Weizhen's argument about the legitimacy of the ruling dynasty likely did so to make the Jurchens (ancestors of the Manchus) appear as the legitimate successors of the Liao dynasty. They did not understand that both the Liao and Jin dynasties originated in the north and had no legitimate succession, unlike the Song and Yuan dynasties. If we were to base our argument on this, it would seem narrow-minded, and there would surely be those in the future who would rise to debate it. Therefore, it is necessary to clarify this. I believe that not only should the argument about the legitimacy of the ruling dynasty in the Chuogeng Lu by Yang Weizhen not be deleted, but also his collected works should be included in this compilation. Furthermore, this edict should be placed at the beginning of each volume to clarify the principles of the Spring and Autumn Annals and the Gangmu. This is hereby specially ordered.

    4

    乾隆辛丑孟春

    　　钦定四库全书　　　　　子部十二
    　　辍耕録　　　　　　　　小説家一【杂事之属】提要
    　　【臣】等谨案辍耕録三十巻元陶宗仪撰宗仪有国风尊经已着録此书乃记闻见琐事前有至正丙午孙作序书中称明兵曰集庆军或曰江南游军葢丙午为至正二十七年犹未入明时所作也郎瑛七修稿谓宗仪多録旧书如广客谈通本録之率皆攘为己作今其书未见本无由证瑛説之确否但就此书而论则于有元一代法令制度及至正东南兵乱之事纪録颇详所考订书画文艺亦多足备防证惟多以俚俗戏谑之语闾里鄙之事颇乖著作之体叶盛水东日

    Explanation

    4

    In the first month of spring in the Xinchou year of the Qianlong era,

    The Complete Library in Four Branches of Literature, officially compiled, Zibu (Subsection) Twelve,
    Records of a Plowman's Rest, the first category under the Novelist section [Miscellaneous Matters], Introduction,

    We officials respectfully note that Records of a Plowman's Rest, thirty volumes, was written by Tao Zongyi of the Yuan dynasty. Tao Zongyi has another work, Respect for the Classics in the National Style, which has already been recorded. This book records trivial matters observed and heard, with a preface written in the Bingwu year of the Zhizheng era by Sun Zuo. In the book, the Ming army is referred to as the Jiading Army or the Southern Jiangnan Army. The Bingwu year corresponds to the twenty-seventh year of the Zhizheng era, before the Ming dynasty had been established, so it was written during that time. Lang Ying's Seven Revisions states that Tao Zongyi often copied entire books, such as Expanded Guest Talks, and claimed them as his own. However, since the original text of that book is not available, it is impossible to verify whether Lang Ying's claim is accurate. Nevertheless, in this book, Records of a Plowman's Rest, Tao Zongyi provides detailed accounts of the laws, institutions, and the military upheavals in southeastern China during the Zhizheng era of the Yuan dynasty. The verification of calligraphy, painting, and literary arts is also abundant, providing sufficient evidence. However, the book includes too much vulgar and humorous language, as well as trivial matters of village and rustic life, which are contrary to the style of scholarly writing. Leaf Sheng's Water East Diary also mentions this point.

    5

    记深病其所载猥防良非苛论然其首尾赅贯要为能留心于掌故故朱彛尊静志居诗话谓宗仪练习旧章元代朝野旧事实借此书以存而许其有禆史学则虽瑜不掩瑕固亦论古者所不废矣惟第三卷中载杨维桢正统辨二千六百余言大防欲以元承南宋之统而排斥辽金攷隋先代周继乃平陈未闻唐宋诸儒谓隋承陈不承周也持论殊为纰缪后维桢编东维子集不载此文葢已自悟其谬而削之宗仪乃掇拾缕载尤为寡识今删除此条用昭公义焉乾隆四十六年正月恭校上
    　　总纂官【臣】纪昀【臣】陆锡熊【臣】孙士毅
    　　总　校　官【臣】陆　费　墀

    　　辍耕録序
    　　余友天台陶君九成避兵三呉间有田一廛家于松南作劳之暇每以笔墨自随时时辍耕休于树隂抱膝而叹鼔腹而歌遇事肯綮摘叶书之贮一破盎去则埋于树根人莫测焉如是者十载遂累盎至十数一日尽发其藏俾门人小子萃而録之得凡干条合三十卷题曰南村辍耕録上兼六经百氏之防下极稗官小史之谈昔之所未考今之所

    Explanation

    5

    Translation of the Source Text

    Preface to Nan Cun Chudeng Lu

    My friend Tao Zongyi from Taishan, known as "Jiu Cheng," fled to the Three Wu regions during times of war. He had a small plot of land in the south of Song and, in his spare time from farming, would often indulge in writing with pen and ink. Whenever he paused his work, he would rest under the shade of a tree, cross his knees, and sigh; at other times, he would sing with a full belly. Whenever he encountered a significant point, he would jot it down on a leaf, store it in an old jar, and then bury it at the base of a tree. No one could fathom what he was doing. This went on for ten years, and by then, he had accumulated dozens of jars. One day, he unearthed all his writings, instructed his disciples to compile them, and gathered a total of several thousand entries, which were compiled into thirty volumes. He titled the work Nan Cun Chudeng Lu, which encompasses the wisdom of the Six Classics and the teachings of various schools of thought, as well as the lesser-known tales of minor historians and folklorists. It covers what was previously unexamined and what is now discussed.

    On Nan Cun Chudeng Lu

    Tao Zongyi is deeply criticized for the content of his work as being overly cautious and restrained, yet it is not a harsh critique. From beginning to end, the work is comprehensive and coherent, essentially showing a careful attention to historical anecdotes. Zhu Yizun, in his Jingzhi Ju Shi Hua, remarks that Tao Zongyi is familiar with old customs and regulations, and that the old facts of the Yuan Dynasty's court and countryside are preserved through this book. He acknowledges its benefit to historical studies, though even with its merits, it cannot hide its flaws. It is not discarded by those who discuss ancient matters.

    However, in the third volume, there is an entry on Yang Weizhen's Zhengtong Bian, a work of over two thousand six hundred words. The argument is that the Yuan Dynasty should inherit the Southern Song Dynasty's legacy while rejecting the Liao and Jin Dynasties. Upon examination, it is noted that the Sui Dynasty succeeded the Zhou Dynasty after defeating Chen, and there is no record of the Tang and Song scholars arguing that the Sui Dynasty inherited Chen rather than Zhou. The argument is particularly flawed. Later, when Yang Weizhen compiled his Dongwei Zi Ji, he did not include this essay, as he had realized its error and removed it. Tao Zongyi, however, picked it up and recorded it in detail, which shows a lack of insight. Therefore, this entry has been deleted to clarify public righteousness.

    Respectfully submitted for review in the 46th year of the Qianlong Emperor's reign.

    Chief Editors: [Minister] Ji Yun, [Minister] Lu Xixiong, [Minister] Sun Shiyi
    Chief Proofreader: [Minister] Lu Feci

    6

    未闻其采摭之博侈于白帖研覈之精拟于洪笔论议抑有伤今慨古之思铺张盛美为忠臣孝子之劝文章制度不辨而明疑似根据可览而悉盖唐宋以来专门史学之所未让虽周室之藏郯子之对有不待环辙而后知又岂抵掌谈笑以求贤于优孟者哉九成名宗仪少工举子业晩乃弃去阖戸著书此其一云至正丙午夏六月江隂孙作大雅序

    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷一　　　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰大元宗室世系

    <子部,小说家类,杂事之属,辍耕录,卷一>

    Explanation

    6

    I have not heard of any work that is as broadly and richly collected as the Baitie (anthology) and as finely researched and argued as the great writing of Hongbi (grand and powerful writing), with balanced discussions that lament the present and reflect on the past, elaborating on grand and beautiful themes to encourage loyalty and filial piety. The rules and conventions of literary composition are clearly and distinctly explained, and the evidence and explanations are so clear that they can be fully understood. This work surpasses the specialized historical scholarship of the Tang and Song dynasties and is so comprehensive that it is comparable to the vast knowledge of the Zhou dynasty and the wisdom of Tán Zǐ, without needing to follow the circular paths of others. How could it be that one would casually and informally seek wisdom from the likes of Yōu Měng (a famous jester)?

    Nine Chengming Zōngyí, who was known for his success in the imperial examination system in his youth, later abandoned this career and devoted himself to writing. This is one of his works. The preface was written by Sun Dàyǎ in June of the year 1356 (丙午年) in Jiangyin.

    Translation Plan:

    • Use of idiomatic expressions: "采摭之博侈" is translated as "broadly and richly collected," and "伤今慨古" is translated as "lament the present and reflect on the past."
    • Cultural context: References to the Zhou dynasty and Tán Zǐ are preserved to maintain the cultural and historical significance.
    • Syntactic clarity: The long, complex sentence is broken down into several key points to ensure clarity and readability.
    • Consistent terminology: The author's name, "九成名宗仪," is consistently translated as "Nine Chengming Zōngyí," and the place name "江隂" is consistently translated as "Jiangyin."

    <子部,小说家类,杂事之属,辍耕录,卷一>
