    　　列圣授受正统
    　　始祖【讳勃端察尔】
    　　烈祖神元皇帝【讳伊苏克依姓却特氏】
    　　太祖应天啓运圣武皇帝【讳特穆津国语曰青吉斯】
    　　宋开禧二年丙寅十二月即位于鄂诺河自号为汗至宋寳庆三年丁亥七月己丑崩于萨里川在位二十二年夀六十六葬起辇谷
    　　太宗英文皇帝【讳谔格徳依】
    　　宋绍定二年己丑八月己未即位于固尔班苏巴克至宋淳祐元年辛丑十一月崩于呼兰山在位一十三年寿五十六起辇谷　六皇后塔纳竒纳临朝称制皇后鼐玛锦氏
    　　睿宗仁圣景襄皇帝【讳托垒】
    　　宪宗追諡
    　　定宗简平皇帝【讳库裕克】
    　　宋淳祐二年壬寅至乙巳皇后当国丙午七月归政即位于达兰达巴至戊申三月崩于和硕玛勒昻吉尔在位三年夀四十三起辇谷皇太后塔纳竒纳复治国事

    Explanation

    7

    The Legitimate Succession of the Sage Emperors

    The founding ancestor, Bodonchar.
    The illustrious ancestor, divine origin emperor, Yisüngge, of the Qutu clan.
    The he who responds to heaven, initiates fortune, saintly warrior emperor, Temüjin, known in Mongolian as Chinggis.
    In the twelfth month of the year 1206 CE, he declared himself Khan on the Onon River. He reigned until the seventh month of the year 1227 CE, when he died at the Sarayu River, having ruled for twenty-two years and lived to be sixty-six. He was buried in the Quriltai Valley.
    The great emperor, literary emperor, Ögedei.
    In the eighth month of the year 1225 CE, he ascended the throne at Gurban Sutai. He reigned until the eleventh month of the year 1241 CE, when he died on the Khulanshan Mountain, having ruled for thirteen years and lived to be fifty-six. He was buried in the Quriltai Valley. His six consorts, Töregene Khatun and Naimanjin Khatun, took turns ruling in his absence.
    The wise ancestor, benevolent and saintly, illustrious and extensive emperor, Tolui.
    Posthumously honored by Möngke Khan.
    The settled emperor, simple and peaceful emperor, Guyuk.
    From the second year of the Renyin period (1222 CE) to the fourth year of the Yihai period (1225 CE), the empress regnant ruled. In the seventh month of the year 1225 CE, she returned power to Guyuk, who ascended the throne at Dalan Daba. He reigned until the third month of the year 1248 CE, when he died at the Hoshomaralanggir, having ruled for three years and lived to be forty-three. He was buried in the Quriltai Valley. The empress dowager, Töregene Khatun, resumed ruling the state.

    8

    宪宗桓肃皇帝【讳孟克】
    　　宋淳祐十一年辛亥六月即位于奎腾敖拉至宋开庆元年己未七月二十七日癸亥崩于钓鱼山在位九年寿五十
    　　世祖圣德神功文武皇帝【讳呼必赉国语曰色辰】
    　　宋景定元年庚申四月一日戊辰即位于开平建元中统至至元三十一年甲午正月十九日庚午崩于紫檀殿在位三十五年寿八十起辇谷中统四至元三十一
    　　裕宗文恵明孝皇帝【讳珍戬】
    　　成宗追諡
    　　顺宗昭圣衍孝皇帝【讳达尔玛巴拉】
    　　武宗追谥
    　　成宗钦明广孝皇帝【讳特穆尔国语曰谔勒哲图】
    　　至元三十一年甲午四月十四日甲午即位于上都改至元三十二为元贞至大德十一年丁未正月八日癸酉崩于玉徳殿在位十三年寿四十二起辇谷元贞二大德十一
    　　武宗仁惠宣孝皇帝【讳哈尚国语曰库鲁克】
    　　大德十一年丁未五月十一日甲戌即位于上都十二月诏改大徳十二为至大至四年辛亥正月八日庚辰崩于玉徳殿在位四年夀三十一葬起辇谷至大四

    Explanation

    8

    Xuanzong Huansu Emperor [Hui Sheng]

    • Ascended to the throne in June of the eleventh year of the Chunyou era (1251) at Kuiten Ola, and died on the twenty-seventh day of the seventh month of the first year of the Kaiqing era (1259) at the Fishing Mountain. He reigned for nine years and lived to be fifty.

    Shizu Shende Shengong Wenwu Emperor [Huobila, Mongolian name: Secen]

    • Ascended to the throne on the first day of the fourth month of the first year of the Jingding era (1260) at Kaiping, and established the era name Zhongtong. He died on the nineteenth day of the first month of the thirty-first year of the Zhongtong era (1294) at the Zitan Hall. He reigned for thirty-five years and lived to be eighty. Buried in Qilin Valley. Zhongtong 4 to the thirty-first year of Zhongtong.

    Yuzong Wenhui Mingxiao Emperor [Zhenjian]

    • Posthumously given this title by Chengzong.

    Shunzong Zhaosheng Yanyao Emperor [Darmabala]

    • Posthumously given this title by Wuzong.

    Chengzong Qinming Guangxiao Emperor [Temür, Mongolian name: Erezetu]

    • Ascended to the throne on the fourteenth day of the fourth month of the thirty-first year of the Zhongtong era (1294) at Shangdu, and changed the era name from the thirty-second year of Zhongtong to Yuanzhen. He died on the eighth day of the first month of the eleventh year of the Dade era (1307) at the Yude Hall. He reigned for thirteen years and lived to be forty-two. Buried in Qilin Valley. Yuanzhen 2 to the eleventh year of Dade.

    Wuzong Renhui Xuanxiao Emperor [Hashang, Mongolian name: Kuruq]

    • Ascended to the throne on the eleventh day of the fifth month of the eleventh year of the Dade era (1297) at Shangdu, and decreed to change the twelfth year of Dade to Zhida. He died on the eighth day of the first month of the fourth year of the Zhida era (1311) at the Yude Hall. He reigned for four years and lived to be thirty-one. Buried in Qilin Valley. Zhida 4.
    9

    仁宗圣文钦孝皇帝【讳阿裕尔巴哩巴特喇国语曰布延图】
    　　至大四年辛亥三月十八日庚寅即位于大明殿九月壬子诏改至大五为皇庆至延祐七年庚申正月二十一日辛丑崩于光天宫在位九年夀三十六起辇谷皇庆二延祐七
    　　英宗睿圣文孝皇帝【讳硕迪巴拉国语曰格根】
    　　延祐七年庚申三月十一日庚寅即位十二月乙巳诏改延祐八为至治至三年癸亥八月四日癸亥遇弑崩于上都途中南坡行幄在位四年夀二十一从诸帝陵至治三
    　　显宗光圣仁孝皇帝【讳噶玛拉】
    　　泰定追諡今出庙
    　　泰定皇帝【讳伊苏特穆尔元封晋王】
    　　至治三年癸亥九月四日癸巳即位于上都龙居河十二月丁亥诏改至治四为泰定至五年戊辰二月庚申改致和七月十日庚午崩文宗追废在位五年夀二十六　泰定四　泰定五改致和文宗即改天厯

    Explanation

    9

    Emperor Renzong, Holy Literature, Revered Filial Piety, [named A-yu-er-ba-li-ba-te-la, known in Mongolian as Bu-yen-tu]
    In the fourth year of the Zhida era, on the 18th day of the third month, on the day Gengyin, he ascended the throne in the Daming Hall. In the ninth month, on the day Renzi, he issued an edict to change the fifth year of the Zhida era to the Huangqing era. In the seventh year of the Yanyou era, on the 21st day of the first month, on the day XinChou, he died in the Guangtian Palace. He reigned for nine years and was thirty-six years old at his death. He was buried in Qiniu Valley. The second year of the Huangqing era and the seventh year of the Yanyou era.

    Emperor Yingzong, Wise and Holy Literature, Revered Filial Piety, [named Shuo-di-ba-la, known in Mongolian as Ge-gen]
    In the seventh year of the Yanyou era, on the 11th day of the third month, on the day Gengyin, he ascended the throne. In the twelfth month, on the day Yisi, he issued an edict to change the eighth year of the Yanyou era to the Zhizhi era. In the third year of the Zhizhi era, on the 4th day of the eighth month, on the day Kuihai, he was assassinated and died en route in the southern slope of the capital. He reigned for four years and was twenty-one years old at his death. He was buried in the imperial tombs. The third year of the Zhizhi era.

    Emperor Xianzong, Radiant and Holy Literature, Revered Filial Piety, [named Ga-ma-la]
    In the Taiping era, he was posthumously honored with the title of Xianzong and is now enshrined in the temple.

    Emperor Taiping, [named Yi-su-te-mu-er, originally enfeoffed as Prince of Jin]
    In the third year of the Zhizhi era, on the 4th day of the ninth month, on the day Kuiyi, he ascended the throne in the capital of Shangdu at Longju River. In the twelfth month, on the day Dinghai, he issued an edict to change the fourth year of the Zhizhi era to the Taiping era. In the fifth year of the Taiping era, on the 7th day of the second month, on the day Gengshen, he changed the era name to Zhizhi. On the 10th day of the seventh month, on the day Gengwu, he died. Emperor Wen posthumously demoted him. He reigned for five years and was twenty-six years old at his death. The fourth year of the Taiping era. The fifth year of the Taiping era, changed to Zhizhi. Emperor Wen then changed it to Tianli.

    10

    明宗翼献景孝皇帝【讳和实拉国语曰胡土克图】
    　　天厯二年己巳正月二十八日丙戌即位于和宁北八月二日大驾次翁郭察图六日暴崩不改元在位八月夀三十起辇谷
    　　文宗圣明元孝皇帝【讳图卜特穆尔国语曰济雅图】
    　　致和元年戊辰九月十三日壬申即位于大明殿改元天厯诏让大兄明宗明年己巳五月帝发京师北迎八月二日丙戌遇于呼察图庚寅明宗暴崩己亥复即位于上都至三年庚午五月戊午改至顺至三年壬申八月十二日己酉崩在位五年夀二十九起辇谷后至元六年庚辰六月丙申以帝谋不轨使明宗饮恨而崩诏撤其庙主　天厯二　至顺三
    　　宁宗冲圣嗣孝皇帝【讳伊埒哲伯】
    　　至顺三年壬申十月四日庚子即位于大明殿至十一月十六日壬午崩不改元年七嵗葬起辇谷
    　　今上皇帝【御名托欢特穆尔】
    　　至顺四年癸酉六月八日己巳即位于上都十月戊辰改元元统至三年乙亥十一月辛丑改至元至七年正月一日改至正元统二　至元六　至正今二十六年氏族

    Explanation

    10

    Emperor Wuzong of Yuan, posthumously titled Mingzong Yixian Jingxiao, whose personal name was Heshila and was known in the national language as Hutuke Tu.

    In the second year of the Tianli era (1329), on the twenty-eighth day of the first month, he ascended the throne at He'ning in the north. On the second day of the eighth month, the imperial entourage stopped at Wengguo Chatu. On the sixth day, he suddenly died, without changing the era name. He reigned for eight months and died at the age of thirty, being buried in the Qiniangu Valley.

    Emperor Wen of Yuan, posthumously titled Wenzong Shengming Yuanxiao, whose personal name was Tumuer and was known in the national language as Jiyatu.

    In the first year of the Zhihe era (1330), on the thirteenth day of the ninth month, he ascended the throne at the Daming Palace and changed the era name to Tianli. He issued an edict ceding the throne to his elder brother, Emperor Wuzong. In the following year, on the fifth day of the fifth month, the emperor departed from the capital to the north to meet him. On the second day of the eighth month, they met at Hucha Tu. On the seventh day of the same month, Emperor Wuzong suddenly died. On the sixteenth day of the same month, the emperor ascended the throne again at Shangdu. In the third year of the era (1333), on the fifteenth day of the fifth month, he changed the era name to Zhishun. In the third year of the era (1337), on the twelfth day of the eighth month, he died. He reigned for five years and died at the age of twenty-nine, being buried in the Qiniangu Valley. In the sixth year of the Houzhixuan era (1340), on the seventeenth day of the sixth month, an edict was issued stating that the emperor had plotted to overthrow the throne, causing Emperor Wuzong to die in grief. The edict ordered the removal of his temple name.

    Emperor Ningzong, posthumously titled Chongsheng Shixin, whose personal name was Ilejebu.

    In the third year of the Zhishun era (1337), on the fourth day of the tenth month, he ascended the throne at the Daming Palace. On the sixteenth day of the eleventh month, he died. He did not change the era name and was seven years old at the time of his death, being buried in the Qiniangu Valley.

    Emperor Toghan Temür, the current reigning emperor, whose personal name is Tuohuan Temür.

    In the fourth year of the Zhishun era (1338), on the eighth day of the sixth month, he ascended the throne at Shangdu. On the fifteenth day of the tenth month, he changed the era name to Yuanzhong. In the third year of the era (1342), on the twenty-eighth day of the eleventh month, he changed the era name to Zhixuan. In the seventh year of the era (1345), on the first day of the first month, he changed the era name to Zhizheng. He reigned for twenty-six years, and his lineage is recorded here.

    11

    古七十二种
    　　阿尔拉　扎拉尔岱　库克新莽固岱　翁吉喇特鸿和岱　云吉喇实　乌尔固　果尔罗斯　必
    　　噜岱　齐哩克岱　图卜岱　巴哩勒呼　竒哩娄伊埒齐勤　扎拉济兰　图噜卜岱　塔塔尔
    　　哈塔锦　萨里岱　济雅岱　娄珠克岱　雅克布哈岱　塔拉尔约苏　沙卜珠岱　黙勒吉岱　阿尔德哷济达　鄂勒对　拜达勒岱　玛勒岱　伊克穆尔哈喇　纳延济达　阿尔德哷济达　伊奇尔岱　和塔济达　摩哩齐　谔尔古岱　鄂摩克岱　阿喇勒岱　巴约特　丹济岱　卫喇特　黙尔吉济特　硕岱台　鸿和岱
    　伯苏岱　雅克布色岱　穆尔温塔岱　莽固岱　塔塔尔岱　纳延济达　阿尔徳哷济达　忽神　塔尼勒　乌噜克岱　沙卜珠岱　黙勒吉　阿古拉里达　扎玛勒岱　乌噜勒岱　布德吉鼐满岱　塔塔尔岱　伊克埒哈喇　昻吉岱　卓啰岱　固岱　鄂摩克岱　图鲁卜岱　巴琳　彻尔济达　巴尔固岱哈达岱　卫喇特

    Explanation

    11

    The 72 Ancient Tribes

    Alara, Zaral Dai, Kukxin Manggu Dai, Wangjilat Honghe Dai, Yungilash, Wurugu, Guoroluo, Bilu Dai, Qilike Dai, Tub Dai, Balirehu, Qililou Yilai Qiqin, Zarajilan, Tulub Dai, Tatar, Hatajin, Saridai, Jiyai Dai, Louzhuke Dai, Yakubuha Dai, Talar Yusu, Shabuzhu Dai, Moleri Dai, Alderji Da, Eledu, Baidale Dai, Maler Dai, Ikemulhala, Nayenji Da, Alderji Da, Yichir Dai, Hetaji Da, Morizi, Eerudu, Emodu Dai, Alale Dai, Bayot, Danji Dai, Weilat, Merji Zhet, Suodaitai, Honghe Dai, Besu Dai, Yakubusai Dai, Muervintai Dai, Manggu Dai, Tatar Dai, Nayenji Da, Alderji Da, Hushen, Tanile, Wuruku Dai, Shabuzhu Dai, Moleri, Agulali Da, Zamale Dai, Wurule Dai, Budujinman Dai, Tatar Dai, Ikeli Hala, Angi Dai, Zhuoluo Dai, gu Dai, Emodu Dai, Tulub Dai, Balin, Cherji Da, Balugudai Hadada, Weilat.

    12

    色目三十一种
    　　哈喇娄　钦察　唐古　阿克苏　图巴　喀喇科尔罗　拉察岱　奇彻勒岱　辉和尔　回回鼐满岱　阿勒呼木　和罗岱　和尔拉　萨拉噶土伯特　永和尔岱　密持斯　巴勒哩　科尔
    　　罗丹　珪齐　实喇娄　图尔哈　哈喇济达岱卓尔齐岱　图鲁卜岱　和尔拉　噶玛拉　齐徳徳哩　克实宻尔
    　　汉人八种
    　　契丹　髙丽　女直　准台　珠尔噶岱　哲衮卓沁台　渤海【女直同】
    　　金人姓氏
    　　完顔汉姓曰王　乌库哩曰商　赫舍哩曰髙　图克坦曰杜　纽祜禄曰郎　乌雅曰朱　富察曰李　延扎曰张温特赫曰温　舒穆噜曰萧　鄂屯曰曹　富珠哩曰鲁伊喇曰刘　沃哷曰石　纳喇曰康　尔佳曰仝　费摩曰麻　尼玛哈曰鱼　威准曰赵　阿克展曰雷　阿里罕曰何　温都曰空　乌噜曰惠　穆延曰孟　都哷曰强萨塔曰骆　阿布哈曰田　乌凌阿曰蔡　布萨曰林　珠格曰董　果尔嘉曰汪

    Explanation

    12

    Thirty-one kinds of colors:
    　　Haraluo, Qinqia, Tanggu, Akusu, Tuba, Kalakeluo, Lachade, Qicheledai, Huihe, Huilunaimandai, Alehumu, Helodai, Helala, Saragatubote, Yonghelodai, Micsi, Baleri, Kuer
    　　Luodan, Guizhi, Shilaluo, Tuluha, Halajidadaizhuoerqida, Tulubudai, Helala, Gamarla, Qididelili, Keshimier
    　　Eight kinds of Han people:
    　　Khitan, Goryeo, Nüzhi, Zhunta, Zhuerqada, Zhegunzhuoqintai, Bohai [same as Nüzhi]
    　　Surnames of the Jin people:
    　　Wanyan, Han surname called Wang; Wukuli, called Shang; Heseli, called Gao; Tuketan, called Du; Niuhuluo, called Lang; Wuya, called Zhu; Fucha, called Li; Yanzha, called Zhang; Wenteh, called Wen; Shumuru, called Xiao; Eton, called Cao; Fuzhilu, called Lu; Ila, called Liu; Wole, called Shi; Nala, called Kang; Qierjia, called Tong; Femu, called Ma; Nimaha, called Yu; Weizun, called Zhao; Akexuan, called Lei; Alihan, called He; Wendo, called Kong; Wulu, called Hui; Muyan, called Meng; Dule, called Qiang; Sata, called Luo; Abuha, called Tian; Wulinga, called Cai; Busa, called Lin; Zhuge, called Dong; Guojia, called Wang.

    13

    平江南
    　　至元十一年甲戌宋之咸淳十年也秋七月世祖命中书右丞相巴延总制大军取宋谕之若曰朕闻曹彬不嗜杀人一举而定江南汝其体朕心法彬事毋使吾赤子横罹锋刃巴延叩首奉命惟谨既而混一职方岂非不嗜杀人之验与独松闗
    　　明年乙亥春诸郡望风降败丞相巴延遣员外郎石天麟诣阙奏闻世皇喜顾谓侍臣曰朕兵已到江南宋之君臣必知畏恐兹若遣使议和邀索嵗币想无不从者遂勅巴延按兵乃命礼部尚书亷希贤侍郎严忠范计议官宋德秀秘书丞柴紫芝等赍奉国书使宋次建康希贤等借兵卫送巴延曰方今两军相阨互有设险宜令行人先往道意便拥兵前

    Explanation

    13

    Translation:

    Pacifying Jiangnan
    In the eleventh year of the Zhiyuan era (1274), which was the tenth year of the Xianchun era of the Song dynasty, during the seventh month of autumn, Emperor Shizu (Kublai Khan) appointed Bayan, the Right Chancellor of the Secretariat, to command the main army against the Song. He instructed him, saying, "I have heard that Cao Bin did not enjoy killing, and with a single campaign, he pacified Jiangnan. You must embody my intentions and follow Cao Bin's example, so that our people do not suffer needlessly from the violence of war." Bayan bowed deeply and accepted the order with utmost diligence. Soon after, the territories were unified under Yuan administration, which was a testament to the principle of minimizing civilian casualties, as seen at the Dusong Pass.
    The following year, in the second month of spring, various regions surrendered at the sight of the advancing Yuan forces. Chancellor Bayan sent the Censorial Officer Shi Tianlin to the imperial court to report this victory. Emperor Shizu was pleased and turned to his attendants, saying, "My troops have reached Jiangnan. The Song rulers and officials must surely know fear. If they now send envoys to negotiate peace and request annual tribute, I believe they will comply." He then ordered Bayan to hold his position and commanded the Minister of Rites, Lian Xixian, the Censorial Officer Yan Zhongfan, the Censorial Officer Song Deshuo, the Secretary of the Imperial Library, Chai Zizhi, and others to carry the imperial letter to the Song court, with their next stop being Jiankang (modern-day Nanjing). Lian Xixian and his entourage were to be accompanied by troops for protection. Bayan said, "At present, both armies are in a state of mutual entrapment, each with its own defenses. It would be wise to send the envoys first to convey the intent of the mission, and then proceed with the troops if necessary."

    14

    进吾恐别生罅隙则和议之事必难成矣希贤等坚请乃简阅鋭卒五百畀之至独松闗戍闗者宋浙西安抚司参议官张濡也以为北兵叩闗率众掩击杀忠范执希贤希贤亦病创死世皇闻之大怒趣进攻嗟夫宋之亡也非有桀纣之恶特以始之以拘留使者肇天兵之兴终之以误杀使者激世皇之怒耳借使独松之使不死宋之存亡未可知其亦有数也与
    　　浙江潮
    　　明年正月甲申丞相巴延驻军臯亭山宋奉表及国玺以降遣千户囊嘉特等入城慰谕令居民门首各贴好投拜三字及闻益王广王如婺州即命分兵屯守诸门范文虎安营浙江沙浒太皇太后望祝曰海若有灵当使波涛大作一洗而空之潮汐三日不至军马宴然文虎吕文焕壻安庆守臣降于我者

    Explanation

    14

    I fear that new rifts may arise, and thus the matter of peace negotiations will surely be difficult to achieve. Xixian and others persistently requested, and thus five hundred elite soldiers were selected and given to them. At the Dusong Pass, the officer guarding it was Zhang Ru, the Censorial Officer of the Zhejiang West Pacification Commission. He believed that the northern troops had attacked the pass and killed Zhongfan and captured Xixian. Xixian also died from his wounds. The Great Emperor heard of it and became greatly angry, urging an advance. Alas, the fall of Song was not due to the wickedness of Jie and Zhou, but rather because it began with the detention of envoys, which initiated the heavenly army's campaign, and ended with the mistaken killing of envoys, which incited the Great Emperor's anger. If the envoy at Dusong had not died, the survival or demise of Song might have been uncertain; it was also fated.

    Zhejiang Tide

    In the following year, on the day of Jia Shen in the first month, Chancellor Bayan stationed his army at Gaoting Mountain. The Song Dynasty submitted a memorial and its imperial seal in surrender, and sent officials such as Nang Jiat to enter the city to comfort and instruct the residents, ordering them to post the words "good surrender" at the entrances of their homes. Upon hearing that Prince Yi and Prince Guang had arrived in Wuzhou, he immediately ordered troops to garrison the city gates. Fan Wenhao set up camp at Shahu, Zhejiang. The Empress Dowager looked out over the sea and wished, "If the sea has any spirit, let it cause great waves to wash everything away." The tides did not come for three days, and the troops remained calm. Fan Wenhao, the son-in-law of Lu Wenhuan, the governor of Anqing, surrendered to us.

    15

    宋兴亡
    　　宋之兴始于后周恭帝显徳七年恭帝方八岁及其亡也终于少帝徳祐元年少帝时四岁名显而显徳二字竟与得国时合周以主幼而失国宋亦以主幼而失国周有太后在上宋亦有太后在上始终兴亡之数昭然如此
    　　万岁山
    　　万岁山在大内西北太液池之阳金人名琼花岛中统三年修缮之其山皆以玲珑石叠垒峰峦暎松桧隆郁秀若天成引金水河至其后转机运汲水至山顶出石龙口注方池伏流至仁智殿后有石刻蟠龙昂首喷水仰出然后东西流入于太液池山上有广寒殿七间仁智殿则在山半为屋三间山前白玉石桥长二百尺直仪天殿后殿在太液池中圆坻上十一楹正对万岁山山之东也为灵囿奇兽珍禽在焉车驾岁巡上都先宴百官于此浙省参

    Explanation

    15

    The Rise and Fall of the Song Dynasty

    The rise of the Song dynasty began in the seventh year of the Xiande era during the reign of Emperor Gong of Later Zhou, when he was just eight years old. Its fall occurred in the first year of the Deyou era during the reign of the young emperor, who was only four years old. The name "Xian" coincidentally matches the founding era "Xiande." Both the Zhou and Song dynasties lost their states due to young rulers, and both had dowager empresses in power. The rise and fall of these dynasties are clearly evident in the historical record.

    The Mountain of Ten Thousand Years

    The Mountain of Ten Thousand Years is located in the northwest of the imperial palace, on the southern side of Taiye Pool. During the Jin dynasty, it was known as Qionghua Island. In the third year of the Zhongtong era, it was renovated. The mountain is constructed entirely of finely carved stones, with peaks and valleys that cast shadows, and pine and cypress trees that are lush and verdant, appearing as if they were created by nature. The Jinshui River is diverted to the rear of the mountain, where a mechanism draws water to the summit, which then flows out through the mouth of a stone dragon, filling a square pool. The water then flows underground to the rear of the Renzhi Hall, where a stone-carved coiled dragon spouts water upward, before the water flows east and west into Taiye Pool. On the mountain, there is a Guanghan Hall with seven rooms, and the Renzhi Hall is located halfway up the mountain, with three rooms. A white marble bridge, 200 chi long, stretches from the front of the mountain to the rear of the Yitian Hall. The rear hall is situated on a circular island in the middle of Taiye Pool, facing directly across from the Mountain of Ten Thousand Years. To the east of the mountain is a sacred garden, where rare beasts and exotic birds can be found. Each year, when the emperor tours the Upper Capital, he first holds a banquet for the officials here, with the Zhejiang provincial governor in attendance.

    16

    政齐达尔尝云向任留守司都事时闻故老言国家起朔漠日塞上有一山形埶雄伟金人望气者谓此山有王气非我之利金人谋欲厌胜之计无所出时国已多事乃求通好入贡既而曰他无所冀愿得某山以镇压我土耳众皆鄙笑而许之金人乃大发卒凿掘辇运至幽州城北积累成山因开挑海子栽植花木营构宫殿以为游幸之所未几金亡世皇徙都之至元四年兴筑宫城山适在禁中遂赐今名云留守司在宫城西南角楼之南专掌宫禁工役者
    　　大军渡河
    　　世皇取江南大军次黄河苦乏舟楫夜梦一老曰陛下欲渡河当随我来引至一所指曰此即是已帝遂以物标识之及觉歴歴可记明日循行河浒寻梦中所见处果是方惊顾间忽有人进曰此间水浅可渡时帝征梦中语因谓曰汝能先涉否其人乃行大军自后从之无一不济帝欲重旌其功对曰富与贵悉非所愿但得自在足矣遂封为达尔罕与五品印拨三百戸以食之今其子孙尚有存者此事杨元诚太史瑀所云

    Explanation

    16

    Cheng Qi Dalai once said that when he was serving as an officer of the Left Guard Office, he heard from the old people that the country had risen from the northern steppes, and on the frontier there was a mountain with a majestic appearance. The Jin people's diviners believed that this mountain had a kingly aura, which was not to their advantage. The Jin people planned to suppress it, but they had no plan for how to do so. At that time, the country was already in turmoil, so they sought to establish friendly relations and send tributes. They then said, "We have no other hopes; we only wish to obtain a certain mountain to suppress our own qi." Everyone laughed at them but agreed. The Jin people then mobilized troops to excavate and transport the mountain, piling it up in front of the northern gate of Youzhou City, forming a mountain. They then excavated a lake, planted flowers and trees, and constructed palaces, to serve as a place for imperial tours and enjoyment. Not long after, the Jin dynasty fell, and the World Emperor (Kublai Khan) moved the capital there. In the fourth year of the Zhiyuan era (1267), the imperial city was constructed, and the mountain happened to be within the imperial palace grounds, and thus was given its current name. The Left Guard Office was located south of the southwest corner tower of the imperial city, responsible for overseeing the construction and maintenance of the imperial city.

    The main army crossed the river. The World Emperor (Kublai Khan) conquered the southern territories, and the main army camped by the Yellow River, lacking boats and rafts. One night, he dreamed of an old man who said, "If Your Majesty wishes to cross the river, follow me." He led him to a place and said, "This is it." The emperor then marked the place with a sign. Upon waking, he could clearly remember it. The next day, he followed the riverbank and found the place he had seen in his dream. It was indeed the place, and just as he was surprised, someone said, "The water here is shallow enough to cross." At that time, the emperor recalled his dream and asked, "Can you cross first?" The person then crossed, and the entire army followed, and none failed to cross. The emperor wanted to reward him greatly, but the person replied, "Wealth and status are not my desires; I am content with freedom." He was then enfeoffed as Dalaihan, given a fifth-rank seal, and granted three hundred households as his fief. Today, his descendants still exist. This is what Yang Yuancheng, the historian Yu, said.

    17

    檄
    　　世皇下江南檄枚举贾似道无君之罪宋国臣民其有不诚服者与其文曰宅中图大天开一统之期自北而南雷动六师之众先谓吊民而伐罪盖将用夏而变夷欲制江浙以削平极汝海隅而混一堪嗟此宋信任非人处之师相之尊委以国柄之重世济其恶真凶悖之贾充谋及乃心效奸雄之曹操不学无识舞术弄权夸浒黄仅免其身比河清莫大之绩承君之宠如彼之专贪天之功确乎不拔惜官爵以总寳货苛条法以苦贤才夺土田而无地可耕变关会而物价溢涌借鄙猥者伴食于庙堂任反侧者失兵于边徼恬视雷星之召异罔闻水火之降灾满朝皆其私人用将因其重赂用

    Explanation

    17

    Proclamation Against Jia Sidao

    O Emperor of the World, the time for a unified empire has come. From the north to the south, the six armies move like thunder, aiming to console the people and punish the guilty. We will use the ways of the Central Plains to transform the barbarians and unify the Jiang-Zhe region. How pitiable is this Song, trusting the wrong people and placing them in the highest positions of the military and government, entrusting them with the heavy responsibility of governing the country. They continue their evil ways, emulating the treacherous Jia Chong and Cao Cao, uneducated and lacking knowledge, playing with power and manipulating it, praising trivial matters to avoid punishment, and claiming the credit for what is due to heaven and firmly refusing to give it up. They value official positions and accumulate wealth, imposing harsh laws to torment talented people, seizing land so that there is no land left to farm, changing the currency system so that prices soar, appointing lowly and contemptible people to high positions, entrusting those who are disloyal with military command at the borders, indifferently watching the signs of disaster, and ignoring the warnings of water and fire disasters. The entire court is filled with his cronies, and generals are appointed based on heavy bribes.

    18

    白劄而破世守之法曲丹笔而容天讨之刑民心已离而不知天命将革而未悟方且贪湖山之乐聚寳玉之珍弗顾母死夺制以贪荣乃乘君宠立幼而固位以已峻功硕徳而自比于周公欺人寡妇孤儿反不如于石勒深懐祸慝自肆奸邪合正两观之诛可纾百姓之怒我大元皇帝聪明智睿神武慈仁焚香祝天誓莫杀而混海宇振兵畧地随所向而宣皇威一战乘胜而渡江诸将列降而献土厥角稽首迎我前矛后实先声易如破竹昭天顺人信之助成风行草偃之功合宇宙以清寜苏人民而镇抚恩寛幼主以下罪止元恶之身自今檄到应守令以境土投拜除大支犒赏外仍其官职谨檄

    Explanation

    18

    A White Decree Breaks the Laws of Generations, and with a Red Pen, it Allows the Punishment Decreed by Heaven. The people's hearts have already turned away, and they do not know that the mandate of heaven will change and have not yet realized it. Greedily enjoying the pleasures of lakes and mountains, gathering precious jewels and treasures, without regard for the death of his mother, he seizes power to gain honor. Taking advantage of the emperor's favor, he installs a young heir to secure his position, boasting of his great achievements and virtues, comparing himself to Duke of Zhou. He deceives widows and orphans, acting worse than Shizhè. Deeply harboring evil, he indulges in wickedness, combining the punishment of the two palaces, which can alleviate the anger of the people. Our great Yuan emperor, intelligent, wise, divinely powerful, and benevolent, burns incense and prays to heaven, swearing not to kill and to unify the world. Mobilizing troops and conquering territory, following wherever and proclaiming the emperor's authority, winning a decisive battle and crossing the river. Generals surrender in succession, surrendering territory, bowing deeply to welcome our advancing troops. Substance follows form, as easy as breaking bamboo. Manifesting the will of heaven and following the will of the people, achieving the success of the wind and grass bending. Unifying the universe for peace, relieving the people and pacifying the land. Mercy for the young ruler, punishing only the chief villain. From now on, upon receipt of this edict, local officials should surrender their territories and submit. Apart from the major rewards and bonuses, their positions will remain unchanged. Carefully issued this edict.

    19

    朝仪
    　　大元受天命肇造区夏列圣相承至于世皇至元初尚未遑兴建宫阙凡遇称贺则臣庶皆集帐前无有尊卑贵贱之辨执法官厌其喧杂挥杖击逐之去而复来者数次翰林承防王文忠公磐时兼太常卿虑将贻笑外国奏请立朝仪遂如其言
    　　科举
    　　皇庆癸丑冬十一月诏曰其以皇庆三年八月天下郡县兴其贤者能者充赋有司明年二月会试京师中选者朕将亲防焉按遗山元公好问所撰廉访使杨文宪公奂墓碑云太宗即位之十年戊戌开举选特诏宣徳课税使刘公用之试诸道进士公试东平两中赋论第一奏授河南路征收课税所长官兼廉访使则国朝科举之设已肇于此寥寥七十余年而布延图皇帝克不坠祖宗之令典尊号曰仁不亦宜乎初焉试论赋盖反宋金余习后则一以经学为本非复向时比矣

    Explanation

    19

    Translation

    Court Etiquette
    In the Great Yuan, receiving the mandate of heaven to establish the Central States, successive emperors passed down the rule until Emperor Shizu of the Zhongyuan era. In the early years of his reign, there was no time to build palaces. Whenever congratulations were offered, officials and commoners would gather before the tent, without any distinction between ranks and classes. The law enforcer was annoyed by the noise and chaos, using a staff to drive them away, but they came back several times. At that time, Wang Wenzhong, a high-ranking official and scholar, who was also the Grand Councillor of Ceremonies, feared that this would be laughed at by foreigners, and proposed to establish court etiquette. His proposal was followed.

    Imperial Examination
    In the eleventh month of the winter of the year of the Earth Rat (癸丑) during the reign of Emperor Renzong (皇庆), an edict was issued: "Let it be in the eighth month of the third year of the reign of Renzong, in all counties and prefectures, promote the wise and capable, to fill the roles of officials. The following February, hold the metropolitan examination in the capital. Those who pass the examination, I will personally interview them." According to the tombstone of Yang Wenxian, the Censor, written by Yuan Hao, during the tenth year of the reign of Ögedei Khan (太宗) in the year of the Earth Rooster (戊戌), examinations were held. The emperor specially appointed Liu Gong, the tax collector, to test the candidates from various regions. Liu Gong passed the examination in Dongping, ranking first in both the composition and essay sections, and was appointed as the director of the tax collection office in Henan and concurrently as the censor. Thus, the establishment of the imperial examination system in the Yuan dynasty began here. After more than seventy years, Emperor Buyantu (布延图) did not fail to uphold the good traditions of his ancestors, and it is fitting to call him the Benevolent Emperor. Initially, the examination included both essays and compositions, against the lingering practices of the Song and Jin dynasties. Later, it was based entirely on Confucian learning, and it was no longer the same as before.

    20

    江南謡
    　　汲郡王公玉堂嘉话云宋未下时江南謡云江南若破百鴈来过当时莫喻其意及宋亡盖知指丞相巴延也
    　　白道子
    　　太宗时诸国来朝者多以冒禁应死耶律文正王楚材进奏曰愿无污白道子从之盖国俗尚白以白为吉故也
    　　官不致仕
    　　大徳七年诏内外官年及七十并听致仕时郭守敬字若思顺徳邢台人知太史院事以旧臣且熟朝廷所施为独不许其请至今翰林太史司天官不致仕者咸自公始
    　　达尔罕
    　　达尔罕译言一国之长得自由之意非戚不与焉太祖龙飞日朝廷草创官制简古惟左右万户次及千户而已丞相顺徳忠献王【哈喇哈逊】之曾祖竒彻勒以英材见遇擢任千戸锡号达尔罕至元壬申世祖録臣后拜王宿衞官袭号达尔罕
    　　皇族列拜
    　　己丑秋八月太宗即皇帝位耶律文正王时为中书令定册立仪礼皇族尊长皆令就班列拜尊长之有拜礼盖自此始

    Explanation

    20

    Translation

    江南謡

    The Collection of Anecdotes from the Jade Hall of Jixian states that during the time when the Song dynasty had not yet fallen, there was a saying in Jiangnan: "If Jiangnan falls, a hundred wild geese will come over." At the time, no one understood its meaning. It was only after the fall of the Song dynasty that it was realized that the saying referred to Chancellor Bayan.

    白道子

    During the reign of Emperor Taizong, many foreign states came to pay homage, and some were guilty of violating prohibitions and should have been executed. Yelü Chucai, the Grand Secretary, proposed to the emperor: "I wish not to defile the White Path." The emperor agreed, as it was the national custom to value white and consider it auspicious.

    官不致仕

    In the seventh year of the Dade era, an edict was issued allowing officials inside and outside the court, who reached seventy years of age, to retire. At that time, Guo Shoujing, known as Ruosi, from Xingtai, Shunde, was in charge of the Imperial Astronomical Bureau. As an old minister well-versed in the court's actions, he alone was not allowed to retire. Since then, all officials of the Hanlin Academy, Imperial Astronomical Bureau, and those responsible for observing the heavens have followed this precedent and do not retire.

    达尔罕

    The term "Darhan" translates to "one who has the freedom of a ruler." Only close relatives are included in this title. On the day of the dragon's flight, when the court was newly established, the official system was simple and ancient, with only the Left and Right万户 (万户) and then the千户 (千户) appointed. The ancestor of Prince of Shunde, a loyal and distinguished minister, was Qicheler. He was highly regarded and appointed as a千户 (千户), and given the title of Darhan. In the ninth year of the Zhizheng era, the emperor honored the loyal ministers and appointed Qicheler's descendant as a宿卫官 (宿卫官), inheriting the title of Darhan.

    皇族列拜

    In the month of August in the year Ji Chou, Emperor Taizong ascended the throne. At that time, Yelü Chucai, the Grand Secretary, established the rituals for the enthronement. All the respected elders of the imperial family were ordered to stand in line and bow. The practice of elders bowing to each other began from this point.

    21

    内八府宰相
    　　内八府宰相八员视二品秩而不降授宣命特中书照会之任而已寄位于翰林之缫琳缫琳宫门外院官会集处也所职视草制诏赦之文则非其掌也至于院之公事亦不得与焉例以国戚与贵之子弟充之
    　　伊勒都齐
    　　国朝有四集赛台官集赛者分宿衞供奉之士为四畨畨三昼夜凡上之起居饮食诸服御之政令集赛之长皆总焉中有伊勒都齐乃侍御之至亲近者虽官随朝诸司亦三日一次轮流入直骨朶于肩佩环刀于要或二人四人多至八人时若上御控则在墀陛之下盖所以虞奸回也虽宰辅之日觐清光然有所奏请伊勒都齐在固不敢进今中书移咨各省或有须备録奏文事者内必有伊勒都齐某等以此之故余又究骨朶字义尝记宋景文笔记云关中人以腹大为【上音孤下音都】俗音谓杖头大者亦曰后譌为骨朶朶平声
    　　大汉
    　　国朝镇殿将军募选身躯长大异常者充凡有所请给名曰大汉衣粮年过五十方许出官

    Explanation

    21

    Translation

    Inner Eight Ministries' Chancellors

    The Inner Eight Ministries' Chancellors are eight officials ranked as the second highest, but they are not appointed through an imperial decree; instead, they are appointed by the Secretariat for a specific duty. They are stationed within the翰林院, specifically at the meeting place of officials outside the宫门. Their primary responsibility is to draft imperial edicts and decrees, including those related to pardons and amnesty, but they do not handle the official business of the院. By convention, these positions are filled by the sons of imperial relatives and nobles.

    伊勒都齐

    In the current dynasty, there are four集赛台官, who are divided into four shifts. Each shift is responsible for the imperial guard for three days and nights, handling all matters related to the emperor's daily life, diet, and attire. The集赛台官 are in charge of these duties, and among them are the伊勒都齐, who are the closest attendants to the emperor. Even though they are officials of various departments, they must report to the emperor every three days, wearing a special belt (骨朶) and a ringed dagger. The number of伊勒都齐 can vary from two to eight. When the emperor is riding, the伊勒都齐 stand below the steps of the throne. This is to prevent any potential threats. Even high-ranking officials must wait for the伊勒都齐 to leave before presenting their petitions. Currently, the Secretariat consults with provinces, and if there is a need to record or draft official documents, the伊勒都齐 are always included. This is why I have also researched the meaning of骨朶. I recall from Song Jingwen's notes that in Guanzhong, people refer to a large abdomen as (pronounced "gū dōu"). The common pronunciation for a large handle is also, and later it was mistakenly written as骨朶, which is pronounced "gǔ dōu."

    大汉

    In the current dynasty, the镇殿将军 are special guards or bodyguards selected for their extraordinary physical stature. They are appointed to handle all requests and provisions, and they are called大汉. They can only be discharged from service after they reach the age of fifty.

    22

    珪齐
    　　珪齐者快行是也每歳一试之名曰放走以脚力便捷者膺上赏故监临之官齐其名数而约之以绳使无后先参差之争然后去绳放行在大都则自河西务起程若上都则自泥河儿起程越三时走一百八十里直抵御前俯伏呼万岁先至者赐银一饼余者赐段匹有差
    　　实保齐
    　　实保齐鹰房之执役者每嵗以所养海青有获头鵞者赏黄金一锭头鵞天鵞也以首得之又重过三十余斤且以进御膳故曰头

    　　辍耕録卷一
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷二　　　　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰圣聪
    　　至元六年二月二十五日上御玉徳殿命史臣榻前草诏黜谪太师巴延诏文有云其各领所部诏书到日悉还本衞上曰自蚤至暮皆一日也可改日字作时字时巴延以飞放为名挟持皇太子在柳林意将犯分诏既成遣中书平章济尔噶岱赍至彼处开读奉皇太子归国而各枝军马即时散去盖一字之中利害系焉亶聪明作元后于此有以见之矣

    Explanation

    22

    Translation:

    Gū Qí

    The Gū Qí is a fast-running event, tested annually and called the "Release Run." Those with agile legs receive the highest reward. Therefore, the officials in charge align their names and numbers and bind them with ropes to ensure there is no dispute over who is ahead or behind. Then, the ropes are removed, and they set off. In Dadu, they start from Xihu Wù, and in Shangdu, they start from Ní Hé Ér. They must cover one hundred and eighty li in three hours, facing directly forward, bowing down, and shouting "Long live the emperor!" The first to arrive is given a silver coin, and the rest are given silk and cloth, with differences.

    Shí Bǎo Qí

    The Shí Bǎo Qí refers to the servants of the falconry. Each year, those who capture the head goose from the geese they raise are rewarded with a gold ingot. The head goose is a swan, captured by the head, and weighs over thirty pounds. It is presented to the imperial table, hence called the head goose.

    Chuò Gēng Lù Juàn Yī

    Qīn Dìng Sì Kù Quán Shū

    Chuò Gēng Lù Juàn Èr

    On the twenty-fifth day of the second month of the sixth year of the Zhì Yuán era, the emperor held a court in the Jade Virtue Hall and ordered the historian to draft an edict to demote the Grand Tutor Bāyǎn. The edict reads: "Each shall lead their own troops. On the day the edict arrives, all shall return to their original posts." The emperor said, "From morning until night, it is still one day. Can the character 'day' be changed to 'time'?" At that time, Bāyǎn used the pretext of a flying release to hold the crown prince hostage in the Willow Forest, intending to violate the proper order. After the edict was completed, it was sent by the Chancellor Jì'ěrgǎdài to read it there. The crown prince was returned to the country, and all branches of the army disbanded immediately. The fate depends on a single character, truly wise to be the first ruler, as seen here.

    23

    隆师重道
    　　文定王实喇巴尔今上之师也为学士时尝在上左右一日体少倦遂于便殿之侧偃卧因而就寐上因以借坐方褥国语所谓都尔伯珍者亲扶其首而枕之后尝患疖额上上于金鉢中取佛手膏躬与贴之上之隆师重道可谓至矣尽矣王字敬臣号山斋辉和尔人受佛戒
    　　累朝皇帝先受佛戒九次方正大寳而近侍陪位者必九人或七人译语谓之囊达实此国俗然也今上之初入戒坛见玛哈噶拉佛前有物为供因问学士实喇巴尔曰此何物曰羊心上曰曾闻用人心肝者有诸曰尝闻之而未尝目睹请问喇嘛喇嘛者帝师也上遂命实喇巴尔防问之荅曰有之凡人萌歹心害人者事觉则以其心肝作供耳以此言复奏上再命问曰此羊曾害人乎帝师无荅
    　　减御膳

    Explanation

    23

    Reverence for Teachers and the Way

    Wen Ding Wang Shilabar was the teacher of the current emperor. When he was a scholar, he often attended the emperor's side. One day, feeling a little tired, he lay down on the side of a convenience hall and fell asleep. The emperor then supported his head with a square cushion, as referred to in the national language as "Duerberzhen," and used it as a pillow. Later, he suffered from a boil on his forehead. The emperor took Buddha's hand ointment from a golden bowl and personally applied it. The emperor's reverence for teachers and the Way can truly be said to be utmost and complete. Wang's name was Jingchen, and he was known as Shan Zhai Huihe. He had received the Buddhist precepts.

    Emperors of many dynasties had received the Buddhist precepts nine times before ascending the throne, and those who attended closely and accompanied must be nine or seven people, as referred to in the language as "Nangdashi," which is the custom of the country. When the current emperor first entered the ordination hall, he saw an offering before the Mahakala Buddha. He asked the scholar Shilabar, "What is this?" Shilabar replied, "It is a sheep's heart." The emperor said, "Have I heard of using human hearts and livers?" Shilabar replied, "I have heard of it but have never seen it." The emperor then asked the Lama, who is the emperor's teacher. The emperor then ordered Shilabar to ask the Lama, who replied, "There is such a thing. When a person harbors evil intentions and harms others, and the matter is discovered, their heart and liver are used as an offering." The emperor then asked, "Has this sheep ever harmed anyone?" The Lama did not answer.

    The emperor then reduced his imperial diet.

    24

    国朝日进御膳例用五羊而上自即位以来日减一羊以岁计之为数多矣
    　　圣俭
    　　太府少监赫噜奏取黄金三两为御鞾刺花用上曰不可因请易以银而镀金者上曰亦不可金银首饰也今民间所用何物对曰用铜上曰可右五事杨太史瑀所言太史居官时日侍上故知其详
    　　后徳
    　　今上皇太子之正位东宫也设谕徳置端本堂以处太子讲读忽一日帝师来啓太子母后曰向者太子学佛法顿觉开悟今乃使习孔子之教恐壊太子真性后曰我虽居于深宫不明道徳尝闻自古及今治天下者须用孔子之道舍此他求即为异端佛法虽好乃余事耳不可以治天下安可使太子不读书帝师赧服而退
    　　端本堂
    　　皇太子方在端本堂读书近侍之尝以飞放从者輙臂鹰至廊庑间喧呼驰逐以惑乱之将勾引出游为乐太子授业毕徐令左右戒之曰此读书之所先生长者在前汝辈安敢防狎如此急引去毋召责也众皆惊惧而退右二事乃贡尚书师泰授经宣文阁下日所目见者至正丙申间避地云间每谈朝廷典故因及此

    Explanation

    24

    The emperor of our dynasty typically uses five sheep for his daily meals. Since he ascended the throne, he has reduced the number of sheep used by one each day. In terms of annual calculation, this reduction is considerable.

    The Virtue of Frugality

    The Taifu Shaomon Herlu presented a request to take three taels of gold for the embroidery on the emperor's shoes. The emperor said, "No, it is not permissible." Herlu then asked if it could be replaced with silver that is plated with gold. The emperor said, "Also not for gold and silver ornaments." When asked what people use nowadays, the response was, "They use copper." The emperor said, "That can be done."

    The Wisdom of the Empress

    One day, the imperial tutor came to inform the crown prince's mother and grandmother. He said, "The crown prince has been studying Buddhism and has become very enlightened. Now, he is being taught Confucianism, which might corrupt his true nature." The empress replied, "Although I live in the palace, I do not understand the principles of morality. I have heard that throughout history, those who govern the country must use the way of Confucius. If one seeks other ways, they are considered heretical. Although Buddhism is good, it is a secondary matter and cannot be used to govern the country. How can we allow the crown prince to stop reading?"

    The Study Hall

    The crown prince was studying at the Study Hall when a servant who had been hunting brought an eagle to the corridor, shouting and running around, causing a disturbance. The crown prince finished his lesson and calmly instructed his attendants to warn the servant, "This is a place for studying. How dare you behave so rudely? Go away immediately, and do not summon me to scold you." The servant was shocked and left.

    These two stories were witnessed by Gong Shangshu Shitai, who taught at the Xuanwen Pavilion, every day. In the year Zhenzheng Geng Shen, he fled to Yunjian and often talked about these court matters.

    25

    征聘
    　　中书左丞魏国文正公鲁斋许先生衡中统元年应召赴都日道谒文靖公静修刘先生因谓曰公一聘而起毋乃太速乎荅曰不如此则道不行至元二十年征刘先生至以为赞善大夫未几辞去又召为集贤学士复以疾辞或问之乃曰不如此则道不尊治天下匠
    　　中书令耶律文正王楚材字晋卿在金为燕京行省员外郎国亡归于我朝从太祖征伐诸国夏人巴勒津者以治弓见知于上诧王曰本朝尚武而明公欲以文进不已左乎王曰且治弓尚须弓匠岂治天下不用治天下匠耶上闻之喜自是用王益密
    　　以官为氏
    　　中书平章政事廉希宪字善甫封恒阳王諡文正本辉和尔氏王之父布鲁堪为回鹘王归朝官至顺徳诸路宣慰使封魏国公諡孝懿拜廉访使之命时适王生顾曰是儿必大吾门吾闻古者以官受氏天将以廉氏吾宗乎吾其从之举族承命

    Explanation

    25

    Translation:

    Summoning

    In the first year of Zhongtong, Xu Heng, the Left Chancellor of the Central Secretariat and the Righteous and Virtuous Duke of Wei, was summoned to the capital. On his way, he met Liu Yin, the Peaceful and Quiet Duke of Wenjing, who said to him, "You have accepted the summons and come immediately. Isn't this too hasty?" Xu Heng replied, "If not done this way, the Dao will not be implemented." In the twentieth year of Zhizhi, Liu Yin was summoned again and appointed as a Zan Shang Daifu. After a short time, he resigned. He was then summoned again to serve as a Jixian Xueshi but declined due to illness. When asked why, he said, "If not done this way, the Dao will not be respected. Governing the country requires skilled individuals."

    Yelü Chucai, the Chief Secretary of the Central Secretariat and the Righteous and Virtuous Duke of Wenjing, was known as Wang Jinqing. During the Jin Dynasty, he served as an official in the Yansheng Province. After the fall of the Jin Dynasty, he returned to the Yuan Dynasty and followed the First Emperor in his campaigns against various states. A Xian man named Balajin was known for making bows and was favored by the emperor. He remarked to Wang, "Our dynasty values martial prowess, yet you wish to advance through literary means. Is this not a mistake?" Wang replied, "Even in making bows, one needs a skilled bowmaker. How can governing the country not require skilled individuals?" The emperor heard this and was pleased, and from then on, he used Wang more frequently and confidentially.

    Taking one's surname from one's official position:

    Lian Xiyan, the Chancellor of the Central Secretariat, was known as the Good Father and was posthumously honored as the Righteous and Virtuous Duke. Originally from the Huiheer Clan, his father, Bulukan, was the King of the Uyghurs. Upon returning to the Yuan Dynasty, he served as the Pacification Commissioner of Shunde and other regions, and was posthumously honored as the Righteous and Virtuous Duke. When his son was born, he said, "This child will surely bring great honor to our family. I have heard that in ancient times, surnames were derived from official positions. Heaven will surely give us the surname Lian. I will follow this tradition, and the whole family will comply."

    26

    受孔子戒
    　　世祖一日命廉文正王受戒于国师王对曰臣已受孔子戒上曰汝孔子亦有戒耶对曰为臣当忠为子当孝孔门之戒如是而已上喜
    　　不食死
    　　谢君直先生枋得号叠山信州弋阳人宋景定甲子江东漕闱校文发防问权奸误国赵氏必亡忤贾似道贬兴国军三年遇赦得还天兵南下郡城溃弃家入闽至元二十三年御史程文海承防留梦炎等交荐累召不赴二十六年春正月福建行省参知政事魏天祐复被诏防集守令戍将迫蹙上道临行以诗别常所往来者曰雪
    中松柏愈青青扶植纲常在此行天下岂无龚胜洁人间不独伯夷清义髙便觉生堪舍礼重方知死甚轻南八男儿终不屈皇天上帝眼分明夏四月至京师不食死年六十有四秋八月子定之奉柩归葬门人诔而题之曰文节先生谢公墓嗟乎伯夷叔齐在周虽为顽民而在商则为义士孰谓数千载后有商义士之风者复见先生焉

    Explanation

    26

    Final Translation

    Receiving the Precepts of Confucius

    One day, Emperor Shizu ordered Prince Liang Wen Zheng to receive precepts from the National Teacher. Prince Liang replied, "I have already received the precepts of Confucius." The emperor asked, "Do you also have precepts from Confucius?" Prince Liang answered, "As a minister, one should be loyal; as a son, one should be filial. The precepts of Confucius are nothing more than these." The emperor was pleased.

    Died by Refusing to Eat

    Mr. Xie Fangde, respectfully known as Mr. Xie, the gentleman of integrity, was from Yizhang, Xinzhou. During the year Jiazi of the Jingding era of the Song dynasty, he was a literary examiner in the Jiangdong region. He was asked a question about powerful villains misguiding the country and the inevitable downfall of the Zhao family. He opposed the corrupt official Jia Sidao and was exiled to the Xingguo army. After three years, he was pardoned and returned home. When the Mongol forces advanced south, the city fell, and he abandoned his home to go to Fujian. In the twenty-third year of the Yuan dynasty, the censor Cheng Wenhai, along with Liu Mengyan and others, recommended him repeatedly, but he refused to serve. In the twenty-sixth year of the Yuan dynasty, in the first month of spring, the vice-premier Wei Tianyou of the Fujian provincial government was again ordered to gather local officials and military leaders, forcing him onto the road. On his way out, he parted with poetry, saying to those he often associated with:

    "Snowy pines and cypresses grow greener still,
    Upholding the norms of propriety in this journey.
    Is there no one like Gong Sheng in the world?
    Not just Bo Yi and Shu Qi are pure and righteous.
    With such high righteousness, one can feel that life is worth giving up.
    With such great respect for ritual, one realizes that death is quite light.
    The Southern Eight men will never yield.
    The eyes of the Heavenly God are clear.
    In the fourth month of summer, he arrived in the capital and died by refusing to eat, at the age of sixty-four. In the eighth month of autumn, his son Ding carried his coffin back to be buried. His disciples wrote an epitaph and inscribed it on his tomb, calling him 'Mr. Xie, the gentleman of integrity.' Alas, Bo Yi and Shu Qi, though they were considered stubborn subjects in the Zhou dynasty, were righteous men in the Shang dynasty. Who would have thought that after thousands of years, the spirit of a righteous man from the Shang dynasty would reappear in Mr. Xie?"

    27

    染髭
    　　中书丞相史忠武王天泽髭髯已白一朝忽尽黑世皇见之惊问曰史巴图汝之髯何乃更黑邪对曰臣用药染之故也上曰染之欲何如曰臣览镜见髭髯白窃伤年且暮尽忠于陛下之日短矣因染之使而报效之心不异畴昔耳上大喜人皆以王捷于奏对推此一事则余可知矣汉人赐名巴图者惟王与太师张献武王范及真定新军张万户兴祖耳杀虎张
    　　真定新军张万戸兴祖中山无极人至元十九年丞相楚国文定公阿尔哈雅以中书右丞南取汉郢公实从有功授前职平生射虎数十一日遇虎一发而踣语人曰吾闻生虎之髭剔齿疾可已风因拔之虎怒爪鞾裂赖其气息垂尽不能伤足由是人目之曰杀虎张后赐名巴图尔讹作防突又讹抜都都与突字虽异而声相近盖译语无正音故也

    Explanation

    27

    Dyeing the Beard
    　　The Chancellor of the Central Secretariat, Shi Tianze, whose beard had turned white, suddenly turned black one day. The Emperor, upon seeing this, was surprised and asked, "Shi Batu, why has your beard turned black again?" Shi replied, "I have dyed it with medicine." The Emperor asked, "Why did you dye it?" Shi said, "When I looked in the mirror and saw my beard turn white, I was saddened by the thought that my time to serve Your Majesty is running out. Therefore, I dyed it black to continue serving with the same loyalty as before." The Emperor was very pleased, and everyone remarked that Wang's quick and clever response in this matter showed his skill in court.

    Those given the name Batu were only Shi Tianze, Grand Tutor Zhang Hongfan, and Zhang Xingzu of the Zhen Ding New Army. Zhang Xingzu was known as "Kill-Tiger Zhang."
    　　Zhang Xingzu, the leader of the Zhen Ding New Army, was from Wuge in Zhongshan. In the nineteenth year of the Zhiyuan era (1282), Chancellor Alghu, a Duke of Chu and a State of Wen Ding, was appointed as the Right Chancellor of the Central Secretariat and led a southern campaign to conquer Han and Jing. Zhang Xingzu, who had previously served with merit, was given his former position. Throughout his life, he hunted tigers frequently. One day, he encountered a tiger and shot it down in one shot. He told others, "I heard that the whiskers of a living tiger can cure toothache. I pulled them out to try to cure the wind." The tiger, enraged, scratched his boots, but its breath was nearly gone, and it could not cause further harm. Thus, people called him "Kill-Tiger Zhang." Later, he was given the name Batu, which was later mispronounced as "Phantuck" and then as "Batu." Although the characters are different, the sounds are similar, as there is no standardized pronunciation in the language.

    28

    御史举荐
    　　姚文公先生燧为中台监察御史时忽御史大夫谓曰我天子以汝贤故擢居耳目之官今且岁余至如兴利除害之事未尝有片言及之但惟以荐举为务何邪先生荅曰某所荐者百有余人皆经世之才其在中外并能上禆圣治则某之报効亦勤矣又何待屑屑于兴利除害然后为监察御史之职任乎大夫曰真宰相器也叹赏久之
    　　切谏
    　　太宗素嗜酒晩年尤甚日与大臣酣饮耶律文正王数言之不听一日持酒槽之金口以进曰此乃铁耳为酒所蚀尚致如此况人之五脏有不损耶上说赐以金帛仍勑左右日惟进酒三钟而止夫以王之切谏不已而上终纳之可谓君明臣良者矣
    　　丁祭
    　　内翰王文康公鹗字百一开州东明人国初自保定应聘北行时故人马云汉以宣圣画像为赠既达北庭值秋丁公奏行释奠礼世祖说即命举其事公为祝文行三献礼礼毕进胙于上上既饮福熟其胙命左右均霑所赐自是春秋二仲岁以为常盖上之所以尊师重道者实公有以啓之也

    Explanation

    28

    Translation

    Imperial Censor's Recommendations
    When Yao Wen Gong (姚文公) served as a Censor at the Central Court, the High Censor (御史大夫) suddenly said to him, "The Emperor has elevated you to the position of an eye and ear because of your virtue. Now, after more than a year, you have not even mentioned once the matters of promoting benefits and eliminating harms, but instead you are solely focused on recommending people. Why is this so?" Yao replied, "I have recommended over a hundred individuals, all of whom are talents capable of managing the world. They can all contribute to the governance of the state, both within and outside the court. Thus, my service has been diligent. Why should I be overly concerned with promoting benefits and eliminating harms to fulfill the duties of a Censor?" The High Censor then said, "You truly possess the qualities of a prime minister," and praised him for a long time.

    Direct Advice
    Emperor Taizong had always enjoyed drinking, especially in his later years, where he would often drink heavily with his ministers. Yelü Chucai (耶律文正王) repeatedly advised him to cut down, but the emperor did not listen. One day, Yelü Chucai presented the golden mouth of the wine vat to the emperor and said, "This is made of iron, yet it is still corroded by the wine. How much more so would the five organs of a person be damaged?" The emperor was persuaded and rewarded Yelü Chucai with gold and silk. He then ordered his attendants to limit his daily intake to three cups of wine. Indeed, the emperor's acceptance of Yelü Chucai's persistent advice shows the qualities of a wise ruler and a loyal minister.

    Festival Sacrifice
    Wang Wenkang (王文康公), also known as Wang Baiyi, was from Dongming in Kaizhou. At the beginning of the Yuan dynasty, he traveled north from Baoding to accept an invitation. His old friend Ma Yunhan gave him a portrait of Confucius as a gift. Upon reaching the northern court, during the autumn Ding festival, Wang proposed to perform the ritual of presenting offerings to the sages. Kublai Khan was pleased and ordered the ritual to be carried out. Wang wrote the prayer text and performed the three-offering ceremony. After the ceremony, he presented the sacrificial meat to the emperor. The emperor drank the blessed wine and ate the cooked sacrificial meat, then ordered his attendants to distribute the gifts equally. From then on, this ritual became a regular event during the middle months of spring and autumn. Indeed, the emperor's respect for teachers and emphasis on moral principles were greatly influenced by Wang Wenkang.

    29

    髙学士
    　　国朝儒者自戊戌选试后所在不务存恤往往混为编氓至于奉一札十行之书崇学校奬秀艺正户籍免徭役皆翰林学士髙公智耀奏陈之力也公河西人今学校中往往有祠之者
    　　大黄愈疾
    　　丙戌冬十一月耶律文正王从太祖下灵武诸将争掠子女玉帛王独取书籍数部大黄两驼而已既而军中病疫惟得大黄可愈所活几万人吁廉而不贪此固清慎者能之若其先见之明则有非人之所可及者
    　　置台宪
    　　御史台至元五年置秩从二品二十一年陞正二品大徳十一年陞从一品台有大夫一人后增一人中丞二人后又增二人随复故侍御史二人治书侍御史二人殿中侍御史二人治朝着之事典事二人掌幙府文书之事后改为都事三人后又以都事之长蒙古若色目一人为经歴检法二人后废管勾三人其一人兼照磨监察御史十二人后增至十六人皆汉人又增蒙古色目人如汉人之数今三十二人至元十四年既取宋置南行台二十七年専莅江南之地号

    Explanation

    29

    High Scholar
    　　Scholars of the Yuan Dynasty, after the Wuxu selection examination, were often neglected and treated as commoners. The promotion of schools, encouragement of talented individuals, correction of household registers, and exemption from corvée labor were all due to the efforts of the high-ranking scholar Gao Zhiyao, who was from the Hexi region. Today, there are often shrines dedicated to him in schools.
    　　Rhubarb Curing Illness
    　　In the month of November of the Bing Xu year, Prince Yelü Chuci, following Emperor Taizu, conquered Lingwu. The generals vied to seize women, children, and valuable goods, but Prince Yelü Chuci alone took several volumes of books and two camels of rhubarb. Soon after, an epidemic broke out among the troops, and only rhubarb could cure it, saving nearly ten thousand lives. How clean and incorruptible he was! This is something that even the most cautious and clean officials could achieve. However, his foresight was truly unparalleled.
    　　Establishing the Office of the Censorate
    　　The Censorate was established in the fifth year of Emperor Shizu's reign, with a rank of the second grade. In the twenty-first year, it was promoted to the first grade. In the eleventh year of Emperor Chengzong's reign, it was further promoted to the first grade. The Censorate had one Grand Censor, who was later increased to two. There were two Vice Censors, who were later increased to four, but then reduced back to two. There were two Vice Censors for oversight, two for legal affairs, and two for court duties, who were responsible for administrative matters. There were two officials for record-keeping, who later became three officials, with one of them being a Mongol or Turkic official. There were two officials for legal matters, who were later abolished. There were three officials for management, with one of them also serving as an auditor. There were twelve officials for inspection and oversight, who were later increased to sixteen, all of whom were Han Chinese. Later, the number of Mongol and Turkic officials was increased to match the number of Han Chinese officials, making a total of thirty-two. In the fourteenth year of Emperor Shizu's reign, after conquering the Southern Song, the Southern Censorate was established. In the twenty-seventh year, it was specifically responsible for the Jiangnan region.

    30

    江南诸道行御史台秩如内台而监察御史今二十四人西行台初由云南廉访司陞行台大徳元年移治陜西号陜西诸道行御史台莅陜西甘肃四川云南之地延祐间暂废随复其官秩如南台而监察御史今二十人各道提刑按察司至元六年置正三品有使副使佥事察判经歴知事二十八年改肃政廉访司使副使佥事各二人大司农奏罢各道劝农司以农事归宪司增佥事二人经歴知事照磨各一人今天下凡二十二道始建台时大夫则塔齐尔也内御史署衘
    　　内监察御史署衔无御史台三字以为天子耳目之官非御史大夫以下所可制也行台则不然
    　　令史
    　　国朝凡省台院吏曰掾史独江南行台作令史者盖縁至元十四年初立行台日御史大夫授三品秩故也后虽陞一品而乐因循者不为申明改正西台立视南台已陞品秩则曰掾史焉

    Explanation

    30

    The rank of the Jiangnan Circuit Censorate is the same as that of the Inner Censorate, and the number of Censorate Censors is now twenty-four. The Western Censorate was initially established from the Yunnan Censorate and, in the first year of the Dade era, was moved to Shaanxi and named the Shaanxi Circuit Censorate, overseeing the regions of Shaanxi, Gansu, Sichuan, and Yunnan. During the Yanyou period, it was temporarily abolished and then reinstated with the same rank as the Southern Censorate, and the number of Censorate Censors is now twenty. The Circuit Criminal Inspection and Censorate Offices were established in the sixth year of the Zhizhi era with a rank of Third Class, with an Envoy, Deputy Envoy, Inspector, Judge, and Clerk. In the twenty-eighth year, it was changed to the Censorate and Inspection Office, with an Envoy, Deputy Envoy, and Inspector, each two people. The Grand Controller of Agriculture proposed abolishing the Circuit Agricultural Promotion Offices and transferring agricultural affairs to the Censorate, adding two Inspectors, one Clerk, and one Scrivener. Today, there are twenty-two circuits in total. When the Censorate was first established, the Grand Censor was Taqir. The Inner Censorate does not include the three characters "Yushi Tai" in its title, to be the ears and eyes of the Son of Heaven, not subject to the control of the Grand Censor and below. The Censorate is different. Clerk. In our dynasty, officials in the provincial and censorate offices are called Clerks, only the Jiangnan Censorate uses the title Clerk, because in the fourteenth year of the Zhizhi era, when the Censorate was first established, the Grand Censor was granted a rank of Third Class. Later, even though it was upgraded to First Class, those who were content with the status quo did not make any changes. The Western Censorate, having the same rank as the Southern Censorate, uses the title Clerk.

    31

    台字
    　　三台凡公文所书台字并从士从口不敢作其字头若然则伪文也按许氏说文台从至从之从髙省则土乃之之正书耳当从土从口为是
    　　诏西番
    　　累朝皇帝于践祚之始必布告天下使咸知之惟诏西番者以粉书诏文于青缯而绣以白绒网以真珠至御寳处则用瑚遣使赍至彼国张于帝师所居处五刑
    　　国初立法以来有笞杖徒流死之制凡七下至五十七下用笞六十七下至一百七十用杖徒之法徒一年杖六十七一年半杖七十七二年杖八十七二年半杖九十七三年杖一百七此丽徒者杖数也盐徒既决而又镣之使居役也数用七者建元以前皆用成数今匿税者笞五十犯私盐茶者杖七十私宰马牛者杖一百旧法犹有存者大徳中刑部尚书王约数上言国朝用刑寛恕笞杖十减其三故笞一十减为七今之杖一百者宜止九十七不当反加十也议者惮于变更其事遂寝流则南之迁者之北北之迁者之南死则有斩有凌迟而无绞

    Explanation

    31

    Final Translation

    The Character "台"

    In all official documents, the character "台" is written with "士" and "口," and one should not write the top part of the character. If written incorrectly, it is considered false writing. According to the Shuowen Jiezi, the character "台" is composed of "至" (to come), "之" (it), and a simplified form of "高" (high). Therefore, the correct form of the character "台" is "土" (earth) and "之" (it).

    Edicts to the Western Regions

    From the beginning of each emperor's reign, edicts were issued to the Western regions to inform all. Specifically, edicts to the Western regions were written in powder on blue silk, embroidered with white wool, and decorated with real pearls. At the place where the imperial seal was placed, coral was used. These edicts were then carried to the Western regions and displayed at the residence of the emperor's teacher.

    The Five Punishments

    Since the beginning of the dynasty, the five types of punishment have been established: caning, beating with a stick, imprisonment, banishment, and capital punishment. The penalties range from seven strokes to fifty-seven strokes for caning, and from sixty-seven strokes to one hundred and seventy strokes for beating with a stick. For imprisonment, the penalties are as follows: one year with sixty-seven strokes, one and a half years with seventy-seven strokes, two years with eighty-seven strokes, two and a half years with ninety-seven strokes, and three years with one hundred and seven strokes. These are the penalties for imprisonment. After being sentenced, salt smugglers were also shackled and made to perform labor. The use of seven as a penalty was common before the reign of Emperor Jianyuan, when whole numbers were used. Now, tax evaders receive fifty strokes, those who illegally sell salt and tea receive seventy strokes, and those who slaughter horses and cattle privately receive one hundred strokes. Some old laws still exist. During the reign of Emperor Dade, the Minister of Justice Wang Yue repeatedly proposed that the current dynasty's use of punishment be more lenient, reducing the number of canings and beatings by one-third. Therefore, ten strokes of caning were reduced to seven, and the current one hundred strokes of beating should be limited to ninety-seven, not increased by ten. However, those who feared change did not implement this proposal. Banishment could involve moving people from the south to the north or from the north to the south, and the death penalty could include decapitation, slow dismemberment, or hanging.

    32

    钱币
    　　世皇尝以钱币问太保刘文贞公秉忠公曰钱用于阳楮用于隂华夏阳明之区沙漠幽隂之域今陛下龙兴朔漠君临中夏宜用楮币俾子孙世守之若用钱四海且将不靖遂絶不用钱迨武宗颇用之不久輙罢此虽术数防纬之学然验之于今果如所言巴尔斯
    　　河南江北行中书省参知政事姚忠肃公天福字君祥平阳人至元十一年拜监察御史弹击权臣无所顾畏世祖赐名巴尔斯国言虎也后条奏宰相阿哈玛特罪二十有四召廷辩公枚数之彼輙引服数至于三气沮色丧上曰此三者罪巳不在宥因目公曰巴尔斯臣下有违太祖之制干朕之纪者汝抨击毋廷臣皆震悚时方倚相理财姑释不问众人莫不为公危之公之太夫人有贤识朂之曰为国者忘其家汝第尽力効忠果不测吾追踪陵母死日犹生年也公泣谢白其长曰万一得谴乞不以老母连坐语闻上叹曰是母子有古义烈勅侍臣符寳郎董文忠宣付史馆书之

    Explanation

    32

    Coins

    The Great Emperor once asked the Grand Protector Liu Wenzheng, Prince Bingzhong, about the use of coins and paper money. He said, "Coins are used in the bright regions, while paper money is used in the dark regions. The bright and sunny areas of China contrast with the dark and shadowy areas of the desert. Now that Your Majesty has risen from the northern desert and rules over China, it is appropriate to use paper money and make descendants observe it for generations. If coins were used, the whole world would not be peaceful. Thus, they decided not to use coins. Later, Emperor Wuzong briefly revived the use of coins, but not long after, it was abandoned. This is though a matter of divination and occult learning, but when tested in the present, it proved true as said."

    Baers

    Yao Zhongsu, the Grand Councillor of the Henan and Jiangbei Province, was posthumously given the title Tianfu. He was from Pingyang and was appointed as a censor in the 11th year of the Zhizhi era. He impeached powerful ministers without fear, and the Great Emperor gave him the name Baers, which means "tiger" in the language of the state. Later, he listed twenty-four crimes of the Chancellor Ahmad. The emperor called for a court debate, and Yao enumerated them, and he readily confessed. He repeated three times, and his spirit was broken and his color changed. The emperor said, "These three crimes are not pardonable." He looked at him and said Baers. "If any subject violates the regulations of the founding emperor and disrupts my authority, you shall criticize without pity, and all the ministers will be shocked." At that time, he was relying on the chancellor to manage finances, and temporarily released without further investigation. Everyone was worried about him. His mother-in-law had wise insight and encouraged him by saying, "Those who serve the country forget their own family. Just do your best to serve with loyalty. If you are in danger, I will follow the example of Lady Lin, who died for her country while living." He cried and thanked his superior, "If I am punished, ask that my old mother not be implicated." The emperor heard and sighed, "This mother and son have ancient righteousness and valor." He ordered the court attendant and the official in charge of the imperial seals, Dong Wenzhong, to announce and record it in the history archives.

    33

    善谏
    　　至元二十四年僧格之为尚书丞相也专权擅政虐焰薰天贿赂公行畧无畏避中书平章武宁正献王彻尔时为利用监独奋然数其奸赃于上前上怒以为丑诋大臣命左右批其颊王辨不为止且曰臣思之熟矣国家置臣子犹人家畜犬譬有贼至而犬吠主人初不见贼乃棰犬犬遂不吠岂良犬哉上悟收僧格籍其家明日王拜御史中丞余按北史宋游道毕义云奏劾游道杨遵彦曰譬之畜狗本取其吠今以数吠杀之恐将来无复吠犬诏除名则王之以犬自况为有所本矣
    　　使交趾
    　　翰林学士元文敏公明善字复初清河人参议中书日会朝廷遣蒙古大臣一员使交趾公副之将还国之伪主赉以金蒙古受之公固辞伪主曰彼使臣已受矣公独何为公曰彼所以受者安小国之心我所以不受者全大国之体伪主叹服

    Explanation

    33

    Good Advice

    In the twenty-fourth year of the Zhiyuan era, when Sengge served as the Chancellor of the Central Secretariat, he usurped power and governed arbitrarily, with the flames of tyranny reaching the heavens. Bribery was openly practiced, and almost no one showed fear or avoidance. The Middle Secretariat Minister, Prince Wuning, the Righteous and Contributing Wang, Che'er, at that time served as the Supervisor of Utilization. Alone, he bravely enumerated Sengge's corruption and embezzlement in front of the emperor. The emperor was angry and considered his words an insult to a high-ranking official, ordering the attendants to slap his face. Wang defended himself without stopping and said, "I have thought about it thoroughly. The state appoints officials like a household raises dogs. For example, when a thief comes and the dog barks, the master does not see the thief and beats the dog, so the dog no longer barks. Is it a good dog?" The emperor understood and arrested Sengge, confiscating his family's property. The next day, Wang was appointed as the Minister of the Imperial Censorate.

    Mission to Jiaozhi

    The Hanlin Scholar, Yuan Wenshen Gong, Ming Shan, whose courtesy name was Fuchu, and who was from Qinghe, served as a consultant to the Central Secretariat. When the court sent a Mongol high-ranking official on a mission to Jiaozhi, Ming Shan served as his deputy. Upon their return, the false master of the country gave them gold for the Mongols. The Mongol official accepted it, but Ming Shan refused firmly. The false master said, "The envoy has already accepted it. Why do you refuse?" Ming Shan replied, "He accepts it to pacify the small country, while I refuse it to uphold the dignity of the great state." The false master admired and was convinced.

    34

    刻名印
    　　今蒙古色目人之为官者多不能执笔花押例以象牙或木刻而印之宰辅及近侍官至一品者得防则用玉图书押字非特赐不敢用按周广顺二年平章李谷以病臂辞位诏令刻名印用据此则押字用印之始也
    　　国玺
    　　文宗开奎章阁作二玺一曰天厯之寳一曰奎章阁寳命臣虞集文今上作二小玺一曰明仁殿寳一曰洪禧命臣杨瑀文洪禧璞纯白而纽墨色宣文阁
    　　天厯初建奎章阁于西宫兴圣殿之西廊为屋三间髙明敞爽南间以藏物中间诸官入直所北间南向设御座左右列珍玩命群玉内司掌之阁官署衔初名奎章阁阶正三品隷东宫属官后文宗复位乃陞为奎章阁学士院阶正二品置大学士五员并知经筵事侍书学士二员承制学士二员供奉学士二员并兼经筵官幕职置参书二员典籖二员并兼经筵防赞官照磨一员内掾四名内二名兼检讨宣使四名知印二名译史二名典书四名属官则有羣玉内司阶正三品置监羣玉内司一员司尉一员亚尉二员佥司二员典簿一员令史二名典吏二名司钥二

    34

    Translation:

    Carving Name Seals

    Today, many Mongol and Central Asian officials who hold office are unable to write their signatures with a pen and ink; instead, they use ivory or wood to carve and stamp their seals. Ministers and close attendants of the first rank may use jade seals, but one may not use it without a special imperial decree. According to the regulations of the second year of Guangshun during the Later Zhou Dynasty, Chancellor Li Gu, due to illness in his arm, resigned from his position, and the emperor ordered that a name seal be carved for him. This is the beginning of the practice of using seals for signing.

    Imperial Seals

    Emperor Wenzong established the Kuichang Pavilion and created two seals: one called the "Treasure of Tianli" and the other called the "Treasure of Kuichang Pavilion." He ordered Minister Yu Ji to inscribe them. Later, the emperor created two smaller seals: one called the "Treasure of Mingren Hall" and the other called "Hongxi." He ordered Minister Yang Yu to inscribe them. The "Hongxi" seal is made of pure white jade with a turtle-shaped seal knot in black color, and it is kept in the Xuanwen Hall.

    At the beginning of the Tianli era, the Kuichang Pavilion was built in the west wing of the Xing Sheng Hall in the Western Palace. It consisted of three rooms, high, bright, and spacious. The southern room was used to store items, the middle room was where officials attended to their duties, and the northern room had a throne facing south, with treasures and curiosities placed on both sides. The Jade Treasury Office was appointed to manage it. The official title of the Kuichang Pavilion was initially "Kuichang Pavilion, Third Class," under the jurisdiction of the Eastern Palace officials. After Emperor Wenzong resumed his position, it was promoted to the "Kuichang Pavilion Academy of Scholars, Second Class." Five Grand Secretaries were appointed, all in charge of the Imperial Academy. Two Attendant Secretaries, two Scholars in Charge of Imperial Edicts, and two Scholars in Charge of Imperial Services were also appointed, all of whom were also in charge of the Imperial Academy. Officials in charge of secretarial duties included two Assistants in Charge of Writing and two Assistants in Charge of Seals, both of whom were also in charge of the Imperial Academy and advisory duties. One Inspector, four Internal Clerks (two of whom were also in charge of reviewing), four Messengers, two Seal Keepers, two Translators, and four Librarians were also appointed. The official title of the Jade Treasury Office was "Jade Treasury Office, Third Class," with one Supervisor, one Chief, two Deputy Chiefs, two Assistants, one Clerk, two Clerks, and two Key Keepers appointed.

    35

    名司膳四名给使八名専掌秘玩古物艺文监阶正三品置太监兼检校书籍事二员少监同检校书籍事二员监丞防检校书籍事二员或有兼经筵官者典簿一员照磨一员令史四名典吏二名専掌书籍鉴书博士司阶正五品置博士兼经筵参赞官二员书吏一名専一鉴辨书画授经郎阶正七品置授经郎兼经筵译文官二员専一训教集赛官大臣子孙艺林库皆从六品置提典一员大使一员副使一员司吏二名库子一名専一收贮书籍广成局阶从七品置大使一员副使一员直长二员司吏二名専一印行祖宗圣训及国制等书特恩剙制象齿小牌五十

    Explanation

    35

    Four officials in charge of the kitchen, eight servants, and specialists in rare antiquities and artistic works. The Art and Literature Department is a third-rank official position, with two eunuchs also serving as book inspectors, two undersecretaries also serving as book inspectors, and two deputy supervisors also serving as book inspectors. Some may also serve as officials in the imperial academy. One official is responsible for record-keeping, and one for auditing. Four clerks and two lower-ranking officials are in charge of books. The Book Inspection Department is a fifth-rank official position, with two officials serving as consultants in the imperial academy and one clerk specializing in authenticating books. The Teaching Department is a seventh-rank official position, with two officials serving as translators in the imperial academy and specializing in educating the children of high-ranking officials. The Art and Literature Department is a sixth-rank official position, with one official in charge of supervision, one in charge of the department, one deputy, two clerks, and one lower-ranking official in charge of storing books. The Printing Bureau is a seventh-rank official position, with one official in charge of the department, one deputy, two officials in charge of overseeing, two clerks, and specializing in printing imperial edicts and other official documents. A special imperial decree was issued to create fifty small ivory plaques.

    36

    上书奎章阁三字一面字一面蒙古字与辉和尔字分散各官悬佩出入宫门无禁学士院凡与诸司往复惟劄送参书防行移而已命侍读学士虞集撰记御书刻石阁中今上皇帝改奎章曰宣文其记曰大统既正海内定一乃稽古右文崇徳乐道以天厯二年三月作奎章之阁备燕闲之居将以渊潜遐思缉熙典学乃置学士员俾颂乎祖宗之成训毋忘乎创业之艰难而守成之不易也又俾陈夫内圣外王之道兴亡得失之故而以自儆焉其为阁也因便殿之西庑释髙明而有容不加饰乎采斵不重劳于土木不过啓乎牖以顺清燠树阁以栖图书而已至于

    Explanation

    36

    The Emperor Ordered the Inscription of the Kui Zhang Ge

    The Emperor ordered the inscription of the Kui Zhang Ge, with the words "Kui Zhang Ge" on one side and other characters, including Mongolian script and the name "Hui He Er," distributed among officials to be worn as pendants, freely carried in and out of the palace gates. The scholar-officials in the academy conducted all communications with various departments through formal documents and official correspondence. The Emperor ordered the scholar-official and reader, Yu Ji, to write an inscription, which was then carved on stone and placed in the hall. The current Emperor renamed the Kui Zhang Ge to Xuan Wen Ge. The inscription reads: "The great order is established and the empire is unified; thus we examine ancient texts, value literature, cultivate virtue, and enjoy the way. In the third month of the second year of Tianli, the Kui Zhang Ge was built as a place for leisure and contemplation, in order to engage in deep contemplation and far-reaching thoughts, and to compile and study classics. Scholar-officials were appointed to praise the established teachings of our ancestors, to remember the difficulties of founding the dynasty, and the difficulty of maintaining it. They were also to present the way of inner sagehood and outer kingship, the causes of rise and fall, success and failure, to warn oneself. The construction of the hall used the western wing of the convenience hall, with a high and spacious design, without elaborate decoration, without excessive labor on construction, only opening windows to regulate temperature, and setting up shelves to store books. As for all these roles and pursuits, nothing is unhelpful in matters."

    37

    器玩之陈非古制作中法度者不得在列其为处也跬步戸庭之间而清严邃密非有朝会祠享时巡之事几无一日而不御于斯于是宰辅有所奏请宥密有所图维争臣有所绳纠侍从有所献替以次入对从容密勿盖终日焉而声色狗马不轨不物者无因而至前矣自古圣明睿知善于怡心养神培本浚源泛应万变而不穷者未有易乎此者也盖闻天有恒运日月之行不息矣地有恒势水土之载不匮矣人君有恒居则天地民物有所系属而不易矣居是阁也静焉而天为一动焉而天弗违庶乎有道之福以保我子孙民于无穷哉至顺辛未孟春二日记占验

    Explanation

    37

    The arrangement of objects for play or display must adhere to ancient standards and norms, and those not meeting these criteria are not allowed to be included. The place where it is, within the span of a few steps between the door and the courtyard, is clear, strict, and deep. It is not used during times of court gatherings, ancestral worship, or imperial inspections, and there is hardly a day when it is not used. Thus, ministers have matters to present, and in secret, there are plans and thoughts. Officials who dare to correct and reprimand, and attendants with advice to offer, enter in turn to present their matters, engaging in leisurely and meticulous deliberation throughout the entire day. Those who indulge in music, color, dogs, and horses, and who act improperly, do not come before. From ancient times, the wise and enlightened are skilled in soothing the mind and nourishing the spirit, nurturing the root and deepening the source, and adapting to myriad changes without limit. There is nothing easier than this. It is said that heaven has constant motion, and the movement of the sun and moon never stops. The earth has constant form, and the carrying capacity of water and soil is inexhaustible. The ruler has a constant residence, thus heaven, earth, and the people are connected and remain unchanged. In this hall, in stillness, heaven becomes one, and in action, heaven does not oppose. Perhaps this brings the blessings of the Dao to preserve my descendants and the people forever. Recorded on the second day of the first month of the year X in the reign of Emperor Shun.

    38

    傅初庵先生立以占筮起东南时杭州初内附世皇以故都之地生聚浩繁赀力殷盛得无有再兴者命占其将来如何卦既成对曰其地六七十年后会见城市生荆棘不如今多也今杭连厄于火自至正壬辰以来又数燬于兵昔时歌舞之地悉为草莽之墟军旅填门畜豕载道乃知立之占亦神矣立乃番昜祝卜泌甥泌精皇极数
    　　权臣擅政
    　　中书右丞相巴延所署官衔计二百四十六字曰元徳上辅广忠宣义正节振武佐运功臣太师开府仪同三司秦王达尔罕中书右丞相上柱国録军国重事监修国史兼徽政院侍正昭功万戸府都总使虎符威武阿克蘓衞亲军都指挥使司达噜噶齐忠翊侍衞亲军都指挥使奎章阁大学士领学士院知经筵事太史院宣政院事叶赫千戸哈必齐千戸达噜噶齐宣忠乌鲁斯扈衞亲军都指挥使司达噜噶齐提调回回汉人司天监群牧监广惠司内史府左都威衞使司事钦察亲军都指挥使司事宫相都总管府领太禧宗

    Explanation

    38

    Master Li of Fu Chu'an used divination to predict the rise of the southeast. At that time, Hangzhou had initially submitted to the World Emperor, using the land of the former capital. The population and resources were abundant, and the wealth and resources were plentiful. Would there not be a revival? He ordered a divination to predict the future. When the divination was completed, he said, "In sixty to seventy years, the city will be overgrown with thorns, not as it is now." Hangzhou has been repeatedly afflicted by fire since the year Renchen of the Zhi Zheng era, and it has been destroyed several times by war. What was once a place of song and dance is now a wasteland of grass and weeds. Soldiers fill the gates, pigs roam the streets. Thus it is known that Master Li's divination is indeed miraculous. Master Li then engaged in divination and numerology, possibly using the Yijing (Book of Changes) for his predictions.

    Powerful Ministers Usurped Power

    The Right Chancellor of the Central Secretariat, Ba Yan, held an official title consisting of 246 characters. It read: "Original Virtue, Upper Auxiliary, Broad Loyalty, Promote Righteousness, Upright Integrity, Vigorously Martial, Assist in Governance, Meritorious Official, Supreme Commander, Open Government Office, Equal in Rank to the Three Dukes, Prince of Qin, Darhan, Right Chancellor of the Central Secretariat, Supreme Commander, Overseer of Military and State Affairs, Supervisor of National History, Attendant of the Imperial Academy, Director of the Imperial Library, Director of the Imperial Archives, Director of the Imperial Treasury, Director of the Imperial Mint, Director of the Imperial Mint, Imperial Mint, Director of the Imperial Mint, Director of the Imperial Mint, Director of the Imperial Mint, Director of the Imperial Mint, Director of the Imperial Mint, Director of the Imperial Mint, Director of the Imperial Mint, Director of the Imperial Mint, Director of the Imperial Mint, Director of the Imperial Mint, Director of the Imperial Mint, Director of the Imperial Mint, Director of the Imperial Mint, Director of the Imperial Mint, Director of the Imperial Mint, Director of the Imperial Mint, Director of the Imperial Mint, Director of the Imperial Mint, Director of the Imperial Mint, Director of the Imperial Mint, Director of

    39

    禋院兼都典制神御殿事中政院事宣镇侍衞亲军都指挥使司达噜噶齐提调宗人蒙古侍衞亲军都指挥使司事提调哈喇齐额布根彻尔领隆祥使司事当其擅政之日前后左右无非隂邪小辈惟恐献谄进佞之不至孰能告以忠君爱民之事有一王爵者译奏云色辰二字人皆可以为名自世祖皇帝庙号之后遂不敢用今太师巴延功髙徳重可以色辰名字与之时御史大夫特穆尔布哈亦其心腹每隂嗾省臣奏允其请文定王实喇巴尔时为学士从容言于上曰万一曲从所请关系非轻遂命学士欧阳监丞掲徯斯会

    Explanation

    39

    The official who was in charge of the Imperial Temple, the central government, the imperial guard, and other related institutions was a high-ranking official named Ba Yan. During the time when he held power, there were no good people around him, only evil and sycophantic individuals. He feared not being flattered and deceived enough, and no one could tell him about loyal service and caring for the people. A prince who was translating said that the two characters "色辰" could be used as a name. Since the posthumous title of the founding emperor, no one dared to use it. The Grand Tutor Ba Yan, who had great achievements and virtues, it would be appropriate to give him the name "色辰". The Grand Censor Temur Buka, who was also his close associate, often secretly urged provincial officials to agree to his request. Duke Wending Shi La Ba Er, who was a scholar, spoke calmly to the emperor, saying that if we were to agree to his request, it would have serious implications. He then ordered the scholar Ouyang Xuan and the supervisor Jie Xi Si to convene.

    40

    议以元徳上辅四字代之加于功臣之上又典瑞院都事某建言凡省官提调军马者必佩虎符今太师巴延难与他人同宜锡龙凤牌以宠异之制可遂制龙凤牌一面其三珠各函径寸真珠一枚而饰以红埓阿勒寳石牌身脱钑元徳上辅功臣号字仍用白玉嵌造牌成计直数万锭既被贬黜毁其牌就以珠寳给还物主盖督勒有司和买元价尚未酬也又京畿都运尼雅斯拉上言太师巴延功盖世所授宣命难与百官一体合用泥金书词以尊荣之省台院官议不可行宛转禀白止金书上天眷命皇帝圣防八字余仍墨笔云
    　　懐孟蛙

    Explanation

    40

    The proposal was made to replace the title "元徳上辅" and bestow it upon the highest-ranking meritorious officials. A certain official of the Dianrui Institute also proposed that all officials overseeing military matters must wear the tiger amulet. Now, Grand Tutor Ba Yan should be distinguished from others and given a dragon and phoenix plaque to honor him. The proposal was approved, and a dragon and phoenix plaque was then made. Each of the three pearls on the plaque is enclosed in a one-inch shell, with one real pearl, and decorated with red and green jade and gemstones. The plaque's body is engraved with the characters "元徳上辅" and the title "Meritorious Official," still using white jade for inlaying. The plaque's value was calculated to be several tens of thousands of taels. After being demoted, the plaque was destroyed, and the jewels were returned to their owners because the supervising officials had not yet paid the original price for the materials. The Director of the Capital Region, Niyasila, also proposed that Grand Tutor Ba Yan's achievements are unparalleled, and the imperial decree he received is difficult to equate with those of the hundred officials; it should be written in gold ink to honor him. The officials of the Ministry, the Court, and the Academy debated and found it infeasible, so they gently reported back, limiting the gold ink to the eight characters "上天眷命皇帝圣防," with the rest written in black ink.

    41

    大徳间仁宗在潜邸日奉达尔结太后驻辇辉黙图苦群鼃乱喧终夕无寐翼旦太后命近侍传防谕之曰吾母子方愦愦鼃忍恼人耶自后其毋再鸣故至今此地虽有鼃而不作声后仁宗入京诛安西王阿南达等迎武宗即位时大徳十一年也越四年而仁宗继登大寳则知元后者天命攸归岂行在之所虽未践祚而山川神以隂来相之不然则虫鱼微物耳又能听令者乎但迄今不鸣尤可异矣
    　　贼臣摄祭
