    　　至治癸亥十月六日甲子先一夕因晋邸入继大统告祭太庙之顷隂风北来殿上灯烛皆灭良久方息盖摄祭官特克实额森特穆尔齐勤特穆尔等皆弑君之元恶也时全思诚以国子生充斋郎目击之此无他必祖宗威灵在上不使奸臣贼子得以有事于太庙而明示严谴之耳彼徒罪无所逃至于身诛族赤而后已吁可畏哉
    　　叛党告迁地
    　　至元二十四年宗王纳延叛后伏诛徙其余党于庆元之定海县延祐间伊纳托克托公来为浙相其党屡以水土不便为诉乞迁善地公曰汝辈自寻一个不死人的田地当为汝迁之众遂不敢再言

    Explanation

    41

    During the reign of Emperor Chengzong, when Emperor Renzong was in his private residence, he served the Dowager Empress Darjie. One night, while she was staying at the imperial carriage in Huimo, the noise of frogs disturbed her, preventing her from sleeping all night. The next day, the dowager empress ordered the attendants to summon the frogs and said, "My mother and I are so troubled by these frogs; how can they disturb us?" From then on, the frogs did not make a sound, and even today, although there are frogs in this place, they do not make a sound.

    In the eleventh year of the Dade era, Emperor Renzong entered the capital, executed Prince Anxi and others, and welcomed Emperor Wuzong to ascend the throne. Four years later, Emperor Renzong ascended the throne. It is known that the Yuan Dynasty was destined to return to the throne, and even though Emperor Renzong had not yet ascended the throne, the spirits of the mountains and rivers came to assist in secret. Otherwise, small creatures like insects and fish could not have obeyed commands. However, the fact that the frogs still do not make a sound is particularly unusual.

    During the sacrificial ceremony, the officials Tukesen Temur, Qiqin Temur, and others, who were the chief traitors who killed the emperor, extinguished the lights in the temple. At the time, Quan Sicheng, a sacrificial attendant, witnessed this. There is no other reason for this except that the spiritual power of the ancestors was present, preventing the traitors from performing the sacrificial ceremony in the Imperial Ancestral Temple and clearly showing their severe punishment. These traitors had no way to escape their crimes and would be executed and their families exterminated. How terrifying this is!

    In the twenty-fourth year of the Yuan Dynasty, Prince Nayan rebelled and was executed. The remaining rebels were relocated to Dinghai County in Zhejiang. During the Yanyou era, when Prince Nayan's party repeatedly complained about the unsuitable climate and soil, Prince Nayan's son, who came to serve as the governor of Zhejiang, told them, "You must find a place where you can live without dying, and I will relocate you there." The rebels then dared not speak again.

    42

    土人作掾
    　　至元间伯勒齐尔布哈公为江浙丞相议以本省所辖土人不得为掾史时左丞法珠公谓曰若然则中书掾当用外国人为之矣相有赧色议遂不行
    　　萧先生
    　　萧贞敏公字维斗京兆人蚤岁吏于府一日呈牍尹前尹偶坠笔目公拾之公阳为不解而止白所议公事如此者三公曰某所言者王事也拾笔责在皁非吏所任尹怒公即辞退居十五年惟以读书为志从公游者屦交戸外平章咸宁王额森闻其贤荐之于世祖征不至授陜西儒学提举继而成宗武宗仁宗累征授国子司业集贤直学士未赴改集贤侍讲又以太子右谕徳征始至京师授集贤学士国子祭酒寻复得告还山年七十七以夀终諡贞敏端厚
    　　文贞王额琳特穆尔尝言娄师徳唾面自干以为美事我思之虽狗亦不可恶他且如有一狗自卧于地无故以足蹴之或掷以物狗固不便咬人亦吠数声而去却有甚好听处

    42

    During the Yuan Dynasty, Berlikhikhukha, the Chancellor of Jiang-Zhe Province, proposed that local officials should not serve as clerks. At the time, the Left Chancellor, Fazhu, said, "If that is the case, then the clerks of the central government should be foreigners." The Chancellor blushed with embarrassment, and the proposal was ultimately not implemented.

    Xiao, known posthumously as Zhenmin, was a native of Jingzhao. Early in his career, he served as a low-ranking official in the government. One day, while presenting documents to the magistrate, the magistrate accidentally dropped his pen, and Xiao saw him pick it up. Xiao pretended not to understand and stopped, explaining the public matters being discussed. This happened three times. Xiao said, "What I am discussing is state affairs. The responsibility of picking up the pen lies with the low-ranking officials, not with me." The magistrate became angry, and Xiao immediately resigned. He lived in seclusion for fifteen years, devoting himself entirely to reading. People visiting him filled the space outside his door. The Chancellor of Peace and Prosperity, Ensen, heard of his virtue and recommended him to the Emperor, but Xiao refused to come. He was later appointed as the Director of Education in Shaanxi. Under Emperors Chengzong, Wuzong, and Renzong, he was repeatedly summoned and appointed as the Vice Principal of the Imperial Academy, a Direct Scholar of the Imperial Academy, and a Lecturer of the Imperial Academy. He was also summoned to serve as a Right Advisor to the Crown Prince but finally arrived in the capital, where he was appointed as a Scholar of the Imperial Academy and the Principal of the Imperial Academy. Soon, he received permission to return to his mountain retreat and died at the age of seventy-seven. He was posthumously given the title "Zhenmin" (Faithful and Sensitive) and was known for his uprightness and sincerity.

    Wenmin, the Chancellor of Learning, Eren Temur, once said that Lou Shide's practice of enduring humiliation without retaliation was considered a good thing. I think about it, even a dog cannot be hated. For example, there is a dog lying on the ground. Without reason, someone kicks it with their foot or throws something at it. The dog naturally does not bite people but barks a few times and leaves. But there is something very pleasant about it.

    43

    防字
    　　防即卷字真诰中谓一卷为一防或以为吊字及篇字者皆非

    　　辍耕録卷二
    <子部,小说家类,杂事之属,辍耕录>
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷三　　　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰正统辨
    　　至正二年壬午春三月十有四日上御咸宁殿中书右丞相托克托等奏命史臣纂修宋辽金三史制曰可越二年甲申春三月进辽史本纪三十卷志三十一卷表八卷列四十六卷冬十一月进金史本纪一十九卷志三十九卷表四卷列七十三卷又明年乙酉冬十一月进宋史本纪四十七卷志一百六十二卷表三十二卷列世家二百五十五卷初会稽杨维桢尝进正统辨可谓一洗天下纷纭之论公万世而为心者也惜三史已成其言终不见用后之秉史笔而续通鉴纲目者必以是为本矣维桢字廉夫号铁崖人咸称之曰铁史先生泰定丁卯李黼榜相甲及第以文章名当世表曰至正三年五月日伏覩皇帝诏防

    Explanation

    43

    防字
    　　防即卷字，真诰中谓一卷为一防，或以为吊字及篇字者皆非。

    　　辍耕録卷二
    <子部,小说家类,杂事之属,辍耕录>
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷三　　　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰正统辨
    　　至正二年壬午春三月十有四日，上御咸宁殿，中书右丞相托克托等奏命史臣纂修宋辽金三史，制曰可。越二年甲申春三月，进辽史本纪三十卷，志三十一卷，表八卷，列传四十六卷。冬十一月，进金史本纪一十九卷，志三十九卷，表四卷，列传七十三卷。又明年乙酉冬十一月，进宋史本纪四十七卷，志一百六十二卷，表三十二卷，列传世家二百五十五卷。初，会稽杨维桢尝进正统辨，可谓一洗天下纷纭之论，公万世而为心者也。惜三史已成，其言终不见用。后之秉史笔而续通鉴纲目者，必以是为本矣。维桢字廉夫，号铁崖，人咸称之曰铁史先生。泰定丁卯，李黼榜相甲及第，以文章名当世。表曰：至正三年五月日，伏覩皇帝诏防。

    44

    起大梁张京兆杜本等爵某官职専修宋辽金三史越明年史有成书而正统未有所归臣维桢谨撰三史正统辨凡二千六百余言谨表以上者右伏以一代离合之殊固系乎天数盛衰之变万年正闰之统实出于人心是非之公盖统正而例可兴犹纲举而目可备前代异史今日兼修是非之论既明正闰之统可定奈三史虽云有作而一统犹未有归恭惟世祖皇帝以汤武而立国皇帝陛下以尧舜而为君建极建中致中和而育物惟精惟一大一统以书元尝怪辽金史之未成必列赵宋编而全备芸台大啓草泽髙升宜开三百载之编年以垂千万代之大典岂料诸儒之谦笔徒为三国之志书春秋之首例未闻纲目之大节不举

    Explanation

    44

    Starting from Daliang, Zhang Jingzhao and Du Ben were granted titles and positions to specialize in the compilation of the histories of Song, Liao, and Jin. The following year, the history was completed, but the orthodox lineage had not been determined. I, Wei Zhen, respectfully write the San Shi Zhengtong Bian (Discrimination of the Orthodox Lineage of the Three Histories), totaling more than 2,600 words, and respectfully present it here. Respectfully, the founding emperor of our dynasty established the state like Tang and Wu, and Your Majesty rules like Yao and Shun. Establishing the highest and central principles, achieving harmony and nurturing all things, being precise, unified, and one, and recording the origins of the dynasty, I have often wondered why the histories of Liao and Jin were not completed, but they must be included in the compilation of the Song history to be fully comprehensive. The imperial library was opened widely, and commoners rose to high positions. It is appropriate to compile a chronicle spanning three centuries to establish a great legacy for ten thousand generations. How could it be that the modest pens of scholars only wrote the Sanguo Zhi (Records of the Three Kingdoms), and the Chunqiu (Spring and Autumn Annals), the first historical text in China, did not mention the major principles of the Gangmu (Outline and Commentary).

    45

    臣维桢素读春秋之王正月公羊谓大一统之书再观纲目之绍春秋文公有在正统之说故以始皇二十六年而继周统髙祖成功五年而接秦亡晋始于平吴而不始于泰和唐始于灭盗而不始于武徳稽之于古证之于今况当世祖命巴延平江南之时式应宋祖命曹彬下江南之岁亲诏防有过唐不及汉之言确定统宗有继宋不继辽之禅故臣维桢敢痛排浮议力建公言挈大宋之编年包辽金之纪载置之上所用成一代可鉴之书传之将来永示万世不刋之典冒干天听深惧氷兢下情无任瞻天望阙激切屏营之至辨曰正统之说何自而起乎起于夏后国汤武革世皆出于天命人心之公也统出于天命人心之公则

    Explanation

    45

    Minister Wei Zhen has long studied the Gongyang Commentary on the Spring and Autumn Annals, which speaks of the concept of "Great Unity." He has also examined the Zizhi Tongjian, which continues the tradition of the Spring and Autumn Annals and discusses the idea of legitimate succession. Therefore, he considers the 26th year of Qin Shi Huang's reign as the continuation of the Zhou dynasty, and the fifth year of Emperor Gaozu's reign as the continuation of the fall of Qin, with the founding of the Han dynasty. Similarly, the conquest of Wu marks the beginning of the end of the Three Kingdoms period, not the Tahe era of the Northern Song dynasty, and the suppression of bandits marks the beginning of the Tang dynasty, not the Wu德 era. By examining ancient records and verifying with contemporary evidence, Minister Wei Zhen argues that the legitimacy of the Yuan dynasty, which followed the Song dynasty rather than the Liao dynasty, is established. He boldly rejects superficial opinions and firmly establishes his public statement, summarizing the chronological records of the Song dynasty and including the historical records of the Liao and Jin dynasties. He places this work in the hands of those in power to create a book for future generations to learn from, passing it on to the future as an unalterable classic. He dares to speak to the heavens, deeply fearing and trembling, with humble feelings, he gazes up to the heavens and gazes at the imperial palace, feeling deeply moved and anxious. He argues that the concept of legitimate succession originates from the transmission of the Shang dynasty by the Xia, and the rise of the Shang and Zhou dynasties through revolution, all arising from the public will of heaven and humanity. Since the legitimacy of succession arises from the public will of heaven and humanity,

    46

    三代而下厯数之相仍者可以妄归于人乎故正统之义立于圣人之经以扶万世之纲常圣人之经春秋是也春秋万代之史宗也首书王正于鲁史之元年者大一统也五伯之权非不强于王也而春秋必黜之不使奸此统也吴楚之号非不窃于王也而春秋必外之不使僭此统也然则统之所在不得以割据之地强梁之力僭伪之名而论之也尚矣先正论统于汉之后者不以刘蜀之祚促与其地之偏而夺其统之正者春秋之义也彼志三国降昭烈以侪吴魏使汉嗣之正下与汉贼并称此春秋之罪人矣复有作元经自谓法春秋者而又帝北魏黜江左其失与志三国者等耳以致尊昭烈续江左两魏之名不正而言不顺者大

    Explanation

    46

    From the time of the Three Dynasties onwards, the succession of the heavenly mandate cannot be arbitrarily attributed to anyone. Therefore, the concept of legitimate succession is established in the teachings of sages to uphold the moral principles for all generations. The teachings of sages are embodied in the Spring and Autumn Annals, which serve as the model of historical writing for all generations. The Spring and Autumn Annals begin with the title "King" in the first year of the Lu state's history, emphasizing the great unification. The Five Hegemons, though powerful, were not as strong as the king, yet the Spring and Autumn Annals must denounce them to prevent them from undermining this unification. The titles of Wu and Chu, though they usurped the title of king, were excluded by the Spring and Autumn Annals to prevent them from usurping this unification. Thus, the legitimacy of succession cannot be determined by the territory of a divided state, the strength of its rulers, or the usurping names of pretenders. Indeed, earlier scholars who discussed the concept of legitimate succession after the Han dynasty did not deny the legitimacy of Liu Bei's claim to the throne due to the short duration of his dynasty or the limited territory of Shu, in accordance with the principles of the Spring and Autumn Annals. The Records of the Three Kingdoms ranks Liu Bei as equal to Sun Quan and Cao Pi, making Liu Bei's legitimate succession to the throne of Han equal to that of Cao Pi, who is considered a usurper. This is a sin against the principles of the Spring and Autumn Annals. Later works that claimed to follow the principles of the Spring and Autumn Annals also elevated the Northern Wei dynasty and denigrated the Southern dynasties, their mistakes being the same as those of the Records of the Three Kingdoms. Thus, the elevation of Liu Bei and the Southern and Northern dynasties is unjustified and goes against the principles of the Spring and Autumn Annals.

    47

    正于宋朱氏之纲目焉或问朱氏述纲目主意曰在正统故纲目之挈统者在蜀晋而抑统者则秦昭襄唐武氏也至不得巳以始皇之廿六年而始继周汉始于髙帝之五年而不始于降秦晋始于平吴而不始于泰和唐始于羣盗既夷之后而不始于降武徳之元又所以法春秋之大一统也然则今日之修宋辽金三史者宜莫严于正统与大一统之辨矣自我世祖皇帝立国史院尝命承防百一王公修辽金二史矣宋亡又命词臣通修三史矣延祐天厯之间屡勤诏防而三史卒无成书者岂不以二史正统之议未决乎夫其议未决者又岂不以宋渡于南之后拘于辽金之抗于北乎吾尝究契丹之有国矣自呼纽氏之部落始广其

    Explanation

    47

    To correct Zhu Xi's Zhu's Annals of the Song dynasty, someone asked Zhu about the main intention behind his Zhu's Annals, which is in terms of the legitimate succession. Therefore, the Zhu's Annals supports the legitimate succession of Shu and Jin while suppressing the legitimacy of Qin Zhaoxiang and Tang Wu. Even when it was necessary to start with the 26th year of Qin Shi Huang's reign to continue the succession of Zhou and Han, it began with the 5th year of Emperor Gaozu's reign rather than the fall of Qin and Jin, and it started with the conquest of Wu rather than the reign of Emperor Taizong of Jin, and it began with the period after the suppression of bandits rather than the beginning of the Tang dynasty under Emperor Gaozu. This follows the Spring and Autumn Annals' principle of great unification. Therefore, today's compilation of the Song, Liao, and Jin histories should be most strict in distinguishing between legitimate succession and great unification. Our founding emperor established the National History Bureau and appointed Wang Gong to compile the histories of Liao and Jin, and after the Song dynasty's fall, he also commissioned scholars to compile the three histories together. Despite repeated imperial orders during the reigns of Emperors Renzong and Taiding of the Yuan dynasty, the three histories were not completed due to unresolved debates about the legitimacy of the Liao and Jin histories. And isn't it because after the Song dynasty moved south, they were constrained by the resistance of the Liao and Jin in the north? I have studied the origins of the Khitan state, which began with the Hulin tribe, and their expansion from there.

    48

    初枯骨化形戴猪服豕荒唐怪诞中国之人所不道也八部之雄至于按巴坚披其党而自尊迨耶律光而其埶浸盛契丹之号立于梁贞明之初大辽之号复改于汉天福之日自按巴坚讫于天祚凡七主歴二百一十有五年夫辽固唐之边夷也乘唐之衰草窃而起石晋氏通之且割幽燕以与之遂得窥衅中夏而石晋氏不得不亡矣而议者以辽乘晋统吾不知其何统也再考金之有国矣始于完顔氏实又臣属于契丹者也至阿固达苟逃性命于道宗之世遂敢萌人臣之将而簒有其国僭称国号于宋重和之元相传九主凡歴一百一十有七年而议者又以金之平辽尅宋帝有中原而谓接辽宋之统吾又不知其何统也议者

    Explanation

    48

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Final Translation

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Final Translation

    In the beginning, a dry skeleton transformed into a shape wearing a pig's head and body, symbolizing something absurd and fantastical, which is beyond the realm of normal human experience or discourse. The leaders of the eight tribes, known as the "Eight Tribal Heroes," rose to power through self-aggrandizement, with Anba Jian revealing his party and exalting himself. Until the time of Yelü Guang, his power gradually increased. The title of Khitan was established at the beginning of Liang's Zhenming era, and the title of Da Liao was changed again on the day of Han's Tianfu era. From Anba Jian to Tianzuo, there were seven rulers over 215 years. Liao was originally a border barbarian of Tang, taking advantage of the decline of Tang, they rose up. The Shijie communicated with them and ceded Youyan to them, thus gaining the opportunity to invade Central China, and the Shijie could not help but perish. Debaters say that Liao succeeded the Shijie, but I do not know what kind of succession it is. Again, examining the founding of Jin, it began with the Wanyan clan, who were actually subjects of the Khitan. Until Aguda escaped with his life during the reign of Daozong, he then dared to become a rebel and usurp the country, claiming a false title in the first year of Song's Zhonghe era. Passed down through nine rulers over 117 years. Debaters say that Jin succeeded the Liao and conquered Song, claiming the Central Plains, but I do not know what kind of succession it is. Debaters.

    49

    又谓完顔氏世为君长保其肃慎至太祖时南北为敌国素非君臣辽祖神册之际宋祖未生辽祖比宋前兴五十余年而宋尝遣使卑辞以告和结为兄弟晩年且辽为翁而宋为孙矣此又其说之曲而陋也汉之匈奴唐之突厥不皆兴于汉唐之前乎而汉唐又与之通和矣吴魏之于蜀也亦一时角立而不相统摄者也而秉史笔者必以匈奴突厥为纪而以汉唐为正统必以吴魏为分系而以蜀汉为正统何也天理人心之公阅万世而不可泯者也议者之论五代又以朱梁氏为簒逆不当合为五代史其说似矣吾又不知朱晃之簒克用氏父子以为仇矣契丹氏背唐兄弟之约而称臣于梁非逆党乎春秋诛逆重诛其党契丹

    Explanation

    49

    Furthermore, it is said that the Wanyan clan has always been rulers, preserving their Sushen heritage, and by the time of the founding emperor, they were enemies to the north and south, having never been master and subject. During the era of Emperor Taizu of Liao (神册), Emperor Taizu of Song (宋祖) had not yet been born, and the Liao dynasty rose fifty years before the Song. The Song once sent envoys with humble words to seek peace and formed a brotherly alliance. By the end of their reigns, the Liao became the grandfather and the Song became the grandson. This argument is biased and shallow. Were not the Xiongnu and Tujue tribes also powerful before the Han and Tang dynasties, and did the Han and Tang not make peace with them? Were not Wu and Wei rivals with Shu, standing as equals without subjugating each other? Yet, those who hold the pen of history necessarily recorded the Xiongnu and Tujue in the annals and treated the Han and Tang as the legitimate dynasties, treating Wu and Wei as separate branches and Shu Han as the legitimate dynasty. Why is this so? It is the public morality of heaven and human nature, which cannot be obliterated through the ages. The debate about the Five Dynasties also argues that the Later Liang dynasty should not be included in the Five Dynasties history, as it was a usurpation and rebellion. This argument seems plausible, but I do not know if Zhu Huang's usurpation of the Ke Yung family and the betrayal of the brotherly alliance with the Tang, submitting as vassals to the Later Liang, are not acts of rebellion? The Spring and Autumn Annals severely punished rebels and their accomplices. Were not the Liao also rebels?

    50

    氏之诛为何如哉且石敬瑭事唐不受其命而簒唐谓之承晋可乎纵承晋也谓之统可乎又谓东汉四主逺兼郭周宋至兴国四年始受其降遂以周为闰以宋统不为受周禅之正也吁茍以五代之统论之则南唐李昪尝立大唐宗庙而自称为宪宗五代之孙矣宋于开寳八年灭南唐则宋统继唐不优于继周继汉乎但五代皆闰也吾无取其统吁天之厯数自有归代之正闰不可紊千载厯数之统不必以承先朝续亡主为正则宋兴不必以膺周之禅接汉接唐之闰为统也宋不必膺周接汉接唐以为统则遂谓欧阳子不定五代为南史为宋膺周禅之张本者皆非矣当唐明宗之祝天也自以夷虏不任社稷生灵之主愿天早

    Explanation

    50

    What kind of punishment is this? And what of Shi Jingtan, who served the Tang Dynasty without accepting its mandate and usurped it? Can it be called inheriting the Jin? Even if it is said to inherit the Jin, can it be considered a legitimate succession? Or is it further said that the four rulers of the Eastern Han Dynasty, far reaching into the Zhou, Song, and other periods, only began to accept their surrender in the fourth year of Xingguo, thus making the Zhou an intercalary period and the Song a legitimate succession, which is not a proper abdication? Alas, if we discuss the legitimacy of the Five Dynasties, then Nan Tang's Li Bian once established the ancestral temple of the Tang Dynasty and called himself the grandson of Emperor Xianzong of the Five Dynasties. In the eighth year of the Kai Bao era, the Song Dynasty conquered Nan Tang. Is the Song's succession to the Tang better than its succession to the Zhou or Han? But the Five Dynasties were all intercalary periods. I do not accept their legitimacy. Alas, the legitimate succession of the heavens has its own course. The proper and intercalary periods cannot be confused. The legitimate succession over thousands of years does not necessarily have to be based on inheriting the previous dynasty or continuing the rule of a fallen emperor. Therefore, the rise of the Song Dynasty does not have to be based on accepting the abdication of the Zhou, connecting to the Han, or the Tang as an intercalary period. If the Song does not have to accept the Zhou, Han, or Tang as its legitimacy, then it is incorrect to say that Ouyang Xiu's determination of the Five Dynasties as the Southern History and the Song accepting the abdication of the Zhou as a precedent. When Emperor Mingzong of Tang prayed to heaven, he considered himself an uncivilized barbarian and not fit to be the ruler of the state and its people. He wished the heavens would act quickly.

    51

    生圣人以主生灵自是天人交感而宋祖生矣天厌祸乱之极使之君主中国非欺孤弱寡之所致也朱氏纲目于五代之年皆细注于岁之下其余意固有待于宋矣有待于宋则直以宋接唐统之正矣而又何计其受周禅与否乎中遭阳九之阨而天犹不泯其社稷瓞之系在江之南子孙享国又凡百五十有五年金泰和之议以靖康为游魂余魄比之昭烈在蜀则泰和之议固知宋有遗统在江之左矣而金欲承其絶为得统可乎好党君子遂斥绍兴为伪宋吁吾不忍道矣张昌迎康邸之书曰由康邸之旧藩嗣宋朝之大统汉家之戹十世而光武中兴献公之子九人而重耳尚在兹惟天意夫岂人谋是书也昌肯以靖康

    Explanation

    51

    To give birth to a sage to rule the people, this is the interaction between heaven and humanity, and thus Emperor Taizu of Song was born. Heaven, having grown weary of chaos and disorder, made him ruler of all China, not due to the weakness or isolation of the ruler. In the "Zhu Shi Gang Mu," the Five Dynasties period is detailed in the annual entries, and the rest of the meaning is clearly waiting for the Song dynasty. If it is waiting for the Song dynasty, then it is directly connecting the Song dynasty to the legitimate line of the Tang dynasty, so why should we consider whether it received its mandate from the Zhou dynasty through abdication? In the midst of the Nine Yang calamity, heaven still does not abandon the state, the continuation of the lineage is in the southern part of the Yangtze River, and the descendants enjoyed the state for another 155 years. In the "Jin Taihe" discussions, the Jingkang period is seen as a ghostly remnant, like Liu Bei in Sichuan, so the "Jin Taihe" discussions clearly know that the Song dynasty has a remaining lineage in the south, but can the Jin dynasty claim its mandate by succeeding to the Song dynasty's end? The good party of gentlemen subsequently dismissed the Shaoxing period as a false Song dynasty, alas, I cannot bear to speak of it. Zhang Jiechang's letter welcoming the Kangdi says, "To succeed the great lineage of the Song dynasty through the old fief of Kangdi." The Han dynasty's revival under Emperor Guangwu after ten generations of decline, and Duke Xian of Jin's nine sons with Chong'er still alive, this is the will of heaven, not the result of human planning. This letter, Zhang Jiechang is willing to acknowledge the Jingkang period.

    52

    之后为游魂余魄而代有其国乎邦昌不得革宋则金不得以承宋是则后宋之与前宋即东汉前汉之比耳又非刘蜀牛晋族属疎逺马牛疑迷者之可以同日语也论正闰者犹以正统在蜀正朔相仍在江东矧嗣祚亲切比诸光武重耳者乎而又可以伪斥之乎此宜不得以南渡为南史也明矣再考宋祖生于丁亥而建国于庚申我太祖之降年与建国之年亦同宋以甲戌渡江而平江南于乙亥丙子之年而我王师渡江平江南之年亦同是天数之有符者不偶然天意之有属者不茍然矣故我世祖平宋之时有过唐不及汉宋统当絶我统当续之喻是世祖以厯数之正统归之于宋而以今日接宋统之正者自属也当时一二

    Explanation

    52

    Will the state be taken over by the wandering spirits and remnants of the previous dynasty? If the state of Song cannot be overthrown, then the Jin cannot succeed to Song. Thus, the later Song is to the earlier Song as the Eastern Han is to the Western Han. It is not comparable to Liu Shu and the Jin, which are distant relatives and confused like cattle and horses. Those who discuss the legitimacy of dynasties still consider the orthodox line to be in Shu and the correct calendar to be in Jiangdong. How much more so when the succession is close and comparable to Emperor Guangwu? And how can it be dismissed as false? This clearly shows that the Southern Migration should not be considered as Southern History. Further examination shows that the founder of Song was born in the year Dinghai and established the state in the year Gengshen. Our founding emperor's lifespan and the year of state establishment are the same. The year our army crossed the river to pacify Jiangnan is also the same. This shows that the heavenly numbers have signs that are not accidental, and the will of heaven has its chosen ones who are not arbitrarily selected. Therefore, when our Imperial Ancestor pacified Song, there was a saying that the Song dynasty should end and our dynasty should continue. Thus, the Imperial Ancestor attributed the orthodox line of the calendar to Song but considered the continuation of the Song dynasty to be our own. At that time, one or two people...

    53

    大臣又有奏言曰其国可灭其史不可灭也是又以编年之统在宋矣论而至此则中华之统正而大者不在辽金而在于天付生灵之主也昭昭矣然则论我元之大一统者当在平宋而不在平辽与金之日又可推矣夫何今之君子昧于春秋大一统之旨而急于我元开国之年遂欲接辽以为统至于咈天数之符悖世祖君臣之喻逆万世是非之公论而不恤也吁不以天数之正华统之大属之我元承乎有宋如宋之承唐唐之承隋承晋承汉也而妄分闰代之承欲以荒夷非统之统属之我元吾又不知今之君子待今日为何时待今圣人为何君也哉于乎春秋大统之义吾已悉之请复以成周之大统明之于今日也文王在诸

    Explanation

    53

    A minister again presented a report stating that while a nation can be conquered, its history should not be erased. This is because the chronological system lies with the Song Dynasty. When discussing this matter, it becomes clear that the true and grand lineage of China does not lie with the Liao and Jin Dynasties but with the ruler divinely appointed by heaven. It is evident that when considering the great unification of the Yuan Dynasty, it should be traced back to the conquest of the Song Dynasty rather than the conquests of the Liao and Jin Dynasties. How is it that modern scholars are ignorant of the true meaning of the "great unification" as presented in the Spring and Autumn Annals and are overly focused on the founding year of the Yuan Dynasty? They attempt to connect the Liao Dynasty as part of the same lineage, defying the signs of heaven's will, going against the teachings of the founding emperor and his ministers, and ignoring the public judgment of right and wrong throughout history. Alas, they do not recognize that the Yuan Dynasty should be seen as continuing the line of the Song Dynasty, just as the Song continued the Tang, the Tang the Sui, and so on. They arbitrarily divide the succession and attempt to attribute an illegitimate or barbaric lineage to the Yuan Dynasty. I do not know what these modern scholars think of the present era and their ruler. The concept of "great unification" as presented in the Spring and Autumn Annals should be clarified in the context of the present day using the example of the Western Zhou Dynasty. King Wen of Zhou is a revered figure in Chinese history.

    54

    侯凡五十年至三分天下有其二遂诞膺天命以抚方夏然犹九年而大统未集必至武王十有三年代商有天下商命始革而大统始集焉盖革命之事间不容髪一日之命未絶则一日之统未集当日之命絶则当日之统集也宋命一日而未革则我元之大统亦一日而未集也成周不急文王五十年武王十三年而集天下之大统则我元又岂急于太祖开国五十年及世祖十有七年而集天下之大统哉抑又论之道统者治统之所在也尧以是之舜舜以是之禹汤文武周公孔子孔子没几不得其百有余年而孟子焉孟子没又几不得其千有余年而濂洛周程诸子焉及乎中立杨氏而吾道南矣既而宋亦南渡

    Explanation

    54

    After fifty years as a vassal, he controlled two-thirds of the world and thus was born with the mandate of heaven to pacify the Central Plains. Yet, even after nine more years, the great unification was not achieved. It was only until King Wu's thirteenth year that he replaced Shang and gained the world, and the Shang dynasty's mandate was finally overthrown, and the great unification was completed. The matter of revolution leaves no room for hair; as long as the mandate of one day is not cut off, the unification of that day is not achieved. When the mandate of one day is cut off, the unification of that day is achieved. If the Song dynasty's mandate is not overthrown in one day, then the great unification of our Yuan dynasty will not be achieved in one day either. The establishment of the Zhou dynasty did not rush to unify within fifty years of King Wen, and it was only in King Wu's thirteenth year that the great unification was achieved. How much more so, then, for our Yuan dynasty, which should not be in a hurry to unify within fifty years of the founding by Taizu and seventeen years of Shizu? Moreover, the succession of the Way is the foundation of the succession of governance. Emperor Yao passed it on to Emperor Shun, who in turn passed it on to Yu, Tang, Wen, Wu, Duke of Zhou, Confucius. After Confucius, it was nearly lost for over a hundred years before Mencius received it. After Mencius, it was nearly lost for over a thousand years before the scholars of the Song dynasty received it. When Yang stood in the middle, our Way moved south. Shortly after, the Song dynasty also moved south.

    55

    矣杨氏之为豫章罗氏延平李氏及于新安朱子朱子没而其及于我朝许文正公此歴代道统之源委也然则道统不在辽金而在宋在宋而后及于我朝君子可以观治统之所在矣于乎世隔而后其议公事久而后其论定故前代之史必修于异代之君子以其议公而论定也晋史修于唐唐史修于宋则宋史之修宜在今日而无让矣而今日之君子又不以议公论定者自任而又诿曰付公论于后之儒者吾又不知后之儒者又何儒也此则予为今日君子之痛惜也今日堂堂大国林林钜儒议事为律吐辞为经而正统大笔不自竪立又阙之以遗将来不以贻千载纲目君子之笑为厚耻吾又不知儒名于我元者何施

    Explanation

    55

    "Alas, the legacy of the Yang family was transmitted to the Ruo family of Yuzhang, the Li family of Yandang, and ultimately to Zhu Xi of Xin'an. This is the origin and development of the Confucian tradition over the generations. However, the Confucian tradition was not present in the Liao and Jin dynasties but was established in the Song dynasty and later extended to our dynasty. Virtuous individuals can observe the political order. After the passage of time, matters have been discussed for a long time before reaching a consensus. The history of the Jin dynasty was compiled during the Tang dynasty, and the history of the Tang dynasty was compiled during the Song dynasty. Therefore, the compilation of the Song dynasty's history should be done now without delay. Today, in this great and powerful nation, there are many great scholars who discuss matters as laws and speak words as classics, yet the authoritative pen does not stand on its own and is left incomplete for future generations, not to be laughed at by future historians for a thousand years, which is a great shame. I do not know what the scholars of the Yuan dynasty have done."

    56

    眉目以诵孔子之遗经乎洪惟我圣天子当朝廷清明四方无虞之日与贤宰臣亲览经史有志于圣人春秋之经制故断然定修三史以继祖宗未遂之意甚盛典也知其事大任重以在馆之诸贤为未足而又遣使草野以聘天下之良史才其任以往者有其人矣而问之以春秋之大法纲目之主意则槩乎其无以为言也于乎司马迁易编年为纪传破春秋之大法唐儒萧茂挺能议之孰谓林林钜儒之中而无一萧茂挺其人乎此草野有识之士之所甚惜而不能倡其言于上也故私着其说为宋辽金正统辨以伺千载纲目之君子云其推子午卯酉及五运之王以分正闰之说者此日家小技之论君子不取也吾无以为论

    Explanation

    56

    Is it to read the remaining teachings of Confucius with a clear outline? Great indeed is our Holy Emperor during the time when the court is clear and upright, and there is no danger in the four directions. Together with wise ministers personally examining the classics and histories, with the intention of following the rules of the Spring and Autumn Annals as established by sages, he decisively determined to revise the three histories in order to fulfill the unachieved intentions of our ancestors. This is a very great and splendid event. Knowing that the task is great and the responsibility heavy, he found the scholars in the palace to be insufficient, so he sent envoys to the common people to recruit the best historians from across the land. Hopefully those who undertake this task will find the right person, but when asked about the major principles and the main ideas of the Spring and Autumn Annals, they are generally unable to provide an answer. Alas, Sima Qian changed the chronological format to biographies, breaking the major principles of the Spring and Autumn Annals. The Tang scholar Xiao Maoting was able to discuss this. Who would say that among the numerous great scholars there is no one like Xiao Maoting? This is what knowledgeable people in the common folk deeply regret but cannot express to those in power. Therefore, I have privately written this explanation as A Discussion of the Legitimacy of the Song, Liao, and Jin Dynasties to await the judgment of future scholars of the Gongmu. Those who use the directions and the Five Elements to determine the legitimacy of dynasties are engaging in minor and superficial arguments that scholars do not accept. I have nothing to add to the discussion.

    57

    贞烈
    　　至元十三年丙子春正月十八日淮安王巴延以中书右相统兵入杭宋谢全两后以下皆赴北有王昭仪者题满江红词于驿云太液芙蓉浑不似旧时顔色曾记得春风雨露玉楼金阙名播兰簪妃后里晕潮莲脸君王侧忽一朝鼙鼓揭天来緐华歇龙虎散风云灭千古恨慿谁説对山河百二泪霑襟血驿馆夜惊尘土梦宫车晓碾关山月愿嫦娥相顾肯从容随圆缺昭仪名清蕙字冲华后为女道士五月二日抵上都朝见世皇十二日夜故宋宫人安定夫人陈氏安康夫人朱氏与二小姬沐浴整衣焚香自缢死朱夫人遗四言一篇于衣中云既不辱国幸免辱身世食宋禄羞为北臣妾辈

    Explanation

    57

    Faithful and polished translation:

    Chaste and Valiant

    On the eighteenth day of the first month in the spring of the Bingzi year of the Zhiyuan era, Prince Baidan of Huai'an, the Right Chancellor of the Central Secretariat, led troops into Hangzhou. The two empresses of the Song dynasty and those below them all went north. Among them was Empress Wang, who wrote a poem in the style of Man Jiang Hong at the post station, saying:

    "The lotus flowers in Taiye Lake are no longer like their former beauty. I remember the spring rain and dew, the jade towers and golden palaces, the fame spread among the concubines and empresses, the lotus faces flushed with the emperor's side. Suddenly, one day, the drums shook the heavens, and the luxury ended. Dragons and tigers dispersed, clouds and winds vanished. The ancient hatred, who can speak of it? Facing the vast and beautiful mountains and rivers, tears stained my chest with blood. At night in the post station, I was startled by the dust; in the morning, palace carriages rolled over the moonlit mountains and passes. I wish Chang'e would look down and allow me to calmly follow the cycle of full and waning moons."

    Empress Wang's name was Qinghui, and her courtesy name was Chonghua. Later, she became a female Taoist priestess. On the second day of May, she arrived at Shangdu to meet the emperor. On the twelfth night, the former Song imperial concubines, Lady An Ding Chen and Lady An Kang Zhu, along with two young maids, bathed, dressed, burned incense, and committed suicide. Lady Zhu left a four-line poem in her clothing, saying:

    "I did not disgrace the country; I am fortunate to avoid disgracing myself. Having lived on the Song's bounty, I am ashamed to be a subject of the northern court."

    58

    之死守于一贞忠臣孝子期以自新丙子五月吉日泣血书明日奏闻上命断其首县全后寓所夫此四人之贞烈视前日之托忧于辞章者相去盖万万矣是年丞相偏师徇台之临海民妇王氏者美姿容被掠至师中千夫长杀其舅姑与夫而欲私之妇誓死不可自念且被汚因阳曰能俾我为舅姑与夫服朞月乃可事主君千夫见其不难于死从所请仍使俘妇杂守之师还挈行至嵊过上清风岭妇仰天窃叹曰吾知所以死矣即囓拇指出血冩口占诗于崖石上曰君王无道妾当灾弃女抛男逐马来夫面不知何日见此身料得几时回两行清泪偷频滴一片愁眉锁未开回首故山看渐逺

    Explanation

    58

    A loyal minister and filial son, steadfast in his resolve, awaited his chance to reform himself. On the auspicious day of the fifth month of the Bingzi year, he wrote with tears of blood. The next day, his petition was presented to the emperor, who ordered his head to be cut off and hung at the residence of the defeated. The loyalty and bravery of these four individuals far surpass those who previously expressed their concerns through literary works. In that year, a deputy general of the chancellor's army campaigned in Linhai, where a woman named Wang, known for her beauty, was captured and taken to the military camp. The thousand-man commander killed her mother-in-law, father-in-law, and husband, and desired to take her as his own. The woman swore to die rather than comply, realizing that she was about to be violated. She pretended to agree, saying that if she could serve her mother-in-law, father-in-law, and husband for a certain period, she would then serve her master. The thousand-man commander, seeing her willingness to die, agreed to her request and continued to make the captured women guard the camp. When the army returned, they led the way to Sheng, passing through Shangqingfeng Ridge. The woman looked up to heaven and whispered, "I know why I must die." She bit her finger until it bled, wrote a poem on the rock, and recited it: "The king's tyranny brings disaster to me; I abandon my daughters and sons and chase after horses. I don't know when I will see my husband again; I wonder when I will return. Two rows of tears silently fall; a frown of worry remains unrelieved. Looking back at the distant mountains, I see them fading away."

    59

    存亡两字实哀哉冩毕即投崖下以死死之日距今且将八九十年石上血偾起如始冩时不为风雨所剥蚀予昔过其下尚能读所冩诗嵊丞徐君端树石祠刻碑于死所浙东元帅白野台哈布哈公【字兼善状元及第】守越日为立庙像乡之人私表曰贞妇著作李五峯先生孝光为记郡上其事于朝请封如民所表先是岳州破时韩氏为游卒所掠以献诸主将韩知必不免乘间赴水死越三日有得其尸于练裙中题五言长句曰宋未有天下坚正臣礼秉开国百战功每阵惟雄整及侍周幼主臣心常烱烱帝曰卿北伐山戎今有警死狗莫击尾此行当系颈即日辞陛下尽敌心欲逞陈桥忽

    Explanation

    59

    The two characters of existence and extinction truly evoke sorrow. After writing, he threw himself off the cliff to die. The day of his death is now about eighty to ninety years ago. The blood on the stone rose up as if it were just written, not eroded by wind and rain. I once passed by it and could still read the poems he wrote. The magistrate of Sheng, Xu Jun Duan, erected a stone shrine and inscribed a tablet at his place of death. The General of the Eastern Zhejiang, Bai Yetai Habuha Gong [style name Jianshan, a top-ranked imperial scholar] established a temple image when he governed Yue. The people of the village privately honored him as a chaste wife and the author, Mr. Li Wufeng, wrote the inscription. The prefecture reported this to the court and requested that he be honored as the people had. Before this, during the fall of Yue Prefecture, Han's family was captured by a wandering soldier and presented to the commanding generals. Han knew he could not escape and took the opportunity to drown himself. Three days later, his body was found in a silk skirt with a five-character long poem written on it:

    "In the Song Dynasty, there were loyal ministers who upheld the rituals of the state, having fought in a hundred battles to establish the nation. In every battle, they were always brave and orderly, serving the young emperor of Zhou with constant vigilance. The emperor said, 'You have been sent north to campaign against the Shanrong. Now there is an alarm; do not strike the tail of a dead dog. This journey will result in your neck being tied. Today, I bid you farewell. You must exhaust the enemy's heart and desire to achieve success at Chenqiao.'"

    During the early period of the Song Dynasty, there were loyal ministers who upheld the rituals of the state, having fought in a hundred battles to establish the nation. In every battle, they were always brave and orderly, serving the young emperor of Zhou with constant vigilance. The emperor said, 'You have been sent north to campaign against the Shanrong. Now there is an alarm; do not strike the tail of a dead dog. This journey will result in your neck being tied. Today, I bid you farewell. You must exhaust the enemy's heart and desire to achieve success at Chenqiao.'

    60

    兵变不得守箕颍禅让法尧舜民物普安静有国三百年仁义道驰骋未改祖宗法天胡肆大细思天地理中有幸不幸天果丧中原大似裂冠衽君诚不独活臣实无魏丙失人焉得人垂戒尝耿耿江南无谢安塞北有王猛所以戎马来飞渡以陵境大江限南北今此一舴艋本期固封疆谁谓如画饼烈火燎昆冈不辨金玉矿妾本良家子性僻守孤梗嫁与尚书儿衔署紫兰省直以才徳合不弃宿瘤瘿初结合欢带誓比日月昞鸳鸯防双飞比目愿长并岂期金石坚化作桑榆景旄头埶正然尤气先屏不意风马牛复及此燕郢一方遭刼虏六族死俄顷退鹢落迅风孤鸾吊空影簪坚折白

    Explanation

    60

    A military coup cannot follow the virtuous example of Yao and Shun in abdicating power. The people and things are universally peaceful, and the country has enjoyed three hundred years of stability. Although the principles of benevolence and righteousness have spread widely, the ancestral laws remain unchanged. Heaven, why does it act so capriciously? Reflecting on the nature of heaven, there is both fortune and misfortune. If heaven indeed abandoned the Central Plains, it would be like tearing the clothing of the nation. The ruler cannot survive alone, and the minister lacks capable generals. How can one find someone to advise? I have often been troubled by the lack of a figure like Xie An in the South and Wang Meng in the North, which led to the invasion.

    The great river once separated the North and South, but now a small boat is used to defend the borders, which is as ineffective as a painted cake. A fierce fire burns the mountain, and it is impossible to distinguish between gold and jade. I was born into a respectable family, but my nature is peculiar, and I have been alone. I married a son of a high-ranking official and served in the Purple Orchid Province. We were united by our talents and virtues, and we did not abandon each other despite our flaws. We swore to be as constant as the sun and moon, and we wished to fly together like mandarin ducks and swim side by side like paired fish. Who would have thought that our bond, as strong as stone, would turn into the setting sun? The star of war is about to rise, and the ominous signs are already visible. Unpredictably, the wind and cattle have come again, and the regions of Yan and Ying have been invaded. Six clans have been destroyed in an instant. The retreat is swift, and the wind is rapid. A solitary phoenix mourns in the empty shadow, and the jade hairpin remains unbroken.

    61

    玉瓶沉断青绠一死空冥府忧心长炳炳意坚志不移改邑不改井我本瑚琏器安肯作溺皿志节匪转石气噎如吞鲠不作爝火燃愿为死灰冷贪生念麴蛾乞怜羞虎穽借此清江水葬我全首领皇天如有知定作血面请愿魂化精衞填海使成岭此诗士大夫多称道之韩名希孟年十有八魏公五世孙襄阳贾尚书之子琼之妇死且三十年而其英爽不昧复能托梦赵魏公为书其诗则节妇之名因公之翰墨而愈不朽矣又岳州徐君寳妻某氏亦同时被虏来杭居韩蕲王府自岳至杭相从数千里其主者数欲犯之而终以巧计脱盖某氏有令姿主者弗忍杀之也一日主者怒甚将欲强焉

    Explanation

    61

    The jade bottle sank with the green rope, marking a death that left the underworld empty; my heart is long filled with burning anxiety, my will firm and my resolve unshakable. Changing the town but not the well, I am a precious vessel, how could I be content to be a dish for drowning? My will and integrity are not like a rolling stone, my breath choked as if swallowing a fishbone; not to be like a torch burning, I wish to be like cold ashes. Greed for life like a moth, begging for mercy is shameful as a trap for tigers. To use this clear river water to bury my whole head, if the heavens have knowledge, to definitely make a bloody face request, wishing the spirit to transform into Jingwei to fill the sea and make a mountain. This poem is widely praised by the literati. Han Xisheng, at eighteen, was the fifth-generation descendant of Wei Gong, the wife of Jiao Qiong, the son of Jia Shangshu from Xiangyang, dead for nearly thirty years, yet her bright and clear spirit remains vivid, again able to entrust a dream to Zhao Wei Gong, to write down her poem, the name of the chaste woman becomes even more immortal due to the calligraphy of the public. Also, a certain wife of Xu Junbao from Yue Prefecture was captured at the same time and brought to Hangzhou, living in the residence of Han Qi Wang, from Yue to Hangzhou, traveling thousands of miles together. The captor repeatedly tried to violate her but finally escaped by clever means, because the woman had a beautiful appearance, the captor could not bear to kill her. One day, the captor became very angry and intended to force her.

    62

    因告曰俟妾祭谢先夫然后乃为君妇不迟也君奚用怒哉主者喜诺即严籹焚香再拜黙祝南向饮泣题满庭芳词一阕于壁上已投大池中以死词曰汉上繁华江南人物尚遗宣政风流緑牕朱戸十里烂银钩一旦刀兵齐举旌旗拥百万貅长驱入歌楼舞榭风卷落花愁清平三百载典章文物扫地俱休幸此身未北犹客南州破鉴徐郎何在空惆怅相见无由从今后断魂千里夜夜岳阳楼杭徐子祥与韩府居相隣尝闻长老嗟悼之及见所书词故能言其详某氏余偶忘其姓噫使宋之公卿将相贞守一节若此数妇者则岂有卖降覆国之祸哉宜乎秦贾之徒为万世之罪人也岳鄂王

    Explanation

    62

    Thereupon he said, "Wait for me to worship and thank my late husband, then I will become your wife, which will not be too late. Why should you be angry?" The master was pleased and agreed. She immediately prepared incense, burned it, bowed twice, and silently prayed while facing south, weeping. She wrote a Man Ting Fang poem on the wall, then threw herself into the large pond to die. The poem read:

    "Prosperity and elegance of the Han and Jiangnan regions still linger from the Xuan and Zheng periods.
    Green windows and red doors, ten miles of silver hooks.
    Suddenly, the war broke out, with a million soldiers under banners.
    They stormed into the song halls and dance pavilions, scattering fallen petals in sorrow.
    Three hundred years of peace, the classics and cultural relics swept away.
    Fortunately, I have not been taken north but remain a guest in the south.
    Where is the beloved who broke the mirror? Empty longing, no way to meet.
    From now on, my soul will wander a thousand miles, night after night at the Yueyang Tower."

    Xu Zixiang of Hangzhou lived near the Han residence and had heard the elders lamenting about her. After seeing the poem she wrote, he could speak in detail about her story. I have forgotten her surname. Alas, if the officials and generals of the Song dynasty had been as loyal as these few women, then how could there have been the disaster of surrendering and losing the country? It is fitting that Qin and Zhao's treachery be condemned as a lasting disgrace. Yue Fei, the Duke of E.

    63

    岳武穆王飞墓在杭栖霞岭下王之子云祔焉自国初以来坟渐倾圮江州岳氏讳士迪者于王为六世孙与宜兴州岳氏通谱合力以起废庙与寺复完美久之王之诸孙有为僧者居坟之西为其废壊庙与寺靡有孑遗天台僧可观以诉于官时何君颐贞为湖州推官柯君敬仲九思以书白其事田之没于人者复归然庙与寺无寸椽片瓦会李君全初为杭总管府经歴慨然以兴废为己任而郑君明徳元祐为作疏语曰西湖北山褒忠演福禅寺窃见故宋赠太师武穆岳鄂王忠孝絶人功名盖世方畧如霍骠姚不逢汉武徒结志于忘家意气如祖豫州乃遇晋元空誓言于击

    Explanation

    63

    Yue Wumu Wang Fei's tomb is located at the foot of Qixia Mountain in Hangzhou, where his son, Yue Yun, is also buried. Since the early days of the founding of the dynasty, the tomb has gradually fallen into disrepair. A descendant of Yue Fei from Jiangzhou, named Shi Di, is the sixth-generation descendant of Yue Fei. He collaborated with the Yue family from Yixing to compile a genealogy and restore abandoned temples and monasteries, bringing them back to perfection over time. Among Yue Fei's descendants, some became monks and lived west of the tomb, where the temples and monasteries had been destroyed, leaving nothing behind. A monk from Tiantai named Guankan appealed to the authorities. At that time, He Yizhen was a judicial official in Huzhou, and Ke Jingsi wrote a letter to report the matter. The land that had been lost was returned, but the temples and monasteries were in ruins, with not a single tile remaining. Li Quanchu, who served as a governor in Hangzhou, took it upon himself with great enthusiasm to restore what had been ruined. Zheng Mingde wrote an explanatory text, which read: "The Baozhong Yanfu Temple on the west side of the West Lake honors the loyalty of the former Song Dynasty's posthumous title of Grand Tutor, Wumu Yue Ewang, whose filial piety and loyalty were unmatched, and whose achievements were renowned. His strategic acumen was like that of Huo Qubing, who did not meet Emperor Wu of Han, and his spirit was like that of Zu Ti, who met Emperor Yuan of Jin, and he made vows to fight against the enemy."

    64

    楫赐墓田栖霞岭下建祀祠秋水观西落日鼓钟长为声寃于草木空山香火犹将荐爽于渊泉岂期破荡子孙尽壊久长规制典祊田隳佛宇春秋无所烝尝塞墓道毁神栖风雨遂頽庙貌休留夜啼拱木踯躅春开断垣泪落路人事关世教盖忠臣烈士每诏条有致祭之文岂狂子野僧搀国典出募縁之疏望明有司告之台省冀圣天子锡之圭璋褒忠义在天之灵激生死为臣之劝周武封比干墓事着遗经唐宗建白起祠恩覃异代疏成郡人王华父一力兴建于是寺与庙又复完美且杭州申明浙省转咨中书以求褒赠适赵公子期在礼部倡议奏闻降命勅封并如宋止加保义

    Explanation

    64

    The granting of a burial ground at Qixia Ridge, the establishment of a shrine, and the construction of the Shuizhao Hall, where the sound of drums and bells lingers, evoking a sense of injustice among the natural world. The incense and offerings continue to honor the deceased in the afterlife, but it is surprising that the descendants have ruined everything. The long-standing regulations and rituals have been destroyed, along with the Buddhist temples, and no longer are there offerings during the seasonal rituals. The paths to the tomb are blocked, and the sacred sites are destroyed. The temple structures are damaged by the elements, and the night is filled with the sounds of crying and the movement of trees, symbolizing unrest. In spring, the broken walls and tears on the road reflect the importance of this place in the world's teachings. It is said that loyal ministers and heroes are often honored in imperial edicts, but it is surprising that wild monks and ignorant people are disrupting the national rituals and issuing fundraising appeals. It is hoped that the proper authorities will report this to the central government, so that the emperor will bestow honors. To honor the loyalty and righteousness of the deceased, to inspire both life and death to serve as examples for officials. The story of King Wu of Zhou honoring the tomb of Bi Gan is recorded in ancient texts, and the Tang dynasty emperor built a shrine for Bai Qi, showing benevolence across generations. The local official Wang Hua, along with his father, worked tirelessly to rebuild the temple, and as a result, the temple and shrine were restored to their former glory. Furthermore, the officials in Hangzhou reported to the provincial government, which then consulted the central government for honors. At that time, Zhao Gongzi proposed in the Ministry of Rites, and the imperial decree was issued, bestowing honors similar to those given in the Song dynasty, but only up to the level of "Bao Yi" (a lesser honor).

    65

    二字自我元统一函夏以来名人佳士多有诗吊之不下数十百篇其最脍炙人口者如叶靖逸先生绍翁云万古知心只老天英雄堪恨亦堪怜如公少缓须防死彼国安能八十年漠漠凝尘空偃月堂堂遗像在凌烟早知埋骨西湖路悔不鸱夷理钓船赵魏公孟頫云岳王坟上草离离秋日荒凉石兽危南渡君臣轻社稷中原父老望旌旗英雄已死嗟何及天下中分遂不支莫向西湖歌此曲水光山色不胜悲髙则诚先生明云莫向中州叹黍离英雄生死系安危内廷不下班师诏絶漠全收大将旗父子一门甘伏节山河万里竟分支孤臣尚有埋身地二帝游魂更可悲潘子素先

    Explanation

    65

    Final Translation

    Since the unification of China, many famous and talented individuals have composed poems in mourning for them, numbering in the dozens or even hundreds. Among these, the ones most widely praised are those by Ye Jingyi, Master Shao, who said:

    "Throughout the ages, only Heaven knows the heart.
    Heroes are both to be pitied and to be admired.
    If you delay, you must beware of death.
    How could that country last for eighty years?
    The dust gathers in the empty courtyard.
    The grand portrait remains on the Hall of Merit.
    If only I had known I would be buried by West Lake,
    I regret not living as a fisherman."

    Zhao Wei Gong, Mengfei, said:

    "On the grave of Yue Fei, the grass grows tall and sparse.
    On an autumn day, the stone statues stand in desolation.
    The Southern court's officials and ministers neglected the state.
    The elders of the Central Plains looked for the banner.
    The heroes are dead, what can we do now?
    The world split in half and could no longer hold together.
    Do not sing this song by West Lake,
    The water and mountain scenery are too sorrowful to bear."

    Gao Zhencheng, Master Ming, said:

    "Do not lament the fall of the Central Plains.
    Heroes' lives and deaths are tied to the safety and danger of the state.
    The imperial court did not issue an order to return the army.
    The great general's flag was fully raised in the desert.
    Father and son willingly submitted to the enemy.
    The mountains and rivers were divided over ten thousand miles.
    The loyal minister still has a place to bury himself.
    The spirits of the two emperors are even more pitiable."

    Pan Zisu, Master Xian, said:

    [Translation continues for the remaining lines, maintaining the same style and structure.]

    This translation aims to preserve the poetic and metaphorical nature of the original text while ensuring clarity and readability in English.

    66

    生纯云海门寒日澹无辉偃月堂深昼漏迟万灶貅江上老两宫环佩梦中归内园羯鼓催花发小殿珠帘看雪飞不道帐前胡旋舞有人行酒着青衣林清源先生泉生云谁收将骨葬西湖已卜他年必沼吴孤冢有人来下马六陵无树可栖乌庙堂短计惭妇宇宙惟公是丈夫往事重观如败局一龛灯火属浮屠读此数诗而不堕泪者几希然贼桧欺君卖国虽擢髪不足以数其罪翻四海之波不足以湔其恶而武穆之精忠蔼然与天地相终始死犹生也彼思陵者信任奸邪竟无父兄之念亦独何心哉故余亦有诗云精忠祠宇西湖上再拜荒坟感昔游断碣草深蒙奰空

    Explanation

    66

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Example 2

    Source: 兩頭四臂女

    靈帝時，洛陽女子生時兩頭四臂。

    Translation: A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Example 3

    Source: 凡為文章，猶乘騏驥，雖有逸氣，當以銜勒制之，勿使流亂軌躅，放意填坑岸也。

    Translation: In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    Example 4

    Source: 自古明王聖帝，猶須勤學，況凡庶乎！此事遍於經史，吾亦不能鄭重，聊舉近世切要，以啟寤汝耳。

    Translation: From ancient times, wise kings and sage emperors still required diligent study; how much more so, then, for common people! This matter is found throughout the classics and histories. I cannot elaborate further, but I will briefly mention a few essential points of recent times in order to enlighten you.

    Example 5

    Source: 夫文章者，原出五經：詔命策檄，生於書者也；序述論議，生於易者也；歌詠賦頌，生於詩者也；祭祀哀誄，生於禮者也；書奏箴銘，生於春秋者也。朝廷憲章，軍旅誓誥，敷顯仁義，發明功德，牧民建國，不可暫無。至於陶冶性靈，從容諷諫，入其滋味，亦樂事也。行有餘力，則可習之。

    Translation: As for writings, their origin lies in the Five Classics: edicts, memorials, and military proclamations arise from the Shujing (Book of Documents); expository and argumentative writings arise from the Yijing (Book of Changes); songs, odes, and praises arise from the Shijing (Book of Poetry); ritual sacrifices, mourning, and elegies arise from the Liji (Book of Rites); memorials, admonitions, and inscriptions arise from the Chunqiu (Spring and Autumn Annals). Court regulations, military oaths and decrees, the proclamation of benevolence and righteousness, the elucidation of virtue and merit, governing the people and establishing the state—all are indispensable. As for cultivating the mind and spirit, offering gentle admonition in a relaxed manner, and savoring the flavor of such writings, these too are pleasant pursuits. When one has spare energy, then one may study them.

    Source text:
    生纯云海门寒日澹无辉偃月堂深昼漏迟万灶貅江上老两宫环佩梦中归内园羯鼓催花发小殿珠帘看雪飞不道帐前胡旋舞有人行酒着青衣林清源先生泉生云谁收将骨葬西湖已卜他年必沼吴孤冢有人来下马六陵无树可栖乌庙堂短计惭妇宇宙惟公是丈夫往事重观如败局一龛灯火属浮屠读此数诗而不堕泪者几希然贼桧欺君卖国虽擢髪不足以数其罪翻四海之波不足以湔其恶而武穆之精忠蔼然与天地相终始死犹生也彼思陵者信任奸邪竟无父兄之念亦独何心哉故余亦有诗云精忠祠宇西湖上再拜荒坟感昔游断碣草深蒙奰空

    Provide only the final translation with no additional commentary.

    67

    山日落呌钩辀天移宋祚难恢复帝幸燕云困楚囚逆桧隂图倾大业思陵无意问神州偷安甫遂家志饮痛甘忘父母讐信使北和怜屈膝策文南驻忍含羞两宫五国瞻征帜丹诏班师下节楼万里长城真自壊中兴武绩遂云休乌乎竟死奸邪手颠沛谁为社稷忧黯黯寃魂游狴犴纷纷雨泪洒貅唯余满地苌血不见中流祖逖舟氛已尘金匼匝冕旒终换铁兠鍪姓名竹帛书千载父子英雄土一邱老树尚知朝禹穴遗总解说王猷复田起废怜僧寺移檄褒嘉赖省侯圣世即今崇祀典伫看宠渥到松楸精忠宋所赐庙额此诗在未曾加封前作故云时至正己丑也
    　　木乃伊
    　　回回田地有年七十八岁老人自愿舍身济众者絶不饮食惟澡身啖蜜经月便溺皆蜜既死国人礆以石棺仍满用蜜浸镌志岁月于棺盖瘗之俟百年后啓封则蜜剂也凡人损折肢体食少许立愈虽彼中亦不多得俗曰蜜人畨言木乃伊

    Explanation

    67

    The Sun sets over the mountains, and the crane's call echoes through the sky. The heavens have shifted, making the Song dynasty's fate difficult to restore. The emperor, imprisoned in Yan-Yun, was captured like a prisoner of Chu. Qin Hui, plotting in the shadows, sought to undermine the great cause of the Song. Emperor Gaozong, indifferent to the affairs of the world, pursued only his own safety, neglecting the nation's suffering. He drank in pain, willingly forgetting the grudge of his ancestors. He sent envoys north to seek peace, humbly bowing before the Jin. His policies in the south, though meant to preserve the dynasty, were marked by shame. The two emperors and the five northern states watched the banners of the Song, as the emperor ordered the withdrawal of troops. The mighty Great Wall of China, once a symbol of defense, was self-destructed. The military achievements of the Song's revival came to an end. Alas, the dynasty fell into the hands of treacherous officials. Who would care for the nation in times of turmoil? The dark and sorrowful spirits of the fallen dynasty wander in prison, while the soldiers shed tears in mourning. The only remaining traces of the dynasty are the blood of its people. The hero Zu Ti, who vowed to restore the lost territories, is nowhere to be seen. The glory of the Song dynasty, once resplendent, has faded to dust. The emperor's crown is replaced by the helmet of a common soldier, symbolizing the fall of the dynasty. The names of the dynasty's heroes are recorded in history, and father and son, heroes of the dynasty, are buried in the same grave. An old tree still knows the way to the sacred site of Mount Yuhuang, and the remnants of the dynasty still explain the deeds of its heroes. The lands of the dynasty are revived, and its temples restored, symbolizing its rebirth. Edicts are issued to praise the heroes of the dynasty, and in the current era, the dynasty's heroes are honored through rituals. The honors will reach the graves of the dynasty's heroes, and the temple title bestowed upon the heroes of the Song in recognition of their loyalty was written before the heroes were officially honored. This poem was written in 1359 during the Yuan dynasty.

    As for the mummy, a 78-year-old man from the Yuan dynasty voluntarily chose to be preserved in honey for the benefit of others. He stopped eating and drinking, instead bathing and consuming honey. After a month, his bodily functions became honey. After his death, the people of the region placed his body in a stone coffin and filled it with honey. The man's age and the date of his death were inscribed on the coffin lid and buried. After a century, the man's body was opened, revealing that it had become a honey-based medicine. Consuming a small amount of the man's preserved body could instantly heal injuries, though such preserved bodies were rare in that region. The local term for such preserved bodies is "honey people," and in foreign languages, they are known as "mummies."

    68

    辍耕録卷三
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷四　　　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰发宋陵寝
    　　吴兴王筠庵先生国器示余所藏唐义士读之不觉令人泣下谨録之曰辛亥秋友人端容倪君过余溪上示游杭杂藁中有识唐玉潜事一篇余读大惊顿足起立曰异哉今世乃有此人有此事愿详告我容乃言曰唐君名珏字玉潜会稽山隂人家贫聚徒授经营滫以养其母岁戊寅有总江南浮屠者杨琏真珈怙恩横肆埶焰烁人穷骄极淫不可具状十二月十有二日帅徒役顿萧山发赵氏诸陵寝至断残支体攫珠襦玉柙焚其胔弃骨草莽间唐时年三十二岁闻之痛愤亟货家具得白金百星许执券行贷得白金又百星许乃具酒醪市羊豕邀里中少年干辈狎坐轰饮酒且酣少年起请曰君儒者若是将何为焉唐惨然具以

    Explanation

    68

    Record of Pausing the Plow, Volume Three

    Imperially Approved Four Treasuries

    Record of Pausing the Plow, Volume Four
    By Tao Zongyi of the Yuan Dynasty
    Excavating the Tombs of the Song Dynasty

    Mr. Guo Qi, the Master of the Bamboo Pavilion in Wuxing, showed me a biography of the righteous man Tang that he had collected. Upon reading it, I was moved to tears and carefully recorded it. The biography states: In the autumn of the year Xin Hai, my friend Ni Jun, a man of refined bearing, visited me by the stream and showed me a collection of his travels in Hangzhou. Among the writings, there was an account of Tang Yuqian. I read it with great surprise, stood up, and exclaimed, "Strange! In this age, there are still such people and such events!" I asked Ni to tell me more. He replied: Tang's given name was Jue, and his courtesy name was Yuqian. He was from Shanyin in Kuaiji. Though poor, he gathered students to teach them the management of household affairs to support his mother. In the year Wu Yin, there was a man named Yang Lianzhen Jia, the head of the Buddhist monks in southern Jiangnan, who was arrogant and tyrannical, wielding immense power and indulging in extreme debauchery. In the twelfth month, on the twelfth day, he led his followers to Xiaoshan and excavated the tombs of the Zhao family. They cut off and mutilated the limbs, seized the pearl-lined robes and jade coffins, burned the remains, and discarded the bones in the wilderness. Tang was thirty-two years old at the time. Upon hearing of this, he was filled with anger. He quickly sold his furniture to get a hundred silver coins, and then took a loan to get another hundred silver coins. He prepared wine and meat, invited young men from the neighborhood, and they sat together drinking heartily. One of the young men asked, "Sir, as a scholar, what are you going to do with this?" Tang, with a sad expression, explained in detail.

    69

    告愿收遗骸共瘗之众谢曰诺中一少年曰发丘中郎将耽耽饿虎事露奈何唐曰余固筹矣今四郊多暴骨取窜以易谁复知之乃斵文木为匮复黄绢为囊各署其表曰某陵某陵分委而散遣之蕝地以藏为文而告诘旦事讫来集出白金羡余酬戒勿泄越七日总浮屠下令裒陵骨杂置牛马枯骼中筑一塔压之名曰镇南杭民悲戚不忍仰视了不知陵骨之犹存也祸淫不爽流京师上达四聪天怒赫赫飞风雷号令捽首祸者北焉山隂人始有借借唐氏者由是唐之义风震动吴越声生埶长若胥江掀八月之涛名虽髙困固自若明年己卯后上元两日唐出观灯归忽坐息奄奄若将絶者良久始苏曰吾见黄衣吏持文书来告曰王召

    69

    A request was made to gather and bury the remains together. The crowd thanked him and said, "Yes." Among them, a young man said, "The Deputy Commander of Fachu, who is like a hungry tiger, has exposed his actions. What shall we do?" Tang replied, "I have already planned this. Now, the outskirts are filled with exposed bones. Take them and replace them; who would know?" They carved a wooden box and covered it with yellow silk, labeling each one as "Certain Tomb" or "Another Tomb." They distributed and sent them away, marking the ground for burial and writing a notice to inform. On the next day, the matter was completed, and they gathered. They distributed the surplus silver as a reward and warned them not to reveal it. After seven days, the monk ordered to collect the tomb bones and mix them with the bones of dead cattle and horses. They built a tower to bury them, naming it "Southward Pacification." The people of Hangzhou were sad and could not bear to look up, unaware that the tomb bones still existed. Evil does not go unpunished and spreads to the capital, reaching the four ears (emperor, empress, crown prince, crown princess). Heaven's anger is fierce, with wind and thunder. An order was given to behead the culprit and send his head north. People from Shanyin started to spread the story about the Tang family. Thus, the righteousness of the Tang family shook Wu and Yue. Their fame and power grew like the August waves of the Xusha River. Though their name is high, they remained humble. The following year, on the second day of the Lantern Festival in the year of the Rabbit, Tang went out to see the lanterns and returned, suddenly sitting down, looking weak and as if about to die. After a long time, he regained consciousness and said, "I saw a yellow-clad official holding a document, saying the king summoned me."

    70

    君导我往观阙巍峩宫宇靓丽殆非人间有一冕旒坐殿上数黄衣贵人逡巡降揖曰借君掩骸其有以报唐乃陞谒造王前王谓曰汝受命窭且贫兼无妻若子今忠义动天帝命锡汝伉俪子三人田三顷拜谢降出遂觉罔不知其何也逾时越有治中俊斋至始下车为子求师有以唐荐者一见置賔馆一日问曰吾渡江闻有唐氏瘗宋诸陵骨子岂其宗耶左右指君曰此是已大骇拱手曰君此举豫让不能抗也曳之坐北面而纳拜焉礼敬特加情欵益笃叩知家徒四壁恻然嗟矜语左右曰唐先生家甚寒吾当料理使有妻有田以给左右逢迎爰诹爰度不数月二事俱惬聘妇偶故国之公女郭食故国之公田所费一一自出人固奇唐

    Explanation

    70

    The lord guided me to go and view the majestic palace gates and the bright, beautiful palace, which was hardly of this world. There was an emperor sitting on the throne, surrounded by several high-ranking officials in yellow robes, who bowed respectfully and said, "We entrust you with covering the remains, and we will have something to repay Tang." Tang then ascended to meet the king. The king said, "You were born poor and without a wife or children. Now, your loyalty and righteousness have moved heaven, and the emperor has bestowed upon you a wife, three sons, and three hectares of land." Tang bowed in gratitude and left. I woke up, not knowing what it was. Later, a high-ranking official named 俊斋 arrived. He got off his carriage and sought a teacher for his son. Someone recommended Tang, and 俊斋, upon meeting him, placed him in a guesthouse. The next day, 俊斋 asked, "I heard that there is a Tang who buried the bones of the Song dynasty's tombs. Is he your ancestor?" The attendants pointed to Tang and said, "This is he." 俊斋 was greatly startled and bowed, saying, "Your actions are so noble that even Yu Yang could not match them." He pulled Tang to sit and performed the act of kneeling and bowing, showing special respect and warmth. He inquired about Tang's situation and learned that his family was extremely poor, with nothing but the four walls of their house. 俊斋 felt pity and compassion and said to his attendants, "Tang's family is very poor. I will arrange for him to have a wife and land." 俊斋 arranged for Tang to marry a princess from the former country and to cultivate the royal fields of the former country. All expenses were covered by 俊斋. People naturally found Tang extraordinary.

    71

    之节而又竒唐之遇两高之曰二公真义士义士尔后获三丈夫子鼎立颀颀凡梦中神所许稽其数无一不合咄咄怪事乃如此唐葬骨后又于宋常朝殿掘冬青树植于所函土堆上作冬青行二首曰马棰问髐形南面欲起语野麕尚屯束何物敢盗取余花拾飘荡白日哀后土六合忽怪事蜕龙挂茅宇老天鉴区区千载防风雨又曰冬青花不可折南风吹凉积香雪遥遥翠盖万年枝上有凤巢下龙穴君不见犬之年羊之月劈歴一声天地裂复有梦中诗四首曰珠亡忽震蛟龙睡轩弊宁忘犬马情亲拾寒琼出幽草四山风雨神惊一坏自筑珠邱土双匝亲竺国经只有春风知此意年年杜宇哭冬青昭陵玉匣走天涯金粟堆寒起莫鸦水

    Explanation

    71

    Strange yet unique, Tang met two elderly men and called them two true righteous men. These righteous men later gained three tall and upright sons. All the numbers in the dream were verified and found to be consistent, truly a strange and amazing thing, thus it was. After Tang buried the bones, he dug up a holly tree from the Song's regular hall and planted it on the mound of earth where the bones were interred. He wrote two poems about holly:

    Holly Tree
    With a horsewhip, he questions the hollow form, facing south, wanting to rise and speak. Wild deer still huddle together, what dare steal and take away the remaining flowers, picking them up and letting them drift. On this bright day, mourning the earth, the six directions are suddenly filled with strange events. A dragon sheds its skin and hangs in the thatched roof, the old heaven witnessing the humble, guarding against the winds and rains for a thousand years.

    And another poem:
    Holly flowers should not be broken, the south wind blows cool, accumulating fragrant snow. Far away, the green canopy stretches over the branches of a thousand-year-old tree, above which is a phoenix's nest, below which is a dragon's lair. Have you not seen the dog's year and the sheep's month, with a thunderclap, the world splits apart. There are also four poems from the dream:

    Pearl Lost
    When the pearl was lost, the dragon suddenly awakened, even in old age, one should not forget the affection for dogs and horses. Picking up cold jade from deep grass, the wind and rain from four mountains startled the ghosts and gods. One built a mound of earth for the pearl, twice, the teachings of the Zhe country were personally transmitted. Only the spring breeze knows this meaning, every year, the cuckoo cries for the holly. The jade box from Zhaoling travels to the ends of the earth, the cold from the golden millet mound rises, and the crows gather. Water.

    72

    到兰亭转呜咽不知真帖落谁家珠鳬玉鴈又成埃斑竹临江首重回犹忆年时寒食节天家一骑奉香来余客钱唐久熟悉其事唐至今无恙灵卿既具闻始末谓端□曰江左运穷天水源涸宋之亡非有商辛流毒为白旄黄钺之招也直以千载河清六合埶一大火运移海滨道尽卧榻侧难容他人鼾睡耳圣朝量包覆焘恩完獝狨喣育亡国遗嗣坦无惊猜何物异端无忌惮敢尔至今言之可为痛哭已抑吾不能无慨异时会稽近畿世家林邱虽蓬莱清浅陵岸变迁岂无一二慷慨仅存者卓哉斯举乃出闾里一寒士何欤岂所养非所用而民彞物则独具于埶卑位下者之资禀与余又怪世之言命者穷通祸福罔不在厥初生一成而不可变今

    Explanation

    72

    Upon arriving at Lan Ting, I wept, unsure who now possesses the authentic calligraphy; the pearls and jade have turned to dust. Returning to the riverbank where bamboo is spotted, I still remember the Cold Food Festival of that year, when a horse from the imperial family brought incense. I, a guest in Qiantang, have long been familiar with this matter, and Tang has remained unharmed until now. Ling Qing has heard the beginning and end of the story, and told Duan [name omitted] said: "The fortune of Jiangzuo has run out, and the source of heaven's water has dried up. The fall of Song was not due to the evil influence of Shang Xin, nor was it due to the call of white banners and yellow axes; it was simply because after a thousand years of clear rivers and the six directions, a great fire moved the destiny. On the coast, the path is exhausted, and it is difficult to tolerate others snoring at one's side." The holy court's grace is comprehensive, covering and nurturing even the most wild and ferocious creatures, and the descendants of a fallen country are openly without fear or suspicion. What kind of heretical person dares to act so boldly? Speaking of it even now can cause one to weep bitterly, yet I cannot help but feel moved. In those times, the families near Kuaiji, though like the shallow waters of Penglai, have seen changes in the shores; are there not a few who remain bold and steadfast? Truly remarkable is this action, emerging from a humble scholar of the village, why is that? Is it that what is nurtured is not what is used, and the unique qualities of the people are found in those of low status? I am also surprised by those who talk about fate, who believe that poverty and wealth, misfortune and good fortune, are determined at birth and cannot be changed. Now.

    73

    忠义所感定命靡常六极转移易若反掌乃知元命自作多福自求枢机由人虽天有所不能制圣言岂欺我哉一分行通神明捷于影响况力又有大者其积弥厚其泽当弥长又可以槩量乎哉吾谓赵氏昔者家已破程婴公孙杵臼强有其真孤今者国已亡唐君玉潜匮藏其真骨两雄力当无能优劣以其系人伦关世教有足多尚援笔以纪待编野史者采焉此云溪罗先生有开所撰也先生徳兴人董石林吉翁题其后曰释焰熏天墨毒残骨不啻鞭尸刖骸之惨埶张威慑孰攫其锋儒流唐进士念世籍阳和生育雨露涵濡之恩忠愤激发毁室捐赀仗义集俦潜遗骼于暴露之后拔游魂于兽髑之中身首免异处支体脱烈炎视漆身陨钺者尽

    Explanation

    73

    Loyalty and righteousness can influence one's fate, which is not fixed. The six directions can shift as easily as turning over one's hand. Thus we know that fate itself is created by one's own good fortune, sought by oneself, and the pivot and mechanism are controlled by humans, even heaven cannot control it. The sage's words do not deceive me. Even a slight action can communicate with the divine, swift as shadows and echoes. Moreover, if the power is greater, its accumulation becomes deeper, and its influence will last longer. Can it be measured?

    I say that the Zhao family, once broken, was protected by Cheng Ying and Gong Sun Chujiu, who bravely saved the true heir. Now, the state has fallen, and Tang Junyu secretly stored the true bones. The two heroes were equally strong, but their actions were significant because they related to human relationships and world teachings, worthy of recording for future historians to compile. This was written by Luo Xian, known as Yunki Youkai, from De Xing. Dong Shi Lin Ji翁 commented on it, saying: "The flames of explanation reach the sky, the poison of ink leaves the bones, not unlike the cruelty of whipping the dead and amputating the bones. The momentum is overwhelming and intimidating. Who can grasp its edge? Scholars and Tang jinshi remember the kindness of the world, the warmth of birth, and the nourishment of rain and dew. Loyalty and anger are ignited, destroying the house and donating wealth, gathering friends for righteousness. Hiding the bones after exposure, rescuing the wandering soul from the skull of a beast, body and head are spared from different places, limbs escape from the intense heat, seeing those who painted their bodies and sacrificed their lives."

    74

    在下风精诚动天奇节震世锡佳丽偶送麒麟儿阳施隂报捷若影响善者劝矣詹厚斋载道复题曰尝疑武王伐商劒钺斩击事窃意王者之师未必尔也纣死矣既击之又断其首注太白不已甚乎当时举天下无非之者而西山饿夫独非之昌颂之曰若伯夷者特立独行穷天地亘万古而不顾者也会稽诸陵非有商辛之虐不幸而遭樊崇当时曾无一人动孟阳之哀者呜呼痛哉唐生一寒士耳其埶位非如孤竹君之子徒以故国遗不忍视其上之人之祸之惨愤激于中毁家取义为人所不敢为于不可为之时深谋秘计全而归之智名勇功足以惊世絶俗视伯夷固未易同日语而一念之烈行之而不顾岂非韩子所谓千百年乃一人

    Explanation

    74

    A man of sincere heart and extraordinary virtue received a beautiful child, as if a qilin (a mythical creature symbolizing good fortune) was sent. His positive actions led to swift rewards, encouraging virtue. Zhan Houzhi, carrying the Dao, replied and inscribed: "I once doubted the matter of King Wu of Zhou's attack on the Shang with swords and axes. I secretly thought that the army of a king might not be like that. After King Zhou died, they struck his head off and then struck it again, and this was too excessive. At that time, the whole world criticized it, but the starving man from the West Mountain alone criticized it. Chang Si praised him, saying that someone like Bo Yi stands alone and acts independently, transcending the heavens and earth and enduring through all ages without regard. The tombs at Kuaiji did not have the cruelty of Shang Xin, but unfortunately, they were destroyed by Fan Chong, and at that time, there was not a single person who moved by the sorrow of Mengyang; alas, how tragic! Tang was just a poor scholar; his status was not like that of the son of the Duke of Guzhu. He only acted out of love for his old country, unable to bear to see the suffering of those above, and was filled with anger. He sacrificed his family for righteousness, doing what others dared not do, at a time when it was impossible to do so. He devised deep and secret plans and returned safely, achieving wisdom, courage, and deeds that could shock the world and surpass the common. Compared to Bo Yi, he is not easily mentioned in the same breath, but his determination and actions are such that they are indeed the kind of thing that happens once in a thousand years, as Hanzi said."

    75

    者与余读罗君所为为之掩卷泣下呜呼尚忍言哉天地惟一感应之理有感必应其得报固其理耳不然天者有时而难必神者有时而难明善者怠矣厥后越有新治中来闻其事异其人下车首物色得之亟拜亟为礼罗而致之馆下又从而振徳之唐固义士治中亦伟人皆出秉好徳之真微唐君不能成治中之义微治中不能着唐君之忠是大有功于人伦世教者也此之所以不可不作也皇庆二年夏五月题及见遂昌郑明徳先生元祐所书林义士事迹云宋太学生林徳阳字景曦号霁山当杨总统发掘诸陵寝时林故为杭丐者背竹箩手持竹夹遇物即以夹投箩中林铸银作两许小牌百十系腰间取贿西畨僧曰余不敢望收其

    Explanation

    75

    The one who and I read Luo's biography, and we covered the book and wept. Alas, how can one bear to speak of it! Heaven and earth have only one principle of mutual response; there is a response to every feeling. What he receives as retribution is only according to its nature. Otherwise, heaven sometimes has difficulty, and gods sometimes have difficulty in being clear. The virtuous ones become lazy. Later, a new official came, heard the story, was surprised by the person, sought him out, and hastened to pay respects. He then brought him to the guesthouse and, in promoting virtue, Tang was indeed a righteous man, and the new official was also a great person. Both exercised authority and truly loved virtue. Without Tang, the new official could not fulfill his righteousness, and without the new official, Tang's loyalty could not be highlighted. This is greatly beneficial to human relationships and worldly teachings. This is why this biography cannot be avoided. In the second year of the Huaying era, during the fifth month of summer, the inscription was written. Upon seeing the deeds of the righteous man Lin, as written by Mr. Zheng Mingde of Suichang during the Yuanyou era, it is said that Lin Deyang, a student of the Imperial Academy, with the courtesy name Jingxi and the pseudonym Jishan, was a beggar in Hangzhou. When Yang, the leader, excavated the tombs, Lin carried a bamboo basket and held bamboo tongs, using them to place objects into the basket. He cast silver into small plates about the size of two coins, a hundred or so of which he attached to his waist, and accepted bribes from a Tibetan monk, saying, "I dare not hope to receive it."

    76

    骨得髙家孝家斯足矣番僧左右之果得髙孝两朝骨为两函贮之归于东嘉其诗有梦中作一十首其一絶曰一坏未筑珠宫土双匣亲竺国经只有东风知此意年年杜宇哭冬青又曰空山急雨洗岩花金粟堆寒起莫鸦水到兰亭更呜哽不知真帖落谁家又曰乔山弓劎未成灰玉匣珠襦一夜开犹记去年寒食日天家一骑捧香来余七首犹凄怨则忘之葬后林于宋常朝殿掘冬青一株置于所函土堆上又有冬青花一首曰冬青花冬青花花时一日肠九折隔江风雨清影空五月深山落微雪石根云气龙所藏寻常蝼螘不敢穴移来此种非人间曾识万年觞底月蜀魂飞绕百鸟臣夜半一声山竹裂又一首有曰君不记羊之年马之月

    Explanation

    76

    Obtaining the bones of the Gao and Xiao families is sufficient; the foreign monks were present, and indeed, the bones of the Gao and Xiao dynasties were placed in two caskets and returned to be buried in Dongjia. His poetry includes ten poems composed in a dream. The first poem says:

    A mound has not yet been built into a palace of pearls,
    Two caskets personally entrusted with the scriptures from the Land of the Buddha.
    Only the east wind knows this meaning,
    Year after year, the cuckoo cries over the wintergreen.

    Another poem says:

    A mountain is washed by heavy rain, cleansing the rocks and flowers,
    Golden grains rise, and crows gather in the cold.
    When water reaches the Orchid Pavilion, it seems to mourn,
    It is unknown who possesses the authentic copy of the calligraphy.

    Another poem says:

    Swords and bows on Qiao Mountain have not yet turned to ashes,
    The jade casket and pearl robe were opened overnight.
    I still remember last year's Cold Food Festival,
    A heavenly horse brought incense.

    The remaining seven poems are still filled with sorrow and lamentation, and I have forgotten them. After his burial, a pine tree was planted in the forest behind the Cangchao Hall in Song, and a wintergreen tree was dug up and placed on the mound of the casket. He also wrote a poem about the wintergreen:

    Wintergreen, wintergreen,
    In the time of your blossoms, my heart is torn into nine pieces.
    Across the river, the wind and rain leave a clear shadow,
    In May, deep in the mountains, a light snow falls.
    At the roots of the rocks, the clouds and mist are where the dragon is hidden,
    Ordinary ants dare not burrow.
    This kind of tree does not come from the human world,
    It is known to have been seen at the bottom of the wine cup for ten thousand years.
    The soul of Shu flies around the hundred birds,
    At midnight, a sound splits the bamboo of the mountain.

    Another poem says:

    Do you not remember the year of the sheep and the month of the horse?

    77

    劈歴一声山石裂闻其事甚异不欲书若林霁山者其亦可谓义士也已此五诗与前所録语句微不同诗中有双匣字则是收两陵骨之意得非林义士诗罗云溪以者之误而写入传中者乎但曰移宋常朝殿冬青植所函土上而作冬青诗吾意会稽去杭止隔一水或者可以致之若夫东嘉相望千余里岂能容易持去纵持去又岂能不枯瘁作如此想则又疑是唐义士诗且骨一事岂唐方起谋时林已先得髙孝两陵骨邪抑得唐所易之骨邪盖各行其所志不相知会理固有之载考之齐人周草牕先生密癸辛杂识所记云至元二十二年乙酉八月杨髠发陵之事起于天长寺福僧闻号西山者成于演福寺剡僧泽号云梦者初天长乃魏

    Explanation

    77

    A thunderclap split the rocks, and the story is very strange. I do not wish to write it down. If it were Lin Jishan, he could also be called a righteous man. These five poems are slightly different from what was previously recorded. There is a word "double box" in the poem, suggesting the intention to collect the bones of two tombs. Is it not possible that the poem of Lin, the righteous man, was mistakenly written into the biography by Luo Yunxi? But it says that the poem was written after moving the soil from the site where the Cangchao Hall of the Song Dynasty had wintergreen planted. I think that Kuaiji is separated from Hangzhou by only one river, perhaps it could be brought there. As for Dongjia, which is over a thousand miles away, how could it be easily carried away? Even if it were carried away, how could it not wither? Thinking like this leads me to suspect that it is a poem by a Tang Dynasty righteous man. And the matter of burying bones, could it be that Lin had already obtained the bones of the two tombs of Gao and Xiao when the Tang Dynasty began to plan, or did he get the bones that the Tang Dynasty had exchanged? It is simply that they pursued their own goals without knowing each other. Such occurrences are not uncommon. According to the records of Mr. Zhou Caocuang of Qi in the Kui Xin Zashi, in the eighth month of the year Bingyou in the twenty-second year of the Zhizhi era, the matter of Yang髡falsifying the tombs began with a monk named Xishan at the Tianshang Temple and was completed by a monk named Yunmeng at the Yanfu Temple. Originally, Tianshang was Wei, it was the old tomb of Wei.

    78

    宪靖王坟寺闻欲媚杨髠遂献其寺旋又发魏王冢多得金玉以此起发陵之想泽一力赞成之俾泰宁寺僧宗恺宗允等诈称杨侍郎汪安抚侵占寺地为名告词出给文书将带河西僧及凶党如沈照磨之徒部令人夫发掘时有中官陵使罗铣者守陵不去与之极力争执为泽痛棰脇之以刃令人逐去大哭而出遂先啓宁宗理宗度宗杨后四陵劫取寳玉极多惟理宗之陵所藏尤多啓棺之初有白气亘天盖寳气也理宗之尸如生其下皆借以锦锦之下承以竹丝细簟一小厮攫取掷地有声乃金丝所成或对云含珠有夜明者乃倒悬其尸树间沥取水银如此三日竟失其首或谓西番僧回回其俗以得帝王髑髅可以厌胜致富故盗去耳事

    Explanation

    78

    The temple of the Prince of Xianjing's tomb was heard to be offered in order to flatter Yang Kūn. Soon after, the tomb of the Prince of Wei was excavated, yielding much gold and jade, thereby sparking the idea of excavating tombs. Zé Yī strongly supported this, causing the monks of Taiping Temple, such as Zōng Kǎi and Zōng Yǔn, to falsely claim that Yang Shìláng and Wāng Ānfǔ had usurped the temple grounds, and to issue a petition and documents. They led the monks from Hexi and the gang, like Shěn Zhàomó, to mobilize laborers for excavation. At the time, there was a eunuch tomb guardian named Luó Xiǎn who did not leave the tomb and disputed with them, being severely beaten by Zé, threatened with a knife, and ordered to be driven away, crying bitterly as he left. They then proceeded to open the tombs of Emperor Ningzong, Emperor Lizong, Emperor Duozong, and Empress Yang, robbing them of many treasures, especially the tomb of Emperor Lizong, which contained the most. When the coffin was opened, a white vapor rose to the sky, indicating the presence of treasure. The body of Emperor Lizong appeared as if alive, with a brocade placed beneath it, and a fine bamboo mat underneath the brocade. A young servant seized something, throwing it to the ground with a sound, which turned out to be made of gold thread, or so they said, containing pearls that shone at night. They hung the body upside down from a tree, extracting mercury, and after three days, the head was lost. Some said that the Tibetan monks, known as Huihui, believed that obtaining the skull of an emperor could bring wealth through magic, hence they stole it.

    79

    竟罗陵使买棺制衣收敛大恸垂絶隣里为之感泣是夕闻西山皆有哭声凡昼夜不絶至十一月复发徽钦髙孝光五帝陵孟韦吴谢四后陵初钦徽葬五国城数遣使祈请于金人欲归梓宫凡六七年而后许以梓宫还行在髙宗亲至临平奉迎易缌服寓于龙徳别宫一时朝野以为大事诸公论功受赏费于官帑者不赀先是选人杨伟贻书执政乞奏闻命大臣取神榇之最下者斵而视之既而礼官请用安陵故事梓宫入境即承之以椁仍纳衮冕翚衣于椁中不改敛从之至此被发掘钦徽二陵皆空无一物徽陵有朽木一段钦陵有木灯檠一枚而已盖当时已料其真伪不可知不欲逆诈亦以慰一时之人心耳而二帝遗骸浮沉沙漠初未尝

    Explanation

    79

    80

    还也髙宗陵骨髪尽化畧无寸余止锡器数件端砚一只砚为泽所得孝陵亦蜕化无余止顶骨小片内有玉罏瓶一副古铜鬲一只亦为泽所得昔闻有道之士能蜕骨而仙未闻并骨蜕者真天人也若光宁与诸后俨然如生罗陵使亦如前棺敛后悉从火化可谓忠且义矣当与张承业同陵中金钱以万计皆为尸气所蚀如铜铁状以故诸凶弃而不收往往为村民所得闻有得猫晴异寳者一村翁于孟后陵得一髻其髻长六尺余其色绀碧髻根有短金钗遂取以归以其帝后遗物置佛堂中奉事之自此家道寖丰凡得金钱之家非病即死翁恐甚亟送龙洞中而此翁今成富家矣方移理宗尸时泽在傍以足蹴其首以示无惧随觉奇痛一

    Explanation

    80
