    The bones and hair of Emperor Gaozong's tomb have completely transformed, leaving only a few inches. Only a few pieces of tinware and one stone inkstone remain; the inkstone was obtained by Ze. The tomb of the Emperor of filial piety also transformed completely, leaving only a small piece of skull. Inside there is a pair of jade incense burners and an ancient bronze tripod, which were also obtained by Ze. It is said that those who follow the Way can transform their bones and become immortal, but I have never heard of someone transforming their bones completely. Truly, such a person is a celestial being. If Guangning and the other empresses are still as lifelike as they were, and the coffin of Luo Ling was also treated the same way, being cremated after being sealed, this can be called both loyal and righteous. They should be recorded alongside Zhang Chengye. There were tens of thousands of coins in the tomb, all of which were corroded by the corpse's qi, turning them into the appearance of copper and iron. Therefore, the evil ones abandoned them and did not collect them, and they were often found by the villagers. There are rumors of someone finding a rare treasure called "cat eye." An old man from a village found a bun at the tomb of Empress Meng. The bun was over six feet long, its color was dark blue-green, and at the base of the bun, there was a short gold hairpin. He took it back and placed it in his Buddhist shrine as a relic of the emperor and empress. From then on, his family's fortunes gradually improved. Any family that found money either fell ill or died. The old man, greatly fearing, quickly sent it to the Dragon Cave, and this old man has now become a wealthy family. When moving Emperor Lizong's body, Ze was nearby and kicked his head with his foot to show he was not afraid, and immediately felt a strange pain.

    81

    防起于足心自此苦足疾数年以致溃烂双股堕落十指而亡闻既得志且富不义之财复倚杨髠埶豪夺乡人产业后为乡夫二十人伺道间屠而脔之罪不加众各不过受杖而已其恺与杨髠分不平已受杖死尚有允在据此说则云溪所传岁月絶不同盖尝论之至元丙子天兵下江南至乙酉将十载版图必已定法制必已明安得有此事然戊寅距丙子不三年窃恐此时庶事草创而妖髠得以肆其恶与妖髠就戮羣凶接踵陨于非命天之所以祸淫者亦严矣但云髙宗陵骨髪尽化孝宗陵顶骨小片不知唐义士所易者何骨也林义士所收者又何骨也惜余生晩不及识宋季以来老儒先生以就正其是非姑以待熟两朝典故之人问焉

    Explanation

    81

    The disease originated from the sole of his foot, and he suffered from foot ailments for many years, eventually leading to his thighs rotting away and his fingers falling off, resulting in his death. It is said that after he became successful and wealthy through unjust means, he relied on his power and strength to seize the property of his fellow villagers. Later, twenty villagers waited for him on the road and killed him, cutting him into pieces. The punishment did not affect many people; they were only given a flogging. The case of Kai and Yang Kuan, who had a dispute and were punished by flogging, left another person (Yun) involved. According to this account, the time periods recorded by Yun Xi are completely different. I have often discussed that from the year 1276 (丙子) when the Yuan army conquered the south to 1285 (乙酉), nearly ten years had passed. The territory must have been established, and the laws must have been clarified. How could such a thing have happened? However, from 1276 (丙子) to 1278 (戊寅), less than three years, I suspect that at this time, many affairs were just beginning to be established, allowing the evil one (Yang Kuan) to act out his wickedness. The evil one was executed, and many other villains followed, meeting their deaths in unjust ways. The heavens punish the wicked severely. It is said that the bones and hair of Emperor Gaozong's tomb were completely transformed, and a small piece of bone from Emperor Shenzong's tomb. It is unclear what kind of bone the Tang hero exchanged, and what kind of bone the Lin hero collected. It is regrettable that I was born too late to meet the old scholars of the late Song dynasty who could correct my understanding of right and wrong. I will wait for someone familiar with the customs of both dynasties to ask about it.

    82

    相术
    　　国初有李国用者自北来杭能望气占休咎能相人其人崖岸倨傲而时贵咸敬之谢后诸孙字退乐者设早馔延致至即据中位省幕官皆坐下坐不得其一言以及祸福时赵文敏公谓之七司户与谢婣戚屈来同饭文敏公风疮满面李遥见即起迎谓坐客曰我过江仅见此人耳疮愈即面君公辈记取异日官至一品名闻四海方襄阳未破时世皇命其即军中望气行逾三两舍遄还奏曰臣见卒伍中往往有台辅器襄阳不破江南不平置此人于何地噫李之术亦神矣国用登州人尝为卒遇神仙教以观日之法能洞见肺腑世称神相
    　　前辈谦让
    　　延祐间兴圣宫成中官李丞相宁奉太后懿防命赵集贤孟頫书额对曰凡禁扁皆李雪庵所书公宜奏闻既而命李赵偕至雪庵处雪庵曰子昂何不书而以属吾邪李因具言之雪庵遂不固辞前辈推让之风岂后人所可企哉

    Explanation

    82

    Translation

    Physiognomy
    In the early days of the Yuan Dynasty, there was a man named Li Guoyong who came from the north to Hangzhou. He was skilled in observing the qi to foretell auspicious or inauspicious events and in physiognomy. He was aloof and arrogant, but all the wealthy and powerful at the time respected him. A descendant of the imperial family, specifically a grandson of the Empress, named "退樂," prepared an early meal to invite and entertain Li Guoyong. Upon arrival, Li Guoyong sat in the central position, and all the officials of the provincial government sat below him. He did not utter a single word about his fate or fortune. At the time, Zhao Mengfu, known as the "Seven County Magistrate," and a relative of the Empress, invited Li Guoyong to eat together. Zhao Mengfu had a rash all over his face. When Li Guoyong saw him, he immediately got up to welcome him and said to the guests, "I have only seen this person since crossing the river. When his rash heals, he will face the emperor. You should remember this; in the future, he will hold the first-grade official rank and be famous throughout the world." When Xiangyang was not yet conquered, the emperor ordered Li Guoyong to observe the qi in the army. Li Guoyong traveled beyond three or four li and hurriedly returned to report, "I see that among the soldiers, there are often those with the qualities of high-ranking officials. If Xiangyang is not conquered, the south will not be peaceful. What place will this person have?" Ah, Li's skill is indeed divine! Li Guoyong was from Dengzhou and had once been a soldier. He met a deity who taught him the method of observing the sun, allowing him to see through people's innermost thoughts. He was known as a divine physiognomist.

    Courtesy of the Elders
    During the reign of Emperor Renzong, the Xingsheng Palace was completed. An eunuch named Li, the Chancellor, received the decree of the Empress Dowager and was ordered to have Zhao Mengfu, the Scholar of the Imperial Academy, write the inscription. Li replied, "All inscriptions on plaques are written by Li Xue'an. You should inform the emperor of this." Soon after, Li and Zhao were both ordered to go to Li Xue'an's place. Li Xue'an said to Zhao, "Why didn't you write it yourself and instead entrusted it to me?" Zhao explained his reasons, and Li Xue'an did not insist. The custom of senior officials yielding to others is something that later generations cannot aspire to.

    83

    不茍取
    　　胡汲仲先生长儒号石塘特立独行刚介有守赵松雪尝为罗司徒奉钞百锭为先生润笔请作乃父墓铭先生怒曰我岂为宦官作墓铭邪是日先生正絶粮其子以情白坐上诸客咸劝受之先生却愈坚观此则一毫不茍取于人从可知矣故虽冻馁有所不顾也先生送蔡如愚归东阳诗有云薄糜不继袄不暖讴吟犹是钟球鸣语之曰此余秘密藏中休粮方也论诗
    　　虞伯生先生集杨仲先生载同在京日杨先生每言伯生不能作诗虞先生载酒请问作诗之法杨先生酒既酣尽为倾倒虞先生遂超悟其理继有诗送伯长先生桷扈驾上都以所作诗介他人质诸杨先生先生曰此诗非虞伯生不能也或曰先生尝谓伯生不能作诗何以有此曰伯生学问髙余曾授以作诗法余莫能及又以诣赵魏公孟頫诗中有山连阁

    Explanation

    83

    不茍取
    Hu Jizhong, also known as Shitang, was a man of strict integrity and moral principle. Zhao Mengfu once offered him 100 ingots of silver as payment for writing a tomb inscription for Luo Shitu's father. Hu Jizhong angrily replied, "How could I write a tomb inscription for a eunuch?" On that day, Hu Jizhong was in dire need of food, but his son tried to persuade him to accept the payment. All the guests present urged him to take it, but Hu Jizhong refused even more firmly. From this, it is clear that he would not take anything he did not deserve, even if it meant going hungry.

    論詩
    Yu Ben and Yang Zhonghong were both in the capital at the same time. Yang Zhonghong often criticized Yu Ben for not being able to write poetry. One day, Yu Ben invited Yang Zhonghong to drink wine and asked him about the method of writing poetry. As Yang Zhonghong became drunk, he poured out his knowledge. Yu Ben suddenly understood the principles of poetry. Later, Yu Ben wrote a poem to send to Yu Bolong, who was accompanying the emperor to Shangdu. Yu Bolong showed the poem to Yang Zhonghong, who said, "This poem could only have been written by Yu Ben." Someone asked, "But you once said that Yu Ben could not write poetry. Why do you say this now?" Yang Zhonghong replied, "Yu Ben's scholarship is very high. I once taught him the method of writing poetry, which I cannot match. He then went to Zhao Hui Gong, and in his poem, he wrote about 'mountains connected by pavilions.'"

    84

    道晨留辇野散周庐夜属櫜之句公曰美则美矣若改山为天野为星则尤美虞先生深服之故国朝之诗称虞赵杨范揭焉范即徳机先生椁揭即曼硕先生傒斯也尝有问于虞先生曰仲诗如何先生曰仲诗如百战健儿徳机诗如何曰徳机诗如唐临晋帖曼硕诗如何曰曼硕诗如美女簪花先生诗如何笑曰虞集乃汉廷老吏盖先生未免自负公论以为然
    　　贤妻致贵
    　　程公鹏举在宋季被虏于兴元版桥张万戸家为奴张以虏到宦家女某氏妻之既婚之三日即窃谓其夫曰观君之才貌非久在人后者何不为去计而甘心于此乎夫疑其试已也诉于张张命棰之越三日复告曰君若去必可成大器否则终为人奴耳夫愈疑之又诉于张张命出之遂粥于市人家妻临行以所穿绣鞵一易程一履泣而曰期执此相见矣程感悟奔归宋时年十七八以防补入官迨国朝统一海宇程为陜西行省参知政事自与妻别已三十余年义其为人未尝再娶至是遣人擕向之鞵履往

    Explanation

    84

    The Morning of the Road, the Carriage Remains in the Wild, the Zhou-style Pavilion, and the Night Belongs to the Armor. The official said, "It is beautiful, but if 'mountain' is changed to 'heaven' and 'wilderness' to 'stars,' it would be even more beautiful." Mr. Yu deeply admired this suggestion. Therefore, during the national era, poetry was associated with Yu, Zhao, Yang, Fan, and Jie. Fan was Mr. De Ji, and Jie was Mr. Man Shuo. Someone once asked Mr. Yu about the poetry of Zhong Hong. Mr. Yu said, "Zhong Hong's poetry is like a seasoned warrior." Someone asked about the poetry of De Ji. Mr. Yu said, "De Ji's poetry is like a Tang Dynasty imitation of a Jin Dynasty calligraphy." Someone asked about the poetry of Man Shuo. Mr. Yu said, "Man Shuo's poetry is like a beautiful woman wearing flowers." Someone asked about Mr. Yu's own poetry. Mr. Yu laughed and said, "Yu Ji is like an old bureaucrat in the Han court." This suggests that Mr. Yu might be a bit self-praising, but the public opinion agrees with him.

    A Virtuous Wife Brings Honor.

    Gong Pengju, a public official, was captured in the late Song Dynasty and became a slave in the household of Zhang Wan, a local official in Xingyuan. Zhang married Gong to a woman from an official family. After three days of marriage, the woman secretly told her husband that given his talent and appearance, he should not remain a slave and should consider escaping. The husband doubted her sincerity and reported her to Zhang, who ordered her to be beaten. After three more days, she told him again that if he left, he could achieve great things, otherwise he would remain a slave. The husband doubted her even more and reported her again, leading Zhang to expel her. She was then sold to a common family. As she left, she gave him one of her embroidered shoes in exchange for one of his, crying that they would meet again with these shoes. Gong was moved by her words and fled back to Song when he was in his late teens. He later entered the government and became a vice-provincial governor in Shaanxi. After more than thirty years, he sent someone to bring back the shoes and sandals they had exchanged.

    85

    兴元访求之市家云此妇到吾家执作甚勤遇夜未尝解衣以寝每纺绩达旦毅然莫可犯吾妻异之视如己女将半载以所成布匹偿元粥镪物乞身为尼吾妻施赀以成其志见居城南某庵中所遣人即往寻见以曝衣为由故遗鞵履在地尼见之询其所从来曰吾主翁程参政使寻其偶耳尼出鞵履示之合亟拜曰主母也尼曰鞵履复全吾之愿毕矣归见程相公与夫人为道致意竟不再出告以参政未尝娶终不出旋报程移文本省遣使檄兴元路路官为具礼委幙属李克复防护其车舆至陜西重为夫妇焉

    Explanation

    85

    In Xingyuan, someone inquired in the market and said: This woman came to my house and worked very diligently. She never undressed and slept at night, spinning and weaving until dawn, and was firm and inviolable. My wife was surprised by her and treated her as her own daughter for half a year. She compensated with the cloth she had made and asked to become a nun. My wife provided funds to fulfill her wish, and now she resides in a certain nunnery in the southern part of the city. The person sent went to find her, using the excuse of airing clothes, and left her shoes and socks on the ground. The nun asked where she had come from, and she replied, "My master, Counselor-in-Office Cheng, sent me to find her husband." The nun showed her the shoes and socks, which matched, and she immediately bowed and said, "You are my mistress." The nun said, "With these shoes and socks, my wish is completely fulfilled." She returned to see Counselor-in-Office Cheng and conveyed my message to his wife, and she never came out again. She told them that Counselor-in-Office Cheng had never married, and finally did not come out. Shortly after, it was reported that Counselor-in-Office Cheng had moved to the provincial capital. He sent an official to summon the Xingyuan road, and the local official prepared the ceremony, entrusting the deputy Li Kefu to protect the carriage. They arrived in Shaanxi and were reunited as husband and wife.

    86

    奇遇
    　　揭曼硕先生未达时多游湖湘间一日泊舟江涘夜二鼓揽衣露坐仰视明月如昼忽中流一櫂渐近舟侧中有素妆女子敛袵而起容仪甚清雅先生问曰汝何人荅曰妾商妇也良人久不归闻君逺来故相迎耳因与谈论皆世外恍惚事且云妾与君有夙縁非同人间之淫奔者幸勿见却先生深异之迨晓恋恋不忍去临别谓先
    生曰君大富贵人也亦宜自重因留诗曰盘塘江上是奴家郎若闲时来吃茶黄土筑墙茅盖屋庭前一树紫荆花明日舟阻风上岸沽酒问其地即盘塘镇行数步见一水仙祠墙垣皆黄土中庭紫荆芬然及登殿所设像与夜中女子无异余往闻先生之侄孙立礼说及此亦一奇事也今先生官至翰林侍讲学士可知神女之言不诬矣

    Explanation

    86

    A Strange Encounter

    When Mr. Jie Manshuo was not yet renowned, he often traveled through the regions of Lake Dongting and the Xiang River. One night, he moored his boat at the bank of the river. At the second drumbeat (around 10 PM), he stepped out of the boat and sat outside, gazing at the bright moon, which illuminated the night as if it were daytime. Suddenly, a small boat approached from the middle of the river, gradually coming closer to his side. Inside, a woman dressed in simple attire stood up, her skirt folded with respect. Her appearance was exceptionally graceful and elegant.

    Mr. Jie asked her, "Who are you?" She replied, "I am a merchant's wife. My husband has been away for a long time. Hearing that you have come far, I have come to greet you." They began to converse, and she spoke of otherworldly and ethereal matters. She also mentioned, "I have an old karmic connection with you, and this is not like the reckless behavior of ordinary people. Please do not reject me." Mr. Jie was deeply intrigued by her words.

    As dawn approached, he felt reluctant to part from her. Before they separated, she said to him, "You are destined for great wealth and honor. You should also value yourself." She then left a poem for him:

    "By the Disk Pond River is my humble abode.
    My husband, if he has leisure, should come for tea.
    The walls are made of yellow earth, the roof of thatch,
    A tree of purple wisteria blooms in the courtyard."

    The next day, the boat was delayed by the wind, and they went ashore to buy wine. They asked about the location, and it turned out to be the town of Disk Pond. After walking a few steps, they came across a temple dedicated to the water deity. The walls and courtyard were made of yellow earth, and the courtyard was filled with the fragrance of purple wisteria. Upon entering the temple, the statue they saw was identical to the woman they had met the previous night.

    I have heard this story from Mr. Jie's nephew, Lili, and it is indeed a strange and wondrous tale. Now that Mr. Jie has risen to the position of Hanlin Shilang (a high-ranking scholar-official), it is clear that the words of the divine maiden were not false.

    87

    贤烈
    　　戴石屏先生复古未遇时流寓江右武宁有富家翁爱其才以女妻之居二三年忽欲作归计妻问其故告以曾娶妻白之父父怒妻宛曲解释尽以奁具赠夫仍饯以词云惜多才怜薄命无计可留汝揉碎花牋忍写断肠句道傍杨柳依依千丝万缕抵不住一分愁绪捉月盟言不是梦中语后回君若重来不相忘处把杯酒浇奴坟土夫既别遂赴水死可谓贤烈也矣
    　　挽文丞相诗
    　　宋丞相文公天祥其事载在史册虽使三尺之童亦能言其忠义翰林学士徐威卿先生世隆有诗挽之曰大元不杀文丞相君义臣忠两得之义似汉王封齿日忠如蜀将斫顔时乾坤日月华夷见岭海风霜草木知只恐史官编不尽老夫和泪写新诗可谓善风刺者矣虞伯生先生集亦有诗曰徒把金戈挽落晖南冠无奈北风吹子房本为韩仇出诸葛安知汉祚移云暗鼎湖龙去逺月明华表归迟何须更上新亭饮大不如前洒泪时读此二诗而不泣下者几希祷雨

    Explanation

    87

    Translation

    Virtuous and Heroic

    Dai Shiping, known as the Retroactive Master, was living in exile in Wuning, Jiangxi, during a time when he had not yet found success. A wealthy man there admired his talent and gave his daughter to him as wife. After living together for two or three years, Dai suddenly wanted to make plans to return home. His wife asked him why, and he told her that he had previously been married. He informed his father-in-law, who became angry. His wife explained gently and tactfully, and gave all her dowry to her husband. She still sent him off with a poem, which went:

    "Regretted having so much talent, pitied by fate, there is no way to keep you. Tore up the paper, reluctantly wrote heart-wrenching words. Willows by the roadside sway gently, thousands of threads, cannot withstand even a little bit of sorrow. Oath made under the moon, not words spoken in a dream. If you return again in the future, in the place where we won't forget each other, pour a cup of wine on my grave. After he left, he drowned himself. Truly virtuous and heroic."

    Elegy for Chancellor Wen

    Wen Tianxiang, the chancellor of the Song dynasty, whose story is recorded in history, even a child of three feet tall could speak of his loyalty and righteousness. The scholar and official Xu Shilong wrote an elegy for him, saying:

    "The great Yuan dynasty did not kill Chancellor Wen. His loyalty and righteousness, both as a minister and a subject, were achieved. His loyalty is like that of Han Wang, who sealed his teeth; his righteousness is like that of the Shu general, who cut off his face. The sun and moon of the universe, the Chinese and the barbarians, all see the wind and frost of the mountains and forests, knowing that the historian may not record everything. I shed tears and write a new poem. Truly skilled in satire."

    Yu Boheng also wrote a poem in his collection, saying:

    "Only holding a sword to hold back the setting sun, the southern hat is powerless against the northern wind. Zi Fang originally came out to avenge Han, Zhuge Liang did not know that the Han dynasty would be overthrown. The clouds darken as the dragon departs far away, the moon shines brightly as the crane returns late. Why bother drinking on the new pavilion, much less than before when tears were shed. Reading these two poems, who among us would not shed tears?"

    88

    往往见蒙古人之祷雨者非若方士然至于印令旗劎符图气诀之一无所用惟取净水一盆浸石子数枚而已其大者若鸡卵小者不等然后黙持密呪将石子淘漉玩弄如此良久輙有雨岂其静定之功已成特假此以愚人耳抑果异物耶石子名曰鲊荅乃走兽腹中所产独牛马者最妙恐亦是牛黄狗寳之属耳
    　　广寒秋
    　　虞邵庵先生集在翰苑时宴繖萨尔学士家歌儿郭氏顺时秀者唱今乐府其折桂令起句云博山铜细袅香风一句而两韵名曰短柱极不易作先生爱其新奇席上偶谈蜀汉事因命纸笔亦赋一曲曰鸾舆三顾茅庐汉祚难扶日莫桑榆深渡南泸长驱西蜀力拒东吴美乎周瑜妙术悲夫关羽云殂天数盈虚造物乘除问汝何如早赋归欤盖两字一韵比之一句两韵者为尤难先生之学问该博虽一时娱戱亦过人逺矣折桂令一名广寒秋一名天香第一枝一名蟾宫引今中州之韵入声似平声又可作去声所以蜀术等字皆与鱼虞相近

    Explanation

    88

    Often, one sees Mongols who pray for rain, unlike the Daoist magicians. They do not use talismans, flags, swords, diagrams, or incantations. Instead, they only take a basin of clean water and soak a few stones in it. The larger ones are as big as eggs, while the smaller ones vary in size. Then, they silently hold a secret incantation and play with the stones, sifting and filtering them. After doing this for a long time, it soon rains. Is it that their power of stillness and concentration has been achieved, merely using this to fool people? Or is it truly a strange substance? The stones are called "Zhadu," produced in the belly of beasts, with those from cows and horses being the best, perhaps also a type of cow bile or dog treasure.

    Guanghan Autumn

    When Mr. Yu Shao'an was in the imperial academy, he was hosted by Scholar Sunsaer. The singer Guo Shunshi Xiu performed a song from the current Yuefu. The opening line of the Zheguiling form reads, "The fine fragrance of the copper incense burner from Bo Mountain," with each line ending in a rhyme, known as "short column," which is extremely difficult to compose. Mr. Yu loved its novelty and, during a conversation about the Shu Han dynasty at the table, asked for paper and ink to compose a song himself, saying, "The carriage was visited three times at the thatched cottage, the Han dynasty is difficult to support, the sun is setting on the mulberry and elm trees, deeply crossing the Nanlu River, advancing far into Western Shu, strongly resisting Eastern Wu, how admirable is Zhou Yu's wonderful skills, how sad is Guan Yu, the clouds have perished, the numbers of heaven are full and empty, the creator multiplies and divides. Ask yourself how you can return home early." Two characters per rhyme, compared to one line with two rhymes, is even more difficult. Mr. Yu's extensive learning, even in this moment of play, surpasses others. The Zheguiling is also known as Guanghan Autumn, Tianxiang First Branch, and Changong Yin. The rhyme of the Central Plains dialect sounds like a level tone and can also be read as a departing tone, which is why words like Shu and Shu are close to the fish and Yu rhymes.

    89

    无恙
    　　战国防赵威后问齐使岁无恙耶王亦无恙耶楚辞九辨曰还及君之无恙说苑魏文侯语仓庚曰击无恙乎又曰子之君无恙乎汉书元帝诏贡禹曰今生有恙何至不已乃上疏乞骸骨聘礼亦曰公问君賔对公再拜郑注云拜其无恙者顾恺之与殷仲堪牋行人安穏布帆无恙隋日本遣使称日出处皇帝致书日没处皇帝无恙神异经曰北方大荒中有兽咋人则
    疾名曰防防恙也尝入人室屋黄帝杀之人无忧疾谓之无恙尔雅曰恙忧也应劭风俗通曰上古之时草居露宿恙噬人虫也善食人心大患苦之凡相问曰无恙恙或以为兽或以为虫或谓无忧广干禄书兼取忧及虫事物纪原兼取忧及兽广韵防字下云防兽如狮子食虎豹及人恙字下云忧也病也噬虫善食人心是防恙二义神异经合而一之则误矣不乱附妾

    89

    Translation:

    Safe and Sound
    In the Strategies of the Warring States, Zhao Wei Hou asked the envoy from Qi, "Is the year safe and sound? Is the king also safe and sound?" In the Nine Arguments from the Songs of Chu, it is said, "I hope you are safe and sound." In the Discourses of the States, Duke Wen of Wei asked, "Is the bird safe and sound?" He also said, "Is your lord safe and sound?" In the Book of Han, Emperor Yuan of Han issued an edict to Gong Yu, saying, "If you are unwell, why persist in your duties? He then submitted a memorial requesting retirement. In the Rites of the Crown Prince, it is said, "The host asks the guest about their well-being, and the guest responds with a bow." Zheng Xuan commented, "The bow is a sign of being safe and sound." In a letter from Gu Kaizhi to Yin Zhongkan, he wished, "The travelers are safe and the journey is going well." During the Sui dynasty, an envoy from Japan sent a letter to the emperor of China, saying, "Wishing the emperor of the distant land safe and sound." The Strange and Marvelous Classic states, "In the far northern wilderness, there is a beast that bites people." It is called the Fang, which is said to cause harm, hence the term "safe and sound." It is said that once, the Fang entered a house, and Huangdi killed it, and people were free from worry and disease, hence the term "safe and sound." The Erya says, "Worry." Ying Shao in Customs and Folkways says, "In ancient times, people lived in the wild and slept under the open sky, and the yiang was a creature that ate human hearts, causing great suffering. Therefore, when people ask, 'Are you safe and sound?' it can refer to being free from worry, a mythical creature, or simply being well. The Book on Gaining Wealth and Status takes both meanings, as does the Origins of Things. In the Guangyun dictionary, it is said, "The fang is a creature like a lion that eats tigers and leopards and humans," and "Worry or illness, a creature that eats human hearts." The Strange and Marvelous Classic incorrectly combines these two meanings. "A lack of disorder in the household."

    90

    维秦君昭妙年游京师其执友邓载酒祖饯既而舁一殊色小鬟至前令拜秦因指之曰此吾为部主事某人所买妾也幸君便航可以附达秦弗敢诺邓作色曰纵君自得之亦不过二千五百缗耳何峻辞乃尔秦勉强从命迤至临清天渐暄夜多虫蚋可畏内之帐中同寝直抵都下置舍馆主妇处持书往见主事问曰足下与家眷来耶曰无有主事意极不恱随以小车取归逾
    三日谒谢曰足下长者也昨已作答简附便驿报吾邓公且使知足下果能不孤公付托之意矣遂相与痛饮尽欢而散夫柳下惠夜宿郭门有女子来同宿恐其冻死坐之于懐至晓不为乱顔叔子独居夜大雨有女子投之令其执烛至明不二志故千古以为美事今秦之于此女子也相从数千里饮食起居无适而不同又非造次颠沛者之比可谓厚徳君子矣后秦之子孙咸至显宦

    Explanation

    90

    A Man of Virtue

    During his prime, Wei Yang Qin Jun Zhao traveled to the capital. His close friend Deng hosted a farewell banquet for him, bringing wine and other delicacies. Afterward, Deng brought a young woman of extraordinary beauty to the front and had her bow to Qin. Pointing to her, Deng said, "This is the concubine I bought for a certain official in my department. Please, if you have a convenient boat, you can accompany her to her destination." Qin was reluctant to agree, but Deng became angry and said, "Even if you got her yourself, it would still not exceed two thousand five hundred strings of cash. Why are you so sternly refusing?" Qin reluctantly agreed and gradually made their way to Linqing. The weather was gradually becoming warm, and at night, there were many mosquitoes, which were fearful. He put her in the tent and slept with her. They continued directly to the capital, where they stayed in a guesthouse at the wife's place. Qin held a letter and went to meet the official, asking, "Are you coming with your family?" The official replied, "No." The official was extremely unhappy and then took them back in a small carriage. After three days, Qin paid respects and thanked him, saying, "You are a person of virtue. Yesterday, I wrote a reply and sent it through a convenient courier to inform my friend Deng. He will know that you indeed can fulfill his trust and expectations." They then drank heartily together and parted.

    As for Lady Liu Xiaohui, who spent the night at the city gate, fearing that a woman might freeze to death, he sat her in his lap until dawn without acting improperly. As for Yan Shuzi, who lived alone and was caught in heavy rain at night, a woman sought refuge with him, and he ordered her to hold a candle until morning, without having a second thought. Therefore, throughout history, these deeds have been considered beautiful. Now, regarding Qin and this woman, they traveled together for thousands of miles, sharing daily life without any differences. Moreover, they were not like those who hastily and carelessly acted. Qin can be said to be a man of great virtue. Later, his descendants all became high-ranking officials.

    91

    辍耕録卷四
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷五　　　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰角端
    　　金华黄先生溍尝云子将以举子经学取科第有一赋题曰角端亦曾求其事实否乎余曰未也因记史记司马相如兽则麒麟角之语退而阅之按注郭璞曰角音端似猪角在鼻上堪作弓又云似麒麟而无角毛诗疏云麟黄色角端有肉张楫云角端似牛角可以为弓以此推之岂亦麟之属与及考符瑞志名臣事畧癸辛杂识等书乃始得其详盖太祖皇帝驻师西印度忽有大兽其髙数十丈一角如犀牛然能作人语云此非帝世界宜速还左右皆震慑独耶律文正王进曰此名角端乃旄星之精也圣人在

    Explanation

    91

    Translation

    Chuogeng Lu, Volume Four
    Qinding Siku Quanshu
    Chuogeng Lu, Volume Five
    Compiled by Tao Zongyi of the Yuan dynasty

    Jueduan

    Huang Jing of Jinhua once said to me, "Are you planning to pass the imperial examinations using Confucian studies? There is a composition topic called Jueduan. Have you ever looked into its historical facts?" I replied, "No, I haven't." He then recalled a passage from the Records of the Grand Historian by Sima Xiangru, which mentions the Qilin and Jueduan. Later, I reviewed this passage. According to Guo Pu's commentary, Jueduan is pronounced as Duan, resembling a pig's horn on its nose, suitable for making bows. Another account states that it resembles the Qilin but lacks a horn. The Shijing commentary by Zheng Xuan describes the Qilin as yellow with a horn-like projection at the end, which has flesh. Zhang Ji commented that Jueduan resembles a cow's horn and can be used to make bows. From these accounts, can we infer that Jueduan is also a kind of Qilin? Upon consulting the Book of Auspicious Signs, Biographies of Eminent Officials, and Kui Xin Za Shi, I finally gained a clearer understanding.

    It is said that during the reign of the first emperor of the Yuan dynasty, Kublai Khan, when his army was stationed in western India, a large beast suddenly appeared, standing as tall as several dozen zhang, with one horn like that of a rhinoceros. This creature was able to speak human language, saying, "This is not the world of the emperor; you should return quickly." Everyone around was terrified, but only Yelü Wenzheng, a high-ranking official, stepped forward and said, "This creature is called Jueduan, the essence of the Mao star. It is a sign of the presence of a sage ruler."

    92

    位则斯兽奉书而至且能日驰万八千里灵异如神不可犯也帝即回驭载稽之前志神禹氏治水功成天降飞莬日行三万里而未尝善言也又后土趺蹄之兽至善言而未闻其独角也轩辕获飞黄而独角汉武获兽并角而五蹄又未尝闻其能言善驰也及圣祖诞膺天命而角端出焉夫一角者所以明海宇之一万八千里之涉者所以示无逺弗届也此又天将开天下于大一统之象也至正庚寅江浙乡试八月二十二日夜二鼓院中彷佛见一物驰过甚疾其状若猛兽者军卒从而喧哄因出角端为赋题劈正斧
    　　劈正斧以苍水玉碾造髙二尺有奇广半之徧地文藻粲然或曰自殷时流至今者如天子登极正旦天夀节御大明殿会朝时则一人执之立于陛下酒海之前盖所以正人不正之意

    Explanation

    92

    In a position of authority, this creature brings a message and can travel 18,000 li in a day. It is supernatural and invincible, like a ghost or god. The emperor immediately turns back his carriage and consults previous records. Yu the Great, after successfully controlling the floods, was said to have a rabbit sent by heaven that could travel 30,000 li in a day, but it did not speak well. The creature with the hoof of the earth goddess, known for its eloquence, has never been heard to have a single horn. The Yellow Emperor obtained a flying horse with a single horn, while Emperor Wu of Han obtained a creature with two horns and five hooves, but neither was known for its ability to speak or travel swiftly. When the founding emperor was born with a divine mandate, the creature with a single horn appeared. A single horn symbolizes the vastness of the empire, covering 18,000 li, and shows that no distance is too great to reach. This is a sign that heaven is preparing for the unification of the world under one rule. In the year 1350 of the Yuan Dynasty, during the Jiang-Zhe provincial examination, on the night of the eighth month's twenty-second day, at the second drumbeat in the examination hall, a creature was seen rushing by very quickly, resembling a fierce animal. The soldiers followed and created a commotion. The creature was identified as the horned one, and a poem was written to commemorate it. The poem describes a ceremonial axe made from a jade called "苍水玉," about two feet high and half as wide, beautifully decorated. It is said that during the emperor's ascension, New Year, and birthday celebrations, when he holds court in the Grand Hall of Brightness, a person holds this axe standing in front of the wine sea, symbolizing the correction of wrongdoings.

    In a position of authority, this creature brings a message and can travel 18,000 li in a day. It is supernatural and invincible, like a ghost or god. The emperor immediately turns back his carriage and consults previous records. Yu the Great, after successfully controlling the floods, was said to have a rabbit sent by heaven that could travel 30,000 li in a day, but it did not speak well. The creature with the hoof of the earth goddess, known for its eloquence, has never been heard to have a single horn. The Yellow Emperor obtained a flying horse with a single horn, while Emperor Wu of Han obtained a creature with two horns and five hooves, but neither was known for its ability to speak or travel swiftly. When the founding emperor was born with a divine mandate, the creature with a single horn appeared. A single horn symbolizes the vastness of the empire, covering 18,000 li, and shows that no distance is too great to reach. This is a sign that heaven is preparing for the unification of the world under one rule. In the year 1350 of the Yuan Dynasty, during the Jiang-Zhe provincial examination, on the night of the eighth month's twenty-second day, at the second drumbeat in the examination hall, a creature was seen rushing by very quickly, resembling a fierce animal. The soldiers followed and created a commotion. The creature was identified as the horned one, and a poem was written to commemorate it. The poem describes a ceremonial axe made from a jade called "苍水玉," about two feet high and half as wide, beautifully decorated. It is said that during the emperor's ascension, New Year, and birthday celebrations, when he holds court in the Grand Hall of Brightness, a person holds this axe standing in front of the wine sea, symbolizing the correction of wrongdoings.

    93

    兴隆笙
    　　兴隆笙在大明殿下其制植众管于柔韦以象大匏土鼓二韦橐按其管则簧鸣簨首为二孔雀笙鸣机动则应而舞凡燕会之日此笙一鸣众乐皆作笙止乐亦止
    　　尚食麫磨
    　　尚食局进御麦麫其磨在楼上于楼下设机轴以旋之驴畜之蹂践人役之往来皆不能及且无尘土臭秽所侵乃巧工瞿氏造焉
    　　僧有口才
    　　大徳间僧丹巴者一时朝贵咸敬之徳夀太子病防薨布尔罕皇后遣人问曰我夫妇崇信佛法以师事汝止有一子宁不能延其夀邪荅曰佛法譬犹灯笼风雨至乃可蔽若烛尽则无如之何矣此语即吾儒死生有命之意异端中得此亦可谓有口才者矣邓中斋
    　　邓光荐先生剡号中斋庐陵人宋亡以义行着其所赋鹧鸪诗曰行不得也哥哥痩妻弱子羸防防天长地阔多网罗南音渐少北语多肉飞不起可奈何行不得也哥哥其意可见矣又有赞文丞相像曰目煌煌兮疎星晓寒气英英兮晴雷殷山头碎柱兮璧完血化碧兮心丹呜呼孰谓斯人不在世间

    Explanation

    93

    兴隆笙
    The Xīnglóng shēng is positioned under the grand hall. Its construction involves embedding multiple pipes into a soft, flexible material, resembling a large gourd and a clay drum. A double-layered bag is used to support the pipes, and when the pipes are pressed, the reeds produce sound. The top of the frame is adorned with two peacock motifs, and the mechanism that causes the flute to produce sound also makes the peacock motifs move in response, dancing as the flute plays. On the day of formal banquets, when this flute sounds, all other music begins, and when it stops, the music ceases as well.

    尚食麫磨
    The Shàngshí jú (Imperial Food Bureau) presents wheat flour to the emperor. The grinding mechanism is located on the upper floor, while a mechanical axis is set up on the lower floor to rotate it. Even the trampling of donkeys and the movement of human laborers cannot reach it, and it is free from the intrusion of dust, dirt, and foul odors. This ingenious device was created by the skilled craftsman Qú.

    僧有口才
    During the Dàdé period, there was a Buddhist monk named Danba, whom all the high-ranking officials in the court revered. When Dèshòu, the crown prince, fell ill and passed away, Bulu Han, the empress, sent someone to ask, "My husband and I have always believed in Buddhism and treated you as our teacher. We have only one son. Can you not extend his life?" The monk replied, "Buddhism is like a lantern; it can only provide shelter when the wind and rain come. If the candle is extinguished, there is nothing that can be done." This response reflects the Confucian belief that life and death are determined by fate. Even within the heretical Buddhist tradition, this monk demonstrated remarkable eloquence.

    邓中斋
    Mr. Dèng Guāngjiàn, also known as Zhōngzhāi, was from Lúlíng. After the fall of the Song Dynasty, he was known for his righteous actions. He composed a poem about the zhègū bird, which reads: "Go no further, my brother! My thin wife and weak child are frail, the sky is vast and the earth is wide, filled with many dangers. Southern dialects are fading, while Northern dialects are becoming more prevalent. Even meat cannot fly; what can be done? Go no further, my brother!" The meaning of this poem is clear. He also wrote an inscription for a portrait of a statesman, which reads: "Bright and shining eyes, like sparse stars at dawn; cold air that is sharp and clear, like thunder at noon. A broken column on a mountain, a perfect jade disc, blood turning into jade, a heart that is red. Alas, who would say that this person is not in the world?"

    94

    汪水云
    　　汪元量先生大有号水云天兵平杭日诗曰西塞山边日落处北关门外雨来天南人堕泪北人笑臣甫低头拜杜鹃又曰钱塘江上雨初干风入端门阵阵酸万马乱嘶临警跸三宫洒泪湿铃鸾童儿賸遣追徐福疠须当灭贺兰若说和亲能活国婵娟应是嫁呼韩此语尤悲哽先生诗有水云集
    　　厚徳
    　　徐文献公琰字子方至元间为陜西省郎中有属路申解到省误漏圣字案吏指为不敬议欲问罪公改其牍云照得来解内第一行脱去第三字今将元文随此发下可重别申来时皆称为厚徳长者
    　　毁前朝玉玺
    　　后至元间太师巴延出太府监所藏歴代玉玺磨去文改造押字图书及鹰坠等物以分散其党与盖先以奏请故也独唐武氏一玺玉色莹白制作如官印璞仅半寸许因不可他用遂付艺文监收之竟获永存岂武氏之智能料之乎

    Explanation

    94

    汪水云
    　　汪元量先生，号水云，在天兵平定杭州之日，作诗曰：“西塞山边日落处，北关门外雨来天。南人堕泪北人笑，臣甫低头拜杜鹃。”又曰：“钱塘江上雨初干，风入端门阵阵酸。万马乱嘶临警跸，三宫洒泪湿铃鸾。童儿剩遣追徐福，疠鬼须当灭贺兰。若说和亲能活国，婵娟应是嫁呼韩。”此语尤悲哽。先生诗有《水云集》。
    　　厚德
    　　徐文献公琰，字子方，至元年间为陕西省郎中。有属路申解到省，误漏圣字，案吏指为不敬，议欲问罪。公改其牍云：“照得来解内第一行脱去第三字，今将元文随此发下，可重别申来时。”皆称为厚德长者。
    　　毁前朝玉玺
    　　后至元年间，太师巴延出太府监所藏历代玉玺，磨去文，改造押字图书及鹰坠等物，以分散其党与。盖先以奏请故也。独唐武氏一玺，玉色莹白，制作如官印，璞仅半寸许，因不可他用，遂付艺文监收之，竟获永存。岂武氏之智能料之乎？

    95

    披秉歌诀
    　　天子郊祀与祭太庙日百官陪位者皆法服凡披秉须依歌诀次第则免颠倒之失歌曰袜履中单黄带先裙袍蔽膝绶绅连方心曲领蓝腰带玉佩丁当冠笏全
    　　三教
    　　富珠哩翀子翚公在翰林时进讲罢上问曰三教何者为贵对曰释如黄金道如白璧儒如五谷上曰若然则儒贱邪对曰黄金白璧无亦何妨五谷于世岂可一日阙哉上大说
    　　授时厯法
    　　授时厯要法歌曰授时厯法君要知但以九年旧厯推【古云但看九年兔望日便是今年正月一】月大月小起初一看其初一天地支【天不言干者为诗句所拘然举支以见干也当推九年前厯每月初一是何干支便以此干支依后法数去】大月天干五支九【且如大月天干五地支九假令初一日甲子甲至戊五数也子至申九数也即以戊申为今月朔】小月天四地八耦【且如小月天干四地支八假令初一日丙寅丙至已四数也寅至酉八数也即以己酉为今月朔古云前九之年起法大月五九小四八】月大三十日无差【如初一日己酉数至次月朔见己卯

    Explanation

    95

    Unfurling and Holding the Chant

    On the day of the emperor's ritual sacrifices at the Altar of Heaven and the Temple of Ancestors, all officials who accompany the emperor wear ceremonial attire. In unfurling and holding the chant, one must follow the sequence of the chant to avoid mistakes. The chant goes: "First put on the socks and shoes, then the yellow belt. Next, wear the skirt, robe, and sash, with the sash and belt connected. Then, put on the blue sash, jade pendants jingling, and the hat with the tablet."

    The Three Teachings

    During his time in the Hanlin Academy, Gong Zihui was asked by the emperor after a lecture, "Which of the Three Teachings is the most valuable?" He replied, "Buddhism is like gold, Taoism is like a white jade, and Confucianism is like the five grains." The emperor asked, "If so, then is Confucianism lowly?" Gong replied, "Even if gold and white jade are absent, how could the five grains be lacking in the world for even a single day?" The emperor was greatly pleased.

    The Method of Determining the Calendar

    The Method of Determining the Calendar is what the sovereign needs to know. It can be determined by looking at the ninth year of the old calendar. [Ancient people said, "Just look at the ninth year's rabbit full moon day, and that is the first day of the first month this year."] To determine whether a month is large or small, look at the beginning of the month and the Heavenly Stems and Earthly Branches. [The sky does not mention the Heavenly Stems because it is constrained by the poetic form, but by mentioning the Earthly Branches, one can see the Heavenly Stems. One should look at the calendar of nine years ago to see what Heavenly Stem and Earthly Branch the first day of each month was, and then use this Heavenly Stem and Earthly Branch to count forward according to the following method.] For a large month, the Heavenly Stem is five and the Earthly Branch is nine. [For example, if the first day is Jia Zi, Jia to Wu is five, and Zi to Shen is nine. Then, Wu Shen is the first day of this month.] For a small month, the Heavenly Stem is four and the Earthly Branch is eight. [For example, if the first day is Bing Yin, Bing to Ji is four, and Yin to You is eight. Then, Ji You is the first day of this month. Ancient people said, "The method of determining the calendar starts from the ninth year, with large months being five and nine, and small months being four and eight."] A large month has thirty days without error. [For example, if the first day is Ji You, count to the first day of the next month and see Ji Mao.]

    96

    即月大也】月小分明只廿九【如月朔数至次月朔止廿九日即月小也】节气只凭九年厯【假造甲午年厯则看丙戌年节气】二十四气真端的【要知今年节气则看前九年中是何节气】天干三数地支七【假如癸亥日癸见乙三数亥见己七数也】熟记心中须歴歴定时二十四年取逢时遇八君无虑【如逢子时交节气却用未时亦交也中气如之】若依此法个中推方省隂阳奥处闰月本来中气无【古云闰月无中气】何劳物外更他图世人谙得神仙术不是愚氓是丈夫又歌曰九年二月半便是正月一【前九年二月十五日辰即今年正月初一日辰

    Explanation

    96

    This month is a full month. A small month is clearly only twenty-nine days. If the number of days from the first day of one month to the first day of the next month is twenty-nine, then it is a small month.

    The solar terms depend solely on the nine-year calendar. If you create a calendar for the year Jia Wu, you should look at the solar terms of the year Bing Xu.

    The twenty-four solar terms are truly accurate. To know the solar terms of this year, look at what they were in the previous nine years.

    The heavenly stems are three and the earthly branches are seven. For example, on the day Gui Hai, Gui corresponds to Yi (three) and Hai corresponds to Ji (seven).

    Memorize it well in your heart, and the timing will be clear after twenty-four years, meeting the eighth time without worry. If you encounter the Zi hour for the solar term, use the Wei hour instead, and it will also be correct for the middle term.

    If you follow this method, you can understand the profound aspects of yin and yang, and the leap month originally has no middle term. It is said that a leap month has no middle term.

    Why bother with other things outside the world? Those who understand the art of immortality are not fools but men of great virtue.

    A song goes: Nine years ago, the middle of February is the first day of January this year. Nine years ago, the fifteenth day of February at the hour of Chen is the first day of January this year at the hour of Chen.

    97

    该九十七个半月二千八百八十日六甲转四十八周】只九年中取大小无差失又歌曰若要求立春相冲对食神【假如前九年甲子日立春甲食丙子冲午即今年丙午日立春也二十四气准此】闰月无中气说与惺惺人又一法云古有数九九之语盖自至后起数至九九则春已分矣正如至后一百六日为寒食之岂特此为然凡推算皆有约法推闰歌括云欲知来岁闰先算至之余更看大小尽决定不差殊谓如来岁合置闰止以今年冬至后余日为率且如今年十一月二十二日冬至则本月尚余八日则来年之闰当在八月或小尽则七月若冬至在上旬则以望日为

    Explanation

    97

    Within nine years, there is no discrepancy in the number of days in each month. If one wishes to determine the date of the Spring Equinox, they should use the method of opposition and the food god. For example, if the Spring Equinox falls on a Jiazi day in a certain year, the next year's Spring Equinox will be on the day that is opposed to Jiazi. The same method applies to the other 24 solar terms. A leap month must contain a middle solar term, and this rule is explained to the enlightened. To know the leap month of the next year, first calculate the remainder after the Winter Solstice, then observe whether the month is large or small to determine the exact date without error. Another method states that there is an ancient saying about counting the "nine nines," which means starting to count from the Winter Solstice until the "nine nines" are complete, at which point spring has already arrived. Just as the 106th day after the Winter Solstice is the Cold Food Festival, this principle applies to all calculations. The rhyme for determining the leap month goes: "To know the leap month of the next year, first calculate the remainder after the Winter Solstice, then observe whether the month is large or small to determine the exact date without error." For example, if the next year is to have a leap month, it will be determined by the remaining days after the Winter Solstice of this year. If, for instance, the Winter Solstice this year falls on the 22nd day of the 11th month, then there are still eight days left in the month, so the leap month of next year will be in August. If the month is small, it will be in July. If the Winter Solstice falls in the early part of the month, then the full moon day will be used as the reference.

    98

    断十二日足则复起一数焉推节气歌括云中气与节气但有半月隔要知仔细两时零五刻谓如正月甲子日子时初初刻立春则数至己卯日寅时正一刻则是雨水节也推立春歌括云今岁先知来岁春但隔五日三时辰谓如今年是甲子日子时立春则明年合是己巳日卯时立春若夫刻数则用前法推之又求节气歌曰惊蛰五时二刻求清明十时四刻流立夏一日三时六芒种一日九时周小暑二日二时二立秋二日七时四白露三日零六刻寒露三日六时至立冬三日十一二大雪四日四时四小寒四日九时六五日三时交新岁节遇子时加一日此为捷法君须记又

    98

    In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    Final Translation of the Source Text

    After counting twelve days, start again from the beginning. A song or rhyme used to predict or calculate the solar terms states that the solar terms are spaced about half a month apart. To know precisely, two hours and five fifths of an hour (or one hour and one minute) must be considered. For example, if the first day of the first month (甲子日) starts at midnight (子时), the solar term "立春" (Start of Spring) occurs at the initial moment of the first hour. To predict the next year's spring, add five days and three hours to the current year's date. To calculate the exact time of the solar term "驚蛰" (Awakening of Insects), add five hours and two fifths of an hour (or one hour and one minute) to the previous term. For "清明" (Pure Brightness), add ten hours and four fifths of an hour (or two hours and one minute). To calculate the exact time of the solar term "立夏" (Start of Summer), add one day and three hours and six fifths of an hour (or one hour and one minute) to the previous term. For "芒种" (Grain in Ear), add one day and nine hours and six fifths of an hour (or one hour and one minute). To calculate the exact time of the solar term "小暑" (Minor Heat), add two days and two hours and two fifths of an hour (or one hour and one minute) to the previous term. For "立秋" (Start of Autumn), add two days and seven hours and four fifths of an hour (or one hour and one minute). To calculate the exact time of the solar term "白露" (White Dew), add three days and zero six fifths of an hour (or one minute) to the previous term. For "寒露" (Cold Dew), add three days and six hours to the previous term. To calculate the exact time of the solar term "立冬" (Start of Winter), add three days and eleven and two fifths of an hour (or one hour and one minute) to the previous term. For "大雪" (Heavy Snow), add four days and four hours and four fifths of an hour (or one hour and one minute). To calculate the exact time of the solar term "小寒" (Minor Cold), add four days and nine hours and six fifths of an hour (or one hour and one minute) to the previous term. For the New Year, add five days and three hours to the previous term. If a solar term falls at midnight (子时), the next day is considered the start of the new term. These methods are quick and should be remembered.

    99

    一年约法云一周年三百六十五日零三时一月节三十日零五时二刻半月一气十五日零二时五刻又食神定法云甲食丙乙食丁丙食戊丁食己戊食庚巳食辛庚食壬辛食癸壬食甲癸食乙其捷要但取我生之干阳配阳隂配隂是也又时刻约法歌云二十四气渐差除循环时刻四同途单逢正四换初一正三依旧复初初又乘除法推算二十四气时刻云其法不论何岁何月但以日为百数时为十数刻为零数初一至初十于百上下数如过初十于千上下数假如正月十一日亥正一刻立春欲求中气则先下一千一百数十一日故也再下十二数亥时故也如子一丑二之

    Explanation

    99

    The law of one year states that one year is 365 days and 3 hours, one month is 30 days and 5 hours and 2 moments, and one half-month is 15 days and 2 hours and 5 moments. The rule for determining the food star states that for days with a yang element, the food star is yang, and for days with a yin element, the food star is yin. The key to this rule is simply to match the yang element with yang and the yin element with yin. The song of the law of moments states that the 24 solar terms gradually differ in their moments, and they cycle through four paths. On the first day of the fourth month, the moment changes to the first day of the third month, and it returns to the first day of the first month. The method of multiplication and division for calculating the moments of the 24 solar terms states that the method does not depend on the year or month, but uses days as the base unit, with hours as tens and moments as units. For the first ten days of the month, the counting is done in the hundreds, and for days beyond the tenth, the counting is done in the thousands. For example, if it is the eleventh day of the first month at 11 PM and 1 moment, and you want to find the moment of the solar term "Lichun," you first subtract 1,100 for the eleventh day, and then subtract 12 for the hour of 11 PM. The specific hours and their corresponding numbers are as follows: 1 for Zi, 2 for Chou, and so on.

    100

    复加一千五百二十五数共得一七三三则二十七日寅初二刻雨水也何以知为初二刻盖零一数初初刻二数初一刻三数初二刻四数初三刻五数正初刻六数正一刻七数正二刻八数正三刻此立成法也今零三数乃是初二刻矣欲求二月节则于前数上加一五二五【即前一千五百二十五也此项数节气中气皆以之加用】为前正月小尽除去二十九日【如遇大月除去三十日算中气则不除大小月】剩下一三五八则十三日辰时正三刻惊蛰也余仿此【十二时为一日如遇十三时以上则退十二时为一日八刻为一时如遇九刻以上则退八刻为一时也】

    Explanation

    100

    Adding 1525 to the previous number results in a total of 1733, which corresponds to the 27th day at the second watch of the hour of Yin for the Rain Water solar term. How do we know it is the second watch? Because in the traditional method, the first number represents the initial watch, the second number represents the first watch, the third number represents the second watch, the fourth number represents the third watch, the fifth number represents the initial watch of the next hour, the sixth number represents the first watch of the next hour, the seventh number represents the second watch of the next hour, and the eighth number represents the third watch of the next hour. This is a standard method. Now, the third number is the second watch. To find the second month's solar term, add 1525 to the previous number (which is the same as the previous 1525). This number is used for both solar terms and mid-solar terms. For the previous month, subtract 29 days (if it is a large month, subtract 30 days; when calculating mid-solar terms, do not subtract for large or small months). The remainder is 1358, which corresponds to the 13th day at the third watch of the hour of Chen for the Awakening of Insects solar term. The rest follow this pattern. In the traditional time system, 12 hours make up a day. If it is 13 hours or more, subtract 12 hours to get the day. 8 watches make up an hour. If it is 9 watches or more, subtract 8 watches to get the hour.

    101

    丧大记云士葬用国车【国音船示专反或作团又误作国】二綍无碑比出宫用功布注云比出宫用功布则出宫而止至圹无矣旧图云功布谓以大功之布长三尺以御柩居前为行者之节度又义云羽葆功布等其象皆如麾则旌旗无旒者周谓之大麾既夕礼云商祝执功布以御柩执披贾释云谓以葬时乘人故有柩车前执引者及在柩车傍执披者皆御治之又注云居柩车之前若道有低仰倾则以布为抑左右之节使执引者执披者知之也道有低谓下阪时也道有仰谓上阪时也倾谓道之两边在柩车左右辙有髙下也若道有低则抑下其布使执

    Explanation

    101

    The Sangda Ji states that for the burial of a scholar, a state carriage is used, with two ropes (綍) and no stele. When the coffin is moved out of the palace, coarse cloth (功布) is used. The note explains that coarse cloth is used when the coffin is moved out of the palace, and it is used only until the coffin reaches the burial site. An old diagram describes coarse cloth as being made from coarse fabric, three feet long, used to guide the coffin and serve as a guide for those leading it. Another interpretation states that the canopy (羽葆) and coarse cloth are similar in appearance to flags, and flags without tassels are called "great banners" (大麾) in the Zhou dynasty. The Jixi Li states that the ritual official (商祝) holds coarse cloth to guide the coffin and holds the shroud (披). The commentary explains that this is done because during the funeral, there are people in the carriage who guide the coffin, and those holding the shroud are responsible for managing the cloth. Another note explains that when standing in front of the coffin carriage, if the road is uneven, the cloth is used to adjust the height on the left and right sides, so that those guiding and holding the shroud know how to proceed. "Low" refers to descending a slope, "high" refers to ascending a slope, and "inclined" refers to the uneven terrain on either side of the road, with the carriage's wheels at different heights. If the road is low, the cloth is lowered on that side to adjust the balance.

    102

    引者知其下阪也若道有仰则举其布使执引者知其上阪也若柩车左边右边或髙下倾亦左右布使知道有倾也假令车之东辙下则抑下其布向东使西边执披者持之若车之西辙下则抑下其布向西使东边执披者持之所以然者使车不倾也大夫御柩以茅诸侯以羽葆天子以纛指引前后左右皆如功布之施为也又既夕礼将葬啓肂也商祝免袒执功布入自西阶注云功布灰治之布也执之以接神为有所拂防贾释云拂防犹言拂拭也故下经云商祝拂防用功布是拂拭去尘也此始告神而用功布拂拭谓拂拭去凶邪之炁也【出聂崇义三礼图】

    Explanation

    102

    The person guiding knows when the path descends if the road inclines upward, then they raise or lift the cloth so that the person pulling knows it is going uphill. If the left or right side of the funeral carriage is higher or lower, causing it to tilt, they also adjust the cloth on the left or right side so that they know there is a tilt. Suppose the eastern wheel of the carriage is lower, then they lower the cloth toward the east, so that the person holding the cloth on the west side can hold it. If the western wheel of the carriage is lower, then they lower the cloth toward the west, so that the person holding the cloth on the east side can hold it. The reason for this is to ensure the carriage does not tilt. High-ranking officials guide the funeral carriage using straw, princes use plumes, and the emperor uses a flag. Guidance is applied uniformly to the front, back, left, and right, just as with the application of the cloth. Additionally, during the ritual performed at dusk before burial, the coffin is opened. The ritual official removes the upper garment and holds the cloth, entering from the western steps. The cloth is treated with ash, and it is used to wipe away dust when communicating with the spirits. The ritual official wipes away dust using the cloth, which is to say, to wipe away evil and harmful energy. This is the first time to inform the spirits, and the cloth is used to wipe away evil and harmful energy. [From Nèi Chóngyì's Three Rituals Diagrams]

    103

    人中
    　　钱唐陈鉴如以写神见推一时尝持赵文敏公真像来呈公援笔改其所未然者因谓曰唇之上何以谓之人中若曰人身之中半则当在脐腹间盖自此而上眼耳鼻皆双窍自此而下口暨二便皆单窍三画隂三画阳成泰卦也
    　　发烛
    　　杭人削松木为小片其薄如纸镕硫黄涂木片顶分许名曰发烛又曰焠儿盖以发火及代灯烛用也史载周建徳六年齐后妃贫者以发烛为业岂即杭人之所制与宋翰林学士陶公谷清异録云夜有急苦于作灯之缓有知者批杉条染硫黄置之待用一与火遇得焰穗然既神之呼引光奴今遂有货者易名火寸按此则焠寸声相近字之譌也然引光奴之名为新

    Explanation

    103

    Translation:

    Philtrum

    Tian Tancheng, a painter of the Ming Dynasty, was renowned for his skill in capturing the essence of his subjects. Once, he held up a portrait of Zhao Mengfu, the famous calligrapher and painter of the Yuan Dynasty, and said, "Why is the area above the lips called the philtrum? If we say that the middle of the person lies in the navel and abdomen, then it should be there. However, above this point, the eyes, ears, and nose all have two openings, while below, the mouth and the two bodily functions (urination and defecation) all have single openings. The three yin lines and three yang lines form the hexagram 'Tai' (Peace) in the Yijing (Book of Changes)."

    Fire Starter

    People in Hangzhou would cut pine wood into thin slices, as thin as paper, and melt sulfur to apply it to the top of the wood pieces. They called these slices "fire starters" or "cui'er." These were used for starting fires and replacing candles. Historical records show that during the sixth year of the Jiande era of the Northern Qi Dynasty, poor concubines made a living by making fire starters. Could these have been the same as those made by people in Hangzhou? In the Qingyi Lu (Clear and Strange Records) compiled by Tao Gou, a scholar and official of the Song Dynasty, it is written: "In case of an emergency at night, it is difficult to light a lamp quickly. Those who know this would cut strips of pine wood, dye them with sulfur, and keep them ready for use. When touched by fire, they produce a flame. This was so remarkable that they were called 'lighting slaves.' Today, they are sold and renamed 'fire inches.' According to this, 'cui' and 'cun' sound similar, and it may be a misspelling. However, the name 'lighting slave' is new."

    104

    嫁故人女
    　　沈仲说右姑苏人年四十未有子其妻邹氏其他适为置一年少貌美之妾及归命出拜将以奉枕席仲说询其乡贯祖父来歴始不肯言询之再泣而曰妾范复初女也父丧家贫老母见粥于此仲说恻然泪下因嘱妻曰此女父吴中名士乃吾故人岂可以为妾当如己子视之即寻其母使择壻仲说备奁具嫁之人称之至今不置夫嫁人之女为妾为妓为娼者古有其人矣今则未闻也仲说诚贤矣哉
    　　平反
    　　中书左丞李忠宣公徳辉字仲实通州潞县人至元七年庚午公为户部尚书岁旱蝗世祖特命公録山西河东囚行至懐仁民有魏氏发得木偶持告其妻挟左道厌胜谋杀已经数狱服词皆具自以为不免公烛其诬召鞫魏妾榜掠一加服不移晷盖妬其女君谓独陷以是罪可必杀之也即直其妻而杖其夫之溺爱受欺当妾罪死观者神之或咨赏泣下

    Explanation

    104

    Marriage of an Old Friend's Daughter

    Se Zongshuo, a native of the right side of Suzhou, was forty years old and had no son. His wife, Zou, arranged for him to take a younger, beautiful concubine. Upon her arrival, she was ordered to bow, and Se Zongshuo intended to have her serve in the bedroom. When he asked about her hometown, ancestors, and family history, she refused to answer. Upon pressing her again, she wept and said, "I am the daughter of Fan Fuchu. My father died, and my family was poor. My mother sells porridge here." Se Zongshuo felt pity and wept. He then instructed his wife, "This girl is the daughter of a famous scholar in Wu, an old friend of mine. How can she be treated as a concubine? Treat her as if she were your own daughter." He then sought out her mother and arranged for her to choose a husband. Se Zongshuo prepared dowry items for her marriage, and the people spoke highly of him to this day. The act of marrying someone else's daughter as a concubine or prostitute was known in ancient times, but not in modern times. Se Zongshuo truly was a virtuous man.

    Redemption

    Li Zhongxuan Gong Dehui, the Left Chancellor of the Central Secretariat, was a native of Luxian, Tongzhou. In the seventh year of the Zhongtong era (1270), during the reign of Kublai Khan, he was tasked with reviewing the prisoners of Shanxi and Hedong. He arrived in Huai Ren, where a woman named Wei was found with a wooden doll. She accused her wife of using heretical practices to harm her, and the case had already been tried several times with the wife confessing. The woman believed she was guilty and would certainly be executed. Li Zhongxuan Gong Dehui saw through the deception, summoned Wei's wife to be interrogated, and with one beating, she confessed. It turned out that Wei's wife was jealous of her daughter-in-law and had framed her, hoping to kill her. Li Zhongxuan Gong Dehui cleared Wei's wife and punished her husband for his blind love and being deceived. The wife was sentenced to death, and the onlookers were amazed, some even weeping with admiration.

    105

    勘钉
    　　姚忠肃公至元二十年癸未为辽东按察使武平县民刘义讼其嫂与其所私同杀其兄成县尹丁钦以成尸无伤忧懑不食妻韩问之钦语其故韩曰恐顶有钉涂其迹耳验之果然狱定上谳公召钦谛询之钦因矜其妻之能公曰若妻处子邪曰再醮令有司开其夫棺毒与成并正其辜钦悸卒时比公为宋包孝肃公拯云
    　　碑志书法
    　　尝闻诸翰林大老云古碑刻中单书国号曰汉曰宋者盖其建国号诏曰汉曰宋也我朝大元二字在诏防不可单用又凡书官衔俱当从实如廉访使总管之若日监司大守是乱其官制久逺莫可考矣又盖二字止可施诸圹石若于碑须曰额为是雕刻精絶

    Explanation

    105

    Verification of Nails

    Yao Zhongsu, in the year 1283 (the second year of the reign of Emperor Chengzong of Yuan), served as the Censorate Commissioner in Liaodong. A resident of Wuping County, Liu Yi, sued his sister-in-law and her lover for the murder of his brother. Cheng, the magistrate of Cheng County, submitted the case with the body showing no visible injuries. His wife, Han, asked him about the case, and he told her the details. Han suspected that there might be nails covering the traces. Upon verification, this was indeed the case. The case was finalized, and the verdict was submitted for approval. Yao Zhongsu summoned Cheng and carefully questioned him. Cheng then boasted about his wife's abilities. Yao Zhongsu asked, "Is your wife a virgin?" Cheng replied, "She has remarried." Yao Zhongsu ordered that the coffin of her husband be opened, and both the poison and Cheng's body were found. Cheng died of shock. At the time, people compared Yao Zhongsu to Bao Zheng of the Song Dynasty.

    Inscription Style on Steles

    I have heard from the great scholars of the Imperial Academy that in ancient inscriptions, the state name is written as a single character, such as "汉" for the Han Dynasty and "宋" for the Song Dynasty. In our dynasty, the term "大元" (Great Yuan) cannot be used as a single character in imperial edicts. Furthermore, all official titles should be written accurately and truthfully. For example, "廉访使" (censor) and "总管" (governor) should not be simplified to "监司大守" (supervisors and prefects), as this would confuse the official system and cannot be verified. Additionally, the term "盖" (a term for the top of a tombstone) can only be used on tombstones. For inscriptions on stones, one must say "额" (the top of a stone monument) is correct. The carvings should be finely and exquisitely crafted.

    106

    詹成者宋髙宗朝匠人雕刻精妙无比尝见所造鸟笼四面花版皆于竹片上刻成宫室人物山水花木禽鸟纎悉俱备其细若缕而且玲珑活动求之二百余年无复此一人矣
    　　题跋
    　　刘须溪先生会孟题苏李泣别图云事已矣泣何为苏武节李陵诗噫冯海粟先生子振题杨妃病齿图云华清宫一齿痛马嵬坡一身痛渔阳鼙鼓动地来天下痛陈伯敷先生绎曾题杨妃上马娇图云此索清平调词赴沉香亭时邪抑闻渔阳鼙鼓声赴马嵬坡时邪上马固相似情状大不同观者当审诸余观三先生之跋语痛快严峻抑感伤使后世之为人君而荒于色为人臣而失其节者见之宁不知惧乎隆友道
    　　张毅父先生千载庐陵人而宋丞相文公友也公贵显时屡以官辟不就江南既内属公自广还过吉州城下先生来见曰今日丞相赴北某当偕行既至燕寓于公囚所侧近日以美馔餽凡三载始终如一且潜制一椟公受刑日即以藏其首复访求公之室欧阳氏于俘虏中俾出焚其尸先生收拾骸骨袭以重囊与先所函椟南归付公家

    Explanation

    106

    Jian Cheng was a craftsman during the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Song, renowned for his exquisite carving skills. Once, I saw a birdcage he made, with floral panels on all sides, intricately carved from bamboo strips to depict palaces, figures, landscapes, flowers, trees, and birds, all finely detailed and delicate as a thread, yet also elegant and functional. In two hundred years, there has been no one like him.

    Inscriptions
    Dong Xuxi, courtesy name Huisheng, inscribed the painting "Su Wu and Li Ling's Tearful Farewell," saying: "The matter is over; why cry? Su Wu's integrity and Li Ling's poetry, alas!" Feng Haishu, courtesy name Zizhen, inscribed the painting "Yang Guifei's Toothache," saying: "At the Huaqing Palace, a toothache; at Ma嵬 Slope, a whole body in pain. The drums of the An Lushan Rebellion resound, bringing pain to the world." Chen Bufu, courtesy name Yizeng, inscribed the painting "Yang Guifei Mounting Her Horse Gracefully," saying: "Is this the time she went to the Chensha Pavilion to recite the 'Qingping Diao' poems? Or did she hear the drums of the An Lushan Rebellion and go to Ma嵬 Slope? Mounting the horse is the same, but the circumstances are very different. The viewer should examine this." I observe the three scholars' inscriptions, which are both forceful and stern, yet also evoke a sense of melancholy and sorrow, making those who would be rulers and indulge in pleasure, or ministers and lose their principles, see them and surely feel fear. This is the path of friendship and loyalty.

    Zhang Yifu, a thousand-year resident of Luzhou, was a friend of the Song Chancellor Wen Gong. When the Chancellor was in high office, he was repeatedly invited to official positions but declined. When the Southern Song was under Mongol rule, the Chancellor returned from Guangzhou to Jizhou. Zhang Yifu came to see him and said, "Today, the Chancellor is going north; I shall accompany him." After arriving, they stayed in a place near the Chancellor's prison. For three years, Zhang Yifu provided him with fine food, always with the same devotion. He also secretly made a box. On the day the Chancellor was executed, Zhang Yifu used the box to collect his head. He then searched for the Chancellor's family among the captives, ordered them to burn the body, and collected the remains, wrapping them in a heavy bag. He returned the box and the remains to the south, delivering them to the Chancellor's family for burial.

    107

    之后公之子忽梦公怒云绳锯髪断明日起视果有绳束髪其英爽尚如此刘须溪纪其事赞于公画像上曰闲居忽忽万古咄咄天风惨然如动生髪如何寻约亦念束刍岂其英爽犹累形躯同时之人能不颡泚昔忌其生今妬其死邓中斋题曰目烱烱兮疎星晓寒气郁郁兮晴雷殷山头碎柱兮璧完血化碧兮心丹呜呼曾谓斯人不在世间
    　　朱张
    　　宋季年群亡赖子相聚乘舟钞掠海上朱清张瑄最为雄长隂部曲曹伍之当时海濵沙民富家以为苦崇明镇特甚清尝佣杨氏夜杀杨氏盗妻子货财去若捕急輙引舟东行三日夜得沙门岛又东北过髙句丽水口见文登夷维诸山又北见燕山与碣石往来若风与影迹不可得稍怠则复来亡虑十五六返私念南北海道此固径且不逢浅角识之【杭吴明越楚与幽莱解密辽解俱岸大海固舟航可通相朐山海门水中流积堆于江沙其长无际浮海者以竿料浅深此浅生角故曰料角明不可度越云】廷议兵方兴请事招懐奏可清瑄即日来以吏部侍郎左迁七资最下一等授之令部其徒属为防海民义

    Explanation

    107

    After the official, his son suddenly dreamed that the official was angry and said, "Hair cut by a rope saw." The next day, upon seeing, there was indeed a rope binding the hair. His spirit still remained as such. Liu Xuxi recorded this incident and praised it on the official's portrait, saying, "Idle and aimless, through all time, constantly criticizing. The wind of heaven is mournful, as if stirring up living hair. How to find an agreement, also thinking of binding straw? Is the spirit still burdensome to the physical form? How can contemporaries not feel ashamed? Once envied his life, now envy him after his death." Deng Zhongzhai inscribed, "Eyes bright and clear, sparse stars in the cold dawn. The air is heavy and oppressive, the clear thunder roars. The mountain top is broken by a column, the jade remains unbroken. Blood turns to jade, heart is red. Alas, how can one say this person is not in the world."

    During the late years of the Song Dynasty, a group of outlaws gathered and sailed boats for plundering along the coast. Zhu Qing and Zhang Xuan were the most powerful among them, secretly organizing their followers. At that time, the wealthy families along the coastal areas were greatly troubled, especially in Chongming Town. Zhu Qing once worked for Yang's household but at night killed Yang and took away his wife, children, and wealth. If captured, they would immediately lead their boats eastward, traveling for three days and nights to reach Shamen Island. Then they would head northeast, passing through the mouth of Goguryeo, seeing Wendi and Yiwu mountains, and further north to see Yan Mountain and Jieshi. Their movements were swift and elusive, like the wind and shadows, making them hard to track. If they relaxed for a moment, they would return, and it was estimated that they would come back fifteen or sixteen times. Privately, they thought the sea route between the north and south was direct and rarely encountered shallow waters, so they were familiar with it. [Hangzhou, Wu, Ming, Yue, Yang, Chu, and Youlaie, Jie, Liaodao all have coastlines, and the sea routes are connected. It is said that the water flows through the Jiangshan, and the length is boundless. Those who sail the sea use poles to measure the depth. This shallow water produces a horn, hence it is called a measuring horn, clearly indicating that it cannot be crossed.]

    The court discussed the rising military affairs and proposed to recruit and pacify them. The proposal was approved, and Zhu Qing and Zhang Xuan came immediately. They were demoted to the lowest rank of seven levels and granted the position of Left Censor of the Ministry of Personnel. They were ordered to organize their followers as coastal defense volunteers.

    108

    提刑节制水军江南既内附二人者从宰相入见授金符千戸时方挽漕东南供京师运河隘浅不容大舟不能百里五十里輙为堰潴水又絶江淮遡泗水吕梁彭城古称险处会通河未凿东阿茌平道中车运三百里转输艰而糜费重二人者建言海漕事试之良便【至元十九年也】上方注意向之初年不过百万石后乃至三百万石二人者父子致位宰相弟侄甥壻皆大官田园宅馆徧天下库藏仓庾相望巨艘大舶帆交番夷中舆骑塞隘门巷左右仆从皆佩于莵金符为万户千戸累爵积赀气意自得二人者既满盈父子同时夷戮殆尽没赀产县官党与家破禁锢大徳六年冬也【见胡石塘先生所撰何长者】交谊

    Explanation

    108

    The Censor-in-Chief, who controlled the naval forces, reported that the southern regions had submitted to the central authority. Two individuals, having accompanied the Chancellor in an audience, were granted the rank of Thousand-Household Commander with golden seals. At the time, grain was being transported from the southeast to supply the capital. The canals were narrow and shallow, unable to accommodate large ships, and every hundred or fifty li, a dam would be built to store water. Another route cut through the Yangtze and Huai rivers, traveling upstream along the Si River, passing through the historically dangerous areas of Luliang and Pengcheng. The Grand Canal had not yet been excavated, and the route from Dong'e to Ciping involved transporting grain by cart for three hundred li, which was both difficult and highly wasteful. The two individuals proposed the idea of ocean transport, which proved to be very convenient (this occurred in the nineteenth year of the Zhiyuan era). The emperor paid close attention to this in the early years, and the grain supply increased from not exceeding a million dan to eventually reaching three million dan. Both father and son attained the position of Prime Minister, and their brothers, nephews, sons-in-law, and other relatives all held high-ranking positions. Their farmlands, houses, and mansions were spread throughout the country, and their treasuries and granaries were in sight of each other. They owned large ships and vessels, with sails interwoven, and their carriages and horsemen blocked the narrow streets and alleys. Their servants and attendants all wore golden seals as Thousand-Household or Ten-Thousand-Household Commanders. They accumulated titles and wealth, and were proud and self-satisfied. However, their arrogance eventually led to their downfall. Father and son were simultaneously executed, and their property was confiscated by the officials. Their allies were ruined and banned. This occurred in the winter of the sixth year of the Dade era (as recorded in Mr. He's biography by Mr. Hu Shitang).

    109

    陈子方闵仲达同舍生也皆待次杭府史陈月日在前闵以计力反先之陈殊无怒意因赴都以荐举入仕歴官浙西廉访司佥事闵方陞书吏闻陈来叹曰复何面目见之遂称疾不出陈下车即问左右曰闵仲达何在众以疾对陈曰必为我故非疾也亟造其家闵皇恐出肃陈曰吾与君气谊契厚君昔先我而食禄者命也使非此吾又能致是耶今幸同一公署惟有以教正之幸甚宁舍我与闵感激从事相好如初假宅以死
    　　吾乡周待制先生仁荣字本心筑一室才落成友人杨公道舆疾至门曰愿假君新宅以死先生让正寝居之妻子咸不然先生弗顾未几杨死箱财廿八莫有主者杨之弟诣先生求分财先生曰若兄寄死于我意固在是丧事之费自己出终不利其一毫对众封籍自平阳呼其子来悉付与之
    　　清风堂尸迹

    Explanation

    109

    Chen Zifang and Min Zhongda were fellow students in the same academy, both awaiting their turn for appointment as historians in the Hangzhou prefecture. Chen Yueri was ahead of them in rank. Min, through cunning, managed to advance ahead of Chen, but Chen showed no sign of anger. Chen therefore went to the capital to be recommended for official service and held a series of positions, including that of an inspector in the Zhejiang Western region. When Min was appointed as a scribe, he heard that Chen had arrived and sighed, saying, "How can I face him again?" He then claimed illness and did not leave home. Upon arriving in his new post, Chen immediately asked his attendants, "Where is Min Zhongda?" They replied that he was ill. Chen said, "He must be doing this for me; it is not a real illness." He quickly went to Min's home. Min was extremely frightened and came out to greet Chen, saying, "I and you share a deep bond of friendship. You were appointed before me, which was fate. If it were not for this, how could I have achieved this? Now that we are in the same official residence, I only hope you will correct and guide me." Chen forgave Min and they worked together with gratitude, resuming their friendship as before. Min rented a house until death.

    In my hometown, there was Mr. Zhou Rong, courtesy name Benxin, a distinguished official. He built a new room, and just as it was completed, his friend Yang Gongdaoyu, who was ill, came to the door and said, "I wish to rent your new house until death." Mr. Zhou offered him the main bedroom, but his wife and children all objected. Mr. Zhou ignored them and allowed Yang to stay. Soon after, Yang died. There were twenty-eight boxes of his belongings, but no one claimed them. Yang's brother came to Mr. Zhou and asked to divide the property. Mr. Zhou said, "Your brother entrusted his life to me, and his intention was clear. The expenses for his funeral were covered by me, and I will not profit from any of it." He sealed and recorded the property, then summoned his son from Pingyang to give it all to him.

    The "Qingfeng Hall" is a reference to a historical site or event, but its exact meaning is unclear without further context.

    110

    福州郑丞相府清风堂石阶上有卧尸迹天隂雨时迹尤显盖其当宋季以暮年登科未几拜相至今闾巷表之曰耆徳魁辅之坊郑显时侵渔百姓至夺其屋庐以广居宅有被逼抑者遂自杀于此今所居为官埶豪夺子孙不絶如线因记宋临川吴曾能改斋漫録云建炎四年五月杨勍叛卒由建安宼延平道出小常村掠一妇人逼胁欲犯之妇人毅然誓死不受汚遂遇害横尸道傍贼退人为收瘗之而其尸枕
    籍处痕迹然不灭每雨则其迹干晴即湿宛如人影往来者莫不嗟异乡人或削去之随即复见覆以他土而其迹愈明今三十年矣与顺昌军员范旺事畧同但范现迹街砖而此现于土上耳范死以忠妇死以节小常村去劎浦县治二十里以漫録言之则二人之死足以惊动万世宜其英烈之气不泯如此清风堂者不过寃抑之志不得伸以决絶于一时耳亦何为而然哉岂幽愤所积结致是耶此理殆不可晓
    　　坐右铭
    　　翰林学士卢疎斋先生摰字处道涿郡人坐右铭大书一天字其下细注六字云有记性不急性可谓知畏天者矣

    Explanation

    110

    In the residence of Chancellor Zheng of Fuzhou, there are traces of a corpse on the stone steps of Qingfeng Hall. These traces become particularly visible during rainy and overcast days. It is said that during the late Song Dynasty, Zheng passed the imperial examinations in his old age and was soon appointed as chancellor. To this day, the local people honor him with a commemorative archway inscribed with the words "Virtuous and Capable."

    Zheng Xian was known for exploiting the common people, even seizing their homes to expand his own residence. Those who were oppressed by him committed suicide there. Now, his descendants are barely surviving, having been seized by officials and powerful people.

    The reason for this is recorded in the Nenggai Zhai Manlu by Wu Zeng of Linchuan, a historian of the Song Dynasty. In the fourth year of the Jianyan era (1128 CE), rebel soldiers of Yang Qiang invaded through Jian'an and attacked Xiaochang Village along the Yanping route. They seized a woman and threatened to assault her. The woman vowed to die rather than submit and was killed, lying dead by the roadside. After the rebels retreated, the locals buried her, but the traces of her body remained visible, never fading. Every time it rained, the traces would dry up, and when the sun came out, they would become wet, appearing like a human shadow. Passersby could not help but feel pity and amazement. Some locals tried to erase the traces, but they reappeared immediately. Even when covered with other soil, the traces became even clearer. This has been the case for thirty years now.

    This incident is similar to that of Fan Wang, a military officer of Shunchang. Fan's traces appeared on street bricks, while Zheng's appeared on the ground. Fan died for his loyalty, and the woman died for her virtue. Xiaochang Village is twenty miles from Jianpu County. According to the Nenggai Zhai Manlu, the deaths of these two individuals were sufficient to shock the world, and their heroic spirit remains undiminished. The traces in Qingfeng Hall are merely a manifestation of Zheng's suppressed resentment, which could not be resolved at the time, leading to his ultimate fate. Why did this happen? It is as if accumulated deep-seated anger had manifested in this way. The reason behind it is hard to comprehend.

    Sitting-Right Inscription

    Huang Lin, a scholar-official of the Hanlin Academy, was from Zhuo County. His courtesy name was Chudao. He inscribed a large "Tian" character on his sitting-right inscription and below it wrote six small characters: "With memory but not impulsive." This can be considered as a sign of his reverence for heaven.

    111

    掘坟贼
    　　杭玛瑙寺僧温日观能书所画蒲萄须梗枝叶皆草书法也性嗜酒然杨总统饮以酒则不一沾唇见輙骂曰掘坟贼掘坟贼云
    　　廉介
    　　李仲谦思让滕州邹县人前至元间由嘉兴路吏贡补浙西按察司书吏廉介有为上侍父母下抚两弟毎退食自公则闭户读书稽今考古而教训之俸薄奉养不给妇躬纺绩以益薪水之费仲谦止有一布衫或须浣濯补纫必俟休暇日至是若賔客见访则俾小子致谢曰家君治衣弗可出雷彦正号苦斋者清正慎许可人也时为使偶戯谓曰外郎穿布衲到敢裹着珍珠仲谦畧不答徐至本案书写辞退呈状压几上而归使知深悔失言亲谒谢过请其出终不允使去他使来复往请始复役后仕至宪官
    　　甲午节气
    　　至元三十一年甲午岁节气正月一日壬子立春二月二日癸未惊蛰三月三日癸丑清明四月四日甲申立夏五月五日甲寅芒种六月六日乙酉小暑七月七日己卯立秋八月八日乙酉白露九月九日丙辰寒露十月十日丙戌立冬十一月十一日丁巳大雪十二月十二日丁亥大寒

    Explanation

    111

    The Grave-Digging Thief
    In the Hangzhou Nanzhu Temple, there was a monk named Wen Riguang who was skilled in calligraphy and painting. His brushwork for grapes, stems, branches, and leaves all followed the style of cursive script. He had a fondness for alcohol, yet whenever Yang President offered him wine, he would not touch it even once. Upon seeing the wine, he would immediately curse, calling it a "grave-digging thief," repeating the term for emphasis.

    The Honest and Upright
    Li Zhongqian, courtesy name Si Rang, was from Zou County, Tengzhou. During the early Yuan Dynasty, he was recommended from Jiaxing Prefecture and appointed as a scribe in the Zhejiang Western Censorate. Li was honest and upright, serving his parents above and caring for his two younger brothers below. After completing his official duties, he would close the door and read books, studying both contemporary and ancient texts to educate himself and his family. His salary was meager and insufficient for supporting his family. His wife diligently spun and wove to supplement household expenses. Li had only one cotton robe, which he would not wash or mend until he had free time. If guests came to visit, he would send his son to apologize, saying, "My father is busy with his clothes and cannot come out." Lei Yanzheng, known as Kuzhai, was a man of integrity and caution. At the time, he was an envoy and playfully said to Li, "A low-ranking official wearing a simple robe dares to wear pearls." Li did not respond, calmly returning to his desk to write a letter of resignation and place it on the table before leaving. The envoy realized his mistake and deeply regretted his words, personally apologizing and asking Li to return to his post, but Li refused. When another envoy came to ask again, Li finally agreed to resume his duties and later rose to the position of Censor.

    The Solar Terms of the Year Jia Wu
    In the thirty-first year of the Yuan Dynasty, during the year Jia Wu, the solar terms were as follows:

    • New Year's Day: January 1st, Renzi (Rat)
    • Start of Spring: February 2nd, Guiwei (Pig)
    • Awakening of Insects: March 3rd, GuiChou (Ox)
    • Qingming: April 4th, Jia Shen (Monkey)
    • Start of Summer: May 5th, Jiayin (Tiger)
    • Grain in Ear: June 6th, Yi You (Rooster)
    • Minor Heat: July 7th, Ji Mao (Rabbit)
    • Start of Autumn: August 8th, Yi You (Rooster)
    • White Dew: September 9th, Bingchen (Dragon)
    • Cold Dew: October 10th, Bingxu (Dog)
    • Start of Winter: November 11th, Ding Si (Snake)
    • Great Snow: December 12th, Dinghai (Pig)
    • Great Cold: December 12th, Dinghai (Pig)
    112

    先辈谦让
    　　武林钱思复先生惟善尝言年十六七时以诗见息斋李公于州桥寓居既拜公公答拜命坐辞之再公曰仲尼之席童子隅坐因不敢辞徐永之先生为江浙提举日客往访之者无间亲疏贵贱必送之门外凡客请纳步则曰不可妇人送迎不逾阈右二事可见前辈诸老谦恭退抑汲引后进待人接物者如此双竹杖
    　　白廷玉先生珽号湛渊钱唐人家多竹忽一竿上岐为二人皆异之赋双竹杖诗未几先生殁先生有二子或以为先兆云

    　　辍耕録卷五
    <子部,小说家类,杂事之属,辍耕录>
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷六　　　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰兰亭集刻
    　　兰亭一百一十七刻装禠作十册乃宋理宗内府所藏每版有内府图书钤缝玉池上后归贾平章至国朝有江南八十余年之间凡又易数主矣往在钱唐谢氏处见之后陆国瑞携至松江因得再三披阅并録其目真传世之寳也

    Explanation

    112

    Translation:

    Precedents of Humility and Courtesy

    Mr. Qian Si Fu, a respected scholar from Wulin, once said that when he was sixteen or seventeen, he presented his poetry to Mr. Li at his residence in the State Bridge. After bowing to Mr. Li, Mr. Li returned the bow and invited him to sit, but Mr. Qian declined twice. Mr. Li then said, "Even Confucius allowed a child to sit in the corner during a lesson," and Mr. Qian, feeling compelled by this example, did not dare to decline the invitation to sit. When Mr. Xu Yong was the governor of the Jiang-Zhe region, he would always send visitors out of the door regardless of their status. Whenever guests asked to be accompanied inside, he would say it was not possible. Women were not allowed to cross the threshold when greeting or seeing off guests. These two examples illustrate the humility and courtesy of the older generation, their willingness to guide younger scholars, and their proper conduct towards others, as symbolized by the bamboo walking stick.

    The Collected Carvings of the Orchid Pavilion

    The Orchid Pavilion collection consists of one hundred and seventeen carvings, bound into ten volumes. These were originally collected in the imperial treasury of Emperor Lizong of Song, with each page bearing the imperial seal and the mark of the treasury. Later, they were acquired by General Jia and remained in his possession for over eighty years during the Southern Song dynasty, passing through several owners. I had the opportunity to view them at the residence of Mr. Xie in Qiantang, and later, Mr. Lu Guorui brought them to Songjiang, allowing me to read and study them repeatedly, recording their contents. They are indeed treasures of the world.

    113

    甲集一十二刻【州郡】
    　　修城本【叶仲山防】　定武濶行【合一契行濶】定武肥定武瘦　　　定武板刻【霍子明防】　定武缸石定武断石　　定武古刻　　　两京断石
    　　永兴　　　　古懿郡斋　　　宣城
    　　乙集一十三刻
    　　旧梅花　　　三衢板刻
    　　安吉古苔真草　　　　　　临川麻石
    　　临贺　　　　豫章二　　　　静江府
    　　复州　　　　鼎州【后有武陵二字】　　古潭
    　　新梅花　　　宣城南陵
    　　丙集一十刻
    　　苏州府治　　福州府治　　　福州枣木
    　　道州　　　　金陵三米【米芾米友仁米尹知】
    　　永嘉　　　　古霅断石　　　隆州
    　　郴州　　　　兰亭重言
    　　丁集一十刻
    　　绍兴府治二　绍兴仓司　　　绍兴府学绍兴古刻　　余姚县治　　　曲水诗兰亭曲水诗前　　曲水诗后

    113

    Final Translation

    Collection A: Twelve Plates [Prefectures and Counties]

    • 修城本【叶仲山防】 - The "修城" (Xiu Cheng) edition, protected by Ye Zhongshan.

    • 定武濶行【合一契行濶】 - The "定武" (Ding Wu) edition with wide characters, standardized by a unified contract.

    • 定武肥定武瘦 - The "定武" (Ding Wu) edition with thick and thin characters.

    • 定武板刻【霍子明防】 - The "定武" (Ding Wu) edition printed by woodblock, protected by Huo Ziming.

    • 定武缸石定武断石 - The "定武" (Ding Wu) edition on well-preserved and broken stones.

    • 定武古刻 - Ancient inscriptions or rubbings from the "定武" (Ding Wu) edition.

    • 两京断石 - Broken stones from the two capitals (likely Chang'an and Luoyang).

    • 永兴 - Prefecture or county of Yongxing.

    • 古懿郡斋 - Ancient administrative office or study hall in a prefecture.

    • 宣城 - Prefecture or county of Xuanzhou.

    Collection B: Thirteen Plates

    • 旧梅花 - Ancient inscriptions or rubbings of the "梅花" (Plum Blossom) style.

    • 三衢板刻 - Woodblock-printed edition from the Sanqu region.

    • 安吉古苔真草 - Ancient inscriptions or rubbings from Anji with a specific style (苔真草).

    • 临川麻石 - Inscriptions or rubbings made on hemp stone from Linchuan.

    • 临贺 - Prefecture or county of Linhe (modern-day Lianzhou, Guangdong).

    • 豫章二 - Inscriptions or rubbings from Yuzhang (modern-day Nanchang, Jiangxi), possibly with a specific classification.
