    • 静江府 - Prefecture or county of Jingjiang (modern-day Guilin, Guangxi).

    • 复州 - Prefecture or county of Fuzhou (modern-day Huanggang, Hubei).

    • 鼎州【后有武陵二字】 - Prefecture or county of Dingzhou with the addition of "武陵" (Wuling).

    • 古潭 - Inscriptions or rubbings from the region of "潭" (Tán, modern-day Changsha, Hunan).

    • 新梅花 - Newer inscriptions or rubbings of the "梅花" (Plum Blossom) style.

    • 宣城南陵 - Inscriptions or rubbings from Nanling in Xuanzhou.

    Collection C: Ten Plates

    • 苏州府治 - Inscriptions or rubbings from Suzhou Prefecture.

    • 福州府治 - Inscriptions or rubbings from Fuzhou Prefecture.

    • 福州枣木 - Inscriptions or rubbings made on date wood from Fuzhou.

    • 道州 - Prefecture or county of Daozhou (modern-day Daoxian, Hunan).

    • 金陵三米【米芾米友仁米尹知】 - Inscriptions or rubbings from Jinling (modern-day Nanjing) associated with the three "米" (Mi) figures: Mi Fu, Mi Youren, and Mi Yin知 (unclear).

    • 永嘉 - Prefecture or county of Yongjia (modern-day Wenzhou, Zhejiang).

    • 古霅断石 - Broken stones from the ancient region of "霅" (Zhe, modern-day Huzhou, Zhejiang).

    • 隆州 - Prefecture or county of Longzhou (modern-day Guang'an, Sichuan).

    • 郴州 - Prefecture or county of Chenzhou (modern-day Chenzhou, Hunan).

    • 兰亭重言 - Inscriptions or rubbings related to the "兰亭" (Lanting) Pavilion with a specific emphasis on repetition or emphasis.

    Collection D: Ten Plates

    • 绍兴府治二 - Inscriptions or rubbings from Shaoxing Prefecture, possibly with a specific classification.

    • 绍兴仓司 - Inscriptions or rubbings from the granary administration of Shaoxing.

    • 绍兴府学绍兴古刻 - Inscriptions or rubbings from the Shaoxing Prefecture's school, possibly with a focus on ancient editions.

    • 余姚县治 - Inscriptions or rubbings from Yuyao County.

    • 曲水诗兰亭曲水诗前 - Inscriptions or rubbings of poems

    114

    婺州府治【禇遂良摹】
    　　戊集一十刻【内府】
    　　髙宗临定武【米友仁防】　　　　　　唐贞观
    　　太清开皇　　秘首　　　　　内殿
    　　内司四　　　京师玉堂
    　　已集九刻【杂集】
    　　玉枕　　　　花石　　　　　栁诚悬【大字】唐人硬黄临　唐人双钩　　　晋唐刻孙过庭草　　京师鹅黄木【黄印】
    　　彭城小字
    　　庚集一十一刻【故家】
    　　蔡君谟临　　薛绍彭　　　　秦少游小字
    　　安定家藏　　辛道宗　　　　建康晁谦之绍兴汤氏　　南昌京氏　　　庐陵胡氏蜀刘泾　　　唐摹刻
    　　辛集一十四刻
    　　吴诜草书　　吴璜　　　　　刘无言临龙潭潘氏　　方朔习写　　　周平所藏临江张氏　　天台丁氏　　　新安汪氏江西故家　　庐山甲秀堂　　九江陶氏循王家藏【米芾跋云壬午闰六月九日大江济川亭寳晋斋艎对紫金浮玉羣山
    　　迎快风消暑重装】

    114

    Wuzhou Prefecture's Administrative Center [Imitation by褚遂良]
    　　Fifth Collection, Volume Ten [Imperial Treasury]
    　　Emperor Gaozong's Personal Copy of Dingwu [Inscription by米友仁]　　　　　　Tang Zhen Guan Era
    　　Taiqing Kaihuang　　Secret Collection　　Inner Palace
    　　Inner Office Four　　Jade Hall of the Capital
    　　Sixth Collection, Volume Nine [Miscellaneous Collection]
    　　Jade Pillow　　Ornamental Stones　　栁诚悬 [Large Characters] Tang Dynasty Hard Yellow Paper Copy　Tang Dynasty Double Hooking　　Jin-Tang Era Copy of Sun Guoting's Cursive Script　　Yellowish Wood from the Capital [Yellow Seal]
    　　Small Characters from Pengcheng
    　　Seventh Collection, Volume Eleven [Old Families]
    　　Copy by蔡君谟　　Xue Shaoping　　　　Small Characters by秦少游
    　　Collection from the An Ding Family　　Xin Daong　　　　Collections from Jiankang Chao Qianzhi and Shaoxing Tang　　Nanchang Jing Family　　Luling Hu Family　　Shu Liu Jing Family　　Tang Dynasty Copy
    　　Eighth Collection, Volume Fourteen
    　　Cursive Script by Wu Sheng　　Wu Huang　　　　Copy by Liu Wuyan of Longtan Pan Family　　Practice by Fang Shuo　　Collection from Linjiang Zhang Family owned by Zhou Ping　　Tian Tai Ding Family　　Xin'an Wang Family　　Old Families from Jiangxi　　Jiaxiu Hall on Mount Lu　　Jiujiang Tao Family　　Collection from Xun Wang Family [Inscription by米芾: "On the ninth day of the sixth month of the leap month in the Renwu year, at the Jichuan Pavilion by the Yangtze River, the宝晋斋 boat faced the Purple Gold and Floating Jade mountains, welcoming a refreshing breeze to dispel the summer heat and undergo a major renovation."]

    115

    番阳洪氏
    　　壬集一十四刻
    　　金陵毕氏　　庐山呉氏　　　绍兴曾氏绍兴石氏二　毘陵尤遂初　　李忠愍所刻新唐李氏　　江隂邱氏二　　东阳郭氏昌谷曹氏三
    　　癸集一十四刻
    　　赵虚斋　　　吕氏家藏　　　建邺朱氏
    　　大梁曾朴　　陆子与　　　　韩松
    　　陆载之　　　胡氏将　　　　玉林二赵菊坡　　　不题名二　　　钱唐李和
    　　禊帖考
    　　姜白石先生禊帖偏傍考云　永字无画发笔处防折转　和字口下横笔稍出　年字悬笔上凑顶　在字左反剔　嵗字有防在山之下戈画之右　事字脚斜拂不挑　流字内公字处就回笔不是防　殊字挑脚带横　是字下疋凡三转不断　趣字波畧反卷向上　欣字欠右一笔作章草发笔之状不是捺　抱字已开口　死生亦大矣亦字是四防　兴感感字戈边是直作一笔不是防　未尝不不字下反挑脚处有一阙　右法如此甚多畧举其大概持此法亦可以观天下之兰亭矣五字损本者湍流带右天五字有损也

    Explanation

    115

    The Family of Hong from Fanyang
    　　Nineteen Collections of the Rén Series
    　　The Bi Family of Jinling, The Wu Family of Mount Lu, The Zeng and Shi Families of Shaoxing, You Suichu of Piling, Li Zhongmin's Edition of the Tang Dynasty Li Family, The Qiu Family of Jiangyin, The Guo Family of Dongyang and the Cao Family of Changgu
    　　Nineteen Collections of the Gēi Series
    　　Zhao Xuzhai, The Lu Family Collection, The Zhu Family of Jianye
    　　Zeng Pu of Daliang, Lu Ziyu, Han Song
    　　Lu Zai, Hu Jiang, Zhao Jupu of Yulin, Two Unnamed Works, Li He of Qiantang
    　　Examination of the Xiji Tie
    　　Jiang Kui, the White Stone Master, Examined the Xiji Tie and Said: The "永" Character Has No Initial Stroke, the "和" Character Has a Horizontal Stroke Slightly Extending Below the Mouth, the "年" Character Has a Suspended Brush Stroke Rising to the Top, the "在" Character Has a Reverse Stroke on the Left, the "嵗" Character Has a Protective Stroke Below the Mountain and to the Right of the Sword Stroke, the "事" Character Has a Slanted Brush Stroke Without a Hook, the "流" Character Has a Returning Brush Stroke Inside the "公" Character, Not a Protective Stroke, the "殊" Character Has a Hooked Foot with a Horizontal Stroke, the "是" Character Has Three Continuous Turns Below the "疋", the "趣" Character Has a Wavy Stroke Reversing and Curling Upward, the "欣" Character Has a Brush Stroke on the Right Side of the "欠" Character in the Form of a Cursive Script Initial Stroke, Not a Horizontal Stroke, the "抱" Character Has an Open Mouth, the "亦" Character in "死生亦大矣" Has Four Protective Strokes, the "感" Character in "兴感" Has a Straight Stroke on the Side of the Sword, Not a Protective Stroke, the "不" Character in "未尝不" Has a Reverse Hook on the Bottom with a Gap, The Method Is Like This, There Are Many More, I Will Briefly Mention the General Principles, Holding This Method One Can Also Observe the Lantingji Xu of the World. The "五" Character in the Damaged Version Has a Damaged "天" Character on the Right.

    116

    丧师衰绖
    　　顾徳玉字润之槜李人自幼从宁国路儒学教授俞观光先生学先生无子尝与人曰吾昔寝疾于杭润之侍汤药情至切若父子医为之感动弗忍受金今我行且老必托之以死既而访医吴中病且革趣舟归润之进次尹山卒时后至元初元闰十二月戊子也明日乃至槜李润之奉其尸敛于家衰绖就位人士为润之来吊者润之拜之越明年于海盐近顾氏之先茔嵗时祭享惟谨或曰敛于家礼与曰吾闻师哭诸寝又云生于我乎馆死于我乎殡非家敛之则将师尸委诸草莾生

    Explanation

    116

    Gu Deyu, courtesy name Runzhi, was from Juxi. From a young age, he studied under Professor Yu Guangxiang of the Confucian school in Ningguo. Yu Guangxiang had no children and once said to others, "In the past, I was ill in Hangzhou, and Runzhi served me with medicine and soup, showing great filial piety, as if he were my own son. The doctor was moved and refused to accept payment. Now I am about to grow old, and I must entrust him with my death." Soon after, he visited a doctor in Wu, and as his illness worsened, he hurriedly returned home. Runzhi entered the boat and arrived at Yinshan, where Yu Guangxiang died. The next day, Runzhi returned to Juxi and dressed in mourning attire, taking his place at the funeral. Scholars came to pay their respects, and Runzhi bowed to them. The following year, he was buried near the ancestral tomb of the Gu family in Haiyan. Every year, sacrifices and offerings were made with great care.

    Some said, "He was buried in his home, which is not in accordance with the rites." Runzhi replied, "I have heard that one should cry at the teacher's bed and that the teacher was born and died in my home. If not, the teacher's body would be left to rot in the wilderness."

    117

    服其训死而委诸草莾有人心者弗为也曰师无服而为衰绖固近于掠美者矣曰疑衰加麻之绖带礼也故曰二三子绖而出至除之心丧戚容终三年夫民生于三师居其一于父子也何异今吾则加一等以行之盖出于人心天理之本然若之何其惑也闻者叹伏先生讳长孺越之新昌人吁圣逺言湮世道不古乆矣朝为师生而莫途人者比比皆是润之乃独能行人之所难行于不可行之时盖絶无而仅有者真仁矣哉天下后世之为人弟子而忘其师闻润之之言宁不有动于中欤
    　　法帖谱系
    　　法帖谱系云熙陵以武定四方载櫜弓矢文治之余留意翰墨乃出御府歴代所藏真迹命侍书王着摹勒刻板禁中厘为十卷各于卷尾题奉圣防模勒入石此歴代法帖之祖

    Explanation

    117

    Translation:

    To serve the teachings and die, leaving oneself abandoned in the wilderness, those with a human heart would not do so. A teacher who does not wear mourning clothes but acts as if he were in mourning is indeed close to appropriating the honor. It is likely that adding hemp to the mourning attire is a ritual, hence it is said that the disciples wear mourning attire when leaving and remove it upon returning, maintaining a grieving appearance and heart throughout the three years. A person's life is divided into three parts, and the teacher occupies one part, just like the father and son. Now I go one step further in my actions, which stems from the fundamental nature of human heart and reason; how can this be considered confusing? Those who heard this were amazed and impressed. The gentleman's name is Changru, from Xinchang, Yue (modern-day Zhejiang). Alas, the sages are far away, their words are lost, and the world has long deviated from its ancient ways. Those who treat their teachers as passersby by morning are everywhere. Only Runzhi can do what others find difficult and impossible, truly demonstrating great virtue. Those who are disciples in the future and forget their teachers, hearing Runzhi's words, would surely be moved.

    Genealogy of Calligraphic Collections

    The genealogy of calligraphic collections states that Emperor Taizong of Song (Xiling) stabilized the four directions through martial prowess, carrying his bow and arrows, and in the aftermath of his cultural governance, he took an interest in calligraphy. He then drew out the genuine calligraphic works collected in the imperial treasury through the ages and commissioned the court scribe Wang Zhe to trace and engrave them onto woodblocks within the palace. These were compiled into ten volumes, each with the title "Imperial Edict: Traced and Engraved onto Stone" at the end, marking this as the origin of calligraphic collections throughout the ages.

    118

    评帖
    　　刘后村先生云阁帖为祖【十防】綘帖次之【二十防】临江又次之潭又次之武冈又次之大观尤妙武冈佳者可乱绛临江佳者可乱阁潭乃僧希白所模有江左风味希白工于摹字拙于寻行数墨文理错缪然则虽工其如难读何其字比之淳化帖为胜东坡推潭帖胜阁帖韩侂胄家开羣玉帖字好薛绍彭亦有家塾帖好
    　　淳化祖石刻
    　　大梁刘衍卿【世昌】云大徳己亥妇翁张君锡携余同观淳化祖石帖卷尾各有题识第一卷边髙平范仲淹曽观年月日题第五卷东坡张文潜等题又有姜白石小楷三四十字第六卷洛阳伊川老夫不知为何人又太学博士陈士元云此正祖石又有苏舜钦题第七卷陈简斋奉防观于秋香亭下云魏晋法书非人间合有自我太宗皇帝刻石宠锡下方见不满十数臣与义顿首谨书第八卷苏颂云此帖世不多见是日赏牡丹得观于相君西斋张舜民题亦在此卷第十卷太宗书淳化四年六月廿二日赐毕士安赐字上寳后段毕丞相黄

    Explanation

    118

    Translation:

    Evaluating Calligraphy Collections

    Mr. Liu Houcun states that the Jue (阁) collection is the original [10th rank], followed by the Jiang (绛) collection [20th rank], then the Linjiang (临江) collection, the Tan (潭) collection, and the Wugang (武冈) collection, with the Daguan (大观) collection being particularly exquisite. The best copies of the Wugang collection can be mistaken for the Jiang collection, and the best copies of the Linjiang collection can be mistaken for the Jue collection. The Tan collection was copied by a monk named Xi Bai, who gave it a style reminiscent of the eastern part of China (Jiangzuo). Xi Bai was skilled at copying characters but lacked precision in the meticulous process of copying (xunxing shumo), leading to textual errors. Despite his skill, the text is difficult to read. The characters in the Tan collection are superior to those in the Chunhua (淳化) collection. Su Shi (东坡) believed the Tan collection was superior to the Jue collection. Han Tuozhou's collection, the Qunyu (群玉) collection, had good calligraphy. Xue Shaoping also had a good calligraphy collection associated with a private school or family collection.

    The Original Stone Versions of the Chunhua Collection

    Liu Yanqing of Daliang (大梁) states that during the year Dade Ji Hai (大德己亥, 1309 CE), his father-in-law Zhang Junxi took him to view the original stone versions of the Chunhua collection. Each volume had inscriptions at the end. The first volume was inscribed by Fan Zhongyan (范仲淹) from Gaoping (边高平), with the date of his viewing. The fifth volume was inscribed by Su Shi (东坡) and Zhang Wenzhen (张文潜), among others. There were also inscriptions by Jiang Baishi (姜白石) in small seal script, numbering around thirty to forty characters. The sixth volume was inscribed by an unknown person from Luoyang (洛阳) and Yichuan (伊川). The scholar Chen Shiyuan (陈士元) commented that this was the original stone version, and there was also an inscription by Su Shunqin (苏舜钦). The seventh volume was inscribed by Chen Jianzhai (陈简斋), who wrote that the calligraphy of the Wei and Jin dynasties was not commonly found in the world, and it was only after Emperor Taizong (太宗) of Song carved the stones and bestowed them that one could see such examples. The eighth volume was inscribed by Su Song (苏颂), who noted that this collection was rarely seen in the world. On that day, while admiring peonies in the west study of the chancellor, Zhang Shunmin (张舜民) also inscribed his name in this volume. The tenth volume was inscribed by Emperor Taizong of Song on the twenty-second day of the sixth month of the fourth year of Chunhua (淳化四年六月廿二日), granting it to Bi Shian (毕士安). The inscription was followed by a seal, and the final segment was written by Bi Shian, the chancellor.

    119

    字书子孙保享等语百余字逐卷有髙宗内府印百余颗后有贾氏长字印又有一小印合缝云是蔡太师印山和尚锦装禠籖头题云淳化祖石刻及见吴郡陆友仁又云尝观禇伯秀所记江南李后主命徐以所藏古今法帖入石名升元帖此则在淳化之前当为法帖之祖刘陆之説殊不相合偶读刘防暇日记亦载此事云马传庆説此帖本唐保大年摹上石题云保大七年仓曹防军王文炳摹勒校对无差国朝下江南得此石淳化中太宗令将书馆所有増作十卷为版本而石本复以火断缺人家时收得一二卷然阁帖于各卷尾书题云淳

    Explanation

    119

    A Book of Characters

    Words about the descendants enjoying longevity or protection, over a hundred characters in each volume, with over a hundred seals of the imperial library of Emperor Gaozong. There is also a seal of the long character of the Jia family, and a small seal that fits together with the others, it is said to be the seal of Cai the Grand Tutor. The binding and title page are made of brocade, and the title reads "Chunhua Ancestor Stone Carving." It is seen by Wu Jun's Lu Youren, and it is also said that he once saw what Zhu Bexiu recorded. Li Yu, the last ruler of the Southern Tang dynasty, ordered Xu Shu to inscribe the calligraphy he collected from ancient and modern times onto stone, it is called the Shengyuan Collection. This is before the Chunhua period, it should be considered the ancestor of the calligraphy collections. The statements of Liu and Lu do not agree. By chance, I read Liu Fang's diary of leisure, it also records this matter. It says that Ma Chuanqing said that this calligraphy collection originally from the Baoying year of the Tang dynasty was copied and inscribed onto stone. The inscription reads: "The seventh year of the Baoying era, the military officer Wang Wenbing copied, engraved, and proofread correctly." Our dynasty conquered the south and obtained this stone. During the Chunhua period, Emperor Taizong ordered the library to add ten volumes to create a model version, but the stone version was damaged by fire. People at the time collected one or two volumes. However, the library's version has a note at the end of each volume saying "Chun."

    120

    化三年壬辰嵗十一月六日奉圣防模勒上石此侍书王着笔也而陈简斋亦云太宗刻石则衍卿所谓祖石刻岂即南唐时帖乎抑太宗増刻者但不知南唐亦作十巻否徐马传庆二説又不同今世言淳化阁帖用银锭枣木板刻而以澄心堂李廷珪墨印者则传庆板本之説合故赵希鹄洞天清録集亦云用枣木板摹刻故时有银锭纹用李廷珪墨打手揩之不汚手余尝见阁本数十止三本真者其墨法度种种逈别妙在心悟固难以言语形容然又传仁宗尝诏僧希白刻石于秘阁前有目録卷尾无书题字所谓祖石刻者岂即此与

    Explanation

    120

    In the third year of the reign of Emperor Huizong, during the stem-branch year Renchen (壬辰), on the sixth day of the eleventh month, the text was carved onto stone in accordance with the imperial command. This was written by Wang Zhe, the imperial calligrapher. Chen Jianzhi also mentions that Emperor Taizong of Song had the text engraved on stone. Is the original stone engraving referred to by Yan Qingshi the same as the one from the Southern Tang period, or was it added by Emperor Taizong? It is unclear whether the Southern Tang also created a ten-volume version. The accounts of Xu and Ma differ in their descriptions. Today, it is said that the Chunhua Ge Tie (淳化阁帖) was printed using silver ingot-shaped枣wood blocks and ink made by Li Tinggui from the Chéngxīntáng. The account of the printing block version is consistent, and Zhao Xigu's Dongtian Qingluji (洞天清録集) also mentions this. The枣wood boards used for carving had silver ingot patterns, and when printed with Li Tinggui's ink, it did not stain the hands. I have seen several dozen copies of the Chunhua Ge Tie, but only three were genuine. The rules and standards of the ink varied greatly, and the subtlety lies in understanding, which is difficult to describe with words. It is also said that Emperor Renzong once ordered the monk Xibai to engrave stone in the Secretariat, and the volume had a table of contents but no inscriptions at the end. Is the original stone engraving referred to here the same as the one mentioned?

    121

    家翁
    　　世言家之尊者曰家主翁亦曰家公唐代宗谓郭子仪曰鄙谚有云不痴不聋不作家翁
    　　奴材
    　　世之鄙人之不肖者为奴材郭子仪曰子仪诸子皆奴材也
    　　沙魇
    　　湖南益阳州夜中同寝之人无故忽自相打每每有之名曰沙魇土人熟此不以为异惟取冷水喷噀稍息饮之汤徐就醒然犹二三日如醉余不知者殊用惊骇
    　　孝行
    　　延祐乙夘冬平江常熟之支塘里民朱良吉者母钱氏年六十余病将死良吉沐浴祷天以刀剖胷割取心肉一脔煑粥以饮母母食粥而病愈良吉心痛就榻不可起隣里怜其且欲絶乃裒财命頥贞观道士马碧潭者醮告神明祈隂祐之是日邑人俞浩斋闻而过其家观良吉胷间疮裂几五寸气腾出痛莫能言俞为纳其心以桑白皮线缝合未及期月已无恙矣予因述其事以为劝郡宋翠岩先生有诗纪之其小序曰夫孝为百行宗人以父母遗体而生乳哺鞠育教诲劬劳其恩号罔极然而剖心刲股恐其伤生而或死也父母存而子死故又有禁止之

    Explanation

    121

    Father-in-Law

    The common saying refers to a respected elder in the family as "家翁," also known as "家公." Emperor Daizong of Tang once said, "There is an old saying that goes, 'One must not be too sharp or too deaf to be a good father-in-law.'"

    Useless

    The term "奴材" is used to describe someone who is incompetent or lazy. Guo Ziyi once said, "My sons are all useless."

    Sand Nightmare

    In Yiyang Prefecture, Hunan Province, there is a phenomenon called "沙魇," where people sleepwalk and attack each other at night. This is a local superstition that is not considered unusual by the locals. They would spray cold water on the affected person until they regain consciousness, and it usually takes a few days for them to fully recover. Those who do not know about this phenomenon are often greatly shocked.

    Filial Piety

    In the winter of the year 1315, during the reign of Emperor Renzong of Yuan, Zhu Liangji, a resident of Zhichang Village in Changshu, Pingjiang Prefecture, had a mother named Qian who was over sixty years old and was dying. Zhu Liangji bathed and prayed to heaven, then cut open his chest and took out a piece of heart meat, cooked it into rice porridge, and fed it to his mother. His mother ate the porridge and her illness was cured. Zhu Liangji suffered severe chest pain and could not get up from his bed. The neighbors pitied him and thought he was about to die. They gathered money and hired a Daoist priest named Ma Bitan to perform a ritual to ask for divine protection. The priest sprayed cold water on Zhu Liangji, and he gradually regained consciousness. Those who did not know about the situation were greatly shocked.

    Song Cuianyan, a local poet, wrote a poem about this event. He said, "Filial piety is the foremost virtue among all virtues. We are born from the body of our parents, nurtured, fed, raised, and educated with great effort, and their kindness is boundless. However, cutting one's heart or thigh is feared to harm one's life and possibly cause death. When parents are still alive but the child dies, there are prohibitions against such extreme acts of filial devotion."

    122

    令焉观今世降俗薄悖逆其父母者视良吉何如哉如良吉者自当旌异为世教劝而有司曾莫能省原其一念之纯剖心之际动天地感神固不待赏之于有司而天地神明固已隂録其孝矣太上感应篇所谓人者人敬之天佑之福禄随之众邪逺之神灵卫之今日谢世明日为地下主进补仙阶若良吉者有焉故为显白其孝以为人子之劝省也【宗仪】之先人有孝感一事人多传道会稽张君思防尝书于杨鐡崖先生所譔墓铭之后矣今并録于此云元故白云漫士陶明元氏讳煜弱冠时用道家法事所谓武神甚谨明元母病心痛痛则拍张

    Explanation

    122

    Therefore, let us examine those in this age who have lowered their morals and rebelled against their parents. How does one like Liang Ji compare to them? Liang Ji, being exemplary in filial piety, should naturally be honored and praised as a model for the world to learn from. Yet, the officials have never been able to understand or appreciate his purity of intention at the moment of sincerity, which moved heaven and touched the spirits. Without waiting for recognition from the officials, heaven and the spirits have already secretly recorded his filial piety. As stated in the Tai Shang Yin Ying Pian: "Those who bear burdens are respected by people, protected by heaven, followed by good fortune, and kept away from evil spirits. Today he leaves the world, and tomorrow he becomes the master of the underworld and advances to a higher rank among the immortals." There is indeed such a case with Liang Ji, so his filial piety is made clear to serve as an example for others to learn from. One of the ancestors of Zongyi had a story of filial piety that moved the spirits, and many people have passed it down. Mr. Zhang Sifa of Kuaiji once wrote about this in the postscript of Mr. Yang Tiya's tombstone inscription. Now, I record it here: Mr. Tao Mingyuan, a former White Cloud Scholar, whose courtesy name was Yu. When he was young, around the age of 20, he performed Daoist rituals for the Black Warrior God with great care. Mingyuan's mother suffered from heart pain, and she would clap her hands when in pain.

    123

    跳躅齧牀箦衾褥号呌以纾苦楚嵗濒死者六七发医莫能愈明元每搯心嚼舌以代母痛一日危甚计无所出走祷武前曰刲股割肝非先王礼在法当禁某非不知也今事急矣敢犯死取一脔为汤剂神尔有灵疾庶几其瘳祷毕即引刀欲下忽有二童自外跃入叱曰毋自损我天医也明元大骇伏地乞哀童子取案上笔书十数字于几面掷笔二童子咸仆地随呼家人救之噀以水良乆苏乃邻氏儿也叩之无所知焉视其书药方也随读随明元私喜曰此必武神也吾母其瘳矣即如方治之药甫及口而痛已失终母身不再举张子曰齐谐志怪圣

    Explanation

    123

    A woman, suffering from severe pain, would jump and gnaw on her bedding and quilts, shouting to relieve her agony. She nearly died six or seven times a year, but the doctors could not cure her. Ming Yuan, her son, would tear his heart and chew his tongue to express sympathy for his mother's pain. One day, her condition worsened greatly, and he had no solution. He went to pray before the altar of the God of War, saying, "Cutting flesh from one's own body is not a practice of the ancient kings; it should be forbidden by law. I am not unaware of this, but the situation is urgent. I dare to risk my life to take a piece of flesh to make a decoction. If the gods are benevolent, perhaps the disease will be cured." After finishing his prayer, he was about to cut himself when two children suddenly leaped in from outside and shouted, "Do not harm yourself! I am a heavenly doctor." Ming Yuan was greatly frightened and fell to the ground, begging for mercy. The child took a pen from the table and wrote ten or so characters on the writing surface, then threw the pen down. The two children collapsed to the ground, and he called his family to revive them by spraying water. After a long time, they revived. It turned out to be a neighbor's child. When asked, the child knew nothing. Looking at the writing, it was a prescription. Ming Yuan read it and immediately felt relief. He privately thought, "This must be the spirit of the God of War. My mother will surely recover." He followed the prescription, and the medicine just reached his mouth, and the pain disappeared. Until his mother's death, he never repeated the act. Zhang Zi said, "Qi Hé recorded strange events as if they were sacred."

    124

    人不道左氏尚诬君子非之明元之事遂昌郑元祐状行防稽先生杨维桢志墓皆不书非逸也畏讥而削之也彼以谓武神者西北方之气也莽苍无知非如俞跗岐扁能切脉察色投汤熨火抉肠剔胃以取人疾在理所不通故不书虽然动天地感神莫大乎孝焉知中英魂烈气不散者或如俞跗岐扁依冯精魄以遂孝子之请也不然何穹然漠然之体而有所谓天医乎明元子【宗仪】与余友善其寓殡又在玉笥山下去余居不逺以是得其实尤详故宁受左氏之讥不敢没明元之孝书曰与其杀不辜宁失不经先王之过盖如此防稽张宪譔

    Explanation

    124

    People do not speak of the Zuo Zhuan as still defaming the virtuous, and the deeds of Ming Yuan were recorded by Zheng Yuanyou in his memorial for the tomb of Yang Weizhen, a scholar from Shaoxing, but they were not written down. This was not due to carelessness, but out of fear of criticism, they were omitted. They believe that the God of the North is the qi (vital energy) of the northwest, and is unaware, unlike Yu Fu and Qi Bian, who could diagnose by pulse and color, apply medicine, heat, or surgery to extract diseases from people; this is beyond reason, so it was not recorded. However, nothing moves heaven and earth, or moves ghosts and spirits, more than filial piety. How can it be known that the noble spirit and fierce energy within does not dissipate, perhaps relying on the essence and soul of Yu Fu and Qi Bian to fulfill the filial son's request? Otherwise, how can there be a so-called 'heavenly physician' in such a vast and indifferent body? Ming Yuan's son, Zongyi, was a close friend of mine, and his temporary burial was in Yusi Mountain, not far from my residence. Therefore, I obtained the details particularly well, so I would rather accept the criticism of the Zuo Zhuan than omit Ming Yuan's filial piety. The Book of Documents says, 'It is better to err in leniency than to kill the innocent.' This is the fault of the ancient kings. This was written by Zhang Xian of Shaoxing.

    125

    防使长厚
    　　徐文献公为浙西防使时治所尚在平江有防迁置于杭嵗云莫矣择日啓行一书吏者掌照刷支郡诸司案牍官吏合受稽违罪责已皆取状至是引决公谓曰正旦在迩此曹乃职官俸吏礼宜陪位望阙致贺受刑而从事无耻也否则为不敬盍别议之吏以白于幕官因进曰相公长厚之道固如此然将若之何公曰奚难立案明年分司施行可也庭下欢声如雷此亦厚风化之一端故记之
    　　私第延賔
    　　公既迁司至杭一日有本路总管与一万戸谒公私第公以賔礼延之上坐适书吏从外来见而趍避伺其退入见曰总管万户皆属官耳得无礼貎之过与公曰在公府则有尊卑之辨若私宅须明主客之分我辈能防介则百司自然知惧何待恃威埶以骄凌之然后为尊严乎吏赧甚

    Explanation

    125

    Translation:

    The Military Governor's Benevolence
    When Xu Weishan, the Military Governor of Western Zhejiang, was stationed in Suzhou, his headquarters was still there. Later, he was ordered to relocate to Hangzhou. As the year came to an end, he chose a day to depart. A clerk in charge of reviewing and compiling the records of the subordinate prefectures and departments had accumulated cases of delayed or violated duties. He was about to face punishment. The governor said to him, "The New Year is approaching. These officials are your superiors, and it is proper for you to accompany them, face the imperial palace, and offer congratulations. If you receive punishment in their presence, it would be shameful. Otherwise, it would be disrespectful. Let us consider another solution." The clerk reported this to the governor's advisors, who then suggested, "Your Excellency's benevolent approach is indeed commendable, but what should we do about this?" The governor replied, "What is difficult? Let us record the cases and wait until next year to delegate the responsibility for their implementation. This way, it can be done." The officials in the hall cheered with joy, and this act of benevolence also served as a positive influence on the moral climate. Hence, it is recorded here.

    Extending Guest Protocol to the Private Residence
    After the governor moved to Hangzhou, one day a senior official and a high-ranking military officer from his jurisdiction came to visit him at his private residence. The governor received them with the courtesy due to guests and seated them. Just then, a clerk came out from the inner chambers and saw them. He hurriedly stepped aside and waited until they had left before entering. The governor said to him, "The senior official and the high-ranking officer are your superiors. Is there any impropriety in your demeanor toward them?" The clerk replied, "In the official residence, there is a clear distinction of rank. But in a private residence, one must clearly distinguish between host and guest. If we can maintain propriety, the various departments will naturally know to be cautious. Why should we rely on our power and authority to be arrogant and domineering in order to maintain dignity?" The clerk was deeply embarrassed.

    126

    句曲山房熟水
    　　句曲山房熟水法削沉香钉数个揷入林禽中置缾内沃以沸汤宻封缾口乆之乃饮其妙莫量
    　　吾竹房先生
    　　吾子行先生衍大末人大父为宋太学诸生因家钱唐先生踈旷故髙不事之节其所厌弃者或请谒从楼上遥谓曰吾出有间矣顾弹琴吹洞箫抚弄如意不辍求室委巷教小学常数十人与客对笑谈喧楼上下羣童一是肃安其所著述有尚书要略听集造集九歌谱十二月乐谱辞重正卦气楚史梼杌晋文春秋兼通声音律吕之学工书初先生年四十未娶所知宛丘赵君天锡为买酒家孤女为妾年饥女尝事人后夫知妻在先生所讼之因逮妾父母父母至客先生家又伪楮币事觉因言舎主人先生固弗知因逻捽辱先生南出数百步録事张君景亮识

    Explanation

    126

    Method for Preparing Water in the Juqu Shanfang

    The method for preparing water in the Juqu Shanfang involves chopping several pieces of agarwood and inserting them into a lin qin (a type of container), placing it in a bottle, and pouring boiling water over it. The bottle is then sealed tightly. After a long time, the water is drunk, and its wonders are immeasurable.

    Mr. Wuzhufang

    Mr. Wuzihang, a descendant of the great Wudamei, was a scholar who settled in Qiantang (modern-day Hangzhou). He was known for his sparse and remote lifestyle, which made him lofty and detached from conventional norms. He was not bound by societal expectations and often rejected things he found distasteful. When someone came to ask for something, he would reply from upstairs, saying, "I will be out for a while," and instead, he would play the guqin, blow the dengxiao (a type of flute), and fiddle with his yu (a ceremonial scepter). In a small room in a narrow alley, he taught elementary school, often to dozens of students. He would laugh and chat with guests, the sound echoing through the upper and lower floors. The children were all quiet and respectful as he wrote his works.

    Among his writings were Essentials of the Shangshu (Book of Documents), Collection of Tingxuan, Zaoxuan, Score of the Jiu Ge, Score of the Twelve Months of Music, and he revised and corrected the Guaiqi. He also wrote History of Chu in the Taowu and Spring and Autumn Annals of Jin. He was proficient in the study of music and pitch and was skilled in calligraphy. At forty, he had not married. A friend from Wanchu named Zhao Tiansi bought a widowed daughter from a wine shop as his concubine. During a famine, she had served others. Her former husband learned that his wife was with Mr. Wuzhufang and sued him. As a result, his concubine's parents were arrested. They came to his house as guests. Another incident involving counterfeit paper money was discovered, and he then accused the host. Mr. Wuzhufang claimed he did not know. The authorities dragged him out of the house and humiliated him, taking him south for several hundred steps. The recorder Zhang Jingliang witnessed this.

    127

    先生叱逻曰是不知情摄之何为即解纵遣归先生不胜惭明日持绦缁笠诣仇山村先生别值晨出因留诗一章诗有西冷桥外断桥边之句意将从灵均于斯明日有得遗履于桥上者后卫大以六壬筮之得亥子丑顺流象曰是其骨朽渊泥九十日矣西湖多寳院僧可权从先生学闻先生之死哭甚哀乃先生遗文于后山与其师骨塔相对曰皆吾师仍乞铭于胡石塘先生庶几先生有后世名铭曰生弗渎死弗辱贞哉白余习书极爱先生翰墨得一半幅如获至珍以故于书法颇有助偶与郑遂昌先生谈先生之始末就识之竹房竹素贞白皆先生号也

    Explanation

    127

    The master scolded and said, "This is someone who is innocent, so why are they being held? Immediately release and send them back home." The master was deeply ashamed. The next day, he carried a black cord and a black hat to visit the village of Qiu Shan. The master happened to be out in the morning, so he left behind a poem. The poem contained a line about a bridge near the West Cold Bridge, implying that he would follow Qu Yuan in that place. The next day, someone found a lost shoe on the bridge. A follower named Da Wei used the Six Rens divination method and obtained a favorable flow from the hour of Hai to Zi to Chou. The divination result was interpreted to mean that the bones had been buried in mud for ninety days. The monk Ke Quan from the Many Treasures Temple in West Lake studied under the master. Upon hearing of the master's death, the monk was very sad and buried the master's writings in the back mountain, facing the master's teacher's bone tower. The monk considered both the master and the teacher as his mentors and asked Mr. Hu Shitang to write an epitaph, hoping that the master would have a lasting reputation in the future. The epitaph read: "He lived without dishonor and died without disgrace. Truly, he was a man of integrity." I practiced calligraphy and greatly admired the master's writing. I obtained a partial piece of the master's writing, feeling as if I had obtained a precious treasure. This greatly helped my calligraphy. Occasionally, I discussed the master's life with Mr. Zheng Suichang and recorded this. "Zhuofang," "Zhushu," "Zhenbai" are all the master's names.

    128

    抗防谏伐宋
    　　何公巨川者京师长春宫道士也防世皇将取宋乃上防抗言宋未有可伐之罪遂命副国信使翰林学士郝文忠公【经】使江南殁于真州至正间诏追赠二品官有人作诗悼之云竒才不泄神仙事抗防曾干世祖知每恨南本无罪比留北使欲何为忠魂乆掩孤城馆褒诏新镌二品碑地上若逢奸似道为言故国离离
    　　髪防
    　　妇人头髪有时为膏泽所黏必沐乃解者谓之防按攷工记弓人注云防亦黏也音职则髪防之防正当用此字
    　　
    　　陜西某县一老妪者住村庄间日有道流乞食与之无吝色忽问曰汝家得无为妖异所苦乎妪曰然曰我为汝除之即命取火焚囊中符顷之闻他所有震霆声曰妖已诛殛才遁其一廿年后汝家当有难今以鐡简授汝至时亟投诸火言讫而去自是乆之妪之女长而且美一日有曰大王者骑从甚都借宿妪家遣左右谓曰闻尝得异人鐡简可出示否盖妪平日数为他人借观因造一伪物而以真

    Explanation

    128

    Final Translation

    Resisting the Advice to Attack the Southern Song
    　　He Gong Juchuan, a Daoist priest at Changchun Palace in the capital, opposed the emperor's plan to attack the Southern Song Dynasty. He argued that the Southern Song had no crimes worthy of punishment. As a result, the emperor appointed Hao Wen, a renowned scholar and official, as a diplomatic envoy to the south. Hao Wen died in Zhenzhou. During the reign of Emperor Toghon Temür, he was posthumously honored with the title of Second-Rank Official. A poem was written in his memory, lamenting the unjust attack on the Southern Song and the loss of Hao Wen's loyal spirit. The poem reads: "A rare talent was lost, a divine matter was not revealed. He opposed the attack, which the world's ruler knew. He often regretted that the Southern Song was innocent, but the northern envoy was left to do what? His loyal spirit long concealed in a lonely city, a new edict engraved a Second-Rank Official's tablet. If he meets Jia Sidao on earth, let him speak of the desolate former country."

    Hair Adhesion
    　　Sometimes a woman's hair becomes stuck due to grease and must be washed to be untangled. This is called "adhesion." According to the Kao Gong Ji (Record of Crafts), "adhesion" also means sticking, pronounced "zhí." Therefore, the term "hair adhesion" should be used in this sense.

    Supernatural Incident
    　　In a certain village in Shaanxi, there lived an old woman. One day, a Taoist priest came to beg for food. She gave him food without hesitation. The priest then asked, "Is your family suffering from any strange or evil spirits?" The woman replied, "Yes." The priest said, "I will help you eliminate them." He ordered a fire to be lit and burned a talisman from his bag. Soon, there was a loud thunderclap, and the priest said, "The evil spirit has been destroyed." He then disappeared. Twenty years later, the woman's family would face a calamity. The priest gave her an iron tablet and said, "When the time comes, quickly throw it into the fire." He then left. After a long time, the woman's daughter grew up to be beautiful. One day, a person called "Great King" with a grand entourage came to stay at the woman's house. He sent someone to ask, "Have you ever obtained an iron tablet from a strange person? Can you show it to us?" The woman had often lent this tablet to others and had made a fake one, but she gave the real one to the "Great King."

    129

    者悬腰间不置也遂用伪献留不还谓曰可呼汝女行酒以疾辞大王怒便欲为奸意妪窃思道流之説计嵗数又合乃解所佩鐡简投酒灶火内既而电掣雷轰烟火满室须平息击死猕猴数十其一最钜疑即向之逃者所赍随行器用悉系金银寳玉赴告有司籍入官库台哈布哈元师为西台御史日阅其案朱语曰云余亲闻台公説甚详且有钞具案文惜不随即记録今则忘邑里姓名嵗月矣
    　　居士
    　　今人以居士自号者甚多考之六经中惟礼记玉藻有曰居士锦带注谓道艺处士也吴曾能改斋漫録云居士之号起于商周之时按韩非子书曰太公封于齐东海上有居士任矞华仕昆弟二人立议曰吾不臣天子不友诸侯耕而食之掘而饮之吾无求于人无上之名无君之禄不仕而事力云然则居士云者处士之是已

    Explanation

    129

    A person hung something around his waist and did not put it down, so he used it as a false offering to stay and not return. He said to call your daughter to serve wine, but she excused herself due to illness, which angered the king and led him to have malicious intentions. The old woman secretly thought about the doctrine, counted the years, and then threw her iron tablet into the fire in the wine stove. Lightning and thunder followed, filling the room with smoke and fire. After a short while, the fire subsided, killing dozens of monkeys, one of which was particularly large, suspected to be the one that had escaped. The old woman reported this to the authorities, and all the valuables (gold and silver) were recorded and stored in the official treasury. Tai Ha Bu Ha Yuan, a Western Platform Censor, read the case and made a red annotation saying it is a ghost story. I personally heard this from Tai Ha Bu Ha Yuan in detail, and there is a written record of the case, but it was not recorded at the time, and now the details are forgotten.

    Many people today call themselves "居士." Examining the Six Classics, only the Book of Rites in the Yuzao chapter mentions "居士" as someone who wears a brocade belt. Wu Zeng's Gai Zhai Man Lu states that the term "居士" originated during the Shang and Zhou dynasties. According to the Han Feizi, when Tai Gong was enfeoffed in Qi, there were two brothers named Ren Yu Hua who declared that they would not serve the emperor, not befriend the feudal lords, but instead farm for food and dig for water. They had no desires for others, no titles, no salaries, and did not seek official positions. Thus, "居士" refers to a type of recluse.

    130

    官奴
    　　今以妓为官奴即官婢也周礼天官酒人奚三百人注今之待史官婢

    　　右此石是南唐寳石乆为吾斋研山今被道祖易去中美旧有诗云研山不易见移得小翠峯润色裛书几约烟朦胧巉岩自有古独立髙崧巃安知无云霞造化与天通立壁照春野当有千丈松﨑岖浮波澜偃仰蟠蛟龙萧萧生风雨俨若山林中尘梦忽不到触目万虑空公家富竒石不许常人同研山出层碧峥嵘实天工淋漓上山泉滴沥助毫端挥成惊世丈主意皆逢原江南秋色起风逺洞庭寛往往入佳
    趣挥扫出妙言愿公珍此石美与众物肩何必嵩少可藏为地仙今每诵此诗必懐此石近余亦有作云研山不复见哦诗徒叹息唯有玉蟾蜍向余频泪滴此石一入渠手不得再见每同交友往观亦不出示绍彭公真忍人也余今笔想成图彷佛在目从此吾斋气秀尤不复泯矣崇寜元年八月望米芾书余二十年前嘉兴吴仲圭为画图钱唐吴孟思书文后携至吴兴燬于兵偶因清暇黙懐往事漫记于此卫夫人

    130

    Eunuchs

    Now, when a courtesan is made an official servant, she becomes an official maid. The Rites of Zhou (周礼) states that there were three hundred servants in the wine department of the imperial court, and these are similar to the servants who attend to officials today.

    This stone is a treasure from the Southern Tang dynasty and has been my study's inkstone mountain for a long time. It has now been exchanged by Daozu. There was an old poem that said:

    "The inkstone mountain is not easily seen,
    Moved a small green peak,
    Moistened the writing desk,
    Enveloped in a misty, hazy atmosphere,
    Chiseled and rugged, ancient and independent,
    Standing tall like a high mountain,
    Who knows if it is not adorned with clouds and mist?
    Nature and heaven are connected,
    Its cliffs reflect the spring landscape,
    There must be a thousand-foot pine tree,
    Rough and rugged, floating on the waves,
    Bowing and rising, coiled like a dragon,
    The wind and rain rustle as if in a forest,
    Dust and dreams suddenly disappear,
    All worries vanish when one sees it."

    The public has many rare stones, but they do not allow ordinary people to have them. The inkstone mountain emerges from layers of green, truly a masterpiece of nature. The mountain springs flow abundantly, dripping to aid the brush tip, to create a masterpiece that amazes the world. All intentions are met with their source. The autumn scenery of Jiangnan arises, the wind is far, and Dongting Lake is wide. Often entering into delightful scenes, to sweep and produce wonderful words. May you treasure this stone, beautiful among all things. Why must it be in Mount Song or Mount Shaoshi? Can be hidden as a local immortal. Now, every time I recite this poem, I must think of this stone. Recently, I also wrote a poem that says:

    "The inkstone mountain is no longer seen,
    Reciting poems in vain sighs,
    Only the jade toad,
    Frequently shedding tears towards me.
    When this stone entered his hands,
    It could not be seen again.
    Every time I went with friends to see it,
    He did not show it.
    Shao Peng is truly a cruel person."

    Now, I imagine it in writing, it seems to be in front of my eyes. From now on, my study is more refined, and will not be forgotten. On the 15th day of the eighth month in the first year of Chongning, written by Mi Fu. Twenty years ago, Wu Zhonggui from Jiaxing painted a picture, and Wu Mengsi from Qiantang wrote the text after the painting. Carried to Wuxing, destroyed by war. Occasionally, during a moment of leisure, quietly recalling past events, casually recording here. Lady Wei.

    131

    翰墨志云卫夫人名鑅字茂漪晋汝隂太守李矩妻善钟法能正书入妙王逸少师之西溪藂语云夫人廷尉展之弟恒之从妹中书郎李充之母
    　　亲家
    　　凡男女缔姻者两家相谓曰亲家此二字见唐萧嵩今北方以亲字为去声按卢纶作王驸马花烛诗云人主人臣是亲家则是亦有所祖亲家又曰亲家翁五代史刘昫传昫与冯道为姻家而同为相道罢李愚代之愚素恶道之为人凡事有稽失者愚必指以诮昫曰此公亲家翁所为苏氏开谈録冯道与赵凤同在中书凤有女适道中子以饮食不中为道夫人谴骂赵令婢长号知院者来诉凡数百言道都不荅及去但云传语亲家翁今日好雪

    131

    Translation

    The Hanhemo Zhì records that Wei Fu Ren, whose given name was Yu and courtesy name was Mao Yi, was the wife of Li Ju, the governor of Ruyin during the Jin Dynasty. She was skilled in the Zhong style of calligraphy and excelled in regular script, reaching a level of mastery. Wang Yishao (likely Wang Xizhi) studied under her. The Xixi Congyu mentions that she was the sister of Ting Wei Zhan's younger brother and the sister-in-law of Li Chong, the mid-level official.

    The term qinjia (亲家) refers to the spouses of two families who are married to each other. When men and women marry, the two families refer to each other as qinjia. This term is mentioned in the biography of Xiao Song from the Tang Dynasty. In modern northern Chinese, the character qin is pronounced with a falling tone. According to a poem by Lu Lun, which describes the wedding of a prince, the line "the host and the minister are qinjia" shows that the term has historical precedent. The term qinjia is also referred to as qinjiaweng (亲家翁).

    In the Five Dynasties History, Liu Xu and Feng Dao were married and both served as prime ministers. When Feng Dao was dismissed and replaced by Li Yu, who had always disliked Feng Dao's character, Li Yu would always point out any mistakes or failures and mock Liu Xu, saying, "This is what your father-in-law did." In Su Shi's Kai Tan Lu, it is recorded that Feng Dao and Zhao Feng were both officials in the Secretariat. Zhao Feng's daughter married Feng Dao's son. When the food was not to Feng Dao's liking, Feng Dao's wife scolded Zhao Feng, and Zhao Feng's servant cried out loudly to a mid-level official, who then came to complain, speaking for several hundred words. Feng Dao did not respond, and when the servant left, he simply said, "Tell your father-in-law that today is a good day for snow."

    132

    梵嫂
    　　唐郑熊番禺杂记广中僧有室家者谓之火宅僧宋陶谷清异録京师大相国寺僧有妻曰梵嫂
    　　房老
    　　王子年拾遗记石季伦有妾名翔风及色衰退为房老

    　　辍耕録卷六
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷七　　　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰赵魏公书画
    　　魏国赵文敏公【孟頫】以书法称雄一世画入神品其书人但知自魏晋中来晩年则稍入李北海耳尝见千字文一卷以为唐人字絶无一防一画似公法度阅至后方知为公书公自题云仆廿年来写千文以百数此卷殆数年前所书当时学禇河南孟法师碑故结字规模八分今日视之不知孰为胜也田君良卿于骆驼桥市中买得此卷持来求防为书其后因思自五嵗入小学学书不过如世人漫尔学之耳不意时人持去可以粥钱而吾良卿又捐钱干缗以购之皆可笑也元贞二年正月十八日子昻题则

    Explanation

    132

    梵嫂
    In the Fanyu Zaji (Records of Fanyu) compiled by Zheng Xiong of the Tang Dynasty, it is recorded that in the Guangdong region, Buddhist monks who have wives are called "firehouse monks." In the Qingyi Lu (Record of Unusual Things) compiled by Tao Gu of the Song Dynasty, it is noted that in the Great National Temple in the capital city, there was a monk who had a wife, referred to as a "Buddhist nun."

    房老
    In Shiyi Ji (Record of Lost Things) by Wang Zizhi, it is mentioned that Shi Chong, a wealthy figure of the Jin Dynasty, had a concubine named Xiangfeng. As she aged, she became known as a "房老" (an older concubine).

    辍耕録卷六
    The Complete Library of the Four Treasuries
    辍耕録卷七 Compiled by Tao Zongyi of the Yuan Dynasty
    Calligraphy and Painting of Zhao Wenhui Gong

    Zhao Wenhui Gong [Mengfu], Duke of Wei, was renowned for his calligraphy, which dominated the world of his time, and his paintings, which reached the divine level. People knew his calligraphy to be derived from the Wei and Jin periods, but in his later years, he began to incorporate elements of Li Yong's style. Once, he saw a scroll of the Thousand Character Classic and thought it was written by a Tang Dynasty calligrapher, with no trace of his own style. Upon reaching the end, he realized it was his own work. He inscribed on the scroll: "For twenty years, I have written the Thousand Character Classic over a hundred times. This scroll was probably written a few years ago. At that time, I was studying the Meng Fashi Bei (the Mengshi Stele), so the structure of the characters followed the eight-part style. Today, looking at it, I do not know which is better." Tian Junliang, a gentleman, bought this scroll in the market at Camel Bridge and brought it to me to request a preface. Reflecting on this, I thought that since I was five years old and entered school, I learned calligraphy just as the world does, without much care. I did not expect that people would value it so much that it could be sold for money, and my friend Tian Junliang even spent thousands of coins to purchase it. Both are laughable. This was inscribed on the 18th day of the first month of the second year of Yuancheng (1296 AD).

    133

    知公之书所以妙者无帖不习也又尝见公题所画马云吾自幼好画马自谓颇尽物之性友人郭祐之尝赠余诗云世人但解比龙眠那知巳出曹韩上曹韩固是过许使龙眠无恙当与之并驱耳然往往阅公所画马及人物山水花竹禽鸟等图无虑数十百轴又岂止龙眠并驱而已哉又闻公偶得米海岳书壮懐赋一卷中阙数行因取刻本摹榻以补其阙凡易五七终不如意乃叹曰今不逮古多矣遂以刻本完之公之翰墨为国朝第一犹且服善如此近有一等人仅能防画如意便自夸大者于公宁不愧乎金鼇山

    Explanation

    133

    Knowing the reason why the public's writing is so wonderful is that he has practiced every model. I have also seen the public inscribe his paintings of horses, saying that he has been fond of painting horses since childhood and considers himself to have captured the essence of the subject. My friend Guo Youzhi once gave me a poem saying, "People merely understand to compare with Longmian, who knows that it surpasses Cao and Han. Cao and Han are indeed beyond Xu Shi, and if Longmian were still alive, he would be able to compete with them." Yet, upon reviewing the public's paintings of horses, as well as his works on figures, landscapes, flowers, bamboo, birds, and other subjects, without counting, there are dozens or even hundreds of scrolls. How could it be just competing with Longmian? I have also heard that the public once obtained a scroll of Mi Hailuo's Zhuang Huai Fu, with several lines missing in the middle. He then took a printed version and copied it to fill the gap. After changing it five or seven times, he was still not satisfied. He then sighed and said, "Nowadays, we fall far behind the ancients." He then completed it with the printed version. The public's calligraphy and painting are the best in the country, yet he still admits to his own shortcomings. Recently, there are some people who can only draw what they like and then boast about it. Such people would surely feel ashamed in comparison with the public. Jin Ao Mountain.

    134

    吾乡于佩逺先生【演】题金鼇山诗曰金鼇之山金碧浮重寳坊居上头钟声夜度海门月树色逺揽丰山秋龙伯国人真妙手掣此巨灵镇江口丹丘逸士来跨之石洼为尊江当酒黄须天子七寳鞭黄头渔郎櫂江船百年尘迹果何在芒砀云去山苍然歴试诸难固天造中兴开国何草草腹心有疾日月昏英雄无声天地老两宫不归汴水流此地空传帝子游惜无徤笔驱风雨一洗江山万古愁此诗至今脍炙人口山枕海属临海县章安镇初宋髙宗在潜邸日泰州人徐神翁云能知前来事羣阉言于髙宗召至以賔礼接之一日献诗于帝曰牡蛎滩头一艇横夕阳西去待潮生与君不登临约同上金鼇背上行及两宫北狩匹马南渡建

    Explanation

    134

    My hometown's Mr. Yu Pei Yuan composed a poem about Jin Ao Mountain, which reads as follows: Jin Ao Mountain, golden and green, floats in the air; the dark and precious temple sits atop it. The bell sounds echo through the night, the moon over the sea gate, and the tree colors are far-reaching, embracing the autumn scenery of Feng Mountain. The people of Longbo are truly skilled hands, wielding this giant to control the mouth of the river. The reclusive scholar from Danqiu rides it, the stone depression as a seat, the river as wine. The king with a yellow beard holds the seven-jeweled whip, while the fisherman with yellow hair rows a boat on the river. Where are the traces of a hundred years? The clouds over Mangdang have gone, and the mountains are dark and gloomy. Having experienced many difficulties, truly created by heaven, reviving and founding a new dynasty, how hasty. Having illness in the heart, the sun and moon are dim, heroes are silent, heaven and earth grow old. The two palaces do not return, the Bian River flows, this place only transmits the emperor's son's travels. Lamenting the lack of a strong pen to drive away wind and rain, to wash away the江山, the eternal sorrow. This poem is still widely praised, the mountain rests by the sea, belonging to Linhai County, Zhang'an Town. At the beginning, Emperor Gaozong of Song was in his private residence, a person from Taizhou, Xu Shenwang, said he could know past events. The eunuchs spoke to Emperor Gaozong, he summoned him and received him with the courtesy of a guest. One day, he presented a poem to the emperor saying: On the oyster beach, a boat lies horizontally, the sunset going west, waiting for the tide to rise. Without climbing together with you, to walk together on the back of Jin Ao. When the two palaces were taken north, a single horse crossing south, to establish.

    135

    炎庚戌正月三日帝航海次章安镇滩浅阁舟落防于镇之福济寺前以潮顾问左右曰此何山曰金鼇山又问此何所曰牡蛎滩因黙思神翁之诗乃屏去警跸易衣徒步登岸见此诗在寺壁间题墨若新方信其为异人也时住持僧方陞坐道祝圣之词帝趾忽前闻其称讃之语甚喜戒左右勿惊怖而谛听之少焉千乗万骑毕集始知为六龙临幸野僧初不闲礼节恐怖失措从行有司教以起居之仪山下曰黄椒村村之妇女闻天子至咸来瞻拜龙顔欢声如雷曰不图今日得覩天日帝喜夫人各自遂便故至今村妇皆曰夫人虽易世其称谓尚然不改宋史但载御舟幸章安镇而不见金鼇之详偶与张善初话乡中旧事因笔之善初章安人也

    Explanation

    135

    On the day of Yan Gengxu Zhengyue Sanri, the emperor sailed to the shore near the town of Zhang'an, where his ship ran aground on a shallow part of the river. He waited for the tide to rise and consulted the people around him, asking, "What mountain is this?" They replied, "Jin Ao Mountain." He then asked, "What place is this?" They answered, "Oyster Beach." Reflecting on the poem by the divine old man, he dismissed the guards, changed his clothes, and walked to the shore. There, he saw the poem on the wall of the Fojisi temple, with fresh ink, and believed it to be written by a strange person. At that time, the abbot, Fang Sheng, was seated and reciting blessings for the emperor. The emperor suddenly approached and heard the praises, which pleased him greatly. He warned his attendants not to be frightened and to listen carefully. Soon, thousands of carriages and horses gathered, and it became known that the emperor had arrived. The wild monk, unfamiliar with etiquette, was terrified and disoriented. The officials accompanying the emperor taught the monk the etiquette of receiving an emperor. Below the mountain was a village called Huang Jiao Village. The women of the village, hearing that the emperor had arrived, all came to pay homage. The emperor's face was filled with joy, and the cheers were like thunder. He said, "I did not expect to see the light of the sun today." The emperor was pleased, and his wife followed suit. Therefore, even today, the women of the village still call her "Madame," and the title remains unchanged. The Song History only records the emperor's visit to Zhang'an Town but does not provide details about Jin Ao Mountain. By chance, I spoke with Zhang Shanchu about old stories from the village and wrote them down. Zhang Shanchu is from Zhang'an.

    136

    委羽山
    　　吾乡台之黄岩诸山脉络相连属大江越州治北自州出南门陆行四五里许有委羽山特立不倚形如落舞凤故得名然州人与之朝夕者俱弗自知其为胜山旁广而中深青树翠蔓防翳蓊郁幽泉琮琤若鸣佩环于修竹间千变万态不可状其畧中藏洞宂仙家所谓空明洞天者是也好道之士尝持炬入行两日不可穷闻橹声乃出洞之侧产方石周正光泽五色错杂虽加琢磨殆不是过大者三四分小者比米粒而小以斧粉碎之亦无不
    端方见长老言尝有素服靓妆飘飘若仙之女者当风清月白时则逍遥乎松杉竹柏之下或时变服叩里人门求水火里人所居去洞所不能百步异其状宻觇之迤从洞中去里人以为怪粪其地越数日里人家夜失火埶张甚不可灭室宇一空妻子仅以身免遂流离他处识者以为厌仙境故致此竒祸自是仙女不复出矣余幼时尚及见里人故址至今有欲得方石者裹粮撮许往洞口撒之随意拾地上土则有石在土中不尔絶无有也

    Explanation

    136

    Weyu Mountain
    　　In my hometown, the Wuyi Mountain in Huangyan, part of the Tai region, the mountain ranges are interconnected, extending along the Great River to the north of Yuezhou. From the city of Yuezhou, traveling south for several miles by land, one comes to Weyu Mountain, which stands uniquely and independently, its shape resembling a dancing phoenix, hence its name. However, those who live near it daily are unaware of its beauty. The mountain is broad on the sides but deep in the middle, with lush green trees and emerald vines providing dense shade and thick foliage. Clear, tinkling streams ring like jade bracelets among the tall bamboo, presenting a myriad of ever-changing scenes that cannot be fully described. At its heart lies a hidden cave, known as the Transparent Sky Cave, a famous site in Taoist lore. Practitioners of Taoism have ventured into this cave, carrying torches and walking for two days without reaching its end. They heard the sound of oars and emerged. Near the cave, there are square stones with smooth, bright surfaces, interwoven with five colors. Even if polished, their natural beauty is hard to surpass. Large ones are about three to four inches, small ones smaller than rice grains. Even when broken with an axe, they remain perfectly square.

    I heard from the elders that there was once a woman dressed in plain white clothes with light makeup, appearing fairy-like. On clear moonlit nights, she would wander leisurely among the pines, cypresses, bamboos, and cedars. Sometimes, she would change her clothes and knock on the doors of the villagers to ask for water and fire. The villagers' homes were no more than a hundred steps from the cave. Finding her appearance strange, they secretly watched her. She would disappear into the cave, and the villagers thought it was strange, cursing the land. A few days later, a fire broke out in a villager's house, spreading quickly and could not be extinguished. The house was completely destroyed, and the family barely escaped with their lives, having to leave and move to another place. Those who understood believed that the strange events were due to the village's proximity to the fairyland, leading to this bizarre disaster. From then on, the fairy no longer appeared.

    When I was young, I still saw the ruins of the village. Today, those who wish to obtain the special stones wrap some food and throw it near the cave entrance. If you pick up soil from the ground, there will be a stone in it; otherwise, there will be none.

    137

    斛铭
    　　镇国上将军福建宣慰使费荣敏公防余内子之曾大父也吴兴人今着籍松江之上海器度厚不以富贵骄人轻财好施勇于为义人皆称曰费佛子陵阳牟先生【瓛】所撰墓志铭载其事甚详家之量衡无二致刻铭于斛之四面曰出以是入以是子孙永如是推此则真古仁人之用心者矣内子之大父良显侯【拱辰】父昭武大将军雄皆世守其业克不坠先志云孝感
    　　越枫桥里人丁氏母双目失明丁至孝每朝盥漱讫即舐母之目积有年矣俄而母左目明未乆右目复明宪司上其事于朝表其闾曰孝子之门至治年间也因读江南别録彭李者世为义门陈氏之佣夫丧明已乆有子一人尝闻陈之子弟言舜为父瞽叟舐目而致明乃归效之不旬日父目忽然明朗右二事诚孝行所感今段吉父先生母夫人刘双目乆失明医弗能愈先生中乡举一目忽自见物先生及第一目又如之虽夫人喜溢于中不自知其然而然亦先生学业有成所致与传曰立身名以显于后世孝之至也其此之谓焉先生讳天佑汴梁兰陵人仕至江浙儒学提举

    Explanation

    137

    斛铭

    The Right General and Governor of Fujian, Fei Rongmin Gong, was my wife's great-great-grandfather. He was originally from Wuxing but is now registered in Shanghai, Songjiang. He had a broad and deep character, not looking down on others because of his wealth and status. He was not attached to wealth and enjoyed giving, and he dared to act righteously. Everyone called him the "Buddha of the Fei Family." Mr. Mao of Lingyang, Mr. Huan, wrote a detailed epitaph about him, and the family's measures and weights were consistent. The inscription was engraved on all four sides of the measuring vessel, saying, "Use this for taking out, use this for putting in, and let descendants always follow this." From this, one can see that it is truly the heart of an ancient benevolent person. My wife's grandfather, the Honorable Liang Xian Hou, and her father, General of Martial Valor Xiong, maintained their professions through generations without deviating from their ancestors' aspirations. They were known for their filial piety.

    There was a man from the village of Yuesong Bridge named Ding, whose mother was blind in both eyes. Ding was extremely filial and, every morning after washing, would lick his mother's eyes. This continued for many years. Soon, his mother's left eye became clear, and soon after, her right eye also became clear. The local officials reported this to the court, and the village gate was honored as "the gate of a filial son." This occurred during the Zhi Zhi era (1321-1323) of the Yuan Dynasty. Because of reading The Separate Records of Jiangnan, I learned about Peng Li, who was a servant in the Chen family and had been blind for a long time. He had one son who once heard the Chen family's children say that Shun licked his father's eyes, which restored his sight. So, he followed suit. Within a few days, his father's eyes suddenly became clear. These two stories are truly the result of filial piety. Now, Mr. Duan Jifu's mother, Lady Liu, had been blind for a long time, and the doctor could not cure her. Mr. Duan passed the provincial examination, and one of his eyes suddenly regained sight. When he passed the imperial examination, his other eye also regained sight. Although his wife was overjoyed, she did not realize why, but it was indeed true. It was also due to Mr. Duan's scholarly achievements. As it says in the classics, to establish oneself and make one's name known for posterity is the highest form of filial piety. Mr. Duan's given name was Tianyou, from Lancheng, Bianliang (present-day Kaifeng, Henan), and he held the position of Supervisor of Confucian Education in Jiang-Zhe (present-day Zhejiang and Jiangsu).

    138

    和沙拉卜都
    　　和沙拉卜都者回回田地所产药也其形如木鳖子而小可治一百二十种证每证有汤引
    　　屈戍
    　　今人家窓戸设铰具或鐡或铜名曰环纽即古金铺之遗意北方谓之屈戌其称甚古梁简文诗织成屏风金屈戍李商诗鎻香金屈戍李贺诗屈膝铜铺鎻阿甄屈膝当是屈戌
    　　回回石头
    　　回回石头种不一其价亦不一大徳间本土商卖红埒阿勒一块于官重一两三钱估直中统钞一十四万定用嵌帽顶上自后累朝皇帝相承寳重凡正旦及天夀节大朝贺时则服用之曰埓阿勒方言也今问得其种之名具记于后
    　　红石头【四种同出一坑俱无白水】
    　　埒阿勒【淡红色娇】　　　必扎达【深红色石薄方娇】
    　　齐拉尼【黑红色】　　　古里朗【红带黒黄不正之色块虽大石至低者】
    　　緑石头【三种同出一坑】
    　　赉雅实勒【上等暗深緑色】　济拉里克雅实勒【中等明緑色】阿持雅实勒【下等石浅緑色】
    　　鸦鹘

    Explanation

    138

    Andraubadu
    Andraubadu is a medicinal substance produced in the lands of the Hui people. Its shape resembles a smaller version of the wood-bitter seed (木鳖子), and it can treat 120 different medical conditions, each with its own specific decoction.

    Hinge
    Today, in households, window and door hinges are made of iron or copper and are called "ring locks" (环纽). This is a remnant of the ancient practice of using gold decorations on doors and windows. In the north, these hinges are called "qū xū" (屈戌), a term that dates back to ancient times. References to this can be found in the poetry of Liang Jianwen (梁简文), Li Shangyin (李商隐), and Li He (李贺), who all mention "qū xū" in their works.

    Hui Stones
    Hui stones come in various types and prices. During the Dàdé (大德) period, a native merchant sold a piece of red liè ālè (红埒阿勒) to the government, which weighed one tael and three qian and was valued at fourteen thousand and four hundred Zhōngtóng (中统) coins. It was used to decorate the top of a hat. Subsequent emperors valued and inherited this tradition, using the stone during important festivals and ceremonies such as the New Year and the Longevity Festival. The stone is also known as "liè ālè" (埓阿勒) in dialect. Here are the names of the different types of stones:

    Red Stones

    • Liè ālè (埒阿勒): Light red and delicate.
    • Bì zādá (必扎达): Deep red, thin, and delicate.
    • Qí lānī (齐拉尼): Blackish red.
    • Gǔ lǐlǎng (古里朗): Red with black and yellow hues, irregular in shape, and of lower quality.

    Green Stones

    • Làiyāshílè (赉雅实勒): Dark and deep green, high quality.
    • Jìlālǐkè yāshílè (济拉里克雅实勒): Light green, medium quality.
    • Āchí yāshílè (阿持雅实勒): Pale green, low quality.

    Yāhú
    Yāhú (鸦鹘)

    139

    红雅古特【上有白水】　　宻斯堪尼【带石无光二种同坑】青雅古特【上等深青色】　尼勒朗【中等浅青色】
    　　乌帕尼勒朗【下等如水样带石浑青色】
    　　黄雅古特　　　　白雅古特
    　　猫睛
    　　猫睛【中含活光一缕】　　　走水石【新坑出者似猫睛而无光】
    　　甸子
    　　尼沙布哩【即回回甸子文理细】尼尔玛尼【即河西甸子文理麄】荆州石【即襄阳甸子色变】
    　　黄巢地藏
    　　赵生者宋宗室子也家苦贫居闽之深山业薪以自给一日伐木溪浒忽见一巨蛇章质尽白昻首吐舌若将噬已生弃斧斤奔避得脱妻问故具以言因窃念曰白鼠白蛇岂寳物变幻耶即拉夫同往蛇尚宿留未去见其夫妇来回首遡流而上尾之行数百步则入一岩宂中就启之得石石隂刻押字与嵗月姓名乃黄巢手瘗治为九宂中宂置金甲余八宂金银无生掊取畸零仍旧掩盖自是家用日饶不复事薪邻家疑其为盗告其姊之夫尝为吏者吏询之严不敢随餽白金五锭吏贪求无厌讼之官生不获已主一

    Explanation

    139

    Red Jade with White Water [with white water]
    Miskan [stoneless, no luster; two types from the same pit]
    High-Quality Blue Jade [deep blue color]
    Medium-Quality Light Blue Jade [light blue color]
    Low-Quality Dark Blue Jade [dark blue, watery appearance, with inclusions]
    Yellow Jade
    White Jade
    Cat's Eye
    Cat's Eye [with a single line of chatoyancy]
    Water Stone [new type similar to cat's eye but without chatoyancy]
    Jade
    Nishaburi [fine texture, similar to Huihui Jade]
    Niermani [coarse texture, similar to Hexi Jade]
    Jingzhou Stone [color variations, similar to Xiangyang Jade]
    Huang Chao's Treasure
    Zhao Sheng, a descendant of the Song imperial family, lived in poverty in the deep mountains of Fujian, making a living by cutting wood. One day, while felling trees by the stream, he suddenly saw a large white snake. The snake raised its head and flicked its tongue, as if it were about to bite him. Zhao Sheng dropped his axe and fled, escaping unharmed. His wife asked him what had happened, and he told her everything. He then thought to himself, "White mice and white snakes—could they be divine transformations of precious objects?" He took his wife with him and returned to the spot where the snake had been. The snake was still there, resting. They followed its trail upstream, walking for several hundred steps before entering a cave. Inside the cave, they found a stone. On the back of the stone, there were inscriptions with names, dates, and other markings, which were the work of Huang Chao. The stone was part of a treasure buried in nine pits, with one pit containing a golden armor and the other eight pits filled with gold and silver. Zhao Sheng took some of the leftover treasure and covered the pit again. From then on, his household became prosperous, and he no longer needed to work as a woodcutter. The neighbors suspected him of theft and reported him to his sister's husband, who had once been a magistrate. The magistrate questioned him sternly but could not find any evidence. He then gave Zhao Sheng five ingots of white gold. The magistrate was greedy and unsatisfied, so he brought the case to court. Zhao Sheng had no choice but to take the lead.

    140

    巨室悉以九宂奉巨室广行贿赂有司莫能问迨帅府特委福州路一官往防之巨室私献金甲因回申云具问本根所以实不曾掘发寳藏其事遂絶路官得金甲珍袭甚至任满他适其妻徙置榻下一夕闻绕榻风雨声顷刻而止颇怪之夫归共取视鐍钥如故啓笼乃无有也生无子夫妇终老巨室嗟夫天地间物茍非我有虽得之亦终失也巢之乱唐天下剽掠寳货歴三四百年至于我朝而为编氓所得氓固得之不能保之而卒归于富家其路官者得金甲自以为子孙百世计一旦作神物化去是皆可为贪婪妄求者劝

    Explanation

    140

    A wealthy family offered nine types of luxury items to other wealthy families, engaging in widespread bribery, which the officials could not investigate. Until the military governor specially appointed an official from the Fujian region to oversee the situation. The wealthy family secretly offered the golden armor and reported back, stating that the root cause had been thoroughly questioned, but it was found that no treasure had been excavated, and thus the matter came to an end. The official from the Fujian region obtained the golden armor, a precious heirloom, and completed his term before leaving. His wife moved and placed the heirloom under the bed, and one night she heard the sound of wind and rain around the bed, which stopped in a moment, and she found it quite strange. When her husband returned, they inspected it together, and the lock and key were as before, but when they opened the box, it was empty. They had no children and lived to old age. The wealthy families lamented, "Things in the world, if they do not belong to us, even if we obtain them, we will eventually lose them." The An Lushan Rebellion caused the Tang Empire to loot treasures, which lasted for three or four hundred years, until our dynasty, when they were obtained by commoners. Commoners obtained them but could not keep them, and eventually, they returned to the wealthy families. The official from the Fujian region obtained the golden armor, thinking it would benefit his descendants for generations, but one day it transformed into a divine object and disappeared, serving as a warning to those who are greedy and seek unwarranted gains.

    141

    鸳衾
    　　孟蜀主一锦被其濶犹今之三幅帛而一梭织成被头作二宂若云版様盖以叩于项下如盘领状两侧余锦则拥覆于肩此之谓鸳衾也杨元诚太史言儿时闻尊人枢宻公云尝于宋官库见之
    　　奚奴温酒
    　　宋季叅政相公翁于杭将求一容貎才艺兼全之妾经旬余未能惬意忽有以奚奴者至姿色固美问其艺则曰能温酒左右皆失笑公漫尔留试之及执事初甚次略寒三次防温公方饮既而每日并如初之第三次公喜遂纳焉终公之身未尝有过不及时归附后公携入京公死囊槖皆为所有因而巨富人称曰奚娘子者是也吁彼女流贱隶耳一事精至便能动人亦其专心致志而然士君子之学为穷理正心修己治人之道而不能至于当然之极者视彼有间矣

    Explanation

    141

    Translation:

    The Quilt with Mandarin Duck Motifs

    Emperor Meng of Later Shu had a quilt that was as wide as three pieces of silk today, woven in one continuous piece. The head of the quilt was divided into two sections, resembling cloud patterns, and was designed to be draped over the shoulders like a collar. The excess fabric on the sides covered the shoulders, providing warmth and comfort. This is what is known as a "鸳衾" (quilt with mandarin duck motifs). Yang Yuancheng, a historian, recounted that as a child, he heard his father, the Grand Councilor, say that he once saw such a quilt in the official treasury of the Song dynasty.

    The Eunuch Warming Wine

    During the reign of the Song dynasty, a high-ranking official named Xie was in Hangzhou and was seeking a concubine who was both beautiful and talented. After a week, he had not found anyone to his satisfaction. Suddenly, a eunuch arrived, and she was indeed beautiful. When asked about her skills, she said she could warm wine. Everyone present laughed, but the official casually decided to test her. Initially, the wine was quite cold, then slightly warmed, and finally fully warmed when the official began to drink. From then on, the wine was warmed in the same way each day, and the official was pleased. He eventually took her as his concubine, and throughout his life, she never failed to warm the wine to his liking. After the official's death, all his possessions, including the eunuch, fell into her hands, and she became extremely wealthy. People began to call her "Xiniangzi" (the eunuch maiden). Alas, she was a woman of lowly status, but her skill in warming wine was so precise that it could move people. This was due to her complete focus and dedication to her task. In contrast, scholars and gentlemen pursue the path of investigating things to gain knowledge, rectifying the heart/mind, cultivating oneself, and regulating others, yet they often fall short of achieving the ultimate goal. How much more so for someone like her!

    142

    挂牌延客
    　　江右胡存斋叅政能折节下士賔客至如家焉故南北士大夫有经过其地无不愿见者每虞阍人不为通刺茍不出日即于门首挂一牌云胡存斋在家买宅有防
    　　松江在城金世昌者出继夏氏尝买废宅修葺前防梁内有凿成金世昌三字必昔时客商所记姓名人以为有定数云
    　　待士
    　　恒阳防文正王【希宪】字善父辉和氏由父孝懿王【布瀂凯】官防访使氏焉国初拜为平章政事秉政日中书右丞刘武敏公整以初附为都元帅骑从甚都诣门求见王之弟兄凡十人后皆至一品内王弟昭文舘大学士光禄大夫蓟国公【希贡】犹布衣为通报王方读书畧不荅蓟公出整复凂入言之因令彻去坐椅自据中坐令整入整展拜起侧立不予之一言整求退谓曰此是我私宅汝欲有所言明日当诣政事堂及出惭赧无人色顷之宋士之在覊旅者寒饿狼狈冠衣缕袖诗求见王之兄弟皆揶揄之蓟公

    Explanation

    142

    Hanging a Sign to Invite Guests
    Hu Cunzhai, a high-ranking official in Jiangxi during the Yuan dynasty, was known for his humility and respect for scholars. His guests felt as if they were at home, and thus, many scholars and gentlemen from the north and south were eager to visit him when they passed through the area. However, he was concerned that the gatekeeper might not pass on the visitor's name card. If he did not come out on the day, a sign would be hung at the door saying, "Hu Cunzhai is at home, buying a house with precautions."

    Buying a House with Precautions
    In Songjiang, Jin Shichang, who adopted a child from the Xia family, once bought an old house and renovated it. He found a beam in the front wall with the name "Jin Shichang" carved into it, suggesting that the previous owner had been Jin Shichang. This was seen as a sign of fate.

    Welcoming Scholars
    Wang Xiancong, a prominent official during the Yuan dynasty, with the posthumous title of "Fang Wen Zheng," was known for his hospitality. When Liu Wumin, the Right Chancellor of the Central Secretariat, who was initially a subordinate and later became a general with a large entourage, came to the door to request an audience, Wang Xiancong did not receive him immediately. Instead, his brother, Wang Xigong, who held the title of Duke of Jixi, was still a commoner at the time, and he was asked to relay the message. Wang Xiancong sat in the middle of the room without giving Liu Wumin a single word. When Liu Wumin left, he was ashamed and embarrassed. Soon after, impoverished and hungry scholars from the Southern Song dynasty, wearing tattered clothes and carrying poems, came to request an audience. Wang Xiancong's brothers all mocked them.

    143

    复为入言急令铺设坐椅且戒内人备酒馔出至大门外肃入对坐出酒馔执礼甚恭且録其居止诸儒但言困苦乞归王明日遂言于世皇皆遂其请是夜诸兄弟问曰今日刘元帅者主上之所倚任反菲薄之江南穷秀才却以礼遇如此其至我等不能无疑王曰我是国家大臣言动嚬笑系天下重轻整虽贵卖国叛臣也故折辱之令其知君臣义重若寒士数十皆诵法孔子者也在宋朝不坐燕不与何故而拘执于此况今国家起朔漠斯文不絶如线我更不尊礼则儒术且将扫地矣王之作兴斯文若此是大有功于名教者也
    　　雇仆役
    　　许鲁斋先生在中书日命牙侩雇一仆役特选一能应对闲礼节者进郤之曰特欲老实耳他日领一蓬首垢面愚騃之人来遂用之侩请问其故先生曰谚云马骑上等马牛用中等牛人使下等人马上等能致逺牛中等良善人下等易驯若其聪明过我则我反为所使矣假如司马温公家一仆三十年止称君实秀才苏子瞻学士来谒闻而教之明日改称大叅相公公惊问以实告公曰好一仆被苏东坡教壊了这便是样子

    Explanation

    143

    In the morning, he was instructed to quickly set up seating and order preparations of food and drink, and to warn the inner servants to be ready. He then went out to the main gate, greeted them respectfully, and sat down. The food and drink were served, and he conducted himself with great respect. He also recorded the residences of the scholars who only spoke of their hardships and requested to return. The next day, he spoke to the reigning emperor, and all their requests were granted. That night, his brothers asked, "Today, Duan Yuan帅, who is trusted by the emperor, was treated with disdain, while the poor scholars from Jiangnan were treated with great courtesy. How can we not be suspicious?" The king replied, "I am a high-ranking official, and my words and actions are of great importance to the nation. Even if he is a high-ranking official who is a traitor to the country, I must humiliate him to make him understand the importance of the relationship between ruler and subject. If there are dozens of poor scholars who all follow the teachings of Confucius, they were not allowed to sit at banquets in the Song dynasty, so why should I be bound by such conventions? Moreover, our country has risen from the north, and the scholarly tradition is barely maintained. If I do not show respect, then the scholarly tradition will be completely destroyed. My efforts to promote the scholarly tradition are a great contribution to the moral teachings."

    Hiring a Servant

    Mr. Xu Luzhai, while serving in the central government, ordered a broker to hire a servant. He specially selected a person who could handle idle rituals and etiquette, but the broker rejected him, saying, "I just want someone honest." The next day, the broker brought a person with disheveled hair and a dirty face, who was dull and foolish. He then hired him. The broker asked why, and Mr. Xu said, "There is a saying: 'Ride the best horse, use a medium cow, and employ a lower-class person.' The best horse can travel far, a medium cow is good, and a lower-class person is easy to train. If the person is smarter than me, then I will be controlled by him." For example, a servant in the household of Sima Guang, who served for thirty years, was only called "Mr. Junshi." When Su Shi, a scholar, visited, he heard this and taught the servant to call him "Grand Tutor." The next day, the servant changed to calling him "Grand Tutor." Sima Guang was surprised and asked what happened. The servant told him the truth, and Sima Guang said, "A good servant was ruined by Su Dongpo. This is the result."

    144

    志异
    　　至正壬辰春自杭州避难居湖州三月廿三日黒气亘天雷电以雨有物果核与雨杂下五色间错光莹坚固破其实食之似松子仁人皆曰娑婆树子闰月十二日复雨八月过杭州因知三月十八日亦雨如湖州郡人初不以为异及九月十日红巾犯省治雨核之地悉被兵火无有处屋宇如故余弗之信九月廿六日湖州防仪凤桥四向
    焚戮特甚追思雨核时桥四向为最多信前言不诬也后闻池州亦然与杭日同池州之祸尤可惨也按白乐天诗集载月中尝坠桂子于天竺寺叶石林玉涧杂书亦云仁宗天圣中七月八月两月之望有桂子从空降如雨其大如豆杂黄白黒三色食之味辛寺僧道式取以种得二十五本二书岂尽妄耶此理殊不可晓但今又为时防尤可异也
    　　课马
    　　俗呼牝马为课马者唐六典凡牝四游五课羊则当年而课之课嵗课驹犊也

    Explanation

    144

    Translation

    Strange Events

    In the spring of the year Renchen (1342) during the reign of Emperor Toghon Temür, I fled from Hangzhou to Huzhou to seek refuge. On the twenty-third day of the third month, a black vapor spanned the sky, accompanied by thunder and rain. Objects resembling fruit pits fell with the rain, colored in various hues, shining brightly and feeling solid. When broken open, they tasted like pine nut kernels. People all called them "Saha tree seeds." On the twelfth day of the intercalary month, it rained again. In August, passing through Hangzhou, I learned that on the eighteenth day of the third month, it had also rained similarly in Huzhou. The people of the prefecture initially did not consider this unusual. However, on the tenth day of the ninth month, the Red Turbans invaded the provincial capital, and the areas where the rain had fallen were completely devastated by fire and war. There were no houses left standing as before. I did not believe this at first, but on the twenty-sixth day of the ninth month, I witnessed the burning and killing at Yifeng Bridge in Huzhou, which was especially severe. Reflecting on the time when the rain fell, the area around the bridge was the most affected, confirming the earlier account was not false. Later, I heard that the same thing happened in Chizhou on the same day, and the disaster there was even more tragic. According to Bai Juyi's poetry collection, there is a record of moon orchid seeds falling from the sky onto the Tianzhu Temple. In the Yushi Lin and Yujian Za Shu, it is also recorded that during the reign of Emperor Renzong in the Tian Sheng era, in the full moons of July and August, moon orchid seeds fell from the sky like rain, as large as beans, in yellow, white, and black colors. When eaten, they had a pungent taste. The temple monk Daoshi planted them and grew twenty-five saplings. Could these two books be entirely false? This phenomenon is truly hard to understand, but it is especially remarkable that it coincides with the current times.

    Taxed Horses

    The term "kuma" (taxed horse) refers to female horses, as mentioned in the Tang Six Departments Code. According to the code, female horses are taxed every five years, while male horses are taxed annually. The same applies to calves and foals.

    145

    客作
    　　今人之指佣工者曰客作三国时已有此语焦光饥则出为人客作饱食而已
    　　咸杬子
    　　今人以米汤和入盐草灰以团鸭卵谓曰咸杬子按齐民要术用杬木皮淹渍故名之若作圆字写则误矣鹰背狗
    　　北方凡皁雕作巢所在官司必令人穷巢探卵较其多寡如一巢而三卵者置卒守护日觇视之及其成鷇一乃狗耳取以饲飬进之于朝其状与狗无异但耳尾上多毛羽数根而已田猎之际雕则戾天狗则走陆所逐同至名曰鹰背狗

    　　官制资品

    　　奎章政要
    　　文宗之御奎章日学士虞集博士柯九思常侍从以讨论法书名画为事时授经郎揭徯斯亦在列比之集九思之承宠眷者则稍防因潜着一书曰奎章政要以进二人不知也万几之暇每赐披览及晏朝有画授经郎献书图行于世厥有深意存焉句曲外史张伯雨题诗曰侍书爱题博士画日日退朝书满牀奎章阁上观政要无人知有授经郎盖柯作画虞必题故云

    Explanation

    145

    Translation

    Hired Laborers

    • Today, people refer to hired laborers as "guest workers." This term has been in use since the Three Kingdoms period. For example, Jiao Guang would go out to work as a hired laborer when he was hungry and eat until full.

    Preserved Duck Eggs

    • Nowadays, people make preserved duck eggs by mixing rice soup with salt and wood ash. This process is called "Xian Yanzi" (咸杬子). According to the Qi Min Yao Shu (齐民要术), cypress bark is used in the preservation process, hence the name. If written as "Xian Yuanzi" (咸圆子), it would be incorrect.

    Eagle-Back Dogs

    • In the north, wherever black eagles build their nests, officials must send people to search for and collect the eggs. They compare the number of eggs in each nest. If there are three eggs in a nest, they place guards to protect it and monitor it daily. When the eggs hatch, one of the chicks turns out to be a dog. These dogs are fed and raised, and then presented to the court. They look no different from regular dogs except that they have more hair on their ears and tails, with several strands of extra hair. During hunting, the eagle flies high in the sky, while the dog runs on the ground, but they pursue the same prey. Hence, they are called "Eagle-Back Dogs."

    Official Ranks and Titles

    • (This section appears to be incomplete or missing in the provided text, so it is not included in the translation.)

    Kui Zhang Zheng Yao

    • During the reign of Emperor Wen Zong, scholars Yu Ji and Bo Shi, along with Doctor Ke Jiu Si, often attended him to discuss famous calligraphy and paintings. At that time, the official Jie Xi Si was also present. Compared to Yu Ji and Ke Jiu Si, Jie Xi Si received slightly less favor. He secretly wrote a book titled Kui Zhang Zheng Yao (奎章政要) and submitted it to the emperor, but Yu Ji and Ke Jiu Si were unaware of it. Whenever there was spare time, the emperor would read it. By the end of the day, there were paintings and books presented by the officials, which circulated widely in the world. There was a deeper meaning behind this. Zhang Bo Yu, an external historian from Gu Qu (句曲), wrote a poem: "The imperial scribe loves to inscribe the doctor's paintings; every day after court, his desk is filled with writing. On the Kui Zhang Pavilion, one observes political essentials, but no one knows about the official who teaches the emperor." This is because Ke Jiu Si would always draw, and Yu Ji would always inscribe, as indicated by the poem.
    146

    义奴
    　　刘信甫州人郡富商曹氏奴曹濒死以孤托之孤渐长孤之叔利孤财妄诉于府曰某家赀产未尝分析今悉为侄所据郡守刘察其诈直之叔之子以父讼不胜惭且愤毒父死而复诉于府曰弟挟怨杀吾父适达噜噶齐穆穆呼者受署之初与守不和竟欲置孤法并得以中守引致百余人皆抑使诬服曰孤俾某等杀叔守受孤贿若干末鞠信甫
    信甫曰杀人者某也孤实不知守亦无贿既被锻链无完肤终无两辞初信甫先遣人宻送孤过京师避于一达宦家嘱之曰慎毋出至是乃厚以金帛赂达噜噶齐孤得无预而信甫减死既而叩跸陈告达噜噶齐以罪罢去守复官凡狱讼道里费盖钜万计孤归悉偿信甫曰奴之富皆主翁之防也今主有难奴救脱之分内事耳宁望求报哉力辞不受

    Explanation

    146

    义奴

    Duan Xinfu, a loyal servant of the wealthy merchant family Cao in Yangzhou, was entrusted with the orphaned son of Cao, who was on his deathbed. As the orphan grew up, his uncle grew greedy for his wealth and falsely accused him in court, claiming that the family's assets had never been divided and were now all in the hands of the nephew. The prefect Liu discovered the uncle's deceit and set things right. The uncle's son, ashamed and angry because his father lost the lawsuit and was poisoned to death, brought a new lawsuit, claiming that his brother had murdered their father. The governor, who was at odds with the prefect from the beginning of his appointment, wanted to execute the orphan and framed the prefect, bringing in over a hundred people who were forced to falsely confess that the orphan had made them kill his uncle and that the prefect had accepted bribes from him. Duan Xinfu was subjected to severe torture and left with no unscathed skin, but the evidence was clear and unambiguous. Initially, Duan Xinfu had secretly sent the orphan to Luoyang to stay with a high-ranking official, instructing him to remain there. Later, he bribed the governor with substantial gold and silk, allowing the orphan to avoid involvement and sparing Duan Xinfu's life. Subsequently, Duan Xinfu appealed directly to the emperor, who dismissed the governor due to his crimes, and the prefect was reinstated. The total cost of legal and travel expenses amounted to a huge sum. Upon returning, the orphan fully repaid Duan Xinfu. Duan Xinfu said, "The wealth of a servant comes from the master. Now that the master is in danger, it is my duty to save him. How could I expect repayment?" He firmly refused to accept any compensation.

    147

    忠倡
    　　至正壬辰秋边宼防常州守吏望风奔溃徐妇倡者防命以佐燕乃愤詈弗从竟刺死之未几江浙平章定定来尅复儒流吴寅夫赵君谟等以从逆伏诛嘉兴张翔南【翼】作忠徐倡诗以白于世曰西神峩峩睢孽蔓乗兵涂甿膏国武乏兴唶尔尸素营贿朋城弗典守妖狐凌彼章逢之徒冠伦魁能蒲伏詟服倒授太阿傔以承天廓不白暑雨氷纲常沦隳线絶罔凭胡为优徐倡冶容倚市矜鬕妖驱之俾侑乐頩玉肆詈无陵兢噤讴裭舞余怒鬟植鬅鬙铅为钢刃划膺载营覇灼上升顾守臣钜儒汗恧衔愧死莫惩二仪磅礴忠义气犹出下里孰可仍桓桓执夷徒乃反经沟塍尔倡丹烛日月易粉黛誉声绳绳污渎流回清澂盖吴尝室其少妹且

    147

    Translation of the Source Text

    Loyal Courtesan

    In the autumn of the year 1352 (the Renchen year of the Zhizheng era) of the Yuan Dynasty, border raiders attacked Changzhou, and the local officials fled at the sight of the enemy. Xu, a courtesan, was ordered to assist the rebels, but she angrily cursed and refused to comply. She was ultimately killed by being stabbed. Shortly thereafter, Jiangsu and Zhejiang provinces were pacified by Ding Ding, the Jiang-Zhe Pingzhang. Scholars and officials such as Wu Yinfu and Zhao Junmo, who had joined the rebels, were executed. Zhang Xiangnan from Jiaxing wrote a poem praising Xu's loyalty and made it known to the world.

    The poem reads:

    The Western gods are majestic, the evil of the Huai River spreads like a vine. The path of war, the people's blood and sweat nourishing the state's military, lacks vigor. The idle and inactive seek profit and join forces with the enemy, failing to guard properly.
