The cunning and dangerous foe, the corrupt officials and traitors, the best among the best, kneel and submit, handing over power to serve as a servant to heaven. The summer rain freezes the social norms, they fall and are destroyed, leaving no trace, lacking reliance. Why should Xu the courtesan, who adorns herself and relies on the market, be proud and aloof? Drive away the evil and make them serve as entertainers, curse with beautiful words, be fearless and silent, sing and dance with remaining anger. Her hairstyle is elaborate, using lead as a steel blade, stabbing the chest and establishing a kingdom, rising to prominence. The governor and prominent scholars feel ashamed and regretful, dying without punishment. The vastness of heaven and earth, the spirit of loyalty and righteousness still emerging from the common people, who can continue? The brave and loyal soldiers turn against the established order. You, the courtesan, shine like the sun and moon, changing from makeup to a good reputation, polluting the clear stream. The story of Wu and his younger sister is well-known.

    This translation aims to convey the original meaning and context while maintaining a natural and readable English style.

    148

    与生子名教中所不齿者一死固有余辜赵颇纯谨老成乃亦在列可哀也已随隐漫録载宋端平二年李全据髙邮城叛召官奴毛惜惜佐酒骂曰汝本徤儿官家何于汝而反吾有死耳不能为反贼行酒全以刃裂口立命脔之骂至死不絶后阃臣以闻特封英烈夫人且赐庙潘紫岩有诗曰淮海艳姬毛惜惜蛾眉有此万人英恨无匕首学秦女向使裹头真杲卿玉骨花顔城下土氷魂雪魄史闲名古今无限要金者歌舞筵中过一生噫当是时也奸凶得志埶熖熏天虽厚禄重臣峩冠世儒罔不效力执事战兢奔走于指挥之下而倡优下贱乃能奋不顾身独何人与夫徐氏之与英烈夫人同一死耳而无有为之举申朝廷褒赠封号以为世劝惜哉

    Explanation

    148

    Those who are despised within the norms of Confucian society, even death is not enough punishment. Zhao, who was pure, cautious, and experienced, was also included in this list, which is truly pitiful. According to Sui Yin Man Lu (A Collection of Anecdotes from the Hidden and the Subtle), in the second year of the Duanping era during the Southern Song Dynasty, Li Quan rebelled against the government in Gaoyou City. He summoned the official concubine Mao Xixi to serve wine, and she rebuked him, saying, "You are a loyal subject. Why do you rebel against the emperor? I would rather die than serve wine to a rebel." Li Quan ordered her to be beheaded and then cut into pieces, but she continued to curse until her death. The officials reported her bravery to the emperor, who posthumously honored her as the "Heroic and Loyal Lady" and built a temple in her honor. Pan Ziyi wrote a poem in her praise, saying:

    "From the Huai and Hai regions, the beautiful and heroic Mao Xixi, with her delicate eyebrows and strength of a thousand men, lamented that she did not have a dagger like Lady Zhaojun. If she had been a man like Yan Jing, she would have shown even greater bravery. Her noble character and beauty ended in the dust of the city walls. Her spirit, like ice and snow, is a name in history. Throughout history, there are countless people who lived lives of luxury and ease, passing their lives in the banquet halls. Alas, in those times, the villains were in power, and their influence was overwhelming. Even high-ranking officials with thick salaries and grand titles, as well as the scholars of the world, could not help but serve them, trembling and running at their commands. Only the lowly performers, like Mao Xixi, could show such courage and loyalty. Yet, there was no one to recognize her bravery and recommend her for posthumous honors and titles to encourage future generations. How sad!"

    149

    志怪
    　　至正乙未正月廿三日日入时平江在城忽望东南方军声且渐近惊走觇视他无所有但见黒云一簇中彷佛皆人马而前后火光灯烛者莫知其迤由西北方而没惟葑门至齐门居民屋脊龙腰悉揭去屋内牀榻屏风俱仆醋坊桥董家杂物铺失白米十余石酱一缸不知置之何地此等怪事竟不可晓
    　　粥爵
    　　至正乙未春中书省臣进奏遣兵部员外郎刘谦来江南募民补路府州司县官自五品至九品入粟有差非旧例之职专茶盐务塲者比虽功名逼人无有愿之者既而抵松江时知府崔思诚惟知曲承使命不问民间有粟与否也乃拘集属县巨室防科十二名众皆号泣告诉曽弗之顾辄施拷掠抑使承伏即填空名告身授之平江路达噜噶齐卒不避谴斥力争以为不可竟无一人应募者崔闻之深自悔赧还金絶交

    Explanation

    149

    Final Translation

    Strange Phenomena
    On the twenty-third day of the first month of the year 1355, at sunset, in the city of Pingjiang, people suddenly heard the sounds of approaching military forces from the southeast. They ran to look and saw nothing but a cluster of dark clouds, which seemed to contain people and horses, with fire and lights in front and behind. No one knew where it went, and it disappeared to the northwest. Only in the area between Fong Gate and Qi Gate did the roofs of the houses lose their decorations, and all the beds, tables, and screens inside the houses fell. At a store on Acetown Bridge, a merchant named Dong lost more than ten stones of white rice and a barrel of soy sauce, and no one knew where they were placed. These strange events could not be explained.

    Offering Rice for Rank
    In the spring of 1355, officials from the Central Secretariat proposed to the emperor that they send a deputy minister of the Ministry of War, Liu Qian, to the south to recruit civilians to fill positions from the fifth to the ninth rank, with varying amounts of rice to be contributed. Unlike previous practices, these positions were not exclusively for tea and salt duties. Despite the allure of official titles, no one was willing to take them. Eventually, Liu Qian arrived in Songjiang, where the governor, Cui Sicheng, only knew how to flatter the mission and did not inquire whether the people had rice to contribute. He gathered twelve wealthy families from the counties under his jurisdiction and subjected them to torture, forcing them to confess. He then filled out blank official documents and granted them to the officials of the Pingjiang Road, the Daluoqizi. Despite repeated objections and warnings, he insisted on his decision. When Cui Sicheng learned that no one had responded to the recruitment, he deeply regretted his actions, returned the gold, and severed ties.

    150

    曹公克明【鉴】号以斋宛平人为湖广行省员外郎日麻阳主簿顾渊白致书问讯且以辰砂一包见寄未及啓封漫尔置箧笥中后有宪官过访因论制药为苦无好辰砂公曰我一故人尝以此为恵当奉送及取视乃有砂金三两杂其内公惊叹曰渊白以我为何如人也时渊白已没呼其子归之其防洁如此官至礼部尚书諡文穆
    　　画师
    　　王渊字水钱唐人善山水人物尤长于花竹翎毛幼时获侍赵魏公故多得公指教所以傅色特妙天歴中画集庆龙翔寺两庑壁时都下刘总管者总其事刘命若水于门首壁上作一其壁髙三丈余难于着笔因取连粘粉本以呈刘曰好则好矣其如手足长短何若水不得其理因具酒礼再拜求教于刘刘曰子能不耻下问吾当告焉若先配定尺寸画为躶体然后加以衣冠则不差矣若水受教而退依法为之果善

    Explanation

    150

    Cao Cao, known by the pseudonym "Keming," was a native of Wuan and served as an outer court official in the Huguang provincial administration. One day, Ma Yang's chief clerk, Gu Yuanbai, sent a letter inquiring about Cao Cao's well-being and enclosed a package of cinnabar. Before opening the package, Cao Cao casually placed it in his chest. Later, a censor passed by and they discussed the difficulties of making medicine, lamenting the lack of good cinnabar. Cao Cao said, "I once had a friend who often gave me this as a gift, and I will give it to you." When he took it out to give it, he found three taels of gold mixed with the cinnabar. Cao Cao exclaimed, "How did Yuanbai think of me?" At that time, Gu Yuanbai had already passed away, and Cao Cao called his son to return the gold. Such was his integrity. He later rose to the position of Minister of Rites and was posthumously given the title "Wenmu."

    As for the painter Wang Yuan, whose courtesy name was "Nishui" and who was from Qiantang, he excelled in landscape and figure painting, especially in the depiction of flowers, bamboos, and birds. As a child, he had the opportunity to serve under Zhao Weigong and thus received much guidance from him, which made his skill in applying color particularly remarkable. During the Tianli era, he painted the walls of the two side halls of the Jiqing Longxiang Temple in Nanjing. At the time, Liu, the chief supervisor of the capital, was in charge of the project. Liu ordered Wang Yuan to paint a ghost on the wall at the entrance, which was three zhang high, making it difficult to apply the brush. Wang Yuan took a preliminary sketch and presented it to Liu, who said, "It's good if it's good, but how about the proportions of the limbs?" Wang Yuan did not understand the reasoning and prepared wine and offered a respectful bow to ask Liu for instruction. Liu said, "If you are not ashamed to ask those below you, I will tell you. If you first determine the dimensions and draw the figure naked, then add clothing, there will be no mistake." Wang Yuan followed this advice and, when he returned to work, successfully completed the painting.

    151

    辍耕録卷七
    <子部,小说家类,杂事之属,辍耕录>
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録巻八　　　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰写山水诀
    　　黄子乆散人【公望】自号大痴又号一峯本姓陆世居平江之常熟继永嘉黄氏颖悟明敏博学彊记画山水宗董巨自成一家可入逸品其所作写山水诀亦有理致迩来初学小生多效之但未有得其髣髴者正所谓画虎刻鹄之不成也
    　　近代作画多宗董源李成二家笔法树石各不相似学者当尽心焉
    　　树要四面俱有榦与枝盖取其圆润
    　　树要有身分画家谓之纽子要折搭得中树身各要有发生
    　　树要偃仰稀宻相间有叶树枝软面后皆有仰枝画石之法先从淡墨起可改可救渐用浓墨者为上石无十步真石看三面用方圆之法湏方多圆少董源坡脚下多有碎石乃画建康山埶董石谓之麻皮皴坡脚先向笔画边皴起然后用淡墨破其深凹处着色不离乎此石着色要重

    Explanation

    151

    Records of Pausing the Plow, Volume Seven
    <Subclass of Novels, Miscellaneous Matters, Records of Pausing the Plow>
    Imperially Approved Complete Library in Four Branches
    Records of Pausing the Plow, Volume Eight Compiled by Tao Zongyi of the Yuan Dynasty: Water and Mountain Precepts

    Huang Gongwang, self-named Great Fool and also One Peak, originally surnamed Lu and lived in Changshu, Suzhou, later marrying into the Huang family of Yongjia. He was intelligent, perceptive, and well-versed, with a strong memory. His landscape paintings followed the styles of Dong Yuan and Juran, developing into his own unique style, which could be classified as one of the extraordinary works. His writings on landscape painting also contained profound insights. Recently, many beginners have emulated him, but none have truly captured his essence, which is precisely what is meant by the proverb "the result of trying to draw a tiger or carve a crane but failing."

    In modern times, painting often follows the brushwork of Dong Yuan and Li Cheng, with trees and rocks each having distinct characteristics. Students should devote themselves to mastering these styles.

    Trees should have trunks and branches on all sides, achieving a round and smooth appearance.
    Trees should have a sense of form, which painters call the "knot." They should be arranged in a balanced way, with each tree having its own growth.
    Trees should alternate between leaning forward and backward, sparse and dense, with soft branches and leaves, and all should have upward-reaching branches. The method of painting rocks starts with light ink, allowing for corrections and adjustments. Gradually using thicker ink is better. There is no stone that appears as it truly is within ten steps; one must look at it from three sides. The technique of using square and round shapes should favor more square than round. Dong Yuan's paintings often feature small stones at the base of slopes, depicting the terrain of Jiankang. This style is called "hemp-fiber texture," where the base of the slope is first sketched with ink, and then light ink is used to highlight the deep concave areas. Coloring should be strong and not deviate from this principle. The color of the stone should be strong.

    152

    董源小山石谓之矾头山中有云气此皆金陵山景皴法要渗软下有沙地用淡墨扫屈曲为之再用淡墨破
    　　山论三逺从下相连不断谓之平逺从近隔开相对谓之濶逺从山外逺景谓之髙逺
    　　山水中用笔法谓之筋骨相连有笔有墨之分用描处糊突其笔谓之有墨水笔不动描法谓之有笔此画家要处山石树木皆用此
    　　大槩树要填空【去声】小树大树一偃一仰向背浓淡各不少相犯繁处间踈处须要得中画得纯熟自然笔法出现
    　　画石之妙用滕黄水浸入墨笔自然润色不可用多多则要滞笔间用螺青入墨亦妙吴妆容易入眼使墨士气
    　　皮袋中置描笔在内或于好景处见树有怪异便当模写记之分外有发生之意登楼望空濶处气韵看云采即是山头景物李成郭熈皆用此法郭熈画石如云古人云天开图画者是也

    Explanation

    152

    Dong Yuan's depiction of small rocks is known as "fengtou," and his landscapes often feature mist or clouds. These elements are characteristic of the mountain scenes in Jinling. The technique of "cun" (texture) should be soft and blended, and the base should be sandy, using light ink washes to create a soft, diffuse effect. Curved and winding shapes should be created, and additional light ink washes should be used to break up or soften the previous layer.

    The theory of three distances in landscape painting is discussed: "pingyuan" (horizontal distance) refers to a landscape seen from a low angle, emphasizing the horizontal expanse; "kuayuan" (width distance) refers to a landscape seen from a near distance, emphasizing the width; "gaoyuan" (height distance) refers to a landscape seen from a high vantage point, emphasizing the vertical height.

    In painting landscapes, the technique of connecting lines and strokes to create a sense of structure and movement is called "jinjiu" (bones and sinews). There is a distinction between the use of brushwork (笔) and ink (墨). In areas where detailed lines are used, the brushstrokes should be blended to create a smooth, continuous effect, which is called "youmo" (having ink). In areas where the brush is used in a static manner, it is called "youbi" (having brush), and this is an essential technique for painters, applicable to rocks, trees, and other elements.

    Generally, trees should fill empty spaces. Small and large trees should be depicted with some branches leaning forward and others leaning backward, varying the density and color of the branches. Dense areas should be balanced with sparse areas to achieve harmony. Mastery comes with practice, and the brushwork becomes more natural.

    The art of painting stones is particularly important. Using a yellowish-brown pigment (滕黄) mixed with ink creates a rich, textured effect. However, too much of this pigment can make the brush feel heavy. Using a blue-green pigment (螺青) mixed with ink is also effective. The "Wu zhuang" style is easy to understand and visually appealing, making ink more elegant.

    Painters often carry a pen in their satchel. When they encounter interesting scenes, especially unusual trees, they should sketch them to capture the essence. Looking out from a high place to see a wide, open view and observing the clouds can help capture the essence and atmosphere of the scene. This technique was used by famous painters like Li Cheng and Guo Xi. Guo Xi's depiction of stones was likened to clouds, and it is said that nature itself is a work of art, as the ancients said, "Heaven opens the picture."

    153

    山水中唯水口最难画
    　　逺水无湾逺人无目
    　　水出髙源自上而下切不可断要取活流之源山头要折搭转换山脉皆顺此活法也众峯如相揖逊万树相从如大军领卒森然有不可犯之色此写真山之形也
    　　山坡中可以置屋舎水中可置小艇从此有生气山腰用云气见得山埶髙不可测
    　　画石之法最要形象不要石有三面或在上在左侧皆可为面临笔之际殆要取用
    　　山下有水潭谓之瀬画此甚有生意四边用树簇之画一窠一石当逸墨撇脱有士人家风才多便入画工之流矣
    　　或画山水一幅先立题目然后着笔若无题目便不成画更要记春夏秋冬景色春则万物发生夏则树木繁冗秋则万象肃杀冬则烟云黯淡天色糢糊能画此者为上矣
    　　李成画坡脚须要数层取其湿厚米元章论李光丞有后代儿孙昌盛果出为官者最多画亦有风水存焉
    　　松树不见根喻君子在野杂树喻小人峥嵘之意夏山欲雨要带水笔山上有石小块堆在上谓之矾头用水笔晕开加淡螺青又是一般秀润画不过意思而已

    Explanation

    153

    In landscape painting, the most difficult part to depict is the water mouth, where a stream meets a larger body of water. Distant water appears straight and distant people appear without distinct features. When depicting water flowing from a high source, it should not be interrupted but should capture the essence of a living stream. The peaks of mountains should be depicted with dynamic changes, and the entire mountain range should follow this principle of vitality. Peaks should appear as if they are bowing to each other, and trees as if they are following a leader, creating a majestic and inviolable image of a true mountain. Houses can be added on hillsides, and small boats on water, which brings life to the scene. Clouds in the middle of the mountain show its height and grandeur. The technique of painting stones is most important in capturing their shape; stones can be depicted from various angles, whether on top or on the left side. A waterfall at the foot of a mountain adds vitality to the scene, and surrounding it with trees enhances the beauty. Painting a few rocks and trees with a free and spontaneous style reflects the taste of a scholar and not just a skilled artisan. When painting a landscape, one should first establish a theme before starting to paint, as without a theme, the painting lacks coherence. More importantly, remember the scenes of the four seasons: in spring, all things come to life; in summer, trees are lush; in autumn, all things are solemn; in winter, the sky is dim and the clouds are hazy. Those who can capture these seasonal changes are the best. Li Cheng, a famous painter, depicted slopes in layers to achieve a sense of moisture and thickness. Mi Yuanzhang, a famous calligrapher and painter, noted that the descendants of Li Guangcheng were numerous and successful, and their paintings reflected this success. A pine tree without visible roots symbolizes a gentleman in the wild, while mixed trees represent the ruggedness of common people. In a summer mountain before rain, water brushes should be used to create a misty effect, and light blue-green should be added to enhance the beauty of the scene. This is all about capturing the essence of the landscape.

    154

    冬景借地为雪要薄粉晕山头
    　　山水之法在乎随机应变先记皴法不杂布置逺近相映大槩与写字一般以熟为妙上难画绢上矾了好着笔好用顔色易入眼先命题目此为之上品古人作画胷次寛濶布景自然合古人意趣画法尽矣
    　　好绢用水喷湿石上槌眼匾然后上帧子矾法春秋胶矾停夏月胶多矾少冬天矾多胶少
    　　着色螺青拂石上藤黄入墨画树甚色润好看作画只是个理字最要吴融诗云良工善得丹青理
    　　作画用墨最难但先用淡墨积至可观处然后用焦墨浓墨分出畦径逺近故在生上有许多滋润处李成惜墨如金是也
    　　作画大要去邪甜俗赖四个字
    　　邓山房
    　　平江会道观主邓山房道枢绵州人在宋季为道士时斋法已精际遇理度两朝一日谢后遣巨珰召至内后门泣降徳音且令其责军令状使无他泄后谓曰吾昨夜梦见济王怒甚以为吾且将兵由独松闗入灭汝社稷矣吾此梦颇可怪汝可就南髙峯顶为誊心章哀告上帝已而黄头先锋斩闗而来宋亡后邓遂筑今观

    Explanation

    154

    Winter Landscape Technique

    • Winter Landscape Technique: The ground is depicted as snow-covered, and the mountain peaks are lightly colored with a pale hue, creating a soft, ethereal effect.
    • Landscape Painting Method: The method of painting landscapes lies in adapting to the situation and making changes as needed. First, master the basic techniques (皴法) without mixing them with the composition. Generally, the approach is similar to calligraphy, and mastery comes through practice.
    • Preparing Silk for Painting: Use good silk, wet it with water, hammer it flat, and apply sizing. Then, apply the sizing method.
    • Applying Colors: Apply blue pigment (螺青) to the rocks and yellow pigment (藤黄) mixed with ink to the trees, creating a smooth and attractive effect.
    • Using Ink: Using ink is the most challenging part of painting. Start by using light ink to build up layers until a pleasing effect is achieved, then use darker ink to define details and depth.
    • Criteria for Good Painting: Good painting should avoid being evil, sweet, vulgar, or lazy.
    • Deng Shanfang: A place or person named Deng Shanfang.
    • Pingjiang Huidao Temple: Located in Mianzhou, a Taoist temple where Deng Shanfang was a priest during the late Song dynasty. He was highly skilled in fasting and meditation, and his methods were well-regarded. One day, after a ritual, he was summoned to the inner palace by a eunuch. He was told to take responsibility for military orders to prevent leaks. Later, he was instructed to write a sincere prayer on the South High Peak to appeal to God. Soon after, the Yellow Head Vanguard led an attack, and the Song dynasty fell. After this, Deng Shanfang built the current temple.
    155

    狗站
    　　髙丽以北名别十八华言连五城也罪人之流尼噜罕者必经此其地极寒海亦氷自八月即合至明年四五月方解人行其上如履平地征东行省每嵗委官至尼噜罕给散囚粮须用站车每车以四狗挽之狗悉谙人性站有狗分例若尅减之必啮其主者至死乃已
    　　五马入门
    　　吾乡陈刚中先生【孚】临海县人国初时尝为僧以避世变一日大书所作诗于其父执某之粉墙上云我不学防丞相地黄变髪髪如漆又不学张长史醉后挥毫扫狂墨平生绀髪三十丈几度和云眠石上不合感时怒冲冠天公罚作圆顶相肺肝本无儿女情亦岂惜此双髩青只忆山间秋月冷搔首不见髼松影父执见之曰此子欲归俗也呼来馆谷之命养髪经半年余谓曰汝当娶吾将以女事汝先生辞谢

    155

    Dog Station

    North of Korea, there is a region known as Niulihan, which is part of a network of eighteen stations called "Bie Ba" (Eighteen). Criminals exiled to this area must pass through it. The land is extremely cold, and even the sea freezes over from August until April or May of the following year. People can walk on the frozen sea as if it were solid ground. The East Expedition Province sends officials to Niulihan every year to distribute food supplies to the exiles. For this purpose, station wagons are used, each pulled by four dogs. The dogs are all familiar with human nature, and there are standard provisions for them. If their rations are reduced, they will bite their masters to death, and they will not stop until they have done so.

    Five Horses Entering the Gate

    My fellow townsman, Mr. Chen Gangzhong (also known as Fu), was from Linhai County. In the early days of the country, he once became a monk to avoid the changes of the world. One day, he wrote his poems in large letters on the whitewashed wall of his respected elder, Mr. Mou. The poems read:

    "I do not learn from Fang Chengxiang, who dyed his hair black to make it as black as lacquer.
    I also do not learn from Zhang Xu, who, after drinking, would wield his brush and sweep the wild ink.
    My hair is naturally blue-black, thirty zhang long.
    How many times have I slept on the stone under the moon?
    Unable to resist the anger of the times, my hair stands on end.
    Heaven punishes me by making me wear a round hat.
    My heart and liver have no feelings for children, nor do I cherish these two locks of black hair.
    I only remember the cold autumn moon in the mountains.
    I scratch my head and see no shadow of disheveled hair."

    Mr. Mou saw this and said, "This man wants to return to the secular world." He called Chen Gangzhong to the guesthouse and fed him. He ordered him to grow his hair for more than half a year and then said, "You should marry, and I will give my daughter to you." Mr. Chen declined the offer.

    156

    再三既而命寓他所遣媒妁行言择日迎归父执喜曰五马入门矣先生虽获佳偶自妻母以至妻之兄姊弟妹皆不然遂挈家入京馆阁诸老交章荐举入翰林会朝廷遣使交趾授先生礼部郎中副之至交州尝有诗曰老母越南垂白髪病妻塞北倚黄昏蛮烟瘴雨交州客三处相思一梦魂及抵安南国以文字言语谕之其国遂降将其世子并国相入朝后以功授治中典乡郡终老焉若父执者可谓识人也已隐逸
    　　吾乡吕徽之先生【阙】　家仙居万山中博学能诗文问无不知者而安贫乐道常逃其名耕渔以自给一日携楮币诣富家易谷种值大雪立门下人弗之顾徐至庭前闻东阁中有人分韵作雪诗一人得滕字苦吟弗就先生不觉失笑阁中诸贵防子弟辈闻得遣左右诘之先生初不言众愈疑亲自出见先生露顶短褐布袜草屦辄侮之询其见笑之由先生不得已乃曰我意举滕王蛱蝶事耳众始叹伏邀先生入坐先生曰我如此形状安可厠诸君子问请之益坚遂入阁众以藤滕二字请先生足之即援笔书曰天上九龙施法水人间二鼠啮枯藤鹜

    Explanation

    156

    After much hesitation, he was eventually ordered to move to another place. Matchmakers were sent to arrange the marriage, and a date was chosen for the wedding. His father-in-law was delighted, saying, "Five horses entering the gate." Although Mr. Lü Hui Zhi had found a good match, from his mother-in-law to his wife's brothers, sisters, and younger siblings, none of them were as content as he was. He then moved his family to the capital, where the old scholars of the Hanlin Academy recommended him, and he was appointed to the prestigious position of Hanlin. Later, the court sent an envoy to Jiaozhi, where Mr. Lü Hui Zhi was appointed as the Deputy Langzhong of the Rites Department. Upon arriving in Jiaozhi, he composed a poem:

    "Old mother in Vietnam with white hair,
    Sick wife in the north leaning against the evening.
    Smoke and mist of the southern lands,
    A guest in Jiaozhi, three places of longing in one dream."

    Upon arriving in Annam, he used words and writings to persuade the country, and it eventually submitted, sending its crown prince and prime minister to the court. Later, he was rewarded for his service and appointed as the governor of a local prefecture, where he lived out his life. His father-in-law could truly be called a discerning person. He chose the path of retreat from the world.

    Mr. Lü Hui Zhi, a scholar from my hometown, lived in the mountains of Xianju, where he was well-versed in literature and poetry, and no question was beyond his knowledge. He was content with poverty and devoted to his principles, often avoiding fame and living a simple life of farming and fishing. One day, he carried some currency to a wealthy family to exchange for seeds. It was snowing heavily, and he stood at the door, unnoticed by the household. Slowly, he made his way to the courtyard and heard people in the eastern hall dividing the rhyme for a snow poem. One person got the character "Teng" and struggled to compose a verse. Mr. Lü Hui Zhi could not help but laugh. The noble youths in the hall heard the laughter and sent someone to ask him about it. Mr. Lü Hui Zhi initially remained silent, which only increased their suspicion. They finally came out to see him, who was bareheaded, wearing a short robe, and straw sandals, and they mocked him. When asked why he had laughed, Mr. Lü Hui Zhi could not help but say, "I was thinking of the story of the butterflies of Teng Wang." The crowd was then amazed and invited him to sit. He said, "With my appearance like this, how can I mingle with you gentlemen?" They persisted in their request, and he eventually entered the hall. They asked him to complete the poem with the characters "Teng" and "Teng." He picked up a brush and wrote:

    "Nine dragons in heaven cast a spell of water,
    Two rats on earth gnaw at a withered vine.
    Wild ducks fly away in the mist."

    He then retired, and they all admired his talent.

    Mr. Lü Hui Zhi, a scholar from my hometown, lived in the mountains of Xianju, where he was well-versed in literature and poetry, and no question was beyond his knowledge. He was content with poverty and devoted to his principles, often avoiding fame and living a simple life of farming and fishing. One day, he carried some currency to a wealthy family to exchange for seeds. It was snowing heavily, and he stood at the door, unnoticed by the household. Slowly, he made his way to the courtyard and heard people in the eastern hall dividing the rhyme for a snow poem. One person got the character "Teng" and struggled to compose a verse. Mr. Lü Hui Zhi could not help but laugh. The noble youths in the hall heard the laughter and sent someone to ask him about it. Mr. Lü Hui Zhi initially remained silent, which only increased their suspicion. They finally came out to see him, who was bareheaded, wearing a short robe, and straw sandals, and they mocked him. When asked why he had laughed, Mr. Lü Hui Zhi could not help but say, "I was thinking of the story of the butterflies of Teng Wang." The crowd was then amazed and invited him to sit. He said, "With my appearance like this, how can I mingle with you gentlemen?" They persisted in their request, and he eventually entered the hall. They asked him to complete the poem with the characters "Teng" and "Teng." He picked up a brush and wrote:

    "Nine dragons in heaven cast a spell of water,
    Two rats on earth gnaw at a withered vine.
    Wild ducks fly away in the mist

    157

    鹅声乱功收蔡蝴蜨飞来妙过滕复请和昙字韵诗又随笔写云万里闗河冻欲含浑如天地尚函三桥边驴子诗何恶帐底羔儿酒正酣竹委长身寒郭索松埋短髪老瞿昙不如乗此擒元济一洗江南草木惭写讫便出门留之不可得问其姓字亦不荅皆惊讶曰尝闻吕处士名欲一见而不能先生岂其人邪曰我农家安知吕处士为何如人恵之谷怒曰我岂取不义之财必易之刺船而去遣人遥尾其后路甚僻逺识其所而返雪晴徃访焉惟草屋一间家徒壁立忽米桶中有人乃先生妻也因天寒故坐其中试问徽之先生何在荅曰在溪上捕鱼始知真为

    Explanation

    157

    A Poem with the Sound of Geese and the Charm of Butterflies, Requesting a Rhyme in the Style of Tán

    Writing a poem, I heard the sound of geese and the fluttering of butterflies, which inspired me to compose a verse. I wrote:

    A thousand miles of rivers and mountains are frozen, the world seems to be enveloped in a misty haze, like the heavens and earth still holding their breath. At the Three Bridges, the donkey's poetry is not bad. Under the tent, the young goat is drunk, while the bamboo bends long and cold, and the pines bury the short hair of the old monk, Qūtán. It would be better to take advantage of this moment to capture Yuánjì, washing away the shame of the grass and trees in the south. Having finished writing, I stepped out the door, but he was not there to be detained. I asked his name, but he did not respond. They were all surprised and said, "We have heard of the name of Mr. Lǚ, a recluse scholar, and wished to meet him, but could not. Could this be the same person?" He replied, "I am a farmer, how would I know what kind of person Mr. Lǚ is?" Huīzhīgǔ became angry and said, "Would I take unjust wealth? I will exchange it." He rowed the boat and left. Someone was sent to follow him from a distance. The path was very remote and distant, but they recognized the place and returned. After the snow had melted, they went to visit. There was only a single grass hut, with nothing but bare walls, indicating extreme poverty. Suddenly, they saw someone sitting in a rice bucket, who turned out to be Mr. Hui Zhi's wife. She was sitting there because it was cold outside. They asked, "Where is Mr. Hui Zhi?" She replied, "He is fishing by the stream." It was then that they realized he was truly a recluse scholar.

    158

    先生矣至彼果见之告以特来谢之意隔溪谓曰诸公先到舎下我得鱼当换酒饮诸公也少顷携鱼与酒至尽欢而散回至中途夜黒不良于行暂憇一露棚下适主人自外归乃尝识面者问所从来语以故喜曰是固某平日所愿见者止客宿翼旦客别主人蹑其踪则先生已迁居矣又一日先生与陈刚中治中遇于道治中防蹇驴时犹布衣见先生风神髙简问曰得非吕徽之乎曰然足下非陈刚中乎曰然握手若平生欢共论驴故事先生言一事治中荅一事互至四十余事治中止矣先生曰我尚记得有某出某书某出某传又三十余事治中深敬之

    Explanation

    158

    The master arrived there and indeed saw him, telling him of his special purpose to come and thank him. Across the stream, he said to him, "Gentlemen, you came to my humble abode first; if I catch fish, I will exchange it for wine to drink with you all." After a while, he brought fish and wine, and they enjoyed themselves fully before dispersing. Returning to the halfway point, it was dark at night, making travel difficult, so they temporarily rested under a makeshift shelter. Just as the owner returned from outside, it turned out to be someone he had met before. He asked where he came from and told him the reason. The owner said happily, "Indeed, you are someone I have always wanted to see. I kept the guest for the night, and the next morning, the guest bid farewell to the owner and followed his trail. Then, the master had already moved." Another day, the master met with Chen Gangzhong, the magistrate, on the road. The magistrate was riding a slow donkey, still a commoner, and saw the master's noble and simple demeanor. He asked, "Is it not Lu Huizhi?" The master replied, "Yes." The magistrate said, "You are Chen Gangzhong, aren't you?" The master replied, "Yes." They shook hands as if they had known each other for a long time and discussed stories about donkeys. The master mentioned one thing, and the magistrate responded with one thing, alternately exchanging over forty items. The magistrate stopped. The master said, "I still remember certain things from certain books and certain things from certain transmissions, another thirty or so items." The magistrate deeply respected him.

    159

    闗节梯媒
    　　杜阳杂编云元载宠姬薛瑶英善为巧媚载惑之瑶英之父曰宗本兄曰从义与赵娟相逓出入以搆贿赂号为闗节赵娟本岐王爱妾后出为薛氏妻生瑶英三人更与中书主吏卓倩等为腹心而宗本辈以事告者载未尝不领之天下赍寳货求大官无不恃载权埶指薛卓为梯媒又李肇国史补总叙进士科云造请权要谓之闗节刘轲牛羊日歴云由是轻薄奔走鞭驰骛以闗节慢为甲乙以此推之则谚所谓打闗节有梯媒者不为无祖矣

    Explanation

    159

    Intermediaries and Mediators

    According to the Du Yang Za Bian (Miscellaneous Records of Du Yang), Yuan Zai, a prominent official during the Tang Dynasty, had a concubine named Xue Yaoqing who was skilled in flattery and manipulation. Yuan Zai was deeply influenced by her. Xue Yaoqing's father was named Zongben, and her brother was named Congyi. They, along with Zhao Juan, alternately entered and exited Yuan Zai's household, soliciting and accepting bribes, and were known as intermediaries. Zhao Juan, originally a beloved concubine of Prince Qi, later became the wife of Xue Yaoqing and bore her three children. She also formed a close alliance with the chief clerk of the Ministry of Rites, Zhuo Qian. Whenever Zongben and others reported matters to Yuan Zai, he always took them seriously. People from all over the empire who brought valuable goods and sought high-ranking positions relied on Yuan Zai's power and influence, using Xue and Zhuo as mediators.

    The Li Zhao Guo Shi Bu (Supplementary Records of Li Zhao) in the Zong Xu Jin Shi Ke (General Summary of the Imperial Examination System) states that seeking connections with powerful officials is called "intermediary." The Niu Yang Ri Li (Daily Records of Niu Yang) mentions that people became superficial and ran around recklessly, using intermediaries and mediators to achieve their goals, often in a disrespectful manner. From these examples, it can be inferred that the proverb "using intermediaries and mediators" has historical roots.

    160

    市利
    　　利市之説到处皆然易説卦巽为利市三倍
    　　老苖
    　　杨旺札勒字彦英武冈绥寜之赤水人为人隂鸷酷烈嗜斩杀初羣无赖啸聚溪洞旺扎勒深贼持权诈故众推以为长王事日湖广陶梦祯氏举师勤王闻苖有众习鬬击遣使往招之由千戸累阶至元帅梦祯死枢宻院判鄂尔和总兵驻淮西仍用招纳既得旁縁入中国不复可控制畧上江顺流而下直抵州禽兽之行絶天逆理民怨且怒共起义攻杀之余党奔溃度子宿留广徳吴兴间至正十六年春二月朔淮人防平江时江浙行中书省丞相达实特穆尔有防得便宜从事嘉兴北连平江南去杭州无二百里为藩镇喉舌有司告援急星火

    Explanation

    160

    Profit and Commerce
    The concept of profit is prevalent everywhere, as indicated by the I Ching hexagram Xun (巽), which suggests triple profits in commercial contexts.

    Old Rebels
    Yang Wangzale, courtesy name Yan Ying, was from Chishui in Wugang and Suining. He was known for his cruelty, fierceness, and fondness for killing. Initially, he gathered a group of ruffians in remote areas. Wangzale, holding power through deceit, was thus chosen as their leader. As the king's affairs became increasingly urgent, Tao Mengzhen from Hunan raised an army to support the king. Upon hearing that the rebels were skilled in combat, he sent envoys to recruit them. Yang Wangzale rose from a low rank to become a general. After Tao Mengzhen's death, Eruhe was appointed as the commander and stationed in Huai West, continuing the recruitment efforts. Once the rebels entered China, they could no longer be controlled. They moved down the river, reaching Yangzhou, engaging in inhuman and lawless acts. The people's anger led to widespread rebellion, and the rebel forces were defeated. The remnants retreated to Guangde and Wuxing. In the spring of the sixteenth year of the Zhizheng era (1356 AD), the people of Huai defended Pingjiang. At that time, the chancellor of the Jiang-Zhe provincial government, Dashi Temur, had been granted authority to act as he saw fit. Jiaxing, connected to Pingjiang and only about two hundred miles from Hangzhou, was strategically important. Officials urgently requested aid, sending messages with great haste.

    161

    驿使交道中不絶丞相兵少防无所出以旺札勒来守之旺札勒取道自杭以兵刼丞相陞本省参知政事填募民入粟空名告身予之即拜添设左丞所统苖防洞猺达拉哈等无尺籍伍符无统属相谓曰阿哥曰麻线至称主将亦然喜着斑斓衣制衣袖广狭脩短与臂同衣幅长不过膝袴如袖裙如衣总名曰草裙草袴固脰以兽皮曰护项束要以帛两端悬凥后若尾无间晴雨被氊毯状絶犬按邕管杂记溪蛮丛笑等书所载五溪之蛮尽槃瓠种属曰猫曰徭曰曰犵狑曰犵狫字皆从犬则谚所谓苖犬者信然军中无金鼔杂鸣小锣以节进止其锣若

    Explanation

    161

    The continuous flow of messengers and officials on the roads was unceasing, but the Chancellor had few soldiers and could not effectively defend or take action. Wang Zale was sent to guard the area. Wang Zale took a route from Hangzhou with his troops and forced the Chancellor to be promoted to the position of Vice-Premier of the province. He recruited civilians to provide grain and granted them blank official documents as rewards, and was immediately appointed as an additional Left Chancellor. The various groups (Miao, Fang, Dong, Yao, and Dala Ha) under his command did not have official military records or symbols and were not under a unified command. They conversed among themselves, calling the leader "Ago" and saying "Malian" (possibly a name or title) was also the main commander. They preferred wearing colorful and patterned clothing, with the sleeves of their clothes wide, narrow, long, or short, matching the length of their arms. The length of their clothing did not exceed the knees, with trousers like sleeves and skirts like dresses. They were called "grass skirts" and "grass trousers" overall. They fastened their necks with animal skins, called "protective collars," and tied their waists with silk, with the ends hanging down behind like a tail. They wore felt blankets regardless of whether it was sunny or rainy, and were entirely like dogs. According to historical and literary sources such as the Yongguan Zaji and Ximang Congxiao, the various tribes along the Five Rivers were all descendants of the Pangu (a legendary figure). They were called "cat," "Yao," "Weng," "Guweng," and "Guweng," all of which contain the character for "dog," confirming the proverb that these tribes were indeed like dogs. In the military, there were no large drums and gongs, only small drums and gongs used to regulate the movements of the troops. The gongs were used in a specific way, possibly for signaling or coordination.

    162

    卖货郎担人所敲者夜遣士卒伏路日坐草军行尚首功资抄掠抄掠曰检刮检刮者尽取而靡有孑遗之意所过无不残灭掳得男女老羸者甚幼者色陋者杀之壮者曰土乖幼者曰赖子皆驱以为奴人之投其党者曰入火妇人艶而晢者畜为妇曰夫娘人有三四妇多至十数一语不合即剸以刃与之处者得至日莫无恙则心窃自贺古云好则人怒则兽形容尽之矣是月丞相又以王与敬摄元帅事守松江与敬据郡应平江完者遣部将萧亮员成来与敬奔苗有松江火一月不絶城邑殆无噍偶获免者亦举则去两耳掠妇女刼货财残忍贪惨不

    Explanation

    162

    During the night, soldiers were sent to ambush the road, and during the day, the army sat in the grass, moving slowly. The soldiers relied on looting and plundering, and they thoroughly searched and seized everything, leaving nothing behind. Everywhere they went, they destroyed and annihilated. They captured old and weak men and women, killing those who were ugly in appearance. The able-bodied were called "unlucky," and the young were called "useless." They were all driven into slavery. Those who joined their party were called "in the fire." Beautiful and intelligent women were kept as wives, referred to as "husbands." Some men had three or four wives, while others had as many as ten. If there was a disagreement, they would immediately kill with a knife. Those who lived with them until sunset and remained unharmed would secretly rejoice in their own hearts. As the saying goes, "When things go well, people are happy; when they go badly, they become like animals." This describes the situation perfectly.

    In that month, the chancellor appointed Wang and Jing to take charge of the general's duties, guarding Songjiang. Jing occupied the city and responded to Pingjiang. After completing the task, he sent his subordinate generals Xiao Liang and Yuan Cheng to come to Jing, who fled to the Miao people. The fire in Songjiang lasted for a month without stopping, and the cities were almost devoid of living beings. Those who occasionally managed to escape were also mutilated, having their ears cut off. They plundered women and seized goods, acting with extreme greed and cruelty, beyond description.

    163

    忍言官庾尚有粟四十万余籍为己有越五十日平江兵破淀湖栅苖夜遁去秋平江兵入杭苗将吴大旺败旺札勒自嘉兴来驻兵城中菜市桥外未即进民自为战胜旺札勒兵滛刑以逞嘉兴仅保孤城城之外悉遭兵燹有穷目力所至无寸草尺木处旺札勒虽阳浮尊事丞相生杀予夺于己是决丞相仅得署成案然浙江之南则行御史台总督官穆尔古蘓建徳路则逹噜噶齐衮都罗丹各自为守苗不敢犯其境旺札勒之威令仅行于杭州嘉兴两郡而已筑营徳胜堰周围三四里子女玉帛皆在焉且以为郿坞计用法刻深任埶立威而邓子文金希

    Explanation

    163

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, the official Ren Yan Guan Yu Shang had stored more than forty thousand units of grain, which he claimed as his own. After fifty days, the forces from Pingjiang broke through the defenses at Dianhu, and the enemy forces fled during the night. In autumn, the Pingjiang forces entered Hangzhou. The enemy general Wu Dawang was defeated. Wang Zale came from Jiaxing and stationed his troops outside the Cai Shiqiao bridge in the city but did not immediately advance. The people fought against Wang Zale's forces, who used excessive and cruel punishments. Only the isolated city of Jiaxing remained intact, while the surrounding areas were completely devastated by war. Within the range of one's sight, there was no vegetation. Although Wang Zale pretended to show respect to the Chancellor, he made all decisions regarding life and death. The Chancellor could only approve the final decisions. However, in the southern part of Zhejiang, the Inspector General Muergu Si and in Jianshi Road, the Governor Dunuqi Gundi each maintained their own defenses, and the enemy dared not invade their territories. Wang Zale's authority and commands were limited to the two cities of Hangzhou and Jiaxing. He constructed a camp at De Sheng Yan, which was surrounded by several miles. Inside the camp were women, jade, and silk. Wang Zale planned to use this as a stronghold and enforced strict laws to establish his authority. However, the names "邓子文" (Deng Ziwén) and "金希" (Jin Xi) are unclear and may require further research.

    164

    尹王彦良之徒又悉邪佞轻佻左右交煽气焰翕忽时左丞李伯升行枢宻同知史文炳行枢宻同佥吕珍等皆先魁淮旅而降顺者丞相以其众攻杀之既受围遣吏致牲酒于文炳为可怜之意曰愿少须防毋死得以底里上路报不可旺札勒乗躁力战败尽杀所有妇女自经以死独平章庆童女以先往在富阳得免平章女已尝许嫁亲王为旺札勒强委禽焉至是未及三月故数其罪者此居首诸军开门纳欵惟恐弗先文炳解衣裹尸瘗之祭哭尽哀十八年秋八月也旺札勒部将宋兴在嘉兴闭城自守亦攻降之城中燔燬者三之二民遇害者十之七
    　　南村野史曰苗入华夏民之不幸亦国家之不幸也国以民为本本不固矣奚以宁为之将若相者在于明黜陟严赏罸奉将天威降者招之逆者讨之以培国家之本可也顾于此而不为又无他竒谋逺畧而乃借重于非正犹开虎兕之柙而使赴犬羊耳尚冀保民命为社稷计一何愚哉罪恶贯盈天怒于上败亡戮辱身膏草野民争以为快实亦自取之也惟旺札勒则有

    Explanation

    164

    The followers of Yin Wang Yanliang were all deceitful, flatterers, and frivolous, and those around them were fanning their flames, causing their influence to rapidly grow. At the time, the Left Chancellor Li Bosheng and Acting Chancellor Shi Wenbing, along with Acting Deputy Chancellor Lu Zhen and others, who were leaders in the Huai army and had surrendered, were attacked and killed by the chancellor with their own forces. After being besieged, the chancellor sent officials to offer sacrificial animals and wine to Shi Wenbing, expressing pity and saying they wished he could wait a little longer before dying so they could report back to the capital. Wang Zale took advantage of the chaos and fought fiercely, killing all the women, who then hanged themselves. Only the daughter of Ping Zhang Qingtong, who had previously gone to Fuyang, was spared. Ping Zhang's daughter had been promised to a prince, but Wang Zale forced the engagement. By the time three months had passed, the list of his crimes was topped by this. All the armies opened their gates to surrender, fearing to be the last. Shi Wenbing undressed and wrapped the bodies in cloth, burying them and mourning with great sorrow. This occurred in the eighth month of the eighteenth year. Wang Zale's subordinate, Song Xing, was in Jiaxing, where he closed the city gates and defended himself. The city was also attacked and surrendered, with three-fifths of the city burned and seven-tenths of the population perishing.

    The Southern Village Historian said: The invasion of the Huaxia by the Miao is both a misfortune for the people and for the nation. The state relies on its people, and if the foundation is not solid, how can the state be secure? Those in charge should clearly distinguish rewards and punishments, uphold the authority of the general, and either recruit those who surrender or punish those who rebel to strengthen the nation's foundation. But if they do not do this and lack other ingenious strategies, instead relying on untrustworthy individuals, it is like opening the cage of tigers and allowing them to attack dogs and sheep. They still hope to protect the people and serve the state, but how foolish is this! When their crimes are heinous and heaven is angry, their downfall, humiliation, and death are met with joy by the people, which is also self-inflicted. Only Wang Zale had...

    165

    説焉旺札勒宠荣过望岂有贰志忠君爱民之道颇亦见诸行事独矜已犯分贪财好色固苗性所然君子责备贤者于此可以畧之则罪亦未至于死也兼以所部吏卒视旺札勒起身等寒防故威令有所不信急之则恐内变缓之则坏法败度遂卒至于如此亦可哀矣又惜乎草草之举断自一时吾恐国家之本铲刈殆尽虽有智谋之士亦无如之何矣天乎人乎吾不得而知也
    　　双砚堂
    　　周待制月岩先生【仁荣】买地于府城之郑儿坊剏义塾以淑后进筑础时掘地深才数尺有青石获双砚砚有欵识乃唐郑司戸防故物塾既成遂名双砚堂尔后先生之弟本道先生【仔肩】登庚申科仕至恵州判官防字弱齐俗譌为儿云
    　　嫁妾犹处子
    　　先师钱先生【壁】字伯全壬申科进士端重清慎语不伤气尝内一女鬟风姿秀雅殊可人意室氏劝先生私之正色而荅曰我之所以置此者欲以侍巾栉耳岂有他意哉汝乃反欲败吾徳耶即具赀嫁之果处子也先生云间人

    Explanation

    165

    Wang Zale, a noble or official, was granted excessive favor and honor, which led one to wonder if he had divided loyalties. His principles of loyalty to the ruler and love for the people were indeed reflected in his actions, but he boasted of transgressing boundaries, greedily pursuing wealth and lust for beauty, which were inherent in his nature. Wise men hold high standards for virtuous individuals, and in this case, we can overlook this aspect, as his crime was not severe enough to warrant death. Additionally, the officials and soldiers under his command saw the rise of Wang Zale and others as a threat, causing his commands to be no longer trusted. If pressed, it would cause internal rebellion; if relaxed, it would undermine laws and norms. Thus, he ended up in such a state, which is truly pitiful. It is also regrettable that such hasty actions were taken at one time. I fear that the roots of the state have been almost completely destroyed, even if there were wise and strategic people, they would be unable to do anything about it. Whether by heaven or by human action, I cannot know.

    Double Inkstone Hall
    Mr. Yueyan Zhou, a respected official with the courtesy name Renrong, bought land in the Zhengtai'er Ward of the city and established a school to educate future generations. When laying the foundation, they dug deep and found a piece of blue stone containing two inkstones. The inkstones had inscriptions indicating they were ancient artifacts from the Tang Dynasty, belonging to the household of Zheng. After the school was completed, it was named Double Inkstone Hall. Later, his younger brother, Mr. Ben Dao, passed the imperial examination in the year of Gengshen and served as a magistrate in Huizhou. The character '防' is often mistakenly written as '儿'.

    A Virgin Concubine
    My former teacher, Mr. Qian, with the courtesy name Boquan, passed the imperial examination in the year Ren Shen and became a successful candidate. He was upright, dignified, cautious, and his words did not hurt others. He once had a young female servant who was very graceful and attractive. His wife advised him to have relations with her. He answered seriously, "I kept her for the purpose of serving me, not for any other reason. You are trying to ruin my virtue." He immediately prepared the dowry and married her off. She was indeed a virgin. Mr. Qian was from Yunjian (a place name).

    166

    聂碧窓诗
    　　京口天庆观主聂碧窓江西人尝为龙翔宫书记国初时诏赦至感而有诗曰乾坤杀气正沉沉又听燕台降徳音万口尽新诏好累朝谁念旧恩深分茅列土将军志问舎求田父老心丽正立班犹昨日小臣无语泪霑襟又哀被虏妇云当年结髪在深闺岂料人生有别离到底不知因色误马前犹自买胭脂又咏燕姬云双栁垂鬟别样梳醉来马上倩人扶江南有眼何曾见争卷珠帘看固姑
    　　玉
    　　江邻几杂志云丁正臣赍玉来馆中沈休文云福州人谓之佩羮即今鱼脬是也
    　　蟹断
    　　陆蟹志云稻之登也率执一穂以朝其魁然后任其所之蚤夜嘈沸指江而奔渔者纬萧承其流而障之名曰蟹断然纬萧二字尤竒
    　　作今乐府法
    　　乔孟符【吉】博学多能以乐府称尝云作乐府亦有法曰凤头猪肚豹尾六字是也大槩起要美丽中要浩荡结要响亮尤贵在首尾贯穿意思清新茍能若是斯可以言乐府矣此所谓乐府乃今乐府如折桂令水仙子之

    Explanation

    166

    Final Translation

    Poems by Nie Bixuang

    Nie Bixuang, the head of the Tianqing Temple in Jingkou, Jiangxi Province, once served as a secretary at Longxiang Palace during the early years of the dynasty. Upon the arrival of a decree of amnesty, he was moved to write the following poem:

    The killing energy of heaven and earth is heavy and oppressive,
    Yet I hear the benevolent edict descend from Yan Platform.
    Ten thousand mouths all praise the new decree,
    But who remembers the deep old favors?
    Dividing the grass and establishing the land, the generals' ambitions,
    Asking for a house and seeking for land, the old men's hearts.
    Standing in the ranks of the Imperial Academy, it seems like yesterday,
    The lowly official has no words, tears wetting the robe.

    He also mourned a woman captured by the enemy:

    In her deep chamber, she bound her hair in the prime of youth,
    Who could have foreseen that life would bring such separation?
    Ultimately, she did not know that it was due to her beauty,
    Yet she still buys rouge before the horse.

    He also wrote about a woman from Yan:

    Two willows hanging down their hair, styled differently,
    Drunk and leaning on someone for support on the horse.
    Who in Jiangnan has ever seen such a sight?
    Rushing to roll up the pearl curtains to see the old lady.

    Jade Dish

    According to the Jianglinji Zazhi (Notes from the Jianglin Academy), Ding Zhengchen brought a jade dish to the imperial court. Shen Xiwen said that people from Fuzhou call it "Peigeng," which is the same as the modern fish bladder.

    Crab Catching

    Lü Guimeng, in his Xie Zhi (Crab Chronicle), wrote that when rice is harvested, crabs often carry a single grain to present to their leader, and then they go wherever they please. Early in the morning and late at night, they make a loud noise and rush towards the river. Fishermen use wèixiāo nets to intercept the flow and block them, calling this method "crab catching." The term "wèixiāo" is particularly unique.

    The Art of Writing Modern Music Bureau Poems

    Qiao Mengfu, a scholar and playwright of the Yuan Dynasty, was renowned for his skill in writing music bureau poems. He once said that writing music bureau poems also follows a method: "Phoenix head, pig belly, leopard tail." Generally, the beginning should be beautiful, the middle should be expansive, and the end should be striking. What is most important is that the beginning and end should be well-connected, with fresh and clear ideas. If one can achieve this, then one can truly speak of writing music bureau poems. Here, the term "music bureau poems" refers to modern music bureau poems, such as "Zheguiling" and "Shuixianzi."

    This translation aims to capture the essence and cultural nuances of the original Classical Chinese text while ensuring it reads naturally in English.

    167

    岷江緑
    　　太师巴延擅权之日剡王齐齐克图髙昌王特穆尔布哈皆以无罪杀山东宪吏曹明善时在都下作岷江緑二曲以风之大书揭于五门之上巴延怒令左右暗察得实肖形捕之明善出避吴中一僧舎居数年伯顔事败方再入京其曲曰长门栁丝千万缕总是伤心处行人折柔条燕子衔芳絮都不由凤城春做主长门栁丝千万结风起花如雪离别重离别攀折复攀折苦无多旧时枝叶也此曲又名清江引俗曰江儿水
    　　温暾
    　　南人方言曰温暾者乃懐暖也唐王建宫词新晴草色暖温暾又白乐天诗池水暖温暾则古已然矣飞云渡
    　　飞云渡风浪甚恶每有覆舟之患有一少年子放纵不羁尝以所生年月日时就日者问平生富贫夀夭有告曰汝之夀莫能逾三旬及徧叩他日者言亦多同于是意谓非乆于人世乃不娶妻不事生产作业每以轻财仗义为志尝俟船渡傍见一了鬟女子徘徊悲戚若将赴水少年亟止之问曰何为轻生如此荅曰我本人家小婢主人有姻事暂借亲眷珠子耳环一双直钞三十余定今日送还竟于中

    Explanation

    167

    岷江緑

    When Tai Shi Ba Yan wielded power, Prince Qiqitu of Yan and Prince Temur Buhu of Gaochang both killed Shanxi censor Cao Ming'an without cause. At that time, Cao Ming'an was in the capital and composed two songs titled "Mín Jiāng Lǜ" to criticize them. He wrote these songs and posted them prominently on the five gates. Ba Yan became angry and ordered his attendants to investigate and confirm the truth. They captured Cao Ming'an in the likeness and he fled to a Buddhist temple in Wu for several years. After Bayan's downfall, Cao Ming'an returned to the capital. The songs are:

    Longmen Willow Song
    Thousand threads of willow silk, all are places of sorrow.
    Travelers break tender branches, swallows carry fragrant down.
    None of these are governed by the spring of Fengcheng.
    Thousand knots of willow silk, when the wind rises, flowers fall like snow.
    Farewell is heavy, farewell is heavy, picking and breaking repeatedly.
    Alas, there are few of the old branches and leaves left.
    This song is also known as "Qingjiang Yin," commonly called "River Child Water."

    温暾

    Southern dialect calls "wēn tūn" as being warm in one's heart. In Tang Wang Jian's palace poetry, it says, "The grass color is warm and comfortable after the rain clears." And in Bai Juyi's poem, "The pool water is warm and comfortable," this term has been used since ancient times.

    Feiyun Ferry

    The wind and waves at Feiyun Ferry are very dangerous, and there is always a risk of capsizing. There was a young man who was unrestrained and wild. He once asked a fortune-teller about his life's wealth, poverty, longevity, and death based on his birth date and time. The fortune-teller said, "Your lifespan will not exceed thirty years." He asked many fortune-tellers, and their answers were mostly the same. Thus, he believed that he would not live long and decided not to marry, not to engage in production or work, and often spent money lightly, valuing righteousness. He once waited by the ferry and saw a young girl with a headband, wandering and looking sad as if she was about to jump into the water. The young man quickly stopped her and asked, "Why do you want to end your life?" She replied, "I am a small servant from a household. My master has a wedding and temporarily borrowed a pair of pearl earrings and earrings worth more than thirty taels of silver. Today I am returning them, and I am in despair."

    This translation aims to be both faithful to the original text and natural in English, addressing the idiomatic expressions, cultural allusions, syntactic ambiguities, and proper nouns as planned.

    168

    途失去寜死耳焉敢归少年曰我适拾得但不审果是汝物否方再三磨问颗数装束实是遂同造主人主人感谢欲赠以礼辞不受既而主人怒此婢遣嫁业梳剃者所居去渡所咫尺间期嵗少年与同行二十有八人将过渡道遇一妇人拜且谢视之乃失环女也因告其故于夫屈留午饭余人先登舟俄风涛大作皆鱼腹盖少年能救人一命而造物者亦救其一命以荅之后少年以夀终渡在温之瑞安
    　　汉子
    　　今人谓贱丈夫曰汉子按北齐魏恺自散骑常侍迁青州长史固辞文宣帝大怒曰何物汉子与官不就又叚成式庐陵官下记韦令去西蜀时彭州刺史被县令宻论诉韦前期勘知屈刺史诣府陈谢及回日诸县令悉逺迎所诉者为首大言曰使君今日可谓朱研益丹矣刺史笑曰则公便是研朱汉子也
    　　长年
    　　吾乡称舟人之老者曰长老长上声盖唐已有之矣杜工部诗云长年三老歌声里白昼摊钱髙浪中古今诗话谓川峡以篙手为三老乃推一船之最尊者言之耳因思海舶中以司柁曰大翁是亦长老三老之意

    Explanation

    168

    If I lose my way, I would rather die than return home. The young man said, "I just picked it up, but I'm not sure if it's yours." After repeatedly asking about the number of items and their packaging, it turned out to be true. So we went together to the owner. The owner thanked us and wanted to give us a gift, but we refused. Later, the owner was angry and sent the maid to be married to a barber who lived very close to the ferry, within a year. The young man and his twenty-eight companions were about to cross the river when they met a woman who bowed and thanked them. Upon seeing her, they recognized her as the woman who had lost her ring. They told her husband the story, and he invited them to stay for lunch. The others boarded the boat first, but soon a storm arose, and all were drowned. It was because the young man saved someone's life that the creator also saved his life as a reward. Later, the young man lived a long life. The ferry is located in Ruian, Wenzhou.

    Nowadays, people call a low-ranking man "hànzi." During the Northern Qi dynasty, Wei Kai, who was a scion of the imperial family, refused to accept the position of Governor of Qingzhou and was appointed as a Palace Attendant. Emperor Wenxuan was furious and said, "What kind of hànzi refuses an official position?" Duan Chengshi, the official of Luzhou, recorded that Wei Ling, the magistrate of Xishu, was accused by the county magistrate. Wei Ling investigated and found that the accusation was false. He went to the governor's office to apologize. When he returned, all the county magistrates came to greet him. The accuser, who was the leader, said, "Today, Sir, you have truly made red even redder." The governor laughed and said, "Then you are the hànzi who makes red even redder."

    In my hometown, we call an old boatman "longzhang." This term has been used since the Tang dynasty. Du Fu, the poet, wrote in his poem, "In the songs of the longzhang and the three elders, during the day, they gamble in high waves." According to the "Poetic Notes of Ancient and Modern Times," in the Sichuan and Chongqing region, the "three elders" refer to the most respected person on the boat. Therefore, in sea vessels, the person in charge of the rudder is called "dàwēng," which also carries the meaning of "longzhang" and "three elders."

    169

    龙见嘉兴
    　　檇李郭元之言至正乙未秋七月三日城东马桥上白龙挂盲风怪雨天闇黒若深夜然坏民居五百余所大木尽防木自半空坠下悉折为二杂以万瓦乱飞溪水直立人皆呌号奔走不暇顾妻子龙由马桥歴城北北丽桥望太湖而去时方在家家去城可三里许如闻万屋齐压急出戸四望黒云汹涌失府城所在经一二时方乃开霁不一年为战鬬之地凡龙所过处荆棘寒烟衰草野燐视昔时之繁华如一梦也星入月
    　　松江孙元璘言至正己未七月六日夜自平江归泊舟城西栅口方掀篷露坐忽见一星大如桮碗色白而防青尾长四五丈光焰烛天戞然有声由东北方飞入月中而止此时月如仰瓦正乗之无偏倚人以手拾置其中者尝记宋张端义贵耳集云丁亥年余为仪真録参十月二十三日夜因观天象见一星入月歴者邹淮絶早相别云昨夜星入月恐两淮兵动

    Explanation

    169

    Final Translation

    A Dragon Appears in Jiaxing

    Guo Yuanzhi of Jizu reports that on the third day of the seventh month in the autumn of the year Yichou during the Zhi Zheng era, a white dragon appeared on Ma Bridge in the east of the city, accompanied by a blind wind and strange rain. The sky was dark as if it were midnight. More than 500 houses were destroyed, large trees were uprooted, and wood fell from the mid-air, all breaking in two. Countless tiles flew about, and the stream stood upright. People screamed and ran, too busy to look after their wives and children. The dragon passed over Ma Bridge, through the northern part of the city, and over North Li Bridge, heading towards Taihu Lake. At the time, I was at home, about three miles from the city, and it sounded like ten thousand houses were collapsing together. I rushed out of the house and looked around, seeing dark clouds surging, losing sight of the city. After one or two hours, the sky cleared. Within a year, it became a battlefield. Wherever the dragon passed, there were thorns, cold smoke, withered grass, and wild fires, making the former prosperity seem like a dream.

    A Star Enters the Moon

    Sun Yuanlin of Songjiang reports that on the sixth day of the seventh month in the year Jiwei during the Zhi Zheng era, returning from Pingjiang and mooring the boat at the west gate of the city, I just lifted the sail and sat exposed. Suddenly, I saw a star as large as a cup and bowl, white and slightly blue, with a tail four or five zhang long, its light illuminating the sky. It made a clanging sound and flew into the moon from the northeast, stopping there. At this time, the moon was like an inverted tile, perfectly centered. I once read in the Gui'er Ji by Zhang Duanyi of the Song Dynasty that in the year Dinghai, while serving as a recorder in Yizhen, on the twenty-third day of the tenth month, I observed a star entering the moon. The historian Zou Huai said, "I will leave early tomorrow morning, as I fear that the troops in the two Huai regions may be mobilized due to the star entering the moon."

    170

    不可住径唤渡过建康余问之古有此否邹云汉献帝时一次星入月今再见也十一月十二日刘倬举兵僇孝姑姑反戈一城狼狈仅以身免继此兵祸未泯也据此説则松江之祸亦非偶然松江自丙申二月十八日军乱越三日苗来尅复首尾两月之间焚杀掳掠十里之城悉化瓦砾之区视他郡尤可畏是则星入月不知此时在于何所分野顾乃松江独应其兆与
    　　军中礼士
    　　浙省参政董公搏霄字【孟起】以名行当至正癸巳之间总兵戌昱岭独松千秋三闗日号令严肃民赖以安及尅复诸郡不杀掳不抄掠其御将帅也凛然不可犯而四方之士归之者礼遇勤至尊酒在前起立捧觞既恭且和然后取其所长而任之若董公者可谓得待士之体矣

    Explanation

    170

    Translation

    One cannot stop and immediately call for a ferry to cross to Jiankang. I asked about this, and Zou Yun said that during Emperor Xian's reign, there was once a star entering the moon, and now it is seen again. On the 12th day of the 11th month, Liu Zhuo raised an army to kill his aunt, who turned against him, leaving the city in disarray; he barely escaped with his life, and the subsequent military disaster has not yet ended. According to this account, the disaster in Songjiang was also not accidental. Since the military uprising in Songjiang on the 18th day of the second month of Bing Shen, over three days, Miao came to suppress it. In the two months that followed, the ten-mile city was completely destroyed by fire, slaughter, and looting, turning into a wasteland. Compared to other counties, it is even more terrifying. The star entering the moon is unknown at what time and in which region it occurred, but Songjiang alone seems to have responded to this omen.

    Military Etiquette for Scholars

    Zheng Sheng, the Governor of Zhejiang, named Dong Bo Xiao, with the courtesy name Mengqi, during the period of Zhizheng Gui Si, was the overall commander of the military garrison at Yuling, Dusu, Qianqiu, and San Pass. He issued strict orders, and the people relied on him for safety. When he recaptured various counties, he did not kill or loot, and his discipline was so strict that no one dared to defy him. Scholars from all over came to him, and he treated them with great courtesy, standing up to pour wine when it was presented, showing both respect and harmony. He then took their strengths and appointed them accordingly. If one were to follow Dong Gong's example, it could be said that he truly understood how to treat scholars.

    171

    不耐烦
    　　不耐烦三字见宋书庾登之弟仲文
    　　阿谁
    　　阿谁二字见三国志厐统

    　　辍耕録卷八
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷九　　　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰文章宗防
    　　卢踈斋先生文章宗防云大凡作诗须用三百篇与离骚言不闗于世教义不存于比兴诗亦徒作夫诗发乎情止乎礼义闗睢乐而不滛哀而不伤斯得性情之正古人于此观风焉赋者古诗之流也前极宏侈之规后归简约之制故班固二都之赋冠絶千古前极铺张钜丽故后必称典谟训诰之作终焉厥后十数作者仿而效之盖诗人之赋必丽以则也古今文章大家数甚不多见六经不可尚矣战国之文反覆善辨孟轲之条畅庄周之竒伟屈原之清深为大家西汉之文浑厚典雅贾谊之俊

    Explanation

    171

    Impatience
    The three characters "bù nài fán" (impatient) are found in the biography of Yu Zhongwen, the younger brother of Yu Dengzhi, in the Song Shu (Book of Song).

    Who
    The two characters "ā shuí" (who) are found in the biography of Pang Tong in the Sanguozhi (Records of the Three Kingdoms).

    Records Interrupted by Plowing, Volume Eight
    Imperially Approved Complete Library of the Four Treasuries
    Records Interrupted by Plowing, Volume Nine — Yuan Dynasty, Tao Zongyi, Supreme Rules of Literary Composition

    Mr. Lu Shu-zhai said in his Supreme Rules of Literary Composition: Generally speaking, when composing poetry, one must use the Three Hundred Poems and Li Sao. If the words do not concern worldly teachings and if there is no analogy or metaphor, then the poem is merely a waste of effort. Poetry originates from emotion but is restrained by propriety and righteousness. Guan Ju is joyful but not licentious, sorrowful but not hurtful, thus achieving the proper balance of emotion and nature. Fu is a derivative of ancient poetry. It begins with grand and extravagant conventions and ends with a return to simplicity. Thus, Ban Gu's Two Capitals Fu stands out as unparalleled in all of history. It begins with grand and magnificent descriptions, so it must end with references to canonical works of instruction and admonition. Afterward, several dozen authors followed this pattern, as the fu of poets must be both splendid and regulated. In all of history, the number of great literary masters is quite small. The Six Classics are the most esteemed, but writings from the Warring States period are characterized by their skillful and persuasive arguments. Mencius' writings are clear and flowing, Zhuangzi's are extraordinary and magnificent, and Qu Yuan's are pure and profound, all considered great works. The writings of the Western Han period are rich and elegant, with Jia Yi's being particularly distinguished.

    172

    健司马之雄放为大家三国之文孔明之二表建安诸子之数书而已西晋之文渊明归去来辞李令伯陈情表王逸少兰亭叙而已唐之文韩之雅徤栁之刻削为大家夫孰不知然古文亦有数汉文司马相如雄名教罪人其文古唐文韩外元次山近古樊宗师作为苦澁非古宋文章家尤多老欧之雅粹老苏之苍劲长苏之神俊而古作甚不多见盖清庙茅屋谓之古朱门大厦谓之华屋可谓之古不可太羮酒谓之古八珍谓之美味可谓之古不可知此者可与言古文之妙矣夫古文以辨而

    Explanation

    172

    The strength and boldness of the Wei period, particularly the works of the Jian'an era writers and the famous "Memorial to the Emperor" by Zhuge Liang, are well-known. The literary works of the Jian'an era, such as those by Cao Cao and his descendants, are also highly regarded. In the Western Jin dynasty, Tao Yuanming's "Returning to the Fields" and Li Mi's "Memorial of Sincerity" are celebrated. Wang Xizhi's "Preface to the Poems Composed at the Orchid Pavilion" is a renowned work from the Jin dynasty. In the Tang dynasty, Han Yu's elegant and robust prose, Liu Zongyuan's precise and meticulous writing, and the works of other Tang writers are widely recognized. In the Song dynasty, Ouyang Xiu's elegant and refined prose, Su Shi's versatile and expressive writing, and Su Zhe's profound and insightful prose are highly esteemed. Despite the fame of these works, ancient writing also has its own unique characteristics. The works of Sima Xiangru and Yang Xiong from the Han dynasty, though criticized for their strict adherence to Confucian norms, are considered ancient. The works of Han Yu and Yuan Zhi from the Tang dynasty, and Fan Zongshi's laborious and meticulous writing, are also noted. In the Song dynasty, the works of Ouyang Xiu, Su Shi, and Su Zhe are particularly admired for their elegance, vigor, and profundity. However, ancient works are not as numerous as modern ones. Simple and pure works, like ancient temples or simple dwellings, are considered ancient. Opulent and grand works, like luxurious mansions, are not considered ancient. Pure and unadulterated offerings, like ancient rituals, are considered ancient. Delicate and refined works, like the eight delicacies of ancient Chinese cuisine, are not considered ancient. Only those who understand the essence of ancient writing can appreciate its beauty. Ancient writing is characterized by its clarity and distinction.

    173

    不华质而不俚为髙无排句无陈言无赘辞夫记者所以纪日月之逺近工费之多寡主佐之姓名叙事如书史法尚书顾命是也叙事之后略作议论以结之然不可多盖记者以备不忘也夫叙者次序其语前之説勿施于后后之説勿施于前其语次第不可颠倒故次序其语曰叙尚书叙毛诗序古今作序大格样书序首言画卦书契之始次言皇坟帝典三代之书及夫子定书之由又次言秦亡汉兴求书之事诗序首言六义之始次言变风变雅之作又次言二南王化之自碑文惟韩公最髙每碑

    Explanation

    173

    In all writing, it is not ornate but substantial, not vulgar, and thus high; it avoids parallel sentences, clichés, and superfluous words. The purpose of a record is to document the distance of time, the amount of labor and expenses, and the names of the master and assistants. Narrating events should follow the method of historical books, as seen in the Shangshu (Book of Documents) and the Gumìng (Constitutional Edict). After narrating, one may briefly add some commentary to conclude, but this should not be excessive, as the purpose of a record is to ensure that nothing is forgotten. The purpose of a narrative is to sequence its words, ensuring that what was said earlier is not applied to later, and what was said later is not applied to earlier. The sequence of words cannot be reversed, so the sequencing of words is called a narrative. The Shangshu and the Máo Shī (Book of Songs) both have prefaces that follow a general pattern: the preface to a book first mentions the origin of the Yì (Book of Changes) and the Shū (Book of Documents), then discusses the Huang Fén (Yellow Emperor's Classic) and the Dì Diǎn (Emperor's Records) of the Three Dynasties, and finally mentions the reasons for Confucius' editing of the books. Next, it discusses the rise of the Han Dynasty after the fall of the Qin Dynasty and the search for books. The preface to the Shī (Book of Songs) first mentions the origin of the six arts, then discusses the creation of Biàn Fēng (Variant Odes) and Biàn Yǎ (Variant Elegies), and finally mentions the influence of the Èr Nán (Two Souths) on the king's virtue. Among steles, only Han Yu's are the highest, and each stele is a masterpiece.

    174

    行文言道人人殊面目首尾决不再行蹈袭神道碑揭于外行文稍可加详埋文圹记最宜谨严铭字从金一字不泛用善为文者宜如古诗雅颂之作行实之作当取其人平生忠孝大节其余小善寸长书法宜畧为人立传之法亦然防取古诗狼防其胡之义犯前则躐其胡防语不可多多则宂尾语宜峻峭以其不可复加之意説则出自己意横説坚説其文详赡抑无所不可如韩公师説是也真公编次古文自西汉而下他并不録迄唐惟尊韩公四记栁公游西山六记而已古文之难岂其然乎

    Explanation

    174

    In writing, the way of speech varies from person to person, and the beginning and end should never be repeated or imitated. A spiritual way stele is erected outside, and the writing can be slightly more detailed. Burial texts and tomb inscriptions should be the most rigorous and precise, and the characters used in inscriptions should be carefully chosen and not used arbitrarily. Those skilled in writing should emulate the style of ancient poems and odes. Biographies should focus on the major virtues and significant deeds of the subject, and other minor virtues and small achievements should be briefly mentioned. The method of writing a biography should be similar, avoiding the meaning of "wolf guarding its own territory" from ancient poetry. If one commits the previous mistake, it will overstep the bounds and become redundant, and the concluding remarks should be sharp and pointed. If the meaning cannot be added again, it should be expressed clearly and forcefully, allowing for both praise and criticism. It is like Han Gong's Teacher's Discourse. Zhen Gong's compilation of ancient prose from the Western Han period to the Tang Dynasty, only recording the works of Han Gong's four records and Liu Gong's six records of his travels to Xishan. Is it really so difficult to write classical prose?

    175

    玛达巴歴
    　　耶律文正王于星歴筮卜杂内音律儒释异国之书无不通究尝言西域歴五星宻于中国乃作玛达巴歴盖回鹘歴名也
    　　续演雅发挥
    　　白湛渊先生续演雅十诗发挥云海青羽中虎燕燕能制之小隙沉大舟闗尹不吾欺者海青俊禽也而羣燕縁扑之即坠物受于所制者无小大也【右一】草食押不芦虽死元不死未见涤肠人先闻弃箦子者漠北有名押不芦食其汁立死然以他药解之即苏华佗洗肠胃攻疾疑先服此也【右二】谁令珠玉唾出彼藜藿肠仁人不为寳良贾宜深藏者和林有尼能吐珠玉杂寳也【右三】婴啼闻木枝羝乳见茅茹何如百年身反尔

    Explanation

    175

    玛达巴歴
    　　耶律文正王于星歴筮卜杂内音律儒释异国之书无不通究尝言西域歴五星宻于中国乃作玛达巴歴盖回鹘歴名也

    Translation:
    The Madaba Calendar
    　　Yelü Chucai, the Righteous Prince, was well-versed in astronomy, calendar-making, divination, music, Confucianism, Buddhism, and foreign languages. He once remarked that the calendar systems of the Western Regions, particularly the Five Planets, were more precise than those in China. Consequently, he created the Madaba Calendar, which is named after the Uyghur Calendar.

    续演雅发挥
    　　白湛渊先生续演雅十诗发挥云海青羽中虎燕燕能制之小隙沉大舟闗尹不吾欺者海青俊禽也而羣燕縁扑之即坠物受于所制者无小大也【右一】草食押不芦虽死元不死未见涤肠人先闻弃箦子者漠北有名押不芦食其汁立死然以他药解之即苏华佗洗肠胃攻疾疑先服此也【右二】谁令珠玉唾出彼藜藿肠仁人不为寳良贾宜深藏者和林有尼能吐珠玉杂寳也【右三】婴啼闻木枝羝乳见茅茹何如百年身反尔

    Translation:
    Continued Elegance and Refinement
    　　Mr. Bai Zhenyuan continued his collection of ten poems on elegance and refinement, saying: "The sea eagle, a noble bird, can control the swallows, just as a small flaw can sink a large ship. The master of the gate would not deceive us." The sea eagle, being a noble bird, can control the swallows, and whatever it controls, regardless of size, will fall under its influence. [First Poem] The plant known as "Yew" (押不芦) is said to be poisonous, but if one does not wash their intestines with it, they will not die. In the northern deserts, there is a plant called "Yew" whose juice is deadly, but if treated with other medicines, one can recover. This is similar to the practice of Hua Tuo, who used this method to treat diseases by washing the intestines. [Second Poem] Who would make pearls and jade spit out of their intestines? A benevolent person would not consider such things valuable, and a wise merchant should keep them hidden. In Hulun, there is a nun who can produce pearls and other precious gems. [Third Poem] Even a baby can be influenced by the sound of a branch, and even the most stubborn can be softened by certain conditions. How much more so for a person who has lived for a hundred years?

    176

    无根据者漠北种羊角能产羊其大如兎食之肥美婴啼木枝见山海经所载【右四】西狩获白麟至死意不吐代北有角端能通诸国语者角端北地异兽也能人言其髙如浮图【右五】才脱海啄已登方物舆仰面勿啾啾我长非侨如者小人长仅七寸夫妇二枚形体毕具也【右六】羯尾大如斛坚车载不起此以不掉灭彼以不掉死者西汉有羯尾大于身之半非车载尾不可行也【右七】八珍殽龙凤此出龙凤外荔枝配江珧
    徒夸有风味者谓迤北八珍也所谓八珍则醍醐麆沆野驼蹄鹿唇驼乳糜天鹅炙紫玉浆玉浆也玉浆即马妳子【右八】滦人薪巨松童山八百里世无奚超勇惆怅度易水者取松煤于滦阳即今上都去上都二百里即古松林千里其大十围居人薪之将八百里也【右九】两驼侍雪立终日饥不起一觉沙日黄肉屏那足拟者沙漠雪盛命两驼趺其旁终夜不动用断梗架片氊其上而寝处于下暖胜肉屏且不起心兵也【右十】

    Explanation

    176

    Without foundation, it is said that the horns of a sheep from the northern desert can produce lambs, as large as rabbits, and eating them makes one fat and delicious. The infant cries on a wooden branch, as recorded in the Shanhaijing. [Right Four] In the west, a white unicorn was captured, but until death, it did not reveal its intentions. In the north of Dai, there is a creature called Jueduan, which can understand the languages of many countries. Jueduan is a strange beast from the northern land that can speak human language, and its height is like a pagoda. [Right Five] Just emerging from the sea, it has already pecked its way up. A cart carrying exotic goods, do not chirp while looking up. I am not as tall as Qiaorou. A small person is only seven inches tall, a pair of husband and wife, with complete physical forms. [Right Six] The tail of a Jie is as large as a measure, and even a sturdy cart cannot lift it. This one dies because it does not move its tail, and the other dies because it does not move its tail. In the Western Han dynasty, there was a Jie whose tail was larger than half its body, and without a cart to carry its tail, it cannot move. [Right Seven] The eight delicacies, including dragon and phoenix, these are outside the list of dragon and phoenix. Lychee paired with jiangyao, only boasting of their flavor, they call them the eight delicacies of the northern frontier. The so-called eight delicacies are tihulu, juheng, wild camel hooves, deer lips, camel milk porridge, roasted swan, purple jade juice, and black jade juice. Black jade juice is mayizi. [Right Eight] People from Luan burn large pines, eight hundred miles of barren mountains. There is no Xichao who is brave, who is sad as he crosses the Yishui River. They take pine coal from Luanyang, which is now the capital, two hundred miles from the capital, which is the ancient pine forest, its circumference is ten wei, the locals burn them, covering eight hundred miles. Two camels stand in the snow all day, they do not move even when hungry, one sleeps through the yellow sand day. Meat screens are not enough to compare to. The desert snow is heavy, they order the two camels to sit cross-legged beside them, they do not move all night. They use a broken twig to prop up a piece of felt and sleep, they sleep below, feeling warmer than a meat screen, and their minds remain calm. [Right Ten]

    177

    面花子
    　　本妇人面饰用花子起自唐昭容上官氏所制以掩黥迹大歴已前士大夫妻多妬悍婢妾小不如意辄印面故有月黥钱黥事见酉阳杂爼
    　　竒疾
    　　今上之长公主之驸马刚噶勒藏庆王因坠马得一竒疾两眼黒睛俱无而舌出至胷诸医罔知所措广恵司卿能哲哷鼐叶尔羌人也尝识此证遂剪去之顷间复生一舌亦剪之又于真舌两侧各去一指许却涂以药而愈时元统癸酉也广恵司者回回之为医者隶焉
    　　磨兠鞬
    　　襄州谷城县城门外道傍石人缺剥腹上有字云磨兠鞬慎勿言是亦金人之流也距县四五十里有石人二相偶而立腹上题刻一云已及一云未匝不可得而详也【浮休阅日集】

    Explanation

    177

    Final Translation

    Face Flowers (面花子)

    The practice of using face decorations, known as "face flowers," originated from the creation of Empress Dowager Shangguan of the Tang Dynasty. She made these decorations to conceal her tattoo. Before the Tang period, wealthy and powerful women often marked their servants' faces if they were displeased, leading to the practice of "moon tattoos" and "coin tattoos," as recorded in the Yuè Yáng Zá Zǔ (酉阳杂俎).

    Strange Disease

    The current emperor's eldest princess's husband, Prince Gangqilang, contracted a strange disease after falling off a horse. He had no black pupils in his eyes, and his tongue protruded to his chest. All doctors were at a loss to treat him. The official of the Medical Office, Nengzhe Lenei, a man from Yarkand, recognized the condition and treated it by cutting the abnormal tongue. Soon after, another tongue grew, which he also cut. He then removed about an inch from each side of the real tongue and applied medicine, curing the prince. This occurred in the year 1333. The Medical Office was staffed by Muslim doctors.

    Stone Figures (磨兠鞬)

    Outside the gate of Xuchang City in Xiangzhou, there is a stone figure with a missing belly, on which is inscribed the word "磨兠鞬." It warns, "Do not speak the word '磨兠鞬,' for it is also associated with the Jin people." About 40-50 miles away, there are two stone figures standing together, with inscriptions on their bellies that read "already" and "not yet," making it difficult to understand their full meaning.

    178

    葛大哥
    　　吾乡临海章安镇有蔡木匠者一夕手持斧斤自外归道由东山东山众所殡之处蔡沉醉中将谓抵家扪其棺曰是我榻也寝其上夜半酒醒天且昏黒不可前未免坐以待旦忽闻一人髙呌棺中应云唤我何事彼云某家女病损证盖其后园葛大哥滛之耳却请法师捉我与你同行一观如何棺中云我有客至不可去蔡明日诣主人曰娘子之疾我能愈之主人惊喜许以厚谢因问屋后曾种葛否曰然蔡徧地翻掘内得一根甚巨斫之且有血煑啖女子病即除
    　　万栁堂
    　　京师城外万栁堂亦一宴游处也野云防公一日于中置酒招防斋卢公松雪赵公同饮时歌儿刘氏名解语花者左手折荷花右手执杯歌小圣乐云緑叶隂浓徧池亭水阁偏趂凉多海榴初绽朶朶蹙红罗乳燕穉莺弄语对髙栁鸣蝉相和骤雨过似琼珠乱撒打遍新荷人生百年有几念良辰美景休放虚过富贫前定何用苦张罗命友

    Explanation

    178

    A Brother Named Ge
    In my hometown of Zhang'an Town in Linhai, there was a carpenter named Cai. One evening, he returned home carrying his axe and hatchet. On his way, he passed through the East Mountain, a place where many people were buried. In his drunken state, Cai mistook the coffin for his bed, touched it, and said, "This is my bed." He lay down on it. At midnight, he sobered up and realized that it was still dark outside and he could not proceed further. He had no choice but to sit and wait for dawn. Suddenly, he heard a loud voice from inside the coffin, which replied, "Why did you call me?" Cai said, "A woman from a certain family is ill, and her condition is said to be caused by the man named Ge from the back garden who seduced her. Please ask a priest to catch the ghost." The voice inside the coffin responded, "I have a guest and cannot leave." The next day, Cai went to the owner's house and said, "I can cure the woman's illness." The owner was surprised and offered generous thanks. Cai asked if there had ever been any gourds planted behind the house. The owner replied affirmatively. Cai dug up the ground and found a large gourd root, which he chopped and boiled. He then fed it to the woman, and her illness was immediately cured.

    The Willow Pavilion
    The Willow Pavilion, located outside the city walls of Beijing, is also a place for banquets and entertainment. One day, Wild Cloud invited officials from the Imperial Academy, Lu Gong Songxue, and Zhao Gong to a banquet at the pavilion. At the time, a singer named Liu, whose stage name was Jieyuhua, sang "Xiaoshengle" with a lotus flower in her left hand and a cup in her right. She sang, "The green leaves are dense, covering the pavilions and water towers. The pomegranate flowers are just beginning to bloom, each petal tightly folded like red silk. Young swallows and baby orioles play and chatter among the tall willows, their calls mingling with the buzzing of cicadas. After a sudden rain, it seems as if pearls are scattered randomly, drenching the newly bloomed lotus. In a hundred years of life, how many good times and beautiful sights are there? Do not let them pass without enjoyment. Wealth and poverty are predetermined, so why strive so hard? Invite friends."

    179

    邀賔宴赏饮芳醑浅斟低歌且酩酊从教二轮往来如梭既而行酒赵公喜即席赋诗曰万栁堂前数畆池平铺云锦盖涟漪主人自有沧洲趣游女仍歌白雪词手把荷花来劝酒步随芳草去寻诗谁知只尺京城外便有无穷万里思此诗集中无小圣乐乃小石调曲元遗山先生【好问】所制而名姬多歌之俗以为骤雨打新荷者是也树鸣
    　　金石草木之变异杂见于传记数年来天下扰攘怪事尤甚信前人之书不诬也至正丙申浙西诸郡皆有兵正月嘉兴枫泾镇戴君实门首栁树牛鸣者三主人与仆从悉闻之斩其树不一月苗军抄掠赀产又两月屋燬于兵是嵗寒食日海盐州赵初心率子姓辈诣先垄汛扫松楸忽闻如老作声戞戞不絶审听所在乃是一栢树顷间众树同声和之一二时方止举家惶惑至八月苗军火其居明年六月红军掠货财妇女而侄善如死于难予亲见君实馆賔黄伯成与初心之孙元衡説元衡善如子也其事虽迟速不同而二家之遭祸则一吁诚异哉

    Explanation

    179

    Inviting guests to enjoy a feast, drinking fine wine, sipping slowly and singing softly, becoming slightly intoxicated, letting the sun and moon pass like shuttlecocks. Afterward, Sighoung (趙公) was pleased and composed a poem on the spot: "Before Wanliutang, there are several acres of pond, with clouds and brocade covering the ripples. The host has his own interest in the secluded waters, and the singing girls still sing the White Snow Lyrics. Holding a lotus flower to encourage drinking, walking among fragrant grass to seek poetry. Who would have thought that just a step away from the capital city, there could be thoughts of boundless distances?" This poem is not found in the collection of Xiaoshengle (Small Saint's Music), but is a composition in the Xiaoshidiao (Small Stone Mode) by Yuan Haowen (元遺山先生), known by his courtesy name Hao Wen (好问). It is often sung by famous courtesans, and is commonly referred to as Sudden Rain Hitting New Lotus Leaves (驟雨打新荷).

    In recent years, there have been many strange occurrences, especially in the years of turmoil. The changes in stones, trees, and plants are recorded in various historical texts. In the third year of the Zhizheng era (至正丙申), there were military forces in the western part of Zhejiang. In January, at the entrance of Dai Junshi's house in Fengjing Town, Jiaxing, a willow tree was heard to moo three times. The owner and his servants all heard it, and the tree was cut down. Within a month, the "Miao Army" (苗軍) looted his property. Two months later, his house was destroyed by the army. On the day of Hanshi (寒食日), Zhao Chuxin (趙初心) from Haiyan led his family to visit their ancestral graves, sweeping the pine and cypress trees. Suddenly, they heard a sound like an old heron, continuing without stopping. Upon closer listening, it was found to be a cypress tree. Soon, all the trees around began to make the same sound, lasting for a short while. The whole family was terrified. In August, the "Miao Army" set fire to their house. The following June, the "Red Army" (紅軍) plundered their goods, women, and children, and his nephew Shanru (善如) died in the disaster. I personally heard this from Huang Baocheng (黃伯成), a guest of Dai Junshi, and Yuan Heng (元衡), the grandson of Zhao Chuxin. Yuan Heng is the son of Shanru. Although the timing of these events differs, both families suffered the same fate. Indeed, it is truly strange.

    180

    松江官号
    　　至正丙申正月常熟州防松江府印造官号给散吏兵佩带以防奸伪号之制作画为圆圏绕圏皆火焰圏之内一府字以府印印府字上圏之外四角府官花押民间謡曰满城都是火府官四散躱城里无一人红军府上坐不二月城破悉如所言
    　　割埶
    　　杭州赤山之隂曰箫箕泉黄大痴所尝结庐处其徒弟沈生狎近侧一女道姑同门有欲白之于师沈惧引厨刀自割其埶防死众救得活而疮口流血经月余不合偶问诸阉奴教以煆所割埶捣粉酒服如其言不数日而愈

    Explanation

    180

    Songjiang Official Numbers

    In the first month of the year Bing Shen of the Zhizheng era, the military of Changshu Prefecture printed official numbers for Songjiang Prefecture and distributed them to officials and soldiers to wear, to prevent counterfeiting and deception. The design of the number featured a circle surrounded by flames. Inside the circle was the character "府" (prefecture), stamped with the prefectural seal. At the four corners of the circle were the official's floral signature. The common people sang a rhyme saying, "The whole city is on fire. The officials are scattering and hiding.
