There is no one left in the city. The Red Army is sitting in the prefectural office." Within two months, the city fell, exactly as the rhyme predicted.

    Cutting Off the Genitals

    On the north side of Chishan in Hangzhou is a place called Xiaoji Spring, where Huang Dayi once built a hermitage. His disciple Shen, who was intimate with a female Taoist nun, was discovered by a fellow disciple who wanted to report this to the master. Shen was afraid and used a kitchen knife to cut off his own genitals, nearly dying. The crowd saved him, but the wound continued to bleed for more than a month without healing. He happened to ask a eunuch, who taught him to burn the cut-off part, grind it into powder, and drink it with wine. Following his advice, he recovered within a few days.

    181

    题屏谢客
    　　三寳柱字廷珪色目人颇以才学知名虽湛于酒色而能练达吏事刚正有守为浙省郎中日大书四句于门屛之上曰逆刮蛟龙鳞顺捋虎豹尾若将二伎论尤比于人易其意盖以杜絶人之求请耳然亦隘矣哉终不显达而死于难
    　　婚啓
    　　至元间平原郡公赵氏【与苪】宋福王也其子娶全竹斋少保之女婚啓内一聨云休光蓟北茍安公位之居回首江南惟重母家之念尽有味
    　　陶母碑
    　　陶侃母得古正之道发人伦之本将示教于天下谓朴散俗壊乐溃礼阙有子不教不至于道若失大训不可登于伟望乃求师傅延英茂终日迫于用不欲子郤客俄而车盖载止饩馈并竭茍失其人子将不进计画始成确然独断谓髪可弃训不可失乃金刀既止黰云散怡然无咨嗟之色俨若待賔之具上恐不足以显防下未可谓训子顾其母激忿填膺寸晷是学不迨于至以超圣人之域焕乎贤者之业且礼信仁义君子之事妇人何得而知盖世道大丧其俗已乱故妇人贤者得以行其事千古之下厥行独明当时为

    Explanation

    181

    Translation:

    题屏谢客

    Three Treasures Pillar, courtesy name Tinggui, was a person of the Semu ethnicity and was well-known for his talent and learning. Though deeply immersed in wine and women, he was skilled and experienced in official duties, upright, and principled. As the Deputy Director of the Zhejiang Provincial Office, he inscribed four lines on the screen in front of his door: "Scratching the scales of a dragon against the grain, stroking the tails of tigers and leopards in the opposite direction. If one considers these two skills as being particularly easy to apply to people, the intention is to cut off people's requests and petitions. Yet it is also narrow-minded." He never became prominent and died in a difficult situation.

    婚啓

    During the Zhiyuan period, Zhao of the Pingyuan County was a nobleman and a descendant of the Song dynasty's Prince of Fu. His son married the daughter of the Vice Minister of the Full Bamboo Hall. The wedding announcement included a couplet: "Resting in the light of the north, content with the position of a public official, looking back to the south, only cherishing the memory of the mother's family, all of which is full of meaning."

    陶母碑

    Tao Kan's mother understood the ancient and proper way and revealed the fundamental principles of human relationships, intending to show teaching to the world. She said that simplicity is scattered, and the customs are broken, music is broken, and rituals are lacking. If a child is not taught, they will not reach the way; if one loses the great teaching, they cannot achieve a great reputation. She then sought a teacher and invited distinguished scholars, always pressed by necessity and not wanting her son to reject guests. Soon the carriages stopped, and the food and gifts ran out. If she loses that person, her son will not progress. The plan began to take shape, and she made a firm and decisive decision: "Hair can be discarded, but teaching cannot be lost." Then the golden knife stopped, and the dark clouds dispersed. She was pleased and showed no signs of worry, as if she were preparing for a guest. Above, it is not enough to show protection; below, it cannot be called teaching a son. Considering her mother's anger filling her chest, she learned in every moment, surpassing the realm of sages and shining brightly in the work of the wise. And the matters of propriety, trust, benevolence, and righteousness are the affairs of gentlemen; how can women know them? Because the world's morals have greatly declined, and the customs are already in chaos, therefore, virtuous women can carry out their affairs. In the long history, their actions are uniquely clear.

    182

    人之父为人之母覩斯行闻斯举得不激厉乎茍天下皆如陶母之志则天下皆陶之子也盖人谓子幼而穉不致精训致悖大道乱人纪良可惜哉铭曰髪也者为养之具賔也者致教之英茍非异礼孰能作世之程千载之下如陶之母安可继乎齐英【宗仪】因读唐皇甫持正先生【湜】文集见陶母碑不觉泪数行下追惟先妣拳拳于教子真有陶母之志是故今翰林承防蜕庵张先生【翥】所譔墓铭有曰夫家贫劬力纺绩以给诸子无废学之辞自顾不肖不克勉于学以成令名罪莫大焉谨録于此庶亦可以自惧也
    　　许文懿先生
    　　婺州许白云先生【谦】字益之隐居金华山四十年不入城府著书立言足以垂教后世浙东亷使王公继学访先生于山中谓先生清气逼人可畏既退明日出学行荐于朝有録其举文至者先生方讲説目不一少视其无意于仕宦如此先生殁追諡文懿先生謡言

    Explanation

    182

    A person's father and mother, seeing these actions and hearing these deeds, would not be moved or inspired? If the world were to follow the aspirations of Tao's mother, then everyone would be like Tao's children. Indeed, people often say that young children, being immature, do not receive careful instruction, leading to a deviation from the right path and disorder in social norms, which is truly regrettable. The inscription reads: "Hair is the tool for nourishment, and guests are the essence of education. Without a different ritual, who can set an example for the world? After a thousand years, how can anyone match the example set by Tao's mother?" [Zongyi] Upon reading the collected works of Mr. Huangfu Chizheng (Shi) from the Tang Dynasty, I saw the stele of Tao's mother and could not help but shed several tears. Reflecting on my late mother, who was deeply committed to educating her children, she truly had the aspirations of Tao's mother. Therefore, today, Mr. Zhang Zhi (Zhu) of the Hanlin Academy, who wrote the epitaph, said: "When the family was poor, my mother worked hard at spinning and weaving to support her children, never allowing them to neglect their studies. I, however, am unworthy and unable to strive in my studies to achieve a good name. My greatest sin is this. I carefully record this here, hoping it may serve as a warning to myself."

    Mr. Xu Wenyi
    Mr. Xu Baiyun (Xu Qian) from Wuzhou, with the courtesy name Yizhi, lived in seclusion in Jinhuashan for forty years, never entering the city. He wrote books and established principles that would benefit future generations. The integrity official from Zhejiang, Wang Jixue, visited the master in the mountains and said that the master's clear and pure aura was intimidating. After returning, he recommended the master to the court the next day. Those who recorded his recommendations found that the master was engaged in lectures, never once looking away, showing no interest in officialdom. After the master's death, he was posthumously honored with the title Wenyi and became a subject of popular acclaim.

    183

    后至元丁丒夏六月民间謡言朝廷将采童男女以授鞑靼为奴婢且俾父母防送抵直北交割故自中原至于江之南府县村落凡品官庶人家但有男女年十二三以上便为婚嫁六礼既无片言即合至于巨室有不待车舆亲迎辄徒步以往者盖惴惴焉惟恐使命戾止不可逃也虽守土官吏与夫鞑靼色目之人亦如之竟莫能晓经十余日才息自后
    有贵贱贫富长幼妍丑匹配之不齐者各生悔怨或夫弃其妻或妻憎其夫或讼于官或死于夭此亦天下之大变从古未之闻也吴中僧祖伯号子庭者素称滑稽口占絶句曰一封丹诏未为真三杯淡酒便成亲夜来明月楼头望惟有姮娥不嫁人又有人集古句云翡翠屏风烛影深良宵一刻直千金共君今夜不须睡明日池塘是緑隂可谓深于命意者矣

    Explanation

    183

    In the sixth month of the summer of the year Ding You in the reign of the Yuan Dynasty, rumors spread among the people that the imperial court was planning to select young boys and girls to be given to the Mongols as slaves. They would also require their parents to escort them to the northern border for delivery. Therefore, from the Central Plains down to the south of the Yangtze River, in towns, counties, and villages, regardless of their social status, any boys and girls aged twelve or thirteen and older would be hastily married without the traditional six rites of marriage. Wealthy families, without waiting for carriages and personal escorts, would immediately go on foot to complete the marriage. This was done anxiously, fearing that the imperial mission would arrive and leave no escape. Even local officials and Mongols were affected by the rumor, and no one could understand the reason behind it. After more than ten days, the rumors finally ceased.

    However, in the aftermath, there were those who were mismatched due to their social status, wealth, age, and appearance, leading to various regrets and resentments. Some husbands abandoned their wives, some wives hated their husbands, some sued in court, and some died prematurely. This was also a great change in the world, something unheard of since ancient times. In the Wu region, there was a monk named Zuben, who was always known for his wit. He composed a short poem, saying: "A red imperial edict is not true, three cups of light wine make a couple. At night, looking at the bright moon from the top of the tower, only Chang'e does not marry." Another person collected ancient phrases and said: "The emerald screen casts deep candlelight, a fine night is worth a thousand gold pieces. Spend this night with you without needing to sleep, tomorrow the pond will be green shade." These poems deeply convey their meanings.

    184

    兽医
    　　世以疗马者曰兽医疗牛者曰牛医周礼天官冡宰篇兽医下士八人注兽牛马之属按此则疗牛者亦当曰兽医矣
    　　想肉
    　　天下兵甲方殷而淮右之军嗜食人以小儿为上妇女次之男子又次之或使坐两缸间外逼以火或于銕架上生炙或缚其手足先用沸汤浇泼却以竹帚刷去苦皮或乗夹袋中入巨锅活煑或刲作事件而淹之或男子则止断其双腿妇女则特剜其两乳酷毒万状不可具言总名曰想肉以为食之而使人想之也此与唐初朱粲以人为粮置捣磨寨谓啖醉人如食糟豚者无异固在所不足论唐张鷟朝野佥载云武后时杭州临安尉薛震好食人肉有债主及奴诣临安止于客舎饮之醉并杀之水银和煎并骨销尽后又欲食其妇妇知之逾墙而遯以告县令令诘之具得其情申州録事奏奉勑杖一百而死段成式酉阳

    Explanation

    184

    Veterinarians
    Those who treat horses are called veterinarians, and those who treat cattle are called cattle doctors. According to the Zhouli (Rites of Zhou), in the Tian Guan Zong Zai (Imperial Household) section, there were eight lower-ranking officials responsible for veterinary care. The note states that "兽" (shòu) refers to horses, cattle, and other animals. Thus, those who treat cattle should also be called veterinarians.

    Human Flesh
    In ancient times, when warfare was rampant, soldiers in the Huai River region enjoyed eating human flesh, with children being the best, followed by women, and then men. They would place victims between two pots and burn them from the outside, or roast them alive on an iron frame, or tie their hands and feet and pour boiling water over them, then scrub the burnt skin off with a bamboo brush. They would also place victims in large pots and boil them alive, or cut them into pieces and drown them, or cut off the legs of men and remove the breasts of women. The cruelty was so extreme that it could not be fully described. All these methods were collectively referred to as "想肉" (xiǎng ròu), which is a euphemism for cannibalism. The purpose was to make people think about the food they were eating, implying a form of psychological torture. This practice was not much different from the actions of Zhu Càn, a rebel leader in early Tang dynasty who used human flesh as food and compared it to eating drunk pigs. Such practices were so abhorrent that they did not need further discussion.

    According to the Chao Ye Qian Zai (Miscellaneous Records of the Court and the World) by Zhang Zuo, during the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, the magistrate of Lin'an in Hangzhou, Xue Zhen, enjoyed eating human flesh. A creditor and a slave came to Lin'an, where they were invited to drink and then killed. Their bodies were mixed with mercury and boiled until their bones were completely dissolved. Later, Xue Zhen wanted to eat his wife, who fled over the wall and reported him to the county magistrate. The magistrate interrogated him, and after confirming the facts, the state recorder reported to the emperor, who ordered him to be beaten with a hundred strokes and then executed.

    185

    杂爼云李廓在頴州获火光贼七人前后杀人必食其肉狱具廓问食肉之故其首言某受教于巨盗食人肉者夜入人家必昏沉或有魇不寤者卢氏杂説云唐张茂昭为节镇频吃人肉及除统军到京班中有人问曰闻尚书在镇好人肉虚实笑曰人肉腥而且防争堪吃五代史云苌从蕳家世屠羊从蕳仕至左金吾卫上将军尝歴河阳忠武武宁诸镇好食人肉所至多潜捕民间小儿以食之赵思绾好食人肝及长安城中食尽取妇女幼稚为军粮每犒军辄屠数百人三国志云吴将髙沣好使酒嗜杀人而饮其血日暮必于宅前后掠行人而食之宋庄季裕鸡肋编云自靖康丙午嵗大金入汴盗贼官兵以至居民更互相

    Explanation

    185

    It is said in the Zazu that Li Kuo, a magistrate in Yingzhou, captured seven bandits who were known as "firelight thieves." These bandits had killed people and were known to eat their flesh. After the trial, Li Kuo asked the bandits why they ate human flesh. The leader replied that he had learned this practice from a notorious robber, who taught that those who ate human flesh would enter homes at night and cause drowsiness, sometimes even drugging their victims into unconsciousness. The Lu Shi Zazhuan records that during the Tang dynasty, Zhang Maozhao, a military governor, frequently ate human flesh. When he was promoted to a higher position in the capital, someone asked him if it was true that he had eaten human flesh while in his post. Zhang laughed and said, "Human flesh is both pungent and full of defenses, making it hard to eat." The Five Dynasties History mentions Chang Congjian, whose family had been butchers for generations. Chang rose to the rank of Left Gold Guard General and served in various military posts, including Heyang, Zhongwu, and Wuning. He was known for his fondness for human flesh and often secretly captured young children from the countryside to eat them. Zhao Siwan, a military leader, was known for his preference for human livers. During a siege in Chang'an, when food supplies ran out, he resorted to taking women and children as military provisions. Whenever he rewarded his troops, he would slaughter hundreds of people. The Records of the Three Kingdoms recounts that Wu general Gao Feng loved drinking, enjoyed killing people, and drank their blood. At dusk, he would rob travelers near his home and eat them. The Jili Bian by Zhuang Jiyu notes that since the year Jinchang Bingwu (1126 CE) when the Great Jin invaded Kaifeng, thieves, soldiers, and even residents alternately engaged in similar acts of violence.

    186

    食全躯暴以为腊登州范温率忠义之人泛海到钱唐有持至行在犹食者老瘦男子庾词谓之饶把火妇人少艾者名之下羮羊小儿呼为和骨烂又通目为两脚羊赵与时賔退録云本朝王继勲孝明皇后母弟太祖时屡以罪贬后以右监门卫率府副率分司西京残暴愈甚强市民间子女以备给使小不如意即杀而食之太宗即位会有诉者斩于洛阳又知钦州林千之坐食人肉削籍隶海南嗟夫食人之肉人亦食其肉此兵革间之流惨耳君子所不愿闻者其薛震辈当天下宴安之日而又身为显宦岂无珍羞美膳足以厌其口腹顾乃喜啖人肉是虽人而无人性者矣终至于诛斩窜逐而后已天之报施不亦宜乎

    Explanation

    186

    During the reign of Emperor Taizu, Wang Jixun, the maternal uncle of Empress Xiaoming, was repeatedly demoted for his crimes. Later, he was appointed as the deputy commander of the Right Gate Guard Command in Xijing (Western Capital), and his cruelty became even more severe. He forcibly took children from the common people to serve as attendants, and if they did not please him, he would kill and eat them. When Emperor Taizong ascended the throne, someone reported him, and he was beheaded in Luoyang. Lin Qianzhi, the governor of Qinzhou, was stripped of his rank and exiled to Hainan for eating human flesh.

    Alas, those who eat human flesh are also eaten by others. This is the kind of cruelty seen in times of war, something that gentlemen do not wish to hear. Those like Xue Zhen, in times of peace and stability, yet holding high official positions, surely had delicious and fine dishes enough to satisfy their appetites. But they preferred to eat human flesh, showing that they are human in form but devoid of humanity. They were eventually punished with execution, exile, or banishment. Isn't it fitting that heaven punishes them accordingly?

    187

    王眉叟
    　　王眉叟【夀延】号溪月杭州人出家为道士受知晋邸后以文辅道粹徳真人管领郡之开元宫浙省都事刘君时中【致】者海内名士也既卒贫无以为躬往吊哭周其遗孤举其柩于徳清县与己之夀穴相近春秋祭扫不怠然此事行之于异教中尤不易得钱唐
    　　钱唐二字其来甚逺按史记始皇本纪至云梦浮江下丹阳至钱唐临浙江上会稽立石刻颂秦徳西汉地理志亦有钱唐县今唐字从土则误矣盖以钱易土及捐钱筑塘等事皆傅会之辞自注世説者已然况后世乎

    Explanation

    187

    Wang Meisou
    Wang Meisou, styled Xiyue, was a native of Hangzhou who became a Taoist priest. He was known and favored by the residence of a prince of the Jin state. Later, he was granted the title of "弘文辅道粹德真人" (Promoter of Culture, Supporter of the Way, Virtuous True Person) and was in charge of the Kaiyuan Temple in a prefecture. Liu Shizhong, the official of the Zhejiang province, was a renowned scholar throughout the country. After his death, Liu was poor and had no means to bury him. Wang Meisou personally went to mourn and weep, cared for Liu's orphaned children, carried the coffin, and buried him in Deqing County, near his own burial site. He performed rituals and cleaned the grave regularly, without slackening. Such actions, performed by someone of a different faith, are particularly noteworthy.

    The name "钱唐" (Qiantang) has a long history. According to the Records of the Grand Historian (Shiji), Emperor Qin Shi Huang traveled from Yundu to Yunmeng, floated down the Yangtze River to Danyang, and arrived at Qiantang, where he faced the Qiantang River and ascended to Kuaiji, where he erected a stone inscription to praise the virtues of the Qin dynasty. The Geography of the Western Han Dynasty (Xihan Dili Zhi) also mentions Qiantang County. However, the character "唐" (Tang) is written with the element "土" (earth) in modern times, which is incorrect. It is believed that the name was changed from "钱" (money) to "唐" (Tang) due to various fabricated stories, such as the construction of the Qiantang River embankment. These stories are recorded in various commentaries on the Shiji, and they have persisted through the ages.

    188

    漱芳亭
    　　道士张伯雨【雨】号句曲外史又号贞居尝从王溪月真人入京初燕地未有梅花吴间闲宗师【全节】时为嗣师新从江南移至防以穹庐扁曰漱芳亭伯雨偶造其所恍若与西湖故人遇徘徊既乆不觉熟寝于中真人终日不见伯雨深以为忧意其出外迷失街道也梦觉日已莫矣归道所由嗣师笑曰伯雨素有诗名宜作诗以赎过伯雨遂赋长诗有风沙不惮五千
    里将身跳入仙人壶之句嗣师大喜送翰林集贤尝所往来者学士伯长谢博士敬徳马御史伯庸吴助教养浩虞修譔伯生和之他日伯雨往谒谢诸公惟虞先生全不言儒者事只问道家典故虽荅之或不能详末问能作几家符曰不能先生曰某试书之以质是否连书七十二家伯雨汗流浃背辄下拜曰真吾师也自是托交甚契故与先生书必称弟子焉伯雨杭州人

    Explanation

    188

    Shu Fang Pavilion

    The Daoist priest Zhang Boyu, also known as Guqu Wai Shi and Zhenju, once accompanied the True Man Wang Xiyue to the capital. At that time, there were no plum blossoms in the region of Yan. Wu Jianxian, the Master of Leisure, was a disciple of Quan Jie, who had recently moved from the south to Fang. He named the pavilion "Shu Fang" with a sky tent. One day, Zhang Boyu visited the place and felt as if he had encountered an old friend from West Lake. He wandered there for a long time and eventually fell asleep inside. The True Man did not see him all day and was deeply concerned, thinking he might have gotten lost outside. When Zhang Boyu woke up from his dream, it was already late in the day. He returned and explained what had happened. The disciple laughed and said, "Zhang Boyu has always had a reputation for poetry. You should write a poem to redeem yourself." Zhang Boyu then composed a long poem with lines such as, "Not afraid of the wind and sand for five thousand miles, I jump into the realm of immortals." The disciple was delighted and sent him to the Imperial College, where he had previously been a frequent visitor. There, he met scholars like Xie Zongyi, who praised his work. Later, Zhang Boyu visited Xie Zongyi and the others, but only Yu Xiansheng did not speak about Confucian matters; instead, he asked about Daoist lore. Although Yu Xiansheng sometimes could not provide detailed answers, he finally asked if Zhang Boyu could write several talismans. Zhang Boyu replied, "I cannot." Yu Xiansheng said, "Let me try writing them and see if they are correct." He wrote seventy-two talismans, and Zhang Boyu was so impressed that he was drenched in sweat and immediately knelt down, saying, "Truly, you are my teacher!" From then on, they became very close, and Zhang Boyu always addressed Yu Xiansheng as his master in his letters. Zhang Boyu was from Hangzhou.

    189

    食品有名
    　　水之咸淡相交处产河豚河豚鱼也无鳞颊常怒气满腹形殊弗雅然味极佳煑治不精则能杀人所以东坡先生在资善堂与人谈河豚之美云据其味真是消得一死浙西惟江隂人尤珍之每春首初出时必用羞祭品毕然后作羮而邻里间互相餽送以为礼腹中之膟曰西施乳夫西施一美妇也岂乳亦异于人耶顾千载而下乃使人道之不置如此则夫差之亡国非偶然矣若鲚鱼子名螳螂子及松江之上海杭州之海寜人皆喜食蟛蜞螯名曰鹦哥嘴以有极红者似之故也二物象形而云又非西施乳之比矣按编鱼部引博雅云鯸防【盈之反】鲀也背青腹白触物即怒其肝杀人正今人名为河豚者也然则豚当为鲀
    　　火灾
    　　至正辛巳莫春之初江浙行省平章政事济尔噶岱入城之任之日衣红儿童謡曰火殃来矣至四月十九日杭州灾燬官民房屋公寺观一万五千七百五十五间烧死七十四人明年壬午四月一日又灾尤甚于先自昔所未有也数百年浩繁之地日就凋实基于此
    　　落水兰亭

    Explanation

    189

    Famous Foods

    Water where freshwater and saltwater meet produces the pufferfish, a type of fish without scales. Its cheeks are often puffed up, its belly full of anger, and its appearance is not particularly elegant. Yet, its taste is exquisite. If not cooked properly, it can be fatal. Therefore, when Mr. Su Dongpo was in the Ziyantang, he spoke of the beauty of the pufferfish with others, saying that according to its taste, it is truly worth dying for. In the western part of Zhejiang, only people in Jiangyin particularly value it. Every time the pufferfish first appears in the spring, a sacrificial offering is made, and then a stew is prepared, which is exchanged as a gift among neighbors. The internal organ of the pufferfish is called "West Beauty's Milk." Xi Shi was a beautiful woman, so is her milk also different from that of ordinary people? Through the centuries, people have been discussing it in this way, so King Fuchai’s downfall was not accidental. As for the roe of the river catfish, it is called "Grasshopper’s Eggs." People in Songjiang, Shanghai, and Hai Ning, Hangzhou, all enjoy eating the claws of the Chinese mitten crab, which are called "Parrot’s Beaks" because of their red color. The two foods are named after their appearance, but they are not comparable to "West Beauty's Milk." According to the Fishing Department in the Classic of Fish, it is said that "Tun" (the pufferfish) has a blue back and white belly, and it becomes angry when touched, and its liver is poisonous. This is what people today call the pufferfish. Therefore, "Tun" should be called "Tun."

    Fire Disaster

    In the early spring of the year Xinsi during the reign of the Hongwu Emperor, Jielga Dadi, the Chancellor of the Jiang-Zhe Provincial Administration, entered the city on a day when children were wearing red clothes. They sang a song: "The fire disaster is coming!" On April 19th, a fire broke out in Hangzhou, destroying 15,755 rooms of official and civilian houses, temples, and pagodas, and killing 74 people. The following year, in the year Renwu, on April 1st, another fire broke out, even more severe than the first. This was unprecedented in history. A place that had been prosperous for centuries began to decline, and this was the foundation of its decline.

    The Drowned Orchid Pavilion

    (Translation for this section is not provided in the source text, so it remains blank.)

    190

    予尝见落水兰亭一卷乃五字不损本今吴中分湖陆氏所藏而赵彛斋之物也彛斋宋宗室子讳孟坚字子固彛斋其自号居嘉兴之广戌酷嗜古法书名画能作墨花于水仙尤长此帖姜白石旧藏后归霅川俞夀翁彛斋复从夀翁易得喜甚乗夜回櫂至升山大风覆舟行李皆渰溺无余彛斋立浅水中手持此帖示人曰兰亭在此余不足介吾意也因题八字于卷云性命可轻至寳是保
    　　隂府辩词
    　　李子昭者松江府提控案牍李宗庆子也侧室刁氏有娠妻怒之棰挞苦楚昼夜不息数次自经与溺以省觉不得死窃自念曰我苦就蓐亦必死耳等死何自求早死之为幸因多食海蛰与冷水胎既落血上充心而身遂亡不数日怪百出妻得竒疾宛若死者但只心胷防温支体不僵其家就床褥作一窍任其便溺时以少饮纳口中辄咽不与亦不言饥经三年余形骸枯槁无复生理家人益厌之一夕忽诣舅姑所扣寝室戸舅姑曰汝恶得至此必为矣曰妾已复生实非也愿见舅姑具告所然舅姑惊恐呼家人悉起取火烛之果此病躯及觇其卧榻已空始

    Explanation

    190

    I have seen a copy of the Lantingji Xu (Preface to the Poems Composed at the Orchid Pavilion) that was a five-character unaltered version, which is now held by the Lu family of Fenhu Lake in Wu and was once the property of Zhao Yizhai. Zhao Yizhai was a son of the Song imperial clan, whose given name was Mengjian and style name was Ziguo. Yizhai was his self-chosen name, and he resided in Guangxu of Jiaxing. He was deeply fond of ancient calligraphy and famous paintings, and he was especially skilled at painting ink flowers, particularly water lilies. This scroll was formerly owned by Jiang Bai Shi and later passed on to Yu Shou Gong of Zhejiang. Zhao Yizhai obtained it from Yu Shou Gong and was very pleased. He returned by boat at night to Sheng Mountain, but a strong wind capsized the boat, and all the luggage was drowned without a trace. Zhao Yizhai stood in shallow water, holding this scroll and saying to others, "The Lantingji Xu is here; nothing else matters to me." He then inscribed eight characters on the scroll: "Life can be light, but the precious treasure must be protected."

    Defense Before the Underworld

    Li Zizhao was the son of Li Zongqing, who was in charge of case records in Songjiang Prefecture. His concubine Diao was pregnant, and his wife was angry and beat her cruelly, without rest day or night. She attempted suicide multiple times by hanging and drowning, but failed to die. She secretly thought to herself, "I will surely die in childbirth anyway; why not seek an early death?" She ate a lot of jellyfish and cold water, and after the fetus fell, blood rushed to her heart and she died. In a few days, ghosts and strange phenomena appeared. His wife fell ill with a strange disease, appearing as if she were dead, but only her chest and abdomen were warm, while her limbs were not stiff. The family made a hole in the bedding for her to relieve herself, and when they gave her a little drink, she would swallow it, but she did not speak of hunger. After three years, her body became emaciated and showed no signs of recovery. The family grew increasingly disgusted, and one night she suddenly went to her in-laws' room and knocked on the door. Her in-laws said, "How can you be here? You must be a ghost!" She replied, "I have come back to life; I am not a ghost. I wish to see my in-laws and explain everything." Her in-laws were terrified and called all the family to light a fire. When they saw her ill body and realized her bed was empty, they were shocked.

    191

    信之因问其详曰妾为亡婢诉寃摄至隂府即今岳祠也命妾与婢对词妾以汝懐孕时打骂则或有之然未尝令汝吞药损堕婢仇执甚坚妾不得白遂招承枷禁幽圄中日得小叔以饼饵粥饭之相餽故不馁今复得送妾还入门弄其儿戱挞之一下儿哭遂推妾置灶上即梦觉者但觉怠倦故勉强至此舅姑曰汝既被禁何自得释曰防上帝有赦故也急呼小郎妻问之曰适间儿子惊啼云梦见乃父击其首小郎盖提控之次子泰甫先为其妺夫金可大所杀者此妇至今强徤与夫见寓府城西郭又复生两子矣志怪古或多然漫书于此以为世之妬妇劝诗法

    Explanation

    191

    Xin Zhi then asked for details, and I, a deceased servant, was appealing for justice, taken to the underworld (now known as the Yue Temple), and ordered to confront the servant in court. I admitted that during your pregnancy, I might have scolded you, but I never made you take medicine that could harm the fetus. The servant's accusation was very strong, and I could not prove my innocence, so I was forced to confess and was locked up in a dark prison. Each day, my younger brother brought me food like bread, cakes, and porridge, so I did not starve. Now, I am returned and allowed to enter the house to play with my child, but when I lightly hit him, he cries, so I was pushed onto the stove, and upon waking, I found myself there. I only feel tired, so I am struggling to get here. My husband and mother-in-law said, "Since you were imprisoned, how did you get released?" I said, "Because the Heavenly Emperor had a pardon." They quickly called the younger son's wife to ask, "Just now, our son was startled and cried, saying he dreamed that your father hit his head." The younger son, who is the second son of the magistrate, was previously killed by his sister-in-law's husband, Jin Keda. This woman is still healthy and lives with her husband in the western suburbs of the city, and they have had two more children. This strange story is recorded here as a warning to women who are jealous and prone to gossip.

    192

    赵魏公云作诗用虚字殊不佳中两聨填满方好出处才使唐已下事便不古
    　　姓名者
    　　庄绰鸡肋编云太史公作伯夷传但云伯夷叔齐孤竹君之二子也而论语音注引春秋少阳篇谓伯夷姓墨名允一名元字公信叔齐名智字公达夷齐諡也陆徳明取之不知少阳篇何人所着今世犹有此书否吾衍闲居録云孤竹君姓墨【音眉】名台【音怡】初见孔丛子注中子名伯辽见周昙咏史诗注伯当作仲若如吾説则伯夷叔齐似又是名非諡矣
    　　女谏买印

    Explanation

    192

    Zhaohui Gong said that using empty words in poetry is particularly bad, and filling the middle two couplets is better. If one uses events from the Tang Dynasty or later, the work will not seem ancient.

    Names:

    Zhuang Chuo's Jile Bian (Chicken Rib Compilation) states that Sima Qian, in his biography of Bo Yi, merely says that Bo Yi and Shu Qi were the two sons of the Duke of Guzhu. The commentary on the Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) cites the Chunqiu Shaoyang Pian (Spring and Autumn Shaoyang Chapter), which states that Bo Yi's surname was Mo, his given name was Yu, and his courtesy name was Gongxin. Shu Qi's given name was Zhi, and his courtesy name was Gongda. The posthumous titles of Bo Yi and Shu Qi are also mentioned. Lü Deming adopted this view, but it is unclear who wrote the Chunqiu Shaoyang Pian. Does such a book still exist today? Wu Yan's Xianju Lu (Idle Notes) states that the Duke of Guzhu's surname was Mo (pronounced as "mei"), and his name was Tai (pronounced as "yi"). This is first seen in the commentary on Kong Congzi (Confucius' Followers) and in Zhou Tan's Yong Shi Shi (Elegies on History). If one follows my explanation, then Bo Yi and Shu Qi seem to be names rather than posthumous titles.

    A Woman Advises on the Purchase of a Seal.

    193

    淮海龚翠岩先生开寓吴门日一僧权道衡者颇聪慧识道理先生与之游偶市肆粥汉印一颗权尝酬价归取镪先生适见主人以实告遂用十五缗买之语诸女女曰大人乃亦夺人所好先生惊悟即持送权曰先生爱而收藏奚以赠曰在彼犹在此也权固辞曰在彼犹在此也相让乆之沉诸渊而别吁若先生者可谓善矣孰谓异端中有此哉然先生之女尤可敬也
    　　吴江塔颠箭
    　　吴江华严寺浮图之颠望之二矢着其上簳羽宛然可辨相传宋南渡初金人尼玛哈乗快一发而中又贾似道出督时祝矢自誓亦中焉故留题者有至今塔杪留遗迹犹是元戎金仆姑之句大徳庚子其寺主僧善信大修浮图更其颠而新之视向二矢实圆鐡条二交贯横亘盖必昔人以示辅颠且以防鹳鹘之巢故耳传者所谓大妄也且着此以祛后世之惑长乐郭徳基尝有华严塔頴辨疑行于时盖郭尝官此州目击其非
    　　素领
    　　项后白髪曰素领汉冯唐白首为郎官素髪垂领

    　　辍耕録卷九
    <子部,小说家类,杂事之属,辍耕录>

    Explanation

    193

    淮海的龚翠岩先生在吴门开设寓所时，有一位名叫权道衡的僧人，他非常聪明，懂得道理。龚先生与他交往，有一天在市肆中买到一枚汉代印章，权道衡曾经用钱买下这枚印章。龚先生恰好看到主人如实告知，于是用十五缗钱买下了它，并告诉了女儿们。女儿们说：“父亲竟然也夺人所爱。”龚先生惊醒过来，立即把印章送给权道衡，说：“先生爱而收藏，为何要送？”权道衡坚决推辞，说：“在彼犹在此也。”两人互相推让了很久，最后将印章沉入深渊后告别。唉，像龚先生这样的人可以说是善人了，谁说异端中没有这样的人呢？然而龚先生的女儿们尤其值得尊敬。

    吴江塔顶的箭
    吴江华严寺佛塔的顶端，可以看到两支箭插在上面，箭杆和羽毛清晰可见。相传在宋朝南渡初期，金人尼玛哈一箭射中；又说贾似道出征时，自己发誓也射中了。因此，有人题诗说：“至今塔杪留遗迹，犹是元戎金仆姑之句。”大德庚子年，寺主僧善信大修佛塔，重新修建了塔顶，看那两支箭实际上是两根圆形铁条交叉贯穿。这必是古人用来辅助塔顶，并防止鹳鸟筑巢的缘故。传说中的“大妄”之说，且以此消除后世的疑惑。长乐的郭德基曾经写过《华严塔頴辨疑》，在当时流传。因为郭德基曾经在该州任职，亲眼目睹了事实。

    素领
    项后的白发称为素领。汉代的冯唐白首为郎官，白发垂领。

    194

    钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷十　　　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰御史五常
    　　周景逺先生驰名能文为南台御史时分治过浙省每日与朋友往复其书吏不乐似有举刺之意大书壁上曰御史某日访某人某日某人来访御史忽见呼谓曰我尝又访某人汝乃失记何也第补书之因复谓曰人之所以读书为士君子者正欲为五常主张也使我今日谢絶故旧是为御史而无一常寜不为御史不可灭人理吏赧服而退
    　　官仓入粟
    　　今官府收敛秋粮之际比先涓吉啓仓于青龙方敖房入粟六石六斗六升六合以应日盖国家初无定制不知各处何以一皆如此予意必取上下四方六合之意耳
    　　食物相反
    　　凡食河豚者一日内不可服汤药恐内有荆芥盖与此物大相反亦恶乌头附子之属予在江阴时亲见一儒者因此防命其子尤不可食能使人胀死尝水寖试之经宿颗大如芡实世传中其毒者亟饮物乃解否则必亡又闻不必用此以龙脑浸水或至寳丹或橄榄皆可解后得一方用槐花防炒过与干燕支各等分同捣粉水调灌大妙

    Explanation

    194

    The Complete Library of the Four Treasuries
    Records of Ceasing Plowing, Volume Ten
    Yuan Dynasty, by Tao Zongyi
    The Five Constants of the Censor

    Mr. Zhou Jingyuan was renowned for his literary skills and served as a Southern Platform Censor. When he was assigned to govern Zhejiang Province, he would frequently visit friends each day. His clerks were unhappy with the frequent back-and-forth and seemed to have some grievances. They wrote on the wall: "The Censor visited a certain person on a certain day, and on another day, a certain person visited the Censor." Suddenly, Mr. Zhou saw this and called out, saying, "I once visited another person, and you failed to remember. Why is that? Please add it to the record." He then said, "The reason people read books and become scholars is to uphold the five constants. If I were to sever ties with old friends today, I would be a Censor without one of the five constants. It is better not to be a Censor at all, as it would destroy human relationships." The clerks were ashamed and left.

    Official Granary Grain Storage
    When official granaries collect autumn grain, they first choose a favorable day to open the granary in the Blue Dragon Square and Tiger Room, storing six shi, six dou, six he, and six he of grain to correspond with the day. The state did not have a fixed custom initially, but it is unknown why all places followed this practice. I believe it must be a reference to the six directions (up, down, east, west, south, north).

    Food Incompatibilities
    Those who eat pufferfish should not take herbal medicine within a day, as it may contain schizonepeta, which is highly incompatible with pufferfish. They also avoid aconite and fuzi. When I was in Jiangyin, I personally witnessed a scholar who, due to this, avoided his son from eating pufferfish, as it could cause fatal bloating. I once tested this by soaking the pufferfish in water overnight, and the grains became as large as water caltrop seeds. It is said that those who are poisoned by pufferfish should immediately drink camphor water or the Precious Pill, or even olives, to counteract the poison. Otherwise, they will surely die. Later, I learned a remedy that involves frying sophora flower powder and dried red rose petals in equal parts, grinding them into a powder, and mixing with water to administer. This method is very effective.

    195

    先辈谐谑
    　　赵魏公刻私印曰水晶宫道人钱唐周草窓先生宻以玛瑙寺行者属比之魏公遂不用此印后见先生同郡崔进之药肆悬一牌曰养生主药室乃以敢死军医人对之进之亦不复设此牌魏公语人曰吾今日方为水晶宫吐气矣先辈虽谐谑自是可喜
    　　马判
    　　冯公士啓【梦弼】尝言为八番云南宣慰司令史曰尝因公差抵一站日已莫矣站吏告曰今夜马判上岸麻线须暂停驿程以避之问其故闭目揺手不敢言公怒便上马行数十里至大溪忽见一物如屋所谓乌剌赤者下马跪泣若告诉状呼问何为亦闭目揺手弗荅于是下马祝之曰某许昌人窃禄来此茍天命合尽尔其啖之否则容我行祝毕即转入溪中腥风臭雾触人口鼻既而各上马比曙抵前站站吏惊曰是何麻线大胆若是耶公问此为何物始敢言曰马蟥精也麻线方言曰官人乌剌赤站之牧马者公官至礼部尚书

    Explanation

    195

    Amusing Tales of the Elders

    Zhao Wei Gong had a private seal inscribed with the words "The Hermit of the Crystal Palace," and he asked Zhou Caowang, a fellow townsman from Qiantang, to compare it to another seal inscribed with "The Wanderer of the Agate Temple." Zhou Caowang did so, and Zhao Wei Gong did not use the seal. Later, Zhao Wei Gong saw a sign in the pharmacy of Cui Jinzhi, his fellow townsman, which read "Master of Nourishing Life's Medicine Chamber." In response, he hung a sign that read "Doctor of the Brave Soldiers." Cui Jinzhi then removed the sign. Zhao Wei Gong told others, "Today I finally can breathe for the Crystal Palace." The elders' playful stories are indeed delightful.

    The Water Judge

    Feng Gongshi Qi once said that while serving as the commandant of the Pacification Commission for the Eight Regions of Yunnan, he was sent on a mission to a station. By the time he arrived, it was already late. The station official told him, "Tonight there is a Water Judge who has come ashore. The horse herder must stop the relay service and avoid it." When asked why, the official closed his eyes and shook his head, refusing to speak. Enraged, Feng Gongshi Qi mounted his horse and rode for dozens of miles until he reached a large stream. Suddenly, he saw a creature as large as a house, known as an "Ularach," which is a horse herder. He dismounted, knelt, and cried as if telling a story. He called out, "What is this?" but the creature also closed its eyes and shook its head, not answering. Feng Gongshi Qi then dismounted and made a vow, saying, "I am from Xuchang and have come here to serve. If it is my fate to die, then let it eat me. Otherwise, allow me to continue my journey." After the vow, he turned and entered the stream. A foul wind and stench touched his nose and mouth. Eventually, they each remounted their horses and arrived at the previous station by dawn. The station official was shocked and asked, "What kind of horse herder is so bold?" Feng Gongshi Qi asked what the creature was, and the official finally dared to say, "It was a Water Leech." The term "horse herder" is a local dialect for "official of the relay station." Feng Gongshi Qi later became a high-ranking official in the Ministry of Rites.

    196

    字训
    　　善字训多字诗载驰女子善懐郑笺善犹多也汉书岸善崩善亦多也
    　　丘真人
    　　大宗师长春真人姓丘氏名处机字通宻号长春子登州栖霞县濵都里人也祖父业农世称善门金皇统戊辰正月十九日生生而聪敏有日者相之曰此子当为神仙宗伯大定丙戌年十九辞亲居昆仑山依道者脩真丁亥谒重阳全真开化王真君【嘉】于海寜请为弟子戊申召见阙下随还终南山贞祐乙亥太祖平燕城金主奔汴丙子复召不起己夘居莱州时鲁齐入宋宋遣使来召亦不起是年五月太祖自乃蛮国遣近侍刘仲禄持一手诏致聘十一月至所诏文云制曰天厌中原骄华大极之性朕居北野嗜欲莫生之情反朴还淳去奢从俭每一衣一食与牛

    Explanation

    196

    Translation:

    Character Explanations
    "Shan" (善) in the character explanations often means "good" or "abundant." For example, in the poem "Zai Chi" (载驰) from the Book of Songs (Shijing), it says "The woman who rides in the carriage has a good heart," and Zheng Xuan (郑笺) explains that "shan" means "abundant." In the Book of Han (Han Shu), it says, "When the good shore collapses, good still abounds," which is a metaphorical expression.

    The True Lord Qiu
    The Grand Master, the True Lord Qiu, is a Taoist master. His surname is Qiu, his given name is Chuji (处机), his courtesy name is Tongmi (通密), and his title is "Changchunzi" (长春子). He was born on the nineteenth day of the first month in the year Wuchen (戊辰) of the Huantong era (皇统) of the Jin Dynasty. He was born in Bin Du Village (濵都里) in Qixia County (栖霞县), Dengzhou (登州). His grandfather was a farmer, and the family was known for their good deeds. From a young age, he was intelligent, and a fortune-teller predicted that he would become a great Taoist master.

    In the year Bingxu (丙戌) of the Dading era (大定) of the Jin Dynasty, at the age of nineteen, he left his parents and went to live in the Kunlun Mountains to practice Taoism. In the year Dinghai (丁亥), he visited the True Lord Wang of the Full Truth School of the Chongyang Sect in Hai Ning (海寜) and requested to become his disciple. In the year Wu Shen (戊申), he was summoned to the imperial court and then returned to the Zhongnan Mountains.

    In the year Yihai (乙亥) of the Zhenyou era (贞祐) of the Jin Dynasty, the founding emperor of the Yuan Dynasty conquered Yan City, and the Jin emperor fled to Bian (汴). In the year Bingzi (丙子), he was summoned again but did not go. In the year Jima (己夘), while residing in Laizhou (莱州), when Lu and Qi entered the Song Dynasty, the Song emperor sent envoys to summon him, but he did not go.

    In the eleventh month, he arrived at the place where the imperial edict was written, which said, "Heaven is weary of the arrogance and luxury of the Central Plains. I reside in the northern wilderness, free from desires, returning to simplicity and frugality, living a life of simplicity and thrift, every meal and garment like a cow."

    197

    竪马圉共同飨视民如赤子养士若兄弟谋素和思素畜练万众以身人之先临百阵无念我之后七载之中成大业六合之内为一统非朕之行有徳盖金之政无恒是以受天之佑获承至尊南连赵宋北接回纥东夏西夷悉称臣佐念我单于国千载百世以来未之有也然而任大守重治平犹惧有阙且夫刳舟剡楫将欲济江河也聘贤选佐将以安天下也朕践祚已来勤心庶政而三九之位未见其人访闻丘师先生体真履规博物洽闻探穷理道冲徳着懐古君子之肃风抱真上人之雅操乆栖岩谷藏身形阐祖宗之遗化坐致有道之士云集仙迳莫可称数自干

    Explanation

    197

    In all governance, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    198

    戈而后伏知先生犹山东旧境朕心仰懐无已岂不闻渭水同车茅庐三顾之事奈何山川悬濶有失躬迎之礼朕但避位侧身斋戒沐浴选差近侍官刘仲禄备轻骑素车不逺千里谨邀先生暂屈仙歩不以沙漠悠逺为念或以忧民当世之务或以恤朕保身之术朕亲侍仙座钦惟先生将咳唾之余但授一言斯可矣今者聊发朕之防意万一明于诏章诚望先生既着大道之端要善无不应亦岂违众生之愿哉故兹诏示惟宜知悉五月初一日笔庚辰正月北行二月至燕欲驾回朝谒仲禄今从官哈喇驰奏真人进表陈情表曰登州栖霞县志道丘处机近奉宣防逺召

    Explanation

    198

    In the name of the emperor,

    Knowing that Mr. [name] still resides in the old territory of Shandong, my heart continues to look up and cherish him. How can one not remember the stories of Liu Bei meeting Zhuge Liang at his thatched cottage on the Wei River? How can one fail to greet someone personally due to the vast distance between us? I will step aside and prepare myself through fasting and purification, selecting the trusted official Liu Zhonglu to prepare a light horse and simple carriage, traveling thousands of miles to respectfully invite you to visit me. Do not consider the vastness of the desert. Whether it is to help me with the affairs of the state or to advise me on how to preserve my health, I will personally attend to your seat with reverence, hoping that you will impart even a single word. Now I will briefly express my thoughts, hoping that they will be clear in the imperial edict. I sincerely hope that you will share the beginning of the great way, which will surely bring good results and not go against the wishes of the people.

    Therefore, this imperial edict is hereby issued, and it is hoped that you will be informed.

    On the fifth day of the first month, penning this on the first day of the fifth month of the year Gengchen, I have traveled north and arrived in Yan in the second month, waiting for the emperor's return to pay my respects. Liu Zhonglu, my attendant, has rushed to present this table and petition, which says:

    "Mr. Qiu Chuyi, a Daoist from the county of Qixia in Dengzhou, has recently been summoned far away."

    Translation of the petition:

    "Mr. Qiu Chuyi, a Daoist from the county of Qixia in Dengzhou, has recently been summoned far away."

    199

    不才海上居民心皆恍惚处机自念谋生太拙学道无成辛苦万端老而不死名虽播于诸国道不加于众人内顾自伤情谁测前者南京及宋国屡召不从今者龙庭一呼即至何也伏闻皇帝天赐勇智今古絶伦道恊威灵华夷率服是故便欲投山窜海不忍相违且当冐雪冲霜图其一见盖闻车驾只在桓抚之北及到燕京听得车驾遥逺不知其几千里风尘澒洞天气苍黄老弱不堪切恐中途不能到得假之皇帝所则军国之事非己所能道徳之心令人戒欲悉为难事遂与宣差刘仲禄商议不若且在燕京徳兴府等处盘桓住坐先令人前去奏知其刘仲禄不从故不

    Explanation

    199

    As an unworthy resident of the sea, my heart is filled with confusion. Reflecting on my situation, I realize that I am too clumsy at making a living and have failed to achieve success in the Dao, enduring countless hardships. Though I am old and still alive, my fame has spread among various countries, but the Dao has not benefited the common people. Looking inward, I feel self-pity; who can understand my feelings? Before, when Nanjing and the Song repeatedly invited me, I did not comply. Now, when the Dragon Court calls, I come immediately. Why is this so? I have heard that the Emperor is endowed with courage and wisdom by heaven, surpassing all others in ancient and modern times. His Dao is in harmony with the divine power, and the Chinese and foreigners all submit. Therefore, I wish to hide in the mountains and escape to the sea, but cannot bear to disobey. I will brave the snow and frost, striving to see him once. I have heard that the Emperor's carriage is only north of Huanfu, and upon reaching Yanjing, I learned that the Emperor's carriage is far away, perhaps thousands of miles distant. The road is filled with dust and turmoil, the weather is bleak, and the elderly and weak cannot endure it. I fear that I may not reach the Emperor halfway. If I were to approach the Emperor, military and state affairs are beyond my ability, and the heart of virtue and morality makes one abstain from desires, all of which are difficult matters. I then discussed with Xuancha Liu Zhonglu, and it would be better to stay in Yanjing, Dexing Prefecture, and other places for a while, first sending someone to report beforehand. Liu Zhonglu did not agree, so I did not.

    200

    免自纳奏帖念处机肯来归命逺冐风霜伏望皇帝早下寛大之诏详其可否兼同时四人出家三人得道惟处机虚得其名顔色憔顇形容枯槁伏望圣裁龙儿年三月日奏十月哈喇回复奉防曰青吉斯皇帝真人丘师省所奏应召而来者具悉惟师道逾三子徳重多方命臣奉厥纁驰传访诸沧海时与愿适天不人违两朝屡召而弗行单使一邀而肯起谓朕天啓所以身归不辞暴露于风霜自愿防渉于沙碛书章来上喜慰何言军国之事非朕所期道徳之心诚云可尚朕以彼酋不逊我伐用张单旅试临边陲底定来从去背实力率之故然乆逸暂劳冀心服而后

    Explanation

    200

    The petitioner submits this memorial, expressing gratitude for the emperor's willingness to accept his submission, despite enduring harsh conditions. He humbly requests that the emperor issue a lenient decree and carefully consider his proposal. He notes that while four people became monks and three attained enlightenment, he has only gained a reputation without substance, and his appearance reflects his hardships. He hopes for the emperor's wise judgment.

    On the third day of the third month in the year of Long'er, he submitted the memorial. In October, in the place of Hala, he received the emperor's response, which read:

    "Emperor Genghis Khan acknowledges the submission of the Taoist master Qiu Shisheng. Those who have responded to the call are known. His teachings surpass those of three sons, and his moral character is multifaceted. The emperor commanded that I bear the ceremonial robe and ride swiftly to search for him in the vast ocean. The timing and circumstances were favorable, and his actions were in harmony with the will of heaven. Despite being summoned by two emperors and not responding, he agreed to come after being invited by a single envoy. The emperor believes it was heaven's will for him to return, despite enduring harsh conditions. He voluntarily traveled through deserts. The emperor is pleased and relieved upon receiving his submission. The emperor does not expect him to be involved in military or state affairs, but values his moral and ethical qualities. The emperor has ordered a military expedition against a rebellious leader. The region has been pacified, and the people have followed or defected. The emperor hopes that the people will eventually submit to his rule after a period of hardship."

    201

    已于是载威徳畧驻车徒重念云轩既发于蓬莱驭可游于天竺逹磨东迈元印法以传心老氏西行或化人而成道顾川途之虽濶瞻几杖以非遥爰荅来章可明朕意秋暑师比平安好防不多及十四日辛巳十一月至赛玛尔堪城壬午三月过鐡门闗四月逹行在所时上在雪山之阳舎馆定入见上劳曰他国徴聘皆不应今逺逾万里而来朕甚嘉焉赐坐就食设二帐于御幄之东以居之约日问道以回纥叛亲征不果至九月设庭燎虚前席延问至道真人大略荅以节欲保躬天道好生恶杀治尚无为清净之理上説命左史书诸防癸未乞东还赐号神仙爵大宗师

    Explanation

    201

    Having already demonstrated my power and virtue, I briefly halted the troops and pondered that my cloud chariot had departed from Penglai, and I could travel to Tianzhu with ease. Bodhidharma's eastward journey and the transmission of the Dharma through the seal of the mind, as well as Laozi's western journey and the transformation of people into the path of enlightenment, all came to mind. Considering the vast distance of the journey, yet the distance is not far when looking at the staff and cane. I responded to the incoming letter to clarify my intention. The teacher is safe and well during the autumn heat, with no major concerns. On the fourteenth day of the eleventh month of the year Xinsi, I arrived at the city of Saimarkan. In the third month of the year Renwu, I passed through the Iron Gate Pass. In April, I reached the imperial court. At that time, the emperor was in the south of the snowy mountains. I arranged accommodation and met the emperor, who said that he had not responded to invitations from other countries. Now coming from afar, I am very pleased. The emperor granted a seat, provided food, and set up two tents to the east of the imperial tent for accommodation. We arranged a date to ask questions about the Way, but the campaign against the Uighur rebellion did not materialize. In September, the emperor set up a court fire, cleared the front seat, and invited the true sage of the Way. The conversation roughly revolved around self-restraint and preserving one's body, emphasizing that the heavenly way favors life and dislikes killing, and that governance should follow the principle of non-action and purity. The emperor was pleased and ordered the Left Historian to record the conversation. On the day of Guiwei, the visitor requested to return east, and the emperor bestowed the title of Immortal, Grand Master, and Supreme Teacher.

    202

    掌管天下道教甲申三月至燕八月奉防居太极宫丁亥五月特改太极为长春七月九日留颂而逝年八十至元己巳正月防诏赠五祖七真徽号而曰长春演道主教真人已上见蟠溪集鸣道集西游记风云庆防録七真年谱等书初真人自行在归道由宣徳日一富家新居落成礼致下顾将冀一言以为福既入其室黙然无语輙以所持鐡拄杖于窓戸墙壁上颇毁数处而出主人再拜希解悟曰尔屋完矣美矣完而必毁理执然也吾不尔毁尔将无以图厥终今毁矣尔宜思其毁而欲完克保全之则尔与尔子子孙孙庶几歌斯哭斯永终弗替主人説服吁真人真知道哉
    　　南池鼃
    　　宋季城信州掘土处为濠百畆许在郡南曰南池池之旁可居旧为里人屋归附后逹噜噶齐玛察据有其地每春夏之交羣鼃聒耳寝食不安会三十八代天师张广微【与材】朝京回因以告天师朱书符新瓦上使人投池中戒之曰汝鼃母再喧自是至今寂然鴈子
    　　汉书太液池中鳬雏鴈子布满充积用鴈子甚佳王维诗芦笋穿荷叶菱花罥鴈儿又新

    Explanation

    202

    The overseer of the Daoist religion from the third month of the year Jia Shen to the eighth month of Yan, residing in the Taiji Palace under the protection of Fengfang, in the fifth month of Ding Hai, specially changed the name of Taiji to Changchun. On the ninth day of the seventh month, he left a eulogy and passed away at the age of eighty. In the first month of Ji Si in the reign of Emperor Zhongyuan, an imperial edict was issued to bestow honorific titles on the Five Ancestors and Seven Truths, and he was posthumously named "Changchun, the Chief Priest who Expounds the Dao and the True Person." This is recorded in works such as Panyi Ji, Mingdao Ji, Xiyou Ji, Fengyun Qingfang Lu, and the Seven Truths' Chronology. Initially, the True Person returned on his own accord from the capital via Xuande. One day, a wealthy family's new house was completed, and they invited him to visit in hopes that his words would bring good fortune. After entering the room, he remained silent without speaking. Then, he took the iron cane he held and destroyed several places on the windows and walls before leaving. The host bowed twice, hoping for an understanding. The True Person said, "Your house is complete and beautiful, but it must be destroyed, for this is the way of things. I will not destroy it, but you will not be able to plan for the end. Now it is destroyed; you should think about its destruction and strive to preserve it. Then you and your descendants may perhaps sing and cry, forever without replacement." The host was convinced and exclaimed, "Ah, the true person truly knows!"

    South Pool Frogs

    During the late Song dynasty, in the city of Xinzhou, a place where earth was dug to create a moat of about a hundred acres was called South Pool. Beside the pool, there were houses suitable for living, formerly belonging to the villagers. After submission, Dalu Gazi Maka took possession of the land. Every spring and summer, the frogs' noise disturbed the ears, making sleep and eating uneasy. Happened to meet the 38th generation Celestial Master, Zhang Guangwei, who returned from the capital. Then told the Celestial Master, who wrote a red ink talisman on new tiles and sent someone to throw it into the pool, warning them saying, "Your frog mothers will no longer make noise." From then on until now, it has been quiet. The Book of Han records that in Taiye Pool, ducklings and ganders were abundant and accumulated. Using ganders was very good. In Wang Wei's poem, reed shoots pierce through lotus leaves, water lilies trap ganders, and they are new.

    203

    趂办官钱
    　　浙省广济库嵗差杭城諲实戸若干名充役库子以司出纳比一家中侵用官钱太多无可为偿府判王某素号残忍乃拘其妻妾子女于官又无可为计则命小舟载之求食于西湖以赀纳官妾马不肖辈羣趋焉鲜于伯几先生【枢】作湖边曲云湖边荡浆谁家女緑惨红愁羞不语低回忍泪傍郎船贪得纒头强歌舞玉壶美酒不须忧鱼腹熊蹯弃如土阳台梦短匆匆去鸳鎻生寒愁日暮安得义士掷千金遂令桑濮歌行路后王之子孙有为娼者天之报施一何捷也
    　　鼎作牛鸣
    　　义兴王子明家饶于财所藏三代彛鼎六朝以来法书名画实冠浙右每年必祈一籖于烈帝庙以卜休咎一嵗籖词有曰开沟凿井当得古鼎殊不以为意家人以商贾至汴夹谷郎中者藏一商彛絶精妙示之曰恐尔主翁未必有此物也归以白即遣赍金购得之比旧藏皆不能及至正壬辰宼起蕲黄将由义兴取道犯浙西子明罄其所藏凿深窖以埋之彛亦在列既入窖作牛鸣者七夜颇可怪取出寄田家其窖后遭发掘独此彛获存

    Explanation

    203

    Raising Funds Through Exploitation

    In Zhejiang province, the Guangji Treasury annually assigned several households from Hangzhou to serve as treasurers, responsible for managing the treasury's finances. When the misappropriation of public funds by one household became too great to repay, Magistrate Wang, known for his harshness, detained the wives, concubines, and children of the household. Having no other options, he ordered boats to carry them to beg for food along the West Lake, with the expectation that they would pay off their debts through money. Unscrupulous and desperate individuals flocked to them. Xian Yu Boji (also known as Xian Yu Shu), a poet, composed a poem titled "Lake Side Song," which reads:

    Who is the woman paddling the boat by the lake?
    Her green and red attire, sorrowful and sad, she wears with shame.
    She hesitates and holds back tears, approaching the boat of the man.
    Greedy for the money offered as a reward, she forces herself to dance and sing.
    Fine wine in a jade pot, no need to worry.
    Fish and bear paws, discarded like dirt.
    A fleeting dream, gone in a moment.
    The lock of the swan feels cold, worrying about the end of the day.
    How quickly heaven rewards or punishes!
    How can a righteous man spend a thousand gold coins,
    So that the songs of prostitutes on the road are silenced?
    Later generations of the descendants of the king became prostitutes.

    A Bronze Vessel Making a Sound Like a Cow

    Wang Ziming of Yixing was wealthy, with a collection of bronze vessels from the Three Dynasties and calligraphy and paintings from the Six Dynasties that were unparalleled in Zhejiang. Every year, he would pray for a lottery ticket at the temple of the Martial Emperor to predict good or bad fortune. One year, the lottery ticket read: "Digging a ditch and well, you will find an ancient bronze vessel." He did not take it seriously. A merchant from Bianjing named Jia Gu, who had a highly exquisite bronze vessel, showed it to him and said, "I doubt your master has such an item." Returning to report, he sent money to buy it. Even his old collection could not match it. In the year 1352, bandits rose in Qichang and planned to pass through Yixing to invade western Zhejiang. Wang Ziming emptied all his collection and dug a deep pit to bury it, with the bronze vessel also among them. After being buried, it made a sound like a cow for seven nights, which was quite strange. Taking it out and leaving it with a farmer, the pit was later excavated, but only this bronze vessel remained.

    204

    鏖糟
    　　俗语以不洁为鏖糟按霍去病传鏖皋兰下注以世俗谓尽死杀人为鏖糟然义虽不同却有所出
    　　越民考
    　　穆尔古蘓字善卿西夏人侨居松江家贫授徒以养母性至孝然落落不羁善谐谑名人士多与之游至正甲午进士及第授绍兴路録事司达噜噶齐比视天下云扰所在悉瘵君抚字周至民爱之如父母乙未秋杭破遄即克复浙省左丞杨谔勒哲以本部苖将持露布至统洞蛮甚众意实觇视虚实又将流毒于我民也纵虐恣暴民皆束手惴惴不敢与争无故刼府架阁照磨陈修家妻妾几被汚君激怒填膺指挥吏兵收之郡民讙呼从事苗遂尽死后谔勒哲闻越民结义且固终不敢调兵渡浙江方集庆防时江南行台官流避抵庆元奉防置治所于越遂檄君总统义民防城池君更募得勇悍者二千余

    Explanation

    204

    Filth and Disorder
    In common parlance, filth is equated with disorder. According to the Biography of Huo Qubing in the Records of the Grand Historian, the term "鏖糟" is used to describe the aftermath of a battle where all combatants were killed. Despite the different meanings, the term has its origins in historical usage.

    Examination of the Yue People
    Mu'er Gu Su, also known as Shan Qing, was a Western Xia person who lived in Songjiang. He was poor and taught to support his mother. He was known for his filial piety and his carefree, humorous nature. Many notable people of his time associated with him. In the year 1344 (至正甲午), he passed the imperial examination and was appointed as the head of the Lusi Si (录事司) in Shaoxing. He was known for his diligent governance and was loved by the people like a parent.

    In the autumn of 1345 (乙未), Hangzhou was captured, and the city was quickly recaptured. Left Chancellor Yang E Lezhe sent his local general with a message to the Tongdong Man (统洞蛮), a large group of people who were suspected of spying and planning to harm the local population. They were violent and oppressive, and the people were too afraid to resist. The general's men even attacked the home of Chen Xiu, a government official, and almost raped his wife and concubines. The official, filled with anger, ordered his officials and soldiers to arrest the perpetrators. The local people cheered and the general's men were captured. After this, Yang E Lezhe heard that the Yue people had formed a coalition and were determined to defend themselves, so he did not dare to send troops across the Yangtze River. When the court officials fled to Quanzhou, they established their headquarters in Yue and ordered the official to lead the defense of the city. He then recruited more than 2,000 brave and fierce men.

    205

    人以果毅二字为号曰果毅军练习武事分拨守要害乃日与常所徃来者击鲜饫醲酣咏呌啸以为娱乐虽戸外上官坌至不少延纳永康防起据有县境君收复朝廷旌其功除江南浙西道亷访司知事未上又除江东建康道经歴浙省丞相达实特穆尔便宜除行枢宻院判官君即自署诸叅谋为幕官曰经歴曰都事者不可枚举时御史大夫拜珠克任奸吏为爪牙又自统军三千曰台军纪律不严民横被扰害有诉于君君辄抑之众军皆怨怒然拜委龌龊惟以钩距致财为务君不礼之或以谏君曰吾知上有君下有民耳安问其他拜颇闻衔之遂与台军元帅埒克戬永安张某万戸阎塔斯布哈王哈喇

    Explanation

    205

    People named the military unit "果毅军" because it was resolute and courageous, and it was tasked with practicing martial arts and guarding strategic points. They would daily indulge in fine food and drink, singing and shouting in joy as their form of entertainment. Even though many officials gathered outside the door, they did not allow them to enter. When the rebellion in Yongkang broke out and occupied the county, the official restored order. The court honored his achievements by appointing him as the head of the Inspection Office in the Jiangnan and Zhejiang West region. Before he could take up this post, he was also appointed as the head of the Inspection Office in the Jiangdong and Jiankang region. Later, the Chancellor of Zhejiang, Dashi Temur, at his discretion, appointed him as the head of the Military Affairs Office. The official then appointed various advisors as staff officers, including those with the titles of "经历" and "都事," which cannot be listed exhaustively. At the time, the御史大夫拜珠克 appointed corrupt officials as his enforcers and personally led three thousand troops called the "台军." The troops had lax discipline and arbitrarily harassed and harmed the people. When complaints were made to the official, he would suppress them, causing the troops to resent and be angry. However,拜珠克 only cared about accumulating wealth through cunning methods and did not show respect to him. When someone advised him, he replied, "I only care about the ruler above and the people below. Why bother with anything else?"拜珠克 heard this and resented him, so he allied with the commander of the "台军,"埒克戬永安, and other officials like张某,阎塔斯布哈, and王哈喇.

    206

    特穆尔等谋杀之未得间戊戌十月廿二日首事出兵逾曹娥江与平章方国珍部下万戸冯某鬬既不利驻军东闗单骑驰归拜意决矣廿三日迟明召君私第议事入至中门左右以鐡槌挝杀之初甚秘守阍军自相谓无已杀总督官我辈幸也民始有闻之者走白君部将浙东佥元帅黄中诸叅谋闻变奔避不顾至有坠城以出行四五十里者初夜二鼔中提军入城屯戒珠山拜未及知中卧病方饮药得少汗尚昏溃困顿左右扶翼擐甲上马遇台军于江桥鬬十数合破阵防坚身当矢石郡民老幼皆号泣曰杀我总督官我尚何生为壮者助中军殊死战台军一败涂地屠其二营入拜家姬侍奴死者相枕藉

    Explanation

    206

    On the day after the failed assassination attempt, on the twenty-second day of the tenth month in the year of the dog, the rebels led an army out and crossed the Ca'e River to fight against Fang Guozhen's subordinate, Wanhu Feng. They were not successful and stationed their army at Dongguan. Bai returned alone, deciding his course of action. On the twenty-third day, early in the morning, he summoned the governor to his private residence to discuss matters. Upon entering the main gate, his attendants killed him with iron hammers. Initially, the plot was kept very secret, and the guards at the gate told each other that they were glad that the governor had been killed. The people began to hear about it and rushed to inform the governor's generals. The deputy commander of the Zhejiang East region, Huang Zhong, and the various advisors heard the news and fled, some even falling off the walls and traveling forty or fifty miles away. Early in the night, the second drumbeat, Huang Zhong led his troops into the city and camped at Jiezhu Mountain. Bai did not know about this because he was ill in bed, just having taken medicine and sweating slightly, still in a confused and exhausted state. His attendants supported and helped him put on armor and mount his horse. He met the rebel army at the Jiang Bridge and fought for several rounds, breaking through their defenses and facing enemy arrows and stones. The old and young of the city cried out, saying, "They have killed our governor; how can we live on?" The strong men joined the main army in a desperate fight. The rebel army was completely defeated, and they slaughtered the two camps. They entered Bai's home and killed his concubines, servants, and slaves, who lay dead in piles.

    207

    一女为队官陈某所掠举君尸无元大索三日得于溺池中拜与二子匿梵宇幽处民搜见之齐唾其面且骂曰瞎贼我总督官何罪而令致于此耶不自杀执以归中冀中杀之中解其缚率诸军罗拜之曰总督官忠肝义胆照映天地人神所共知公信任憸邪使国家之柱石陨于无辜我之复讐明大义也杀我主将者既已斩之公幸毋罪拜执中以泣曰我之罪尚何言尚何言继而军民为君持服为位以祭私諡曰越民考越六日拜自劾纳印绶去其印是夜遗失中以白金百两购得于一卒以还行台者君未死先三日有星大如桮椀红光烛天坠镇粤门化为石及君出师识者已卜君之有死兆矣至是果验云
    　　南村野史曰兵凶器也战逆徳也圣人不得已而用之故吾夫子必以临事而惧好谋而成荅子路行三军之问夫穆尔古蘓受任之初殊有古贤县令之风一握兵柄志满意得酣觞废事轻谋首乱不旋踵而身首异处盖亦平昔越己之过有以酿成此祸与防中则老母稚子亦皆几上之肉耳原其忠君爱民之心昞然与日星相昭明者则无可议也

    Explanation

    207

    A woman was abducted by the captain T某某. The body of the deceased was searched for three days without success and was found in a drowning pool. The captain hid with his two sons in a secluded place within a Buddhist temple. The people searched for him and when they found him, they spat on his face and called him a blind thief, saying, "What crime did our governor commit to be brought here like this?" He did not kill himself but was taken back to the camp where he was to be executed. The commander untied his bonds and led the troops to prostrate themselves before him, saying, "The governor's loyalty and righteousness shine brightly in heaven and earth, known by both humans and gods. You trusted the wicked, causing the pillars of the state to fall unjustly. My revenge is to uphold great righteousness. Those who killed my commander have already been executed, please do not punish me." The commander knelt and wept, saying, "What more can I say? What more can I say?" Subsequently, the soldiers and civilians mourned for him and set up altars to offer sacrifices. They privately posthumously honored him as the "Exemplar of the People" for six days. The captain then submitted his own case, handed over his seal and sash, and left. That night, his seal was lost, but it was recovered the next day with a hundred taels of silver offered by one of his soldiers and returned to the headquarters. Three days before his death, a star as large as a bowl, shining with red light, fell from the sky and struck the gate of the Canton region, transforming into a stone. Those who recognized it had already predicted that the governor would meet his demise. Indeed, this proved true.

    The Nan Cun Ye Shi (Chronicles of South Village) states: "War is a dangerous tool, and battle is contrary to virtue. Sages use it only when absolutely necessary. Therefore, our Master Confucius always said to be cautious and well-prepared in matters of state, and to plan carefully. This was the advice he gave to Zilu regarding the command of the three armies. When Mu'er Gusu was first appointed, he had the exemplary conduct of ancient virtuous county magistrates. Once he took control of the military, he became arrogant and indulged in drinking, neglecting his duties and leading to chaos. He did not even complete a full step before his head and body were separated. This was also due to his past arrogance and mistakes, which led to this disaster. His mother and children were also like meat on the chopping block. His loyal heart to the emperor and love for the people are as bright as the sun and stars, and there is nothing to criticize about him."

    208

    拜珠为国大臣坐镇四省百官庶司孰不听令迈之不奉台檄擅兴师旅明问其罪黜之可也斩之可也而乃阴结小丑作为此态是盗杀之非公论矣民心之所以不服良以是也噫享有尊爵重禄而当国步艰难之日既不思防埃补报之道又不责自己贪饕之非反以谋害忠良为先务谓之无罪得乎故其妻妾子女遭罹戮辱实自取之尚复可怜哉
    　　三姑六婆
    　　三姑者尼姑道姑卦姑也六婆者牙婆媒婆师婆防婆药婆稳婆也盖与三刑六害同也人家有一于此而不致奸盗者几希矣能谨而逺之如避蛇蝎庶乎净宅之法
    　　不中用
    　　不中用不可用也左传成二年郤子曰克于先大夫无能为役杜预注不中为之役使

    Explanation

    208

    The official, Bai Zhu, was appointed as a high-ranking minister to govern the four provinces, and all officials and commoners obeyed his commands. However, Mai Zhi failed to follow the official documents and initiated military campaigns without authorization. It is clear that he should be punished—either by demotion or execution. Instead, he secretly aligned with petty villains and acted in such a manner, which is akin to killing like a thief and is not a fair judgment. The reason why the people do not comply is truly due to this. Alas, while enjoying high rank and generous stipends during difficult times for the state, he did not consider how to repay the state or blame himself for his greed, but rather prioritized plotting against loyal ministers. Can he be considered innocent? Therefore, his wife, concubines, and children suffered punishment and humiliation—it was indeed self-inflicted, yet they are still pitiable.

    Three Gū and Six Bāo
    The Three Gū are nuns, Taoist nuns, and fortune-tellers. The Six Bāo are matchmakers, midwives, fortune-tellers, nurses, herbalists, and wet nurses. These are similar to the three punishments and six harms. If there is one of these in a household, it is rare for it not to lead to adultery or theft. One must be cautious and keep away from them, like avoiding snakes and scorpions, to keep the home clean.

    Useless
    Useless means it cannot be used. In the Zuo Zhuan (Commentary of Zuo), the second year of the Cheng period, Xi Zi said, "I am unable to serve as a servant to my ancestors." Respected commentator Du Yu explained, "Not suitable to serve as a servant."

    209

    国字
    　　杜清碧先生【本】字伯原有所编五声韵自大小分隶真草以至于外蕃书及国朝古新字靡不收録题曰华夏同音至正壬午中书奏修三史以翰林待制聘先生起至武林辞疾不行盘桓久之浙省平章喀喇子山公库库时来访一日语及声律之学因问国字何以用可侯【此喉音也有音无字】字为首先生曰正如婴儿初堕地时作此一声乃得天地之全气也平章甚説服
    　　水畜
    　　陶朱公养鱼经曰夫治生之法有五水畜第一水畜鱼也此二字亦竒
    　　纒足
    　　张基墨庄漫録云妇人之纒足起于近世前世书传皆无所自南史齐东昏侯为潘贵妃凿金为莲花以帖地令妃行其上曰此歩歩生莲花然亦不言其弓小也如古乐府玉台新咏皆六朝词人纎艶之言多体状美人容色之姝丽及言妆饰之华眉目唇口要支手指之无一言称纒足者如唐之杜牧之李白李商之辈作诗多言闺帏之事亦无及之者韩偓香奁集有咏屧子诗云六寸肤圆

    Explanation

    209

    Translation:

    The National Script

    Mr. Du Qingbi, whose given name was Boyuan, compiled a five-tone rhyme dictionary, classifying characters from large and small regular script to clerical and cursive styles, as well as foreign scripts and new characters from the current dynasty. He titled it "Huaxia Common Sounds." In the year 1352, the Secretariat proposed to compile the Three Histories and invited Mr. Du, who was a翰林待制, to participate. However, he declined due to illness and lingered in Wulin for a long time. During this period, the provincial governor, Prince Kala Zishan, visited him. One day, they discussed the study of phonetics, and the prince asked why the character "可侯" (a phonetic term, possibly referring to a specific sound or character) was used as the first character. Mr. Du explained that it was like the first sound an infant makes when born, which acquires the full essence of heaven and earth. The prince was very convinced by this explanation.

    Fish Farming

    In the Fish Farming Classic of Tao Zhu Gong, it is said that there are five methods of making a living, with fish farming being the first. This is a peculiar statement, as the two characters "水畜" (water livestock) are used to describe it.

    Foot Binding

    According to Zhang Xuanji's Mozhuang Manlu, foot binding for women began in recent times. Ancient texts have no mention of it. In the Southern History, Emperor Donghun of Qi made gold lotuses for his concubine Pan and placed them on the ground for her to walk on, saying, "This is stepping on lotuses." However, it does not mention the small size of her feet. In ancient poetry collections like the Yutai Xinyong, the delicate and elegant words of poets from the Six Dynasties often describe the beauty of a woman's appearance, makeup, eyes, lips, fingers, etc., but none of them mention foot binding. Even famous Tang Dynasty poets like Du Mu, Li Bai, and Li Shangyin, whose poems often talk about women's affairs, do not mention foot binding. In the Xianglian Ji of Han Yu, there is a poem about shoes that says, "Six inches of round flesh."

    210

    光致致唐尺短以今校之亦自小也而不言其弓惟道山新闻云李后主宫嫔窅娘纎丽善舞后主作金莲髙六尺饰以寳物细带缨络莲中作品色瑞莲令窅娘以帛绕脚令纎小屈上作新月状素韈舞云中回旋有凌云之态唐镐诗曰莲中花更好云里月长新因窅娘作也由是人皆效之以纎弓为妙以此知札脚自五代以来方为之如熈寜元丰以前人犹为者少近年则人人相效以不为者为耻也
    　　溺水不跃
    　　漳州龙溪县澳里人陈端才之妻蔡氏三玉后至元间本处防起掠其里里媪集里中妇女同舟避难防追及三玉亟以水渍衣防视三玉有姿色欲先污之三玉绐曰衣湿更求衣间防取衣投水死防曰溺者必跃以长竿络钩俟其跃而举之尸竟不跃防退三玉之父端广舟次上流尸遂逆流附父舟捭之不去移舟遡河而上尸从之上者三父异甚视则其女也夫三玉一妇人耳宁死不辱出于天性宜其贞爽不昧如此

    Explanation

    210

    Translation

    The Tang dynasty's measurement standards were shorter than modern ones, and when compared to today, they were also small. However, the text does not mention the bow. According to the Dao Shan News, Emperor Li Yu of Southern Tang had a palace maiden named Yǎo Niáng, who was slender, beautiful, and skilled in dance. The emperor created a golden lotus six feet high, decorated with precious objects, fine ribbons, and a variety of auspicious lotuses inside. He instructed Yǎo Niáng to wrap her feet with silk to make them slender and small, bend them into a crescent shape, and dance in white socks, spinning in the clouds with a soaring appearance. Tang Hao wrote a poem: "The lotus in the water is even more beautiful, the moon in the clouds is ever new." The poem was written because of Yǎo Niáng. Therefore, people all imitated her, considering slender feet to be the best. From this, we know that foot binding began in the Five Dynasties period and was less common before the Xining and Yuanfeng eras. In recent times, it has become widespread, and not practicing it is considered shameful.

    Drowning Without Leaping

    Cai Sanyu, wife of Chen Duocai from Zhangzhou Longxi County, was a woman who preferred to die rather than be dishonored. During the Yuan dynasty, local authorities began to raid the village, and the women gathered on a boat to escape. The raiders caught up with them. Cai Sanyu quickly wet her clothes to avoid being seen by the raiders, who were interested in her beauty. She tricked them by saying she needed to change her wet clothes. When they took her clothes and threw them into the water, she did not jump out. The raiders retreated, and her father, Chen Duan Guang, moved the boat upstream. The body of Cai Sanyu followed the boat three times, which was seen as a miraculous event. Her father was greatly surprised and saw that it was his daughter. Cai Sanyu, as a woman, preferred to die rather than be dishonored, which was in line with her natural character and virtue.

    211

    鎻阳
    　　鞑靼田地野马或与蛟龙交遗精入地乆之发起如笋上丰下俭鳞甲栉比筋脉连络其形絶男阴名曰鎻阳即肉从容之或谓里妇之滛者就合之一得阴气勃然怒长土人掘取洗涤去皮薄切晒干以充药货功力百倍于从容也
    　　辊吝谝三卦
    　　淮南潘子素【纯】尝作辊卦讥世之仕宦人以突梯滑稽而得显爵者虽曰资一时之谑浪调笑不为无补于名教卦辞曰辊亨可小事亦可大事曰辊亨天地辊而四时行日月辊而昼夜明上下辊而万事成辊之时义大矣哉象曰地上有木辊君子以容身固位初六辊出门无咎象曰出门便辊又何咎也六二传于銕轊象曰传于銕轊天下可行也六三君子终日辊辊厉无咎象曰终日辊辊虽危无咎也九四模棱吉象曰模棱之吉以随时也六五神辊象曰六五神辊老于事也上六或锡之髙爵天下揶揄之

    Explanation

    211

    Translation:

    Mao Yang
    　　In the land of the Tatars, wild horses sometimes mate with dragons, leaving their essence in the earth. Over time, it rises like bamboo, with a wide top and narrow bottom, its scales arranged like fish scales, and its muscles and veins connected. Its shape is extremely similar to male genitalia, and it is called "Mao Yang," a type of meat product known as "Rong Rong." Some say that when a village woman engages in sexual activity, she may receive the yin energy, causing it to grow rapidly. Local people dig it up, wash it, remove the skin, slice it thinly, and dry it to use as medicine. Its efficacy is hundreds of times greater than that of Rong Rong.

    　　Guan Li Pian San Guai
    　　Pan Zisu of Huainan once created the "Guan Hexagram" to criticize officials who gained high ranks through flattery and jest. Although this may seem like a form of jest and humor, it is not without benefit to the moral teachings. The hexagram says, "Guan is auspicious; it can be used for small matters and also for great matters." The commentary says, "Guan is auspicious; Heaven and Earth roll, and the four seasons proceed; the sun and moon roll, and day and night are made clear; the upper and lower roll, and all things are accomplished. The significance of Guan is great indeed." The image says, "There is a tree on the ground, Guan; the gentleman uses it to preserve his position and stability." For the initial line, "Guan, going out, no blame," the image says, "Going out and rolling, what blame can there be?" For the second line, "Passing on the iron axle," the image says, "Passing on the iron axle, it can be done throughout the world." For the third line, "The gentleman rolls all day, rolling and dangerous, no blame," the image says, "Rolling all day, even if dangerous, there is no blame." For the fourth line, "Ambiguous is auspicious," the image says, "The auspiciousness of ambiguity is due to adapting to the times." For the fifth line, "Divine is Guan," the image says, "Divine is Guan; it is experienced in matters." For the sixth line, "Sometimes it is given a high rank, but it is ridiculed by the world."

    212

    象曰以辊受爵亦不足敬也此篇或者又谓自宋末即有非潘所造未审是否后平江蔡宗鲁【卫】作吝卦爻配之曰吝亨利居闲不利有所为曰吝鄙啬也利居闲无所求也不利有所为恐致祸也初六居富吝于周急悔亡无攸利象曰吝于周急不恤其贫也悔亡无攸利己终有望也六二听妇言至吝不养其亲不恤其弟贞凶象曰听妇言昵于私也不养其亲忘大恩也不恤其弟失大义也虽养弗时亦致灾也故贞凶九三极吝吝其财不吝其身于行非宜象曰吝其财斯致富也不吝其身乃轻生也六四太吝

    Explanation

    212

    The Image says: To receive a title with a roller is also not worthy of respect. This passage may also be said to have originated from the late Song Dynasty, not by Pan; it is unclear whether this is true. Afterward, Pianjiang Cai Zonglu [Wei] attempted to match the Lìn hexagram and its lines, saying: "It says: Lìn (stinginess), benefits from inaction, unfavorable for action." The Image says: Lìn means meanness. It is beneficial to remain inactive and not seek anything, but it is unfavorable to take action, as it may lead to disaster. The first line: When wealthy, stingy in helping others in need, no regret, no benefit. The Image says: Stingy in helping others in need, showing no concern for their poverty. No regret, no benefit, but there is still hope in the end. The second line: Listening to a woman's words, extremely stingy, not caring for one's parents, not caring for one's younger brothers, inauspicious. The Image says: Listening to a woman's words leads to favoring personal interests, neglecting one's parents (forgetting great kindness), and neglecting one's younger brothers (losing great righteousness). Even if one tries to care for others, if it is not done at the right time, it will still bring disaster, so it is inauspicious. The third line: Extremely stingy, stingy with wealth but not with one's life, which is inappropriate for action. The Image says: Stingy with wealth leads to wealth, but not stingy with one's life means risking one's life. The fourth line: Excessively stingy.

    213

    君子吉小人凶象曰吝于君子虽有言无尤也吝于小人虽不有言终有悔也六五不吝于色务所欲终以死亡凶朋来吝于酒食弗克欢无咎象曰不吝于色惑于淫也务所欲乐其顺从也终以死亡凶可知也朋来从其也吝于酒食诚大谬也虽弗克欢而无咎也上九居其家不吝于内吝于教子弗叶吉象曰居其家妄自尊也不吝于内畏寡妻也吝于教子终无所成也近扶风马文璧【琬】又作谝卦曰谝贞亨初吉终凶利见小人不利于君子曰贞正也亨通也通乎正言谝或庶几也终凶谝不由初也利见

    Explanation

    213

    Gentlemen are auspicious, while petty people are ominous. The commentary says: A gentleman is reluctant, even if there are words, there is no blame. A petty person is reluctant, even if there are no words, there will be regret in the end. The sixth line does not hesitate in appearance, striving for what one desires will ultimately lead to death and misfortune. Friends come and are reluctant about wine and food, cannot be happy but without blame. The commentary says: Not hesitating in appearance, one is confused by lust. Striving for what one desires, one finds pleasure in obedience. Ultimately leading to death and misfortune is known. Friends come and follow their nature, being reluctant about wine and food is truly a great mistake. Even if they cannot be happy, there is no blame. The ninth line dwells in one's home, not hesitating internally, but hesitant in educating children, which is not auspicious. The commentary says: Dwelling in one's home, one is vainly self-important. Not hesitating internally, one fears a weak wife. Hesitant in educating children, one will ultimately achieve nothing. Near Fufeng, Ma Wenbi (also known as Ma Wang) wrote another version of the hexagram, saying: "Pian" (谝) is auspicious and smooth, initially auspicious but ultimately ominous. It is favorable to see petty people but unfavorable to see gentlemen. The commentary says: "Pian" means to be sincere and upright, to be smooth and open. To be smooth through righteousness, "pian" may almost be so. Ultimately ominous, "pian" does not follow the beginning. Favorable to see.

    214

    小人犹同也不利于君子入于邪也象曰丽口掉舌谝君子以求名干禄初九谝于同朋无咎象曰同朋于谝又谁咎也九二略施于民吉象曰九二之吉以新众听也六三来其谝酒食用享象曰来其谝民取则也享其酒食以崇功也九四饰言如簧以娱彼心用获南金象曰娱人获金不足道也九五君子终日髙谝王用徴安车以迎终嵗弗寜后有凶象曰以谝受徴不羞也终嵗弗宁祗足烦劳也后有凶不副实也上六莾谝不已四方欲杀之象曰莾谝众怒杀之何过也右三卦切中时病真得风刺之正因并録之

    Explanation

    214

    Translation

    Small people are like this, and it is not beneficial to the virtuous to enter into evil. The image says: using smooth speech to flatter the virtuous to gain fame and profit. At the beginning, flattery among friends is without blame. The image says: flattery among friends, who else could blame them? At nine two, slightly benefiting the people is auspicious. The image says: the auspiciousness of nine two is to refresh the people's ears. At six three, bringing their flattery, wine, food, and offerings. The image says: bringing their flattery, the people take it as an example. Offering their wine and food to honor achievements. At nine four, using speech as smooth as a musical instrument to entertain his heart and obtain southern gold. The image says: entertaining people to gain gold is not worth mentioning. At nine five, the virtuous person spends the whole day in high praise, and the king uses a safe carriage to welcome, but all year long, there is no peace, and later there will be misfortune. The image says: receiving praise and being welcomed is not shameful. All year long, there is no peace, only enough to cause trouble. Later there will be misfortune, not matching reality. At upper six, excessive flattery without end leads to everyone wanting to kill him. The image says: excessive flattery, the people's anger to kill him, what is the fault? The three hexagrams accurately capture the ills of the times, truly embodying the spirit of satire. Therefore, they are recorded together.

    215

    乌蜑戸
    　　广东采珠之人悬絙于腰沉入海中良乆得珠撼其絙舶上人挈出之于鼋鼍蛟龙之腹者比比有焉有司名曰乌蜑戸蜑音但仁宗登极特防故免时敬公戴卿为江西行省叅知政事俾该管掾史立案令广东帅府抄具乌蜑戸一一籍贯姓名置册申解他省官曰中书咨文无是恐不必也公曰万一乃申明旧典庶不害及良民未几太后中使至人咸服公先见之明
    　　重台
    　　凡婢役于婢者俗谓之重台按左氏传昭公五年日之数十故有十时亦当十位自王以下其二为公其三为卿注云日中为王食时为公平旦为卿鸡鸣为士夜半为皂人定为舆黄昏为日入为僚晡时为仆日昳为台防日中出阙不在第等王公旷其位又昭公七年天有十日人有十等故王臣公公臣大夫大夫臣士士臣皁皁臣舆舆臣台则所谓台者十等之至卑今岂亦本是与然加以重字尤有意

    Explanation

    215

    U-Dan Households
    People in Guangdong who collect pearls tie a rope around their waist and sink into the sea for a long time. After obtaining pearls, they shake the rope, and people on the ship pull them out. Those who are buried in the bellies of turtles, crocodiles, and dragons are many. The authorities named them as U-Dan households. The character "蜑" (dan) refers to people who live by water, often associated with fishing and pearl diving. During the reign of Emperor Renzong, they were specially exempted due to special circumstances. At that time, Dai, a high-ranking official serving as a vice-premier in the Jiangxi provincial government, ordered the relevant clerks to file a record and instructed the Guangdong military governor to copy the names and places of origin of the U-Dan households and place them in a register to be submitted. Officials from other provinces said there was no such thing in the central government's documents, and they doubted the necessity of this action. Dai replied, "In case it is necessary to restate the old regulations, to avoid harming the good people." Soon after, an imperial messenger from the empress arrived, and everyone admired Dai's foresight.

    Heavy Platform
    All servants who work for other servants are commonly called heavy platforms. According to the Zuo Zhuan from the fifth year of the reign of Duke Zhao, the sun has ten parts, so there are ten times and ten positions. From the king down, two are princes and three are ministers. The commentary states that at noon, the king is the ruler, at mealtime, the prince is the ruler, at dawn, the minister is the ruler, at cockcrow, the scholar is the ruler, at midnight, the servant is the ruler, at the hour of man, the commoner is the ruler, at dusk, the clerk is the ruler, at sunset, the attendant is the ruler, at evening, the servant is the ruler, and at midday, the platform is the ruler. The platform is not included in the hierarchy. According to the Zuo Zhuan from the seventh year of the reign of Duke Zhao, the sky has ten suns, and people have ten ranks. Therefore, the king's subjects are princes, princes' subjects are ministers, ministers' subjects are scholars, scholars' subjects are servants, servants' subjects are commoners, commoners' subjects are clerks, clerks' subjects are platforms. Thus, the term "platform" refers to the lowest rank in the ten ranks. Is this also the case now? Adding the character "重" (heavy) has a particular meaning.

    216

    日子
    　　文选曹公檄吴将校部曲文年月朔日子注发檄时也

    　　辍耕録卷十
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録巻十一　　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰写像秘诀
    　　王思善【绎】自号痴絶生其先睦人居杭之新门笃志好学雅有才思至正乙酉间檇李叶居仲【广居】寓思善之东里教授余从永嘉李五峯先生【孝光】往访之时思善在诸生中年方十二三已能丹青亦能写真先生即俾作一圆光小像面部仅大如钱而宛然无毫髪异先生喜作文以华之尔后余复托交于其尊人日华【曅】遂与思善为忘年友思善继得吴中顾周道【逵】绪言开发益造精防是故于小像特妙非惟貎人之形似抑且得人之神气尝授余秘诀并采绘法今着于此与好事者共之

    Explanation

    216

    The Day

    From the Wenxuan, a proclamation by Cao Cao to the generals and troops of Wu, dated the first day of the month, annotated and issued as a proclamation at that time.

    Chapter Ten of Chuogenglu
    Compiled in the Siku Quanshu
    Chapter Eleven of Chuogenglu
    Written by Tao Zongyi of the Yuan dynasty
    Secrets of Painting Portraits

    Wang Sishan, self-styled "Chijuesheng," was originally from Muzhou and lived in Xinmen, Hangzhou. He was deeply devoted to learning, had a refined and talented mind, and during the year 1355, Ye Juzhong (also known as Ye Guangju) lived in a place called "East Village" near Wang Sishan. I, accompanied by Li Wufeng (also known as Li Xiaoguang), a respected teacher from Yongjia, visited him at that time. Wang Sishan, then a young student of about twelve or thirteen years old, was already skilled in painting and could create portraits. His teacher asked him to paint a small circular portrait. The face was as small as a coin but still lifelike and without any flaws. The teacher was pleased to write a poem to embellish the portrait.
