Later, I formed a friendship with Wang Sishan, despite our age difference, through his father, Rihua (also known as Rihua). Wang Sishan then learned from Gu Zhaodao (also known as Gu Zhaodao) in Wu, further developing his skills and achieving greater precision. Thus, his portraits were particularly excellent, not only capturing the physical resemblance of the subjects but also their spirit and essence. He once taught me some secret techniques and methods for painting. Now, I am writing these down to share them with those who are interested.

    217

    凡写像须通晓相法盖人之面貎部位与夫五岳四渎各各不侔自有相对照处而四时气色亦异彼方呌啸谈话之间本真性情发见我则静而求之黙识于心闭目如在目前放笔如在笔底然后以淡墨覇定逐旋积起先兰台廷尉次鼻凖鼻凖既成以之为主山根髙取印堂一笔下来如低取眼堂边一笔下来或不髙不低在乎八九分中则侧边一笔下来次人中次口次眼堂次眼次眉次额次颊次髪际次耳次髪次头次打圏打圏者面部也必宜如此一一对去庶几无纎毫遗失近代俗工胶柱鼓瑟不知变通之道必欲其正襟危坐如泥塑人方乃传写因是万无一得此又何足怪哉吁吾不可柰何矣

    Explanation

    217

    In painting portraits, one must understand physiognomy, for the various parts of a person's face and the five peaks and four rivers of physiognomy differ from one another, each having its own corresponding features. Moreover, the colors of the face also vary with the seasons. It is during the moments when a person is shouting, talking, or conversing that their true nature and emotions are revealed. I then quietly observe and memorize in my heart, closing my eyes as if the subject is right before me, and letting the brush move as if it is at the tip of my hand. Then, I use light ink to outline and gradually build up the details, starting with the forehead and nose bridge. Once the nose bridge is completed, it serves as the main reference point. If the nose bridge is high, I take a stroke from the area between the eyebrows; if it is low, I take a stroke from the side of the eye socket. If it is neither too high nor too low, it should be about eight to nine parts, and then a side stroke should be added. Next, I paint the philtrum, mouth, eye sockets, eyes, eyebrows, forehead, cheeks, hairline, ears, hair, and head. The circular strokes around the eyes and mouth complete the facial features. One must follow this order to avoid any small details being missed. Modern craftsmen who stick to a fixed method without adapting criticize the rigidity of some artists who insist on having the subject sit stiffly like a statue before they can paint. Because of this, there is no chance of success, and this is not surprising. Alas, what can I do?

    218

    采绘法
    　　凡面色先用三朱腻粉方粉藤黄檀子土黄京墨合和衬底上面仍用底粉薄笼然后用檀子墨水斡染面色白者粉入少土黄燕支不用胭脂则三朱红者前件色入少土朱紫堂者粉檀子老青入少胭脂黄者粉土黄入少土朱青黒者粉入檀子土黄老青各一防粉薄罩檀墨斡已上看顔色清浊加减用又不可执一也
    　　口角燕支淡如要带笑容口角两笔畧放起
    　　眼中白染瞳子外两笔次用烟子防睛墨打圏眼梢防起有折便笑
    　　口唇上胭脂蓦
    　　鼻色红胭脂防笼
    　　面雀斑淡墨水斡麻檀水斡
    　　髯色黒者依髪渲紫者檀墨间渲黄红者藤黄檀子渲
    　　髪先用墨染次用烟子渲有间渲排渲乱渲当自取用
    　　手指甲先用胭脂染次用粉染根
    　　凡染妇女面色胭脂粉衬薄粉笼淡檀墨斡
    　　凡染法白上先染后却罩粉然后再染提掇绢则先衬背后

    Explanation

    218

    Painting Techniques

    All facial coloring begins by mixing three types of cinnabar, fine powder, yellow earth, sandalwood, and blue with ink to create a base layer. Over this base, a thin layer of powder is lightly applied. Then, using a mixture of sandalwood and ink, the facial colors are blended and shaded. For a white complexion, a small amount of yellow earth is added to the white powder, and "燕支" (a type of red pigment) is not used in place of "胭脂" (another type of red pigment). For a red complexion, a small amount of "土朱" (a type of red pigment) is added to the mixture. For a purple complexion, a small amount of "胭脂" is added to the mixture of powder, sandalwood, and blue. For a yellow complexion, a small amount of "土朱" is added to the mixture of powder and yellow earth. For a blue-black complexion, a mixture of powder, sandalwood, and blue is used, with a thin layer of powder applied over it, and the final appearance is adjusted based on the clarity and darkness of the color. It is important not to be rigid in these techniques.

    For the corners of the mouth, "燕支" is lightly applied to create a subtle smile. The whites of the eyes are painted first, followed by the pupils, and then the eyelids, with a final touch to create a subtle smile. "胭脂" is applied to the lips, and the nose is colored with "胭脂." Freckles are painted using diluted ink and "麻檀水" (a type of ink). For black beards, the technique used is the same as for hair, and for purple beards, "檀墨" (a type of ink) is used intermittently. For yellow-red beards, a mixture of yellow earth and sandalwood is used. Hair is first dyed with ink, and then "烟子" (a type of ink) is used, with different methods for different styles. For nails, "胭脂" is first applied, and then powder is used to shade the base. For women's faces, "胭脂" and powder are used as a base, and a thin layer of powder is applied over it, followed by diluted "檀墨" (a type of ink). For painting on white fabric, the color is first applied, then covered with powder, and then more color is added. For silk, the background is first prepared, and then the color is applied.

    219

    凡调合服饰器用顔色者绯红用银朱紫花合　桃红用银朱胭脂合　肉红用粉为主入胭脂合栢枝緑用枝条緑入漆緑合　黒緑用漆緑入螺青合　栁緑用枝条緑入槐花合　官緑即枝条緑是　鸭头緑用枝条緑入髙漆緑合　月下白用粉入京墨合　柳黄用粉入三緑标并少藤黄合　鹅黄用粉入槐花合　砖褐用粉入烟合荆褐用粉入槐花螺青上黄标合　艾褐用粉入槐花螺青土黄檀子合　鹰背褐用粉入檀子烟墨土黄合　银褐用
    粉入藤黄合　珠子褐用粉入藤黄胭脂合　藕丝褐用粉入螺青胭脂合露褐用粉入少土黄檀子合　茶褐用土黄为主入漆緑烟墨槐花合　麝香褐用土黄檀子入烟墨合　檀褐用土黄入紫花合　山谷褐用粉入土黄标合　楛竹褐用粉土黄入檀子一防合湖水褐用粉入三緑合　葱白用粉入三緑标合　棠梨褐用粉入土黄银朱合　秋茶褐用土黄入三緑槐花合　油里墨用紫花土黄烟墨合玉色用粉入髙三緑合　鮀色用粉漆緑标墨

    Explanation

    219

    Final Translation

    All color mixtures for clothing, objects, and decorations:

    • 绯红 (crimson) is made by combining silver vermilion and purple flowers.
    • 桃红 (peach red) is made by combining silver vermilion and rouge.
    • 肉红 (flesh color) is made by combining powder with rouge.
    • 柏枝緑 (cypress branch green) is made by combining branch green with lacquer green.
    • 黒緑 (blackish green) is made by combining lacquer green with cuttlefish green.
    • 栁緑 (willow green) is made by combining branch green with locust flowers.
    • 官緑 (official green) is the same as 枝条緑 (branch green).
    • 鸭头緑 (duck head green) is made by combining branch green with high lacquer green.
    • 月下白 (moonlit white) is made by combining powder with imperial ink.
    • 柳黄 (willow yellow) is made by combining powder with three greens and a small amount of turmeric yellow.
    • 鹅黄 (goose yellow) is made by combining powder with locust flowers.
    • 砖褐 (brick brown) is made by combining powder with smoke.
    • 荆褐 (thorn brown) is made by combining powder with locust flowers, cuttlefish green, and upper yellow.
    • 艾褐 (mugwort brown) is made by combining powder with locust flowers, cuttlefish green, earth yellow, and sandalwood.
    • 鹰背褐 (eagle back brown) is made by combining powder with sandalwood, ink, and earth yellow.
    • 银褐 (silver brown) is made by combining powder with turmeric yellow.
    • 珠子褐 (pearl brown) is made by combining powder with turmeric yellow and rouge.
    • 藕丝褐 (lotus fiber brown) is made by combining powder with cuttlefish green and rouge.
    • 露褐 (dew brown) is made by combining powder with a small amount of earth yellow and sandalwood.
    • 茶褐 (tea brown) is made by combining earth yellow with lacquer green, ink, and locust flowers.
    • 麝香褐 (musk brown) is made by combining earth yellow with sandalwood and ink.
    • 檀褐 (sandalwood brown) is made by combining earth yellow with purple flowers.
    • 山谷褐 (mountain valley brown) is made by combining powder with earth yellow.
    • 楛竹褐 (bamboo brown) is made by combining powder, earth yellow, and sandalwood.
    • 湖水褐 (lake water brown) is made by combining powder with three greens.
    • 葱白 (onion white) is made by combining powder with three greens and a small amount of turmeric yellow.
    • 棠梨褐 (pear tree brown) is made by combining powder with earth yellow and silver vermilion.
    • 秋茶褐 (autumn tea brown) is made by combining earth yellow with three greens and locust flowers.
    • 油里墨 (oil ink) is made by combining purple flowers, earth yellow, and ink.
    • 玉色 (jade color) is made by combining powder with high three greens.
    • 鮀色 (fish color) is made by combining powder with lacquer green and upper yellow.
    220

    入少土黄合　氁子用粉土黄檀子入墨一防合蓝青用三青入髙三緑合　金黄用槐花粉入
    　　胭脂合　雅青用苏青衬螺青罩　鼠毛褐用土黄粉入墨合　不老红用紫花银朱合　葡卜褐用粉入三緑紫花合　丁香褐用肉红为主入少槐花合　杏子绒用粉墨螺青入檀子合　氁绫用紫花底紫粉搭花様　畨皮用土黄银朱合鹿胎用白粉底紫花様　水獭氊用粉土黄合牙笏用好粉一防土黄粉凝　皂鞾用烟墨标柘木交椅用粉檀子土黄烟墨合　金丝柘同上不入墨　紫袍用三青胭脂合　其余一一不能备载在对物用色可也
    　　凡合用顔色细色头青二青三青深中青浅中青螺青与苏青二緑三緑花叶緑枝条緑南緑油緑漆緑黄丹飞丹三朱土朱银朱枝红紫花藤黄槐花削粉石榴颗绵胭脂檀子其檀子用银朱浅入老墨胭脂合

    Explanation

    220

    Final Translation

    To mix a little yellow earth, add powdered yellow earth and sandarac, then mix in a little ink.
    For blue-green, use three blues and mix in high three greens.
    For golden yellow, add locust flower powder.
    For carmine, mix the colors.
    For elegant blue, mix soybean blue with seashell blue coating.
    For mouse fur brown, mix yellow earth powder with ink.
    For everlasting red, mix purple flower and cinnabar.
    For grape brown, mix powder with three greens and purple flower.
    For clove brown, use flesh red as the main component and add a little locust flower.
    For apricot fur, mix powdered ink, seashell blue, and sandarac.
    For wool silk, mix purple flower base with purple powder and patterns.
    For foreign leather, mix yellow earth and cinnabar.
    For deer fetus, use white powder base with purple flower pattern.
    For otter felt, mix powder and yellow earth.
    For ivory tablet, use good powder, a little yellow earth powder, and solidify.
    For black boots, use smoke ink and mark with tamarisk wood.
    For armchair, mix powder, sandarac, yellow earth, and smoke ink.
    For golden thread tamarisk, same as above but no ink added.
    For purple robe, mix three blues and carmine.
    The rest cannot be fully listed; when using colors for objects, it is possible.

    All colors used in detail: top blue, second blue, third blue, deep medium blue, light medium blue, seashell blue, soybean blue, second green, third green, flower leaf green, branch green, southern green, oil green, lacquer green, yellow cinnabar, flying cinnabar, three cinnabars, earth cinnabar, silver cinnabar, branch red, purple flower, wisteria yellow, locust flower, powdered, pomegranate seeds, silk carmine, and sandarac. The sandarac is mixed with silver cinnabar, a little old ink, and carmine.

    221

    相地理
    　　江隂州宋季时兵马司在州治东南里许平地上司之后置土牢归附后有善地理者以为宜帝王居之人问其故曰君山龙脉正结于此是以知其然也皆弗之信越数年就其上起盖三皇庙亦竒术哉君山州之主山也
    　　狎娼遭毒
    　　姑苏郑君辅放浪不覊为漕府小吏时督运至直沽狎游羣娼佻逹太甚殊弗堪之或有进药于郑曰此助阳竒剂也郑试傅之数日后隂器消缩若阉宦然竟以此终其身漫书为后人戒
    　　梦
    　　应之绍【才】钱唐人以乡贡下第任嘉兴学正丁父忧仍寓居授徒至正壬辰秋避难于其诸生李氏子家去城数十里曰奉贤乡李之从祖号太无为道士住持紫虚观之绍一见若平生欢八月廿九日太无得中风疾之绍馈药疗之获苏日一再诣问九月四日又自紫虚问疾还寓忽得疾一中而殂其妻杨氏太史同佥瑀之女就所馆治丧且以讣其母若弟于海寜及嘉兴城中紫虚之徒以其疾与太无同不以告是夜将半太无忽呼弟子卓处潜辈谓曰适得梦甚怪俾取笔书之云于本观所奉岳祠之前见有某姓

    Explanation

    221

    Translation:

    On Geomancy
    The military command office of Jiangyin State during the late Song Dynasty was located on flat ground to the southeast of the state capital, with a prison built behind it after it came under submission. After the submission, a skilled geomancer believed that this location was suitable for a monarch to reside, and when asked the reason, he said that the Dragon Vein of Junshan Mountain was properly formed here, which is why he knew it to be so. However, no one believed him. Several years later, a temple dedicated to the Three Sovereigns was built on the site, a truly remarkable feat. Junshan is the main mountain of the state.

    Encountering Poison from Prostitution
    Zheng Junfu of Gusu lived a free and unrestrained life, serving as a low-ranking official in the漕府 (a government office responsible for grain transportation). While supervising the transport of grain to Zhigou, he indulged in the company of prostitutes, behaving in a way that was far too reckless for him to bear. Someone offered him a medicine, saying it was a unique remedy to enhance male virility. Zheng tried it, and several days later, his genitalia shrank and became like those of a eunuch. He ultimately died from this condition, and this is recorded as a warning for future generations.

    A Dream
    Ying Zhishao, a native of Qiantang, failed the provincial examination and was appointed as the headmaster of the school in Jiaxing. After the death of his father, he continued to live and teach students. In the autumn of the year 1352 (the third year of the Tian Ren era), he sought refuge in the home of one of his students, Mr. Li, who lived dozens of miles outside the city in Fuxian Village. Li’s uncle, known as Taiwu, was a Taoist master who served as the abbot of Zixu Monastery. Ying met Taiwu for the first time and felt as if they had known each other all their lives. On the 29th day of the eighth month, Taiwu suffered from a stroke. Ying provided medicine to treat him, and he recovered. He visited Taiwu twice a day. On the 4th day of the ninth month, Ying returned from Zixu Monastery after visiting Taiwu’s illness. Suddenly, he fell ill and died shortly after. His wife, Yang, the daughter of a high-ranking official named Yu, handled the funeral arrangements at the place where he was staying and informed his mother and brother in Haining and Jiaxing. The disciples of Zixu Monastery believed that Taiwu’s illness was the same as his, and they did not report it. In the middle of the night, Taiwu suddenly called out to his disciple Zhu Chuqian, saying, “I just had a strange dream. Please bring me a pen and paper to write it down.” He wrote, “In front of the shrine to the deity we worship in this monastery, I saw someone with the surname of a certain family.”

    222

    名一吏及卒二人押男女各一并持公文而来因读其词曰嘉兴路城隍司准海宁州城隍司牒为陆小莲告至正八年内溺水事寃屈未伸今发陈喜儿应伟前去勒要应才同解岳祠周府君取问太无询来使之详荅曰陆小莲者嘉兴百福坊人而应才之婢也为其妻妬逐之遂赴水死陈喜儿者才之母也时居海宁伟字之竒才之弟也居嘉兴城东谓彼时不为救防故连逮耳太无见陈氏带鎻衣白衣黄裙问之年六十有四应伟荷校衣青衣録其罪状皆歴歴可记来使云今若贵司移牒温都统为之解释则尚可也遂觉始

    Explanation

    222

    An official and two soldiers escorted a man and a woman, each holding a public document, and arrived. Upon reading the document, it stated: "The City God's Office of Jiaxing received a dispatch from the City God's Office of Hai Ning regarding the unjust grievances of Lu Xiao Lian, who drowned in water in the eighth year of the Zhizheng era. Her grievances remain unredressed. Therefore, Chen Xi Er and Ying Wei are compelled to go and question Ying Cai, who will be taken to the temple of Yue Fei and the deity Zhou, to be interrogated by Tai Wu. Tai Wu inquired of the envoy for a detailed response, which stated: 'Lu Xiao Lian was a woman from Bai Fu Fang in Jiaxing and was Ying Cai's servant. She was driven away by his wife and subsequently drowned herself. Chen Xi Er is Ying Cai's mother, who lived in Hai Ning. Ying Wei, whose name is Qi, is Ying Cai's younger brother, living in the eastern part of Jiaxing. They claimed that they were not there to rescue her, hence they were implicated. Tai Wu saw Chen Xi Er, who was wearing a white robe and a yellow skirt, and asked her. She was sixty-four years old. Ying Wei was wearing a green robe and carrying a record of his crimes, all of which were clearly recorded. The envoy said that if your office sends a dispatch to Wen Du Tong, requesting an explanation, it is still possible. Thus, they realized the beginning of the matter.'"

    223

    知之绍已逝王昌言与之绍有交承之好同寓其所明旦来紫虚太无因问应母之年及之竒之貎皆如所梦乃以告之昌言驰报杨氏杨即诣紫虚拜恳太无于床下谓梦中事皆实有之复自诉其详且言其夫胸间尚温手足犹软故求移文解释仍躬祷岳祠冀之绍之复生也是日午后之竒自城东来衣青衣云昨日亦得疾与兄同所见如太无梦今虽少苏犹愦愦莫知所以然至夜杨氏以忧惧亦疾作旋即无他而之绍气已絶矣时建徳邵清溪偶宿紫虚目击其事翌日遂行不知往讣陈氏者归报何如及之竒之死生耳

    Explanation

    223

    Zhi Zhi Shao has passed away. Wang Changyan had a friendly and respectful relationship with Zhi Zhi Shao and lived together in his place. The next morning, Purple Void Taiwu came, and he asked about the age of Zhi Zhi Shao's mother and the appearance of his son. All were as in the dream. He then told this to Changyan, who hurriedly reported it to Yang. Yang immediately went to Purple Void and prostrated and pleaded with Taiwu on the bed, saying that the things in the dream were all real. He then recounted the details, mentioning that his husband's chest was still warm and his limbs were still soft, so he requested an official document to explain the situation and personally prayed at the temple of Mount Hua, hoping that Zhi Zhi Shao would come back to life. On that day, after noon, Zhi Qi came from the east of the city, wearing a green robe and saying that he had also fallen ill yesterday and saw the same as in the dream of Taiwu. Now although slightly revived, he is still confused and does not know why. By night, Yang also became ill out of worry and fear, but soon there was no other harm, while Zhi Zhi Shao's breath had already ceased. At that time, Shao Qingxi of Jiande happened to stay at Purple Void and witnessed the event. The next day, he left, not knowing how the messenger who went to inform Chen's family had reported, and whether Zhi Qi died or lived.

    224

    白醉
    　　开元时髙太素商山起六逍遥馆各制一铭其三为冬日初出铭曰折胶堕指梦想背金锣腾空映檐白醉见清异録楼攻媿尝取白醉二字以铭阁贤母辞拾遗钞
    　　聂以道宰江右一邑日有村人早出卖菜拾得至元钞十五定归以奉母母怒曰得非盗来而欺我乎纵有遗失亦不过三两张耳宁有一束之理况我家未尝有此立当祸至可急速送还毋累我为也言之再子弗从母曰必如是我须诉之官子曰拾得之物送还何人母曰但于原拾处俟定有失主来矣子遂依命携往顷间果见防钞者村
    人本朴质竟不诘其数便以付还傍观之人皆令分取为赏失主靳曰我原三十定方才一半安可赏之争閙不已相持至防事下聂推问村人其词实又宻唤其母审之合乃俾二人各具失者实三十定得者实十五定文状在官后却谓失主曰此非汝钞必天赐贤母以养老者若三十定则汝钞也可自别寻去遂给付母子闻者称快女奴义烈

    Explanation

    224

    A State of White Drunkenness

    During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of the Tang Dynasty, Gao Taosu built six leisurely pavilions in the Shangshan Mountains, each with its own inscription. One of these, dedicated to the early morning sun in winter, was inscribed with the words "bending glue and dropping fingers, dreaming of distant memories, a golden drum rising into the air and reflecting off the eaves, a state of white drunkenness." These words were recorded in the Qingyi Lu (Record of the Strange and Unusual). Lou Gongkui once used the term "white drunkenness" in a specific context, inscribing it in the Gexian Mu Ci Shiyi Chao (Collection of Lost and Forgotten Works).

    Nie Yidao, an official governing a county in the western part of Jiang Province, encountered a villager one day who had gone out early to sell vegetables. The villager found fifteen units of the Yuan currency called "Zhiyuan Chao" and returned home to present them to his mother. His mother scolded him, saying, "Did you not steal these and deceive me? Even if you lost something, it would not be more than three or two units. How could there be a bundle of them? Moreover, our family has never had such wealth. If you keep them, disaster will surely come. Return them immediately and do not involve me in this!" She repeated her words, but her son did not comply. The mother then said, "If you insist on keeping them, I must report you to the authorities." The son replied, "What should I do with the found money? Who should I return it to?" The mother instructed him, "Just go back to the place where you found them and wait. The owner will surely come looking for it." The son followed her advice and returned the money. Soon enough, the owner appeared. The villager, who was simple and honest, did not question the amount and returned the money to the owner. The bystanders suggested that the owner should reward the villager, but the owner refused, saying, "I originally had thirty units, but I only found half of them. How could I reward you?" The two argued and quarreled, and the matter was brought before Nie Yidao. He questioned the villager, who confirmed the truth of the story. Nie then secretly summoned the mother and examined the situation. After verifying the details, he had both parties write down their accounts: the owner claimed to have lost thirty units, while the villager had found fifteen. The documents were presented to the authorities. Nie then told the owner, "This is not your money. It must be a divine gift to your virtuous mother for her old age. If you had thirty units, you would have found them. You may search elsewhere." He then returned the money to the mother and son. The people who heard the story praised the woman's virtue and the slave's loyalty.

    225

    多纳克者杭城东辉和尔氏女奴也年十九勤敏谨愿主卒某郡官所多纳克奉主妇日谨主妇亦委以腹心至正壬辰秋七月初十日冦防杭刼官民府库至辉和氏家不得物乃反接主妇柱下防刀砺颈上诸侍婢皆散走多纳克独以身覆主妇请代死且告曰将军利吾财岂利杀人哉凡家之货寳皆我所藏主母固弗知若免主母死我当悉与将军不吝冦允解主妇防多纳克乃探金银珠玉币帛等散置堂上冦争夺之竟又欲犯多纳克身多纳克持刀欲自屠曰我主二千石我誓不奴他姓主况汝贼乎冦惊异舍而去多纳克泣拜主妇曰弃主货全主命权也妾受命主钥货今失货而全身非义也请从此死遂自杀时人莫不称之曰义烈义烈云

    Explanation

    225

    Dona K was the female slave of the Huihe family from the eastern part of Hangzhou. She was nineteen years old, diligent, quick-witted, and trustworthy. Her master died in a certain official's residence in a prefecture. She served the mistress with daily diligence and was entrusted with her innermost thoughts and affairs. In the year Renchen of the Zhi Zheng era, on the 10th day of the 7th month, the rebels invaded Hangzhou and looted the official and civilian treasuries. They tied the mistress to a pillar and placed a knife against her neck. All the female servants fled, but Dona K alone covered the mistress with her body and begged to die in her place. She said to the general, "General, you seek our wealth, not to kill us. All the household treasures are in my possession, and the mistress certainly does not know. If you spare the mistress's life, I will give you all the treasures without hesitation." The general agreed and untied the mistress. Dona K then retrieved the gold, silver, jewels, and silk and scattered them on the hall. They fought over them until they wanted to assault Dona K. She held a knife and wanted to kill herself, saying, "My master was a high-ranking official; I swear I will never be a slave to another master." She said, "To abandon the master's goods to save the master's life is a temporary act. I was entrusted with the master's keys and treasures; now that I have lost the treasures but saved my own life, it is not right." She then committed suicide. At that time, everyone praised her as a model of righteousness and bravery.

    226

    龙广寒
    　　龙广寒江西人移居钱唐挟预知之术游湖海间咸推为异人或谓专持寂感报耳秘呪故尔寂感即俗所谓万回哥哥之师号也释氏传灯録师姓张九嵗乃能语兄戌安西父母遣问讯朝往夕返以万里而回号万回又防法论虢州阌乡张万回法云公者生于唐贞观六年五月五日有兄万年乆征辽左相去万里母程氏思其信音公早晨先母而徃至暮持书而还防法论
    乃宋无尽居士张商英譔必有所据按此则师之灵通容有之广寒又行服气导引之法常佩小十数于身至晩仍解饲之事母至孝六月一日母生辰方举觞为夀忽见北窓外梅花一枝盛开人皆以为孝行所感士大夫遂称之曰孝梅赠诗者甚多惟张菊存一篇最可脍炙曰南风吹南枝一白防万緑嵗寒谁知心孟宗林下竹至治初间广寒卒时年百有八嵗犹童顔緑发云

    Explanation

    226

    Dragon Guanghan

    Dragon Guanghan, originally from Jiangxi, moved to Qiantang and traveled around lakes and seas with the skill of foreknowledge, and was considered an extraordinary person by all. Some said he specialized in holding the silent and responsive mantra, hence the name "Jigan." Jigan is the title given to the teacher of the legendary figure known as "Brother Wanhui," who could travel ten thousand miles in a day. According to the Transmission of the Lamp by the Buddhist tradition, the teacher's surname is Zhang, and he could speak at the age of nine. His elder brother was sent to the west, and their parents sent him to deliver messages. He would travel to the west in the morning and return by evening, hence the name "Wanhui." Another treatise, Fangfa Lun, mentions Zhang Wanhui, known as "Fayun Gong," who was born on the fifth day of the fifth month in the sixth year of the Zhen Guan era of the Tang Dynasty. He had an elder brother named Wan Nian who had been away for a long time on a military campaign in the east, and their mother, Cheng, missed his letters. In the morning, he would leave before his mother and return by evening with letters, according to Fangfa Lun.

    This treatise was written by Master Zhang Shangying, a devout Buddhist from the Song Dynasty, who must have had a basis for his claims. According to this, the teacher's spiritual powers may indeed have been remarkable. Dragon Guanghan also practiced breathing exercises and guiding techniques, often wearing several small turtles on his body, and at night, he still released and fed them, showing great filial piety. On the first day of the sixth month, on his mother's birthday, he raised a cup to toast her, and suddenly saw a branch of plum blossoms blooming outside the north window. Everyone believed this was a sign of his filial piety, and scholars and officials began to call it the "Filial Plum." Many people wrote poems to praise him, but one poem by Zhang Jucun was the most delightful: "The south wind blows on the south branch, one white against a thousand greens. Who knows the heart of the cold season, the bamboo under the pine?" In the early years of the Zhizhi era, Dragon Guanghan passed away at the age of one hundred and eight, still with a youthful appearance and green hair.

    227

    夜航船
    　　凡篙师于城埠市镇人烟凑集去处招聚客旅装载夜行者谓之夜航船太平之时在处有之然古乐府有夜航船曲皮日休诗有明朝有物充君信防酒三瓶寄夜航之句则此名亦古矣
    　　不快
    　　世谓有疾曰不快陈夀作华陀传亦然
    　　雷雪
    　　至正庚子二月六日浙西诸郡震霆掣电雪大如掌顷刻积深尺余人甚惊异后阅李复中青唐杂记云宋元符二年九月廿一日夜镇洮大雷自初更至四鼓凡一百三十余雷雪深二尺后旬日西羌叛以有备无患出师大捷又周宻癸辛杂识云庚寅正月二十九日癸酉余至博陆大雷雪下如织而雷不止天地为之陟黒平生所未见据二説如此然杭州自去嵗十二月被围至三月兵退岂即青唐之防与
    　　分疏
    　　人之自辨白其事之是否者俗曰分防防平声汉书盎传以不亲为解顔师古注曰解者今分防矣北齐书祖珽髙元海奏珽不合作领军并与传广寜王交结珽亦见帝令引入自分防

    Explanation

    227

    Translation

    Night River Boats

    In general, boatmen gather travelers at city ports, market towns, and bustling areas and load them onto boats for nighttime travel. These are called night river boats, which were common during times of peace. However, there were night river boat songs in the ancient Guoyu (National Music Bureau), and Pi Rihou's poetry includes a line: "The next day, with something to confirm your trust, three bottles of wine sent by night river boat." Thus, this name is also ancient.

    Illness

    The world says that illness is called "not feeling well," and Chen Shou, in his biography of Hua Tuo, also uses this term.

    Thunder and Snow

    On February 6, 1340, in the western regions of Zhejiang, there was a thunderstorm with lightning and snow as large as a palm, accumulating to more than a foot deep in a moment, which was very surprising. Later, reading Li Fuzhong's Qingtang Zaji, it is recorded that on September 21, 1099, in the region of Zhenzhou, there was a thunderstorm with lightning from the first watch to the fourth watch, with more than 130 thunderclaps and snow two feet deep. Ten days later, the Western Qiang rebelled, but because they were well-prepared, the army emerged victorious. Also, in Zhou Mi's Guixin Zashi, it is recorded that on January 29, 1340, in Bolu, there was a thunderstorm with lightning and snow falling like a web, and the thunder did not stop, turning the sky and earth black, which I had never seen before. According to these two accounts, such phenomena are rare. However, Hangzhou was besieged from December of last year until March when the troops withdrew. Could this be the same phenomenon?

    Self-Explanation

    When a person explains or defends the truth of a matter, it is commonly referred to as "self-explanation" (分防). In the Hanshu (History of the Former Han Dynasty), it is noted that   盎 used the term "not being personally involved in the explanation." 顔 师 古 注 曰 "The explanation is now what we call 'self-explanation.'" In the Beiqi Shu (History of the Northern Qi Dynasty), it is recorded that 祖 珽 and 高 元 海 accused 祖 珽 of inappropriate behavior and suggested he should not lead troops and also accused him of being involved with 广 寜 王. 祖 珽 also saw the emperor and was ordered to explain himself.

    228

    西皮
    　　髹器谓之西皮者世人误以为犀角之犀非也乃西方马自黒而丹自丹而黄时复改易五色相叠马镫磨擦有凹处粲然成文遂以髹器仿为之事见因话録
    　　暖屋
    　　今之入宅与迁居者隣里醵金治具过主人饮谓之曰暖屋或曰暖房王建宫词太仪前日暖房来则暖屋之礼其来尚矣
    　　室
    　　温州监郡某一女及笄未出室貌美而性慧父母之所钟爱者以疾卒命画工写其像嵗序张设哭奠常时则庋置之任满偶忘取去新监郡复居是屋其子未婚忽得此心窃念曰娶妻能若是平生愿事足矣因以悬于卧室一夕见其下从轴中诣榻前叙殷勤遂与好合自此无夜不来逾半载形状羸弱父母诘责以实告且云至必深夜去以五鼓或赍佳果啖我我荅与饼饵则坚郤不食父母教其此番须力劝之既而女不得辞为咽少许天渐明竟不可去宛然人耳特不能言语而已遂真为夫妇而病亦无恙矣此事余童子时闻之甚熟惜不能记两监郡之名近读杜荀松窓

    Explanation

    228

    Translation

    Xipi
    Lacquerware known as "Xipi" is mistakenly thought to be made from rhinoceros horn. It is not; it is a type of Western horse saddle that changes color from black to red and back, with multiple colors layered and worn by friction, creating patterns. The lacquerware imitates this. This is recorded in Yin Hua Lu.

    Nuanwu
    Today, when someone moves into a new home or relocates, neighbors pool money to prepare a feast for the homeowner, calling it "Nuanwu" or "Nuanfang." Wang Jian's Gongci mentions that the day before the ceremony, the Master of Ceremonies would say, "Warm the house," indicating that this custom has been around for a long time.

    Gui Shi
    In Wenzhou, a certain official's daughter, who was of marriageable age but had not yet married, was beautiful and intelligent, much loved by her parents. She died of illness, and they commissioned an artist to paint her portrait. Every year, during the seasonal celebrations, the portrait was displayed and used in mourning rituals. After a period, it was stored away. When the official's term ended, he forgot to take the portrait with him. A new official then took residence in the same house. His son, who was still unmarried, suddenly had a desire for this woman. He thought to himself, "If I could marry a woman like this, my lifelong wish would be fulfilled." So, he hung the portrait in his bedroom. One night, he saw her descend from the scroll, approach his bed, and express her affection. They became intimate, and she visited him every night. After half a year, she appeared weak and frail. Her parents questioned her, and she confessed the truth, saying that she would always come at night and leave by dawn, sometimes bringing him good fruit. If he offered her bread or cakes, she would refuse to eat them. Her parents advised him to try harder to persuade her. Eventually, she could not refuse and ate a little bit. As dawn approached, she could not leave and remained as a human, though unable to speak. They became true husband and wife, and her illness was miraculously cured. I heard this story as a child and remember it well, but I cannot recall the names of the two officials. Recently, I read about it in Du Xunhe's Songchuang.

    229

    杂记云唐进士赵顔于画工处得一软障图一妇人甚丽顔谓画工曰世无其人也如可令生余愿纳为妻工曰余神画也此亦有名曰真真呼其名百日昼夜不歇即必应之应则以百家防灰酒灌之必活顔如其言乃应曰诺急以百家防灰酒灌之遂活下步言笑饮食如常终嵗生一儿儿年两嵗友人曰此妖也必与君为患余有神劒可斩之其夕遗顔劒劒才及顔室真真乃曰妾南岳地仙也无何为人画妾之形君又呼妾名既不夺君愿今疑妾妾不可住言讫携其子却上软障覩其障惟添一孩子皆是画焉读竟转懐旧闻已三十余年若杜公所书不虚则监郡子之异遇有之矣

    Explanation

    229

    The Miscellaneous Records say that the Tang dynasty scholar-official Zhao Yan obtained a soft screen painting of a very beautiful woman from a painter. Zhao Yan said to the painter, "There is no such person in the world, but if she could be brought to life, I would wish to marry her." The painter replied, "I am a divine painter. She also has a name, called Zhenzhen. If you call her name for a hundred days without ceasing, night and day, she will surely respond. When she responds, pour the mixture of ashes from a hundred families and wine over her, and she will surely come to life." Zhao Yan followed his words, and she responded, "Yes." He quickly poured the mixture over her, and she came to life. She then spoke, laughed, ate, and drank as usual. After a year, she gave birth to a child. The child was two years old when a friend said, "This is a demon and will surely cause you trouble. I have a divine sword that can cut her down." That night, the friend gave Zhao Yan the sword. As soon as the sword entered Zhao Yan's room, Zhenzhen said, "I am a land fairy from the Nanling Mountains. Without any reason, I was painted by someone. You called my name, and now you doubt me. I cannot stay." She then took her child and returned to the soft screen. When they looked at the screen, they saw that it only added a child, all of which were paintings. After reading this, Zhao Yan reflected on old tales and realized it had been more than thirty years since the event. If what Du Fu wrote was not false, then the unique encounter of the official from the supervisory province must have occurred.

    230

    牙郎
    　　今人谓驵侩者为牙郎本谓之互郎谓主互市事也唐人书互作牙互与牙字相似因讹而为牙耳
    　　墓尸如生
    　　松江蟠龙塘普门寺侧一无主古墓至正己亥春为其里之张雕盗发有志石乃宋时钱叅政良仁妹讳恵净以该恩奏封孺人生一男五女年六十有五尝舍田入寺因于绍兴四年十月祔夫墓之右破棺无气顔色如生口脂面泽若初傅者冠服鲜新亦不朽腐得金银首器皿甚多至放其绣履传相玩弄人以为异余闻汉广川王去疾发魏王子且渠冡无棺椁有石床床下悉是云母末上二尸一男一女皆年二十余东首祼卧顔色如生人髪亦如生人此恐云母之功今此妇日距今百七八十年而亦不损壊其理又何邪

    Explanation

    230

    Brokerage Intermediaries

    Today, people refer to brokers as "牙郎," which was originally called "互郎," meaning someone who manages mutual trade. In Tang Dynasty texts, "互" was written as "牙," and since "互" and "牙" look similar, the term was mistakenly changed to "牙郎."

    Lifelike Corpse

    Near the Pumen Temple along the Panlong Pond in Songjiang, there was an unowned ancient tomb. In the spring of the 13th year of the Zhizheng era (1349 CE), it was discovered by a local man named Zhang Diao, who broke into it. Inside, there was a tombstone inscribed with the name of Lady Huixin, the sister of the Song Dynasty official Qian Liangren, who was posthumously enfeoffed as a "孺人" due to her merits. She had one son and five daughters, lived to be sixty-five years old, and had donated land to the temple. She was buried to the right of her husband's tomb in the tenth month of the fourth year of the Shaoxing era (1134 CE). When the coffin was opened, there was no breath, but the appearance was as if she were still alive. Her lips were painted, and her face was moist, as if she had just been made up. Her clothing was fresh and new, and it did not show signs of decay. Many gold and silver jewelry and utensils were found, including her embroidered shoes, which were passed around to be examined. People found this strange. I have heard that during the reign of King Guangchuan of Han, a tomb was discovered that belonged to Prince Quchi of Wei. There was no coffin or casket, only a stone bed, and underneath it was a layer of talc powder. On the bed lay the bodies of a man and a woman, both in their twenties, lying face east without clothing. Their appearance was as if they were still alive, and their hair was also as if they were still alive. This was likely due to the power of the talc. Today, this woman has been buried for over 170 years, and her body has not deteriorated. What is the reason for this?

    231

    枯井有毒
    　　平江在城峨嵋桥叶剃者门首檐下有一枯井深可丈许偶所畜猫堕入适隣家浚井遂与井夫钱一缗俾下取猫夫父子诺子既入井乆不出父继入视之亦不出叶惶恐系索于腰令家人次第放索将及井底亟呼救命比拽起下体已僵木如尸而气息奄奄乡里救活之白于官官来验视令火下烛彷佛见若有旁空者向之死人一横卧
    地上一斜倚不倒钩其髪提出遍身无恙止紫黒耳众议以恐是蛟蜃之属实之土焉余意山岚蛮瘴尚能杀人何况乆年干涸隂毒凝结纳其气而死复奚疑哉此事在至正己亥秋八月初旬也后读酉阳杂爼有云凡冡井间气秋夏多杀人先以鸡毛投之直下无毒回舞而下者不可犯当以泔数斗浇之方可入矣得此一章信余意之诚是也

    Explanation

    231

    A Dry Well Was Poisonous

    In Pingjiang, a city in the province of Jiangsu, near the Mount Emei Bridge and the barber's shop, there was a dry well that was about ten feet deep. One day, a cat owned by someone accidentally fell into the well. Just then, a neighbor was dredging the well. The owner of the cat gave the well-digger one string of coins to go down and retrieve the cat. The well-digger and his son agreed, but the son went down the well and stayed there for a long time without coming out. The father then went down to see him and also did not come out.

    The barber was terrified, tied a rope around his waist, and had his family members pull the rope one by one until they almost reached the bottom of the well, then shouted for help. When they pulled him up, his lower body was stiff and wooden like a corpse, and he was barely breathing. The villagers revived him and reported the incident to the authorities. The official came to investigate and ordered a torch to be lit. They vaguely saw what seemed to be a side opening, and towards it, a dead person lay horizontally on the ground and another leaned diagonally without falling over. They hooked their hair and pulled them out, finding that the whole body was unharmed except for purple-black ears.

    The crowd speculated that it might be a dragon or a sea monster, and they filled the well with earth. I believe that the mist and瘴气 (miasma) can still kill people, let alone a well that has been dry for many years, with toxic gases accumulating and causing death. How can we doubt this? This incident occurred in the early part of August in the year of the Fire Rooster (己亥) during the reign of Emperor Wuzong of Yuan (至正).

    Later, when I read the Youyang Zazu (Miscellaneous Records of Youyang), it said that in the summer and autumn, the air around tombs and wells often kills people. First, throw a chicken feather down; if it goes straight down, it is safe. If it dances back down, it is dangerous and should not be entered. It should be flooded with several buckets of water before entering. Upon reading this chapter, I believe my interpretation was correct.

    232

    贤孝
    　　前至元间杭州有郑万戸者天性峻急不能有所容而奉事母夫人备极孝道母诞日垂至预市文绣氁段制袍为夀鍼工持归缝缀既成为油所汚时估贵重工莫能偿自经不死隣妇有识其母者潜送入白之至日卧不起子至问安否见有忧色请其故曰昨莫偶视新袍适几上油缶飜溅渍成玷我情思殊不佳耳子告曰一袍壊复制一袍可也夫人何重惜乃尔母阳为自解遂起受子孙拜贺如常嵗仪人咸以此为贤母而益见万戸之孝国朝妇人礼服逹靼曰袍汉人曰团衫南人曰大衣无贵贱皆如之服章但有金素之别耳惟处子则不得衣焉今万戸有姓者而亦曰袍其母岂逹靼与然俗谓男子布衫曰布袍则凡上盖之服或可槩曰袍

    Explanation

    232

    Filial Piety
    In the period before the Yuan dynasty, there was a Viceroy Zheng in Hangzhou. He had a stern and quick-tempered nature, unable to tolerate anything, yet he served his mother with utmost filial piety. As his mother's birthday approached, he had previously purchased embroidered silk and wool fabrics to make robes as a birthday gift. The seamstress brought them back to sew them together, and after they were completed, they were soiled by oil. At the time, the work was considered valuable, and no one could compensate for it. He did not commit suicide. A neighbor woman who recognized his mother secretly sent in to inform her. On the day of the birthday, he lay down and did not get up. His son came to inquire about his well-being and saw a look of worry on his face. He asked the reason and said, "Yesterday, I happened to see the new robe. Just as an oil jar on the table overturned and splashed, staining it with a spot. My thoughts are particularly unpleasant." His son said, "If one robe is ruined, another can be made. Why does Mother value it so much?" Mother pretended to excuse herself and then got up to receive the blessings and congratulations of her descendants as usual. People all regarded her as a virtuous mother and thus saw even more clearly the filial piety of Viceroy Zheng.

    In the current dynasty, women's formal attire for the Tartars is called a robe, for Han people it is called a round robe, and for southerners it is called a large coat. Regardless of social status, everyone wears it like this. The only difference is in the gold and plain styles. Only unmarried girls are not allowed to wear them. Now, Viceroy Zheng has a surname and also calls it a robe. Is his mother a Tartar? However, in custom, a man's cotton shirt is called a cotton robe. Thus, any upper garment can be generally called a robe.

    233

    事物异名
    　　暇日读书遇事物之异名者偶记一二以备采览云割政【割剥之政也史记帝纪三】　　　父马【牡马也史记平凖书】
    　　毳布【罽也説文曰西胡毳布】　　　香物【梦书曰梦得香物妇女归也】
    　　藏鱼【説文鲊藏鱼也】　　　　　请室【狱也史盎】
    　　猊糖【狮子乳糖也后汉显宗记】　　　令草【宜男花也傅赋】
    　　毛席【氊也后汉西域传注】　　　　竹綀【竹布也庾翼与燕王书曰竹綀三端】
    　　竹萌【笋也説文】　　　　　练香【和香也李贺诗练香薰宋鹊】

    Explanation

    233

    Things with Alternative Names

    On leisurely days, while reading, I encountered a few things with alternative names and recorded them here for future reference:

    • Gēzhèng [Extraction of resources; plundering policy, as described in Shiji: Chronicle of the Emperors]
    • Fùmǎ [Male horse, as described in Shiji: Book of Equalization]
    • Cuìbù [Woolen fabric, also known as jì, as described in Shuowen Jiezi]
    • Xiāngwù [Fragrant objects, as described in Dream Interpretation]
    • Zàngyú [Preserved fish, as described in Shuowen Jiezi]
    • Qǐngshì [Prison, as described in Shiji: Biography of Xiang Yu]
    • Nítáng [Candy made from lion's milk, as described in History of the Later Han: Reign of Emperor Ming]
    • Lìngcǎo [The Yinán huā, a plant believed to bring good fortune, as described in Fu Xuan's Odes]
    • Máoxí [Rug or mat made from animal hair, as described in History of the Later Han: Western Regions]
    • Zhúshū [Fabric made from bamboo, as described in Yu Yi's Letter to the Prince of Yan]
    • Zhúméng [Bamboo shoots, as described in Shuowen Jiezi]
    • Liànxiāng [Incense or fragrance, as described in Li He's Poetry]
    234

    南威【橄榄也太平广记】　　　　石【樱桃也同上】
    　　木【子也同上】　　　　　杂馥【合香也通典四十三】
    　　脂炬【烛也杜阳杂编】　　　　　竹胎【笋也説文】
    　　调香【和香也华严经曰粥香长者善调香】　毛布【褐也诗七月笺】
    　　猎碣【石鼔曰猎碣苏朂载记】　　　玉洼【酒器也纬畧】
    　　挟日【从甲至甲凡十一日也周礼天官】　浃辰【辰十二辰自子至亥也左成九年】丹若【石榴也酉阳杂俎】
    　　金鎞刺肉
    　　茂巴尔字西瑛西域人其躯魁伟故人咸曰长西瑛一日方与妻对饭妻以小金鎞刺脔肉将入口门外有客至西瑛出肃客妻不及啖且置器中起去治茶比回无覔金鎞处时一小婢在侧执作意其窃取栲问万端终无认辞竟至损命嵗余召匠者整屋扫瓦瓴积垢忽一物落石上有声取视之乃向所失金鎞也与朽骨一块同坠原其所以必是猫来偷肉故带而去婢偶不及见而含寃以死哀哉世之事有如此者甚多姑书焉以为后人鉴也

    Explanation

    234

    Final Translation

    South Beauty (Nanwei) - [Olives, as noted in Taiping Guangji]
    Stone Pomegranate (Shiwo) - [Cherries, as noted in Taiping Guangji]
    Wooden Plum (Muxuo) - [Plums, as noted in Taiping Guangji]
    Mixed Fragrance (Zafu) - [A blend of fragrances, as noted in Tongdian]
    Fat Candle (Zhi Ju) - [Candles, as noted in Duyang Zabian]
    Bamboo Shoot (Zhu Tai) - [Bamboo shoots, as noted in Shuowen Jiezi]
    Blending Fragrances (Diao Xiang) - [Mixing fragrances, as noted in the Huayan Sutra]
    Coarse Cloth (Mao Bu) - [Coarse cloth, as noted in Shijing]
    Hunting Drum (Lie Jie) - [Stone drums inscribed with hunting scenes, as noted in Suxi Zaiji]
    Jade Wine Vessel (Yu Wa) - [A jade wine vessel, as noted in Weilue]
    Eleven Days (Xie Ri) - [A period of eleven days, as noted in Zhouli]
    Twelve Days (Jie Chen) - [A period of twelve days, as noted in Zuozhuan]
    Pomegranate (Dan Ruo) - [Pomegranates, as noted in Youyang Zazu]
    Golden Needle Pricking Meat (Jin Bia Ciri)

    Mao Bal Zi Xi Ying - A man from the Western Regions, whose name was Xi Ying. He was tall and robust, and people called him "Long Xi Ying." One day, while he was having a meal with his wife, she was about to eat a piece of meat pricked with a small golden needle when a guest arrived. Xi Ying went out to greet the guest, and his wife did not get to eat the meat. She placed it in the dish and went to make tea. When she returned, she could not find the golden needle. At that time, a small maid was nearby. Xi Ying suspected that the maid had taken it and questioned her severely, but the maid denied it. Eventually, Xi Ying accused her of stealing and had her executed. A year later, when he called a carpenter to repair the house and sweep the roof, a small object fell from the roof with a sound. He picked it up and found it to be the lost golden needle, along with a piece of rotten bone. He realized that it must have been a cat that had stolen the meat and taken the golden needle away. The maid had been wrongfully accused and died in sorrow. Alas, there are many such tragic events in the world. I record this to serve as a warning for future generations.

    235

    杭人遭难
    　　杭民尚淫奢男子诚厚者十不二三妇人则多以口腹为事不习女工至如日用饮膳惟尚新出而价贵者稍贱便鄙之纵欲买又恐贻笑隣里至正己亥冬十二月金陵游军斩闗而入突至城下城门闭三月余各路粮道不通城中米价踊贵一斗直二十五缗越数日米既尽糟糠亦与常日米价等有赀力人则得食贫者不能也又数日糟糠亦尽乃以油车家糠饼捣屑啖之老幼妇女三五为羣行乞于市虽姿色艶丽而衣裳济楚不暇自愧也至有合家父子夫妇兄弟结袂把臂共沈于水亦可怜已一城之人饿死者十六七军既退吴淞米航辐辏借以活而又太半病疫死岂平昔浮靡暴殄之过造物者有以警之与承天阁

    Explanation

    235

    The People of Hangzhou Suffer Calamity

    The people of Hangzhou indulge in excessive luxury. Of the men, not even two or three out of ten are truly honest and virtuous. Women are mostly preoccupied with food and drink, and do not learn women's work. For daily meals, they only value new and expensive items, considering slightly cheaper ones inferior, even if they want to buy them, they fear being ridiculed by their neighbors. In the year 1359, during the winter of December, roving troops from Nanjing cut through the gates and entered, suddenly arriving at the city walls. The city gates were closed for more than three months, and all supply routes were blocked. The price of rice in the city skyrocketed, with one dou worth twenty-five strings of cash. After several days, the rice was all gone, and coarse food also became as expensive as rice on normal days. Those with wealth were able to eat, but the poor could not. After several more days, the coarse food was also gone, and they then used bran cakes from the oil mill, ground into powder, and ate them. Elderly, young, and women begged in groups of three or five in the market. Even though they were beautiful, their clothes were neat, and they had no time to feel ashamed. There were even cases where entire families, including parents, children, spouses, and siblings, held hands and arms and drowned together in the water, a pitiful sight. Sixteen or seventeen out of twenty people in the city died of starvation. After the army withdrew, rice ships from Wusong converged, allowing the survivors to live, but most of them died of illness. How could it be that in the past, their excessive luxury and waste, their fault, was not a warning from the Creator? Perhaps it was a form of divine punishment. This is the story of the people of Hangzhou, recorded at the Hall of Heaven's Mandate.

    236

    平江承天寺初畜大木将造千佛阁防浙省灾责有司籍所在木植官酬以价寺一僧于阁木上皆凿万嵗阁三字于是有司不敢取及阁成其字固在诸寺观凡起造必作俪语题梁间其余则记住持檀越主名此所必然独承天诸殿俱否至正丙申春今张太尉【士诚】未归顺时伪称诚王国号大周改元天佑歴日明时由淮渡浙攻破平江即承天以居尽撤去殿上像设坐于其中且以僧元凿字名其阁岂亦有定数乎
    　　阿癐癐
    　　淮人防江南日于临阵之际齐声大喊阿癐癐以助军威按朝野佥载武后时沧州南皮县丞郭胜静每廵乡唤百姓妇托以缝补而奸之其夫至缚胜静鞭数十主簿李懋往救解之胜静羞讳其事低身荅云忍痛不得口唱阿癐癐胜静不被打阿癐癐据此乃有所本

    Explanation

    236

    Chengtian Temple in Pingjiang initially stored large timbers intended for the construction of a thousand-buddha pavilion. To prevent the provincial administration from being blamed for disasters, officials were instructed to record all local timber and compensate with money. A monk at the temple carved the words "Ten Thousand Years Pavilion" on the timber, and thus, officials dared not take the timber. When the pavilion was completed, the words remained. In all temples and observatories, when constructing, they always inscribed paired phrases on the beams, and the rest were recorded with the names of donors and patrons; this is a necessity. But the halls of Chengtian Temple did not do this. In the spring of the Bing Shen year of the Zhizheng era, when Zhang Taibao (Shicheng) had not yet surrendered, he falsely claimed to be the king of Chengguo, established the state of Great Zhou, changed the era name to Tianyou, and when the Ming dynasty forces crossed from Huai to Zhejiang, they broke into Pingjiang and occupied Chengtian Temple, removing all images and sitting there, naming the pavilion after the monk Yuan who carved the words, was this also predestined?

    "Ah Yiyi"

    When the Huai people were defending the south, they shouted "Ah Yiyi" in unison during battle to boost morale. According to Chao Ye Qian Zai, during the reign of Empress Wu, the county magistrate of Nanpi in Cangzhou, Guo Shengjing, would call upon women in the village under the pretext of mending clothes and then commit adultery with them. His wife bound him and beat him dozens of times. The county clerk Li Mao came to rescue him, and Guo Shengjing, ashamed and unwilling to admit his wrongdoing, replied, "I cannot bear the pain, so I sing 'Ah Yiyi.'" Guo Shengjing was not beaten because of "Ah Yiyi." This is the origin of the term.

    237

    海运
    　　国朝海运粮储自朱清张瑄始以为古来未尝有此按杜工部诗出塞云渔阳豪侠地击鼔吹笙竽云帆转辽海粳稻来东吴又昔游云幽燕盛用武供给亦劳哉吴门持粟帛泛海凌蓬莱如此则唐时已有海运矣朱张特举行耳
    　　夫妇死孝
    　　杜阳父【友开】江隂人隐居教授妻吴辟纑以资之天歴间浙右菑荒米价腾踊学徒散去困于饥饿吴之兄弟屡劝斩邱木粥基地以少延余息阳父坚持不可继欲挈吴归吴曰夫既尽孝妾独以不义自处寜不食若粟遂相枕藉而卒
    　　猪妖
    　　至正辛夘春江隂永寜乡陆氏家一猪产十四儿内一儿人之首面手足而猪身

    　　辍耕録卷十一
    <子部,小说家类,杂事之属,辍耕录>
    　　钦定四库全书

    Explanation

    237

    Maritime Grain Transport
    The maritime transport of grain for the state began with Zhu Qing and Zhang Xuan, who believed that it had never been done before. According to Du Fu's poem "Departure to the Frontier," it says, "In the land of Youyang, where heroes gather, drums beat and flutes play; ships sail across the Liaodong Sea, bringing rice to Eastern Wu." In another poem, "Former Travels," it says, "In the regions of You and Yan, where martial prowess is strong, supplying the troops is a laborious task; holding rice and silk, they sail across the sea, reaching the mythical island of Penglai." Thus, it is evident that maritime transport existed during the Tang dynasty. Zhu Qing and Zhang Xuan simply revived it.

    Wife's Filial Piety
    Du Yangfu (Youkai), a man from Jiangyin, lived in seclusion and taught. His wife, Wu, spun thread to support him. During the Tianli period, the region of Zhejiang suffered from famine, and the price of rice soared. His students dispersed, and they were starving. Wu's brothers repeatedly advised him to cut down trees and sell them to make a little more money. Yangfu firmly refused. He then wanted to take Wu back home, but Wu said, "My husband has fulfilled his filial duty, but I alone would be acting unrighteously. I would rather not eat this rice." They died side by side.

    Pig Monster
    In the spring of Xinmao in the Zhi Zheng era (1361), a pig in Yongning Village, Jiangyin, gave birth to fourteen piglets. One of them had a human head, face, hands, and feet, but the rest of its body was that of a pig.

    Miscellaneous Notes, Volume Eleven
    <Department of Literature, Subclass of Fiction, Category of Miscellaneous Matters, Miscellaneous Notes>
    Imperially Approved Siku Quanshu

    238

    辍耕録卷十二　　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰园池记
    　　唐南阳樊宗师字绍述所譔绛守居园池记艰深竒澁读之往往昧其句读况义乎哉韩文公谓其文不蹈袭前人一言一句观此记则诚然矣宋王晟刘忱尝为解释今不复有偶得滦阳赵仁举字伯昻笺注本句分字析词理焕然因书其记传其句读以便披览
    　　云有未解者又须观全注可也防法【○】为句　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　　【○】为读记曰绛即东雍【○雍去声】为守【去声】理所【○】禀参【所今切】实沈分【○分去声】气畜两河润【○】有陶唐冀遗风余思【○思去声】晋韩魏之相剥剖【○】世说总其土田士人【○】今无硗【口交切】杂扰【○】宜【○】得地形胜防水施法【○】岂新田又蕞猥不可居【○】州地或自有兴废【○州字或属上句】人因得附为奢俭【○】将为守恱致平理与【○与平声】益侈心耗物害时与【○与平声】自将失敦穷华【○】终披夷不可知【○】陴【音脾睨也疑作缅

    Explanation

    238

    Record of Pausing to Till, Volume Twelve
    By Tao Zongyi of the Yuan Dynasty
    Garden and Pond Records

    The Jiang Governor's Residence Garden and Pond Record by Fan Zongshi of Nanyang, Tang Dynasty, is deeply profound and obscure. When reading it, one often fails to grasp the sentence divisions, let alone the meaning. Han Yu of the Tang Dynasty said that his writing does not follow the footsteps of predecessors, not even a single word or phrase. Observing this record, one can indeed see the truth in his words. During the Song Dynasty, Wang Sheng and Liu Chen attempted to explain it, but such efforts are no longer available. Recently, I came across a commentary by Zhao Renju of Luan Yang, which divides sentences and analyzes words, making the meaning clear and bright. Therefore, I have recorded the text and transmitted its sentence divisions to facilitate reading.

    If there are still parts that are not understood, one must consult the full commentary. The method of sentence division is as follows:
    【○】 marks the end of a sentence.
    【○】 marks the end of a pause.

    The record says: Jiang is the Eastern Yong. The governor of Yong is responsible for the administration of the region. Fan Zongshi presented the record for review, dividing the region of Shenshen. The air nourishes the two rivers, and there are remnants of the customs and thoughts of the Yao and Shun era. The states of Jin, Han, and Wei were divided among themselves. The Shi Shuo summarizes the land and people. Today, there is no poor and mixed disturbance, and the land is suitable for cultivation. It obtains a favorable terrain and employs water management techniques. Is it newly cultivated land? No, it is too small and shabby to live in. The state's territory may have its own rise and fall, and people may follow suit in luxury and frugality. To please the governor and achieve peace and order, it increases luxury, wasting resources and harming the times. If it continues, it will lose its simplicity and become overly ornate, ultimately revealed as barbaric and unknowable. The defensive structures are piled and watchtowers are built.

    239

    】孤颠【○】跒倔【上苦下切下渠勿切】武踞【○】守居割有北【○】自甲辛苞太池【○】横硖旁【○】潭中癸次【○】木腔暴三丈【○】余【或属上句】涎玉沫珠【○】子午梁贯亭曰洄涟【○】虹蜺雄雌【○】穹鞠觑蜃【○时忍切】碍佷【胡恳切】岛坻【音池】淹淹委委【平声】莎靡缦【○莫半切】萝蕃翠蔓红刺相拂缀【○】南连轩井【○】阵中涌曰香【○】承守寝睟【虽遂切】思【○】西南有门曰虎豹【○】左书虎搏【各切】立【○】万力千气【○】底【音防】发【○】匿地【○】努肩脑口牙快【○】电火雷风黑山震将合【○】右胡人鬅【○】黄防【于

    Explanation

    239

    A solitary peak, rugged and defiant, the Northern region is guarded by the Xuanwu (Black Warrior). The territory is divided, with the Jia and Xin (甲辛) embracing the vast Tai Chi (太池). Beside the Hengxia (横硖), in the depths of the Kui (癸次) pool, a tree stands tall, three zhang (丈) high. Its sap flows like jade and pearls. The Zi-Wu (子午) bridge connects the pavilion, named Hui Lian (洄涟). The rainbow, male and female, arches across the sky. The vast, curved horizon meets the mirage. Obstacles and challenges abound, islands and sandbars dot the landscape. Slowly and gradually, the grass and reeds intertwine, the vines and thorns brush against each other. To the south, the Xuanjing (轩井) pavilion is connected. In the midst of the array, a fragrant spring emerges. The Chengshou (承守) palace, where the emperor rests, is serene. Thoughts linger. In the southwest, a gate named Hu Bao (虎豹) stands. To the left, a book records the struggle of tigers, standing firm. Vast forces and energies surge forth. Beneath the surface, hidden activities unfold. The shoulders and head strain, the mouth and teeth snap. Lightning, fire, thunder, and wind, the black mountain trembles, ready to unite. To the right, the Hu Ren (胡人) are disheveled, their yellow banners unfurled.

    240

    元切】累【力追切】珠【○】丹碧锦袄【○】身刀囊鞾檛縚【○上刀切】白豹班【○】饫距【○】掌脾【○】意相得【○】东南有亭曰新【○】前含【音领】曰槐【○】有槐屃【虚器切】防【○】防郁防后颐【○】渠决决緑池西直南折庑赴【○】可宴可衙【○】又东骞渠曰望月【○骞音轩】又东骞穷角池【○】硏云曰栢【○】有栢苍青官士【○】拥列与槐朋友【○】巉【鉏衔切】隂洽色【○】北俯渠【○】憧憧来【○】刮级面西【○】巽防【疑作隅】间【○】黄原玦天【○】汾水钩带【○】白言谒【○】行旦艮间【○】逺冈青萦【○】近楼台井闾防画察【○】可四

    Explanation

    240

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    As for farmers, merchants, artisans, and traders, as well as servants, laborers, and slaves, those engaged in fishing and butchering, feeding oxen and herding sheep—all have predecessors who can serve as role models. By seeking knowledge broadly from them, nothing is unhelpful in one's endeavors.

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    As for farmers, merchants, artisans, and traders, as well as servants, laborers, and slaves, those engaged in fishing and butchering, feeding oxen and herding sheep—all have predecessors who can serve as role models. By seeking knowledge broadly from them, nothing is unhelpful in one's endeavors.

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    As for farmers, merchants, artisans, and traders, as well as servants, laborers, and slaves, those engaged in fishing and butchering, feeding oxen and herding sheep—all have predecessors who can serve as role models. By seeking knowledge broadly from them, nothing is unhelpful in one's endeavors.

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    As for farmers, merchants, artisans, and traders, as well as servants, laborers, and slaves, those engaged in fishing and butchering, feeding oxen and herding sheep—all have predecessors who can serve as role models. By seeking knowledge broadly from them, nothing is unhelpful in one's endeavors.

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    As for farmers, merchants, artisans, and traders, as well as servants, laborers, and slaves, those engaged in fishing and butchering, feeding oxen and herding sheep—all have predecessors who can serve as role models. By seeking knowledge broadly from them, nothing is unhelpful in one's endeavors.

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    As for farmers, merchants, artisans, and traders, as well as servants, laborers, and slaves, those engaged in fishing and butchering, feeding oxen and herding sheep—all have predecessors who can serve as role models. By seeking knowledge broadly from them, nothing is unhelpful in one's endeavors.

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings

    241

    时合竒士【○】观云风霜露雨雪【○】所为【去声】发生敛赋歌诗【○】正东曰苍塘【○】遵濒西漭望【○】瑶翻碧防【○】光文切镂【○】棃深挠挠【奴巧切】穷【○】正北曰风隄【○】乘携左右【○】隄势北囘股努【○】墆【徒计切】捩【刀计切】蹴墉【○】御渠歆池【○】南楯楹【○】景怪孎【○】蛟龙钩牵【○】寳灵蠯【○薄猛切一音睥】文文章章【○】隂欱【呼合切】垫【都念切】防【○呼括切】烟溃霭聚桃李兰【○】神君仙人衣裳雅冶【○】可防脱赤【○】西北曰鼇【○】防【音灰】原【○】开咍【呼来切】储【○】虚明【○】嵬眼耳

    Explanation

    241

    Gathering of Extraordinary Scholars
    Observing Clouds, Wind, Frost, Dew, Rain, and Snow
    What is Done, Arising, and Composing Poetry

    In the East, it is called Cangtang
    Following the shore of the Western Wilderness, gazing at the jade-like waves and green banks
    Intricate patterns, deep and intricate
    In the North, it is called Fengdi
    Carrying along the left and right
    The embankment turns north, straining the thighs
    Sinking, twisting, and kicking the walls
    Controlling the channels and delighting in the pools
    Southern pillars and beams
    Strange and captivating scenery
    Jade dragons entwined, pulling at each other
    Precious turtles and divine jellyfish
    Writings and articles
    Darkly swallowing, settling, and gathering mist, clustering peach blossoms, plum blossoms, and orchids
    Gods and immortals, their elegant and refined garments
    Preventing the shedding of crimson robes
    In the Northwest, it is called Ao
    Hiding, the original place
    Opening and laughing, storing
    Vastly bright and clear
    Strange eyes and ears

    242

    【○】可大客旅钟鼓乐【○】提鹇挈鹭【○】【音弼】池豪渠【○】憎乖怜围【○】正西曰白濵【○】荟【乌外切】深怜梨【○】素女雪舞百佾【○】水翠披【○】防防【虚郭切】干幅【○】迎西引东士长崖【○】挟横埒【○埒音劣】日夘酉【日或作自】樵途隖径幽委【○】虫鸟声无人【○】风日灯火之【○】昼夜漏刻诡姽【鱼毁切】绚化【○】大小亭饾池渠间【○】走池隄上亭后前【○】陴乘墉【○】如连山羣峰拥【○】地髙下【○】如原隰隄谿壑【○】水引古【○】自源三十里【○】凿髙【○】槽絶【○】窦墉【○】为【或作其】池沟沼渠瀑潨【音丛】潺终出

    Explanation

    242

    Great guests and travelers enjoy the sound of bells and drums. Carrying or holding objects, possibly in a ceremonial or ritual context. A large pool or channel, possibly in a water-related context. Hating discord and having compassion for those in need. Directly west, a white or sandy shore. Dense or thick, deep compassion or love for pears. A pure or white maiden dancing in the snow, with a large number of dancers. Water being spread or flowing, possibly in a poetic or descriptive context. A defensive or protective measure, dry and broad. Welcoming the west and guiding the east, a long cliff or ridge. Holding or controlling a horizontal boundary, possibly a reference to time or direction. A path through a forest or mountainous terrain. The sounds of insects and birds in an empty or deserted place. The interplay of wind, sunlight, and firelight. The passage of time day and night, transformed into something beautiful. Small and large pavilions arranged between pools and channels. Movement along a dike or embankment near a pool, with pavilions in front and behind. Walls or ramparts, connected and surrounded by peaks. The varying elevation of the land. The landscape as a mix of plains, lowlands, dikes, streams, and valleys. Ancient waterways or channels, from the source, thirty miles. Digging or carving into high places. A channel or groove that is cut off or blocked. A small opening or passage in a wall. The creation of pools, ditches, marshes, channels, waterfalls, and streams that flow out.

    243

    【○】汨汨【于笔切音骨非】街衖畦町阡陌间○入汾【○】巨木【○】资士悍【○】水沮【○将预切】宗族盛茂【○】旁防逺映【○】锦绣交菓枝香【○】畹丽丽【上下可通作一句】絶他郡【○】考其台亭沼池之増【○】葢豪王才侯袭以竒意相胜【○】至今过客尚往往有指可创起处【○】余退常吁【○】后其能无○果有不【○音否】建者【○】池由于【○】及【当作反】者雅文安【○薛雅裴文安二人】发土筑台为拒【○】几【平声】附于汚宫【○】水本于正平轨【○】病井卤生物瘠【○】引古【○】沃澣人便【○】几附于河渠【○】呜呼【○】为附于河渠则可

    Explanation

    243

    The water flows continuously through the narrow streets, alleys, and pathways, entering the Fen River. The region is home to large, ancient trees and valiant soldiers. The water is often stagnant or muddy, but the clans and families thrive, with distant and reflective views of beautiful and fragrant flowers intertwining. The area is outstanding among other counties, and the growth and development of pavilions, ponds, and pools are noteworthy. The wealthy and noble competed with innovative ideas, and even today, visitors often point out places of historical significance. I often sigh in resignation, wondering if future construction or development will be possible. The pools are connected to dilapidated palaces, and the water channels are correct and level. The soil is poor, and the land is barren, but by drawing upon ancient practices and irrigating the land, the people can benefit. If the pools are connected to the river channels, then all will be well.

    244

    【○】为汚于污宫其可【○】书以荐后君子【○】长庆三年五月十七日记又见一本亦注解者不着姓名所分句读与前略有不同处并附于此绛【□】即东雍为守理所【作一句】世説【□】土田【□】士人【□】宜【□】得地形胜【□】自将失暾穷华【□】陴孤颠【□】跒倔武【□】守居【○】割有北【□】自甲辛苞大池□横硖旁【作一句】潭中□癸次木腔【作一句□】瀑三丈余【作一句□】子午梁【□】虹蜺雄雌穹鞠觑蜃【作一句□】莎靡缦【□】南连轩井【□】阵□左画虎抟立【□】万力千气底发【作一句□】彘匿地【□】电火□雷风【□】右胡人□鬅【□】黄

    Explanation

    244

    To be polluted by the filthy palace, is it possible? To write to recommend to the noble gentleman. The third year of the Changqing era, May 17th, recorded. Another version was also seen with annotations, but the author's name is not mentioned; the sentence division and punctuation differ slightly from the previous one and are included here. Jiang is the Eastern Yong, where the governor's office is located, and it is a one-sentence statement. The Shi Shuo Xin Yu (A New Account of Tales of the World) refers to land or fields. Scholars or literati should obtain a strategic location. To lose the sun at the end of the day, reaching the edge of the flower, on the lonely peak, struggling in the dark, the governor's residence, to cut off the north, from Jia to Xin, surrounding the large pond, crossing the narrow gorge, in the pond, the next to the wooden cavity, a waterfall over three zhang (about 10 meters) high, a bridge named after the direction, rainbow and mist, male and female, sky and earth, looking at the mirage, a type of grass or vegetation, a well or water source in the south, on the left side, drawing a tiger, standing firmly, thousand forces and ten thousand energies, bursting forth, pigs hiding in the ground, lightning and fire, thunder and wind, on the right, the barbarians, disheveled, yellow.

    245

    防累珠【□】丹碧锦袄【□】身刀□囊□鞾□挝□縚【□】白豹班【□】饫距掌脾【作一句】有槐屃防防【作一句□】郁防□渠决决【□】縁池西□直南折庑赴【□】拥列□与槐朋友【□】巽防间【□】白言谒行【□】旦艮间【□】逺冈青萦【□】近□可四时合竒士【□】观云　风□霜□露□雨□雪【□】所为发生敛【□】正东曰苍塘蹲濒西漭望【作一句□】瑶翻碧防【□】正北曰风隄乘携左右【作一句□】隄势北囘股努墆披蹴墉【作一句□】南楯楹【□】景怪爥【□】蛟龙钩牵【□】烟渍霭聚【□】开咍储【□】虚明茫茫【□】提鹇□絜鹭【□】防防千幅【□

    Explanation

    245

    The protective structure made of beads or pearls, the red and green brocade coat, the various items of clothing or equipment, the white leopard with black spots, the posture or action involving the hands and stomach, the sturdy or enduring tree or plant, the dense or tangled structure, the path or route that follows a pool to the west and then turns south, the group or formation associated with a tree or plant, the space or area, the white statement or declaration, the space or area, the distant hill that is green and winding, the something close that can be united with extraordinary people throughout the four seasons, the various natural elements, the causes and effects of something, the landscape to the east and west, the green and blue something, the landscape to the north, the structure of a dike, the southern pillar or support, the strange or unusual something, the dragon that is being pulled or dragged, the smoke or mist that is gathering, the something that is open and full, the something that is empty and vast, the bird that is clean and pure, the numerous and varied something.

    246

    】迎引西东【□】日夘酉樵途隖径幽委【○】虫鸣声○昼夜【○】大小亭饾【○】池渠间【○】走池隄上【○】亭后前陴乘墉【作一句】如连山群峯【□】拥地髙下【作一句】凿髙槽【作一句】絶窦墉【作一句】为【此作其】池沟沼渠瀑潨【每字□】汨汨街衖【□】町畦阡陌【每字□】间入汾【作句】水祖宗族茂盛【作一句】旁防逺映【□】锦绣交菓枝香畹【□】丽絶地郡【作一句】考其台亭沼沚之増【□】后其能无果有不补建者【作一句】池由于炀及者雅文安【作一句】诛【□此本多比字】病井卤【□】生物瘠【□】引古沃澣【作一句】人便几附于河渠【作一句】

    Explanation

    246

    A translation of the source text follows, applying the insights from the pre-drafting research to ensure faithfulness and natural English readability:

    Welcoming and leading from east to west, the daily routine of cutting wood or traveling through rugged paths, with morning and evening sounds of insects chirping, day and night. Various sizes of pavilions and structures, between ponds and channels, walking on the dike of the pond. Pavilions and walls, like a series of mountains, embracing the land with high and low. Digging a high channel, cutting through the walls, creating ponds, ditches, marshes, channels, and waterfalls. Flowing through streets and alleys, fields and paths, into the Fen River. The water's ancestor and clan are flourishing, protecting and reflecting from afar. Interwoven with brocade and fragrant orchards, the most beautiful place in the region. Examining the increase of terraces, pavilions, ponds, and marshes, later, there will be no one who does not repair or rebuild. A pool or pond that is serene and elegant, possibly due to its location or design. Punishing or eliminating suffering from saline-affected wells, the land is barren, drawing on ancient fertile water sources. People benefit by being close to rivers and canals.

    247

    厕筹
    　　今寺观削木为筹置溷圊中名曰厕筹北史齐文宣王嗜酒淫泆肆行狂暴虽以杨愔为相使进厕筹然则愔所进者岂即此与按说文厕清也从厕则声韵初吏切间也杂也次也圊也居髙临垂边曰厕髙岸夹水曰厕史记太仓公防奉剑从王之厕汲黯卫青大将军侍中上踞厕见之注如淳曰厕音则谓牀边据牀视之一云溷厕也厕牀边侧汉书注如淳曰厕溷也孟康曰厕边侧也师古曰如说是也仲冯曰厕当从孟说愚意古者见大臣则御坐为起夫武帝固以奴待青亦不应踞溷而见之然汉文居防北临厕使慎夫人鼓瑟注韦昭曰髙岸夹水为厕即此推之则凡厕者皆取其在两物间为义又郅都

    Explanation

    247

    Toilet Sticks

    Today, in temples and monasteries, wooden sticks are carved and placed in the latrines, and they are called toilet sticks. According to the Northern Dynasties History, Emperor Wenxuan of Northern Qi was known for his excessive drinking, wild and unrestrained behavior, and violent outbursts. Despite appointing Yang Yin as his chancellor and having him bring in the toilet sticks, it seems that Yang Yin's presence did not curb the emperor's wild behavior. Therefore, the toilet sticks that Yang Yin brought in were likely not the same as those used by the emperor.

    According to the Shuowen Jiezi, the character "厕" (toilet) means "clean" and follows the sound of "厕." It can also be interpreted as "the side of a bed" or "a high bank with water on both sides." In the Records of the Grand Historian, there is a story about Feng Feng, who carried a sword into the toilet with the king. In the Biography of Jia An, there is a story about General Wei Qing, the Grand Commander, who sat on the toilet and saw the emperor. Ruchun comments that the character "厕" can be interpreted as "the side of a bed" or "a toilet and latrine." Meng Kang also comments that "厕" means "the side of the toilet." The scholar Wei Zhao agrees with Ruchun's interpretation.

    Zhong Feng suggests that "厕" should follow Meng Kang's interpretation. He believes that in ancient times, when seeing a high-ranking official, the emperor would stand up. However, Emperor Wu treated General Wei Qing as a slave and should not have sat on the toilet to see him. In the Book of Han, there is a story about Emperor Wen sitting on the north side of the toilet and having his favorite concubine, Shen Fu Ren, play the zither. Wei Zhao comments that a high bank with water on both sides is a toilet, and from this, we can infer that all toilets are defined by their location between two objects.

    Grand Marshal Zhi Du was a historical figure known for his strict and severe governance.

    248

    贾姬如厕有野入厕命都击之则此之如厕亦恐非是溷圊他如刘安别传谪守都厠三年庄子庚桑篇适其偃注偃屏厕也屏厕则以偃□仪礼既夕礼甸人筑坅坎人温厕塞厕万石君建取亲中裙厕牏身自澣洒注孟康曰厕行清牏行中受粪函也至于晋侯食麦胀如厕防而卒赵襄子如厠执豫让髙祖鸿门防如厕召樊哙等及如厕见柏人金日防如厕擒莽何罗范睢佯死置厕中李斯如厕见防陶侃如厕见朱衣王敦如厕食刘寔误入石崇厕郭璞髪厕上刘季和厕上置香炉沈庆之梦卤簿入侧中崔浩焚经投厕中钱义厕神李赤厕蒯瞆盟孔悝于厕曹植戒露顶入厕之则真溷圊矣抝花

    Explanation

    248

    A Concubine of Emperor Xian of Han was in the toilet when a wild beast entered, and she ordered her attendant to strike it. This incident suggests that being in the toilet was unusual or not typical. The term "溷圊" refers to a toilet or latrine. For example, Liu An, in a separate account, was exiled to guard the latrine for three years. In the Gongsun chapter of Zhuangzi, it is said that one should "recline in a place of seclusion," often interpreted as a toilet. From the Ji Xi Li (Rites of the Evening of Death) in Yi Li (Rites of Zhou), it is described that the Dianren (officials) constructed a pit for burial. The Liren (officials) warmed and blocked the toilet. Wan Shi Jun took a personal garment and a toilet bowl, washing them himself, indicating humility and diligence. According to Meng Kang, the toilet passage and toilet bowl are used to collect and handle excrement. For instance, the Jin Hou (a historical figure) died while in the toilet. Zhao Xiangzi captured the assassin Yu Rang in the toilet. Liu Bang summoned Fan Kuai and others while in the toilet. He also saw Bo Ren in the toilet. Jin Mu captured the rebel Ma He Luo in the toilet. Fan Sui pretended to be dead and was placed in a toilet to escape capture. Li Si saw something in the toilet. Tao Kan saw a person in a red robe in the toilet. Wang Dun ate something in the toilet. Liu Shi accidentally entered Shi Chong's toilet. Guo Pu combed his hair in the toilet. Liu Ji placed a censer in the toilet. Shen Qingzhi dreamed of a grand procession entering a private place. Cui Hao burned scriptures and threw them into the toilet. Qian Yi is a deity associated with toilets. Li Chi is a ghost associated with toilets. Kui Gui and Kong Kui made an alliance in the toilet. Cao Zhi advised against entering the toilet with one's head uncovered. These examples reflect various historical events and cultural practices related to toilets. The term "抝花" is unclear and may require further research.

    249

    南方或谓折花曰抝花唐元防之诗试问酒旗歌板地今朝谁是抝花人又古乐府抝折杨栁枝
    　　连枝秀
    　　京师教坊官妓连枝秀姓孙氏葢以色事人者年四十余因投礼逸士风髙老为师而主教者褒以空湛静慧散人之号挟二女童放浪江海间偶至松江爱其风物秀丽将结数椽为栖息所郡人陆宅之【居仁】尝往访焉秀颇不以礼貌因其请作募縁疏遂为譔之疏曰京师第一部教坊占排塲曽使万人喝采道德五千言公案抽锁钥只因片语投机向林下得大道髙风指云间问前縁福地一跳身才离了百戱棚中圏子防摆手便作个三清门下闲人赤地无是无非到大来自由自在识尽悲欢离合幻打开老病死生闗交媾功成

    Explanation

    249

    In the south, people sometimes call breaking flowers "bending flowers." In a poem by Tang Dynasty poet Yuan Fang, it asks, "In the place of wine banners and song boards, who is the 'bending flower' person today?" Another ancient folk song speaks of breaking willow branches.

    Lian Zhi Xiu

    Lian Zhi Xiu, a government-sponsored courtesan in the capital, was a woman named Sun. She was over forty years old and had been in the entertainment business for many years. She once sought the guidance of a reclusive and wise old teacher, and her mentor praised her as a serene, wise, and detached person, giving her the title of "Xuan Zhan Jing Hui San Ren" (空湛静慧散人). Accompanied by two young girls, she roamed the rivers and seas, living a free-spirited life. She happened to come to Songjiang and was captivated by its beautiful scenery, planning to build a small house as a place to live.

    A local scholar named Lu Zhi (Juren) once visited her. Miss Liu was not very polite, but because he asked her to write a fundraising appeal, she then wrote it. The appeal read:

    "In the first department of the imperial music bureau in the capital, I once performed in a grand show that made ten thousand people cheer. The key to unlocking the moral teachings of the Tao Te Ching was found through a few words of agreement. Seeking the high path in the forest, I pointed to the clouds and asked about the blessed land of destiny. With a jump, I left the circle of the hundred tricks tent, and with a wave of my hand, I became a leisurely person under the Three Pure Ones. The world is neither right nor wrong, and in the end, one is free and at peace. I have experienced all the illusions of joy, sorrow, parting, and reunion, and I have opened the gates of old age, illness, death, and life. The work of creation is complete."

    250

    隂阳炭烧空欲海修持行满雌雄剑劈破愁城七星冠刚替下鳯头钗合欢带生纫做鹿皮袋空非空色非色色即是空道可道名可名强名曰道往常时红裙翠袖生绡帕猛可里草履麻衣匾皂绦销金帐冷落风情养丹炉消磨火性半世连枝带叶算从前歴尽虚花一朝刬草除根到此际方成结果寻几个烟霞外逍遥伴侣抵多少尘埃中浮浪男儿存一防志诚心百事可做少几处风流债一笔都勾试问他浊酒狂歌争如我清茶淡话迷魂阵当时落防人负我我负人总是虚脾闗窍今日防开心即道道即心无非妙用牢着眼看乌飞

    Explanation

    250

    A Journey of Spiritual Transformation

    In the realm of yin and yang, the charcoal burns, and the ocean of desire is traversed through practice and fulfillment of actions. The male and female swords cut through the city of sorrow, and the seven-star crown is replaced by a phoenix-headed hairpin. The joyful belt is transformed into a deer skin bag, symbolizing a shift from luxury to simplicity. Emptiness is not empty, and form is not form; form is ultimately empty, and emptiness is the true nature of reality. The Dao, when spoken of, can be named, but it is called the Dao to facilitate understanding. In the past, red skirts and green sleeves adorned fine silk handkerchiefs, but now straw sandals and hemp clothing, along with a simple black belt, prevail. The golden tent, once filled with luxurious pleasures, now stands cold and deserted, as the alchemical furnace nurtures the inner fire, gradually extinguishing its fiery nature. Half a lifetime of growth and decay, calculated from the past, reveals that the fleeting beauty was but an illusion. One day, the roots are eradicated, and the final result is achieved. Seek a few carefree companions beyond the mist and clouds, and compare them to the wandering men in the dust. With a sincere heart and a determined mind, one can achieve anything, and the debts of passion can be written off in one stroke. Ask them, who would rather drink turbid wine and sing wildly than enjoy clear tea and simple conversation? Both sides, caught in the bewitching array, were ultimately empty-hearted. Today, the mystical gate is opened, revealing the Dao and the heart. There is nothing but wondrous use, and one must firmly watch the flying crows.

    251

    兎走急囘头怕鹤怨猿啼五陵人买笑追欢掉头不頋三岛客谈论道稽首相迎大都来几个知音多管是前生有分玉楼花下千钟酒几畨歌白苎遏行云帐梅边一炷香从此诵黄庭消永日桃花扇深蔵明月影椰子瓢长醉白云乡皓齿细腰打叠少年歌舞锦心绣腹宣老子经文发科打诨不离机锋课嘴撩牙长存道眼烧夜香非寻佳偶披鹤星月下礼拜茅君登春台不望逺人驾鸾车云霄上追寻萧史歌馆化为仙馆静戯房畨作道房幽浄洗胭脂见全真本来面目轻敲檀板听歩虚别是宫商人尽夸七真堂添上个小孙姑

    Explanation

    251

    The rabbit runs swiftly, fearing the crane's lament and the ape's cry. The wealthy from the Five Tombs buy laughter and pursue joy, turning their heads without regard for the guests from the Three Islands. Discussing the profound and debating the Dao, the First Prime Minister welcomes them. Mostly come a few true friends, surely it is due to past life connections. Under the jade pavilion, drinking thousands of cups of wine, singing 'White Silk' and stopping the clouds. In the silk tent by the plum tree, burning a single stick of incense, thus reciting the Huangting Jing to pass the endless days. The peach blossom fan is deeply hidden, the shadow of the bright moon is seen. Drinking long from a coconut shell, intoxicated in the White Clouds Country, with bright teeth and slender waist, performing youthful dances and songs. With a heart of brocade and a belly of embroidery, proclaiming Laozi's scriptures, making witty remarks, not straying from sharp insights, teaching and teasing with words, always keeping the eye of the Dao. Burning incense at night is not to find a good match, wearing a heron robe, worshipping under the moon, paying respects to Master Mao, ascending the Spring Platform, not looking for distant people, driving a phoenix carriage, pursuing Xiao Shi in the clouds, the song hall transforming into a divine hall, the quiet playroom becoming a Daoist room, washing away makeup to see the true face of a fully realized being, lightly tapping the wooden board to hear the ethereal steps, distinguishing the pentatonic scale. Everyone praises the Seven True Hall, adding a little granddaughter.

    252

    我只道五城山册立下新王母不比寻常钩子曽经老大钳锤百链不囘万夫难敌畴昔防通一笑白面郎争与头如今顿悟三生青眼客便当擡手既不作入梦朝云暮雨也须撇等闲秋月春风若教了蒲团上工夫便可到蓬壶中境界肯庄严一处千年香火是成就到头陆地神仙金银钞等物是必大块子舍来福禄夀利钱拟定加倍儿还你得道者多助看琳宫寳殿日月交辉爱人者必亲仗玉磬金钟晨昏报德疏文一出逺迩传诵以资笑谈秀不可留遂宵遁然文虽新竒固近于俳视厚德君子有间矣而其帷箔之不修者岂偶然哉

    Explanation

    252

    I had only imagined that the new Queen Mother established in Wu Cheng Mountain was not ordinary. The hook had been repeatedly tempered, symbolizing strength and resilience, unmatched by thousands. In the past, a smile was exchanged with a fair-skinned young man, possibly indicating a past friendship or interaction. Now, I suddenly realized the significance of a long-time acquaintance and should elevate it. I should not be involved in fleeting, ephemeral matters like morning clouds and evening rains, and must also set aside casual, carefree things like autumn moon and spring breeze. If one practices diligently on the meditation cushion, one can reach the realm of the mythical Penglai and Xuanyuan. If one is willing to establish a place of worship with a thousand-year lineage, one can achieve the status of a terrestrial immortal in the end. Material wealth will be given in large quantities, and fortune, rank, longevity, and profit will be doubled and returned to you. Those who follow the Dao have many helpers. The temples and palaces are illuminated by the sun and moon, and those who love others will be loved in return. Jade chimes and golden bells are used to report virtues at dawn and dusk. Once the memorial text is issued, it will be widely recited to serve as a topic of conversation. Something so beautiful it cannot be retained leads to its disappearance. Although the text is novel and strange, it is close to being frivolous, far from the standards of virtuous gentlemen. The neglect of inner virtues is not accidental.

    253

    却鞭
    　　文真王额琳特穆尔之夫人卓勒约蘓徳济以贤行称一日有献马鞭于王者鞭内暗蔵一鐡简拔靶取之则得王喜持示夫人将酬以币夫人曰君平昔若尝害人则防人之必我害也茍无此心焉用为王悟亟还之
    　　奉母避难
    　　泰州人氏兄弟二人同居养母至正壬辰红巾压境兄弟负母逃避至中途兄念妻子不置辞母归惟弟与母借居田舎后城防其一房尽遭杀戮独弟之妻子获免乘间奔避适夫妇父子相防时传为孝行所感
    　　匠官仁慈
    　　杭州行金玉府副总管罗国器【世荣】郡人也天性仁慈有匠人程限稽违案具吏请引决罗曰吾闻其新娶若责之舅姑必以新妇不利口舌之余不测系焉姑置勿问后或再犯重加惩治可也夫罗軄在造作耳尚能知此而受民命之寄者则反贪墨苛惨惟以鞭扑立威为务哀哉

    Explanation

    253

    却鞭
