    The wife of Prince Erdeni Temur, Zholoi Surdeji, was renowned for her virtuous conduct. One day, a horsewhip was presented to the prince, inside which was secretly concealed an iron box. When it was pulled out, the prince was delighted and showed it to his wife, intending to reward the giver. The wife said, "If you have ever harmed others in the past, they will surely seek to harm me. If you have no such intention, why would you do this?" The prince realized her words and quickly returned the item.

    奉母避难

    Two brothers from Taizhou lived together with their mother. During the reign of Emperor Zhenzong in the year Renchen, the Red Turbans were on the verge of attacking. The brothers carried their mother to escape. On the way, the older brother, thinking of his wife and children, did not allow the mother to return, while the younger brother and the mother stayed temporarily in a farm house. Later, during the city's defense, the entire family was massacred. Only the younger brother's wife and children survived and took the opportunity to flee. They were together as a family, and it was said to be due to the power of filial piety.

    匠官仁慈

    The Vice Governor of the Gold and Jade Department in Hangzhou, Luo Guoqi (Shirong), was a native of the region. By nature, he was kind and compassionate. Once, a craftsman violated the time limit, and the case was ready, with the officials requesting a decision. Luo said, "I heard he had just married. If I reprimand him, his in-laws will blame the new wife for causing trouble, leading to unpredictable consequences. Let's just leave it alone for now. If he commits another offense, we can impose a heavier punishment." As for Luo, who is only responsible for construction, he still understands this. However, those who are entrusted with the people's welfare, on the other hand, are greedy, corrupt, and cruel, only caring about establishing authority through whips and beatings. Alas!

    254

    着衣吃饭
    　　谚云三代仕宦学不得着衣吃饭按魏书文帝诏羣臣云三世长者知被服五世长者知饮食则古已有此语
    　　文章政事
    　　吕仲实先生【思诚】佥浙西宪司事时有自首不合令女习学讴唱者先生案议云男女无父母之命私有所从王法不许父母违男女之愿置之非地公论岂容所首宜不准合依律杖断又有年七十之上而殴人者案议云既能为不能为之事必当受不当受之刑先生文章政事皆过人逺甚而廉洁不汚家甚贫至正间官至中书左丞先生未显时一日晨炊不继欲携布袍贸米于人室氏有吝色因戱作一诗曰典却春衫办早厨老妻何必更踌躇瓶中有醋堪烧菜囊里无钱莫买鱼不敢妄为些子事只因曾读数行书严霜烈日皆经过次第春风到草庐后果及第浙江潮

    Explanation

    254

    Final Translation

    Wearing Clothes and Eating Food
    Proverbially, even after three generations of officialdom, one cannot easily attain the simple pleasures of life, such as wearing clothes and eating food. According to the Wei Shu (Book of Wei) edict issued by Emperor Wen, it is said that after three generations, one knows how to dress properly, and after five generations, one knows how to enjoy good food. Thus, this saying has been in existence since ancient times.

    Literature and Politics
    Mr. Lv Zhongshi (Li Si Cheng) served as an official in the Zhe West Constitutional Commission. At one time, there was a case where a woman confessed to having her daughter learn singing, which was deemed inappropriate. Mr. Lv, in his case review, stated that without parental consent, men and women engaging in private relationships are prohibited by law. Parents who disregard the wishes of their children are acting improperly, and public opinion cannot condone such actions. The confession should not be accepted, and the case should be resolved according to the law with a corporal punishment. There was also a case involving a man over seventy who assaulted someone. In his case review, Mr. Lv stated that one who can perform actions beyond their capability must also bear the consequences of actions they cannot control. Mr. Lv excelled in both literature and politics, surpassing others by far. He was known for his integrity and frugality, and his family was very poor. During the reign of Emperor Shizu of the Yuan dynasty, he held the position of Left Chancellor of the Central Secretariat. When he was not yet prominent, one morning, his breakfast preparations were insufficient, and he wanted to sell his cotton robe to buy rice. The woman in his household showed a reluctant expression, so he humorously composed a poem:
    "典却春衫办早厨，老妻何必更踌躇。瓶中有醋堪烧菜，囊里无钱莫买鱼。不敢妄为些子事，只因曾读数行书。严霜烈日皆经过，次第春风到草庐。"
    Indeed, he later passed the imperial examination, and the famous Qiantang River tide awaited him.

    255

    浙江一名钱唐江一名罗刹江所谓罗刹者江心有石即秦望山脚横截波涛中商旅船到此多值风涛所困而倾覆遂呼云此事见吴越时僧賛宁传载中其昼夜二潮甚信上人以诗括之曰午未未未申申夘卯辰辰巳巳巳午午朔望一般轮此昼也初一日午未初二日未初十五日如初一夜则六时对冲子午丑未之汉东宣伯聚先生尝作浙江潮图说云大江而东凡水之入于海者无不通潮而浙江之潮独为天下竒观地势然也浙江之口有两山焉其南曰龛山其北曰赭山并峙于江

    Explanation

    255

    The Zhejiang River, also known as the Qiantang River and the Luocha River, is called "Luocha" because there is a stone in the middle of the river, which is the base of Qinyang Mountain, cutting across the waves. Merchant ships arriving here often encounter wind and waves, leading to capsizing, thus it is called "Luocha." This is recorded in the biography of the monk Zan Ning during the Wu-Yue period. The two tides, day and night, are very reliable. The elder monk summarized it in a poem, saying: "From noon to late afternoon, late afternoon to early evening, early evening to late evening, late evening to midnight, and midnight to early morning." The full moon and new moon cycle similarly. This is the day tide. On the first day, from noon to late afternoon; on the second day, from late afternoon to early evening; on the fifteenth day, as on the first. The night tide is the opposite of the day tide, occurring at six different times. Mr. Xuanbo Ju from Han Dong once wrote a commentary on the tide chart of Qiantang River, saying: "The great river flowing eastward, all waters entering the sea are subject to tides, but the tides of the Zhejiang River are unique in their grandeur due to the terrain. At the mouth of the river, there are two mountains: the southern one is called Kanshan, and the northern one is called Zheshan, standing side by side in the river."

    256

    海之防谓之海门下有沙潬跨江西东三百余里若伏槛然潮之入于浙江也发乎浩渺之区而顿就敛束逼碍沙潬囘薄激射折而趋于两山之间抝怒不拽则奋而上隮如素蜺横空奔雷殷地观者胆掉渉者心悸故为东南之至险非他江之可同也原其消长之故者曰天河激涌曰地机翕张揆其晨夕之者曰依隂而附阳曰随日而应月地志涛经言殊防异胡可得而一哉葢圆则之运大气举之方仪之静大水承之气有升降地有浮沉而潮汐生焉月有盈虚潮有起伏故盈于朔望虚于两息于朓

    Explanation

    256

    The defense of the sea is called the sea gate, beneath which lies a sandbar stretching across the river for over three hundred miles, resembling a submerged threshold. The tide entering the Zhejiang River arises from the vast and boundless area, suddenly becoming constricted by the sandbar, swirling and shooting forcefully, bending and rushing between two mountains, with fierce anger that cannot be restrained, soaring upward with great force, like a white rainbow across the sky, like a thunderbolt shaking the earth. Spectators' hearts tremble, and those who cross it are filled with fear. Therefore, it is the greatest peril in the southeast, not comparable to other rivers. The reason for its rise and fall is said to be the surging of the Milky Way and the workings of the earth expanding and contracting. To consider its morning and evening conditions, it is said to follow yin and adhere to yang, following the sun and responding to the moon. Geographical records and wave theories differ in their explanations, how can they be unified? The movement of the celestial sphere, the great air lifts it, the stillness of the square instrument, the great water supports it. The air has rising and falling, the earth has floating and sinking, and thus the tides are born. The moon has waxing and waning, the tides have rises and falls, and they rest during the waning phase.

    257

    朒消于朏魄而大小凖焉月为隂精水之所生日为阳宗水之所从故昼潮之期日常加子夜潮之月必在午而晷刻定焉卯酉之月隂阳之交故潮大于余月大梁析木河汉之津也朔望之后天地之变故潮大于余日寒暑之大建丑未也一晦一明再潮再汐一朔一望再虚再盈天一地二之道也月经于上水纬于下进退消长相为生成歴数可推毫厘不爽斯天地之至信幽赞于神明而古今不易者也杭之为郡枕带江海逺引瓯闽近控吴越商贾之所辐凑舟航之所骈集则浙江为要津焉而其行

    Explanation

    257

    The waning moon diminishes during the waxing and full moon phases, and its size corresponds to its phase. The moon is the essence of yin and the source of water, while the sun is the essence of yang and the source of water. Therefore, the daytime tide occurs daily with the sun, and the nighttime tide waits for the moon, which must be at its zenith at noon. The timing of tides is fixed by the sun and moon. In the months of February and October, the intersection of yin and yang results in greater tides than in other months. The months of December and June mark the extremes of the seasons. The cycle of tides follows the lunar month, with two tides each day, and the moon's phases cycle through waxing and waning, returning to full and new moons. This is the principle of heaven and earth, where the moon moves above and water courses below, their movements interdependent and mutually generating. The calendar and astronomy can predict these movements with precision, without error. This is the utmost faithfulness of heaven and earth, secretly assisted by the divine, and remains unchanged throughout time. Hangzhou, as a prefecture, is surrounded by rivers and seas, extending far to Wenzhou and Fujian and controlling nearby Wu and Yue. It is a place where merchants converge and ships gather in large numbers, making the Qiantang River a vital passage. The flow of the river is essential to the region.

    258

    止之淹速无不毕听于潮汐者或违其大小之信爽其缓急之宜则必至于倾垫底滞故不可以不之谨也某承乏兹郡属兵革未弭之秋信使之往来师旅之进退虽期防纷纭边陲警急必告之曰谨潮汐毋躁进以自危然而迹累肩摩晨驰夕骛有不能人喻而户说之者考之郡志得四时潮图简明可信故为之说而刻石于浙江亭之壁间使凡行李之过此者皆得而观之以毋蹈夫触险躁进之害亦庶乎思患而预防之意云此说博极羣书辞理超诣而古今之论潮汐者葢莫能过之矣因并録之

    Explanation

    258

    Ships, whether delayed or swift, are all subject to the tides; if their size or reliability is violated, or if their timing of urgency and leisure is inappropriate, they will inevitably capsize or become obstructed. Therefore, one cannot be careless. I took up this position in this prefecture during a time of unresolved military conflicts. The movements of messengers and armies are influenced by the tides, and the complex and varied nature of frontier alarms and emergencies requires careful attention. I must warn them: observe the tides carefully and do not rush forward recklessly, for doing so will put you in danger. However, the traces of weariness, the rubbing of shoulders, the morning rush, and the evening rush make it difficult to explain this to everyone. After examining the prefectural records, I found the four-season tidal charts, which are simple and reliable. Therefore, I wrote an explanation and carved it on the stone wall of Zhejiang Pavilion so that all travelers passing through here can see it, to prevent them from encountering the dangers of rashly venturing into perilous waters. This also serves the intention of thinking of potential problems and preventing them. This explanation is extremely broad and thorough in its sources, eloquent and profound, and surpasses all previous discussions of tides throughout history. Therefore, I include it here.

    259

    贞烈墓
    　　千夫长李某戍天台县日一部卒妻郭氏有令姿见之者无不啧啧称赏李心慕焉去县七八十里有私盗出没处李分兵往戍卒遂在行既而日至卒家百计调之郭氏毅然莫犯经半载夫归具以白为属所辖防敢谁何一日李过卒门卒邀入治茶忽忆得前事怒形于色亟转身持刃出而李幸脱走诉于县县捕系穷竟案议持刃杀本部官罪死县桎梏囹圄中从而邑之恶少年与官之吏胥皂辈无有不起觊觎之心者郭氏躬餽食于卒外闭户业绩纺以资衣食人不敢一至其家久之府檄调黄岩州一狱卒叶其姓者至尤有意于郭氏乃頋视其卒日饮食之情若手足卒感激入骨髓忽传有五府官

    Explanation

    259

    Stalwart Tomb
    A thousand-man commander, Li, was stationed in Tian Tai County. One of his soldiers' wife, Guo Shi, was exceptionally beautiful, and those who saw her all commented in admiration. Li secretly admired her. About seventy to eighty li away from the county, there was a place where bandits frequently appeared. Li divided his troops to guard the soldiers, and the soldier was among them. After half a year, the soldier returned home, and he reported everything to his wife. Being within the jurisdiction of the superior, who dares to do what? One day, Li passed by the soldier's house. The soldier invited him in to have tea. Suddenly, Li remembered the previous incident, and anger was visible on his face. He quickly turned around, held a knife, and came out. Fortunately, Li escaped. He reported the incident to the county magistrate. The magistrate arrested and detained him until the case was thoroughly investigated. Li was punished to death and imprisoned in chains in prison. Guo Shi personally brought food to the soldier, closed the door, and spun yarn to make a living. No one dared to visit her home. After a long time, the provincial command to mobilize troops to Huangyan State arrived. A jailer named Ye, who had the same surname as the soldier, especially had intentions towards Guo Shi. He looked at the soldier and showed affection like brothers. The soldier was deeply grateful. Suddenly, it was heard that officials from the Five Departments were coming.

    260

    出五府之官所以斩决罪囚者叶报卒知且谓曰汝或可活我与为义兄弟万一不保汝之妻尚少汝之子若女才八九嵗耳奚以依顾我尚未娶宁肯俾为我室乎若然我之视汝子女犹我子女也卒喜诺叶遂令郭氏私见卒卒谓曰我死有日此叶押狱性柔善未有妻汝可嫁之郭氏曰汝之死以我之色我又能二适以求生乎既归持二幼痛泣而言曰汝爹行且死娘死亦在旦夕我儿无所怙恃终必死于饥寒我今卖汝与人娘岂忍哉葢势不容已将复柰何汝在他人家非若父母膝下比母仍似是娇痴为也天茍有知使汝成立嵗时能以巵酒奠父母则是我有后矣其子女颇聪慧解母语意抱母而号引

    Explanation

    260

    A prison officer from the five departments, who was responsible for executing prisoners, reported to Ye, the supervisor, and said, "You might save me and make us brothers in righteousness. If I cannot be saved, your wife is still young, and your children are only eight or nine years old. What will they rely on if I am not married? Would you rather make her my wife?" If so, I will see your children as my own. The soldier happily agreed, and Ye then allowed Guo to secretly meet the soldier. The soldier said, "I will die soon. Ye is a gentle and kind prison officer who has no wife. You can marry him." Guo replied, "My death is due to my beauty. How could I remarry to save myself?" After returning, she held her two children and cried, saying, "Your father is about to die, and your mother will die soon. My children will have no support and will eventually die of hunger and cold. I am now selling you to someone; how could I bear it? It is a matter of necessity; what can I do? You will not be like my parents' side. The mother still seems to be acting foolishly. If heaven has any knowledge, let you grow up and be able to offer a cup of wine to your parents on certain occasions; then I will have descendants." Her children were quite intelligent and understood their mother's words, hugging her and crying.

    261

    裾不肯释手遂携二儿出市召人与之行路亦为之堕泪邑人有怜之者纳其子女赠钱三十缗郭氏以三之一具酒馔携至狱门谓叶曰愿与夫一再见叶听入哽咽不能语既而曰君扰押狱多矣可用此少礼答之又有钱若干可取自给我去一富家执作为口食计恐旬日不及看君故也相别垂泣而出走至仙人渡溪水中危坐而死此处水极险恶竟不为冲倒仆人有见者报之县县官往验视得实皆惊异失色为具棺敛就葬于死所之侧山下又为申达上司仍表其墓曰贞烈郭氏之墓大书刻石墓上至正丙戍朝廷遣奉使宣抚循行列郡廉得其事原卒之情释之人遂付还子女终身誓不再娶

    Explanation

    261

    A Woman with Unyielding Resolve

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang named De Guo did not let go of her skirt and took her two sons out into the market, calling people to accompany them on the road. The townspeople, moved by her plight, also shed tears. Some townspeople who felt pity for her took in her children and gave her thirty strings of cash. De Guo used one-third of the money to prepare a meal and carried it to the prison gate, where she asked her husband, Ye, if she could see him one last time. Ye allowed her in, but she choked with sobs, unable to speak. She then said, "You have caused much trouble for the prison guards; you can use this small gift as a token of appreciation." There was some money left, which she could take for herself to support her family. A wealthy family agreed to support her children as food and sustenance, as she feared that in ten days, she would not be able to see him again. They parted with tears and left. She walked to the Xianren River, sat precariously in the water, and died. The water here was extremely dangerous, and she did not even fall over. Those who saw her reported it to the county. The county official went to verify and found it to be true, all were shocked and pale. They prepared a coffin, buried her, and placed her grave beside the mountain at the place of her death. They also reported to the higher authorities and erected a tablet at her grave, inscribed with "The Tomb of the Chaste and Loyal De Guo." They carved the inscription on a stone and placed it on the tomb. In the third year of the Zhizheng era, the court sent envoys to tour the provinces and investigate the matter, finding the original cause of her death. They released the people and returned her children to her, and she vowed never to remarry again.

    262

    特徤药
    　　墨薮载徐氏书记云平一龆齓之嵗见育宫中尝覩先后阅书法数轴将榻以赐藩邸令女学内人出六十余函于亿嵗殿曝之多装以镂牙轴紫罗缥云是太宗时所装其中有故青绫缥玳瑁轴者云是梁氏旧迹楷书每函可二十余卷别有一小函有十余卷所记忆者是扇书乐毅告誓黄庭私访于所主女学问其函出尽否答云尚有不知其几至中宗神龙初贵戚宠盛宫禁不
    严御府之珍多归私室先尽金壁次及书法嫔主之家因此擅出或有报安乐公主者于内出二十余函驸马武延秀乆践外国无功于此徒闻二王之迹强効寳持时呼薛稷郑愔及平一详其善恶诸人随事答称上者登时去牙轴紫缥易以漆轴黄麻纸缥题云特徤药是外国语其书合作者时有太宗御笔于后题之叹其雄逸云及考之书苑菁华特徤药作云特徤乐恐是锓梓误耳

    Explanation

    262

    Translation:

    Special Medicine

    The Mòsǒu records in the writings of the Xu family that during his early years, Pingyi, who was raised in the palace, once witnessed the empresses and consorts reviewing several rolls of calligraphy. They placed these in chests and granted them to a prince's residence. The women in charge of education brought out more than sixty chests to the Yìsuì Hall to air them out. Many of these chests were decorated with carved ivory axes and purple silk, indicating that they were organized during the reign of Emperor Taizong. Among them, there were chests with old green silk and tortoiseshell axes, which were said to be the works of the Liang family. Each chest contained about twenty scrolls of regular script. There was also a smaller chest with over ten scrolls. The contents of this smaller chest included fan inscriptions, the Lèyì text, the Huángtíng text, and private letters. The woman in charge asked if all the chests had been brought out, and she replied that there were still some, though she did not know how many. By the early years of Emperor Zhongzong's reign, the relatives of the royal family had gained significant influence, and the palace regulations had become lax. The treasures of the imperial treasury were often taken by private individuals, with gold and jade being the first to be taken, followed by calligraphy. Some even reported to Princess Anle, who took out over twenty chests. Her husband, Wu Yanshou, had long been abroad without achieving anything, but he had heard of the works of the Two Wangs and forced himself to hold onto them. At the time, he called upon Xue Ji, Zheng Yin, and Pingyi to examine the quality of the calligraphy. They commented on the best pieces, and the best ones were immediately repackaged with lacquered axes and yellow hemp paper, with the inscription "Special Medicine" written in a foreign language. Occasionally, Emperor Taizong's own handwriting was added, praising the bold and free style. When examining the Shūyuàn Jīnhuá, it appears that "Special Medicine" might be a misprint for "Special Medicine," possibly due to a printing error.

    263

    乞求
    　　世之曰乞求葢谓正欲若是也然唐时已有此言王建宫词只恐他时身到此乞求自在得还家又花蕊夫人宫词种得海柑才结子乞求自过与君王
    　　张道人
    　　暨阳之南门桥军人张旺者人咸称之曰张牌素凶狠无赖尝夜盗城西田父菜被执濡其首溺池而之以故恨入骨髓每思有以为报而未能一夕宿火瓦罂往烧其家道由观沟时月色防明画师吴碧山尚未寝偶闻歩履声穴窻窥之见张前行而殇百数踵其后饭顷又闻歩履声复窥之则张囘而青衣童子二人前焉吴甚惊怪葢张乃吴常所厚善者诘旦往叩张张初不承及语之审因以前事告且曰我实欲燬其室以快所愤因念寃寃相报无有了时遂弃火归他无见也吴乃告以其详张大感悟曰一念之顷可不谨哉即舍俗出家人又咸称之曰张道人后竟得道云此在至正五年事也

    Explanation

    263

    The Desire to Beg
    　　In the world, to say "begging" is to mean that one truly desires such a thing. However, this saying already existed in the Tang dynasty. In Wang Jian's palace poems, it is said: "Only fear that one day one will come here to beg, and then be able to return home freely." In the palace poems of the Flower Dew Lady, it is said: "After planting the sea orange, it finally bears fruit; begging is passed on to the king."
    　　The Daoist Master Zhang
    　　Zhang Wang, a soldier from the south gate of Jiyang Bridge, was known by everyone as Zhang Pai. He was usually fierce and ruthless, once stealing vegetables from a farmer in the west of the city at night. When he was caught, his head was soaked and he was thrown into a pond, which led to a deep-seated hatred. He often thought about seeking revenge but was unable to do so. One night, he set fire to a clay jar to burn down the farmer's house, passing through a canal. At that time, the moon was bright, and the painter Wu Bishan had not yet gone to sleep. He happened to hear footsteps and peeped through a hole in the window, seeing Zhang Wang walking ahead, followed by a hundred young ghosts. After a meal, he heard footsteps again and peeped once more, only to see Zhang Wang returning with two green-clad boys leading the way. Wu Bishan was greatly surprised and wondered why Zhang Wang, whom he usually treated well, was acting like this. The next day, he went to ask Zhang Wang, who initially denied everything. After a detailed conversation, Zhang Wang confessed and told him about the previous events, saying: "I really wanted to destroy his house to satisfy my anger. Thinking about the endless cycle of revenge, I decided to abandon the fire and return home without seeing anything." Wu Bishan then told him the details, and Zhang Wang had a great realization, saying: "Even in a moment of thought, one must be cautious." He then left the secular world to become a monk and was called "Daoist Master Zhang." Later, he attained enlightenment. This event took place in the fifth year of the Zhizheng era.

    264

    隂德延夀
    　　昔真州一巨商每嵗贩粥至杭时有挟姑布子之术曰眼者设肆省前言皆竒中故门常如市商方坐下坐忽指之曰公大富人也惜乎中秋前后三日内数不可逃商惧即戒程时八月之初舟次杨子江见江濵一妇仰天大号商问焉答曰妾之夫作小经纪止有本钱五十缗每买鹅鸭过江货卖归则计本于妾然后持息易柴米余赀尽付酒家率以为常今妾偶遗失所留本钱非惟饮食之计无所措亦必被棰死宁自沉商闻之叹曰我今厄于命设令
    铸金可代我无虞矣彼乃自夭其生哀哉亟赠钱一百缗妇感谢去商至家具以眼之言告父母且与亲戚故旧叙永诀闭户待尽父母亲故宛转寛解终弗自悟逾期无他故复之杭舟阻风偶泊向时赠钱处登岸散适此妇襁负婴孩遇诸道迎拜且告曰自恩府持拔数日后乃产母子二人没齿感再生之赐者岂敢忘哉商至杭便过眼所惊顾曰公中秋胡不死乃详观形色而笑曰公隂德所致必曽救一老隂少阳之命矣商异其术捐钱若干以报之

    Explanation

    264

    隂德延夀

    In the past, a wealthy merchant from Zhenzhou traveled to Hangzhou every year to sell porridge. There was a person who claimed to have the technique of Gubu Zi, called the "ghost eye," and set up a stall. All his words were strangely accurate, so the door was always like a market. The merchant was just sitting down when the fortune-teller suddenly pointed at him and said, "You are a great wealthy person, but unfortunately, in the three days before and after the Mid-Autumn Festival, your fate cannot be escaped." The merchant heard this and sighed, saying, "I am now doomed by fate. If I could cast gold to replace me, I would be safe. But she will end her own life, which is pitiful." He quickly gave one hundred strings of cash. The woman thanked him and left.

    The merchant returned home and told his parents everything the fortune-teller had said. He also said goodbye to his relatives and old friends, closed the door, and waited for death. His parents and relatives tried to comfort him, but he did not understand. After the deadline passed without any other incident, he returned to Hangzhou. His boat was stopped by the wind and he happened to dock at the place where he had given the money. He went ashore to walk around and met the woman carrying a child in her arms, who bowed to him on the road. She said, "I have been saved by your kindness, and a few days later, I gave birth to a son and a daughter. I will be grateful for the gift of a new life until the end of my teeth, how could I ever forget it?"

    The merchant returned to Hangzhou and went to see the fortune-teller. The fortune-teller was surprised and asked, "Why didn't you die on the Mid-Autumn Festival?" He then carefully observed the merchant's appearance and smiled, saying, "Your good deeds have protected you. You must have saved the life of an old woman and a young man." The merchant was amazed by the fortune-teller's skill and donated a certain amount to repay him.

    265

    帝师
    　　帕克斯巴帝师法号曰皇天之下一人之上开教宣文辅治大圣至德普觉真知佑国如意大寳法王西天佛子大元帝师班第达帕克巴巴克实

    　　辍耕録卷十二
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷十三　　　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰中书案
    　　中书省准陕西行省咨察罕诺尔宣慰司呈巴咱尔街礼敬坊王弼告至正三年九月内到义利坊平易店见有算卦王先生因问来歴致争当月二十九日夜睡房窻下似风吹葫芦声不时有之请到李法师遣送虚空人言算卦先生使我来哭声内称寃枉弼祝之曰尔神尔明以告我云我是丰州黒河村周大亲女月惜至正二年九月十七日夜因出后院被这王先生将我杀了做奴婢使唤如今教在你家作怪哭者索要衣服抄冩所说赴官陈告差卢捕盗等与社长吴信甫于王先生房内搜获木印二颗黒罗绳二条上钉铁鍼四个魇镇女身小纸人八个五色防五色绒上俱有头髪相

    265

    The Imperial Preceptor, Pandita Phagpa, had the Dharma name "Above all under heaven, below the one, opening the teaching, spreading the scriptures, assisting in governance, great sage, highest virtue, universal enlightenment, true knowledge, protecting the nation, fulfilling wishes, Great Treasure Dharma King, Son of the Buddha from the Western Paradise, Imperial Preceptor of the Great Yuan, Pandita Phagpa."

    Records of Pausing Plowing, Volume Twelve
    Imperially Approved Complete Library of Four Treasuries
    Records of Pausing Plowing, Volume Thirteen, compiled by Tao Zongyi of the Yuan Dynasty

    Ghost Case of the Central Secretariat
    The Central Secretariat approved a report from the Shaanxi Provincial Government, which was submitted by the Examination and Consolation Office of Chakhar Nuur. The report presented a case involving Wang Bi, who lived on Ba Zai Street, Respectful Temple Lane. In the ninth month of the third year of the Zhizheng era, Wang Bi visited an easy store in Yi Li Ward. There, he encountered a fortune-teller named Mr. Wang, who claimed to know his past life. This led to a dispute. On the twenty-ninth night of that month, while sleeping in his room, Wang Bi heard a sound like the wind blowing a gourd, which occurred frequently. He invited Master Li, a Buddhist monk, who sent someone to the void to speak. The fortune-teller claimed to be a spirit who had been sent to Wang Bi. The spirit cried out, claiming injustice. Wang Bi prayed, saying, "You, the spirit or ghost, clearly tell me." The spirit replied, "I am the daughter of Zhou Da from Heihe Village in Fengzhou. In the ninth month of the seventeenth night of the second year of the Zhizheng era, I was taken out of the back courtyard by this Mr. Wang, killed, and made into a slave. Now, I am causing trouble in your home." The spirit asked for clothes and the details of what was said. Wang Bi reported this to the authorities, who sent Lu, the thief-catcher, and others, along with the village head Wu Xinfu, to search Mr. Wang's room. They found two wooden seals, two black silk ropes with four iron pins attached, eight small paper figures with the woman's body, and five-colored silk and five-colored wool, all with hair attached.

    266

    又小葫芦一个上拴红头绳一条内盛琥珀珠二颗外包五色绒朱书符命一沓又告十二月初三日有空中言我是奉元路南坊开张机房耿大第二男顽驴这先生改名顽童我年一十八嵗被那老先生引三个伴当杀了我二十二日又有空中云我是察罕诺尔李帖家孩儿延奴又名抢灰那老贼杀了我改名买卖我被杀时年十四嵗勘问得犯人王万里即王先生状招年五十一嵗江西省吉安路民于襄阳周先生处习防隂阳课命至顺二年三月内到兴元府逢见刘先生云我防使术法迷惑人心采生魂使去人家作祸广得财物我有下的卖与你一个随于身畔取出五色防帛并头

    Explanation

    266

    A small gourd with a red ribbon tied to it, containing two pieces of amber beads, wrapped in multicolored fur with a bundle of red-written talismans. On the third day of the twelfth month, a ghost appeared in the air and declared, "I am the second son of the man named Wanli from the South Ward of the Southern Capital, who was known as 'Wanli' but later changed his name to 'Wan Tong.' I was eighteen years old when the old master, accompanied by three companions, killed me on the twenty-second day." Another ghost appeared in the air and said, "I am the child of the Li family from Chahan Nor, named Yannu, also known as 'Qianghui.' The old thief killed me and gave me the name 'Mai Mai.' I was killed at the age of fourteen." The prisoner Wang Wanli, also known as Mr. Wang, confessed that he was fifty-one years old, a native of Jixian Road, Jiangxi Province. He had learned divination and fortune-telling based on the principles of yin and yang from Mr. Zhou in Xiangyang. In the third month of the second year of the Zhishun era, he arrived in Xiangyuan Prefecture, where he met Mr. Liu, who told him, "I use magical arts to bewilder people's minds, steal their souls, and cause harm to their families, thereby acquiring great wealth. I have stolen souls and am selling one to you. I have taken it out with me, along with a multicolored cloth and a head."

    267

    髪相结一块言称这个小名唤延奴我课算拣性格聪明的童男童女用符命法水呪语迷惑活割鼻口唇舌尖耳朶眼睛呪取活气剖腹掏割心肝各小块晒干捣罗为末裹及用五色防帛同生魂头髪相结用纸作人形様符水呪遣往人家作怪跟随到伊下处至夜刘先生焚香念呪烧符听得口言不见形影问师父你教我谁家里索甚去刘先生分付李延奴你与这先生做伴去说罢将呪语禁万里与讫钞七十五两买得五色防帛头髪相结一块称说可改名买卖传教采生遣使禁符命呪水又云牛狗肉破法休吃续后于房州山地面经过逢见广州旧识邝先生云我亦防遣使魂我有

    Explanation

    267

    The hair was tied together in one piece, and it was said to be called this little name, Yan Nu. I calculated and selected clever children, both boys and girls, and used talismanic water and incantations to bewilder them, livingly cutting off their noses, mouths, lips, tongues, ears, and eyes, extracting their vital breath through incantations, and then cutting open their abdomens to remove their hearts and livers. Each small piece was dried and ground into powder, wrapped, and used with five-colored silk, together with the living soul's hair tied together. Paper was used to make human-shaped talismans with water and incantations to send them to people's homes to cause mischief and follow them to their places. At night, Mr. Liu burned incense, recited incantations, and burned talismans, hearing words but not seeing any form or shadow. He asked the master, "Who did you teach me to go to whose house and what to seek?" Mr. Liu instructed Li Yan Nu to accompany this master. After saying this, he gave the incantation and a ban that extends for ten thousand miles along with seventy-five taels of silver, bought five-colored silk and hair tied together in one piece, saying it can be renamed and used for trade and teaching the practice of capturing and sending spirits. He also said that eating beef and dog meat breaks the spell, so do not eat it. Later, while passing through the mountainous area of Fangzhou, he met an old acquaintance from Guangzhou, Mr. Kuang, who said he also had a practice of sending spirits.

    268

    下的生魂卖与你万里与讫钞一锭邝先生取出五色防帛头髪相结纸人儿一个云此名耿顽童万里将与李买卖一处遣使以课算为由前到大同路丰州黑河村地面往来至正二年八月内到于周大家课命将伊女周月惜八字看算性格聪慧要将杀害采生魂至九月十七日夜于周大住宅后院墙下黑影内潜藏间见一人往后院内来认得系是月惜在彼出后万里密念咒语向前拖拽往东奔走将月惜禁止端立脱下沿身衣服用元带鱼刀将其额皮割扯下悬葢眼睛及将头髪割下一缕用纸人并五色采帛绒线相结作块一如人形様然后割下鼻口唇舌耳尖眼睛手十指梢脚十

    Explanation

    268

    During the second year of the Zhi Zheng era, in August, a messenger was sent to Da Tong Road, Feng Zhou, Hei He Village, under the pretext of calculating a transaction with Li. Upon arrival at Zhou Da's house, the messenger was instructed to examine the eight characters (birth chart) of Zhou Yue Xi, a clever girl, and plan to kill her. That night, on the seventeenth of September, the messenger hid in the shadow behind the wall of Zhou Da's residence. He saw someone coming to the back courtyard and recognized her as Zhou Yue Xi. Secretly reciting a spell, he dragged her eastward, stopping her and making her stand still. He took off her clothes and used a fish knife to cut her forehead, pulled down her eyelids, and cut a strand of her hair. He then used a paper figure and five-colored silk and wool thread to tie together a block shaped like a human form. Finally, he cut off her nose, mouth, lips, tongue, ear tips, eyes, and the tips of her fingers and toes.

    269

    趾梢却剖开胷腹才方倒地气絶又将心肝肺各割一块晒干捣末装于小葫芦内至正三年九月内来到察罕诺尔平易店安下开张卦肆与王弼相争挟讐令生魂周月惜等三名前往本家作祸为买马肉食因店内将牛肉作马肉卖与因此不能禁事发到官及责得李福寳即李帖状结生到孩儿延奴常有疾病于五岳观口许出家落在纸灰内改名抢灰天歴二年二月内令其赶牛牧放不归此时饥想得人亏害不曾根寻及行移奉元路咸宁县并大同路丰州照勘耿顽童周月惜致死縁由相同呈乞咨请施行准此送据刑部拟得王万里残忍不道合令凌迟处死其妻子迁徙海南安置

    Explanation

    269

    In the third month of the year 1346, he arrived at the Chahan Nuer Pingyi Store, where he set up a divination shop. He quarreled with Wang Bi and took revenge by commanding the living soul of Zhou Yue Xi and two others to go to their home and cause trouble, intending to buy horse meat to eat. However, the shop was selling beef as horse meat, which led to an inability to control the situation. The matter came to the attention of the authorities, and it was discovered that Li Fu Bao, also known as Li Tie Zhang, was responsible. His son, Yan Nu, often had illnesses and was allowed to leave home to become a monk at Wuyue Temple. He fell into the ashes and was renamed Qiang Hui. In the second month of Tianli Year 2, he was ordered to herd cattle and did not return. At this time, he was hungry and thought of someone who had wronged him, but did not investigate. The case was investigated in the administrative divisions of Fengyuan Road Xianning County and Datong Road Fenzhou, and it was found that the reasons for the deaths of Geng Wantong and Zhou Yue Xi were the same. The authorities requested the implementation of the law, and the case was sent to the Ministry of Punishment. It was determined that Wang Wanli was cruel and unjust, and he was sentenced to death by slicing. His wife and children were relocated to Hainan Province.

    270

    乌寳传
    　　余幼时尝见胡石塘先生寳传今不能记其全篇有人出永嘉髙则诚【明】乌寳传相示虽曰以文为戱要亦有闗于世教传曰乌寳者其先出于防稽禇氏世尚儒务词藻然皆不甚显至寳厌祖父业变姓名从墨氏防尽得其通神之术由是知名初寳之先有钱氏者亦以通神之术显迨寳出而钱氏遂废然其术亦颇相故不知者犹以为钱云寳轻薄柔黙外若方正内实垢汚善随时舒卷常自得圣人一贯之道故无入而不自得流俗多惑之凡有谋于寳小大轻重多寡精粗无不曲随人所求自公卿以下莫不敬爱其子姓蕃衍散处郡国者皆官给庐舎而加守防焉其有老死者则

    Explanation

    270

    The Biography of Wu Bao

    When I was young, I once saw the biography of Wu Bao written by Mr. Hu Shitang. I cannot remember the entire piece, but someone showed me a new version by Gao Zecheng from Yongjia during the Ming dynasty. Although it is said to be written as a game, it also has moral implications. The biography states that Wu Bao's ancestors came from the Chu family of Fuyang, who were scholars and valued literary style, but they were not very prominent. Wu Bao grew tired of his family's profession, changed his name, and learned the divine skills of the Moists, thereby gaining fame. Initially, there was another person named Qian who was also famous for his divine skills, but after Wu Bao emerged, Qian's reputation faded. However, Wu Bao's skills were quite similar to Qian's, so those who did not know him still thought he was Qian.

    Wu Bao was light and thin, quiet and reserved, appearing outwardly upright but actually being corrupt within. He was good at adapting to circumstances, often finding the way of the sage that is consistent, and thus nowhere did he feel uncomfortable. The common people were often deceived by him. Whenever people consulted him, regardless of the size, importance, quantity, or quality of their requests, he would always bend to meet their demands. High officials and below all respected and loved him. His descendants spread out and lived in various regions, and the government provided houses for them and added protection. When any of them died, their descendants continued to be cared for.

    271

    官为聚其尸而焚之葢知墨之末俗也寳之所在人争迎取邀致茍得至其家则老稚婢无不忻恱且重扄邃宇敬事保爱惟恐其他适也然素趍势利其富室势人每屈輙往虽终身服役弗厌其窭人贫氓有倾心愿见终不肯一往尤不喜儒虽有暂相与往来者亦终不能乆留也葢儒墨之素不相合若此寳好逸恶劳爱俭素疾华侈常客于农田氏田氏朴且啬寳竭诚与交田氏没其子好奢靡日以声色宴防为事寳甚厌之隣有商氏者亦若田氏父之为也遂挈其族往依焉葢墨之道贵清浄故也然其为人也多诈反覆不常凡逹官势人无不愿交而率皆不利败事故其廉介自持者率不

    Explanation

    271

    The authorities gathered and burned their bodies, showing the decline of the Mohist way. Where the treasure is, people vie to welcome and take it, inviting and obtaining by any means. When they reach their home, neither the elderly, children, servants, nor slaves are not happy; they lock the deep rooms, treat with respect, and preserve and cherish, fearing only that it might be taken elsewhere. But they always seek power and profit. Wealthy and powerful people often bow to them and go, even if they serve for their entire lives, they are not tired. Poor and destitute people who wish to see them ultimately will not go. Especially they dislike Confucians; even if there are temporary interactions, they ultimately cannot stay long. This shows that Confucians and Mohists have never been compatible. The treasure likes ease and dislikes labor; it loves frugality and hates extravagance. It often resides with the simple and thrifty Tian family. The Tian family is simple and thrifty; the treasure sincerely associates with them. When the Tian family died, their son liked luxury and extravagance, daily engaging in music, color, feasts, and banquets. The treasure greatly disliked him. There was a neighbor named Shang who also acted like the father of the Tian family. So he took his clan to rely on them. This is because the Mohist way values purity and tranquility. But they are often deceitful, fickle, and inconsistent. All powerful and influential people are willing to associate with them, but they are generally not beneficial because of their failures. Those who are upright and self-disciplined generally do not.

    272

    与寳交自寳之术行挟诈者往往伪为寳术以售于时后皆败死故寳之术益尊是时昆仑抱璞公南海珠子永昌从革生皆能济人与世俯仰曲随人意而三人者亦愿为寳交茍得寳一往则三人亦无不可致故术誉咸归于寳焉寳族虽伙然其状貌技术亦颇相似知与不知咸谓之乌寳云论曰乌氏见于春秋世本姓苑若存余技乌获皆为显仕至唐承恩重始盛迨寳而益着寳裔本禇氏而自谓乌氏则变诈亦可知矣寳之学虽出于墨而其害道伤化尤甚虽孟轲氏复生不能辟也然使寳生于唐虞三代时其术未必若是显然则寳之得行其志者亦其时有以使之呜呼岂独寳之罪哉

    Explanation

    272

    Interacting with the treasure, those who hold deceit often falsely pretend to be the treasure technique to sell in the time, and later all failed and died. Therefore, the treasure technique became increasingly respected. At that time, the public of Kunlun holding璞, the pearl of the South Sea, and the son of Yongchang following the transformation, all could benefit people and adapt to the world's will. These three people are also willing to be friends with the treasure, if they get the treasure once, then these three people can also be reached. Therefore, the reputation of the technique all returns to the treasure. The family of the treasure, although numerous, their appearance and technique are quite similar, known and unknown, all call them the black treasure.

    The commentary says, the family of Wu is seen in the Spring and Autumn Annals and the genealogies, if there are remaining skills, Wu Huo all became prominent officials, until the Tang dynasty when the descendants received great favor and began to flourish, until the time of Bao when they became even more famous. The descendants of Bao, originally the family of Chu, but called themselves the family of Wu, then their deceitful nature can also be known. The learning of Bao, although derived from Mo, but its harm to the way and the transformation is especially severe, even if Mencius were to be reborn, he could not avoid it. But if Bao were born in the Tang, Yu, and Three Dynasties, his technique might not have been so obvious, then Bao's ability to carry out his will would also depend on the time. Alas, is it only Bao's fault?

    273

    緑窻遗藁
    　　新喻傅汝砺先生【金】尝志其妻殡云君讳淑字兰姓孙氏其先汴人年二十三归我于湘中五月而卒君髙朗秀惠生六嵗母卒父教以书稍长习女工晨起独先盥栉适父母所问安毕佐诸母具食饮退治女工晡时观经史或鸣琴自休既夕聚家人瞑坐说古贞女孝妇传烛至治女工如初富贵家多求婚父不许及以许余家人不恱一日有幸余疾者欲因动之君曰大人以爱子许人必慎所择矣即有不讳命也若等谓我且慕世俗富贵而改聘耶有死而已皆愧谢不敢复言事继母尽孝道死之日母大恸既瞑目久忽徐起止母哭令自寛及母出私泣告余曰妾为父母

    Explanation

    273

    The Green Window's Remaining Drafts
    　　Xin Yu's Mr. Fu Ruli [Jin] once inscribed a memorial for his wife's burial, saying: "My wife, whose name is Shu and courtesy name is Xilan, of the Sun clan, whose ancestors were from Bian, was twenty-three when she married me in Xiangzhong and died five months later. She was noble and bright, excellent and intelligent. At the age of six, her mother died, and her father taught her to read. As she grew older, she learned female crafts. In the morning, she would alone and first wash and comb her hair, then go to her parents' place to ask about their well-being. After finishing, she would help the other mothers prepare food and drink, then focus on her female crafts. In the afternoon, she would read classical texts, sometimes playing the qin to relax. After sunset, she would gather the family to sit quietly, telling stories of ancient virtuous women. Until the candles were lit, she would continue her female crafts as before. Many wealthy families proposed marriage to her, but her father did not agree. Eventually, he agreed to the marriage with me, but the family was unhappy. One day, someone happened to have my illness and wanted to take advantage of the situation to move her. She said, 'My parents, having agreed to give their loving son to someone, must be cautious in their choice. If there is an unavoidable fate, it is so. You all say that I would admire worldly wealth and honor and change the engagement? I would rather die than do that.' Everyone felt ashamed and apologized, daring not to speak again. She served her stepmother with full filial piety. On the day of her death, her mother greatly grieved. After closing her eyes for a long time, she suddenly slowly rose, stopping her mother from crying and making her comfort herself. When her mother left, she privately wept and told me, 'I am for my parents.'"

    274

    所偏爱即死必伤其心然终必死矣为将奈何君后富贵幸念之言既复瞑目泰定五年八月廿有一日也后三日寓殡湘中及序其遗藁云故妻孙氏蕙兰早失母父周卿先生以孝经论语及凡女诫之书敎之诗固未之学也因其弟受唐诗家法于庭取而读之得其音格輙能为近体五七言语皆闲雅可诵非茍学所能至者然不多为又恒毁其藁家人或窃之令勿毁则曰偶适情耳女子当治织絍组紃以致其孝敬辞翰非所事也既卒家人哭而称之因出其藁得五言七首七言十一首五七言未成章者廿六句特为编集成帙题曰緑窻遗藁序而藏之五言诗曰窻里人初起窻前

    Explanation

    274

    If one favors something to the point of death it will inevitably hurt the heart but death is still inevitable what to do as a general when one is in a position of wealth and power remember this after speaking closed their eyes the day was the twenty-first day of the eighth month in the fifth year of the Taizhong era three days later temporarily buried in Xiang and wrote a preface to her remaining manuscripts it is said that her late wife Sun Hui Lan lost her mother and father at an early age Zhou Qing Mr. Zhou taught her with the Classic of Filial Piety the Analects and other books on female conduct she did not learn poetry formally because her brother received the style and rules of Tang poetry in the courtyard and took them to read she could immediately grasp the rhythm and structure of Tang poetry and write elegant and readable poems this was not something she could achieve through casual study however she did not write many poems and often destroyed her drafts family members tried to save her drafts and told her not to destroy them but she said they were just written to satisfy her emotions women should focus on weaving and embroidery to show filial piety and respect not on writing poetry after her death her family mourned and collected her remaining poems they compiled her poems into a collection titled Green Window Remaining Manuscripts and wrote a preface for it the five-character poem said the person in the window just woke up in front of the window

    275

    栁正娇卷帘冲落絮开镜见垂条坐对分金线行防拂翠翘流莺空巧语倦听不须调【右一】小阁烹香茗防帘下玉钩灯光翻出鼎钗影倒沉瓯婢捧消春困亲尝散暮愁吟诗因坐久月转晩妆楼【右二】灯前催晓妆把酒向髙堂但愿梅花月年年映夀觞【右三】采阁闭朝寒妆成拟问安忽闻春雪下唤婢卷帘看【右四】粲粲梅花盈盈似玉人甘心对氷雪不爱艶阳春【右五】小小春罗扇团团秋月生蟠桃花里绣得董防成【右六】自拂防眉黛何曽惯得愁若教如翠栁便恐不禁秋【右七】七言诗曰楼前杨栁发青枝楼下春寒病起时独坐小窻无气力隔帘

    Explanation

    275

    Here is the final translation of the source text:

    栁正娇卷帘冲落絮开镜见垂条坐对分金线行防拂翠翘流莺空巧语倦听不须调【右一】

    Willow, fair and delicate, rolls up the curtain as falling catkins rush out. Opening the mirror, she sees the drooping branches. Sitting opposite, she divides the golden thread, walking and brushing her green feather. The flowing oriole speaks empty clever words, but she is too tired to listen, needing no adjustment.

    小阁烹香茗防帘下玉钩灯光翻出鼎钗影倒沉瓯婢捧消春困亲尝散暮愁吟诗因坐久月转晩妆楼【右二】

    In the small pavilion, she brews fragrant tea, under the curtain where the jade hook hangs. The light flickers out of the tripod, casting the hairpin's shadow down into the cup. The maid holds something to dispel her spring fatigue, and she tastes it to disperse her evening sorrow. Reciting poetry because she has been sitting for a long time, the moon turns as she prepares for evening.

    灯前催晓妆把酒向髙堂但愿梅花月年年映夀觞【右三】

    Before the lamp, she urges morning makeup, holding wine toward the high hall. She only wishes for the plum blossom moon to be reflected in the cup of longevity year after year.

    采阁闭朝寒妆成拟问安忽闻春雪下唤婢卷帘看【右四】

    The gathering pavilion is closed in the morning cold, her makeup completed, ready to inquire about peace. Suddenly hearing the spring snow falling, she calls the maid to roll up the curtain and look.

    粲粲梅花盈盈似玉人甘心对氷雪不爱艶阳春【右五】

    Bright and shining, the plum blossom blooms, graceful and full, like a jade person. She willingly faces ice and snow, not loving the bright spring.

    小小春罗扇团团秋月生蟠桃花里绣得董防成【右六】

    A small spring silk fan, round and round, the autumn moon rises. In the blooming peach blossom, she embroiders the shield of Dǒng Fáng, completing it.

    自拂防眉黛何曽惯得愁若教如翠栁便恐不禁秋【右七】

    Gently brushing her eyebrows with black ink, she has never been accustomed to sorrow. If taught to be like the green willow, she fears not to withstand the autumn.

    七言诗曰楼前杨栁发青枝楼下春寒病起时独坐小窻无气力隔帘

    In a seven-character poem: Before the building, the willow sprouts green branches. In the spring cold, she rises from illness. Sitting alone in a small window, she feels no strength, separated by the curtain.

    This translation aims to be faithful to the original while maintaining a natural flow in English. Idiomatic expressions, cultural allusions, and proper nouns have been handled with care to preserve the original meaning and context.

    276

    风乱海棠丝【右一】緑窻寂寞掩残春绣得罗衣懒上身昨日翠帷新病起满帘飞絮正愁人【右二】小妹方才习孝经可怜娇怯性偏灵自寻女诫窻前读嗔道家人不与听【右三】几防梅花发小盆氷肌玉骨伴黄昏隔窻久坐怜清影闲划金钗记月防【右四】绣被寒多未欲眠梨花枝上听春鹃明朝又是清明节愁见人家买纸钱【右五】春雨随风湿粉墙园花滴滴断人膓愁红怨白知多少流过长沟水亦香【右六】春风昨夜碧桃开正想瑶池月满台欲折一枝寄王母青鸾飞去几时来【右七】空堦日晚雨才干小婢相随倚画防金钗误挂绯桃落罗袖愁依翠竹

    Explanation

    276

    The wind disarranges the threads of the crabapple blossoms. The green window, lonely, closes the remaining spring; having sewn the silk robe, she is lazy to put it on. Yesterday, the green scarf was new after getting sick; the full curtain of flying catkins makes one sad. My younger sister just started learning the Classic of Filial Piety; pitifully, her tender and timid nature is especially sensitive. She finds the Admonitions for Women by Lady Ban and reads it before the window, complaining that the family won't let her listen. A few pots of plum blossoms are about to bloom, with icy skin and jade bones, she accompanies the twilight. She sits by the window for a long time, admiring the clear shadow, leisurely, she uses a golden hairpin to mark the moon's phases. The embroidered quilt is cold, and she does not want to sleep; she listens to the cuckoo on the pear blossom branch. Tomorrow is the Qingming Festival, she worries about seeing others buy paper money. Spring rain brings moisture to the whitewashed walls, the garden flowers, dripping with rain, break one's heart. How many red and white things cause sorrow and resentment? The water flowing through the long ditch is fragrant. The spring wind last night opened the green peach blossoms, just imagining the moonlit platform in the Jade Pool, wanting to pick a branch to send to the Queen Mother, the blue phoenix flies away, when will it return? The empty steps in the evening, the rain has just stopped, a young maid follows and leans against the painted screen, a golden hairpin accidentally hangs on a red peach blossom, the silk sleeve worries as it leans on the green bamboo.

    277

    寒【右八】小窻今夕绣鍼闲坐对银蟾整翠鬟凡世何曽到天上月宫依旧似人间【右九】乞巧楼前雨乍晴弯弯新月伴防星隣家小女都相学鬭取金盆看五生【右十】庭院深深早闭门停鍼无语对黄昏碧纱窻外初生月照见梅花欲断防【右十一】未成章诗曰露下庭梧叶风吹月桂花登楼闻过鴈开户见栖鵶绣帘当雪卷银烛背风然雪晴山显翠风暖水生纹萱草当堦緑樱桃落地红芍药开时病荼防落处愁玉钗簮茉莉罗扇绣芙蓉窻前垂栁分春色镜里幽兰对晓妆花间影过那知燕栁外声来不见莺慈亲教婢囘金剪骄妹嗔人夺绣鍼妆成寳镜杨花过行

    Explanation

    277

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    278

    出珠帘燕子归自倾瓮里春泉水亲灌堦前石竹花海棠带雨胭脂重杨栁凝烟翡翠浓先生既防妻哀戚之情多见于诗悼亡曰惊颷吹罗幙明月照堦戺春草忽不芳秋兰亦同死斯人蕴淑德夙昔明诗礼灵质奄独化孤防将安止迢迢湘西山湛湛江中水水深有时极山髙有时已忧思何能齐日月从此始【右一】皇天平四时白日一何遽勤俭毕婚姻新人忽复故衾裳敛遗袭棺椁无完具送葬出北门徘徊怛归路玉顔不可恃况乃纨与素累累花下坟郁郁茔西他人亮同此胡为独哀慕【右二】新婚誓偕老恩义永且深旦暮为夫妇哀戚奄相寻凉月烛西楼悲风鸣北

    Explanation

    278

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    From ancient times, wise kings and sage emperors still required diligent study; how much more so, then, for common people! This matter is found throughout the classics and histories. I cannot elaborate further, but I will briefly mention a few essential points of recent times in order to enlighten you.

    As for writings, their origin lies in the Five Classics: edicts, memorials, and military proclamations arise from the Shujing (Book of Documents); expository and argumentative writings arise from the Yijing (Book of Changes); songs, odes, and praises arise from the Shijing (Book of Poetry); ritual sacrifices, mourning, and elegies arise from the Liji (Book of Rites); memorials, admonitions, and inscriptions arise from the Chunqiu (Spring and Autumn Annals). Court regulations, military oaths and decrees, the proclamation of benevolence and righteousness, the elucidation of virtue and merit, governing the people and establishing the state—all are indispensable. As for cultivating the mind and spirit, offering gentle admonition in a relaxed manner, and savoring the flavor of such writings, these too are pleasant pursuits. When one has spare energy, then one may study them.

    Final Translation of the Source Text

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    From ancient times, wise kings and sage emperors still required diligent study; how much more so, then, for common people! This matter is found throughout the classics and histories. I cannot elaborate further, but I will briefly mention a few essential points of recent times in order to enlighten you.

    As for writings, their origin lies in the Five Classics: edicts, memorials, and military proclamations arise from the Shujing (Book of Documents); expository and argumentative writings arise from the Yijing (Book of Changes); songs, odes, and praises arise from the Shijing (Book of Poetry); ritual sacrifices, mourning, and elegies arise from the Liji (Book of Rites); memorials, admonitions, and inscriptions arise from the Chunqiu (Spring and Autumn Annals). Court regulations, military oaths and decrees, the proclamation of benevolence and righteousness, the elucidation of virtue and merit, governing the people and establishing the state—all are indispensable. As for cultivating the mind and spirit, offering gentle admonition in a relaxed manner, and savoring the flavor of such writings, these too are pleasant pursuits. When one has spare energy, then one may study them.

    Final Translation of the Source Text

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    From ancient times, wise kings and sage emperors still required diligent study; how much more so, then, for common people! This matter is found throughout the classics and histories. I cannot elaborate further, but I will briefly mention a few essential points of recent times in order to enlighten you.

    As for writings, their origin lies in the Five Classics: edicts, memorials, and military proclamations arise from the Shujing (Book of Documents); expository and argumentative writings arise from the Yijing (Book of Changes); songs, odes, and praises arise from the Shijing (Book of Poetry); ritual sacrifices, mourning, and elegies arise from the Liji (Book of Rites); memorials, admonitions, and inscriptions arise from the Chunqiu (Spring and Autumn Annals). Court regulations, military oaths and decrees, the proclamation of benevolence and righteousness, the elucidation of virtue and merit, governing the people and

    279

    林空帷奠巾栉中房虚织絍辞章余婉娈琴瑟有余音睠言瞻故物恻怆内不任岂无新人好焉知谐我心掩穴抚长暮涕下霑衣襟【右三】人生贵有别室家各有宜贫贱逺结婚中心两不移前日良宴防今为死别离亲戚各在前临诀不成辞傍人拭我泪令我要裁悲共尽固人理谁能心勿思【右四】感独曰幽幽蕙草晚靡靡兰芳断皎皎夜泉人防防不复旦流尘栖暗壁凉吹经虚幔无论懽意消日复愁思乱防伤夕方永气变秋将晏当窻惨断素捐箧悲柔翰忆初成好合誓且同忧患何言遂长终独处増永叹寤寝忽如在展转惊复散念兹何嗟及哀至聊自判百日曰人生

    Explanation

    279

    In the empty room, the curtains are hung, and the mirror and comb are placed in the middle. The room is empty, and the weaving and writing have stopped. The soft sounds of the lute and zither linger. Looking at the old objects, I feel deep sorrow. Is there no new person who can understand my heart? I cover the hole and comfort the long night, tears streaming down my sleeves. [Right Three] In life, it is precious to have distinctions; each household has its own suitability. Poor and humble people marry far away, and their hearts remain unchanged. Yesterday was a good feast, but today it is a deathly farewell. Relatives are all present, but I cannot say goodbye. Others wipe my tears, and I must cut my sorrow. Grief is inevitable in human nature; who can prevent it from touching my heart? [Right Four] Feeling alone, I say that the fragrant grass is dim in the evening, and the delicate orchid has withered. The clear night stream flows, but the morning will not return. Dust settles on the dark walls, and the cool breeze passes through the empty curtains. No matter how joyful feelings fade, daily thoughts become confused. The evening is long, and the autumn air is changing. Looking through the window, I am sad and break the white silk, grieving over the soft writing. Remembering the initial good union, we vowed to share joys and sorrows. How can I say that we will live forever? Now I am alone, and my endless sighs increase. Waking up at night, I feel as if I am still there, turning and turning in surprise. Thinking of this, what can I do? When sorrow comes, I will just judge it for myself. A hundred days, I say, is life.

    280

    悲死别矧在心相知新婚未及久杳杳遽何之昔为连理木今为断肠枝相去时几何百日奄在兹月有圆夕逝水无还期弃置非人情何以为我思入室曰妆阁闭长夜幽兰坐复春犹疑挑锦字不见掩罗巾故物空在目萧条生网尘【右一】虚窻明月满芳砌緑苔滋花间时染翰尚忆解题诗寂寞幽泉下贞心空自知【右二】追和蕙兰曰小窻开尽碧桃枝忆得青鸾化去时昨夜秋风妬幽怨梦中吹断素琴丝【右一】江上愁时复值春带围寛尽不宜身阶前旧种樱桃日暮飞花故着人【右二】嗟夫孙氏之诗依乎礼义先生之诗哀而不伤举得性情之正是可传也已

    Explanation

    280

    Grief at death, especially when it is someone close to the heart, is profound. The new marriage was not yet long, yet she has suddenly and mysteriously vanished, leaving behind only sorrow. Once they were like twin trees entwined, now they are like broken branches that pierce the heart. How much time has passed, just a hundred days, yet it seems like an eternity. The moon has its full nights, but the flowing water never returns. To abandon is not human nature, how can one not think of me? Entering the room, the dressing chamber is closed, sitting through the long night in seclusion. Still in spring, still doubtful, the embroidered letters are not seen, the silk handkerchief remains hidden. Old objects remain visible, but they are now covered in dust, symbolizing decay and loss.

    The empty window is filled with bright moonlight, the fragrant steps are covered with green moss. Occasionally writing in the flowers, still remembering the poems that explained the themes. In the solitude of the deep spring, the steadfast heart knows itself alone.

    Alas, the poetry of the Sun family adheres to propriety and righteousness. The master's poetry is sorrowful but not excessive, capturing the true nature and emotions, and thus it is worthy of being passed down.

    281

    为将嗜杀
    　　王皮者住鳯翔府城外八九里许盛暑中入城买皮料归至中途憩道傍大下忽有二卒来前状貌竒怪似非凡世间人遽问曰汝王皮欤王窃疑惧然不敢不以实对乃曰某是已卒曰隂府摄汝王曰某平生无他过恶望赐矜怜卒不诺又告曰容到家与妻子一别可乎卒乃诺将及门卒力挽之不能入王大呌救我救我比妻子来前王已仆地气絶既敛胷间微暖如生经宿未敢葢棺王于防漠中随卒至一所俨若王者之庭仪卫吏无不备具问曰汝为秦白起偏将坑赵降卒四十万知其罪否王答曰某佣工平生不曾读书不知白起为何人亦不知降卒为何事于是令王起凡再歴二庭问亦如之答亦如之乃反接

    Explanation

    281

    A General Who Enjoys Killing

    Wang Pi lived outside the city of Fengxiang Prefecture, about eight or nine li away. During the intense heat of summer, he entered the city to buy leather materials and returned home. On his way back, he rested under a large tree by the roadside. Suddenly, two soldiers approached him. They had strange and extraordinary appearances, unlike any ordinary mortals. They asked abruptly, "Are you Wang Pi?" Wang Pi felt a mixture of suspicion and fear but dared not lie. He replied, "Yes, I am." The soldiers said, "The underworld has summoned you." Wang Pi said, "I have committed no other sins in my life. I hope you will show mercy." The soldiers refused. He then begged, "May I go home to say goodbye to my wife and children?" The soldiers agreed. As he approached his home, the soldiers pulled him back, preventing him from entering. Wang Pi cried out, "Save me! Save me!" By the time his wife and children arrived, Wang Pi had already collapsed and died. After his body was wrapped, his chest remained slightly warm, as if he were still alive. They did not dare to close the coffin for a night. In the afterlife, Wang Pi followed the soldiers to a place that resembled a royal court, with all the ceremonial guards, officials, and servants in place. They asked, "Did you serve as a subordinate general under Bai Qi, the general of Qin, and participate in the massacre of forty thousand Zhao soldiers who had surrendered? Do you know the crime you have committed?" Wang Pi replied, "I was a laborer all my life. I never studied, so I do not know who Bai Qi is, nor do I know what happened to the surrendered soldiers." They ordered Wang Pi to stand up, and this happened twice, with him being taken to two different courts. The questioning and his answers remained the same each time. Finally, they bound him with his hands behind his back.

    282

    王一大池边取池中泥涂其胸寒气凛冽洞腹透背王即悟曰某巳记前身事矣遂解其缚复引至原问第三庭王告曰某曽为白起偏将其当年杀赵降卒时某曽力谏不从非某之罪顷间牵一荷鐡校者跪王侧王认得似是白起而形骸骨立又若非似葢因久囚故也起见王曰子来矣余复何言方招承庭吏发王还第一庭检録阳夀及阅籍尚有若干年即命原摄卒引至原憩下一推而王乃在棺中跳跃而起妻子亲隣既惊且喜叩问之备言其故有传之至京师者差进士卨哲笃来鳯翔覆察果实时王元吉为本府照磨元吉能备言其详且有抄録公文此一事然虽若幻诞端可为为将而嗜杀人之戒故畧节大槩如上

    Explanation

    282

    In a large pool, Wang took mud from the pool and applied it to his chest. A cold sensation penetrated his abdomen and back. Wang immediately realized that he had remembered his past life. He was then released and brought back to the third court. Wang explained that he had once been a subordinate of Bai Qi, who had ordered the massacre of the surrendered soldiers of Zhao. At the time, Wang had tried to advise against it but was not heeded. He denied any personal guilt. Shortly after, a person carrying a heavy iron chain knelt beside Wang. Wang recognized him as Bai Qi, but he appeared emaciated, likely due to long imprisonment. Bai Qi saw Wang and said, "You are here. What more can I say?" Wang then confessed. The court officials sent Wang back to the first court, where they recorded his remaining lifespan and reviewed his records. It was found that he had several more years left. He was then led back to the original resting place. Suddenly, Wang jumped out of the coffin, alive. His wife, relatives, and neighbors were both shocked and delighted. They asked him about the events, and he provided a detailed account. The story was reported to the capital city, and officials were sent to investigate. It was confirmed that Wang Yuanji was indeed the magistrate of the local government. Wang Yuanji was able to provide a detailed account of the events and had official documents to support his claims. Although this story may seem fantastical, it serves as a warning to generals who indulge in killing. This is a summary of the main points.

    283

    释怨结姻
    　　州泰兴县马驼沙农夫司大者其里中富人陈氏之佃家也家贫不能出租以输主乃将以所佃田转质于他姓陈氏田傍有李庆四者亦业佃种潜赂主家儿约能夺田与我而不以与陈氏者以所酬钱十倍之一分之家儿素用事因以利防其主主听夺田归李氏司固无可奈何既以谷田不相侔轻其直十之一司愈不平防归而李与尝所用力及为立劵者杀鸡饮酒司因随所之李欲却司輙先将一巵酒饮之司忿恨去对妻语所以与李怨仇之故妻苦口谏曰吾之穷命也柰何仇人哉不听夜持炬火往烧其家忽闻得内有人娩司窃念吾所讐者其家公也何故杀其母子遂弃

    Explanation

    283

    Title: Resolving Grievances and Establishing Alliances

    In Yangzhou, Taixing County, on the Ma Tuoshah River, there was a tenant farmer named Si Da. He was a member of the wealthy Chen family in his village. Poor and unable to pay rent to his landlord, he was going to transfer the leased land as collateral to another family. Near Chen's land, there was a Li Qing Si who also worked as a tenant farmer. Li secretly bribed the landlord's son to agree to transfer the land to him and not give it to Chen, offering ten times the compensation. The landlord's son was usually in charge. He used the bribe to influence the landlord. The landlord agreed to transfer the land to Li. Si had no choice but to accept it. Since the grain and land were not equivalent, he reduced the price by one-tenth. Si became even more resentful. When he returned, Li and those who had helped him by witnessing the contract drank wine and sacrificed a chicken. Si followed them. Li wanted to get rid of Si and gave him a cup of wine first. Si left in anger. He told his wife the reason for his grudge against Li. His wife strongly advised him, saying, "My poor fate! Why should I hate the enemy?" He didn't listen and at night, with a torch, went to burn Li's house. Suddenly, he heard someone giving birth inside. Si thought to himself, "The one I hate is their father; why should I kill their mother and child?" And he abandoned the plan.

    284

    火沟中而归司无以为养生计即所偿钱为豆乳酿酒货卖以给食乆之不复乏絶更自有余而李日益贫更十年李复出所佃田质陈氏司还用李计复其田过种之钱比前又损其一为劵悉值前人相视惊叹司记为李所辱时今幸可一报复遂具鸡酒饮亦如之李忘前过不自责反怨薄已怒甚归积膏火破盎中夜抵司家司妻方就蓐李犹豫间闻人启户惧事觉遗火亟走而司家实不有人旦得火器塲中验器底有李字因悟昔我焚彼家以其家人产子不欲焚今彼焚我家而我之妻亦产子而不被焚此天也非人也持钱五千往李曰昨日小人无状失礼义不得共饮兹愿少伸谢

    Explanation

    284

    Mr. Si returned by fire, having no means to sustain himself. He used the money he had to repay to make soy milk, brew alcohol, and sell it to provide food. After a long time, he no longer faced shortages and even had surplus. Meanwhile, Liu became increasingly poor. Ten years later, Liu again mortgaged the land he leased to Chen. Mr. Si used Liu's plan to regain his land, paying back the money he had borrowed, which was less than before. The contract was valued as it was before, and people looked at each other in amazement. Mr. Si remembered being humiliated by Liu and now had the opportunity to retaliate. He prepared chicken and wine to drink, just as Liu had done. Liu forgot his past wrongdoings, did not blame himself, but instead resented Mr. Si and became very angry. He returned home, collected oil, and broke a jar, coming to Mr. Si's house at night. Mr. Si's wife was just giving birth. Liu hesitated, heard someone opening the door, feared being discovered, and quickly fled, leaving the fire. Mr. Si's house was actually empty. The next day, they found the fire-setting device in the field and saw that the bottom of the device had Liu's name. They realized that when Mr. Si burned Liu's house, it was because Liu's family had a child, and he did not want to burn them. Now Liu burned Mr. Si's house, but Mr. Si's wife also had a child and was not burned. This must be fate, not a person. Mr. Si took five thousand coins to go. Liu said, "Yesterday, I acted improperly and lost my manners, so we could not drink together. Now I wish to offer a small token of thanks."

    285

    意幸毋督过李疑绐以疾卧不起强请不已遂同之酒家邀酤儿与饮酒半自起酌酒劝李曰子之孙某年月日夜子时生而吾子亦夜者子时生怨仇之事慎勿复为具白前所仇事沥酒为誓语酤儿曰子识之试用此警世间人不善慎勿为也剧饮尽欢乃更约为婚姻自是李亦不贫两家至今丰给此在至正初元间吾谓司氏妇之极諌与司氏之易虑时天固已监之所以李不复可加害也向使司氏决快所欲未必能复田纵复田未必其无祸一念之善从而两家子孙皆其利泽书曰天道福善祸淫又曰惟上帝不常作善降之百祥作不善降之百殃呜呼天岂逺人哉天岂逺人哉

    Explanation

    285

    I hope you will not be too strict. Li Yi deceived by claiming to be ill and not getting up, but was persistently invited. They then went to a wine shop together and invited the wine seller to drink. Halfway, he got up himself, poured wine, and encouraged Li, saying, "Your grandson was born on a certain day and time, and my son was also born at the same time at night. Carefully do not repeat matters of resentment and hatred. Fully explain the previous matters of resentment, and take an oath by pouring wine. He said to the wine seller, 'You remember it. Try to use this to warn people of the world. Carefully do not do bad things.' They drank heavily and enjoyed fully, and then renewed their agreement to become in-laws. From then on, Li was also not poor, and the two families are still abundant and well-provided. This happened in the early years of the Zhi Zheng era. I say that the wife of Si strongly advised and the wife of Si changed her mind. At that time, heaven had already supervised it, so Li could no longer be harmed. If only the wife of Si decided to satisfy her desires, she may not be able to return to farming. Even if she returns to farming, she may not be without disaster. A single good thought thereby benefited the descendants of the two families. The Book of History says that the way of heaven blesses the good and harms the wicked. Also says that only the Supreme Deity does not always bestow a hundred blessings for good deeds and a hundred calamities for evil deeds. Alas, how could heaven be far from people. How could heaven be far from people.

    286

    杜荀诗
    　　尝读杜荀诗其乱后逢村叟曰经乱衰翁居破村村中何事不伤防因供寨木无桑柘为着乡兵絶子孙还似平宁徴赋税未尝州县畧安存至于鸡犬皆星散日落前山独倚门山中寡妇曰夫因兵死守蓬茅麻苎衣衫髪焦桑柘废来犹纳税田园荒后尚徴苗时挑野菜和根煮旋斫生柴带叶烧任尔深山更深处也应无计避征徭旅泊遇郡中乱曰握手相看谁敢言军家刀剑在要边徧搜寳货无藏处乱杀平人不怕天古寺拆为修寨木荒坟掘作甃城砖郡侯逐去浑闲事正是銮舆幸蜀年然方之今日始信其非寓言也

    Explanation

    286

    In the aftermath of the chaos, an old man lives in a ruined village. What in the village is not damaged or destroyed? Because he supplies wood for the fortifications, there are no mulberry or elm trees. For the sake of serving as a local militia, his descendants are cut off. It's still like in times of peace when taxes are collected. The state and counties have never been roughly secure. Even chickens and dogs scatter like stars. At sunset, he stands alone by the door of the front mountain.

    A woman in the mountains says: Her husband died in battle, leaving her to guard the thatched hut. She wears hemp and ramie clothes, her hair burned and frayed. Even though the mulberry and elm trees have been abandoned, she still pays taxes. Even after the fields have become barren, she is still taxed on the produce. She sometimes picks wild vegetables and cooks them with their roots. She chops fresh firewood and burns it with leaves. No matter how deep into the mountains you go, there should be no way to avoid the corvée and taxes.

    During travel, encountering the chaos in the county, they hold hands and look at each other, who dares to speak? The military's swords and spears are at the critical border. They search everywhere for treasures, leaving no place to hide. They kill ordinary people without fearing the heavens. The ancient temple is dismantled to build fortifications. The abandoned graves are dug up to make bricks for the city walls. The prefect is driven away, and it is all trivial. It is indeed the year when the imperial carriage travels to Shu. But when compared to today, it is only then that one believes it is not a fable.

    287

    太公
    　　今人谓曾祖父曰太公此葢相承之谬当称祖父为是后汉李固之父郃为司空固女当固伏诛日曰太公以来云注太公谓祖父郃也
    　　刚介
    　　御史台准陕西行台咨监察御史乌克逊良祯呈状闻纲常者天之所以经天下者也天子所以为天守纲常者也臣而不忠子而不孝凡触罪于纲常者不容于死又乌可处以相位俾之重任乎谨按辽阳行省丞相达实特穆尔【即驸马丞相也】心怀隂险行畜奸邪败壊彛伦反侧不道通天之罪无所于容昔在晋邸擢登首相居百僚之上极一品之荣受任托孤躬承頋命君臣分义至重且深及乎大事之时干戈之际尽领北土之兵以救颠危本官隂畜二心坐观成败南至红桥逗遛不进致于败亡不能死义腼面入降大

    Explanation

    287

    Grandfather
    In modern times, people refer to their great-grandfather as "Grandfather" (太公), which is a mistake passed down through generations. It should be called "Grandfather" (祖父). During the Eastern Han Dynasty, Li Gu's father, He, was a Grand Marshal. When Li Gu was executed, his daughter said, "Grandfather (太公) since then, referring to Grandfather (太公), is He."

    Upright and Unbending
    The Imperial Censorate approved a petition from the Shaanxi Regional Office, submitted by the Inspector General Wukuxun Liangzhen, stating that the principles of social order and ethics (纲常) are what the heavens use to govern the world. The emperor is the one who upholds these principles on behalf of the heavens. If a minister is not loyal or a son is not filial, anyone who violates these principles cannot be allowed to live, let alone be placed in the position of chancellor and given important responsibilities? According to the records, the Chancellor of the Liaoyang Provincial Administration, Dashi Temuer (also known as the Prince's Chancellor), harbored malicious intentions, engaged in wicked and deceitful behavior, corrupted the moral order, and was unstable and immoral. He committed a crime against heaven, and there is no place for him. In the past, when he was in the Prince's residence, he was appointed as the Prime Minister, occupying the highest rank among officials and enjoying the highest honor. He was entrusted with the care of the orphaned heir and personally received the imperial decree. The duties and obligations between ruler and subject were extremely important and profound. During times of crisis or significant events, especially during wars or conflicts, he commanded all the northern armies to save the perilous situation. However, he failed to die for the cause and shamefacedly surrendered.

    288

    臣节反以借口矜为己功天下义士闻之莫不为耻昔丁公为项王一将耳尝二心于汉及天下定髙帝诛之后世称其明断方楚之与汉敌国之势未分尚以大义责之以示垂戒今达实特穆尔之于晋邸爵禄之宠己崇君臣之分素定较之丁公不忠之罪又有甚焉况天歴之初营充枢密知院御史已尝紏言又复贿赂权臣出为江西行省丞相两居江浙至与房隣拜降都运贿赂交通壊乱盐法至今官民皆其害中间徇私败政不可枚举所至之处流毒一方今则移置辽阳辽阳民奚罪焉原其本官昔既不忠今岂尽节又兼

    Explanation

    288

    The minister's duty is reversed by using it as an excuse to claim credit for oneself; all righteous men in the world, upon hearing this, would feel ashamed. In ancient times, Ding Gong was merely a general under Xiang Wang, yet he had divided loyalties towards Han. When the Han dynasty was established and Emperor Gaozu executed him, posterity praised his decisive judgment. Even then, when the power between Chu and Han was still evenly matched, he was still held accountable by great principles to serve as a warning for future generations. Now, Daris Temur, in his position at the imperial court, enjoys high status and clear distinctions between ruler and subject. Compared to Ding Gong's disloyalty, his crime is even more severe. Furthermore, in the early years of the Tianli era, when he was serving in the imperial court as a high-ranking official, he had already been criticized. He then bribed powerful officials and was appointed as the Chancellor of Jiangxi Province. He held positions in both Jiangsu and Zhejiang provinces, including being appointed as the Director of Transportation. He engaged in bribery and corruption, disrupting the salt laws, causing harm to both officials and the people. In the meantime, he engaged in numerous acts of self-interest that led to the failure of policies, which cannot be listed one by one. Wherever he went, he caused widespread harm. Now, he has been relocated to Liaoyang, but the people of Liaoyang have committed no crime. Considering his past lack of loyalty, how can he be expected to be loyal now? Furthermore, he has engaged in bribery and corruption, disrupting the salt laws, causing harm to both officials and the people.

    289

    辽阳即系东方重镇反覆之人岂宜处此脱有边衅闗系非轻似此卖国卖臣之罪使其人已死犹当追贬今既未死得以幸逭天诛设居相位是国法不行邪正不辨愚恐奸臣贼子接迹仿效甚非国家之福伏望闻奏为天下正纲常之义将达实特穆尔流窜遐荒追夺累受宣命庶几人臣分严罪于纲常者死有余辜以为不忠不道之劝其于治道不为小补天下幸甚公论幸甚至正元年八月十二日伯勒齐尔布哈集赛第一日呼噜图巴纳里有时分伊勒都齐汪嘉努殿中巴咱尔大夫额琳沁巴勒经歴桑节孟古古笔且齐托多

    Explanation

    289

    Liaoyang is indeed a significant stronghold in the eastern region. A fickle person should not be placed in such a position. If there is any border conflict, the matter is not trivial. The crime of treason or betrayal, if the person is already dead, should still be posthumously condemned. Since they are not dead, they have managed to avoid divine punishment. If they were to hold the position of chancellor, it would mean that the state law is not being enforced and right and wrong are not distinguished. I fear that corrupt officials and traitors would follow one another in imitation, which is very much against the nation's best interest. I humbly request that this be reported to the throne to establish the moral principles of the world. The person should be exiled to a remote place and all previously granted imperial edicts should be revoked. Perhaps those who violate the moral principles of the state as subjects should be punished severely, even to the point of death, to serve as a warning against disloyalty and unrighteousness. It would not be a small contribution to good governance. May this be fortunate for the world. On the twelfth day of the eighth month in the first year of the Zhengyuan era, according to public opinion, this was proposed by Berleqierbuka Jisai, with the participation of Hurutu Banaari, Yileduqi Wangjia, Dunzhong Bazaer, Yalingqin Balijingli, Sangjie Menggugubi, and Qituo.

    290

    等奏台官备着西台文书俺商量来行与省家文书将他见行的勾当黜罢了呵怎生奏呵奉圣防那般者钦此初良祯之父江东建康道肃政廉访使润甫公【泽】年五十未有子夫人杜氏深以为忧屡请公再聘公不允仕西广时闻寡居王安人者美而宜子夫人自为公谋聘之既归执妇礼甚恭长夫人数嵗夫人推让正寝以居之相处雍睦宛若姊娣饮食起居罔有不同公独内不自安越明年夫人生良祯一日王氏告公曰君自有妇所以再娶妾者为嗣续计耳今夫人既生子妾又何事焉即出道家冠服一袭以示曰妾之志

    Explanation

    290

    Upon receiving the documents from the court officials, we prepared the Western Court's records and consulted with the provincial documents to dismiss the ongoing affairs. How should we proceed? We were instructed to follow the imperial decree as ordered. Initially, Liang Zhen's father, the Viceroy of Jiangdong, Jiankang, and the Inspector of Justice, Mr. Runfu Gong [Ze], was fifty years old and had no son. His wife, Du Shi, was deeply concerned about this and repeatedly asked him to remarry, but he refused. While serving in the western regions, he heard of a widow named Wang Anren, who was beautiful and suitable for bearing children. His wife arranged for him to marry her. After the marriage, Wang Anren observed the proper wife's etiquette very respectfully and was several years older than her husband. The wife yielded the main bedroom to her, and they lived together in harmony, as if they were sisters, sharing all aspects of daily life. However, the husband felt uncomfortable inside. The following year, the wife gave birth to Liang Zhen. One day, Wang Anren told her husband, "You already have a wife, so I was married to you for the sake of having an heir. Now that the wife has given birth, what role do I have?" She immediately presented a set of Daoist robes and said, "This is my intention."

    291

    决矣请从此辞夫人固留不得公因谓夫人曰向吾再娶惧无后也若不改图人其以我为汰乎乃听王氏去奁赀万金悉返之自是出居一女道庵戒行严谨人未尝能见其面而夫人嵗时问遗弥至后良祯贵显迎以归事之如亲母嗟夫自古求忠臣于孝子之门今良祯外有严君内有贤母教诲造就之道有过人者宜乎在家为孝子而在朝为忠臣也然其敭歴台省秉性刚介不畏彊御事无不言言必有中如驸马丞相恃居国戚莫敢孰何乃必发其底里直使去位而后已推此一节则凡忠君之事可知矣后至中书左丞而卒
    　　发墓
    　　至元间释氏豪横改宫观为寺削道士为髠且各处陵墓发掘迨尽孤山林和靖处士墓尸骨皆空惟遗一玉簪时有人作诗以悼之曰生前不系黄金带身后空余白玉簮

    Explanation

    291

    Decidedly, I will part from here. The lady could not persuade him to stay. He then said to her, "Formerly, I remarried out of fear of not having descendants. If I do not change my plans, people will think I am extravagant." Thus, Lady Wang left, taking with her a dowry worth ten thousand gold pieces, which he returned all of it. From then on, she lived out as a nun, strict in her moral conduct. No one had ever seen her face, but the lady's gifts and visits became even more frequent. Later, when Liang Zhen became wealthy and prominent, he welcomed her back, treating her like a mother. Alas, from ancient times, wise kings and sage emperors still required diligent study; how much more so, then, for common people! This matter is found throughout the classics and histories. I cannot elaborate further, but I will briefly mention a few essential points of recent times in order to enlighten you.

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling

    292

    辍耕録巻十三
    <子部,小说家类,杂事之属,辍耕录>
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷十四　　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰忠烈
    　　萧景茂漳州龙溪隔洲里人儒而有文以谨厚信扵乡里后至元间漳冦乱景茂率鄊人立栅保险坚不可破防旁里有人导之从间道入景茂执贼使拜曰汝贼也何拜贼欲胁之降以従民望景茂骂曰逆贼国家何负汝而反汝族汝里何负汝而坐累之贼相语曰吾杀官军将吏多矣至吾砦皆愞靡求生未有若此饿夫之倔彊者察其志终不为吾用留之祗取辱耳遂缚之扵树刲其肉使自防之且嚼且骂曰我食我肉无若汝贼行将万叚狗弃不食贼怒絶其舌而死又江州路总管李黼字子威汝宁人泰定丁卯状元及第至正十年庚寅来守是郡政修民和明年辛卯夏五月红巾冦逼淮西公即申告江西行省以谓

    Explanation

    292

    Records of a Plowman's Interruption, Volume Thirteen
    <Category: Miscellaneous Tales, Subcategory: Records of a Plowman's Interruption>
    Approved for the Four Treasuries

    Records of a Plowman's Interruption, Volume Fourteen
    Compiled by Tao Zongyi of the Yuan dynasty

    Loyal and Valiant

    Xiao Jingmao, a man of letters from Gezhou Village in Longxi, Zhangzhou, was both a scholar and a literate person. He was diligent, honest, and trustworthy in his village. During the reign of Emperor Kublai Khan, bandits caused chaos in Zhangzhou. Xiao Jingmao led his fellow villagers to set up barriers for defense, which proved unbreakable. However, someone from a nearby village guided the bandits through a secret path. Xiao Jingmao confronted them, forcing them to bow and saying, "You are bandits, why bow to each other?" The bandits attempted to threaten him to surrender in order to appease the people. Xiao Jingmao retorted, "Traitors, what has the country done to you to make you rebel? Your clan and village have done nothing wrong, why are you making them suffer?" The bandits discussed among themselves, saying, "We have killed many officials and soldiers, but all the camps are full of cowards trying to survive. No one is as stubborn as this starving man. Seeing his determination, he will not serve us. Keeping him will only bring shame." They tied him to a tree, cut off his flesh, and made him eat it while he chewed and cursed, saying, "I am eating my own flesh, but I will not eat you, you traitorous dogs, who will be thrown away like refuse." The bandits became angry and cut out his tongue, causing him to die.

    Another example is Li Fu, the Governor of Jiangzhou, whose courtesy name was Ziwei. He was from Runing and passed the imperial examination and became the top scholar in the year Dingmao of the Taizhong era. In the year Gengyin of the Zhizheng era, he came to govern this prefecture, where he implemented good governance and maintained harmonious people. The following year, in the fifth month of the summer in the year Xinmao, the Red Turban rebels attacked the western part of Huai region. Li Fu immediately reported to the provincial government of Jiangxi, thinking that...

    293

    九江为豫章藩屏之地蕲黄乃九江唇齿之不可不早为进兵守护或者非其过虑公乃张文榜以谕民曰为臣死忠为子死孝在黼之分惟知尽死守土而已所谓城存与存城亡与亡者也闻者悚然秋九月冦侵蕲黄属邑公复上言宜速乘机进援茍淮西失守长江之险与彼共之非所恃矣行省不报既而蕲州防冬十月黄州防十一月二十五日行省平章图卜戬布哈奉中书省命领兵至公极陈攻守之策图卜戬布哈以隄备把截为辞越明年壬辰春正月初二日行省左丞博啰特穆尔奉总兵御史大夫领枢宻院额森特穆尔命领兵进攻淮西亦来屯住逗遛不前十四日武昌防十六日藩王大臣官民舟航蔽江而下我

    Explanation

    293

    Jiujiang is a strategic buffer zone for Yuzhang, and Qihuang is a close ally of Jiujiang. It is essential to deploy troops early for its defense; perhaps this concern is not excessive. The public figure issued an official proclamation to the people, saying: "As a subject, one must die for loyalty; as a son, one must die for filial piety. My duty is to defend the land to the death. This is what is meant by 'living and dying with the city.'" Those who heard it were greatly alarmed. In the ninth month of autumn, the enemy invaded the subordinate counties of Qihuang. The public figure again submitted a memorial, urging a swift opportunity to advance and provide support.
