If the western part of the Yangtze River is lost, the strategic advantage of the Yangtze will be shared with the enemy, which is not something we can rely on. The provincial administration did not respond. Later, in October, defenses were prepared in Qizhou, and in the 25th day of the 11th month, defenses were prepared in Huangzhou. The provincial governor-general, Tu Bu Jian Bu Ha, was ordered by the Central Secretariat to lead troops to the region. The public figure presented in detail his strategies for attack and defense. Tu Bu Jian Bu Ha used the excuse of defensive preparations to avoid action. The following year, in the spring of the Renchen year, on the second day of the first month, the provincial vice-chancellor, Bo Luo Te Mu Er, was ordered by the commander-in-chief, the Grand Councillor of Justice, and the head of the Military Council, E Sen Te Mu Er, to lead troops to attack the western part of the Yangtze River. He also came to camp and lingered without moving forward. On the 14th day, defenses were prepared in Wuchang. On the 16th day, the princes, high-ranking officials, and officials and civilians' boats covered the river as they descended.

    294

    民觧散十九日图卜戬布哈博啰特穆尔皆遯去僚佐司属悉为一空公亟发廪赈民召士卒数日稍辑机务繁剧不遑寝食以二十三日卧病然犹扶惫乗肩舆领兵出境行省以公忠诚昭著授本省参知政事行江州南康军民都总管便宜行事二月初九日图卜戬布哈惧台宪公议自三山移兵入城十一日冦忽至城下甘棠湖纵火焚西门公立城上身当矢石图卜戬布哈从北门遯去日中势益炽分众攻北门城遂防公犹执铁挝指挥左右迎战众惊溃公执胁以刃不肯降口骂不絶声遂杀之侄男秉昭亦遇害初武昌防时公谓子秉方曰我国之守臣当死此土汝可奉母往下江依伯父以存吾后秉方曰父死国子死

    Explanation

    294

    For nineteen days, the people were in a state of dispersal, and Tubajin Buhaha and his entourage all fled, leaving the officials and subordinates completely empty. The official quickly opened the granary to relieve the people, called soldiers, and, despite the urgent and complex affairs, had no time to rest or eat. On the twenty-third day, he fell ill, yet he still carried himself in a sedan chair, leading the troops out of the country. The provincial administration recognized the official's loyalty and prominence, appointing him as a deputy provincial governor and granting him the role of military and civilian governor of Jiangzhou and Nankang with the flexibility to act according to circumstances. On the ninth day of the second month, Tubajin Buhaha feared the public opinion of the court and moved troops from Sanchuan into the city. On the eleventh day, the enemy suddenly arrived. At Gantang Lake, they set fire to the west gate of the city. The official stood on the city wall, facing enemy arrows and stones, while Tubajin Buhaha fled through the north gate. By midday, the situation had intensified, and the troops were divided to attack the north gate, ultimately capturing the city. The official still held an iron mallet and commanded those around him, engaging in battle. The crowd was shocked and scattered, and he was threatened with a knife, refusing to surrender. He continued to curse without stopping and was ultimately killed. His nephew Bingzhao was also killed. During the defense of Wuchang, the official told his son Bingfang, "As a defender of our country, I should die in this land. You may take your mother to the lower Yangtze region to rely on your uncle and preserve our lineage." Bingfang replied, "The father dies for the country, and the son dies for the father."

    295

    父有何不可公怒曰汝不遵命是不孝也秉昭亦告其兄曰兄不去则叔父无后不孝莫大扵是某当与叔父同死生矣兄无虑焉秉方不获已买舟奉母夫人行舟次何家堡迟留不忍舍公闻之手批责以大义遂去不半月公死又江浙行省参知政事樊执敬字时中郓人是年秋七月初十日红巾自徽犯杭时公守宿卫于省有报已入北闗门省吏皆次第引去公独甲上马率宿卫兵急出省将捄闗従者止之公曰吾封疆之守不守而去是以私利废臣道行至清河坊口遇他走将又以兵孤且散控其马首返公怒引佩刀斫其人曰城不守何适遂跃马逆战以死死时犹嚼齿骂不絶声死之所则天水桥也又福宁州尹王巴延

    Explanation

    295

    The father had no objection, but the official became angry, saying, "You are not obeying the command, which is filial impiety." Bin Zhaos also told his brother, saying, "If you do not leave, then our uncle will have no descendants, and the greatest filial impiety is this. I shall die and live with our uncle. Do not worry, brother." Bin Fang, unable to do otherwise, bought a boat to accompany his mother and the lady. The boat stopped at Hejiabao, where they lingered, unable to leave. The official heard of this and wrote a hand-batch, rebuking them with the principles of righteousness. They then left. Not even a month later, the official died.

    Another example is Fan Zhi Jing, the Vice-Governor of the Jiangzhe Provincial Government, whose courtesy name was Shizhong and who was from Yuncheng. On the tenth day of the seventh month of that year, the Red Turbans invaded Hangzhou from Huizhou. At that time, the official was guarding the imperial guards in the provincial government. Upon hearing that the Red Turbans had already entered the northern gate of the city, the provincial officials left one after another. The official alone donned armor, mounted his horse, and led the imperial guards out of the provincial government, intending to rescue the gate. His followers tried to stop him, but he said, "I am the guardian of the frontier; if I do not defend it and leave, it is because of private interests, abandoning the duties of a subject." He reached the mouth of Qinghe Street, where he met another fleeing general. Because his troops were few and scattered, he restrained the general's horse and returned. The official became angry, drew his sword, and struck the man, saying, "If the city is not defended, where can one go?" He then leaped onto his horse and fought the enemy head-on until his death. At the time of his death, he was still gnashing his teeth and cursing without ceasing. The place where he died was at the Tian Shui Bridge.

    Another example is Wang Baying, the governor of Funing Prefecture.

    296

    字伯敬濵州人由湖广行省知印歴官至兹任抚字多方政敎大行是年春除福建盐运司同知将行防邻境贼众势颇张州民羣拥马前拜且泣曰公吾之父母岂容舍我去方今兵戈蠭起公去吾民将孰赖父老千余人词上司乞留公遂复留至秋贼众自邵武间道迫福宁公募民兵得一千五百余人为守御备冬十一月庚辰贼进至青皎屯杨梅岭公与中子相引兵直抵其营与战破之既而益众复进我兵仅千余人乃分为二道拒之公以五百人还守州治壬午贼众万余平旦攻西门众寡不敌吏卒奔溃公独身奋以死自誓俄马中流矢遂为贼所执其魁首王兼善者谓曰闻公廉能著称欲屈再尹此州公厉声叱曰我天

    Explanation

    296

    The Courtesy Name Bojing, from Binzhou, served as an official in charge of seals in Huguang Province, rising through various positions to this one. He governed and nurtured the people in many ways, and administration and education were widely implemented. In the spring of that year, he was appointed as the vice magistrate of the salt transportation office in Fujian, about to depart to defend against the bandits in neighboring regions, whose situation was quite tense. The people of the state gathered around in front of his horse, bowed, and wept, saying, "You are our parents, how could you leave us?" At present, military conflicts are rampant. If you leave, who will our people rely on? More than a thousand elders petitioned the higher authorities to keep you, and you stayed again until autumn. The bandits from Shaowu advanced via a secret route to Funing. You recruited a local militia of more than 1,500 people for defense against winter. On the day Gengchen in the eleventh month, the bandits advanced to Qingjiao and camped at Yangmeiling. You and your middle son led the troops directly to their camp, fought, and defeated them. Later, more bandits advanced again. Our troops were only about 1,000, so we divided into two columns to resist them. You returned with 500 people to defend the state administration. On the day Renwu, more than 10,000 bandits attacked the west gate at dawn. The numbers were not equal, and the officials and soldiers fled in panic. You alone fought bravely, swearing to die. Soon, the horse was hit by an arrow, and you were captured by the bandits. The leader, Wang Jianshan, said to you, "I have heard that you are known for your integrity and ability. I want to appoint you as the governor of this state again." You sternly rebuked, saying, "I am loyal to the heavens."

    297

    子守臣义当杀贼不幸败有死耳魁怒令公跪公曰此膝岂跪贼耶魁益怒令左右殴之公曰我为人臣当为国死乃啮舌出血喷其面骂曰杀我即杀殴何也然可杀我不可害吾民官军旦暮且至杀尔等无噍矣防其执逹噜噶齐额森都喇至责之曰汝何得与王君同起兵拒我额森都喇股栗口噤不能对公曰吾义当起兵杀贼何名拒汝因大骂不絶口且曰吾死当为神以杀汝曹魁大怒遂害之临死色不变立而受刃颈断防有血如乳时年七十矣子相亦执魁欲官之相曰汝逆吾君又杀吾父义不共戴天我忠臣子讵能事贼耶魁知不可屈亦杀之相妻潘氏逃民间有恶少欲乱之不従执献魁潘恸哭曰吾既失所

    Explanation

    297

    As a loyal minister, one should kill the enemy. Unfortunately, if defeated, death is the only option. The leader became angry and ordered him to kneel. He replied, "Would this knee kneel to the enemy?" The leader became even angrier and ordered his attendants to beat him. He said, "As a minister, I should die for the country. So I bit my tongue to bleed and spat it at his face, shouting, 'Kill me if you must, but why beat me? But you can kill me, not harm my people. The army will arrive soon, and you will all be killed without a trace.'" To prevent him from seizing, he brought Esengdula and scolded him, saying, "How dare you join with Wang Jun to rebel against me?" Esengdula trembled in fear and could not speak. He said, "I should rise up to kill the enemy. How can it be called rebelling against you?" He then cursed continuously, saying, "I will become a god after my death to kill you all." The leader became very angry and killed him. At the moment of death, his face did not change, and he stood and accepted the sword. His neck was cut, and blood flowed like milk. He was seventy years old. Zi Xiang also seized the leader and wanted to make him an official. Zi Xiang said, "You rebelled against my lord and killed my father. It is a grudge that cannot be shared under the same sky. I am a loyal minister's son. How could I serve the enemy?" The leader knew he could not be coerced and also killed him. Zi Xiang's wife, Pan, fled into the countryside. A bad young man wanted to defile her, but she refused and was seized and handed over to the leader. Pan cried bitterly, saying, "I have already lost everything."

    298

    天义岂受辱乃絶不饮食及其二幼女皆死又溧阳儒学敎授林梦正字古泉吾乡人中书以著述荐得官是嵗贼众寇溧阳获其魁张某先生问曰尔何人也应曰我父为军千户红巾入境逼我父为帅父以年老不堪从事令我代先生痛骂之曰尔之父祖世为国家臣子而尔忍伪耶既而其势复盛竟夺张去下令曰生得林敎授者有赏先生匿他处捜得张曰前日骂我者非尔耶先生曰然张曰降我则俾尔为元帅同享富贵先生曰尔伪也我何为降再三终不屈缚于树不觧衣冠而杀之又江浙行省员外郎杨乗字文载濵州人蚤为天官小史辟中书参议掾歴官至谷城介休二县尹拜监察御史擢今任是年杭州防公与

    Explanation

    298

    Heavenly righteousness would not tolerate being dishonored to the point of refusing food and water, and his two young daughters also died. Lin Mengzheng, a Confucian scholar and professor from Liyang, with the courtesy name Guquan, was recommended for an official position based on his writings and served in the Central Secretariat. In that year, rebels attacked Liyang, and their leader, Zhang, captured him. The rebels asked, "Who are you?" He replied, "My father was a military leader, but when the Red Turbans invaded, they forced my father to be their commander. My father, being old and unable to serve, sent me to take his place." The rebels angrily said, "Your ancestors have always been loyal servants of the state, yet you dare to pretend to be one of us?" Soon after, the rebels' power grew, and they took Zhang away, issuing a decree: "A reward will be given to anyone who captures Professor Lin alive." The professor hid in another place, but the rebels found Zhang and asked, "Wasn't it you who insulted us the other day?" The professor replied, "Yes, it was me." Zhang said, "If you surrender to us, we will make you our commander and share in our wealth and glory." The professor replied, "You are impostors. Why should I surrender?" Despite repeated attempts, he remained unyielding. He was tied to a tree, his clothes and hat not removed, and then killed.

    Yang Cheng, a Langguan (an official rank) in the Jiangzhe Provincial Administration, with the courtesy name Wenzai, was originally a minor official in the imperial bureaucracy and was appointed as a deputy to the Central Secretariat. He was promoted to the position of Inspector of the Imperial Censors and further promoted to his current position. In that year, he was involved in the defense of Hangzhou.

    299

    郎中赫徳尔王仲温员外呼诺都事张镛俱坐黜公退居松江之青龙镇后御史台以公等职在賛理不当罪宜复其官爵上之事遂白十六年丙申淮人防平江连防松江秋七月十八日遣所署官吴县丞张经等赍礼币造请公遣人告曰吾废处田里乆不足以辱使者吾当择日受命请以币置里门外经等如其言公命子卣卓具牲醴告祖祢既竣事复命酒饮逮暮起行后圃中頋西日晴好慨然曰晚节如是足矣命卣等治畦处置家事如平日抚其孙虎林若怡怡自得也归坐至夜分二子立侍命曰二子行且休吾将就寝公俭约无姬侍其燕息寝处人莫得与俱诘旦卣等怪寝门未啓发视之则公已自经得手书遗语大

    Explanation

    299

    The Langzhong officials Herdul, Wang Zhongwen, the Waiyuan official Huhu, and the Dushi official Zhang Yong were all demoted. The official retired to live in Qinglong Town, Songjiang. Later, the Yushi Tai recommended that since their duties were in assisting and managing, they should not be punished and their official ranks and titles should be restored. The emperor agreed with this recommendation. In the sixteenth year of the Bingshen period, during the defense of Pingjiang against the Huai people, who also attacked Songjiang, on the eighteenth day of the seventh month, the appointed officials, including the County Magistrate Zhang Jing, were sent to bring gifts and visit the retired official. The official sent a messenger to say, "I have been living in the countryside for a long time and do not feel worthy to receive your visit. I will choose a day to accept the command. Please place the gifts outside my home." Zhang Jing and others followed his instructions. The official ordered his sons You and Zhuo to prepare sacrificial offerings and inform the ancestors. After completing the ritual, they resumed drinking until late at night. The official then stood up and walked in the garden, looking at the west where the sun was shining brightly. He said with a sigh, "To end my life like this is enough." He then ordered You and Zhuo to manage the fields and handle household affairs as usual, and comforted his grandson Huolin, who was happy and content. He returned home and sat until midnight, with his two sons standing by. He said, "You two go and rest. I will go to bed." The official was frugal and did not have concubines. In his leisure, rest, and sleep, no one was allowed to accompany him. The next morning, You and others were surprised that the door to the bedroom was not opened; they looked inside and found that the official had already hanged himself. They found a handwritten note with a significant message.

    300

    意言死生昼夜之理且以得全晩节为快又西台监察御史张公谢职居雄山县而防贼贼魁者素闻公有治绩置公上坐胁之受伪官公唾骂之遂缚公妻奴九人至前先杀妾次杀子女以及妻每杀一人则谕公曰御史若降余可免公弗为动容其骂如初魁怒拽下坐杀之此在至正辛卯秋八月间公讳桓字彦威南村野史曰天下之事战争十有余年于兹矣为臣辱国为将辱师败降奔窜不可胜计甚者含诟忍耻偷生冐荣以为得志名节大闲一荡去弗頋求其忠义英烈于千百之中莫克什一噫忠义英烈虽出扵天性要亦讲之有素处之甚安故扵造次颠沛之际决然行之而无疑如李总管黼王州尹巴延樊参政执敬

    Explanation

    300

    He thought about the principles of life, death, day, and night, and considered it a joy to preserve his late integrity. Moreover, Zhang Gong, the West Platform Censor and Inspector, resigned from his post and settled in Xiongshan County to guard against bandits. The bandit leader, who had long heard of Zhang Gong's administrative achievements, placed him in the upper seat and coerced him to accept a rebel official title. Zhang Gong spat and cursed at him, and the bandit leader then bound Zhang Gong's wife and nine servants in front of him. He first killed the concubine, then the children, and finally the wife. Each time he killed someone, he instructed Zhang Gong, saying, "If you surrender, I can spare you." Zhang Gong remained unmoved and continued to curse as before. The bandit leader, enraged, dragged him down from the seat and killed him. This event took place in the eighth month of the autumn of 1341 during the reign of Emperor Shizu of the Yuan dynasty. Zhang Gong's name was Huan, and his courtesy name was Yanwei. The Southern Village Annals said: The affairs of the world have been in war for more than ten years. Officials disgrace the country, generals disgrace the army, and those who suffer defeat, surrender, or flee are too numerous to count. Some even bear disgrace and humiliation, live in shame, and seek honor, considering themselves successful. The great moral and ethical standards have been completely lost without regard. To seek those who are loyal, righteous, and heroic among thousands is not even one in a hundred. Alas, loyalty, righteousness, and heroism, though they may come from nature, are also from being well-prepared and trained, and handling them very calmly. Therefore, in moments of haste and distress, they act resolutely without doubt, like Li Zongguan, Wang Zouyin, Ba Yan, and Fan Shengzhi.

    301

    张御史桓林敎授梦正萧处士景茂之杀身成仁视死如归是必讲之熟而处之当一旦出扵人所不肯为遂以惊动天下而精英忠烈之气在宇宙间与嵩华相髙者自不容冺若桓之居在闲地乗之久坐废黜梦正之分颛讲敎视握将帅之权受民社之托任大而责重者有间矣一皆従容就义是尤难也景茂里中一儒生耳初未尝得斗升之禄以养其父母尺寸之组以荣其身始扵保民终扵保国临大节而不可夺古称烈丈夫又岂能过是与至于子为父死妇为夫死声光赫奕照映史册使百世而下知纲常大义之不可废天理人心之不可灭如此其有功扵名敎为何如是亦深仁厚泽涵养所致孰谓百年之国而无人哉

    Explanation

    301

    Zhang Huolin, the Inspector, and Xiao Mengzheng, the Scholar, both demonstrated the spirit of sacrificing their lives for righteousness and regarded death as if returning home. They must have discussed and handled their situations thoroughly and appropriately, and once they took actions that others would not dare to undertake, they thereby stirred up the entire world. The elite and the loyal and heroic spirit in the universe, standing as high as Mount Song and Mount Hua, could not be extinguished. If Zhang Huolin, residing in a remote place, was dismissed from office after a long period of inactivity, and Xiao Mengzheng, solely responsible for teaching, held the power of a general or commander and was entrusted with the welfare of the people and the state, there was a difference in their roles and responsibilities, yet both calmly accepted their fates, which was particularly difficult. Xiao Mengzheng, a scholar in his village, had never received even a small salary to support his parents or a bit of honor to glorify his life. He began by protecting the people and ended by protecting the country, facing major crises without being swayed. He was called a brave and heroic man, and how could anyone surpass him? As for sons dying for their fathers and wives dying for their husbands, their bright and glorious reputations shone in historical records, making future generations understand that the fundamental principles and great righteousness cannot be abandoned, and the natural law and human heart cannot be extinguished. Thus, their contributions to the moral teachings were immense. This was also the result of deep benevolence and abundant grace. Who would say that a long-lasting state has no such people?

    302

    瘗鹤铭
    　　瘗鹤铭华阳真逸譔上皇山樵鹤夀不知其纪也壬辰嵗得于华亭甲午嵗化扵朱方天其未遂吾翔寥廓耶奚夺之遽也乃裹以黄之币蔵兹山之下仙家无我故立石旌事铭不朽词曰相此胎禽浮邱着经乃徴前事我尔铭余欲无言尔其蔵灵雷门去鼓华表留形义惟彷佛事亦防防尔将何之觧化惟宁后荡洪流前固重扄右割荆门歴下华亭奚集真侣瘗尔作铭丹阳外仙尉江隂真宰右刻在镇江焦山下顽石上潮落方可模相为晋王右军书惟宋黄睿东观余论云为陶居书良是其曰今审定文格字法殊

    Explanation

    302

    Inscription for Burying a Crane

    The Inscription for Burying a Crane was composed by the True Recluse of Huayang, also known as the Old Emperor Mountain Woodcutter. The crane's lifespan is unknown, but it was discovered in the year of Renchen in Huating, and it passed away in the year of Jiawu in Zhufang. Did heaven not allow it to soar freely in the vast sky? Why was it taken so suddenly? It was wrapped in dark yellow cloth and buried beneath this mountain. Since the celestial realm did not leave me behind, I have erected this stone to commemorate the event, inscribing it to ensure it will never perish. The inscription reads:

    "Behold this crane, a creature of the embryonic form, recorded by Floating Mountain. It is a testament to past events, and I have inscribed this for you. I wish to remain silent, but you shall preserve its spirit. The Thunder Gate has left behind its drum, and the form of the crane remains as a monument. The meaning is only vaguely understood, and the events are faintly remembered. Where will you go? The transformation is peaceful; behind, the great river surges, and before, the gates are firmly closed. On the right, the passage through Jingmen, the descent to Huating, and the gathering of true companions. I bury you here and inscribe this for you. The outer immortal official of Danyang, the true governor of Jiangyin. This inscription is carved on a stubborn stone at the bottom of Jiaoshan in Zhenjiang. Only when the tide recedes can it be fully seen. It is said to be the work of Wang Xizhi of the Jin dynasty, but according to the later commentary of Huang Rui of the Song dynasty, it was written by Tao Yuanming. This is likely the case. Today, the style and technique of the writing are distinct."

    303

    陶景景自号华阳居今号真逸者岂其别号与又其着真诰但云己卯嵗而不着年名其他书亦尔今此铭壬辰嵗甲午嵗亦不书年名此又可证云壬辰嵗梁天监十一年也甲午者十三年也按居天监七年东防海岳权驻防稽永嘉十一年乙未嵗始还茅山其弟子周子良仙去为之作传即十一年十三年正在华阳矣后又有题丹阳尉江隂宰数字当是效陶书故题扵石侧也王逸少以晋恵帝大安二年癸亥嵗年五十九至穆帝升平五年辛酉嵗卒则成帝咸和九年甲午嵗逸少方年二十三至永和七年辛亥嵗年三

    Explanation

    303

    Tao Hongjing, who gave himself the title "Huayang," is now known as "True Recluse." Is "Huayang" indeed his alias? In his work True Scriptures, he mentions only the year "Ji Mao" (己卯) without specifying the year name, and this is also the case in his other writings. In this inscription, the years "Ren Chen" (壬辰) and "Jia Wu" (甲午) are also not recorded, which can be used as evidence that "Ren Chen" refers to the eleventh year of Emperor Wu's reign (梁天监十一年, 512 CE), and "Jia Wu" refers to the thirteenth year (梁天监十三年, 514 CE). According to the record, during the seventh year of Emperor Wu's reign (天监七年, 508 CE), Tao Hongjing temporarily stationed himself in the eastern defense of the sea and mountains in Shaoxing (防稽). In the eleventh year of the Yongjia era (永嘉十一年, 307 CE), he returned to Mount Mao (茅山) in the year "Yi Wei" (乙未, 305 CE). His disciple Zhou Ziliang ascended to immortality and wrote a biography of him, which was completed in the eleventh and thirteenth years, when Tao Hongjing was residing in Huayang. Later, there are inscriptions mentioning the positions of "Danyang Magistrate" (丹阳尉) and "Jiangyin Prefect" (江阴宰), which were likely modeled after Tao Hongjing's writings and inscribed on the side of the stone. Wang Xizhi, also known as Wang Yishao, was born in the second year of the Dazheng era (大安二年, 303 CE) during the reign of Emperor Huai of Jin (晋惠帝), when he was 59 years old. He passed away in the fifth year of the Shengping era (升平五年, 361 CE) during the reign of Emperor Mu of Jin (晋穆帝). Therefore, in the ninth year of the Xianhe era (咸和九年, 334 CE) during the reign of Emperor Cheng of Jin (晋成帝), Wang Xizhi was 23 years old, and by the seventh year of the Yonghe era (永和七年, 351 CE), he was 39 years old.

    304

    十八始去防稽闲居不应二十三嵗已自称真逸也又未官扵朝及闲居时不在华阳以是考之决非王右军书也审矣欧阳文忠公以为不王右军法而顔鲁公又疑是頋况云道号同又疑王瓉皆非睿字长孺号云林子邵武人又董逌书跋第六卷载南阳张举子厚所记云瘗鹤铭今存扵焦山凡文章句读之可识及防画之仅存者百三十余言而所亡失几五十字计其完书盖九行行之全者二十五字而首尾不预焉熙宁三年春余索其逸遗扵焦山之隂偶得十二字扵乱石间石甚迫隘偃卧其下然后可读故昔人未之见

    Explanation

    304

    At the age of eighteen, he left Fanyi and lived in seclusion without taking office. By the age of twenty-three, he already called himself a true recluse. Moreover, he never held an official position in the imperial court, and during his time in seclusion, he was not in Huayang. Based on this, it is certainly not the writing of Wang Xizhi (the Right General), as confirmed. Ouyang Xiu (the Right Minister of Loyalty) believed that it does not follow Wang Xizhi's style but follows Yan Zhenqing's. He also suspected it was by Guan Kang, as their Daoist titles were the same, and he suspected it was by Wang Zan, as their names and titles were different. Dong You's postscript in the sixth volume records that Zhang Ju of Nanyang said that the 'Yiheming' (Burial Inscription for a Crane) now exists on Jiaoshan. Out of all the words that can be recognized, there are over one hundred thirty characters, and about fifty characters are missing. The complete text is about nine lines, with each line having twenty-five characters, but the beginning and end are not included. In the spring of Xining Year Three, I searched for the lost parts on the north side of Jiaoshan and accidentally found twelve characters among the rocks. The stone was very narrow and tight, and I had to lie down to read it, which is why previous people did not see it.

    305

    而世不其后又有丹阳外仙江隂真宰八字与华阳真逸上皇山樵为似是真侣之号今取其可考者次序之如此又董君自书其后云文忠集古録谓得六百字今以石校之为行凡十八为字二十五安得字至六百疑书之误也余扵崖上又得唐人诗诗在贞观中已列铭后则铭之刻非頋况时可知集古録岂又并诗系之耶君字彦逺号广川东平人又国朝郑枃衍极第二巻论瘗鹤铭而刘有定释云润州图经以为王羲之书或曰华阳真逸頋况号也蔡君谟曰瘗鹤文非逸少字东汉末多善书惟最盛至扵晋魏之分南北差

    Explanation

    305

    While the world does not widely transmit what follows after, there are also eight characters associated with the outer Daoist from DanYang, the true governor of JiangYin, and the true recluse of HuaYang, the imperial mountain hermit, which seem to be the names of true companions. Now, we arrange those that can be verified in this order. Also, Mr. Dong wrote after it saying that Collection of Ancient Records by Wen Zhong mentions obtaining six hundred characters. Now, comparing with the stone, it is arranged in lines totaling eighteen and characters twenty-five. How can there be as many as six hundred characters? There might be an error in the book. I found another Tang poem on the cliff. The poem was already listed after the inscription during the Zhenguan period. Thus, the inscription was not made by Guo Kuang during the Zhenguan period. Could Collection of Ancient Records have included the poem along with the inscription? Mr. Dong's courtesy name is YanYuan, and he is from GuangChuan and DongPing. Also, during the Song dynasty, Zheng En's Yanyi in the second volume discusses the Yihe Ming. And Liu Youding explained it as the Tujing of Runzhou considers it to be written by Wang Xizhi. Or it could be that "Hua Yang Zhen Yi" and "Guo Kuang" are aliases. Cai Junmo said that the Yihe Wen was not written by Yishao (Wang Xizhi). At the end of the Eastern Han dynasty, many were good at calligraphy, with clerical script being the most prominent. By the time of the division between Jin and Wei, the styles differed between north and south.

    306

    异钟王楷法为世所尚元魏间尽习法自隋平陈中国多以楷相参瘗鹤文有楷笔当是隋代书曹士冕曰焦山瘗鹤铭笔法之妙为书家冠冕前辈慕其字而不知其人最后云林子以为华阳居为陶景及以句曲所刻居朱阳馆帖参校然后众疑然其鍳赏可谓精矣以余考之一本山樵下有书字真宰下有立石二字一本我尔铭作出扵上真尔其蔵灵作纪尔嵗辰张举本作丹阳外仙邵亢本作丹阳仙尉又有作丹阳外仙尉者且中间词句亦多先后不同尚俟拏舟过子手自模印以稽其得失之一二可也
    　　风入松

    Explanation

    306

    The style of Zhong and Wang's regular script was highly esteemed by the world. During the Yuan Wei period, everyone practiced the clerical script. Since the Sui Dynasty conquered Chen, China often mixed regular and clerical scripts. The Yì Hè Wén (Inscription for the Buried Crane) has both regular and clerical script, and it should be from the Sui Dynasty. Cao Shiman said that the Yì Hè Míng (Inscription for the Buried Crane) on Jiao Mountain is a masterpiece of calligraphy, the crown of calligraphers. Previous generations admired his writing but did not know the person. Finally, Yun Linzi believed that Huá Yáng Dù Jū was by Tao Hongjing, and he also compared it with the Jù Qū (Crouching Dragon) inscriptions and the Zhū Yáng Guǎn (Purple Yang Pavilion) collection. After careful examination, the doubts were resolved, and his appreciation can be considered very refined. Upon further examination, one version had "Shān Qiáo" (Mountain Hut) with the character "書" (shū, meaning "writing") below it, and "Zhēn Zǎi" (True Master) with the characters "立石" (lì shí, meaning "erected stone") below it. Another version had "Wǒ Chuán" (I Transmit) and "Míng Chū" (Name Begins) above it, with "Zhēn Yě" (True Indeed) and "Cáng Líng" (Hide Spirit) below it, and "Jì Yě" (Record Also) and "Suì Chén" (Year and Month) below that. Zhang Ju's version had "Dān Yáng Wài Xiān" (Dan Yang Outer Immortal), while Shào Kàng's version had "Dān Yáng Xiān Wèi" (Dan Yang Immortal Official). There were also versions that read "Dān Yáng Wài Xiān Wèi" (Dan Yang Outer Immortal Official). Moreover, the wording and order of the sentences varied in different versions. It is still uncertain, and further study is needed to determine the exact differences and merits of each version.

    307

    吾乡柯敬仲先生【九思】际遇文宗起家为奎章阁鍳书博士以避言路居吴下时虞邵庵先生在馆阁赋风入松长短句寄博士云画堂红袖倚清酣华发不胜簮几回晚直金銮殿东风软花里停骖书诏许宫烛香罗初翦朝衫御沟氷泮水挼蓝飞燕又呢喃重重帘幕寒犹在凭谁寄锦字泥缄报道先生归也杏花春雨江南词翰兼美一时争相刻而此曲遂徧满海内矣翦一作试
    　　四卦
    　　睦人邵同先生【桂子】尝作忍黙恕退四卦掲之坐隅真得保身慎言絜矩知止之道者矣其忍卦曰忍亨初难终吉利君子贞不利小丈夫曰忍刚发乎内柔制乎外故亨初若甚难乃终有吉惟君子为能动心忍性不利小丈夫其中浅也象曰刃在心上忍君子以含容成徳初一小不忍则乱大谋象曰小不克忍成大乱也次二必有忍其乃有济象曰能忍于中事克济也次三一朝之忿亡其身以及其亲象曰一朝之忿至易忍也亡身及亲祸孰大焉次四出于跨下以成汉功韩信以之象曰跨下之辱小辱也成汉之功大功也次五张公艺九世同居书一忍字以对

    Explanation

    307

    My translation:

    Mr. Ke Jingzhong of my hometown, known as Jiu Si, met the emperor and rose to become a Doctor of the Library of the Imperial Academy. To avoid the path of criticism, he lived in Wu. At that time, Mr. Yu Shaoyan, who was in the imperial academy, composed a "Wind Enters the Pine" short and long sentence poem and sent it to the Doctor, saying: "In the painted hall, a red sleeve leans in pleasant intoxication, with white hair too thin to hold a hairpin. Several times staying late in the Golden Phoenix Hall, the soft east wind stops the carriage in the midst of flowers. The imperial decree allows the transmission of the palace candle, the fragrant silk just cut, the official robe in the imperial canal where the ice melts and the water is blue, and the flying swallows whisper. The heavy curtains still hold the cold, who will send the embroidered letter sealed with mud, reporting that the master has returned? Apricot blossoms and spring rain in Jiangnan." Both poetry and writing are beautiful, and at the time, they competed to transmit and engrave it, thus spreading throughout the land. "翦" is sometimes read as "试."

    Four Hexagrams
    Mr. Shao Xuantong of Muzhou, known as Gui Zi, once composed the hexagrams of endurance, silence, forgiveness, and retreat, hanging them in the corner of his seat. Truly, he understood the way of preserving one's body, speaking cautiously, and knowing when to stop. His hexagram of endurance says: "Endurance brings good fortune. It is difficult at the beginning but ultimately favorable. The gentleman is steadfast, while the petty man is not. The line says: 'Endurance brings good fortune. It is difficult at the beginning but ultimately favorable. Only the gentleman can cultivate his heart and endure, while the petty man cannot. His heart is shallow.' The image says: 'The knife is on the heart, but the gentleman endures. The gentleman cultivates his heart and achieves virtue. At the beginning, if one cannot endure, one will cause great disorder. The line says: 'If one cannot endure, one will cause great disorder.' The second line says: 'One must endure, and only then will one succeed.' The line says: 'One must endure, and only then will one succeed.' The third line says: 'A moment of anger can destroy one's body and even harm one's relatives.' The line says: 'A moment of anger is easy to endure, but destroying one's body and harming one's relatives is a great calamity.' The fourth line says: 'Emerging from beneath the horse's belly to achieve Han's success, Han Xin did so.' The line says: 'The humiliation of being beneath the horse's belly is a minor humiliation, but achieving Han's success is a great achievement.' The fifth line says: 'Zhang Gongyi, who lived with his nine generations in the same house, wrote the character "endurance" as his answer.'

    308

    于天子象曰同居之义忍克致也积而九世有容徳也上六血气方刚戒之在鬬象曰方刚之气忍则灭也形而为鬬自求祸也其黙卦曰黙无咎可贞不利有所言曰黙不言也乱之所生也则言语以为阶是以君子慎宻而不出故无咎黙以自守其道可贞也不利有所言尚口乃穷也象曰口尚曰黙君子以去辨养静初一守口如瓶终吉象曰守口如瓶谨所出也其初能黙终则吉也次二多言不如守中象曰言不如黙得中道也次三驷不及舌有悔象曰驷不及舌滕口说也一言之失悔何追也次四无以利口乱厥官卿士戒之象曰位髙而言轻亦可戒也次五圣人之敎

    Explanation

    308

    The image of the Son of Heaven says: The principle of cohabitation, enduring and overcoming leads to success. Accumulated over nine generations, it possesses a tolerant virtue. At the uppermost level, when one's blood and qi are strong, one must be cautious of conflict. The image says: The strong and vigorous qi, if endured, will be extinguished. If it takes physical form as a fight, it will invite disaster. The hexagram of silence says: Silence brings no blame, it is favorable to be steadfast, and it is not advantageous to speak. The image says: Silence, not speaking, is where chaos arises. Words serve as a ladder. Therefore, a gentleman must be cautious and do not speak out, so there is no blame. Silence protects one's way, it is favorable to be steadfast. It is not advantageous to speak. To value speech is to be in poverty. The image says: To value speech is to be in poverty. A gentleman avoids argument and cultivates tranquility. At the beginning, one should keep one's mouth shut like a bottle, and the outcome will be favorable. The image says: To keep one's mouth shut like a bottle is to be cautious in what one says. At the start, one can be silent, and in the end, one will be favorable. The second line says: Much speaking is not as good as maintaining the middle way. The image says: Speaking is not as good as silence, for it leads to the middle way. The third line says: Four horses cannot catch up to a tongue, and there will be regret. The image says: Four horses cannot catch up to a tongue, for rash speech leads to regret. One word spoken wrongly, how can one regret it? The fourth line says: Do not use flattering words to confuse one's office. The image says: One's position is high, but words are light, and this too can be a warning. The fifth line says: The teaching of the sage.

    309

    不言而信象曰不言而信渊黙之化也上六君子之道或黙或语象曰时然后言黙不可长也其恕卦曰恕有孚终吉曰恕之为道善推其所为而已以己之心合人之心己所不欲勿施扵人故有孚能以一言终身而行之其吉可知矣象曰如心恕君子以明好恶同物我初一强恕而行求仁莫近焉象曰强而行之恕之始也行而不已违道不逺也次二君子有絜矩之道象曰絜矩之道恕也次三好人之所恶恶人之所好是谓拂人之性菑必逮夫身象曰拂人从欲身之菑也次四己欲立而立人己欲逹而逹人象曰立而逹恕以从人也次五圣人与众同欲象曰与众同欲圣

    Explanation

    309

    In all things, to be credible without speaking is the transformation of profound silence. The way of a gentleman is sometimes silent, sometimes speaking. At the right time, speak; silence should not be prolonged. The practice of forgiveness leads to ultimate good fortune. To act in a way that is good and to extend that goodness to others, it is to act in accordance with one's heart in forgiveness. To forcefully practice forgiveness and seek benevolence, nothing is closer. The gentleman has the way of the square and compass. To like what others dislike and to dislike what others like is to go against human nature, and disaster will inevitably befall oneself. To stand oneself and help others stand, to reach oneself and help others reach, it is to act in a way that aligns with human nature. The sage shares the same desires as the people. To share the same desires as the people is sage-like.

    310

    人之恕也上六责己重以周待人轻以约象曰待人之法可用恕也责己之道不可自恕也其退卦曰退勿用有攸往曰退止也勿用有攸往知止也日中则退而昃月盈则退而四时之运成功者退而况扵人乎退之时义大矣哉象曰艮止其所退君子以晦蔵扵宻初一退无咎象曰其进未锐义无咎也次二难进易退象曰难进易退可事君也次三兼人凶象曰兼人之凶勇不知退也次四见可而进知难而退象曰知难而退终无尤也次五终日如愚以退为进顔子以之象曰顔子之退进不可御也上六蝜蝂升髙踬而不悔象曰蝜蝂升髙其道穷也踬而不悔亦可戒也
    　　防録
    　　文章用事填塞故实旧谓之防録又谓之堆垜死尸见江氏苑

    Explanation

    310

    Translation

    The concept of forgiveness or tolerance is embodied in the saying, "One should be strict with oneself and thorough, but lenient with others and restrained." The I Ching states, "When retreating is not beneficial, one should not advance." It further explains, "Retreat is a stop, and one should not advance when one knows to stop." The passage from the I Ching suggests that a wise person should retreat and hide in secrecy. The metaphor of "a crab climbing high and stumbling but not regretting" illustrates a situation where one rises to a high position but fails and does not regret it. This can serve as a warning.

    In the realm of writing, filling the text with well-known facts and examples is referred to as "a collection of stories" or "a pile of dead bodies," as mentioned in the anthology by Jiang.

    311

    房中术
    　　今人以邪僻不经之术如运气逆流采战之曰房中术按史周有房中乐汉书礼乐志髙祖时有房中词乐唐山夫人所作武帝时有房中歌又曰房中者情性之极至道之际是以圣王制外乐以禁内情而为之节文乐而有节则和平夀考及迷者弗頋以生疾而殒性命礼记曾子问众主人卿大夫士房中皆哭注房中妇人也然房中之谓岂取此一书与
    　　妇女曰娘
    　　娘子俗书也古无之当作娘按说文频扰也肥大也从女襄声女良切其义如此今乃通为妇女之称故子谓母曰娘而世谓穏婆曰老娘女巫曰师娘都下及江南谓男觋亦曰师娘娼妇曰花娘古又谓曰草娘苖人谓妻曰夫娘南方谓妇人之无行者亦曰夫娘谓妇人之卑贱者曰某娘曰几娘鄙之曰婆娘考之风俗通汉何敞为苏珠娘按诛亭长龚夀隋书韦世康为绛州刺史与子弟书云况娘春秋已髙温凊宜奉敎坊记北齐时丈夫着妇人衣行歌傍人齐和云謡娘南史梁元徐妃与帝左右暨季江私通季江曰徐娘虽老

    311

    Sexual Techniques
    Nowadays, people refer to improper and unconventional techniques such as those involving the manipulation of qi and reverse flow, like the practices of "yin-yang cultivation" and "qi collection," as "sexual techniques." According to historical records, during the Zhou dynasty, there was "sexual music," and the Han Shu (Book of Han) in the Rites and Music section mentions that during the reign of Emperor Gaozu, there were songs and music about sexual practices composed by Lady Tangshan. During the reign of Emperor Wu, there were also songs about sexual practices. It is said that "sexual techniques" represent the highest level of emotional and physical expression, a state where the ultimate truth is revealed. Therefore, wise rulers use external pleasures to control internal desires and establish rules and norms. When pleasure is enjoyed in moderation, it leads to peace and longevity. However, those who indulge recklessly may suffer illness and even death. In the Rites of Zhou, it is recorded that during a question posed by Zengzi, all the officials, including the master of ceremonies, the ministers, and the scholars, were weeping in the "room." A note explains that the term "room" refers to women. Thus, the term "room" has become a general term for women. Women are often referred to as "mother" by their children, and in some regions, an elderly woman is called "old mother," a female shaman is called "shaman mother," and in some areas, male shamans are also called "shaman mother." Prostitutes are often referred to as "flower mother" or "grass mother." In some ethnic groups, a wife is called "husband mother," and in southern regions, a woman of poor character is also called "husband mother." Low-status women are referred to as "certain mother" or "several mothers," and a derogatory term for a woman is "old mother." According to the Fengsu Tong (General Records of Customs), during the Han dynasty, He Chang was a ghost known as "Su Zhun Mother." In the Sui Shu (Book of Sui), Wei Shikang, while serving as the governor of Jiangzhou, wrote to his family, mentioning that an elderly woman should be treated with respect. In the Jiaofang Ji (Record of the Imperial Music Bureau), during the Northern Qi dynasty, a man dressed as a woman and sang, and others joined in singing, calling her "Mother of Song." In the Nan Shi (Book of Southern Dynasties), it is recorded that during the reign of Emperor Yuan of Liang, Lady Xu, the wife of Emperor Wu, had an affair with a man named Ji Jiang, who remarked, "Lady Xu may be old, but she still..."

    312

    尚犹多情又梁临川王宏侵魏魏遗以巾帼歌曰不畏萧娘与吴姥但畏合肥有韦虎谓韦睿吕僧珍也大业拾遗隋炀帝宫婢曰雅娘唐史张旭草书见公孙大娘舞劒器而通神又武承嗣闻乔知之婢娘美夺取之杜工部诗耶娘妻子走相送又黄四娘家花满蹊白乐天诗吴娘暮雨萧萧曲韦应物诗春风一曲杜韦娘栁子厚下殇女墓砖记始名和娘乐府杂録张红红唱歌丐于市韦青纳为姬敬宗召入宫号记曲娘又望江南曲始自朱崖李太尉镇浙西日为姬谢秋娘所制明皇杂録呼白鹦鹉为雪衣娘甘泽謡武三思晚获一妓曰

    Explanation

    312

    Still emotionally involved, the Prince of Linchuan, Wang Hong, of the Liang dynasty invaded Wei. Wei sent him a woman's headgear, and sang, "I do not fear Xiao's daughter or Wu's old woman, but I fear the Wei Tiger in Hefei," referring to Wei Rui and Lü Sengzhen. In the "Great Cause" and "Collected Errors," the palace maid of Emperor Yang of Sui was named Ya. In the Tang history, Zhang Xu's cursive script was inspired by Gong Sun's daughter's sword dance, becoming divine. Wu Chengsi heard that Qiao Zhizhi's maid, Xi'er, was beautiful and took her. In Du Fu's poem, "Yen's mother and wife run to see him off." At Huang Si'er's house, the path is full of flowers. In Bai Juyi's poem, "Wu's daughter sings a sad song in the evening rain." In Wei Yingwu's poem, "a spring song about Du and Wei's daughter." In Liu Zihou's tombstone inscription, the name 'He' was first used. In the Miscellaneous Records of Music, Zhang Honghong sang for alms in the market, Wei Qing took her as a concubine, and Emperor Jingzong summoned her to the palace, calling her 'The Song Record Girl.' The 'Song of the Southern Horizon' was first composed by Xie Qiuniang, a concubine of Li Taifu, the Governor of Zhe West. In the Miscellaneous Records of Emperor Ming Huang, he called the white parrot 'Snow-Cloak Girl.' In the Sweet Dew Records, Wu Sansi obtained a courtesan late in life, whose name is not given.

    313

    绮娘狄仁杰至遂逃壁隙中曰我天上花月之妖也樊川集杜秋娘年十五为李锜妾锜败入宫后坐谴归故里又窦桂娘父良建中初为汴州户曹掾李希烈破汴州取桂娘去李贺集贺撰申胡子觱篥歌成翔客喜擎觞起立命花娘出幙徘徊拜客刘賔客集泰娘本韦尚书家主讴者河东记唐进士叚何卧病遇妊娘留诗而愈竒崔氏鸎鸎婢曰红娘霍小玉长安中有媒氏鲍十二娘薛苍驸马青衣也余媚娘叙録陆希声娶余媚娘媚娘约媒曰陆郎中若必得儿侍巾栉须立誓不置侧室及女奴图经神谓之马头娘杜阳杂编南海

    Explanation

    313

    Qiniang, upon arriving at the wall, escaped through a crack and said, "I am a spirit of flowers and moon from heaven." In the Fan Chuan collection, Du Qiunang, at fifteen, was the concubine of Li Qi. After Li Qi's defeat, she entered the palace and was later exiled back to her hometown. Guiniang, whose father was Li Liang, the magistrate of Kaifeng, was captured by Li Xieli, who took her away. In the Li He collection, Li He wrote the Shen Hu Zi Bi Li Song, and when it was completed, the guests were delighted and stood up, holding their cups, and asked Huaniang to come out of the curtain, she hesitated and bowed to the guests. In the Liu Bingshi collection, Tai Niang was originally the main singer in the household of Wei Shangshu. In the He Dong Ji collection, Tang Dynasty scholar Duan He, while ill, met Renniang and left a poem, which helped him recover. In the Chuan Qi collection, Cui's maid, Ying Ying, was called Hongniang. In the Huo Xiaoyu collection, in Chang'an, there was a matchmaker named Bao Shier Niang. In the Tu Jing collection, the deity is called Mare Lady. In the Du Yang Za Bian collection, the South China Sea.

    314

    贡竒女卢媚娘工巧无比丽情集陈敏兄妾越娘貌美兄死遂与欵狎续齐谐记齐颖寓山隂夜见前宰妾万文娘墨荘漫録李后主令宫嫔窅娘以帛绕脚令纎小右畧举一二不能悉载是则今之云云皆有所本然都下自庶人妻以及大官之国夫人皆曰娘子未尝有称夫人郡君等封赠者载考之史隋柴绍妻李氏起兵应李渊与绍各置幕府号娘子军唐平阳公主兵与秦王定京师号娘子军花蕋夫人宫词诸院各分娘子位韩昌黎有祭周氏二十娘子文以此推之古之公主宫妃已与民间共称娘子则今之不分尊卑亦自有来矣

    Explanation

    314

    A Woman of Extraordinary Skill

    During the time of the tribute, there was a woman named Lu Meiniang, whose skillfulness was unparalleled. In the collection Li Qing Ji, it is recorded that Chen Min's elder brother's concubine, Yue娘, was beautiful, and after the elder brother's death, she became intimate with him. In Xu Qi Xie Ji, it is told that Qi Ying, residing in Shanyin, saw the former governor's concubine Wan Wen娘 at night. In Mo Zhuang Man Lu, it is recorded that Li Yu, the last emperor of the Southern Tang dynasty, ordered the palace concubine Yao娘 to tie silk around her feet to make them slender. Briefly mentioning a few of these examples, we cannot fully record them all; thus, all the current sayings have their origins. However, in the capital, from commoner wives to the wives of high-ranking officials, all are called "娘子." Women have never been given titles such as ladies or county princesses. According to historical records, during the Sui dynasty, Li Shi, wife of Chai Shao, rose up to support Li Yuan and each set up their own command, being called the "娘子 Army." During the Tang dynasty, Princess Pingyang led troops with Prince Qin to pacify the capital, also being called the "娘子 Army." In Hua Sui Fu Ren Gong Ci, it is recorded that in each court, she was given the position of "娘子." Han Yu wrote a memorial text for Zhou's Twenty "娘子," from which we can infer that in ancient times, princesses and palace consorts were already called "娘子" along with common people. Thus, today's usage of "娘子" without distinguishing rank also has its origins.

    315

    古刻
    　　至正壬辰春城平江扵古城基内掘得一碑其文云三十六十八子寅卯年至辰巳合张翼同为利不在常不在切湏欵欵细思量且卜水莫问米浮图倒地莫扶起修古岸重开河军民拍手笑呵呵日出屋东头鲤鱼山上逰星従月里过防在午年头右不晓所言何事姑识之或者以为三十六四九也张翼巳午之交也今张太尉第行九四而同首乱者适十八人也岂其然与
    　　上头入月
    　　今世女子之笄曰上头而倡家处女初得荐寝扵人亦曰上头花蕊夫人宫词年初十五最风流新赐云鬟使上头又天癸曰月事黄帝内经女子二七而天癸至月事以时下又曰女子不月史记济北王侍者韩女病月事不下诊其肾脉啬而不属故曰月不下又程姬有所避不愿进注天子诸侯羣妾以次进御有月事者止不御更不口说故以丹注面目的的为识令女史见之王察神女赋施的的即上所云也然入月二字尤新王建宫词宻奏君王知入月唤人相伴洗裙裾

    Explanation

    315

    Translation

    Ancient Stele
    In the spring of the Renchen year during the Zhizheng era, in the city of Pingjiang, a stele was unearthed from the foundation of an ancient city. Its inscription reads: "Thirty-six and eighteen, the years of the Tiger and Rabbit, to the Dragon and Snake, Zhang Yi together for profit. Not in the usual, not in the Yang, must carefully and thoughtfully consider. Divine water, do not ask about rice. The pagoda has fallen, do not lift it up. Repair the ancient embankment, reopen the river. The military and civilians clap and laugh happily. The sun rises from the east side of the house. The carp fish swims on the mountain. The star passes through the moon. Prevent in the year of the Horse." I do not know what this means, so I will record it. Some say that thirty-six and four nine refer to Zhang Yi, who is at the intersection of the years of the Horse and Goat. Today, Zhang Taigong, who is the ninth of the four, and the first to cause chaos, happened to be eighteen people. Could it be so?

    On Top, Entering the Month
    In this world, a woman's coming-of-age ceremony is called "shangtou." A courtesan's maiden, when she first sleeps with a man, is also called "shangtou." The Flower Lady's Palace Song says: "On the fifteenth day of the first month, she is the most charming. Newly granted cloud-like hairpins to wear 'shangtou.'" The term "tiankui" refers to menstruation. The Huangdi Neijing states that when a woman reaches fourteen, her menstruation begins, occurring at regular intervals. It also says that a woman who does not menstruate. The Records of the Grand Historian mentions that a lady-in-waiting of King Jibei had a condition where her menstruation did not come. Upon examination, her kidney pulse was thin and not connected, hence it was said she did not menstruate. The Book of Songs mentions that Cheng Ji had something to avoid and did not want to enter. The emperor and his nobles' concubines would take turns serving, and if a woman had her period, she would not serve, and would not speak about it, so they would mark her face with red dots as a sign, so that the lady-in-waiting could see it. The king would examine the Lady of the Spirit poem, which says "the red dots," referring to the above. However, the term "entering the month" is particularly new. Wang Jian's Palace Song says: "Secretly report to the emperor about the 'entry into the month,' call someone to accompany in washing the skirts."

    316

    人腊
    　　至正乙巳春平江金国寳袖人腊出售余获一观其形长六寸许口耳目鼻与人无异亦有髭头发披至臋下须髪皆黄色间有白髪一根徧身黄毛长二分许脐下隂物乃男子也相云至元间世皇受外国贡献以赐国公阿尔尼格者无几何时即死因剖开背后剜去肠臓实以他物仍缝合烘干故至今无恙按汉武故事东郡送一短人长七寸名巨灵神异经西海有一鹤国人长七寸山海经有小人国名靖人诗含神雾东北极有人长九寸殆为此小人也靖或作竫音同然古尺短今六寸比之周尺将九寸矣则所腊者岂其人与
    　　张翰林诗

    Explanation

    316

    A Mummified Human

    In the spring of the year 1355, during the reign of the Yuan Dynasty, a man from Pingjiang named Jin Guobao carried a mummified human body for sale. I managed to see one. Its form was about six inches long, with a mouth, ears, eyes, and nose no different from those of a human. It also had facial hair, and its hair hung down to its knees. All the hair was yellow, with one white hair in between, and yellow hair covered the entire body, about two inches long. The genitalia below the navel were those of a man. According to legend, during the reign of the Yuan Dynasty, the emperor received a gift from a foreign country and gave it to a nobleman named Alnig. Not long after, he died. The body was then opened, the intestines removed, and replaced with other substances, which were then sewn up and dried. Thus, it remains intact to this day.

    According to the "Records of Emperor Wu of Han," a short person seven inches tall was sent from the eastern region and named Juling. The "Book of Miraculous Beings" mentions that in the western sea, there is a land where people are seven inches tall. The "Classic of Mountains and Seas" describes a land of small people called Jingren. The "Book of Supernatural Beings" mentions that in the far northeast, there are people nine inches tall, likely these are the small people. Jing can also be written as Jing, with the same pronunciation. However, ancient units of measurement were shorter; six inches in ancient times is about nine inches in modern times. Then, the mummified body is likely one of these people.

    Poem by Zhang, the Scholar-Official.

    317

    天子临轩授钺频东南无地不红巾铁衣逺道三军老白骨中原万新义士精灵虹贯日仙家谈笑海尘都将两眼凄凉泪哭尽平生几故人此至正辛丑间张蜕庵承防翥在都下寄浙省周玉坡参政【伯琦】诗也夫翰苑词臣而寓言如此则感时之意从可知矣

    　　辍耕録巻十四
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録巻十五　　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰淳化阁帖
    　　淳化阁帖非精扵鍳赏者莫能辨其真伪非博扵讨论者不可得其源流第六巻中尝记祖石刻之说今复究研大略于稽古之书质正是否扵好事之人用赘于此云宋太宗留意翰墨淳化中出御府所蔵命侍书王着临榻以枣木镂刻厘为一十巻扵每卷末题云淳化三年壬辰嵗十一月六日奉圣防模勒上石至仁宗又诏僧希白刻石于秘阁前有目録卷后无题世以为二王府帖者非也盖元祐中亲贤宅从禁中借板墨百本但用潘谷墨光辉有余而不甚黟黑又多木横裂纹时有皴皵失字处亲贤宅魏王所居魏王二王也又有髙宗绍兴中国子监本

    Explanation

    317

    The Emperor personally bestowed the military command, and the southeastern regions were engulfed in the Red Turban Rebellion. The iron-clad soldiers, far from home, grew old, and the central plains were filled with the bones of the fallen and the newly risen ghosts. The spirits of the righteous soldiers, like a rainbow piercing the sun, were said to be present. Immortals, with their divine ease, could stir the sea, and the generals wept with sorrow, having cried for all their old comrades. This was during the year 1351, when Zhang Tuian, a former official in the capital, wrote a poem and sent it to Zhou Yupo, a provincial official in Zhejiang. Although officials in the imperial literary academy often expressed their feelings through allegory, the sentiment of the times was evident in this poem.

    The Chunhua Ge Tie (淳化閣帖) is a famous collection of calligraphy compiled during the Song Dynasty. Only those skilled in appreciation can distinguish its authenticity, and only those well-versed in discussion can understand its origins and development. In the sixth volume, there is a mention of the stone carvings, and I have further studied this topic, comparing it with ancient texts to verify its accuracy. I will provide this information for those interested in such matters.

    During the reign of Emperor Taizong of Song, he took a keen interest in calligraphy and painting. In the year 993 (the third year of the Chunhua era, Renchen), he ordered the contents of the imperial treasury to be brought out, and commanded the imperial calligrapher Wang Zhe to copy them. The copies were then carved onto jujube wood and divided into ten volumes. At the end of each volume, it was inscribed: "In the third year of the Chunhua era, Renchen, the eleventh day of the eleventh month, by imperial decree, the calligraphy was carved onto stone." Later, during the reign of Emperor Renzong, the monk Xibai was ordered to carve the Chunhua Ge Tie onto stone in the Secretariat. The volumes had a table of contents, but no inscriptions at the end. Some mistakenly believed it to be a collection from the two princely houses, but this is incorrect. During the Yuanfu era, the residence of the imperial princes borrowed a hundred copies from the imperial palace, using only the high-quality ink made by Pan Gu. While the ink was bright, it was not as deep as usual, and the wood often had cracks, causing some characters to be lost. The residence of the Prince of Wei, who was also known as the Prince of Wei, had a version of the Chunhua Ge Tie produced by the Imperial Academy during the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Song.

    318

    其首尾与淳化略无少异当时御前拓者多用匮纸盖打金银箔者也自后碑工作蝉翼本且以厚纸覆板上然为银铤□痕以愚人但损剥非复拓本之遒劲矣初徽宗建中靖国间出内府续所书令刻石即今续法帖也大观中又奉防摹榻歴代真迹刻石于太清楼字行稍髙而先后之次与淳化则少异其间数帖多寡不同各卷末题云大观三年正月一日奉圣防摹勒上石此蔡京书也而以建中靖国续帖十巻易去嵗月名衔以为后帖又刻孙过庭书谱及贞观十七帖总为二十二卷谓之大观太清楼帖绛帖者尚书郎潘师旦以官帖摹刻于家为石本而

    Explanation

    318

    The beginning and end are almost identical to the Chunhua edition; at that time, those who made copies before the emperor often used poor-quality paper, which was used for making gold and silver foil. Later, the stele craftsmen made thin as a cicada's wing editions, and they covered the board with thick paper, but it was done to leave marks like silver ingots to deceive people. The wear and tear is no longer as vigorous as the original rubbing. Initially, during the Jianzhongjingguo period of Emperor Huizong, the books collected from the imperial treasury were ordered to be engraved on stone, which are now known as the Xu Fawei (Continued Collection of Calligraphy). During the Dazhong period, another order was given to copy and engrave the authentic works of历代 on stone at the Taqing Pavilion; the characters are slightly taller, and the order is somewhat different from the Chunhua edition. Some of the collections have more or fewer items, and at the end of each volume, it is inscribed that on the first day of the first month of the third year of Dazhong, the imperial order was given to copy and engrave the works on stone. This was written by Cai Jing. And the ten volumes of the Jianzhongjingguo Xu Fawei were replaced with the year and names of officials to be considered as the later collection. Additionally, the Shu Pu (Calligraphy Spectrum) by Sun Guoting and the Zhen Guan Shiqi Tie (Seventeen Works of Zhen Guan) were engraved, making a total of twenty-two volumes, known as the Dazhong Taqing Pavilion Fawei. The Jiang Tie was made by Pan Shidan, a secretary of the imperial secretariat, by copying official rubbings at home and engraving them on stone for transmission.

    319

    冩字多譌舛世称为潘驸马帖凡二十卷其次序卷帖虽与淳化官帖不同而实则祖之特有所增益耳单炳文曰淳化官本法帖今不复多见其次绛帖最佳而旧本亦已艰得尝以数本较之字画多不侔炜家蔵旧本比之今本第九巻内今本多误笔法且俗曹士冕曰帖总二十卷元无字号及断眼数目单炳文曹士冕各有模刻本世潘氏析居法帖分而为二其后绛州公库乃得其一扵是补刻余帖名东库本第九卷之舛误盖始于此且逐卷逐叚各分字号以日月光天徳等二十字为次第后避金主亮讳但庾亮帖内亮字皆去右边转笔谓之亮字不全本又有

    Explanation

    319

    Writing has many errors and mistakes, known as the Pan Fu Collection, a total of twenty volumes. Although the order and content of the volumes differ from the Chunhua Official Collection, they are fundamentally derived from it, but with some additional content. Shan Bingwen says, "The Chunhua Official Collection is no longer widely available. The Jiàng Collection is the best alternative, and the old versions are also hard to obtain. I have compared several versions, and the characters and strokes are often different. The old version in Wei's family collection, compared to the current version, has many errors in the ninth volume." And Cao Shimian says, "The collection consists of twenty volumes without page numbers or section markers. Both Shan Bingwen and Cao Shimian have their own engraved copies, and the Pan Fu Collection has been divided into two parts. Later, the public treasury of Jizhou obtained one part and supplemented the rest, naming it the Dongku Version. The errors in the ninth volume began here, and each volume and section is numbered using the characters 日 (sun), 月 (moon), 光 (light), 天 (heaven), 德 (virtue), etc., in a specific order. Later, to avoid offending the name of Jin Emperor Liang, all 'liang' characters in Yu Liang's calligraphy were modified by removing the right stroke, known as the 'incomplete liang' version."

    320

    新绛本北方别本武冈新旧本福清乌镇彭州资州本木本前十巻等皆绛帖之别也潭帖者庆歴中刘丞相帅潭日以淳化官帖命慧照大师希白模刻于石置之郡斋增入伤寒十七日王蒙顔真卿诸帖而字行颇髙与淳化阁本差不同逐卷有慧照大师希白重模字而嵗月各异中间缪处甚多朱文公讥其极为可笑者是也潭帖之别则有刘丞相私第本长沙碑匠新刻本三山木本蜀本庐陵萧氏本等甚多戯鱼即临江帖也元祐间刘次荘以家蔵淳化阁帖十卷摹刻于戱鱼堂除去题而増文庆元中四川总领权安节又重摹扵利州黔江者黔人秦世

    Explanation

    320

    The Xinning Stone Rubbings have a northern variant, as well as new and old versions of the Wugang Stone Rubbings, the Fuzhou Stone Rubbings, the Wuzhen Stone Rubbings, the Pengzhou Stone Rubbings, and the Zizhou Stone Rubbings, all of which are wooden block prints. The first ten volumes and others are all variants of the Xinning Stone Rubbings. The Tantie, compiled during the Qingli period by Liu Chengxiang, the Chancellor, who was in charge of潭州, commissioned Master Huizhao Xi Bai to carve the Chunhua Official Rubbings onto stone and place it in the prefectural hall. Additional works such as Shanghan Shiqi Ri and Wang Meng were added, and the font size is larger than that of the Chunhua Official Rubbings. Each volume has the inscription "Master Huizhao Xi Bai re-carved" with different dates, and there are many errors. Zhu Xi criticized the Tantie as extremely laughable. Variants of the Tantie include private editions of Liu Chengxiang, new carvings by stonemasons in Changsha, wooden block prints from San Mountain, Sichuan editions, and editions from Luxian in Lu陵. The Xiyu Tie, also known as the Linjiang Tie, was copied by Liu Cizhuang during the Yuanyou period, who had the ten volumes of the Chunhua Official Rubbings in his house and carved them in the Xiyu Hall, removing the colophons and adding new texts. During the Qingyuan period, Quan Anjie, the military governor of Sichuan, had the Xiyu Tie recarved in Qianjiang, Lizhou, by the Qian person Qin Shi.

    321

    章扵长沙买石摹僧寳月古法帖十巻寳月慧照也谋舟载入黔中壁之黔江之绍圣院后题云长沙汤正臣重摹鼎帖板本校诸帖増益最多沣阳石刻散失仅存者右军数帖而已又有淳熙修内司本北方印成本乌镇张氏福清李氏本若此之大抵皆法帖一再之翻摹殊失笔意无足观者汪逵字季路衢州人官至端明殿学士建集古堂蔵竒书秘迹金石遗文二千巻着淳化阁帖辨记共十卷极为详备末云其本乃木刻计一百八十四版二千二百八十七行其逐叚以一二三四刻扵旁或刻人名或有银铤印痕则是木裂其墨乃李廷珪墨黒甚如漆其字精明

    Explanation

    321

    In Changsha, one purchased stones to copy the ancient calligraphy of the monk Bao Yue, consisting of ten scrolls. Bao Yue was also known as Huizhao. Plans were made to transport the stones by boat to Qianzhong and place them in the wall of the Shao Sheng Temple on the Qian River. Afterward, the inscription read: "Changsha's Tang Zhengchen heavily revised the original calligraphy, adding the most improvements to the various versions." Only a few of Wang Xizhi's calligraphy remain in Fenyang. There are also versions from the Northern Printing Office, the Wuzhen Zhang family, and the Fuqing Li family, among others. Most of these are mere reproductions and lack the original style, making them not worth much. Wang Kui, whose courtesy name was Jilu and who was from Quzhou, served as a scholar at the Daming Palace and built a collection hall to gather rare books, secret traces, and stone inscriptions, totaling two thousand volumes. He wrote a ten-volume "Chunhua Ge Tie" (Chunhua Pavilion Calligraphy) that is extremely detailed. It concludes with the statement: "The original was carved in wood, consisting of one hundred and eighty-four pages and two thousand two hundred eighty-seven lines. Each section is numbered one, two, three, four, and so on, with some sections having names or silver marks, indicating that the wood has split. The ink used is Li Tinggui's ink, which is as black as lacquer, and the characters are clear and refined."

    322

    而丰比诸刻为肥刘潜夫曰近人多不识阁帖某家寳蔵本皆非真真者字极丰穰有神采如潭绛则太痩临江则太媚又用李廷珪墨印造余始得汪端明所记阁帖行数恨无真帖防校晚使江左用二千楮致一本尤伯晦见之曰寳物也夫真帖可辨者有数条墨色一也他本刋巻数在上版数在下惟此本巻数版数字皆相聨属二也他本行数字比帖字小而瘦此本行数字比帖中字皆大而浓三也余所得江左本每版皆全纸无接黏处一部十卷无一版不与端明所记合乃知昔人装背之际宁使每版行数或多或寡而不肯剪截凑合者欲旧帖之真面目四也

    Explanation

    322

    The version of the "Lan亭序" (Lanting Xu) that I obtained is richer and fuller compared to other versions. Liu Qianfu remarked that many people today do not recognize the authenticity of the "Lan亭序" collections. Most of the versions in private collections are not genuine; the authentic ones have characters that are extremely full and rich, with a divine quality. For example, the version known as "Tan Jiang" is too thin, and the version known as "Lin Jiang" is too charming. Moreover, this version was printed using ink made by Li Tinggui. I first obtained a record of the number of lines in the "Lan亭序" from Wang Duanming, and I regretted not having an authentic version to compare it with. Later, I sent two thousand sheets of paper to the south of the Yangtze River to obtain a copy of the text. You Bohui saw it and called it a treasure. There are several ways to distinguish an authentic "Lan亭序." The ink color is one distinguishing feature. In other versions, the number of volumes is listed above the number of pages, but in this version, the numbers of volumes and pages are connected. In other versions, the number of lines is small and the characters are thin, whereas in this version, the number of lines is large and the characters are thick. The version I obtained from the south of the Yangtze River has each page as a whole sheet with no splices. A set of ten volumes has no page that does not match what Wang Duanming recorded. This shows that the ancient collectors preferred to keep the true appearance of the old text, even if it meant having more or fewer lines per page, rather than cutting and pasting to fit a standard format.

    323

    幽圄
    　　太师丞相托克托之死盖副枢哈玛尔与其弟蘓蘓并詹事僧格实哩等挤陷之也哈玛尔辈得侍上帷幄而僧格在东宫为近侍故哈玛尔党僧格而私相誓曰若太师去位后我能作右相则左相必詹事矣既而入中书又虞僧格来其权不颛奏除宣政使而以弟苏苏为御史大夫僧格殊失望未几哈玛尔得罪杖死蘓蘓亦仰药死初僧格有侍从人亦曰僧格实哩服劳执事得僧格意僧格举充院宣使一日奄然长逝经日乃醒云方坐卧室榻上见二卒自外跃入导之往都城隍庙转发岳

    Explanation

    323

    The Death of Toghtogha in Secret Confinement

    The death of Grand Tutor and Chancellor Toghtogha was due to the scheming and framing by Vice Chancellor Halamay and his brother Su Su, along with Sengge, the Grand Tutor. Halamay and his associates were close to the emperor, while Sengge served as a close attendant to the crown prince in the Eastern Palace. Therefore, Halamay supported Sengge and they secretly vowed that if Toghtogha were removed from power, Halamay would become the Right Chancellor, and Sengge would become the Left Chancellor.

    After Toghtogha's removal, Halamay entered the Central Secretariat and feared that Sengge would come to power, thus he proposed removing Sengge from his position as Minister of Foreign Affairs and appointing Su Su as the Chief Censor. Sengge was greatly disappointed. Shortly thereafter, Halamay was convicted of a crime and died by being beaten, and Su Su also took poison and died.

    Initially, Sengge had a servant named Sengge, who served diligently and gained Sengge's favor. Sengge recommended him to be appointed as an assistant to the Minister of Foreign Affairs. One day, Sengge suddenly died, but after a day, he woke up and claimed that he had been sitting on his bed in his bedroom when two soldiers jumped in and led him to the city god temple in the capital, where they sent him to a mountain.

    324

    祠祠吏曰来矣可亟觧去旋又行入祠西北隅大林内有殿宇若王者居入拜殿下已仰视之则太师也太师曰我所摄者院使也于汝无预因俾左右引之观幽圄见哈玛尔兄弟括髪闗械頋僧格泣下及出太师谓曰汝可即归此非人间世也退而觉恍若一梦然明日同寅有来约往院使家僧格辞疾且曰君幸毋泄吾恐院使不久生矣众问其故告以详皆相頋惊愕曰昨日院使将上马以体少不安而入岂遽至此乎语未毕有报院使已暴卒近见浙西宪司经歴何伯大所说甚悉此特其略耳
    　　煑豆帖
    　　黄山谷煑豆帖云庭坚顿首失牛儿来终日惘然至今头昏眼痛虽取所喜者为之亦不能如意也以是不能修问辱手诲喜承日用轻安所湏诸方既无人可抄又意绪不佳嬾动耳煑黑豆法确豆一升挼莎极浄用贯众一斤细剉如骰子同豆斟酌水多少慢火煑豆香熟日干之翻覆令展尽余汁簸取黑豆去贯众空心日防五七粒食百草木枝叶皆有味可饱也世间不强学力行自致扵古人者不可不畜此方庭坚顿首翰礼秘校足下

    Explanation

    324

    The Temple Official said, "Come, you can be quickly released." Then, he again entered the northwest corner of the temple, where there was a large forest with a hall resembling the dwelling of a king. Upon entering and bowing before the hall, he looked up and saw the Grand Tutor. The Grand Tutor said, "The one I am overseeing is the Vice-Inspector. It has nothing to do with you." He then ordered those around to guide him to see the dark prison, where he saw the brothers Hamar with bound hair and shackles. The monk Gu wept. When they left, the Grand Tutor said, "You may return home immediately; this is not a human world." Upon waking, it seemed like a dream. The next day, a colleague came to invite him to visit the Vice-Inspector's house. The monk Gu declined due to illness and said, "Please do not reveal this; I fear the Vice-Inspector will not live long." The others asked the reason, and he told them in detail. All looked at each other in surprise, saying, "Yesterday, when the Vice-Inspector was about to mount his horse, he felt unwell and entered. How could he have suddenly passed away?" Before they finished speaking, there was a report that the Vice-Inspector had suddenly died. Recently, I heard from He Boda, who served as the Inspector of the Zhejiang Western Circuit, that he had a detailed account. This is just a summary.

    Cooking Beans Letter

    Huang Tingjian, respectfully bowing, said: The loss of the cowherd has left me in a daze all day. I am still in a state of confusion, with headaches and eye pain. Even if I try to do what I like, I cannot achieve satisfaction. Therefore, I cannot reply to your letter. I am pleased to hear that you are living a peaceful life. The various places where I need to go have no one to accompany me, and my mood is poor, so I am lazy to move. The method of cooking black beans: one sheng of black beans, rubbed and cleaned very thoroughly. Use one catty of Gancao (a type of herb), finely chopped like dice. With the beans, adjust the amount of water, cook them slowly over a low fire until they are fragrant and cooked, then dry them in the sun. Turn them over to spread out all the remaining juice. Winnow to separate the black beans from the Gancao, eat five or seven grains on an empty stomach each day. All branches and leaves of hundred kinds of grass and trees have flavor and can be eaten to satisfy hunger. Those who do not study and practice diligently to reach the level of ancient sages must not neglect this method. Huang Tingjian, respectfully bowing, to the Secretary of the Imperial Academy, Your Excellency.

    325

    妓妾守节
    　　妓妾之以色艺取怜妬宠扵主家者亦曰我之富与贵有以感动其中耳设遇患难贫病彼必戚戚然求为脱身之计又肯守志不贰者哉如金谷园緑珠燕子楼盼盼韩香之扵叶氏爱爱之扵张逞者真絶无而仅有也大元混一以来得三人焉李翠娥维名娼也石九山万户纳置别业石没李誓不适他姓以辱身终日闭阁诵经而已年及七十余万户之子若孙遇嵗时
    咸往拜之乐籍中相传以为盛事王巧儿京师上色也陈云峤同知与之狎擕至杭陈卒奉正室铁氏以清慎勤俭终其身汪怜怜湖州角妓也讷古伯经歴常属意焉汪曰君若不弃寒防当以侧室处妾防窃狗偷妾决不为此态讷乃遣媒妁备财礼娶之经三载死汪髠发尼寺时公卿士夫有往访之者汪故毁其身形以絶狂念卒老于尼若此者亦可以追踪前古之懿徳矣

    Explanation

    325

    Prostitute and Concubine Loyalty

    Prostitutes and concubines who win pity and jealousy through beauty and talent in the households of their masters also say that my wealth and status have moved them. If they encounter hardship, poverty, or illness, they will surely seek ways to escape and will not remain steadfast in their resolve. Who can truly remain loyal? Like Green Pearl of the Jin Gu Garden, Pan Pan of the Swallow Tower, and Love Love of the Ye family, who were loyal to their lovers, such cases are rare indeed. Since the unification of the Great Yuan, there have been only three such individuals.

    Li Cui'e, a famous courtesan from Weiyang, was taken to a secondary residence by Shi Jiushan, a wealthy man. After Shi's death, Li vowed not to marry another man, and she spent her days in seclusion, reciting scriptures. She lived to be over seventy years old. Shi's descendants would visit her during festivals, and this was passed down as a great event in the entertainment records.

    Wang Qiao'er, a top courtesan in the capital, became intimate with Chen Yunqiao, an official, who took her to Hangzhou, where he died. He served his legitimate wife, Lady Tie, with diligence and frugality until his death.

    Wang Liangliang, a courtesan from Huzhou, was liked by Ne Gube, an official. Wang said, "If you do not despise my humble self, I will be content as your concubine. I would never do such a thing." Ne then arranged for her to be married with gifts and dowry. After three years, he died, and Wang shaved her head and entered a nunnery. At times, officials and scholars would visit her. Wang deliberately disfigured herself to discourage lustful thoughts and died an old woman in the nunnery. Such individuals can also be seen as following the virtuous deeds of ancient times.

    326

    与妓下火文
    　　钱唐道士洪丹谷与一妓通因娶为室病且革頋谓洪曰妾死在旦夕卿湏自执薪还肯作一转语乎夫妾歌儿也卿能集曲调扵妾未死时使预闻之虽死无憾矣洪固滑稽轻佻者遂作文曰二十年前我共伊只因彼此太痴迷忽然四大相离后你是何人我是谁恭惟称呼秀钟谷水声遏楚云玉交枝坚一片心锦传道余二十载遽成如梦令休忆少年逰哭相思两手托空意难一笔勾断且道如何是一笔勾断孝顺哥终无孝顺逍遥乐永遂逍遥聴毕一笑而卒因
    记中吴纪闻载一事云昆山娼周氏系籍部中张子韶为守时娼暴亾适道川来访因命作下火文云可惜许可惜许大家且道可惜许个甚麽可惜巫山一段云眼如新水防绛唇昔年绣阁迎仙客今日桃源忆故人休记丑奴儿脸子便湏抖擞好精神南柯梦断如何也一曲离愁别是春大众还知某人向甚麽处去这里分明防得蓦山溪畔头头尽是喜相逢芳草渡头处处六么花十八其或未然更听下句咦与君一把无明火烧尽千愁万恨心其事颇相并附于此云

    Explanation

    326

    Farewell Letter to a Courtesan

    A Daoist priest from Qiantang, Hong Dangu, had a romantic relationship with a courtesan, whom he eventually married. When she was on the brink of death, she turned to Hong and said, "I am dying soon. Will you take care of yourself and compose a final message for me? I am a singer, and you are skilled in composing melodies. If you could gather a tune for me before I die, so that I might hear it, I would have no regrets." Hong, known for his wit and lightheartedness, then wrote:

    "Twenty years ago, I was with her. It was all because we were too infatuated with each other. Suddenly, the four elements parted, and now you are no one, and I am no one. I respectfully address you as the graceful lady of Zhonggu. Your voice was so powerful it could stop the clouds of Chu, and our souls were intertwined like jade branches. Our love was unwavering, and our hearts were filled with wisdom. After twenty years, it has all turned into a dream. Do not remember the pleasures of youth. Cry out in longing, but hold nothing. How does one decisively sever ties? What is this decisive severance? Even the dutiful son eventually finds no duty, and the carefree joy is forever carefree. When he heard this, he laughed and passed away.

    The Records of Zhongwu also recounts a similar story. A courtesan named Zhou from Kunshan, who was registered in the official records, suddenly died when Zhang Zishao, the local official, was in charge. At that time, Daochuan visited and was asked to compose a farewell letter, which read:

    "Pity me, pity me, oh great one. What is there to pity? Pity the cloud of Wu Mountain, the eyes like new water, the red lips. In the past, she welcomed the immortal in her embroidered hall; today, she remembers her old friend in the Peach Blossom Spring. Do not remember the Chounuer (a type of song), but shake off the dust and be spirited. The South柯 dream has ended. How is it that a song of parting is also spring? The common people know where someone is going. Here, it is clear that at the edge of the Moshan Creek, everywhere is a joyful reunion. At the grassy crossing, everywhere is the Liu'erhua (a type of flower). If it is not so, listen to the next line: Ah, with you, I will burn with the fire of ignorance, burning away a thousand sorrows and a hundred hatreds in my heart. This story is quite similar and is included here as well."

    327

    贺人妾得子启
    　　陆伯麟侧室育子友人陆象翁以啓戱贺之曰犯帘前禁寻灶下盟玉虽种扵蓝田珠将还于合浦移夜半鹭鸶之歩防度惊惶得天上麒麟之儿这囘喝采既可续诗书礼乐之脉深嗅得油盐醤醋之香苏东坡咏婢谑词有掲起裙儿一阵油盐醤醋香之句吊四状元诗
    　　平江一驿舟中有题吊四状元诗者不知谁所作诗曰四榜状元逢此日他年公论定难逃空令太守提三尺不见元戎用六韬元举何如兼善死公平争似子威髙世间多少偷生者黄甲由来出俊髦元举王宗哲字也至正戊子科三元进士时为湖广宪佥兼善台哈布哈字也时为台州路逹噜噶齐公平李齐字也时为髙邮府知府子威李黼字也时为江州路总管此四公者或大臣节或尽忠王事或遇难而亾故云若论其优劣则江州第一台州次之髙邮又次之宪佥不足道也

    Explanation

    327

    Letter Congratulating on the Birth of a Child from a Concubine

    Lu Bokun's concubine has given birth to a son. His friend Lu Xiangweng wrote a humorous letter to congratulate him, saying:

    "Breaking the rules of the inner chambers, making a hasty promise in the kitchen, jade seeds grow in the humble fields of Lantian, pearls return to the sea. Moving cautiously and nervously like a heron walking at midnight, this time, the applause can continue the lineage of poetry, books, rites, and music, and deeply appreciate the aroma of cooking. Su Dongpo's playful verse about a servant girl describes the scent of cooking."

    Poem Mourning Four Top Scholars

    In a boat at a station in Pingjiang, there is a poem titled "Mourning Four Top Scholars," whose author is unknown. It says:

    "Four top scholars met this day, the public judgment of the future will not escape. In vain, the prefect carries a three-foot sword, but the general does not use the Six Strategies. Yuan Jiu, how can he compare to those who died in the name of virtue? Li Qi, as fair and high as Ziwei. How many in the world live by stealth, but the yellow rank (top scholar) comes from the elite."

    Yuan Jiu, Wang Zongzhe, was a top scholar in the Zhi Zheng Wu Zi examination, serving as a censor in Huguang. His name was Jianshan. He died in the name of virtue. Li Qi, also known as Ziqi, served as the governor of Gaoyou Prefecture. Li Fu, known as Ziwai, served as the governor of Jiangzhou. These four scholars either upheld great loyalty, served the king with utmost devotion, or died tragically. If we were to rank them, Jiangzhou would be first, followed by Taizhou, then Gaoyou, and lastly, the censor is not worth mentioning.

    328

    鸡妖
    　　至正丁酉春三月上海李胜一家鸡伏七雏一雏作大鸡状鼓翼长鸣明年戊戌春正月钱唐卢子明家一鸡伏九雏一雏有三足二足在前一足在后三月诸暨彦城家一鸡伏五雏一雏有四足二足在翼下不数日皆死而各家亦无他异
    　　胡烈女
    　　越嵊县剡溪胡氏讳妙端适同邑祝某至正庚子春为苖防虏至金华县将妻之义不受辱乘间啮指血题诗壁上已赴水而死三月廿四日也防帅服其节为立庙祀之邑人咸曰烈女庙诗曰弱质空懐漆室忧捜山千骑入深幽旌旗影乱天同惨金鼓声淫亦愁父母劬劳何日报夫妻恩爱此时休九泉有路还归去那个云边是越州

    Explanation

    328

    Chicken Demons
    In the third month of spring in the year Ding You of the Zhizheng era, in the family of Li Sheng from Shanghai, a hen hatched seven chicks, and one of them appeared as a large chicken, flapping its wings and crowing loudly. In the first month of spring in the following year, Wu Xu, in the family of Lu Ziming from Qiantang, a hen hatched nine chicks, and one of them had three legs, with two legs in front and one behind. In the third month of the same year, in the family of Wen Yancheng from Zhuji, a hen hatched five chicks, and one of them had four legs, with two legs under the wings. Within a few days, they all died, but there were no other unusual occurrences in their families.

    Lady Hu, the Chaste Woman
    From Yanshan Creek in Sheng County, Yue Prefecture, the Hu family, whose given name was Miao Duan, married a man named Zhu from the same county. In the spring of the year Geng Zi of the Zhizheng era, she was captured by the Miao bandits and taken to Jinhua County, where they intended to marry her. She refused to submit to dishonor and took the opportunity to bite her finger, let the blood flow, and wrote a poem on the wall. She then drowned herself on the twenty-fourth day of the third month. The bandit leader was impressed by her virtue and built a temple to honor her. The people of the county all called it the Temple of the Chaste Woman. The poem she wrote said:

    Her delicate nature harbored the sorrow of a woman in a darkened room.
    Thousands of riders searched the mountains and entered the deep, secluded areas.
    The flags' shadows made the sky appear equally gloomy.
    The sound of gongs and drums was so loud that even the ghosts were saddened.
    How can I repay my parents' hard work?
    Our love as husband and wife ends now.
    There is a path to the underworld to return to.
    Which cloud's edge leads to Yue Prefecture?

    329

    鼃狱
    　　卢伯玉【文璧】至正初尹荆山日忽有一巨鼃登防前两目瞠视有所诉者令卒尾之行去县六七里有废井遂跳入不出既得报往集里社汲井获死尸乃两日前二人同出为商一人谋其财而杀之掩捕究问抵罪死者之家属云其在生不食鼃见即买放岂一念之善为造物者固已鍳之鼃能雪寃良有以也沁园春
    　　宋刘改之先生造词赡逸有思致赋沁园春二首以咏美人之指甲与足者尤纎丽可爱一曰销薄春氷碾轻寒玉渐长渐弯见鳯鞵泥污偎人强剔龙涎香断拨火轻翻学抚瑶琴时时欲剪更掬水鱼鳞波底寒纎柔处试摘花香满镂枣成斑时将粉泪偷弹记绾玉曾敎栁傅看算恩情相着搔便玉体归期暗数画徧防干每到相思沉吟静处斜倚朱唇皓齿间风流甚把仙郎暗搯莫放春闲一曰洛浦淩波为谁防歩轻尘暗生记花芳径乱红不损

    Explanation

    329

    Lu Boyu (Wen Bi)

    In the early years of the Zhi Zheng era, Lu Boyu, who served as the governor of Jing Mountain, suddenly saw a large frog appear before the fence, with its eyes wide open as if it had something to say. He ordered a soldier to follow it, and they went six or seven miles away to an abandoned well, where the frog jumped in and did not come out. After receiving the report, he went to the village to gather the community and drew water from the well, where they found a dead body. It was two days ago that two men went out to do business, and one of them plotted to kill the other for his money. They were captured, interrogated, and punished. The family of the deceased said that he never ate frogs and would buy them and release them if he saw them. Could it be that a single good deed was noticed by the Creator? The frog was able to clear the wrong, indeed it had a reason.

    Qinyuan Chun

    Mr. Liu Gai Zhi of the Song Dynasty composed two Qinyuan Chun poems that describe the nails and feet of beautiful women, which are particularly delicate and lovely. One poem reads:

    Melting the thin spring ice, grinding the light cold jade, gradually growing and bending, seen in the phoenix shoes, the mud-stained feet leaning close, the dragon's fragrance broken, the fire flicked lightly, learning to play the jade zither, always wanting to cut, and then taking water from the fish-scale waves at the bottom of the cold, slender place, trying to pick the flower fragrance that fills the carved red spots, sometimes secretly wiping away the powder tears. Remembering the jade hairpin that once taught Liu Fu to see, calculating the affection that binds, scratching the jade body, counting the days of return in secret, drawing through the dry walls, every time when missing, silently contemplating in a quiet place, leaning against the red lips and white teeth, how charming to secretly pinch the immortal youth, do not let spring be idle.

    The other poem reads:

    On the Luo River, the waves ripple gently, who is walking lightly, leaving a faint trace of dust? Remember the fragrant path of flowers, where the red petals remain undamaged.

    330

    歩苔幽砌嫩緑无痕衬玉罗悭销金様窄载不起盈盈一叚春嬉防倦笑敎人欵捻防褪些根有时自度歌声悄不觉防尖防拍频忆金莲移换文鸳得侣绣茵催衮舞鳯轻分懊恨深遮牵情半露出没风前烟缕裙知何似似一钩新月浅碧笼云近邵清溪【亨贞】嗣其体调以咏眉目真隽永有味一曰巧鬭弯环纎凝妩媚明装未似江亭晓望遥山拂翠宫帘暮卷新月横钩扫黛嫌浓涂铅讶浅能画张郎不自由伤春倦为皱多无力翻做娇羞填来
    不满横秋料着得人间多少愁记鱼笺缄启背人偷敛鴈钿胶并运指轻揉有喜先占长颦难效栁叶轻黄金在否双尖锁试临鸾一展依旧风流一曰漆防填眶鳯稍侵天然俊生记隔花瞥见踈星烱烱倚防凝注止水盈盈端正窥帘瞢腾并枕睥睨檀郎长是青端相乆待嫣然一笑宻意将成困酣曾莺惊强临镜挼挱犹未醒忆帐中亲见似嫌罗宻尊前相頋翻怕灯明醉后看承歌防鬭弄防度孜孜频送情难处是鲛绡揾透别泪双零恭敏坊

    Explanation

    330

    Walking on the moss-covered steps, the fresh green color leaves no trace behind, the slender and delicate appearance cannot bear the fullness of spring playfulness. A playful gesture gently pulls back or hides something, sometimes self-composed singing is quiet, the sound of tapping is barely noticed. Remembering the movement of golden lotus feet, the pairing of literary swans, the light separation of embroidered mats. Deep regret and hidden emotions, with a hint of a skirt in the wind, what does it resemble, like a new moon, a faint blue sky, and a nearby clear stream. Continuing the theme, praising the beauty and lasting charm of eyebrows and eyes. One describes the skillful and delicate beauty of curved shapes, the application of makeup, expressing the inability to control the spring melancholy and the resulting helplessness. The autumn is not yet full, but it carries much human sorrow. Recalling the act of writing and sealing a letter secretly, using goose-shaped brooches and gently rubbing them. The anticipation of joy, the difficulty of imitating a long frown, the lightness of golden leaves. Describing the natural beauty of black eyes and the slight intrusion of phoenix feathers. Recalling seeing a bright star through flowers, gazing fixedly, and waiting for a beautiful smile. Hidden emotions, the sleepiness, and the fear of being seen, leading to a sense of longing and separation.

    331

    李恭敏公者所居在江隂之南门其门首巷坊亦题曰恭敏不知当日名坊之义而七八十年来子孙消削第宅倾圯殆尽弃遗故址竟为里豪薛得昭所吞土木一新乡闾徤羡忽有人献謟扵薛云若不除去旧坊终非君家利也薛深然之指数恭敏之族尊且长者惟李唐卿可主其事乃呼至赠泉百缗李欣然撤之一夕呓语呻吟甚苦妻急呼之觉问其故曰我梦见袍笏大官自云是我祖责以不能世守其业又毁其坊既骂且挞我负痛呌号故致此耳语既暴死莫救又数年城燬扵兵薛氏室屋财产悉空贫无为计遂执干役扵时贵之家噫子孙之不肖强覇之用心皆可为后人鍳也

    Explanation

    331

    Li Gongmin, a respected figure, lived in the southern gate of Jiangyin. The alley entrance was also inscribed with the name "Gongmin," but the meaning behind this inscription was unclear. Over seventy to eighty years, his descendants dwindled, and his mansion collapsed almost completely, leaving behind an abandoned site that was eventually taken over by a local powerful figure named Xue Dezao. He renovated the area, and the local community admired it. Suddenly, someone flattered Xue, saying that if the old inscription was not removed, it would not be beneficial for his family. Xue agreed and pointed out that among the Gongmin family, only Li Tangqing, the most respected and senior member, could handle the matter. He called Li to his house and gave him a hundred strings of cash. Li happily removed the inscription. That night, he mumbled and groaned in pain, and his wife woke him up. He said, "I dreamed of a high-ranking official who said he was my ancestor, blaming me for not being able to keep the family business going and for destroying the inscription. He scolded and beat me, causing me great pain, which led to this." He died suddenly and could not be saved. A few years later, the city was destroyed by war, and Xue's property and wealth were completely lost. He became poor and had no way to make a living, so he worked as a laborer for a wealthy family. Alas, the descendants' lack of virtue and the powerful figure's intentions can all serve as lessons for future generations.

    332

    趣
