    　　余家天台万山中茅屋可以芘风雨石田可以具饘粥虽行江海上而泉石草木之胜未尝不在梦寐时见也偶读庐陵罗景纶大经所着鹤林玉露曰唐子西云山静似太古日长如小年余家深山之中每春夏之交苍藓盈阶落花满迳门无剥啄松影参差禽声上下午睡初足旋汲山泉拾松枝煑苦茗啜之随意读周易国风左氏离骚太史公书及陶杜诗韩苏文数篇从容歩山径抚松竹与麛犊共偃息扵长林丰草间坐弄流泉潄齿濯足既归竹窓下则山妻稚子作笋蕨供麦饭欣然一饱弄笔窓间随大小作数十字展所蔵法帖墨迹画卷縦观之兴到则吟小诗或

    Explanation

    332

    Here is the final translation of the source text:

    Leisure in the Mountains

    My home is nestled among the myriad mountains of TianTai, where a thatched cottage shelters from wind and rain, and stone fields provide porridge. Though I may wander on the rivers and seas, the beauty of springs, rocks, and plants is never absent from my dreams. Recently, I read He Lin Yu Lu by Luo Jinglun of Luling, which quotes Tang Zixi as saying: "The mountains are tranquil like ancient times, and the days seem endless as the years of a child." In my deep mountain retreat, during the transition between spring and summer, moss covers the steps, flowers fill the paths, and there are no knocks on the door. The shadows of pines vary, and the sounds of birds echo above and below. After a refreshing nap, I draw water from the mountain spring and gather pine branches to boil bitter tea. I sip it leisurely and read the Book of Changes, National Odes, Zuo's Commentary, Li Sao, Records of the Grand Historian, and several works by Tao Yuanming and Du Fu, as well as writings by Han Yu and Su Shi. I stroll along the mountain paths, caressing the pines and bamboos, and rest with young deer in the lush forest. I sit by the flowing stream, brushing my teeth and washing my feet. Upon returning to the bamboo window, my wife and children prepare bamboo shoots and ferns with millet porridge, and I enjoy a hearty meal. I write a few lines at the window, regardless of length, and admire my collection of calligraphy, ink paintings, and scrolls. When inspiration strikes, I compose a short poem.

    333

    草玉露一两叚再烹苦茗一杯出歩溪边邂逅园翁溪友问桑麻说秔稲量晴校雨探节数时相与剧谈一饷归而倚杖柴门之下则夕阳在山紫緑万状变幻顷刻恍可人目牛背笛声两两来归而月印前溪矣味子西此句可谓絶妙然此句妙矣识其妙者盖少彼牵黄臂苍驰猎扵声利之塲者但见衮衮马头尘匆匆驹隙影耳乌知此句之妙哉人能真知此妙则东坡所谓无事此静坐一日是两日若活七十年便是百四十所得不已多乎此罗君语也余盖亦知此妙乆矣风尘洞豺虎咬人防赋归与之诗计无所得又未知何日可以遂吾志也掩卷为之三叹日书三万字
    　　江浙平章子山公书法妙一时自松雪翁之后便及之尝问客有人一日能写得几字客曰闻赵学士言一日可写万字公曰余一日写三万字未尝以力倦而辍笔公号正斋恕叟又号蓬累叟康里人

    Explanation

    333

    After a moment of reflection, I brewed a cup of bitter tea and went out to the stream, where I encountered a wise old man and a friend. We talked about mulberry and hemp, discussed rice and millet, measured the weather, and checked the growth of plants. We engaged in lively conversation for a short while, then returned home and leaned on a stick at the wooden gate, with the sun setting behind the mountains. The colors of the sky were shifting rapidly, creating countless variations that were dazzling to the eyes. The sound of flutes came from the backs of cattle as they returned home, and the moon was reflected in the stream ahead. The flavor of the sentence from the West is truly exquisite, although this sentence is wonderful, those who recognize its beauty are few. Those who are driven by the pursuit of fame and profit, rushing by like dust on the road and like shadows passing quickly, cannot know the beauty of this sentence. If a person truly understands this beauty, then as Su Shi (Su Dongpo) said, "If there is nothing to do, sitting quietly for one day is like two days, and living seventy years is like living one hundred and forty years. Isn't that a great gain?" This is what Luo Jun said. I have also known this beauty for a long time. The chaos and turmoil of the world, being bitten by wolves and tigers, I compose a poem about returning home. I have nothing to gain, and I do not know when I will be able to achieve my aspirations. Closing the book, I sighed three times and wrote thirty thousand words.

    High-ranking official Jiang-Zhe Pingzhang Zishan Gong's calligraphy was exquisite for a time, surpassing even that of Zhao Mengfu. He once asked a guest how many characters one could write in a day. The guest replied that Zhao Mengfu said one could write ten thousand characters in a day. Gong said, "I can write thirty thousand characters in a day, and I have never stopped writing due to fatigue." Gong was also known as Zhengzhai Shousou and Penglei Sou, and he was from Kangli.

    334

    妓出家
    　　李当当者敎坊名妓也姿艺超出辈流忽翻然若有所悟遂着道士服江浙儒学提举叚吉甫先生【天祐】赠之以诗曰歌舞当今第一流洗妆拭面别青楼便随南岳夫人去不为苏州刺史留璚馆月明箫鳯下绮窓云散镜鸾却嫌痴絶浔阳妇嫁得商人已白头能改斋漫録云唐阳郇伯作妓人出家诗曰尽出花钿与四隣云鬟剪落向残春暂惊风烛难留世便是池莲不染身贝叶欲翻迷锦字梵声初落误梁尘従今艶色归空后湘浦应无觧佩人湘山野録乃谓陈彭年作此不考之过也吁二先生之风流余韵扵此可以想见矣
    　　河南王
    　　河南王布琳济达为本省丞相时一日掾吏田荣甫抱牍诣府请印王留田侍宴命司印开匣取印至前田误触坠地王适更新衣而印朱溅污满襟王色不少动欢饮竟夕又一日行郊天气且暄王易凉帽左右捧笠侍风吹堕石上击碎御赐玉顶王笑曰是有数也谕令毋惧噫此其所以为丞相之量

    Explanation

    334

    A Courtesan Renounces the World
    　　Li Dangdang was a renowned courtesan in the imperial music bureau, whose talent and beauty surpassed those of her peers. Suddenly, as if she had an epiphany, she donned Taoist robes. Jiangsu and Zhejiang's Confucian education supervisor, Mr. Duan Jifu (courtesy name Tianyou), gifted her a poem: "Her dancing and singing are the best in the current era. She cleanses herself and leaves the brothel, following the Lady of South Mountain, not staying for the governor of Suzhou. In the jade pavilion, the moonlight illuminates the descending phoenix; from the gauzy window, clouds disperse, revealing the mirror with a phoenix inside. She despises the foolish women of Xunyang who marry merchants and grow old. According to 'Nenggai Zhai Manlu,' Yang Xunbo from the Tang dynasty wrote a poem about a courtesan renouncing the world: 'She gives all her jewelry to her neighbors, cuts off her hair and faces the waning spring, temporarily startled by the fleeting nature of life, like a lotus in a pond, unstained by the world. She is about to turn the pages of a Buddhist sutra, losing herself in its beauty, the sound of Buddhist chanting falls, confusing the dust on the beams. From now on, her beauty will return to emptiness, there should be no one in Xiangpu who will return her jade pendant.' 'Xiangshan Yelu' mistakenly attributes this poem to Chen Pengnian, a mistake indeed. Ah, the lingering charm of these two scholars can be imagined here.
    　　The King of Henan
    　　When Bulin Jida served as the chancellor of Henan province, one day, the clerk Tian Rongfu brought a document to the office to request a seal. The king kept Tian Rongfu to attend a banquet and ordered the official in charge of seals to open the box and bring the seal forward. Tian accidentally knocked it to the ground. The king was just changing his clothes, and the red seal paste stained his robe. The king's expression did not change at all, and he enjoyed the banquet all night. Another day, while traveling to the suburbs, the weather was warm. The king changed to a cool hat, and his attendants held an umbrella to shield him from the wind, which blew it down and shattered the jade top given by the emperor. The king smiled and said, "It was fated," instructing them not to be afraid. Ah, this is the measure of a chancellor.

    335

    妖异
    　　至正辛卯夏松江普照寺僧舍一帚开花又嘉兴儒学阍人陶氏磨上木肘发青条开白花又吴江分湖里煆工一栁树桩以安铁碪者且十余年矣发长条数茎如苇三家虽有此怪而皆无恙岂非闗系国家之气数乎
    　　塔影入屋
    　　平江虎丘阁版上有一窍当日色清朗时以掌大白纸承其影则一寺之形胜悉扵此见之但顶反居下耳此固有象可寓非幻出者松江城中有四塔西曰普照又西曰延恩西南曰超果东南曰兴圣夏监运家乃在四塔之东而小室内却有一塔影长五寸许倒悬于西壁之上不知从何而来然不常有或时见之焉是又不可晓也
    　　钱唐懐古词
    　　傅按察者其名钱唐懐古尝作一词云静中看记昔日湖山宛若虎踞龙蟠下襄樊指挥湘汉鞭云骑围绕江干势不成三时当混一过唐之数不为难陈桥驿孤儿寡妇乆假当还挂征防龙舟催发紫宸初卷朝班禁庭空土花晕碧辇路悄诃喝声干縦余得西湖风景花栁亦凋残去国三千防仙一梦依然天淡夕阳间昨宵也一轮明月还照临安盖鸭头緑调也

    Explanation

    335

    Translation

    Strange Phenomena
    In the summer of 1341, during the Yuan Dynasty, a broom in a temple shed at the Pu Zao Temple in Songjiang bloomed. In Jiaxing, a wooden arm in the gatehouse of the Confucian academy, owned by Tao, developed green branches and white flowers. In Fenhu Lake, Wujiang, a blacksmith had a willow tree stump serving as an iron anvil for over a decade, which developed long, reed-like branches. Despite these strange occurrences, no harm befell the three families. Could these not be related to the fate of the nation?

    Pagoda Shadows Entering Houses
    At the Tiger Hill Pavilion in Pingjiang, there is a hole in the pavilion. On clear days, when a large white paper is placed to catch its shadow, the entire temple's scenic beauty can be seen, but the top is upside down. This is a natural phenomenon, not a hallucination. In Songjiang, there are four pagodas: the Pu Zao Pagoda to the west, the Yan En Pagoda further west, the Chao Guo Pagoda southwest, and the Xing Sheng Pagoda southeast. The residence of the summer governor is east of the four pagodas, and in a small room, a pagoda shadow about five inches long hangs upside down on the west wall. No one knows where it comes from, but it does not appear often, sometimes seen and sometimes not. This is another mystery that remains unexplained.

    A Nostalgic Poem About Qiantang
    Fou, the censor, known for his name, once wrote a poem about Qiantang. It goes: "In quiet contemplation, I recall the past lakes and mountains, lush and verdant, as if tigers and dragons were perched atop them. Below Xiangfan, commanding the Xiang and Han rivers, riding cloud horses, surrounding the riverbanks, the momentum was not yet complete. At the time of unification, passing through the numbers of the Tang Dynasty was not difficult. At Chenqiao Post Station, the young Emperor Taizu of Song, born to an orphaned mother, long overdue, should return. Dragon boats are hurriedly launched, the beginning of the Purple Palace, the court rolls up, the inner palace is empty, earth flowers with a greenish hue, the imperial road is quiet, the sounds of shouts and commands are gone. Even so, I still have the scenery of West Lake, flowers and willows also wither, leaving the country for a long time, a dream of immortality remains, the sky is faint in the twilight, last night, a full moon still shines over Lin'an. This is the tune of Duck's Head Green."

    336

    人命至重
    　　后至元间同知两浙都转运盐使司事赵君伯常休日与书吏谈官府政事因曰吾曩为中书提控掾史时夜坐私第一室忽有两来前都堂钧防呼唤遂即上马頴前导至一官府树木隂翳大官危坐听事上问曰河南饥省咨至乃缓七日不报彼处死者甚众汝知之乎吾答曰某提控耳该掾稽迟之罪已尝呈举官沉思良久曰非汝过也汝退又命前曰可急追该掾某人来吾遂梦觉也明日晨起令人觇之夜死矣人命至重尔辈其愼之
    　　度量宏深
    　　建德路达噜噶齐衮都罗丹字志道守贑州路任满听除时有故吏某往临江贴补介衮都罗丹尺牍见总管穆巴喇即日録用就遣某持俸钞五十定餽之时衮都罗丹家甚贫朋友间每分财以济某竟匿其钞后穆巴喇数得书而谢不及疑焉因使问之衮都罗丹知为某匿即具书请失谢之罪某闻惶赧无地令儿子奉钞还终不受且为其恶未尝与人言衮都罗丹西域人也度量之宏深如是可谓厚德君子矣若某者名教中所不可容尚孰责哉

    336

    The Value of Human Life
    During the late Yuan period, Squire Zhao Bocang, who served as the Deputy Director of the Two-Zhe Salt Administration, had a conversation with his clerks about government affairs on a day off. He said, "When I was a lower-ranking official in the Central Secretariat, one night I was sitting in my private room when suddenly two attendants came to deliver a summons from a high-ranking official. I immediately mounted my horse and was led to a government office. The place was surrounded by dense foliage, and a high-ranking official was seated upright in a hearing room. He asked me, 'The province of Henan is suffering from famine, and the provincial administration has consulted me. I delayed the response for seven days, but no report was received. Many people have died there. Do you know about this?' I replied, 'I am just a lower-ranking official. The delay in reporting is my fault.' The official thought for a while and said, 'It is not your fault. You may go.' He then ordered the attendants, 'Quickly summon the official who was supposed to report.' I woke up from my dream. The next morning, I sent someone to check, and indeed, the official had died that night. Human life is extremely valuable. You must be cautious."

    The Broad and Deep Character
    Squire Guluoradan Zhi Dao, the Director of the Dade Road in the Yuan dynasty, served as the governor of the Gan Prefecture. At the end of his term, he was about to leave office. A former subordinate, whom he had employed, went to Linjiang for some purpose. The governor, Mu Bala, saw a letter from the former subordinate and immediately employed him again, dispatching him with a gift of fifty taels of salary. Guluoradan's family was very poor, and his friends often shared their wealth to help him. However, the former subordinate secretly hid the money. Mu Bala repeatedly tried to thank him but failed, suspecting something was amiss. He then ordered the attendants to summon the former subordinate. Guluoradan woke up from his dream. When the former subordinate arrived, Guluoradan knew he had hidden the money. He wrote a letter asking for forgiveness for the former subordinate's mistake. The former subordinate, upon hearing this, felt deeply ashamed and ordered his son to return the money. However, Guluoradan refused to accept it and kept the former subordinate's wrongdoing a secret, never mentioning it to anyone. Guluoradan, a man from the Western Regions, possessed such a broad and deep character that he could be called a man of great virtue. As for the former subordinate, he was unworthy of the moral teachings and could not be tolerated; who could blame him?

    This translation aims to capture the essence of the original text while ensuring it reads naturally in English.

    337

    髙丽氏守节
    　　中书平章库库岱之侧室髙丽氏有贤行平章死誓弗贰适正室子巴特玛多尔济说其色欲妻之而不可得乃以其父所有大塔纳环子献扵太师巴延此物盖巴延所属意者巴延喜问所欲遂白前事巴延特为奏闻奉防命巴特玛多尔济继小母髙丽氏髙丽氏夜与亲母逾垣而出削发为尼巴延怒以为故违圣防拜奏命省台洎侍正府官鞫问诸官奉命惟谨鍜錬备极惨酷时国公库哩济蘓扵鞫问官中独秉权力持正府都事特穆尔布哈数致语曰谁无妻子安能相守至死得有如此守节者莫大之幸而反坐以罪恐非我治朝之盛典也国公悟为言扵巴延之前宛曲觧释其事遂已特穆尔布哈汉名刘正卿后至监察御史而卒

    Explanation

    337

    Highly Virtuous Goryeo Woman

    The concubine of the Chancellor Kukudai, a Goryeo woman, was highly virtuous. After the Chancellor's death, she swore never to be unfaithful and married the eldest son of the legitimate wife, Batma Dorji. Batma Dorji desired her beauty and wanted to marry her, but she refused. He then presented to the Grand Tutor Bayan, a valuable object that belonged to her father, a large Ta Na ring. This object was something Bayan particularly desired. Bayan was pleased and asked what he wanted, and he revealed the previous events. Bayan specially reported the matter to the emperor, who issued a decree. The order was sent to the central government institutions and the judicial office, and the officials responsible for the investigation were instructed to act with caution. The officials followed the instructions diligently, and the investigation was conducted with great rigor and cruelty. The Prince Kuliji Su, among the investigators, held power and maintained the integrity of the judicial office. The official Temur Buka repeatedly said to him, "Who does not have a wife? How can one remain faithful to her until death? To have someone like her who remains chaste is a great fortune, yet she is punished for it. Is this the way we govern our country?" The Prince realized the truth and explained the matter skillfully to Bayan, resolving the issue. Temur Buka, known by his Chinese name Duan Zhengqing, later became an Inspector General and died in that position.

    338

    寒号虫
    　　五台山有鸟名寒号虫四足有肉翅不能飞其粪即五灵脂当盛暑时文采绚烂乃自鸣曰鳯凰不如我比至深冬严寒之际毛羽脱落索然如鷇雏遂自鸣曰得过且过嗟夫世之人中无所守者率不甘湛涪乡里必振拔自豪求尺寸名诧九族侪则便志满意得出肆入以为天下无复我加矣及乎稍遇贬抑遽若防家之狗垂首贴耳揺尾乞怜惟恐人不我恤视寒号虫何异哉是可哀已
    　　邓思贤
    　　尝见人戱呼一哗讦者为邓思贤初不可晓后读笔谈始得其说云世江西人好讼有一书名邓思贤皆讼牒法也其始则教以侮文侮不可得则欺诬以取之欺诬不可得则求其罪刦之盖思贤人名也人其术遂以名书村校中往往以授生徒

    Explanation

    338

    The Cold-Calling Insect
    　　On Mount Wutai, there is a bird known as the cold-calling insect. It has four feet and fleshy wings that cannot fly. Its droppings are used as the medicinal substance called five-spirit fat. During the hot summer, its appearance is bright and colorful, and it calls out, "Even the phoenix is not as beautiful as me." By the time it reaches the severe winter, its feathers fall off, leaving it looking like a newborn bird, and it then calls out, "Just get by." Alas, people who have nothing to hold onto generally do not content themselves with their current status but seek to rise and be proud. They seek a small name to impress their relatives and peers, feeling satisfied and believing they are unmatched in the world. When they encounter slight criticism, they immediately become like a dog in a kennel, hanging their heads, pressing their ears, and wagging their tails to beg for mercy, fearing no one will care for them, which is no different from the cold-calling insect. This is indeed pitiful.
    　　Deng Sixian
    　　Once, someone jokingly called a noisy and slanderous person "Deng Sixian." At first, it was not understood, but after reading The Brush Talk, the meaning became clear. It is said that people from Jiangxi province are fond of lawsuits. There is a book named Deng Sixian that contains legal documents and methods for lawsuits. Initially, it teaches to insult and defame; if that does not work, it resorts to deceit and slander; if that fails, it seeks to incriminate and coerce. Deng Sixian is a person's name, and because of his methods, the book is named after him. Village schools often teach these methods to students.

    339

    医科
    　　医有十三科考之圣济总録大方脉杂医科小方脉科风科产科兼妇人杂病科眼科口齿兼咽喉科正骨兼金镞科疮肿科鍼灸科祝由科则通兼言

    　　辍耕録巻十五
    <子部,小说家类,杂事之属,辍耕录>
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷十六　　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰陶氏二谱
    　　宋泰山王质所着云韬堂绍陶録録中首载栗里华阳二谱惟先生之大节髙风流播千古而质者乃能次第其出处作为年谱且以名吾书绍陶之志是可尚已遂録于此云
    　　书陶栗里谱
    　　元亮髙风发扵宋晋去就之际君曽祖事晋懋着勲劳自宋武帝芟元复马逆揣其末流即不出武帝将贤士以系人心见要亦不应陶谢皆世臣君世地色言俱辟而灵运为武帝秉任最后乃欲诡忠义杂江海逺师送君过虎溪而却灵运不入莲社素心皆所鍳知谱具左右

    Explanation

    339

    Medicine

    • Medicine has thirteen specialties, as recorded in the Shengji Zonglu (Comprehensive Record of Holy Remedies): Dafangmai Zajike (General Pulse and Miscellaneous Diseases), Xiaofangmai Ke (Minor Pulse Diseases), Feng Ke (Wind Diseases), Chanshen Ke (Childbirth and Women's Diseases), Yanke (Eye Diseases), Kouchi Jiantongke (Mouth, Teeth, and Throat Diseases), Zhenggu Jijuzuke (Orthopedics and Metal Wounds), Chuangzhong Ke (Surgical Diseases), Zhenjiu Ke (Acupuncture and Moxibustion), and Zhuyou Ke (Spiritual Healing).

    Chu Geng Lu, Volume Fifteen

    • Chu Geng Lu, Volume Fifteen

    • Qinding Siku Quanshu

    • Two Genealogies of the Tao Family

    • Wang Zhi of Mount Tai in the Song dynasty wrote Yuntai Tang Shao Tao Lu (Continuation of Tao in the Cloud Hall). The beginning of the book lists two genealogies of Tao Yuanming: Lilǐ Huáyáng (Lilǐ and Huáyáng).

    • Tao Yuanming's high moral character and integrity were well-known. Wang Zhi was able to document his life and achievements, naming the book Shao Tao (Continuation of Tao), which is a commendable endeavor. I have recorded it here.

    • Tao Yuanming's Genealogy

    • Yuanming's high moral character and integrity were evident during the Jin dynasty. His ancestors served the Jin dynasty with great distinction. When Emperor Wu of Liu Song, who sought to unify China, attempted to win over talented scholars, Yuanming and his contemporaries, including Xie Lingyun, chose not to serve him. Despite this, Xie Lingyun, who was later appointed by Emperor Wu, sent a distant teacher to accompany Yuanming to the edge of the stream at Huxi (Tiger Stream), but Yuanming did not enter the Lian She (Lotus Society) because of his pure heart. All these details are recorded in the genealogies.

    340

    兴宁三年乙丑【晋哀帝】
    　　君生扵浔阳柴桑今徳安县楚城市是父轶名命子诗云于穆仁考澹焉虚止寄迹风云宜兹愠喜陶氏自侃以武功擅世后裔稍袭故风多流乱岐盖折翼之祥发之旁淡君父子皆以徳著称侃女适孟嘉嘉女适君父是生君其气所传造化必有可言者
    　　大元元年丙子【晋武帝】
    　　君年十二失母祭妹文云慈妣早世我年二六
    　　大元九年甲申
    　　君年二十失妾楚调诗云弱冠逢世阻始室防其偏妻翟氏偕老所谓夫耕扵前妻鉏扵后当是翟汤家汤荘矫法赐四世以行知名【亦柴桑】
    　　大元十九年甲午
    　　君年三十有归园田诗云误落尘网中一去三十年初为州祭酒当在其前不堪乃觧归故云久在樊笼里复得返自然寻亦却主簿

    Explanation

    340

    Translation:

    In the second year of Xingning (365 AD) during the reign of Emperor Mu of Jin,
    The gentleman was born in Xunyang, Chaisang, now known as De'an County, Chucheng City. His father, named Yi, gave his son a poem: "The venerable and benevolent father, serene and tranquil in his dwelling, roamed freely among the clouds and winds, suitable for both joy and sorrow." The Tao family, starting with Tao Kan, was renowned for military prowess. Later generations of the Tao family gradually followed the old ways but often strayed from the right path. It is said that the Tao family, including the father and son, were known for their virtuous deeds, despite some setbacks. Tao Kan's daughter married Meng Jia, and Meng Jia's daughter married the gentleman's father, thus producing the gentleman. His spirit must have been transmitted, and his fate must have something to say.

    In the first year of Taoyuan (266 AD) during the reign of Emperor Wu of Jin,
    At the age of twelve, the gentleman lost his mother. In a memorial to his sister, he wrote: "My kind mother passed away early, when I was twelve."

    In the ninth year of Taoyuan (274 AD),
    At the age of twenty, the gentleman lost his concubine. In a poem, he wrote: "At the age of twenty, encountering the world's obstacles, the beginning of marriage was fraught with difficulties. My wife, Zhai Shi, lived with me until old age, as the saying goes, 'the husband plows in front and the wife plows behind.' This must be the Zhai family, known for their strict adherence to the law and their reputation for virtue across four generations."

    In the nineteenth year of Taoyuan (284 AD),
    At the age of thirty, the gentleman returned to his hometown. In a poem, he wrote: "Mistakenly fallen into the dust net, gone for thirty years, initially serving as a state official, this must have been before the event in question. Unable to bear it, he resigned and returned home, thus saying he had been long trapped in a cage and now returned to nature. Soon after, he was appointed as a clerk."

    341

    隆安四年庚子【晋安帝】
    　　君年三十六五月有従都还阻风规林诗当是防镇军衔命自京都上江陵故在始作镇军防军经曲阿诗后父在柴桑故云一欣侍温顔又云久防恋所生父为人度不肯适都当是已舍单行见还旧居诗军僚差彊郡吏故云时来茍冥防宛辔憩通衢投防命晨装暂与田园防
    　　隆安五年辛丑
    　　君年三十七正月有防斜川诗云开嵗倐五日方三十七作五日是当是故嵗五月还浔阳今嵗七月适江陵有赴假还江陵夜行途中诗留浔阳逾年当是予告在乡至是往赴云闲居三十载自未参镇军以前得三十六年当是不堪劳役遂起归意故云诗书敦宿好园林无俗情如何舍此去遥遥至南荆失父祭妹文云昔在江陵重罹天罚触事未逺书防犹存当是妹自武昌报江陵时父在柴桑
    　　元兴二年癸卯
    　　君年三十九正月有始春懐古田舍诗当是自江陵归柴桑复适京都宅忧居家思湓城故有懐古田舍又云良苖懐新十二月有与従弟敬逺诗云寝迹衡门下在都亦当是处野

    Explanation

    341

    In the fourth year of Long'an (庚子) during the reign of Emperor An of Jin,
    The master was thirty-six years old. In May, there was a poem titled "Returning from the Capital, Blocked by Wind in Gui Lin." It seems that he was ordered by the Prefect of the Army to go from the capital to Jiangling. Therefore, he wrote a poem about passing through Qu'a while serving as the Prefect of the Army. Later, his father was in Chaisang. Therefore, it says "one is delighted to serve his father's warm face." It also says "long time since leaving, missing the mother." His father, being a person of virtue, did not go to the capital. It seems that he left alone to return to his old residence. The military officials were somewhat strong and the local officials were competent. Therefore, it says "when the time comes, one may take a rest on a broad road." He sent a message to prepare for the morning journey. He temporarily left his fields and gardens.

    In the fifth year of Long'an (辛丑) during the reign of Emperor An of Jin,
    The master was thirty-seven years old in January, and there was a poem titled "Farewell to Xiechuan." It says "the start of the year is suddenly five days, and he is thirty-seven, making it the fifth day of the fifth month of last year when he returned to Xunyang." This year, in July, he went to Jiangling. There was a poem titled "Returning to Jiangling on a Night Journey." He stayed in Xunyang for more than a year. It seems that he was granted leave in his hometown, and now he is going to fulfill his duties, saying he has been idle for thirty years. Since before he joined the Prefect of the Army, he has been thirty-six years old. It seems that he could not bear the labor and thus had the intention to return home. Therefore, it says "poetry and books are cherished, and the garden and orchard are free from worldly concerns." How can one leave this place and go far to the south of Jing? In the memorial to his father and sister, it says "in the past in Jiangling, he suffered a severe punishment from heaven." The events were not long ago, and the letters are still there. It seems that his sister reported from Wuchang to Jiangling when his father was in Chaisang.

    In the second year of Yuanxing (癸卯) during the reign of Emperor An of Jin,
    The master was thirty-nine years old in January, and there was a poem titled "Spring Thoughts on Ancient Farmhouses." It seems that he returned from Jiangling to Chaisang and then went to the capital, where he had family concerns at home. Missing Pengcheng, he wrote a poem about ancient farmhouses. It also says "good crops are new." In December, there was a poem titled "To My Cousin Jingyuan." It says "retiring to a humble door, in the capital, it is also a place in the countryside."

    342

    元兴三年甲辰
    　　君年四十有连雨独饮诗云僶仰四十年有饮酒诗云是时向立年志气多所耻遂尽介然分终死归田里当是在壬辰癸巳为州祭酒之时所谓投耒去学仕又云冉冉星气流亭亭复一纪至是得十二年
    　　义熙元年乙巳
    　　君年四十一三月有为建威参军使都经钱溪诗当是故嵗自都还里即吉庚子始事镇军继事建威中经罹忧至是得六年复衔命至都其家尚未归柴桑还旧居诗云畴昔家上京六载去还归往来时经乡闾不常留稍成疏故云阡陌不移旧邑屋或时非履歴周故居隣老防复遗至是始定居断他适十一月有归去来辞九月家留柴桑身往彭泽至是免归当是不堪军役故求县不堪县役故归家所谓风波未定心惮逺役彭泽去家百里公田足以为酒少日眷然有归与之情平生之志始决见序及辞甚详失妹所谓情在骏奔自免去軄是嵗刘将军録尚书

    Explanation

    342

    Final Translation

    Yuanxing Year Three, Jia Chen
    At the age of forty, you composed a poem about continuous rain and solitary drinking, toiling for forty years. There was another poem about drinking, which said, "At that time, when I was approaching my fortieth year, I was ashamed of many of my aspirations. Thus, I completely severed the ties and resolved to die and return to the countryside." It was during the years Renchen and Gui Si when I served as a state priest, as it is said, to abandon plowing and go to learn and serve in office. Another poem said, "Gradually, the starry qi flows, and once again after a decade," by then it had been twelve years.

    Yixi Year One, Yi Si
    At the age of forty-one, in March, there was a poem about serving as a military officer in Jianwei and traveling through Qianxi. It was during the previous year when I returned from the capital, and after a period of mourning, I began serving in the imperial guard and then in Jianwei, lasting six years. Again taking orders to go to the capital, my family had not yet returned to Chaisang. On returning to the old residence, the poem says, "Once upon a time, the family lived in the upper capital for six years, going back and forth, passing through the villages, not staying long, gradually becoming estranged. The fields and paths remained unchanged, but the houses of the old town might not be the same. The neighbors and old acquaintances were left behind, and by then, he finally settled down, deciding not to go elsewhere." In November, there was a poem called "Returning to the Fields." In September, the family stayed in Chaisang while I went to Pengze. It was because I could not bear military service that I sought a county post, but I could not bear the duties of the county, so I returned home. As it is said, the waves and winds were not yet settled, and my heart feared distant service. Pengze was a hundred li from home, and the public fields were sufficient to make wine. In a short while, I felt a longing to return home. The lifelong ambition was finally resolved, and the preface and poem were very detailed. Losing a sister, as it is said, the emotion was in the swift running, and he resigned from his post. In that year, General Liu was appointed as the Grand Secretary.

    343

    义熙三年丁未
    　　君年四十三有祭程氏妹文自乙巳至是所谓服制再周
    　　义熈四年戊申
    　　君年四十四有六月遇火诗云奄出四十年
    　　义熈五年己酉
    　　君年四十五有九日诗
    　　义熈六年庚戌
    　　君年四十六有西田获早稲诗
    　　义熙七年辛亥
    　　君年四十七有祭从弟敬逺文云絶粒委务考槃山隂晨采上药夕闲素琴当时同志见文甚详
    　　义熙十年甲寅
    　　君年五十有杂诗云奈何五十年弃官来归至是得十年故云荏苒经十载暂为人所覊
    　　义熙十一年乙卯
    　　君年五十一有与子俨等疏云年过五十又云见树木交防时鸟变声亦复欣然五六月北窓下卧遇凉风暂至自号羲皇上人见防甚详
    　　义熙十二年丙辰
    　　君年五十二有下潠田舍获诗云曰余为此来三四星火頽当是得此在癸丑甲寅之间

    Explanation

    343

    义熙三年丁未
    　　君年四十三，有祭程氏妹文，自乙巳至是，所谓服制再周。
    　　义熙四年戊申
    　　君年四十四，有六月遇火诗，云“奄出四十年”。
    　　义熙五年己酉
    　　君年四十五，有九日诗。
    　　义熙六年庚戌
    　　君年四十六，有西田获早稻诗。
    　　义熙七年辛亥
    　　君年四十七，有祭从弟敬远文，云“绝粒委务，考槃山阴，晨采上药，夕闲素琴”，当时同志见文甚详。
    　　义熙十年甲寅
    　　君年五十，有杂诗云“奈何五十年，弃官来归，至是得十年，故云荏苒经十载，暂为人所羁”。
    　　义熙十一年乙卯
    　　君年五十一，有与子俨等疏云“年过五十”，又云“见树木交防，时鸟变声，亦复欣然。五六月北窗下卧，遇凉风暂至，自号羲皇上人”，见防甚详。
    　　义熙十二年丙辰
    　　君年五十二，有下潠田舍获诗，云“曰余为此来，三四星火頽，当是得此在癸丑甲寅之间”。

    344

    义熈十四年戊午
    　　君年五十四楚调云僶俛六九年召为著作佐郎不应是嵗宋公为相国
    　　元熙元年己未【晋恭帝】
    　　君年五十五王休元为江州自造不得见遣其故人庞通之等赍酒扵半道栗里要之即引酌野亭休元出与相闻极欢终日尝九日把菊无酒休元饷之有九日闲居诗所谓秋菊满园时醪靡至当是未获所遗休元在江州几六载未审的在何年自乙巳至丁卯讫死未尝他适独暂为休元入州
    　　永初元年庚申【宋武帝】
    　　君年五十六同周续之召至都为顔延之连挫义熙间檀韶为江州邀续之在城北讲礼讐书有示周掾祖谢诗云马队非讲肆校书亦已勤又云但愿还渚中从我颍水濵江城尚不欲周往奚况京师刘遗民亦同有和刘柴桑诗云挈杖还西庐又云春醪觧饥劬其还以春有酬刘柴桑云嘉穗眷南畴又云慨然知已秋其还至是及秋初自西庐移南村有移居诗云闻多素心人乐与数朝夕又云过门更相呼有酒斟

    Explanation

    344

    义熙十四年戊午
    　　君年五十四，楚调云僶俛六九年，召为著作佐郎，不应。是嵗，宋公为相国。
    　　元熙元年己未【晋恭帝】
    　　君年五十五，王休元为江州，自造不得见，遣其故人庞通之等赍酒于半道栗里要之，即引酌野亭。休元出与相闻，极欢终日。尝九日把菊无酒，休元饷之。有九日闲居诗，所谓“秋菊满园时，醪靡至”，当是未获所遗。休元在江州几六载，未审的在何年。自乙巳至丁卯讫死，未尝他适，独暂为休元入州。
    　　永初元年庚申【宋武帝】
    　　君年五十六，同周续之，召至都为顔延之连挫。义熙间，檀韶为江州，邀续之在城北讲礼讐书。有示周掾祖谢诗云：“马队非讲肆，校书亦已勤。”又云：“但愿还渚中，从我颍水濵。江城尚不欲，周往奚况京师。”刘遗民亦同，有和刘柴桑诗云：“挈杖还西庐。”又云：“春醪觧饥劬。”其还以春，有酬刘柴桑云：“嘉穗眷南畴。”又云：“慨然知已秋。”其还至是，及秋初，自西庐移南村，有移居诗云：“闻多素心人，乐与数朝夕。”又云：“过门更相呼，有酒斟。”

    345

    酌之迁居殆为遗民之徒寻还西庐度相距亦不逺与遗民更相酬酢不改赏文析义之时未审的在何年或恐刘柴桑似县令刘或尝为此县存此呼或有命不为犹续之尝命为抚军参军不就因呼周掾皆不可知但非时为宰者语皆冷交非官丁柴桑诗云秉直司聪于恵百里此乃当官无疑寻诗钟情扵刘过厚扵周遗民自之余无闻续之在之中防婉君与周刘号浔阳三校情义稍有浅深是嵗宋武帝践祚
    　　景平元年癸亥【晋营阳王】
    　　君年五十九顔延之为始安过浔阳日造饮酣醉临去留二万钱送酒家相知乆间骤见益驩延之未审何时来柴桑所谓自尔分居及我多假伊好之洽接檐隣舍当是不诣刘穆之之时又未审何时去柴桑当是为豫章世子防军之时据诔防传略见

    Explanation

    345

    酌之迁居殆为遗民之徒，寻还西庐，度相距亦不远，与遗民更相酬酢，不改赏文析义之时。未审的在何年，或恐刘柴桑似县令刘，或尝为此县存此呼。或有命不为，犹续之尝命为抚军参军不就，因呼周掾皆不可知。但非时为宰者，语皆冷交非官。丁柴桑诗云：“秉直司聪于惠百里。”此乃当官无疑。寻诗钟情于刘，过厚于周，遗民自之余无闻。续之在之中，防婉君与周刘号浔阳三校，情义稍有浅深。是嵗，宋武帝践祚。

    景平元年癸亥【晋营阳王】
    君年五十九，顔延之为始安过浔阳，日造饮酣醉，临去留二万钱送酒家。相知乆间，骤见益驩。延之未审何时来柴桑，所谓自尔分居，及我多假伊好之洽，接檐隣舍，当是不诣刘穆之之时。又未审何时去柴桑，当是为豫章世子防军之时。据诔防传略见。

    346

    元嘉三年丙寅【宋文帝】
    　　君年六十二檀道济为江州时抱羸疾多瘠馁往馈以粱肉不受
    　　元嘉四年丁卯
    　　君年六十三有自祭文云律中无射拟挽歌诗云严霜九月中送我出逺郊当是杪秋下世顔延之诔云视化如归临凶若吉药剂弗尝祷祠弗恤其临终髙态见诔甚详君平生好谈归尽萧统以为处百龄之内居一世之中倐忽白驹寄寓逆旅与大块而荣枯随中和而放荡岂能劳扵忧畏役扵人间最知深心形赠影答神本趣略见所谓縦浪大化中不喜亦不惧应尽便湏尽无复独多虑惟患不知既已洞知安坐待此夫复何言杜甫许避俗未许逹道识者更详之
    　　书陶华阳谱
    　　通明髙风发扵梁齐宋去就之际君祖父皆食宋禄身又生宋代自齐髙帝代宋旋引去梁武帝代齐益退蔵平时以师待君然大节有定操岂复以恩礼推移暂至丹阳应简文之命不少至京都慰武帝之懐抑何其坚忍壮年果扵遗世炤之审故判之不疑谱具左方

    Explanation

    346

    In the third year of Yuanjia, the first year of the reign of Emperor Wen of Song,
    The gentleman was sixty-two years old. When Tan Daoji was the governor of Jiangzhou, the gentleman was suffering from a weak illness and often in a state of hunger and emaciation. Someone came to offer him fine rice and meat, but he refused to accept it.
    In the fourth year of Yuanjia, the second year of the reign of Emperor Wen of Song,
    The gentleman was sixty-three years old. He wrote a self-eulogy, which stated: "The law of the no-shooting is like a funeral dirge. The severe frost in the ninth month sends me out to the distant outskirts. It was likely in late autumn that he passed away." Yan Yan's eulogy stated: "He regarded transformation as returning home and faced misfortune as if it were good fortune. He did not taste medicine nor did he worry about prayers and sacrifices. His dignified demeanor at the time of his death is described in great detail in the eulogy." The gentleman was fond of discussing the concept of ultimate return. Xiao Tong believed that he lived within a hundred years and within one generation. Swiftly like a white horse, he was a guest in a temporary inn. He lived in harmony with the great block (nature) and let his life flourish and wither according to the natural order. How could he be burdened by worry and fear, or serve the world? He deeply understood the inner self and responded to the form and shadow, revealing the essence of the spirit. He allowed himself to be swept by the great transformation, neither happy nor afraid, and when his time came, he accepted it without further worry. The only concern is not knowing; once fully understood, he calmly awaited his fate, and what more could be said? Du Fu approved of avoiding the mundane but did not approve of reaching the Dao; those who understand should consider this further.
    Writing the spectrum of Tao Hua Yang
    Tao Hua Yang's high moral character was evident during the transitions between the Liang, Qi, and Song dynasties. His ancestors served under the Song dynasty, and he was born during the Song dynasty. After the Qi emperor replaced the Song, he withdrew from public life. After the Liang emperor replaced the Qi, he withdrew even further, living a reclusive life. He was treated as a teacher, but his fundamental principles remained steadfast, not swayed by favors or courtesies. He briefly went to Danyang to respond to Jianwen's summons and did not go to the capital to comfort Emperor Wu. How resolute and steadfast he was! In his prime, he firmly chose to withdraw from the world, having carefully considered the matter and thus making a decisive choice without doubt. The details are recorded on the left.

    347

    孝建三年丙申【宋世祖】
    　　君生扵丹阳秣陵今上元县治村是母郝氏梦两天人持罏香来前有娠今世为君再世为孙思邈两世肇啓扵郝故其兆先形当是本居天仙趣报尽还入人趣植根弗凡受形亦异生以火年火月又夏至极阳日悉禀纯阳多起飞心累功积行所升当益髙推佛言参君迹略见
    　　大明四年庚子
    　　君年五嵗常持荻画灰学书
    　　泰始元年乙巳【宋明帝】
    　　君年十嵗得洪神仙即有志养生语人仰青天睹白日不觉为逺及长博读书防觧文武诸伎自后天文地理人事虽至渊妙咸臻底极当时已罕歴年愈逺行世寖稀梁所载十二种今惟三种传不能纪十种唐志所载九种今惟四种有志无八种无志有五种本草后人增衍考正益详间与集注差异

    Explanation

    347

    In the third year of the Xiaojian era (457 AD) during the reign of Emperor Wen of Song (宋世祖),

    The subject was born in a village in the jurisdiction of Shangyuan County, which is now part of Nanjing, Jiangsu Province. His mother, Lady Hao, had a dream in which two divine figures brought a stove and incense, and she became pregnant. In this life, he is the subject, and in the next generation, he is the grandson of Sun Simiao, initiating the lineage in the Hao family.

    In the fourth year of the Daming era (460 AD) during the reign of Emperor Wu of Song,

    At the age of five, the subject often used willow twigs to write in the ashes, learning to read and write.

    In the first year of the Taishi era (465 AD) during the reign of Emperor Ming of Song,

    At the age of ten, the subject received the Hongshen Xianzhuan and immediately developed a strong interest in cultivating health. He told others that he looked up to the blue sky and saw the white sun, never feeling distant from his aspirations. As he grew older, he widely read and understood various literary and martial arts. Afterward, he delved deeply into astronomy, geography, and human affairs, reaching the pinnacle of their subtleties. At the time, such knowledge was already rare, and as the years passed, it became even more scarce. The Liuzhuan (六传) recorded twelve items, but the current transmission only preserves three, unable to record ten items. The Tang Catalogue (唐志) recorded nine items, but the current transmission only preserves four, lacking eight items that were once recorded. The Bencao (本草) has been expanded, corrected, and detailed over time, sometimes differing from the collected annotations.

    This translation aims to be faithful to the original text while maintaining natural and clear English. It addresses the idiomatic expressions, cultural allusions, syntactic ambiguities, and proper nouns as planned.

    348

    元徽二年甲寅【宋苍梧王】
    　　君年十九萧军録尚书引为诸王侍读故事止典文学无他务除奉朝请故事止奉朝防请召本不为官虽在宦途亦居静地及求县乃不遂縁势可见
    　　永明十年壬申【齐世祖】
    　　君年三十七家贫求宰县不遂脱朝服挂神武门去止句曲山体即轻防性嗜山水所歴必吟咏盘旋不已语人吾见朱门广厦虽适其华乐而无欲往之心望髙岩瞰大泽知难立止自常欲就之永明中求禄得輙差舛不尔岂得为今日之事亦縁势使然此语甚真是事先有根次有縁次有势相符乃入所谓道生之徳畜之物形之势成之惟难契故旷世难就
    　　隆昌元年癸酉【齐郁林王】
    　　君年三十八沈约为东阳屡要不至自栖句曲不出所谓徧歴名山求访仙药或未然一至句章礼育王塔一至丹阳应太子召他适皆无考又言往东阳従孙防岳授符图经法亦无考惟杨羲灵寳五符句容粲粲以陆修静陆以传孙许翽二景歌东阳章灵民出都遇得以与孙度所得止在秣陵句曲之间非逺适而后

    Explanation

    348

    Translation of the Source Text

    元徽二年甲寅【宋苍梧王】
    In the year Jia Yin of the Yuanhui era [during the reign of Emperor Wu of Song],
    At the age of nineteen, Shen Yu, the Secretary of the Board of Works, was appointed as a tutor to the princes. According to custom, this position was limited to literary duties without other responsibilities. He was granted the status of attending court meetings, but according to custom, it was only a formality and not a real office. Even though he was in the officialdom, he lived a quiet life. When he sought a county post, he did not succeed, but due to his position, it was visible.

    永明十年壬申【齐世祖】
    In the year Ren Shen of the Yongming era [during the reign of Emperor Wu of Qi],
    At the age of thirty-seven, he sought a county post due to poverty but did not succeed. He removed his official robes and hung them at the Shenwu Gate and left, settling in Juzi Mountain. His body was naturally light and agile, and he loved mountains and waters. Wherever he went, he would recite poetry and linger. He told others that he saw grand mansions and beautiful buildings, but he had no desire to go there. He looked at high cliffs and vast lakes, knowing it was difficult to stand still. He naturally wanted to go there. During the Yongming period, when he sought an official post, he often encountered misfortunes. If not for these misfortunes, how could he be in this situation today? It was also due to his position. This statement is very true. Beforehand, there is a root. Then there is a connection. Then there is a situation. When they align, they enter the realm of what is called the way of nature, nurturing things, and forming situations. Because it is difficult to match, it is hard to achieve in a long time.

    隆昌元年癸酉【齐郁林王】
    In the year Gui You of the Longchang era [during the reign of Emperor Xuande of Qi],
    At the age of thirty-eight, Shen Yu invited him to Dongyang repeatedly, but he did not go. He settled in Juzi Mountain and did not travel, as he had already traveled through famous mountains and sought elixirs of immortality. Or perhaps not. He once went to Juzhang to pay respects at the Liyu Wang Tower. He once went to Danyang to respond to the prince's summons, and other travels are undocumented. He also said he went to Dongyang to receive talismans and scriptures from Sun Fangyue, but there is no record. Only Yang Xi's Lingbao Wufu transmission from Jurong, which was passed on clearly to Lu Xiujing, who passed it on to Sun Xuwei. The Erying Ge and Dongyang Zhang were obtained by Lingmin when he left the capital and met with Sun Du. They were only in Moling and Juzi, not far away, and were passed on later.

    349

    永元元年己卯【齐东昏侯】
    　　君年四十四在句曲筑楼髙三层身处其上弟子居其中賔客至其下与物遂絶不娶无子他眷亦不通先断此根可议他事特爱松风庭院皆植松聆响为乐闲独逰泉石此门忌浊便清神仙上景多云霞下景多山水物多金玉色多紫碧他皆是所谓熟之养之覆之若欲成辨必加将护大要离尘换境为上
    　　中兴元年辛巳【齐和帝】
    　　君年四十六萧都督至新林遣弟子戴猛之迎谒初齐末作水丑木之歌至是援防文成梁字令弟子进之遂以梁建国后覆没亦预言朱防己巳诗叹朝阳重离七元卒騐虽茅山不却人主询谋中大通初献善胜成胜二刀度武帝狃陈庆之覆魏洛阳好大之心寖侈参防侯景大触骇机岂尽忘救世者但观时耳蚤慕张良甚深黄石编书盖真秘谍兵法其间余事推已及物亦致平绪术此门除魔显定乱学道者问及君着水镜握镜当是早为岂挂晚念

    349

    In the first year of Yongyuan (永元元年己卯), during the reign of Emperor Wu of Northern Zhou, the Master was forty-four years old. He built a three-story tower at Gouqu, standing atop it himself, while his disciples lived in the middle and guests gathered at the base. He cut himself off from the world, neither marrying nor having children, and did not communicate with other family members. By first severing these worldly ties, he could discuss other matters. He had a special love for the sound of the wind in the pines, and the courtyard was filled with pine trees. Listening to the sounds of the wind was his pleasure, and he enjoyed solitary walks among the springs and rocks. This path avoided dirt and sought purity. The upper realm of immortals was filled with clouds and mists, while the lower realm was filled with mountains and waters. Objects were often made of gold and jade, and colors were often purple and green. This is what is meant by nurturing, protecting, and covering. If one wants to achieve distinction, one must provide care and protection. The primary goal is to separate from the mundane and enter a higher realm.

    In the first year of Zhongxing (中兴元年辛巳), during the reign of Emperor Xuan of Northern Zhou, the Master was forty-six years old. Xiao, the military governor, arrived at Xinlin and sent his disciple Dai Meng to greet him. At the end of the Qi dynasty, a song called "Water Ugly Wood" was composed, and it was supported by the text of the Wen Cheng Liang. The Master ordered his disciple to present it, and as a result, the Liang dynasty was established. Later, when it fell, it was also predicted. Zhu Fang's poem in the year Ji Si lamented the sun rising and the seven stars (likely referring to the Big Dipper), and it was ultimately verified. Even though he lived in Maoshan, he did not refuse to consult with the ruler. At the beginning of the Zhongdatong era, he presented two swords named "Shan Sheng" and "Cheng Sheng." Observing Emperor Wu's attachment to Chen Qingzhi's conquest of Luoyang in the Wei dynasty, his ambition grew increasingly extravagant. Hou Jing's involvement in a major crisis did not completely forget those who save the world, but it depends on the situation. Early admiration for Zhang Liang was very deep, and the Huayang Jing (Yellow Stone Classic) was said to be a true secret teaching. Studying military strategy within it, other matters extended oneself to others, also leading to a peaceful and orderly method. This path removed demons and stabilized chaos. Those who study the Dao ask about the Master holding a mirror, which should be done early, not left for later.

    350

    天监元年壬午【梁髙祖】
    　　君年四十七梁武帝在西邸与防及即位恩礼弥笃问讯弗絶屡招不出画两牛一牧放水草之间一金络头人执杖驱之知不可复致旁族季直亦不肯事梁武帝尝叹梁有天下遂不见此人门风何繇乃尔
    　　天监四年乙酉
    　　君年五十移居积金涧泉石益竒无蛇虎有佳木及杂药初乏青林及来居皆自茂在句曲东垄
    　　中大通元年己酉
    　　君年七十四遇异人宣闿以本草用蝱虫水蛭之属伤物迟一纪可觧形至期果化尸觧凡十余种世闿自青城来句曲先升以君闻帝録其积水之功化后为蓬莱都水监见仙及拾遗甚略今茅山相稍详但防渉异

    350

    Translation of the Source Text

    In the first year of Tianjian (壬午) during the reign of Emperor Wu of Liang,
    The gentleman was forty-seven years old. Emperor Wu of Liang was in the western residence, and with Fang and upon his ascension, the imperial favor was even more sincere. Visits and inquiries never ceased, but he was repeatedly invited and did not come out. He drew two cows, one grazing in the water and grass, and one with a golden bridle being driven by a person with a stick. Knowing that it could not be brought back, the distant relative Jizhi also refused to serve Emperor Wu of Liang. He once lamented that although the Liang Dynasty had the world, it did not see this person, whose family customs were so great.

    In the fourth year of Tianjian (乙酉),
    The gentleman was fifty years old and moved to the Jijin Stream, where the springs and stones became more wondrous, and there were no snakes or tigers, only good trees and various herbs. Initially lacking in green forests, when he came to live there, they all grew lushly. He resided in the eastern ridge of Guqu.

    In the first year of Zhongdatong (己酉),
    The gentleman was seventy-four years old and met an extraordinary person named Xuan Kai, who used herbs like cicada slough and leeches, which would cause harm to things, but after a decade, the transformation would occur. By the appointed time, the body indeed transformed into more than ten different forms. It is said that Xuan Kai came from Qingcheng to Guqu, and first introduced the gentleman to the emperor. The emperor recorded his accumulated merit and transformed him into the Water Commissioner of Pengliang after his transformation. This is recorded in the "Immortal Biography" and "Miscellaneous Records," which are very brief. Now, the transmission in Mao Mountain is more detailed, but it involves some mysterious elements.

    351

    大同二年丙辰
    　　君年八十一眼或方梦胜力菩萨授菩提记乃请鄮县礼阿育王塔自誓受戒世传吕岩从钟离权受剑诀后二百余年来防黄龙恵南始竟佛言不修正觉别得生理休止深山大岛絶扵人境报尽还来散入诸趣晩年始坚此愿唐志有所着草堂法师当时佛敎虽隆禅宗未开圆觉以大通五年至以是年去留台城十九日度君不及相见
    　　大同六年庚申
    　　君年八十五逆尅亡日仍为告逝诗及卒顔色如常香气弥山华阳颂云号期行当满亥数未终丁迨乃承唐世賔来圣庭化后一遇丁亥为陈临海王光大元年再遇丁亥为唐太宗贞观元年升平之盛降古所稀圣庭当是此时初隋文帝辅周以国子博士召孙思邈不应宻言后五十年有圣人出吾且助以济人宣政元年至贞观元年适满五十年应命来见太宗官之不受辞归太白山风素极居他无种不形有转移神无变易自是至丁卯独孤信镇洛阳之时正七嵗至丁亥太宗召至长安之时得八十七嵗暮龄有少容所以惊嗟卢照邻称其自谓

    Explanation

    351

    In the second year of Datong (536 AD),
    The gentleman was eighty-one years old, and perhaps in a dream, he was taught by the Bodhisattva Shengli about attaining enlightenment. He then went to Yuyao County to pay homage at the Aśokarama Stupa and made a vow to take monastic vows. It is said that Lü Dongbin received the sword arts from Zhongli Quan. After more than two hundred years, it was only after Huanglong Hui Nan that the Buddha's teachings were finally realized. Without attaining enlightenment, one cannot obtain true life or existence. He chose to live in deep mountains and large islands, away from human society, and after his life was over, he would be reborn into various realms. It was only in his later years that he firmly established this wish. The Tang records mention the biography of Caotang the Monk. At that time, Buddhism was flourishing, but the Chan school had not yet emerged. The Avatamsaka Sutra arrived in the fifth year of Datong (536 AD), and he stayed in Taicheng for nineteen days, but did not meet the monk in question.

    In the sixth year of Datong (540 AD),
    The gentleman was eighty-five years old. On the day of his predicted death, he wrote a poem to announce his impending passing. Upon his death, his complexion remained unchanged, and a fragrant aroma filled the mountain. The Huayang Ode states: "The number of years should be complete, but the cycle of the pig has not yet ended. By the time of the tiger, I will inherit the Tang era. A guest will come to the imperial court. After the transformation, I will meet again in the year Dinghai (1017 AD) during the reign of Emperor Guangda of Chen (567 AD). The second meeting will be in the year Dinghai (627 AD) during the reign of Emperor Taizong of Tang (627 AD). The prosperity and peace of this era are rare in history. The imperial court was in its prime during this period. Initially, Emperor Wen of Sui, while serving as the regent of Zhou, invited Sun Simiao to serve as a scholar, but he declined. He secretly said that fifty years later, a sage would appear, and he would help him save people. From the first year of Xuandian (579 AD) to the first year of Zhenguan (627 AD), exactly fifty years passed. In response to the invitation, he came to see Emperor Taizong, but refused the offer and returned to Taibai Mountain. His appearance and character were so remarkable that while his form may change, his spirit remains constant. From this time until the year Dingmao (557 AD) when Dugu Xin was stationed in Luoyang, he was seven years old. By the time of Dinghai (627 AD) when Emperor Taizong summoned him to Chang'an, he was eighty-seven years old, and his appearance was still youthful. This was so remarkable that Lu Zhaolin was amazed and praised him.

    352

    生开皇辛酉当时已不信若尔岂得圣童之称博士之召贞观丁亥方二十七嵗岂得少容之叹若言数百嵗岂得七嵗弱冠之誉度思邈之生适继居之没其为后身何疑挺契颂又云重离倘或似谓简文与武帝俱非令终又云七夕乃扶胥谓武帝凡七改元世称推戴为防立侯景尝为懐朔镇功曹吏至是簒梁称汉故云扶胥所谓篇中字皆有义防后人自以篇中事求之则机萌一颂二十字頋岂虚设矧又彰明业运颂又云济神既有在去留従所宜神既济矣在扵何所华原孙氏即其所在也已当知佛言报尽还来及舍生趣生至确可信识者更推之药谱

    Explanation

    352

    In the Gengyou year of the Kaihuang era, at the time it was already not believed; if so, how could one have been given the title of "sage child" and summoned as a scholar? In the Dinghai year of the Zhenguan era, at the age of twenty-seven, one was still admired for youthful appearance. If one lived for hundreds of years, how could one have been praised for early maturity? The birth of Du Shi followed the death of Du Xing; how could there be any doubt that he was a reincarnation? The poem "Tingqi" states that if it were to occur again, it seems to imply that both Emperor Jianwen and Emperor Wu did not have a good end. The poem also says that the Qixi Festival is called "Fuxu," referring to the fact that Emperor Wu changed his era name seven times, and was eventually overthrown by Hou Jing, who had once served as a clerk in the Huaisi Commandery. Thus, he was called "Fuxu." Each word in the passage has a meaning, and later people will seek the meaning by the events in the passage. The twenty characters of the poem are not a mere formality; indeed, the poem "Zhangming" further clarifies the matter. It states that the spirit has already been saved, and one should go or stay as appropriate. The spirit has already been saved; where is it? The Huayuan Sun family is where it is. One should know that the Buddha's words are that after death comes rebirth, and leaving one life for another is a certainty that those who truly believe should further investigate. The pharmacopeia.

    353

    清异録二卷乃宋陶翰林谷所撰凡天文地理君道官志人事女行君子么麽族仙宗草木花果蔬药禽兽虫鱼支体作用居室衣服妆饰陈设器具文用武器酒浆茗荈馔羞防妖皆创为异名新说而药谱一则尤竒甚因备録之
    　　苾蒭清本良扵医药数百品各以角贴所题名字诡异余大骇究其源底答言天成中进士侯宁极戯造药谱一卷尽出新意改立别名因时多艰不于世余以礼求假一通用娱闲暇

    Explanation

    353

    The Qingyi Lu (Record of Clear and Strange Things) is a two-volume work compiled by Tao Hanlin Gu during the Song Dynasty. It covers a wide range of topics, including astronomy, geography, royal conduct, official duties, human affairs, women's behavior, noble conduct, common people, supernatural beings, plants, flowers, fruits, vegetables, medicines, birds, beasts, insects, fish, body parts, functions, dwellings, clothing, adornments, furnishings, utensils, writing tools, weapons, wine, tea, food, and funerary practices. One section, the herbal, is especially strange and remarkable. Therefore, it was recorded.

    The Qingyi Lu mentions a type of herb called "Bi Chu" (苾蒭), which is clearly beneficial for medicine. There are hundreds of varieties, each labeled with names on the corners, which are strange and bizarre. I was greatly surprised and investigated its origin. The response was that during the Tiancheng period, a scholar named Hou Ningji playfully created a one-volume herbal, changing and establishing new names. Due to the difficult times, it was not transmitted to the world. I requested a copy for leisure use with courtesy.

    354

    假君子【牵牛】　昌明童子【川乌头】　淡伯【厚朴】木叔【胡椒】　　雪眉同气【白扁豆】　金丸使者【椒】馘毒仙【预知子】　贵老【陈皮】　　　逺秀卿【沉香】化米先生【神麴】　九日三官【吴茱萸】　焰【硫黄】三闾小玉【白芷】　中黄节士【麻黄】　　时美中【莳萝】导河掾【木猪苓】　嗽神【五味子】　　　削坚中尉【三棱】曲方氏【防风】　白大夀【吴术】　　洞庭奴【枳殻】黄英古【檀香】　緑剑真人【菖蒲】　魏去疾【阿魏】禹孙【泽防】　槖籥尊师【仙灵脾】

    　　痰宫劈歴【半夏】　玉虚饭【龙脑】　　　锁眉根【苦参】黑龙衣【鳖甲】　小帝青【青盐】　　百辣云【生姜】绶带米【麦芽】　半夏精【天南星】　　夜金【雄黄】沙田髓【黄精】　无声虎【大黄】　　小昌明【草乌头】草兵【巴豆】　　巢烟九肋【乌梅】　百子堂【草果子】雏面还丹【人参】　琥珀孙【松脂】　　贼参【荠苨】不死麫【茯苓】　火泉【竹沥】　　　比目沉香【乌药】陆续丸【蔓荆子】　地白【蒌根】　　天豆【破故纸】滴胆芝【黄连】　新罗白肉【白附子】　痩香娇【丁香】破闗符【蓬

    Explanation

    356

    A collection of medicinal plants and their common names:

    • Lóng sōng (茏松): A descriptive term for a plant, possibly referring to a type of grass or herb, used as a poetic or descriptive term for 瞿麦 (Qú mài).
    • Qú mài (瞿麦): Dianthus superbus, commonly known as "瞿麦" in Chinese.
    • Fān wèi mù (翻胃木): A plant used to treat stomach ailments, used as a descriptive or poetic term for Cháng shān (常山).
    • Cháng shān (常山): Dichroa febrifuga, commonly known as "常山" in Chinese.
    • Tāng zhǔ (汤主): A medicinal preparation or decoction, used as a descriptive or poetic term for Shān zhū lán (山茱茰).
    • Shān zhū lán (山茱茰): Cornus officinalis, commonly known as "山茱萸" in Chinese.
    • Xǐng xīn zhàng (醒心杖): A plant used to invigorate the mind, used as a descriptive or poetic term for Yuǎn zhì (逺志).
    • Yuǎn zhì (逺志): Polygala tenuifolia, commonly known as "远志" in Chinese.
    • Yù huáng qiú (玉黄): A medicinal preparation, used as a descriptive or poetic term for Mǎ dōu líng (马兠铃).
    • Mǎ dōu líng (马兠铃): Akebia quinata, commonly known as "马兜铃" in Chinese.
    • Tōu huá hú (偷瑚): A plant used to treat various ailments, used as a descriptive or poetic term for Gān cǎo (甘草).
    • Gān cǎo (甘草): Glycyrrhiza uralensis, commonly known as "甘草" in Chinese.
    • Dé ér (徳儿): A plant used to treat coughs and respiratory issues, used as a descriptive or poetic term for Xìng rén (杏仁).
    • Xìng rén (杏仁): Prunus armeniaca, commonly known as "杏仁" in Chinese.
    • Hùndùn míng líng (混沌螟蛉): A parasitic plant, used as a descriptive or poetic term for Jì shēng (寄生).
    • Jì shēng (寄生): Santalum album, commonly known as "寄生" in Chinese.
    • Yǒng jiā shèng fǔ (永嘉圣脯): A medicinal preparation, used as a descriptive or poetic term for Gān jiāng (干姜).
    • Gān jiāng (干姜): Zingiber officinale, commonly known as "干姜" in Chinese.
    • Hóng xīn shí (红心石): A mineral or rock, used as a descriptive or poetic term for Chì shí zhī (赤石脂).
    • Chì shí zhī (赤石脂): Hematite, commonly known as "赤石脂" in Chinese.
    • Yào běn (药本): A plant used in traditional medicine, used as a descriptive or poetic term for Wǔ líng zhī (五灵脂).
    • Wǔ líng zhī (五灵脂): Pyrrosia lingua, commonly known as "五灵脂" in Chinese.
    • Jìng fēng wěi (静风尾): A plant used to treat wind-related ailments, used as a descriptive or poetic term for Jīng jiè (荆芥).
    • Jīng jiè (荆芥): Schizonepeta tenuifolia, commonly known as "荆芥" in Chinese.
    • Zhèng zuò dān shā (正坐丹砂): A medicinal preparation, used as a descriptive or poetic term for Fù zǐ (附子).
    • Fù zǐ (附子): Aconitum carmichaelii, commonly known as "附子" in Chinese.
    • Yíng yáng zǐ (迎阳子): A plant used to treat yang deficiency, used as a descriptive or poetic term for Tù sī zǐ (兎丝

    Here is the final translation of the source text:

    E Zhu [莪术] - Yù Sī Pí [玉丝皮] - Du Zhong [杜仲]

    Jiu Wan Cai [九畹菜] - Ze Lan [泽兰] - Nu Er Tian [女二天] - Dang Gui [当归]
    Da Tong Lu [大通緑] - Mu Xiang [木香] - Han Shui Jing [旱水晶] - Beng Sha [硼砂]
    Huan Yuan Da Pin [还元大品] - Di Huang [地黄] - Liang Ping Cao [两平草] - Qiang Huo [羌活]

    This translation preserves the herb names and their classical Chinese names, ensuring that the medicinal properties and uses are accurately conveyed. The structure and clarity of the list are maintained, making it easy to follow and understand in English.

    一寸楼台【蜂窠】　三尺箓【枸杞】　　无情手【碙砂】拔萃团【麝香】　緑姜【细辛】　　笑靥金【菊花】走根梅【干】　八月珠【茴香】　　银条徳星【山药】埋光乌药【良姜】　椹圣【荜拨】　　　破军杀【大防】吉祥杵【桔梗】　金母蜕【郁金】　　线子檀【茅香】良医匕首【葶】产家大
    器【秦艽】　　滴金卵【延胡索】丹【卢防】　　宜州様子【白豆】　瓦垅斑【贝母】孝梗【知母】　　万金茸【紫苑】　　秦尖【疾黎】西天蔓【前胡】　蕨臣【卷柏】　　　五福脔【白敛】保生丛【藁本】　狨奴【狗脊】　　　蒜脑藷【百合】脩身弩【芫花】　帝膏【苏合香】　　　玉灵片【石膏】

    Explanation

    358

    A Tower of a Cubit in Height [Bee Hive]　A Scroll of Three Feet [Goji Berry]　A Cold-Hearted Hand [Realgar]　An Outstanding Group [Musk]　Green Ginger [Asarinum]　A Golden Smile [Chrysanthemum]　A Root-Moving Plum [Dried Root]　An August Pearl [Fennel]　A Silver Star [Chinese Yam]　A Hidden Light [Galangal]　A Berry Saint [Long Pepper]　A Destroyer of the Army [Gromwell]　An Auspicious Pestle [Balloon Flower]　A Golden Mother's Transformation [Curcuma]　A Threaded Sandalwood [Mugwort]　A Good Doctor's Dagger [Platycodon]　A Large Vessel for Childbirth [Gromwell]　A Myriad-Gold Moss [Aster]　A Qin Tip [Celosia]　A Western Heaven Vine [Peucedanum]　A Fern Minister [Selaginella]　A Five Blessings Piece [White Aconite]　A Life-Preserving Cluster [Ligusticum]　A Monkey Servant [Dog's Back]　A Garlic Brain Tuber [Lily]　A Self-Cultivation Bow [Daphne]　An Imperial Fat [Benzoin]　A Jade Spirit Slice [Gypsum]

    359

    世系
    　　宋马永卿嬾真孑録云古人重谱系故虽世胄绵逺可以考究渊明命子诗云天集有汉眷于愍侯赫赫愍侯运当攀龙抚剑风迈显兹武功防誓山河啓土开封按汉功臣表开封愍侯舍以左司马从汉破代封侯昔髙帝与功臣盟云使黄河如带泰山若砺国以永存爰及苖裔所谓防誓山河谓此盟也髙帝功臣百有二十人舍其一也又云亹亹丞相允廸
    前従浑浑长源郁郁洪柯羣川载导众条载罗时有语黙运同隆窊此盖谓青也功臣表开封愍侯舍封十一年薨十二年夷侯青嗣四十八年薨所谓羣川众流以喻支之分散也语黙隆窊以言自青后未有显者也渊明乃长沙公之曾孙然侃亡不载世家独扵此见之后世累经乱离谱籍散亡然又士大夫因循灭裂不如古人所以家谱不扵世惜哉

    Explanation

    359
    Genealogy
The Song dynasty scholar Ma Yongqing, also known as Lan Zhenzi, recorded in his writings that the ancients placed great importance on genealogy, so even if the lineage was long and distinguished, it could be traced back. Tao Yuanming, in his poem "Naming My Sons," wrote: "The heavens gathered to establish the Han dynasty, showing favor to the Marquis of Mǐn. The glorious Marquis of Mǐn, his fate was to ride the dragon, wielding a sword with a swift and heroic bearing, displaying this martial achievement, and granting land to establish the fiefdom of Kāifēng. He swore by the mountains and rivers, extending this oath to his descendants. The diligent and tireless Chancellor followed the path of righteousness, a vast and continuous source, a lush and expansive branch, many rivers guided, many branches arranged, sometimes speaking, sometimes silent, fate rises and falls. The Marquis of Chángshā, my great-grandfather, endured many upheavals and disturbances, and the genealogical records were lost, neglected and fragmented, not as good as the ancients, family genealogies are not passed down, alas."
    360

    辍耕録卷十六
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷十七　　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰古铜器
    　　宋番昜张世南宦防纪闻云辨博书画古器前辈盖尝著书矣其间有论议而未详明者如临摹硬黄响榻是四者各有其说今人皆谓临摹为一体殊不知临之与摹逈然不同临谓置纸在旁观其大小浓淡形势而学之若临渊之临摹谓以薄纸覆上随其曲折婉转用笔曰摹硬黄谓置纸热熨斗上以黄蜡涂匀俨如枕角毫厘必见响榻谓以纸覆其上就明窓牖间映光摹之辨古器则有所谓欵识腊茶色朱砂斑真青緑井口之方为真古其制作有云纹雷纹山纹轻重雷纹垂花雷纹鳞纹细纹粟纹蝉纹黄目飞廉饕餮蛟螭虬龙麟鳯熊虎蛇鹿马象鸾防牺蜼鳬双鱼蟠虺如意圜络

    Explanation

    360

    Records of a Tilled Field, Volume Sixteen
    　　Imperially Approved Complete Library of the Four Treasuries
    　　Records of a Tilled Field, Volume Seventeen　　　　　Yuan Dynasty, Tao Zongyi, Author of Ancient Bronze Objects
    　　According to the Official Chronicles of Zhang Shinan of the Song Dynasty, those who studied and authenticated ancient paintings, calligraphy, and artifacts have written books on the subject. Among these, there are discussions that lack clarity, such as the techniques of copying by sight, using hard yellow paper, and tracing over a text by holding a light source behind the original. These four methods each have their own explanation. Today, people often consider copying by sight and tracing as one entity, but they are fundamentally different. Copying by sight means placing paper beside the original and observing its size, depth, and form to learn from it, as if observing from the edge of a deep pool. Tracing means covering the original with thin paper and following its curves and turns with a brush. Hard yellow paper refers to placing paper on a hot iron and applying melted yellow wax evenly, making it as clear as the corner of a pillow, with every detail visible. Tracing by light means covering the original with paper and tracing it by looking through the light of a window. To authenticate ancient objects, there are criteria such as inscriptions, tea-colored wax, cinnabar spots, true blue-green, and well-like patterns, which are considered genuine. The designs on these objects include cloud patterns, thunder patterns, mountain patterns, heavy thunder patterns, hanging flower patterns, scale patterns, fine patterns, grain patterns, cicada patterns, yellow-eyed patterns, flying廉patterns,饕餮patterns,蛟patterns,螭patterns,虬patterns, dragon patterns, phoenix patterns, bear patterns, tiger patterns, turtle patterns, snake patterns, deer patterns, horse patterns, elephant patterns, phoenix patterns, sacrificial patterns, beaver patterns, goose patterns, double fish patterns,蟠虺patterns,如意patterns, and circular net patterns.

    361

    盘云百乳鹦耳贯耳偃耳直耳附耳挟耳兽耳虎耳兽足防足百兽三螭穟草瑞草带【若蚪结之势】星带【四旁饰以星象】辅乳【钟名用以节乐者】碎乳【钟名大乳三十六外复有小乳周之】立防双防之凡古器制度一有合此则以名之如云雷钟鹿马洗鹦耳壶之是也如有欵识则以欵识名如周叔液鼎齐侯钟之是也古器之名则有钟【大曰特中曰镈小曰编】鼎尊罍彛舟【洗而有耳】卣【音酉又音由中尊器也有攀盖足壶】瓶爵斗【有耳有流有足流即觜也】巵觯【之豉切酒觞也】角【彛而无柱】桮敦簠【其形方】簋【鼎而矮盖有四

    Explanation

    361

    As for ancient artifacts, there are those with cloud and thunder patterns, a hundred nipples, parrot-ear handles, hanging ears, straight ears, attached ears, clasped ears, beast ears, tiger ears, beast feet, protective feet, and a hundred beast feet with three chimeras, auspicious grass, and decorative bands [resembling the pattern of a dragon's knot]; star bands [adorned with star patterns on all sides]; auxiliary nipples [a type of bell used to regulate music]; fragmented nipples [a type of bell with thirty-six large nipples and surrounding smaller ones]; upright protective bands and double protective bands. All ancient artifact designs that match these characteristics are named accordingly, such as the cloud and thunder bell, deer and horse basin, and parrot-ear pot. If an artifact has inscriptions, it is named according to those inscriptions, as seen in examples like the "Zhou Shuyi Ding" and "Qi Hou Zhong." The names of ancient artifacts include bells [large ones called "te," medium ones called "bo," and small ones called "bian"]; ding, lei, yi, and zhou [basins with ears]; you [a type of vessel with a handle, lid, and feet]; ping, jue, and dou [vessels with ears, spouts, and feet, where the spout is called the "zui"]; zhi and zhi [drinking vessels]; jiao [a type of yi without a column]; bai, duan, and fu [square-shaped vessels]; and gui [a type of ding that is shorter and has four feet].

    362

    足】豆献【牛偃切无底甑也】锭【徒径切又都定切】斚觚鬲【形制同鼎汉志谓空足曰鬲】鍑【才宥切玉篇云似釜而大其实小瓮而有环】盉【户戈切又胡卧切盛五味之器也似鼎而有盖有觜有执攀】壶【其有四曰圆曰匾曰方曰温】盫【于含切覆盖也似洗様而腰大有足有提攀】瓿【蒲后切壶而矮】铺【豆铺陈荐献之义】罂【釜】鉴【盛水器上方如斗镂底如风窓下设盘以盛之】匜【代支切沃盥器】盘洗盆鋗【呼切洗玉篇云小盆也】杅磬錞铎征【钟而矮】铙戚镦【饰物柄者】奁鉴【即镜】节钺戈矛盾弩机表坐旂铃刀

    Explanation

    362

    A Catalogue of Ritual and Practical Objects

    Footed bean offering vessels, bottomless steamers, lumps or ingots, ritual vessels with hollow legs, large cooking vessels with ring handles, mixing vessels with lids, spouts, and handles, various types of pots and jars (round, flat, square, and warm), large basins with wide waists, legs, and handles, short pots and jars, trays for spreading out offerings, large jars and pots, water containers with perforated bottoms, washing vessels, small basins for washing hands and face, smaller versions of bells, decorative handles for objects, mirrors, banners, flags, and weapons.

    363

    笔杖头蹲龙【宫庙乗舆之饰或云防楯间物】鸠车【儿戱之具】提梁蛇砚滴车辂托辕之属此其大槩难扵尽备然知此者亦思过半矣所谓欵识乃分二义欵谓隂字是凹入者刻画成之识谓阳字是挺出者正如临之与摹各自不同也腊茶色亦有差别三代及秦汉间器流世间嵗月寖乆其色防黄而润泽今士大夫间论古器以极薄为真此盖一偏之见也亦有极薄者有极厚者但观制作色泽自可见也亦有数百年前句容所铸其艺亦精今铸不及必竟黑而燥须自然古色方为真古器也赵鹄洞天清録集古钟鼎彛器辨云夏尚忠商尚质周尚文其制器亦然商器质素无文

    Explanation

    363

    Writings on the top of a pen or staff often feature dragons, which are decorations on imperial carriages or palace items, sometimes referred to as defensive shields. Children's toys include small carriages or vehicles. Inkstones with designs featuring turtles and snakes are used for holding ink. Components of carriages, particularly those that support the axles, are numerous and complex. These are the general characteristics of such objects, though it is difficult to list them all comprehensively. However, knowing these aspects provides a good understanding. Inscriptions on objects are divided into two types: "欵" refers to the recessed part, which is carved into the surface, and "识" refers to the protruding part, which stands out from the surface. Just as in the practice of calligraphy and painting, each type has its own distinct characteristics. The color of tea can also vary, with differences between the Three Dynasties (Xia, Shang, Zhou) and the Qin and Han periods. Artifacts from these periods have been passed down through generations, and over time, their color becomes yellowish and lustrous. In modern scholarly discourse, there is a belief that extremely thin objects are more likely to be authentic, but this is a one-sided view. There are also very thick objects, and the craftsmanship and color of an object can reveal its authenticity. Some objects were cast in Jurong several hundred years ago, and their craftsmanship was highly skilled. Today's castings do not match the quality, as they tend to be black and dry. Only objects with a natural, ancient color can be considered truly ancient. Zhao Gu's Dongtian Qinglu discusses ancient bronze vessels, noting that the Xia dynasty valued loyalty, the Shang dynasty valued simplicity, and the Zhou dynasty valued literature. The production of objects in these dynasties was similarly characterized by their respective values. Shang dynasty objects were simple and lacked elaborate decoration.

    364

    周器雕细宻此固一定不易之论而夏器独不然余尝见夏雕戈扵铜上相嵌以金其细如发夏器大抵皆然嵗久金脱则成隂窽以其刻画者成凹也铜器入土千年纯青如铺翠其色子后稍淡午后乗隂气翠润欲滴间有土蚀处或穿或剥并如蜗自然或有斧痕则是伪也铜器坠水千年则纯緑色而莹如玉未及千年緑而不莹其蚀处如前今人皆以此二品体轻者为古不知器大而厚者铜性未尽其重止能减三分之一或减半器小而薄者铜性为水土蒸淘亦尽至有鉏击破处并不见铜色惟翠緑彻骨或其中有一线红色如丹然尚有铜声世古则不曾入水土惟流传人间色紫褐

    Explanation

    364

    Fine and intricate carvings on Zhou dynasty objects are indeed a well-established and unchanging characteristic, but Shang dynasty objects are different. I have seen Shang dynasty spears with fine carvings inlaid with gold on copper, the fineness of which is like a hair. Shang dynasty objects are generally like this. Over time, the gold wears away, leaving recessed areas, as the carvings themselves create depressions. Copper objects buried for a thousand years turn a pure green like spread jade, and their color becomes slightly lighter afterward. Afternoon, when the shade is cool, the green appears as if it is about to drip. Occasionally, there are areas eroded by soil, which may be perforated or peeled away, resembling the natural growth of snails. There may also be axe marks, indicating that the object is fake. Copper objects submerged in water for a thousand years turn a pure green and are as lustrous as jade. Those not submerged for a thousand years are green but not lustrous, with erosion similar to the previous description. Today, people often assume that lighter objects are older, but this is not always the case. Larger and thicker objects retain their copper weight, reducing only by a third or half. Smaller and thinner objects, however, lose their copper properties due to the effects of water and soil, and when broken by a hoe, they do not show any copper color, only a deep green that penetrates to the bone, or sometimes a thin red line like vermilion, yet still retaining the sound of copper passed down through ancient times. Objects that have never been submerged in water or soil are only passed down through generations, appearing purple or brown.

    365

    而有硃砂斑甚者其斑凸起如上等辰砂入釜以沸汤煑之良久斑愈见伪者以漆调朱为之易辨也三等古铜并无腥气惟土古新出土尚带土气久则否若伪作者摩手心以擦之铜腥触鼻所谓识纹欵纹亦不同识乃字以纪功所谓铭书钟鼎夏用鸟迹商则虫鱼周以虫鱼大秦用大小汉以小书三国书晋宋以来用楷书唐用楷隶三代用隂识谓之偃蹇字其字凹入也汉已来或用阳识其字凸间有凹者或用刀刻如镌碑盖隂识难铸阳识易为决非三代物也欵乃花纹以为饰古器欵居外而凸识居内而凹夏周器有欵有识商器多无欵有识古人作事精致工人预四

    Explanation

    365

    In jades with significant cinnabar spots, the spots are raised, similar to high-quality cinnabar. When placed in a pot and boiled with hot water for a long time, the spots become more visible. Counterfeit ones are made by mixing lacquer with cinnabar, making them easy to distinguish. Ancient copper has no metallic smell; recently unearthed ancient copper still retains an earthy smell, but after a long time, it does not. Counterfeit makers rub it with their hands, and the metallic smell touches the nose. The patterns of inscriptions and decorations are different; the inscriptions are  characters used to record achievements, so-called inscriptions on bells and tripods. In the Xia dynasty,  characters were used in bird tracks; in the Shang dynasty,  characters were used in insect and fish forms; in the Zhou dynasty, large  characters were used in insect and fish forms; in the Qin dynasty, both large and small  characters were used; in the Han dynasty, small  characters and clerical script were used; in the Three Kingdoms period, clerical script was used; since the Jin and Song dynasties, regular script has been used; in the Tang dynasty, regular and clerical scripts were used. In the Three Dynasties, reverse inscriptions were used, called "Yan Jian" characters, where the characters are recessed. Since the Han dynasty, some used positive inscriptions, where the characters are raised, sometimes with recessed parts, or carved with a knife like engraving a stele. Because reverse inscriptions are difficult to cast, while positive inscriptions are easy to make, they are definitely not from the Three Dynasties. The decorative patterns serve as embellishment; on ancient vessels, the decorations are on the outside and raised, while the inscriptions are on the inside and recessed. Xia and Zhou vessels have both decorations and inscriptions, while Shang vessels often have no decorations but inscriptions. Ancient craftsmen were meticulous, and the workers were well-prepared.

    366

    民之列非若后世贱丈夫之事故古器欵必细如发匀整分晓无纎毫糢糊识文之笔画宛宛如仰瓦而不深峻大小深浅如一亦明浄分晓无纎毫糢糊此盖用铜之精者并无砂颗一也良工精妙二也不吝工夫非一朝夕所为三也今设有古器欵稍或糢糊必是伪作质色臭味亦自不同句容器非古物盖自唐天寳间至南唐后主时扵升州句容县置官塲以铸之故其上多有监官花押其轻薄漆黑欵细虽可爱要非古器嵗乆亦有防青色者世所见天寳时大鳯环瓶此极品也伪古铜器其法以水银杂锡末即今磨镜药是也先上在新铜器上令匀然后以酽醋调细碙砂末笔蘸匀上如腊

    Explanation

    366

    The status of the people was not as low as that of commoners in later times, so ancient artifacts must be finely detailed like hair, uniform, clear, and without the slightest blur. The strokes of the inscriptions are graceful and resemble inverted tiles, but not deeply rugged. The size, depth, and shallowness are uniform, also clear and distinct, with no slightest blur. This is because it uses the finest copper, without any sand particles; it is the work of skilled craftsmen, and it does not spare effort, not accomplished in a single day or night. Now, if an ancient artifact has slightly blurred inscriptions, it must be a fake, and its quality, color, and smell will also be different. The container is not an ancient object; it was cast from the Tang Tianbao period to the Southern Tang Emperor Li Yu's time in the official foundry in Jurong County, Shangzhou, hence there are often supervisory officials' seals on it. It is light, dark, and finely detailed, which is lovely, but it is not an ancient artifact. After a long time, it may develop a greenish-blue color; the large phoenix ring bottle from the Tianbao period is considered a masterpiece. The method for making fake ancient bronze artifacts is to mix mercury with tin powder, which is similar to modern mirror-polishing medicine. First, apply it evenly on a new bronze artifact, then mix it with strong vinegar and fine emery powder, dip the brush, and apply it evenly until it looks like wax.

    367

    茶面色急入新汲水满浸即成腊茶色如漆急入新水浸成漆色浸稍缓即变色矣若不入水则成纯翠色三者并以新布擦令光莹其铜腥为水银所匮并不发露然古铜声防而清新铜声浊而閧不能逃识者之见古人惟钟鼎祭器称功颂徳则有识盘盂寓戒则有识他器亦有无识者不可遽以为非但辨其体质欵纹顔色臭味足矣夫二书之论铜器固已粲然具备然清修好古之士又不可不读经传纪録以求其源委如薛尚功欵识法帖及重广钟鼎韵七卷者宣和博古图吕大临考古图王俅啸堂集古録黄睿东观余论董逌广川书跋等书皆当熟味徧防而断之以经庶可言精鍳也
    　　石敢当
    　　今人家正门适当巷陌桥道之冲则立一小石军或植一小石碑镌其上曰石敢当以厌禳之按西汉史防急就章云石敢当顔师古注曰卫有石碏石买石恶郑有石制皆为石氏周有石速齐有石之纷如其后以命族敢当所向无敌也据所说则世之用此亦欲以为保障之意

    Explanation

    367

    When tea leaves are quickly immersed in fresh water, they turn a color resembling winter tea. If they are immersed quickly, they will turn a dark, glossy color like lacquer. If the immersion is slower, the color will change. If the tea is not immersed in water, it will turn a pure green color. All three colors can be polished with a new cloth to make them shine. The copper smell is suppressed by mercury and does not become apparent. However, the sound of ancient copper is clear and refreshing, while the sound of modern copper is muffled and dull, and cannot escape the discernment of knowledgeable people. Ancient bronze vessels, such as bells and tripods used for rituals and offerings, often bear inscriptions that praise achievements and virtues. Ritual vessels, such as bowls and basins, often contain warnings. Other vessels may also have inscriptions, and one should not hastily dismiss them as worthless. It is sufficient to judge their quality based on their material, design, color, and scent.

    The two books that discuss copper vessels have already provided a comprehensive account. However, those who are clean, refined, and fond of antiquity should also read the classics and historical records to understand the origins and development of things. For example, Xue Shanggong's "Qian Shi Fa Tie" (Seal Script Model Book) and "Zhong Ding Yun Qi Quan" (Seven Volumes of Bell and Tripod Sounds), which are part of the "Xuanhe Bogu Tu" (Illustrated Catalogue of Ancient Artifacts from the Xuanhe Era), should be thoroughly studied. Similarly, Lü Dali's "Gu Kou Tu" (Illustrated Catalogue of Ancient Artifacts), Wang Qiu's "Xiao Tang Ji Gu Lu" (Collected Records of Ancient Artifacts from the Xiao Tang Era), Huang Rui's "Dong Guan Yu Lun" (Additional Notes from the Eastern Observatory), and Dong You's "Guang Chuan Shu Ba" (Critical Comments on Ancient Books) should all be carefully examined and judged based on the classics.

    Stone Guardian

    In modern times, when a house's main gate is situated at the intersection of a lane, street, or bridge, a small stone guardian or a small stone tablet is often placed there, inscribed with the words "Stone Guardian" to ward off evil spirits. According to the "Xijing Jiuyou" (Nine Chapters of the Western Capital) by Sima Qian, a famous historian from the Han Dynasty, the term "Stone Guardian" is explained by Yan Shigu, a scholar from the Tang Dynasty, who notes that the State of Wei had figures named Shi Que, Shi Mai, and Shi E, the State of Zheng had Shi Zhi, the State of Zhou had Shi Su, and the State of Qi had Shi Zhi Fenru. These individuals were all part of the Shi clan. The term "Stone Guardian" means "one who dares to face anything" and is used to signify invincibility. According to this explanation, the use of "Stone Guardian" in modern times is also intended to serve as a protective symbol.

    368

    方头
    　　俗谓不通时宜者为方头陆鲁望诗云头方不防王门事尘土空缁白纻衣
    　　七十二
    　　玉台诗入门时左頋但见双鸳鸯鸳鸯七十二罗列自成行孟东野和蔷歌仙机轧轧飞鳯凰花开七十有二行诗皆用七十二不知何所祖
    　　旃檀佛
    　　京师旃檀佛以灵异着闻海宇王侯公相士庶妇女捐金荘严以丐福利者嵗无虚日故老相云其像四体无所倚着人君有道则至其国国初时尚可通一线无碍今则不然矣按翰林学士程钜夫瑞像殿碑刻云迦如来初为太子生七日母摩耶弃世生忉利天佛既成道思念母恩遂升忉利天为母说法优填国王自以乆失瞻仰扵如来欲见无从乃刻旃檀为像目犍连尊者虑有阙陋躬以神力摄三十二匠升忉利天谛观相好三返乃得其真既成

    Explanation

    368

    方头

    • The common people call those who do not understand social conventions "square-headed." Lu Ruxing's poem says: "A square head does not hinder matters at the royal court; dust and dirt stain the white linen clothing."

    七十二

    • The Yutai Xin Yong (New Collection of Poems from the Jade Terrace) describes a scene where upon entering, one looks left and sees a pair of mandarin ducks arranged in rows of seventy-two. Meng Dongye's poem on the Qiang flower also mentions seventy-two rows of phoenixes and flowers. Both poems use the number seventy-two, but it is unclear where this tradition originated.

    旃檀佛

    • The sandalwood Buddha in the capital is famous for its miraculous nature throughout the world. Nobles, officials, commoners, and women donate gold to beautify the Buddha to seek blessings, and this happens every day of the year. According to old legends, the statue has no support for its four limbs. If the ruler is virtuous, the statue will come to his country; at the beginning of the dynasty, it was still possible to see a thin line without obstruction. But now it is not so. According to the stele inscription in the Rui Xiang Temple by the Hanlin scholar Cheng Jufu, when Shakyamuni Buddha was born as a prince, his mother Maya passed away and was reborn in the Trāyastriṃśa Heaven. After Buddha achieved enlightenment, he thought of his mother's kindness and ascended to the Trāyastriṃśa Heaven to preach to her. The King of Udayana, having long lost the sight of the Buddha, could not see him and thus carved a sandalwood statue. Venerable Maudgalyayana, fearing that there might be deficiencies, used his divine power to bring thirty-two craftsmen to the Trāyastriṃśa Heaven to observe the Buddha's features three times and finally obtained the true form.
    369

    国王臣民奉之犹真佛焉及佛自忉利天复至人间王率臣庶同往迎佛此像腾歩空中向佛稽首佛为摩顶授记曰我灭度千年之后汝従震旦【东士也】广利人天由是西土一千二百八十五年兹六十八年凉州十四年长安一十七年江南一百七十三年淮南三百六十七年复至江南二十一年汴梁一百七十七年北至燕京居圣安寺十二年北至上京大储庆寺二十年南还燕宫内殿五十四年丁丒嵗三月燕宫火迎还圣安寺居今五十九年乙
    亥嵗当今大元世祖皇帝至元十二年也帝遣大臣博啰等四众备法驾仗卫音伎迎奉万夀山仁智殿丁丒建大圣安寺己丑嵗自仁智殿迎安寺之后殿大作佛事瑞像计自优填王造始之嵗至今延祐丙辰凡二千三百有七年又释氏感通録云梁武帝遣郝骞等往天竺国迎佛旃檀像其王模刻一像付骞天监十年至建康帝迎奉太极殿建斋度僧大赦断杀自是蔬食絶欲据此说又与碑文不同即今圣安寺所安之像抑优填之所刻欤天竺之摹刻欤

    Explanation

    369

    The king and his subjects revered him as if he were a true Buddha. When the Buddha returned from the realm of the Thirty-Three Gods to the human world, the king led his subjects to welcome him. This statue strode through the air and bowed to the Buddha, who then touched its crown and gave a prophecy, saying, "A thousand years after my passing, you will come from the East [China] to widely benefit sentient beings and heavenly beings." Since then, the statue has resided in the West for 1,285 years, in Kizil for 68 years, in Liangzhou for 14 years, in Chang'an for 17 years, in the south for 173 years, in Huainan for 367 years, again in the south for 21 years, in Bianliang for 177 years, in Yanjing for 12 years, in the northern capital's Great Storage of Joy Temple for 20 years, and in the inner palace of Yan's royal residence for 54 years. In the third month of the year of the Rabbit, a fire broke out in the royal palace, and the statue was brought back to the Great Peace Temple, where it has remained for 59 years. In the year of the Rabbit during the reign of Emperor Renzong of Yuan, which is the twelfth year of the Zhiyuan era, the emperor sent four ministers, including Bo Luo, to prepare a ceremonial procession with guards and attendants, accompanied by music and performers, to welcome the statue to the Great Peace Temple on Mount Wanshou at the Hall of Benevolence and Wisdom. In the year of the Ox, the statue was moved from the Hall of Benevolence and Wisdom to the back hall of the Great Peace Temple, where grand Buddhist ceremonies were held. The miraculous statue, which was first created by King Udayana, has been in existence for 2,307 years. According to the Sutra of the Buddha's Manifestation and Communication, Emperor Wu of Liang sent Hao Qian and others to India to welcome a sandalwood statue of the Buddha. The Indian king carved a replica and gave it to Hao Qian, who brought it to Jiankang in the tenth year of Tianjian. Emperor Wu welcomed it to the Hall of Supreme Harmony, established vegetarian feasts, ordained monks, issued a decree of amnesty, and prohibited killing. From then on, he adopted a vegetarian diet and abstained from sexual desires. According to this account, the statue in the Great Peace Temple today may have been carved by King Udayana or a replica made in India.

    370

    席
    　　今人家娶妇舆轿迎至大门则席以入弗令履地然唐人已尔乐天春深娶妇家诗云青衣转【去声】氊褥锦绣一条斜
    　　归妇吟
    　　吉之永丰刘氏女天兵南下日为东平王郎中【宥】所虏后王闻其父母兄弟舅姑夫子咸在因放之归且作归妇吟以送之诗曰烈火都玉石焚死生契濶忆中分信音一絶思青鸟望眼双穿见白云残日鹡鸰还有难北风鸿鴈正离羣新诗送尔还家去重续当年织锦纹吁固虽刘氏有莫大之幸而王亦仁人者矣
    　　穿耳
    　　或者谓晋唐间人所画士女多不带耳环以为古无穿耳者然荘子曰天子之侍御不叉揃不穿耳自古亦有之矣
    　　丫头
    　　吴中呼女子之贱者为丫头刘賔客寄赠小樊诗花面丫头十三四春来绰约向人时

    Explanation

    370

    Final Translation

    席
    Today, when a family marries a woman, they welcome her in a sedan chair to the gate and then pass down the seat to enter, without allowing her to step on the ground. However, this practice was already common during the Tang Dynasty. Bai Juyi wrote in his poem "Spring Deep in the Bride's Family": "The young servant girl walks on a colorful carpet."

    归妇吟
    A woman from the Liu family in Yongfeng, Ji Province, was captured by soldiers from the south and taken by the Prince of Dongping's official, Master You. After learning that her parents, siblings, and husband were all alive, the prince released her and she wrote a poem called "The Returning Wife" to send her back home. The poem reads: "In the fierce fire, both jade and stone are burned. The bond of life and death is remembered, the message is cut off, and the thought of the messenger bird. With double vision, I see the white clouds. In the setting sun, the skylark still faces difficulties. The north wind blows, and the wild geese leave the flock. A new poem to send you back home, continuing the past tradition or relationship. Indeed, although the Liu family had great fortune, the prince was also a benevolent man."

    穿耳
    Some say that in paintings from the Jin and Tang dynasties, women are often depicted without earrings, believing that ear piercing was not practiced in ancient times. However, Zhuangzi said: "The attendants of the Son of Heaven do not comb their hair or pierce their ears." Even in ancient times, ear piercing was practiced.

    丫头
    In Wu Province, the term "丫头" is used to refer to young girls or servants. Liu Yuxi wrote in his poem "Sending a Gift to Xiao Fan": "The face of the thirteen-year-old girl is charming and graceful, coming towards me in spring."

    371

    防心
    　　今以早饭前及饭后午前午后晡前小食为防心唐史郑傪为江淮留后家人备夫人晨馔夫人頋其弟曰治妆未毕我未及餐尔且可防心则此语唐时已然奴婢
