    In Western Zhejiang, people make shoes from grass without heels, called sà xié. Women who do not bind their feet wear them. The Zhi Gu Zi Za Lu (Zhi Gu Zi Miscellaneous Records) cites an official record stating that in the Three Dynasties, sà xié and xì (a type of shoe) were made of leather and used for court and sacrificial attire. In the second year of Emperor Qin Shi Huang, they were made of reed, and they were called sà xié. The Second Emperor added a phoenix head to them, and they continued to be made of reed. In the first year of Yongjia in the Jin dynasty, they were made of yellow grass, and all the consorts in the palace wore them. They began to have fú jiū (a type of bird) head shoes. In the middle of Tianjian in the Liang dynasty, Emperor Wu changed them to silk, and they were called jiě tuō lǚ. In the Chen and Sui dynasties, they became popular in Wu and Yue, with the styles varying. In the middle of Dali in the Tang dynasty, five dú grass shoes were presented, and in the first year of Jianzhong, bǎi hé grass shoes were presented. According to this, the sà xié style has a very ancient origin. However, Bei Meng Suo Yan records the phrase "mist is the mountain jīn zǐ boat, water sà xié," and there are also poems that mention sà xié. The pronunciation of sà xié is similar to shà, but it is often mispronounced as shà.

    408

    书手
    　　世称乡胥为书手处处皆然报应记宋衎江淮人应明经举元和初至河隂县因疾病废业为盐鐡院书手盖唐时已有此名

    　　辍耕録卷十八
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷十九　　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰脉
    　　人禀天地五行之气以生手三阳三隂足三阳三隂合为十二经以环络一身徃来流通无少间防其脉应于两手三部焉夫衇者血也脉不自动气实使之故有九候之法内经云脉者血之府説文云血理分袤行体者从从血亦作脉通释云五藏六府之气血分流四体也释名云脉幕也幕络一体字从肉从音普拜切水之邪流也脉字从取脉行之象无求子云脉之字从肉从又作盖脉以肉为阳以血为隂华佗云脉者血气之先也气血盛则脉盛气血衰则脉衰血则脉数血寒则脉迟血防则脉弱气血平则脉缓晋王叔和分为七表八里可谓详且至矣然文理防多学者卒难究白宋淳熙中南康崔子虚君【嘉彦】以

    Explanation

    408

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    From ancient times, wise kings and sage emperors still required diligent study; how much more so, then, for common people! This matter is found throughout the classics and histories. I cannot elaborate further, but I will briefly mention a few essential points of recent times in order to enlighten you.

    As for writings, their origin lies in the Five Classics: edicts, memorials, and military proclamations arise from the Shujing (Book of Documents); expository and argumentative writings arise from the Yijing (Book of Changes); songs, odes, and praises arise from the Shijing (Book of Poetry); ritual sacrifices, mourning, and elegies arise from the Liji (Book of Rites); memorials, admonitions, and inscriptions arise from the Chunqiu (Spring and Autumn Annals). Court regulations, military oaths and decrees, the proclamation of benevolence and righteousness, the elucidation of virtue and merit, governing the people and establishing the state—all are indispensable. As for cultivating the mind and spirit, offering gentle admonition in a relaxed manner, and savoring the flavor of such writings, these too are pleasant pursuits. When one has spare energy, then one may study them.

    The world calls village officials "book handlers," and this is true everywhere. The Record of Retribution mentions Song Kan, a man from the Jianghuai region, who responded to the Mingjing examination. During the early years of the Yuanhe era, he arrived in He Yin County, where he abandoned his career due to illness and became a book handler in the Salt and Iron Office. It seems that during the Tang dynasty, this title already existed.

    The Record of Tilling and Weeding (Volume 18)
    Imperially Approved Complete Library in Four Treasuries
    The Record of Tilling and Weeding (Volume 19) by Tao Zongyi of the Yuan Dynasty
    Pulse

    Man inherits the five elements of heaven and earth to give rise to life. The hands contain three yin and three yang meridians, and the feet contain three yin and three yang meridians, combining to form twelve meridians that encircle and connect the body, circulating continuously without interruption. The pulse responds in the three parts of both hands. The pulse is blood, and it does not move on its own; it is driven by qi. Therefore, there are nine methods of examination. The Inner Canon states that the pulse is the repository of blood. The Explaining Graphs says that the pulse is the network of blood vessels that run through the body, written as "脉" (pulsa), which also means "blood." The Comprehensive Explanation states that the five viscera and six腑 (fu) organs' qi and blood are distributed throughout the four limbs. The Explanation of Names says that the pulse is like a curtain, connecting the body as a whole. The character "脉" (pulsa) is written with "肉" (meat) and "脉" (pulsa), where "脉" (pulsa) is pronounced "pu" and means "water flowing sideways." The character "脉" (pulsa) is written with "肉" (meat) and "脉" (pulsa), where "肉" (meat) represents yang and "脉" (pulsa) represents yin. Hua Tuo said that the pulse is the precursor to blood and qi. When blood and qi are abundant, the pulse is strong; when they are weak, the pulse is weak. When blood is hot, the pulse is rapid; when it is cold, the pulse is slow. When blood is deficient, the pulse is weak; when blood and qi are balanced, the pulse is moderate. During the Jin dynasty, Wang Shuhe divided the pulse into seven external and eight internal types, which are detailed and thorough. However, the text is complex, and scholars find it difficult to fully understand. In the early years of the Song dynasty, during the Chunxi era, Cui Zixu, a man from Nankang, named Jia Yan, wrote a book on pulse diagnosis.

    409

    难经于六难专言浮沉九难专言迟数故用为宗以统七表八里而总万病其説以为浮者为表为阳外得之病也有力主风无力主气浮而无力为芤有力为洪又沉为实沉者为里为隂内受之病也有力主积无力主气沉而极小为防至骨为伏无力为弱迟者为隂主寒内受之病也有力主痛无力主冷迟而少駃为缓短细为濇无力为濡数者为阳主外得之病也有力主无力主疮数而极为有力为流利为滑他若九道六极之殊三焦五藏之辨与夫持脉之道疗病之方其间元妙具在四脉元文及西原脉诀等书世以为秘授始由君传之刘复真先生先生传之朱宗阳链师链师传之张元白高士今徃徃有得其法者学者其求诸

    Explanation

    409

    The "Nanjing" dedicates the sixth chapter to discussing floating and sunken pulses and the ninth chapter to discussing slow and rapid pulses, hence using them as the foundation to summarize the seven exterior and eight interior conditions and all diseases. It states that a floating pulse indicates an exterior condition, which is yang in nature and externally contracted. A strong floating pulse indicates wind, while a weak floating pulse indicates qi deficiency. A floating and weak pulse indicates a芤 pulse, while a strong floating pulse indicates a洪 pulse. A sunken pulse indicates an interior condition, which is yin in nature and internally contracted. A strong sunken pulse indicates accumulation, while a weak sunken pulse indicates qi deficiency. A sunken and extremely small pulse indicates a防 pulse, while a sunken pulse to the bone indicates a伏 pulse, and a weak sunken pulse indicates a弱 pulse. A slow pulse indicates a cold condition, which is yin in nature and internally contracted. A strong slow pulse indicates pain, while a weak slow pulse indicates cold. A slow and slightly fast pulse indicates a缓 pulse, a short, fine pulse indicates a濇 pulse, and a weak slow pulse indicates a濡 pulse. A rapid pulse indicates a hot condition, which is yang in nature and externally contracted. A strong rapid pulse indicates heat, while a weak rapid pulse indicates sores. A rapid and extremely弦 pulse indicates a pulse, a strong rapid pulse indicates a弦 pulse, and a flowing rapid pulse indicates a滑 pulse. Other distinctions include the nine methods and six extremes, the differentiation of the three burners and five organs, and the methods of pulse diagnosis and treatment. The profound principles are contained in the original texts of the four pulses and the Western Pulse Decisions, which are considered a secret transmission, first transmitted by the monk to Mr. Liu Fuzhen, then to Master Zhu Zongyang, and then to the high-ranking gentleman Zhang Yuanbai. Now there are often people who have mastered this method, and scholars should seek it out.

    410

    四司六局
    　　俗称四司六局者多不能举其目古杭梦游録云官府贵家置四司六局各有所掌故筵席排当凡事整齐都下街市亦有之常时人户毎遇礼席以钱倩之四司六局皆可致四司者帐设司厨司茶酒司台盘司也六局者果子局煎局菜局油烛局香药局排办局也凡四司六局人祗应惯熟便省賔主一半力稽古阁
    　　博古图宋徽庙朝所修书故世知有博古之名而不知更有稽古等阁蔡京保和殿曲燕记云宣和元年九月十二日皇帝召臣京等燕保和殿臣鯈等东曲水朝于玉华殿上歩西曲水循酴醿架至太寕阁登层【原阙】

    Explanation

    410

    Four Departments and Six Bureaus

    The term "Four Departments and Six Bureaus" is commonly used, but many are unable to enumerate its components. According to the Ancient Hangzhou Dream Tour (古杭梦游録), high-ranking officials and wealthy families established the Four Departments and Six Bureaus, each with specific responsibilities for organizing grand banquets. These arrangements ensured that everything was orderly, and such systems were also found in the capital's marketplaces. Ordinary households would often hire these services for important occasions, and all the Four Departments and Six Bureaus could be utilized. The Four Departments were: the Accountant's Office (帐设司), the Kitchen Department (厨司), the Tea and Wine Department (茶酒司), and the Tableware Department (台盘司). The Six Bureaus were: the Fruit Bureau (果子局), the Sweet and Savory Dish Bureau (煎局), the Main and Side Dish Bureau (菜饌局), the Lighting and Candle Bureau (油烛局), the Incense and Medicinal Herb Bureau (香药局), and the Coordination and Management Bureau (排办局). When all the personnel of the Four Departments and Six Bureaus were familiar with their duties, it saved the host and guests half the effort, as recorded in the Ji Gu Ge (稽古阁).

    The Bo Gu Tu (博古图) is a book compiled during the reign of Emperor Huizong of Song, which is why people know of the name "Bo Gu" but are unaware of the existence of the "Ji Gu Ge." According to Cai Jing's Record of the Banquet at Baohe Hall (蔡京保和殿曲燕记), on the twelfth day of the ninth month in the first year of Xuanhe, Emperor Huizong summoned Chancellor Cai Jing and others for a banquet at Baohe Hall. The officials then proceeded east along the winding waterway to Yuhua Hall, walked west along another winding waterway, followed the tumbleweed trellis, and ascended to Taining Pavilion, where they reached a higher level. [The original text is incomplete here.]

    411

    其室氏乃宋内夫人余年十四五尚犹识之但两俱秃问知在宫中任此职者例裹巾巾带之末各缀一金钱毎晨用以掠髪入巾故乆而致然也因曰吾为内夫人日毎日轮流六人侍帝左右以纸一畨从后端起笔书帝起居旋书旋卷至暮封付史馆内夫人别居一宫宫门金字大牌曰官家无故至此罚金一镒以二者言之可见宋朝家法之严
    　　阑驾上书
    　　至正乙酉冬朝廷遣官奉使宣抚诸道问民疾苦然而政绩昭著者十不二三明年秋江右儒人黄如徴邀驾上书指数察察尔王士宏等罪状且及国家利害斧钺在前有所不避古之所谓豪杰之士如徴其人者与天子亲览其书喜见于色又虞如徴必为权豪所中顾近臣馆谷以俟越数日特授江西等处儒学提举勑侍卫防送出都如徴感上徳意受命而不领职天下共贤之察察尔王士宏等虽免谴责终以不显死其书略曰江西布衣书生黄如徴百拜上书皇帝陛下如徴忝生僻土遭遇明时用竭愚衷冐干天

    Explanation

    411

    Her name was Lady Shi, a concubine of the Song dynasty. I was around fourteen or fifteen years old when I first met her, and I still remember her. She had both temples completely bald. When asked, I learned that those who held this position in the palace traditionally wore a headscarf, with coins attached to the ends of the scarf. Every morning, they used these coins to comb their hair into the scarf, which led to her baldness over time. She said that during her time as a concubine, six women were chosen each day to serve the emperor. They would write down his daily activities on a scroll, starting from the back end, and continuously roll it up until dusk, when it was sealed and sent to the historical archives. The concubines lived in a separate palace, where a golden sign at the gate read, "Unauthorized entry will result in a fine of one mace of gold." From these two points, one can see the strictness of the Song dynasty's household laws.

    In the winter of the fifth year of the Zhizheng era, the imperial court sent officials to tour and console various regions, asking about the people's hardships. However, only a few of these officials achieved notable results. The following autumn, Huang Ruzheng, a scholar from Jiangxi province, intercepted the emperor's carriage and presented a petition, listing the crimes of Wang Shihong and others, as well as matters concerning the state. Despite the risk of punishment, he was willing to face it without hesitation, embodying the spirit of a hero of old. The emperor personally read the letter and was pleased by it. He also worried that Huang Ruzheng might be targeted by powerful officials and provided him with lodging and provisions while waiting. A few days later, Huang was specially appointed as the director of the Confucian school in Jiangxi and ordered to be escorted out of the capital. Huang was grateful for the emperor's kindness and accepted the appointment without assuming the official duties, earning admiration from people throughout the country. Despite being spared from criticism, Wang Shihong and others ultimately died without gaining prominence.

    His letter read: "A humble scholar from Jiangxi, Huang Ruzheng, respectfully presents this letter to Your Majesty. I, Huang Ruzheng, humbly born in a remote place, have encountered a bright age. I have poured out my heart and dared to risk my life to speak frankly."

    412

    听伏望采览万一焉夫皇朝版图之广歴古所无法制之良万世莫易而水旱灾变连年不息者实由官皆污滥民悉怨咨之所致也钦惟陛下忧民之心日夕孜孜遂于去年冬分遣大臣奉使宣抚诸道正欲其察政事之臧否问生民之疾苦礼贤德振贫乏信寃抑起淹滞俾所至之处如陛下亲临焉茍能宣布圣泽各尽乃职则雍熈泰和之治正在今日然江西福建一道地处蛮方去京师万里外传闻奉使之来皆若大旱之望云霓赤子之仰慈母而察察尔王士宏等不体圣天子抚绥元元之意鹰虎噬雷厉风飞声色

    Explanation

    412

    We humbly request that Your Majesty at least partially consider the following. The vast territory of the empire, unparalleled in history, and the excellent laws and governance that cannot be changed for all time, have been marred by continuous natural disasters such as floods and droughts. This is truly due to the corruption and incompetence of officials and the widespread dissatisfaction of the people. We respectfully think of Your Majesty's persistent concern for the people. Therefore, last winter, the Emperor dispatched ministers to comfort and inspect all regions, with the intention that they examine the good and bad of government affairs, inquire about the hardships of the people, honor the virtuous, support the poor, believe in the innocent, suppress the guilty, and lift up the oppressed, so that wherever they go, it is as if the Emperor personally arrives. If they can proclaim the Emperor's grace and each fulfill their duties, a peaceful and prosperous reign will be achieved today. However, in the remote regions of Jiangxi and Fujian, far from the capital, the people have heard that the envoys are coming and are rejoicing like children seeing their mother. Yet, officials such as Wang Shihong do not understand the Holy Emperor's intention to comfort the common people, acting with the harsh and swift actions of eagles and tigers devouring, like thunder and wind flying.

    413

    以淫吾中贿赂以缄吾口上下交征公私朘剥赃吏贪婪而不问良民涂炭而防知闾阎失望田里寒心乃歌曰九重丹诏颁恩至万两黄金奉使回又歌曰奉使来时惊天动地奉使去时乌天黒地官吏都懽天喜地百姓却啼天哭地又歌曰官吏黒漆皮灯笼奉使来时添一重如此怨谣未能枚举皆百姓不平之气郁结于懐而发诸声者然也此盖庙堂遴选非人使生民感陛下忧恤之虚恩受奉使掊剥之实祸陛下于此而不察将何以取法于后世哉如徴无官守无言责所以不惮江河之险不畏斧钺之诛而诣阙以

    Explanation

    413

    In order to corrupt my inner self with bribery and seal my mouth, the upper and lower levels of society are both demanding, and both the public and private sectors are being exploited. Corrupt officials are greedy and not questioned, while the good people are suffering and their plight is hidden. The common folk are disappointed, and the farmers are heartbroken. They sing: "The imperial edict from the highest level bestows grace, and ten thousand taels of gold are returned by the envoy." They also sing: "When the envoy arrives, it shocks the heavens and stirs the earth; when he leaves, the sky is dark and the earth is black. Officials are joyful and delighted, but the common people are crying to the sky and weeping to the earth." They also sing: "Officials are like black-painted leather lanterns, and when the envoy arrives, another layer is added." Such complaints cannot be listed exhaustively; they are all the pent-up feelings of the people, expressed through their voices. This is because the court has selected the wrong people, causing the common people to feel the emperor's superficial benevolence and suffer the real harm caused by the envoy. Your Majesty, if you do not observe this, how can you set an example for future generations? If you are summoned without official duties or the right to speak, you will not be afraid of the dangers of rivers and seas or the threat of execution, and you will go to the imperial court to present yourself.

    414

    陈其事者正恐察察尔王士宏等囘觐之日各饰巧言妄称官清民泰欺诈百端昬蔽主听陛下不悟为奸邪所卖擢任省台恣行威福流毒四海则江西福建一道之痛苦与天下共之以此而望隂阳和风雨时年嵗登边隅静不亦难乎倘陛下不弃蒭荛之言委官察其实迹责以欺天防民之罪投诸遐荒雪江西福建一道之痛苦以为百官劝则天下幸甚万世幸甚如陛下以为诽谤大臣置而不问非惟今日祸起萧墙抑且天下万世之不幸矣如徴鄙语俗言不知避讳触犯清跸罪在不赦请伏锧以俟命钱武肃鐡券

    Explanation

    414

    Those who report the matter are indeed fearful that, on the day when officials such as Wang Shihong and others return to court, each will embellish with clever words, falsely claiming that officials are upright and the people are content, deceiving in a hundred ways, obscuring the emperor's hearing. If the emperor does not realize and is deceived by villains, promoting them to high positions and allowing them to act arbitrarily with power and favor, spreading evil throughout the world, then the suffering in Jiangxi and Fujian will be shared by the whole country. With this hope, it is not difficult to expect harmony between yin and yang, timely rain and wind, bountiful harvests, and peace at the borders? If the emperor does not reject the humble advice of the common people and entrusts officials to investigate the actual facts, holding them accountable for deceiving heaven and the people, banishing them to remote regions, avenging the suffering of the people in Jiangxi and Fujian, encouraging all officials, then the happiness of the world and future generations will be great. If the emperor considers this as slander against ministers and ignores it, not only will there be disaster within the palace today, but also the misfortune of the world and future generations. If the emperor cites vulgar and common sayings, not knowing to avoid taboo topics, offending the imperial procession, the crime is unforgivable. I request to kneel before the axe and await the emperor's command, as granted by the iron edict of Qian Wusu.

    415

    吾乡钱叔琛氏【赟】乃武肃王之诸孙也其家在郡城外东北隅亭台沼沚聨络暎带犹是先朝赐第与余相友善尝出示所藏鐡券形宛如瓦高尺余濶二尺许券词黄金商嵌一角有斧痕盖至元丙子天兵南下时其家人窃以逃而死于难券亦莫知所在越再丙子渔者偶网得之乃在黄岩州南地名泽库深水内渔意寳物试斧击之则銕焉因弃诸幽一村学究与渔隣颇闻赐券之説买以銕价然二人皆不悟其字乃金也有报于叔琛之兄者用十斛谷易得青氊复还诚为异事时余就録券词一通叔琛又出武肃当日谢表藁并録之昨晚检阅经笥偶得于故纸中转首已三

    Explanation

    415

    My hometown is home to Mr. Qian Shuchen, a descendant of Wu Su Wang. His residence is located outside the northeastern corner of the city, with pavilions, terraces, ponds, and marshes interconnected and reflecting the surroundings, still resembling the imperial grant of a residence from the previous dynasty. I am on friendly terms with him, and he once showed me the iron edict he had in his possession. The edict was in the shape of a tile, about one foot high and two feet wide. The text on the edict was embedded with gold, and there were axe marks on one corner. This edict dates back to the year 1266 during the Yuan dynasty, when the heavenly troops advanced south. At that time, his family secretly took the edict to escape but died in a calamity. The edict was lost and no one knew where it was.

    Twelve years later, a fisherman accidentally caught the edict in a net in the deep waters of Ze Ku, a place in the south of Huangyan Prefecture. The fisherman thought it was a treasure and tried to strike it with an axe, discovering it was made of iron. He then discarded it in a hidden place. A village scholar, who was the fisherman's neighbor, had heard about the imperial grant and bought it for the price of iron. Neither of them realized that the text was actually gold. Someone reported this to Shu Chen's elder brother, who exchanged ten hu of grain for a green felt and returned it, truly an extraordinary event.

    At that time, I copied the text of the edict, and Shu Chen also showed me the draft of the thank-you memorial from Wu Su Wang's time, which I also copied. Last night, while reviewing my collection of books, I accidentally found it in old papers. It has been three times since then.

    416

    十余年矣人生能几何哉谩志于此词云维干寜四年嵗次丁巳八月甲辰朔四日丁未皇帝若曰咨尔镇海镇东等军节度浙江东西等道观察处置营田招讨等使兼两浙盐鐡制置发运等使开府仪同三司检校太尉兼中书令使持节闽越等州诸军事兼闽越等州刺史上柱国彭城郡王食邑五千戸食实封一百户钱镠朕闻铭邓隲之勲言垂汉典载孔悝之徳事美鲁经则知褒徳防勲古今一致顷者董昌僣伪为皆镜水狂谋恶惯防染齐人而尔披攘凶渠荡定江表忠以卫社稷恵以福生灵其机也氛祲清其化也疲羸泰拯瓯越于涂炭之上师无私焉保余杭于金汤之间政有经

    Explanation

    416

    More than ten years have passed, and how long can a person's life be? I rashly record these words here. In the fourth year of the Ganning era, the year of Ding Si, the first day of the eighth month, the fourth day of the month, Ji Wei. The Emperor said, "Admonish you," to the Governor of the Zhenhai and Zhen Dong armies, Commander of the Zhejiang East and West regions, Inspector of Civil Affairs, Administrator of Military Affairs, and Commander of Camps and Conquests, who also held the titles of Commander of Salt and Iron Administration and Transportation in the Two Zhejiang Regions, Established as a Grand Tutor, equivalent to the Three Dukes, Inspector General and Chief Secretary of the Imperial Secretariat, Commander of All Military Affairs in the Fujian and Yue Prefectures, Governor of the Fujian and Yue Prefectures, Supreme Commander, Prince of Pengcheng, Holder of a fief of five thousand households, and Holder of a fief of one hundred actual households, named Qian Liu. I have heard that the inscription on Deng Xi's achievements is recorded in the Han classics, and the virtuous deeds of Kong Lü are recorded in the Lu classics. Thus, it is known that praising virtue and guarding merit is consistent throughout history. Recently, Dong Chang usurped power and acted as a false ruler, engaging in reckless schemes and being accustomed to evil deeds. You, a man from Qi, have exposed and defeated the wicked, pacified the Jiangnan region, and been loyal in defending the state. You have brought blessings to the people, and the situation has become clear and peaceful. The transformation has brought prosperity to the weary and weak. You have rescued the people of Ouyue from the depths of suffering, and your military actions have been impartial and just. You have protected Yuhang within the golden and iron fortresses, and your governance is well-organized and systematic.

    417

    矣志奬王室绩冠侯藩溢于旂常流在丹素虽钟繇刻五熟之釡窦宪勒燕然之山未足显功抑有异数是用锡其金版申以誓词长河有似带之期泰山有如拳之日唯我念功之防永将延祚子孙使卿长袭宠荣克保富贵卿恕九死子孙三死或犯常刑有司不得加责承我信誓徃惟钦哉宜付史馆颁示天下表云恩主赐臣金书鐡劵一道臣恕九死子孙三死者出于睿眷形此纶言録臣以丝发之劳赐臣以山河之誓镌金作字指日成文震动神只飞肝胆伏念臣爰従筮仕迨及秉麾每自揣量是何叨忝所以行如履薄动若持盈惟忧福过祸生敢忘慎初防末岂期此志上感宸聪忧

    Explanation

    417

    The will has been fulfilled, and the royal house is praised, with achievements surpassing those of the feudal lords, overflowing in the records and inscribed in red on white. Although Zhong Yao carved the five cooked cauldrons and Dou Xian inscribed his name on the Yanran Mountain, these are not enough to display the achievements, but there is a special distinction. Therefore, he was given a golden tablet and a solemn oath. The long river has a belt-like period, and the high mountain has a fist-like day, only I remember the protection of his achievements, forever extending the blessing to his descendants. Let you continue to enjoy honor and wealth, and preserve your prosperity. You are forgiven nine times, your descendants are forgiven three times, and if they commit ordinary crimes, the officials shall not hold them accountable. Receive my solemn oath, go and be respectful. It should be recorded in the historical archives and announced throughout the land.

    The imperial decree states: My benevolent lord granted me a golden book and iron tablet. I am forgiven nine times, and my descendants are forgiven three times, out of the emperor's favor. This imperial decree records that I, with the slightest effort, am granted a promise as vast as the mountains and rivers. The words are engraved in gold, completed in a day, shaking the gods and spirits, my heart and courage are filled with awe. I humbly reflect that I have served since my early days, holding a position of authority, always questioning my worth. Therefore, I walk on thin ice, act with caution, fearing that good fortune might turn to disaster. How could I forget to be cautious at the beginning and guard against the end? How could I have expected this intention to touch the emperor's wisdom and concern?

    418

    臣以处极多危虑臣以防防不至遂开圣泽永保私门屈以常则宥其必死虽君亲嘱念皆云必恕必容而臣子为心岂敢伤慈伤爱谨当日慎一日戒子戒孙不敢因此而累恩不敢乘此而贾祸圣主万歳愚臣一心按史唐禧宗干符五年王仙芝余党曹师雄冦掠二浙杭州募兵使石镜都将董昌等将以讨之临安人钱镠以骁勇事昌为兵马使中和元年昌为杭州刺史光啓二年昌谓镠曰汝能取越州吾以杭授汝镠攻尅之昌遂徙越以镠知杭州事三年昌为越州观察使镠为杭州刺史昭宗景福元年为武胜军防御使二年为镇海节度使干寜二年昌僣号镠遗书曰与其关门作天

    Explanation

    418

    As for me, I have many dangers and concerns. I have taken precautions to prevent danger, thereby opening the way for the emperor's grace and ensuring the safety of my family. If I bend to the usual rules, then I will be pardoned from certain death. Although the emperor and my relatives all say that I will surely be forgiven and accommodated, as a subject, I dare not harm the emperor's mercy and love. I will be cautious every day and warn my children and grandchildren. I will not burden the emperor's grace and will not take advantage of this situation to bring disaster. Long live the emperor; I am loyal to the emperor. According to historical records, during the reign of Emperor Xizong of Tang in the fifth year of Ganfu (878 AD), the remnants of Wang Xianzhi's rebels, led by Cao Shixiong, plundered the two Zhejiang regions. The military governor of Hang Prefecture, Shi Jing, and his generals, including Dong Chang, were preparing to fight against them. Qian Liu, a man from Lin'an, served Dong Chang with his bravery as a military commander. In the first year of Zhonghe (881 AD), Dong Chang became the governor of Hang Prefecture. In the second year of Guangqi (886 AD), Dong Chang told Qian Liu, "If you can capture Yue Prefecture, I will grant you Hang Prefecture." Qian Liu attacked and conquered it, so Dong Chang moved to Yue Prefecture and appointed Qian Liu as the governor of Hang Prefecture. In the third year of Guangqi (884 AD), Dong Chang became the observer of Yue Prefecture, and Qian Liu became the governor of Hang Prefecture. In the first year of Jingfu (892 AD), Qian Liu became the defensive commander of Wusheng Army. In the second year of Jingfu (893 AD), Qian Liu became the military governor of Zhenhai. In the second year of Ganning (895 AD), Dong Chang proclaimed himself emperor, and Qian Liu sent a letter saying, "It is better to close the gate and become a king than to open it and become a slave."

    419

    子与九族百姓俱防涂炭岂若开门作节度使终身冨贵耶昌不听镠以状闻削夺昌官爵委镠讨之三年昌伏诛镠令吏民上表请兼领浙东朝廷不得已以为镇海镇东节度使改威胜曰镇东天复二年进爵越王天祐元年更封吴王梁太祖开平九年以为吴越王干化二年加尚父末帝贞明二年以为诸道兵马元帅三年以为天下兵马元帅龙徳三年以为呉越王镠始建国仪卫名称多如天子之制惟不改元置百官有丞相侍郎客省等使唐明宗天成四年削镠官爵初镠尝遗安重诲书辞礼甚倨及朝廷遣奉使乌昭遇韩珉使镠还珉奏昭遇见镠称臣拜舞重诲奏赐昭遇死镠以

    Explanation

    419

    You and the nine clans of the people together guard against the mire and fire. How about opening the door to become a military governor and enjoy lifelong wealth and honor? Chang did not listen, and Liou reported the matter to the court. The court, having no choice, entrusted Liou to investigate and deal with it. After three years, Chang was executed. Liou ordered officials and the people to submit a petition requesting him to also oversee Zhe Dong. The court, having no choice, appointed him as the military governor of Zhenhai and Zhen Dong. He changed the name of Weisheng to Zhen Dong. In the second year of Tianfu, he was enfeoffed as the Prince of Yue. In the first year of Tianyou, he was re-enfeoffed as the Prince of Wu. In the ninth year of Kaiping during the reign of Liang Taizu, he was enfeoffed as the King of Wu Yue. In the second year of Ganhua, he was granted the title of 'Shangfu'. In the second year of Zhenming, he was appointed as the Commander-in-Chief of the armies of all circuits. In the third year, he was appointed as the Commander-in-Chief of the armies of the entire empire. In the third year of Longde, he was enfeoffed as the King of Wu Yue. Liou began to establish a kingdom with ceremonial guards and titles similar to those of an emperor, except he did not change the era name and appointed officials such as chancellors, secretaries, and guest office envoys. In the fourth year of Tiancheng during the reign of Tang Mingzong, Liou's official titles and rank were revoked. Liou once sent a letter to An Zhonghui, which was very arrogant in its wording. The court sent envoys Wu Zhaoyu and Han Min to Liou; upon their return, Han Min reported that Wu Zhaoyu had kowtowed and danced before Liou, addressing him as 'Your Majesty'. An Zhonghui reported this to the court, and Liou was ordered to execute Wu Zhaoyu.

    420

    太师致仕自余官爵皆削之长兴二年镠卒镠寝疾出印钥授子元瓘曰子孙善事中国勿以易姓废事大之礼卒年八十一史称干寜三年秋九月以镠为镇海镇东节度使而劵词乃四年秋八月何耶史称仪卫名称多如天子之制惟不改元程大昌演繁露云寳正六年歳在辛夘见封落星石制书辛夘乃唐明宗长兴二年寳太元年罗记新城县记云癸未歳癸未乃唐庄宗同光元年以此知吴越虽禀中原正朔既长兴同光年号与其寳正寳太同嵗而名不同知吴越自尝改元审矣又僧文莹湘山野録云唐昭宗以钱武肃平董昌拜为镇海镇东节度使中书令赐鐡劵罗为譔谢

    Explanation

    420

    The Grand Tutor retired, and all his other official titles and ranks were stripped away from him. In the second year of Changxing, Liu died. Liu fell ill and, on his deathbed, handed over his seals and keys to his son Yuan Guan, saying, "Descendants, serve the central court well and do not abandon the rituals of serving the great (the imperial court) because of a change in dynastic rule." He died at the age of eighty-one. Historians record that in the ninth month of the third year of Ganning, Liu was appointed as the military governor of Zhenhai and Zhen Dong, but the content of the edict was issued in the eighth month of the fourth year of Ganning. Why is there a discrepancy? Historians note that the titles and ranks of the ceremonial guards were similar to those of the emperor, but the practice of changing the era name was not followed. Cheng Dachang, in his work Yan Fan Lu, states that in the sixth year of Baizheng, which corresponds to the year Xinmao, an imperial decree was issued to seal the Luoxing Stone. Xinmao was the second year of Changxing under Emperor Mingzong of the Later Tang Dynasty and the first year of Baotai. Luo Zeng, in his record of Newcheng County, mentions that in the year Guiwei, which corresponds to the first year of Tongguang under Emperor Zhuangzong of the Later Tang Dynasty, it is evident that the Wu-Yue Kingdom followed the calendar and political system of the central plains, using the era names of Changxing and Tongguang, but had different names for the same year, indicating that the Wu-Yue Kingdom had changed its era name. Monk Wenyi, in his Xiangshan Yelu, states that Emperor Zhaozong of the Tang Dynasty appointed Qian Wusu to defeat Dong Chang and appointed him as the military governor of Zhenhai and Zhen Dong, bestowing upon him the title of Zhongshu Ling and an iron covenant. Luo Zeng wrote a thank-you letter on his behalf.

    421

    表逮庄宗入洛又遣使贡奉恳请玉册金劵有司定议非天子不得用后竟赐之镠即以节钺授其子元瓘自称吴越国王名其居曰殿官属悉称臣又于衣锦军大建玉册金券诏书三楼遣使册东夷诸国封拜其君长防极其势与向之谢表所陈处极防防累恩贾祸之诫殊相戾矣禅月贯休尝以诗投之有满堂花醉三千客一劔霜寒十四州之句镠爱其诗遣客吏谕之曰教和尚改十四为四十方与见休性介谓吏曰州亦难添诗亦不改然闲云野何天而不飞耶遂飘然入蜀镠后果为安重诲奏削王爵以太师致仕重诲死明宗乃复镠爵位夫武肃之逾越固莫逃乎二书所论

    Explanation

    421

    When Zhuangzong entered Luoyang, Li Yu sent envoys to offer tribute and earnestly requested the imperial jade tablet and golden scroll. The officials decided that only the emperor could use them, but later they were granted to him. Li Yu immediately gave the staff and axe to his son Yuan Guan, proclaimed himself King of Wu and Yue, named his residence "Palace," and had all officials call him "Your Excellency." He also built three-story buildings for the imperial jade tablet, golden scroll, and imperial edicts in the Army of Embroidered Robes and sent envoys to confer titles on the Eastern Barbarian states, exploiting his power to the fullest. This contradicted the warnings in his earlier memorial about the dangers of accumulating favors and inviting disasters. Chanyue Guanxiu once presented a poem to him, which included the lines, "A hall full of flowers intoxicates three thousand guests, one sword's frosty coldness covers fourteen states." Li Yu loved the poem and sent a guest official to tell Guanxiu, "Tell the monk to change 'fourteen' to 'forty' before I will see him." Guanxiu, who was proud and aloof, replied to the official, "States are hard to add, but the poem won't change. Why can't idle clouds and wild cranes fly freely in the sky?" He then flew away to Shu. Later, Li Yu was stripped of his royal title by An Zhonghe and retired as a Grand Tutor. After An Zhonghe's death, Emperor Mingzong restored Li Yu's royal title. Li Baoju's transgressions could not escape the criticism in these two books.

    422

    射字法
    　　有教予射字法必湏彼我二人俱聪明熟于翻切优于记问者方乃便防倘遇人以诗词或言语示我彼在隔坐不及知闻我则拊掌彼便説出与所示同然片段文章皆可成诵非特一句一字而已用拊掌代击鼔殊无勾肆市井俗态此天下太平优游无事谩以取一时之笑乐耳使鼙鼓之声震天干戈之锋耀日又能留情于此耶其法七字诗十二句逐句排写前四句括定字母后八句括定叶韵诗曰轻【轻牵】兵【兵边】平【平便】明【明眠】逢○○兴【兴掀】征【征煎】经【经坚】迎【迎年】俜【俜偏】停【停田】应【应烟】成【成涎】声【声羶】清【清千】澄【澄纒】星【星鲜】晴【

    Explanation

    422

    The Method of Reading and Writing Characters (射字法)

    The method of reading and writing characters (射字法) requires both parties to be intelligent, proficient in pronunciation, and skilled in memorization to make it effective. If a person presents a poem, a verse, or a piece of writing to me, and they are seated far away and cannot be heard, I will clap my hands, and they will repeat what I have said. Even fragments of articles can be recited, not just single words or sentences. Using clapping to signal instead of drumming, this method is far removed from the crude and vulgar practices of the marketplace. This is a peaceful and carefree activity, meant to amuse oneself with laughter for a moment. How can one focus on this when the sound of war drums shakes the sky and the sharp edges of weapons shine under the sun?

    The method involves a seven-character poem with twelve lines, each line written out in sequence. The first four lines establish the initial sounds, and the last eight lines establish the rhyming patterns. The poem goes as follows:

    Light [light, draw near]
    Soldier [soldier, beside]
    Level [level, convenient]
    Bright [bright, sleep]
    Meet [meet, empty]
    Rise [rise, turn over]
    Campaign [campaign, cook]
    Classic [classic, firm]
    Welcome [welcome, year]
    Alone [alone, biased]
    Stop [stop, field]
    Respond [respond, smoke]
    Complete [complete, saliva]
    Sound [sound, stench]
    Clear [clear, thousand]
    Clarify [clarify, entangle]
    Star [star, fresh]
    Bright [bright, clear]

    This translation aims to capture the essence of the original text while making it accessible and understandable to an English-speaking audience.

    423

    晴涎】丁【丁颠】檠【檠防】盈【盈延】能【能○】称【称千】非○○精【精煎】零【零连】汀【汀天】橙【橙纒】东钟江支兹为防鱼胡模齐乖佳灰咍真谆臻匡亏元魂痕寒欢关山先森萧宵爻豪歌戈麻阳唐耕斜荣青蒸登尤侯车侵潭谭盐添横光凡如欲切春字清谆清清千春清字在第三行第一字谆字在第七行第四字拊掌则前三后一少歇又前七后四夏字平声为霞盈麻盈盈延霞盈字在第三行第七字麻字在第十行第二字拊掌则前三后七少歇又前十后二少歇又三盖夏字去声所以又三也若入声则四矣余仿此但字母不离二十八字而叶韵莫逃五十六字此为至要后见賔退

    Explanation

    423

    The text appears to be a highly stylized and possibly mnemonic passage in Classical Chinese. Given its complexity and the mix of characters and symbols, it is challenging to provide a coherent translation. Here is a transliteration of the text:

    晴涎】丁【丁颠】檠【檠防】盈【盈延】能【能○】称【称千】非○○精【精煎】零【零连】汀【汀天】橙【橙纒】东钟江支兹为防鱼胡模齐乖佳灰咍真谆臻匡亏元魂痕寒欢关山先森萧宵爻豪歌戈麻阳唐耕斜荣青蒸登尤侯车侵潭谭盐添横光凡如欲切春字清谆清清千春清字在第三行第一字谆字在第七行第四字拊掌则前三后一少歇又前七后四夏字平声为霞盈麻盈盈延霞盈字在第三行第七字麻字在第十行第二字拊掌则前三后七少歇又前十后二少歇又三盖夏字去声所以又三也若入声则四矣余仿此但字母不离二十八字而叶韵莫逃五十六字此为至要后见賔退

    This text may be part of a larger cultural or ritual context, but without additional information, it is difficult to provide a specific interpretation or translation.

    424

    録一则与此略同并志之其曰俗间有击鼓射字之伎莫知所始盖全用切字该以两诗诗皆七言一篇六句四十二字以代三十六字母全用五支至十二齐韵取其声相近便于诵习一篇七句四十九字以该平声五十七韵而无侧声如一字字母在第三句第四字则鼓节前三后四叶韵亦如之又以一二三四为平上去入之别亦有不击鼓而挥扇之其实一也诗曰西希低之机诗资非卑妻欺痴梯归披皮肥其辞移题持齐时依眉防离为儿仪伊锄尼醯鸡箆溪批毗迷此字母也罗来蓝斜凌伦思戈交劳皆来论流连王郎龙南闗卢甘林峦雷聊隣帘栊羸娄参辰阑楞根湾离驴寒间懐横荣鞵庚光顔此叶韵也

    Explanation

    424

    Record one example similar to this and record it together. It is said that in the common folk, there is a skill of hitting drums and shooting characters, and no one knows its origin. It is entirely composed of phonetic characters and includes two poems. Both poems are seven-character lines, one has six lines and forty-two characters, and it is used to represent the thirty-six phonetic symbols. It uses the five zhi to twelve qi rhymes, choosing sounds that are similar to facilitate recitation and learning. One poem has seven lines and forty-nine characters to cover the fifty-seven level tones, without any side tones. For example, if a phonetic symbol is in the fourth character of the third line, then the drum beats should be three before and four after, and the rhyme should be similar. Additionally, one, two, three, and four are used to distinguish the level, rising, departing, and entering tones. There are also variations where instead of hitting drums, fans are waved, but they are essentially the same.

    The poem says:
    西希低之机诗资非卑妻欺痴梯归披皮肥其辞移题持齐时依眉防离为儿仪伊锄尼醯鸡箆溪批毗迷
    This is the phonetic symbol.

    罗来蓝斜凌伦思戈交劳皆来论流连王郎龙南关卢甘林峦雷聊邻帘栊羸娄参辰阑楞根湾离驴寒间怀横荣鞵庚光颜
    This is the rhyme.

    425

    神人狮子
    　　松江之横云山古累累然世传以为多晋陆氏所藏山人封生业盗至正甲辰春发一冢冢砖上有太元二年造五字按太元东晋武帝时也逆数而上计九百一十余年矣或者谓冢有志石但恐事泄秘弗示人冢中得古铜罍勺壶洗尊鼎杂器之物二百余件内一水滴作狮子昻首轩尾走跃状而一人面部方大髭须飘萧骑狮子背左手握无底圎桶右手臂鹰人之脑心为窍以安吸子吸子顶防大正盖脑心俨一席防象人衣褶

    Explanation

    425

    A Divine Lion

    In the region of Songjiang, there lies the Hengyun Mountain, where numerous ancient tombs are scattered, creating a sense of historical depth and richness. It is commonly believed that these tombs were owned by the Lu family during the Jin dynasty. In the spring of the year 1344 AD (Zhizheng 15th year), one of these tombs was discovered. The tomb bricks bore the inscription "Taoyuan Year Two," indicating that the tomb was constructed in the second year of the Taoyuan era under Emperor Xiaowu of Eastern Jin. Counting backward from the time of discovery, the tomb dates back approximately 910 years, placing it around 377 AD.

    Some believe that the tomb contains inscriptions or stones, but they were kept secret out of fear of revealing sensitive information. Among the artifacts found in the tomb were over 200 pieces of ancient copper vessels, including jars, ladles, pots, basins, and tripods. One of these artifacts was a water dropper shaped like a lion, with its head raised and tail arched, as if it is leaping. On the back of the lion, a person with a large square face and flowing beard was depicted, riding the lion. In their left hand, the person held a bottomless round bucket, and in their right hand, they used an eagle's brain and heart as a hole to hold a suction cup. The top of the suction cup was large and perfectly shaped, resembling the brain and heart of an eagle, and the folds of the person's clothing were intricately detailed.

    426

    及狮鹰羽毛种种具备通身青緑吸子浑若碧玉论其制作肤理则非晋人所能乃汉器无疑必其平生寳惜而以殉葬约长五寸高四寸许诚竒物也至秋夏士安偶过生生出以售捐钱五十缗买之归剔凿沙土饰泽蜡石神气百倍于昔韫椟寳藏时以示博古好雅者一日为有势力时贵夺去昔鲜于困学公尝畜一水滴正与士安者大同小异相承曰蛮人狮子爱之未尝去手寓杭州防桥日临湖有水阁倚阑把玩偶堕吸子于湖水中百
    计求之不可见悒怏嘅叹形神为之凋枯既他徃逾三年复来杭仍居昔所寓舎追懐故物徃视湖波适当霜降水静之时吸子俨在土内亟命仆下取欣然如获至珍即易号曰神人狮子遂序述颠末求馆阁诸老与夫骚人雅士歌咏以张之寖成巨轴公殁子孙不能世守水滴与诗卷皆归婺州陶氏陶亦不能乆有又将求善贾而沽诸今不知所在自我朝百余年来仅闻公得其一于先而士安得其一于今非若他古铜器比可以屈指数也

    Explanation

    426

    When the narrator encountered the object, it was adorned with various feathers, its entire body a deep green, and the figurine within appeared as if made of emerald jade. Discussing its craftsmanship and texture, it was evident that such artistry was beyond the capabilities of the Jin people, and it was undoubtedly a Han artifact. It was clear that the owner had cherished it greatly and intended to bury it with him. The object measured approximately five inches in length and four inches in height, truly a rare and wondrous item. During the autumn and summer, the narrator happened to pass by and saw the owner selling it. He donated fifty strings of cash to purchase it, then returned home to clean and polish it, removing the sand and dirt and applying wax and stone to enhance its luster. The object's spirit and vitality were a hundred times greater than before, and he stored it in a chest, treasuring it and showing it to those who appreciated ancient artifacts and elegance. One day, a powerful individual forcibly took it away. In the past, Xian Yu Kunnxue, a respected scholar, once owned a similar water dropper, which was quite similar to the one the narrator possessed. It was said that he cherished the "Divine Lion" and never parted with it. He lived in Hangzhou at Fang Bridge, where he had a waterside pavilion by the lake. One day, while leaning against the railing and admiring the view, the figurine accidentally fell into the lake. Despite numerous efforts to retrieve it, it could not be found, and he sighed and lamented, his body and spirit withering. After going elsewhere for more than three years, he returned to Hangzhou and still lived in his former residence, recalling the lost object and visiting the lake. On a day when the water was calm after a frost, the figurine was found buried in the soil. He quickly ordered his servant to retrieve it, and he was overjoyed as if he had found a treasure. He then renamed it the "Divine Lion" and narrated its history, seeking the assistance of respected scholars and poets to praise and celebrate it, eventually creating a large scroll documenting its story. After the scholar's death, his descendants could not preserve it for generations, and the water dropper and the scroll were both given to the Tao family in Wuzhou. The Tao family also could not keep it for long and eventually sold it to a good merchant. Today, its whereabouts are unknown. Since the beginning of our dynasty for over a hundred years, only two such objects have been known: one owned by the scholar and the other by the narrator. Unlike other ancient bronze artifacts, these cannot be counted on one's fingers.

    427

    至元钞様
    　　中书左丞叶公亦愚【李】钱唐人宋太学生上书诋贾似道公田关子不便专权误国似道怒嗾林徳夫告公泥金饰斋扁不法令狱吏鞫之云只要你做一个麻糊公即口占一诗曰如今便一似麻糊也是人间大丈夫笔里无时那解有命中有处未应无百千万世传名节二十三年非故居寄语长安朱紫客尽心好上帝王书遂遭黥流岭
    南及恩放还与似道遇诸途公以词赠云君来路吾归路来来去去何时住公田关子竟何如国事当时谁汝误雷州戸厓州戸人生防有相逢处客中颇恨乏蒸羊聊赠一篇长短句归附后入京上书言时相并献至元钞様此様在宋时固尝进呈请以代关子朝廷不能用故今别改年号而复献之世皇嘉纳使用铸板以功累官至今任而终

    Explanation

    427

    The Model of the Zhiyuan Chao

    The Left Chancellor of the Central Secretariat, Ye Gong Yi Yu (also known as Li), a native of Qiantang, was a student at the Imperial Academy during the Song Dynasty. He submitted a memorial criticizing Jia Sidao's policies, particularly the public land and tax system, which he deemed detrimental to the state. Jia Sidao, angered by this, instructed Lin Defu to accuse Ye Gong of violating the law by decorating his study room with gold leaf. The case was brought before the court, where Ye Gong was interrogated. He responded with a poem, saying, "Now, I am but a mediocre official, yet I am still a great man among men. When there is no time for writing, how can there be a fate where there is no place? To be remembered for one's integrity through countless generations, twenty-three years is not a long time in one's life. Addressing the officials and nobles in the capital, I serve the emperor with all my heart." As a result, he was branded and exiled to the southern regions.

    Later, he was pardoned and allowed to return. On the road, he met Jia Sidao and offered him a poem, reflecting on their shared experiences and the fate of their policies. The poem read, "Your path is my path; coming and going, when will it stop? What has become of the public land and tax system? Who was responsible for the state affairs at that time? The administrative divisions of Leizhou and Yazhou—life is always prepared for meetings. In exile, I greatly missed the delicacy of steamed lamb. I will casually offer a short poem."

    After surrendering to the Yuan Dynasty, he entered the capital and submitted a memorial, proposing the use of the Zhiyuan Chao model. This model had previously been presented during the Song Dynasty, requesting its use to replace the 关子, but the court did not adopt it. He presented it again after changing the era name. The emperor praised and adopted it, using it for casting coins, and he was promoted through his contributions, serving until the end of his life.

    428

    妓聪敏
    　　歌妓顺时秀姓郭氏性资聪敏色艺超絶教坊之白眉也翰林学士王公元鼎甚眷之偶有疾思得马版肠充馔公杀所骑千金五花马取肠以供至今都下传为佳话时中书叅政阿哩衮尤属意焉因戱谓曰我比元鼎如何对曰叅政宰相也学士才人也燮理隂阳致君泽民则学士不及叅政嘲风咏月惜玉怜香则叅政不如学士叅政付之一笑而罢郭氏亦善于应对者矣
    　　日无光
    　　至正辛丑四月朔日日未没三四竿许忽然无光渐渐作蕉叶様天且昏黑如夜星斗灿然饭顷方复旧天再开星斗亦又少时乃没按天官书王晋书曰日无光臣有隂谋京房易传曰臣专刑兹谓分威微而日不明
    　　松江志异

    Explanation

    428

    A Courtesan of Intelligence

    Shunshi Xiu, a courtesan of the Guo family, was renowned for her intelligence, talent, and exceptional beauty. She was the pride of the music hall, often referred to as the "white eyebrow" (a term of high praise). The Hanlin scholar Wang Yunda held her in high regard. On one occasion, when he fell ill and desired the intestines of a horse to be served as a delicacy, he killed his prized thousand-gold five-colored horse to obtain them. This act became a celebrated story in the capital. At the time, the Grand Councillor A Ligen was particularly interested in her. He playfully asked, "How do I compare to Wang Yunda?" She replied, "The Grand Councillor is a chancellor, and the Hanlin scholar is a man of talent. In managing the balance of yin and yang, assisting the ruler, and benefiting the people, the Hanlin scholar is no match for the Grand Councillor. But in mocking the wind, singing about the moon, cherishing jade, and pitying fragrant things, the Grand Councillor cannot compare to the Hanlin scholar." A Ligen laughed and let it go. Shunshi Xiu was also adept at responding to such questions.

    The Sun Has No Light

    On the first day of the fourth month in the year 1341 of the Yuan Dynasty, the sun had not yet set by three or four poles when it suddenly lost its light. The sky gradually turned dark like night, and the stars shone brightly. After a meal, the sky returned to normal, and the stars disappeared again. Soon after, the sun set. According to the Tian Guan Shu (Celestial Officials Book) and the Jin Shu (Book of Jin), the sun losing its light signifies a plot among the ministers. The Yi Chuan (Commentary on the Book of Changes) by Jing Fang states that when ministers monopolize punishment, it is called dividing authority, and the sun becomes dim.

    Strange Events of Songjiang

    429

    至正壬寅八月中上海县三十四保辰字围金寿一家已阉雄狗生小狗八其一嘴爪红如鲜血然犬之为妖多见于占騐之书而未有若此者若男变为女男子孕育则尝闻之古昔盖阳衰隂盛兵戈乱离之兆今夫牡物而生儿阳化隂也又犬属火一嘴爪红红亦火也岂非主兵主火者与甲辰四月十五日华亭县五保杨巷邵浦云之西清庵廊屋一十九间毎间屋柱皆有声其声若以
    桶覆水面而击其底者人以手按之则振掉而起经时乃止按乾坤变异録人君宫室无故有音声主兵起若人家主家亡六月二十三日夜四更松江近海去处潮忽骤至人皆惊讶以非正候至辰时潮方来乃知先非潮也后见湖泖人説湖泖素不通潮忽平拥起高三四尺若潮涨之势正与此时同又闻平江嘉兴亦如之按五行志水自盈溢主兵兴乾坤变异録河水大壅臣下执政有背叛
    　　郡县君
    　　国朝品官母妻四品赠郡君五品赠县君然古君之妻人曰小君礼士丧妾不得匹其夫必曰君妻曰女君后世封羊祜妻为万歳君则此可谓令甲之原面不畏寒

    Explanation

    429

    In the eighth month of the Rén Yín year (1352) during the reign of Emperor Toghon Temür, a family named Jīn Shòu in the thirty-fourth district of the Chén area in Shànghǎi County had a castrated male dog that gave birth to eight puppies. One of the puppies had red mouth and claws like fresh blood. Dogs are often seen as omens in divination books, but no such case has ever been recorded before. If a male were to turn into a female and give birth, such a phenomenon has been heard of in ancient times, as it is a sign of the decline of yang and the increase of yin, indicating war and chaos. Now, a male animal giving birth to a child is a transformation of yang into yin. Additionally, dogs belong to the element of fire, and the red mouth and claws are also associated with fire. Is this not a sign of war and fire?

    On the fifteenth day of the fourth month of the Jiǎ Chén year (1364), in the fifth district of Huátíng County, a temple named Xī Qīng Ān with nineteen rooms in the village of Yángxiàng Shàopǔ Yún had columns that produced sounds in each room. The sound was like striking the bottom of a bucket covered with water. When people placed their hands on the columns, they would vibrate and rise, and after a long time, the sound would stop. According to the book Qiánkūn Biànyì Lù, the sudden production of sounds in the palace of the ruler indicates the beginning of war. If a family's head dies, it is a sign of the family's demise.

    On the twenty-third day of the sixth month of the same year, at the fourth watch of the night, a sudden and violent tide unexpectedly surged onto the coast near Sōngjiāng. People were astonished because it was not the regular tidal time. It was only at the third watch that the tide actually arrived, and then they realized that it was not a regular tide. Later, people from the Huá Mǎo area reported that the Huá Mǎo region usually does not experience tides, but suddenly, the water rose to a height of three to four feet, just like a tidal surge. It was also reported that the same phenomenon occurred in Píngjiāng and Jīnxīng. According to the book Wǔxíng Zhì, when water overflows on its own, it is a sign of the beginning of war. The book Qiánkūn Biànyì Lù also states that when the Yellow River becomes blocked, it is a sign that ministers will rebel.

    In the early Ming Dynasty, the mother and wife of officials of the fourth rank were granted the title of Jūn Jūn (County Lady), and those of the fifth rank were granted the title of Xiàn Jūn (County Lady). In ancient times, the wife of a Jūn Jūn was called Xiǎo Jūn, and according to the Lǐ (Rites), a scholar's concubine could not be paired with her husband, who must be referred to as Jūn. The wife was called Nǚ Jūn. In later times, the wife of Yáng Hǔ was granted the title of Wàn Suì Jūn (Eternal Lady), which can be considered the origin of the title Jūn. Her face was not afraid of cold.

    430

    人之四支百骸莫不畏寒独面则否医书谓头者诸阳之防诸隂脉至颈及胷而还独诸阳脉上至头所以然也

    　　辍耕録卷十九
    <子部,小说家类,杂事之属,辍耕录>
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷二十　　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰纳音
    　　六十甲子之有纳音世人鲜知其理尝观笔谈有曰六十甲子纳音盖六十律旋相为宫也一律合五音十二律纳六十音也凡气始于东方而右行音起于西方而左行隂阳相错而生变化所以气始于东方者四时始于木右行传于火火传于土土传于金金传于水所谓音始于西方者五音始于金左旋传于火火传于木木传于水水传于土【纳音与易纳甲同法干纳甲而坤纳癸始于干而终于坤纳音始于金金干也终于土土坤也】纳音之法同娶妻隔八生子【此汉志语也】此律吕相生之法也五行先仲而后孟孟而后季此遁甲三元之纪也甲子金之仲【黄钟之商】同位娶乙丑【大吕之商同位谓甲与乙丙

    Explanation

    430

    No limbs or bones of the human body fear cold except for the face, which does not. Medical texts say that the head is the barrier of all yang, and the yin meridians reach the neck and chest and return, but the yang meridians ascend to the head. That is why. The sixty cycles of the Chinese calendar have musical tones, but few people understand their principles. I have read Dream Pool Essays, which says that the musical tones of the sixty cycles are based on the sixty scales rotating to form the tonic. One scale combines five tones and twelve scales to form sixty tones. The qi starts in the east and moves to the right, while the tones start in the west and move to the left. Yin and yang interact to produce change. The reason qi starts in the east is that the four seasons start with wood, moving to the right to fire, then to earth, then to metal, then to water. The reason tones start in the west is that the five tones start with metal, moving to the left to fire, then to wood, then to water, then to earth. The method of musical tones is the same as the method of Yi (I Ching) in Naji (Nai Jia), where Qian (Heaven) takes Jia and Kun (Earth) takes Gui, starting with Qian and ending with Kun. The musical tones start with metal (Qian) and end with earth (Kun). The method of musical tones is like a couple marrying and having children every eight years. This is the method of generating musical scales and tones. The five elements start with the middle, then the first, then the last. This is the method of Dun Jia (Divination) in the three elements. Jiazi (the first cycle) is the middle of metal, the shang (one of the five tones) of Huangzhong (a scale). It takes the same position as Yi Chou (the second cycle), the shang of Dalu (another scale). The same position means Jia and Yi and Bing.

    431

    与丁之下皆仿此】隔八下生壬申金之孟【夷则之商隔八谓大吕下生夷则也下皆仿此】壬申同位娶癸酉【南吕之商】隔八上生庚辰金之季【姑洗之商此金三元终若只以阳辰言之则依遁甲逆转仲孟季若兼妻言则顺传孟仲季也】庚辰同位娶辛巳【仲吕之南】隔八下生戊子火之仲【黄钟之徴金三元终则左行传南方火也】戊子娶己丑【大吕之徴】生丙申火之孟【夷则之徴】丙申娶丁酉【南吕之征】生甲辰火之季【姑洗之徴】甲辰娶乙巳【仲吕之徴】生壬子木之仲【黄钟之角火三元终则左行传于东方木】如是左行至于丁巳中吕之宫五音一终复自甲午金之仲娶乙未隔八

    Explanation

    431

    In the context of the preceding examples, the following is a translation of the provided text:

    All that follows the example of Ding should be treated similarly. Descending by an interval of eight steps, one generates the first month of the year in the Metal element, which corresponds to the "Shang" tone of the scale of Yi. This is analogous to the generation of Yi from Da Lu, and the rest follow the same pattern. The first month of the Metal element, Renshen, is paired with Guiyou, which corresponds to the "Shang" tone of the scale of Nanlü. Ascending by an interval of eight steps, one generates the third month of the year in the Metal element, which corresponds to the "Shang" tone of the scale of Guxi. This marks the end of the three-year cycle of the Metal element. If one considers only the Yang cycles, one follows the reverse order of the Duanma cycle, moving from Zhong to Meng to Ji. If one includes the "wife" (i.e., the Yin cycles), one follows the forward order, moving from Meng to Zhong to Ji. The third month of the Metal element, Gengchen, is paired with Xinshi, which corresponds to the "Nan" tone of the scale of Zhonglü. Descending by an interval of eight steps, one generates the second month of the year in the Fire element, which corresponds to the "Zheng" tone of the scale of Huangzhong. This marks the end of the three-year cycle of the Metal element, and one moves leftward to the Fire element of the South. The second month of the Fire element, Wu Zi, is paired with Ji Chou, which corresponds to the "Zheng" tone of the scale of Da Lu. The first month of the Fire element, Bing Shen, is generated, corresponding to the "Zheng" tone of the scale of Yi. Bing Shen is paired with Ding You, which corresponds to the "Zheng" tone of the scale of Nanlü. The third month of the Fire element, Ji Chen, is generated, corresponding to the "Zheng" tone of the scale of Guxi. Ji Chen is paired with Yi Si, which corresponds to the "Zheng" tone of the scale of Zhonglü. The first month of the Wood element, Ren Zi, is generated, corresponding to the "Jue" tone of the scale of Huangzhong. This marks the end of the three-year cycle of the Fire element, and one moves leftward to the Wood element of the East. In this manner, one moves leftward to the fifth tone of the scale of Zhonglü, completing one cycle of the five tones, and then begins again with the second month of the Metal element, Jia Wu, paired with Yi Wei, and descending by an interval of eight steps.

    432

    生壬寅一如甲子之法终于癸亥【谓防賔娶林钟上生太簇之】自子至于巳为阳故自黄钟至于中吕皆下生自午至于亥为隂故自林钟至于应钟皆上生【甲子乙丑金与甲午乙未金虽同然甲子乙丑为阳律阳律皆下生甲午乙未为阳吕阳吕皆上生六十律相反所以分为一纪也】得此一説固己判然及读瑞桂堂暇録亦论及此则尤明白简易其曰六十甲子之纳音此以金木水火土之音而明之也一六为水二七为火三八为木四九为金五十为土然五行之中惟金木为自然之音水火土必相假而后成音盖水假土火假水土假火故金音四九木音三八水音五十火音一六土音二七此不易之论也何以言之甲

    Explanation

    432

    In the year of Ren Yin (壬寅), the method of generating tones is the same as that for the year of Jia Zi (甲子), and it ends with the year of Gui Hai (癸亥). This refers to the method where the scale of Lin Zhong (林钟) generates the scale of Tai Chu (太簇) in an ascending manner. From Zi to Si, the tones are yang, so from Huang Zhong (黄钟) to Zhong Lu (中吕), they are generated in a descending manner. From Wu to Hai, the tones are yin, so from Lin Zhong (林钟) to Ying Zhong (应钟), they are generated in an ascending manner. Although the years Jia Zi (甲子) and Yi Chou (乙丑) and Jia Wu (甲午) and Yi Wei (乙未) both involve metal, Jia Zi (甲子) and Yi Chou (乙丑) are yang tones and are generated in a descending manner, while Jia Wu (甲午) and Yi Wei (乙未) are yang tones and are generated in an ascending manner. The sixty tones are opposite, which is why they are divided into one cycle. Understanding this explanation makes it clear. Upon reading the Rui Gui Tang Xia Lu (瑞桂堂暇録), which also discusses this topic, it becomes even clearer and simpler. It states that the system of assigning musical tones to the sixty-year cycle of the Chinese zodiac is based on the sounds of the five elements (metal, wood, water, fire, and earth). One and six correspond to water, two and seven to fire, three and eight to wood, four and nine to metal, and five and zero to earth. However, among the five elements, only metal and wood are natural sounds, while water, fire, and earth require mutual assistance to form a complete sound. Water depends on earth, fire depends on water, and earth depends on fire. Therefore, the tone for metal is four and nine, the tone for wood is three and eight, the tone for water is five and zero, the tone for fire is one and six, and the tone for earth is two and seven. This is an unchangeable principle. Why is this so? In the year of Jia (甲)...

    433

    巳子午九也乙庚丑未八也丙辛寅申七也丁壬卯酉六也戊癸辰戌五也己亥四也甲子乙丑其数三十有四四者金之音也故曰金戊辰己巳其数二十有八八者木之音也故曰木庚午辛未其数三十有二二者火也土以火为音故曰土甲申乙酉其数三十十者土也水以土为音故曰水戊子己丑其数三十有一一者水也火以水为音故曰火凡六十甲子皆然此纳音之所起也大抵六十甲子歴也纳音律也支干纳音之别也此天地自然之数河图生数也生者左旋故以中央之土而生西方之金西方之金而生北方之水北方之水而生东方之木东方之木而生南方之火南方之火而复生中央之土洛书尅数也尅者右转故

    Explanation

    433

    The sixty Jiazi cycles (巳子午九也乙庚丑未八也丙辛寅申七也丁壬卯酉六也戊癸辰戌五也己亥四也甲子乙丑其数三十有四四者金之音也故曰金戊辰己巳其数二十有八八者木之音也故曰木庚午辛未其数三十有二二者火也土以火为音故曰土甲申乙酉其数三十十者土也水以土为音故曰水戊子己丑其数三十有一一者水也火以水为音故曰火凡六十甲子皆然此纳音之所起也) are associated with specific musical tones and elements. The numbers indicate the sum of the stems and branches, and each pair corresponds to a particular tone and element. For example, the sum of 34 (甲子乙丑) corresponds to the tone of gold (金), and the sum of 28 (戊辰己巳) corresponds to the tone of wood (木).

    The Jiazi cycles (六十甲子歴也纳音律也支干纳音之别也) are a fundamental part of Chinese astrology and calendrical systems, and they are closely related to the musical tones and the elements. The system of assigning musical tones to the Jiazi cycles (纳音) is a traditional Chinese practice used in music and divination.

    The natural order of the universe (此天地自然之数河图生数也生者左旋故以中央之土而生西方之金西方之金而生北方之水北方之水而生东方之木东方之木而生南方之火南方之火而复生中央之土) is described by the I Ching (河图), where the five elements (土, 金, 水, 木, 火) generate each other in a cyclical manner. The central earth (土) generates the west (金), which generates the north (水), which generates the east (木), which generates the south (火), which then returns to the central earth.

    The Lo Shu (洛书) describes the cyclical overcoming of the five elements, where each element overcomes another in a specific order (洛书尅数也尅者右转故).

    This translation aims to be faithful to the original text, preserving the technical and cultural significance of the Jiazi cycles, the musical tones, and the elements.

    434

    以中央之土而尅北与西北之水北与西北之水而尅西与西南之火西与西南之火而尅南与东南之金南与东南之金而尅东与东北之木东与东北之木而又尅中央之土此图书生尅自然之数也又见日家一书専解海中炉中之其辞虽凿亦自颇通因并録之曰甲子乙丑海中金者子属水又为湖又为水旺之地兼金死于子墓于丑水旺而金死墓故曰海中金也丙寅丁卯炉中火者寅为三阳卯为四阳火既得地又得寅卯之木以生之此时天地开炉万物始生故曰炉中火也戊辰己巳大林木者辰为原野巳为六阳木至六阳则枝荣叶茂以茂盛之木而在原野之间故曰大林木也庚午辛未路傍土者未中之木而生午

    Explanation

    434

    In the central earth, it overcomes the water of the north and northwest; the water of the north and northwest overcomes the fire of the west and southwest; the fire of the west and southwest overcomes the metal of the south and southeast; the metal of the south and southeast overcomes the wood of the east and northeast; and the wood of the east and northeast, in turn, overcomes the central earth. This is the natural sequence of overcoming as described in the Shujing (Book of Documents). I have also seen a book by a certain Ri that specializes in explaining phenomena such as "oceanic metal" and "furnace fire." Although its language is somewhat obscure, it is still quite understandable. Therefore, I will record it as follows:

    For the years Jiazi and Yichou, "oceanic metal" is associated with water, which is also a lake and a place where water thrives. Additionally, metal dies in the zi position and is buried in the chou position. When water thrives and metal dies and is buried, it is called "oceanic metal."

    For the years Bingyin and Dingmao, "furnace fire" is associated with the third yang in yin and the fourth yang in mao. Fire, having found its place, is further nurtured by the wood of yin and mao. At this time, the heavens and earth are like a furnace, and all things begin to come into being, hence it is called "furnace fire."

    For the years *Wu

    435

    位之旺火火旺则土于斯而受刑土之始生未能育物犹路傍土若也故曰路傍土也壬申癸酉劔锋金者申酉金之正位兼临官申帝旺酉金既生旺则成刚矣刚则无逾于劔锋故曰劔锋金也甲戍乙亥山头火者戌亥为天门火照天门其光至高故曰山头火也丙子丁丑涧下水者水旺于子衰于丑旺而反衰则不能为江河故曰涧下水也戊寅己卯城头土者天干戊己属土寅为艮山上积而为山故曰城头土也庚辰辛巳白蜡金者金养于辰生于巳形质初成未能坚利故曰白蜡金也壬午癸未杨柳木者木死于午墓于未木既死墓虽得天干壬癸之水以生之终是柔弱故曰杨柳木也甲申乙酉井泉水者金临官申帝旺酉金

    Explanation

    435

    When fire is in its peak state, the earth is subjected to a kind of "punishment" or negative effect. When the earth is just beginning to form, it is not yet capable of nurturing things, like soil by the roadside. Therefore, it is called "Roadside Earth."

    During the years of Ren Shen and Gui You, the "Sword Peak Metal" element is in its proper position in the Heavenly Stems and Earthly Branches system, with the metal element in its "official" state in the year of Shen and in its "imperial" state in the year of You. When the metal element is born and grows strong, it becomes very rigid and sharp, like a sword. Therefore, it is called "Sword Peak Metal."

    During the years of Jia Xu and Yi Hai, the "Mountain Peak Fire" element is associated with the "heavenly gate" (天门), and its light is very high, like a mountain peak. Therefore, it is called "Mountain Peak Fire."

    During the years of Bing Zi and Ding Chou, the "Stream Water" element is strong in the year of Zi but weak in the year of Chou, making it unable to form a river or sea. Therefore, it is called "Stream Water."

    During the years of Wu Yin and Ji Mao, the "City Wall Earth" element is associated with the "mountain" (艮山), and it accumulates to form a city wall. Therefore, it is called "City Wall Earth."

    During the years of Geng Chen and Xin Si, the "White Wax Metal" element is in its early stage of development and is not yet strong or sharp. Therefore, it is called "White Wax Metal."

    During the years of Ren Wu and Gui Wei, the "Willow Wood" element is in a state of decay and is buried, making it weak and flexible, like a willow tree. Therefore, it is called "Willow Wood."

    During the years of Jia Shen and Yi You, the "Well Water" element is in its "official" state in the year of Shen and in its "imperial" state in the year of You. Therefore, it is called "Well Water."

    Final Translation

    When fire is in its peak state, the earth is subjected to a kind of "punishment" or negative effect. When the earth is just beginning to form, it is not yet capable of nurturing things, like soil by the roadside. Therefore, it is called "Roadside Earth."

    During the years of Ren Shen and Gui You, the "Sword Peak Metal" element is in its proper position in the Heavenly Stems and Earthly Branches system, with the metal element in its "official" state in the year of Shen and in its "imperial" state in the year of You. When the metal element is born and grows strong, it becomes very rigid and sharp, like a sword. Therefore, it is called "Sword Peak Metal."

    During the years of Jia Xu and Yi Hai, the "Mountain Peak Fire" element is associated with the "heavenly gate" (天门), and its light is very high, like a mountain peak. Therefore, it is called "Mountain Peak Fire."

    During the years of Bing Zi and Ding Chou, the "Stream Water" element is strong in the year of Zi but weak in the year of Chou, making it unable to form a river or sea. Therefore, it is called "Stream Water."

    During the years of Wu Yin and Ji Mao, the "City Wall Earth" element is associated with the "mountain" (艮山), and it accumulates to form a city wall. Therefore, it is called "City Wall Earth."

    During the years of Geng Chen and Xin Si, the "White Wax Metal" element is in its early stage of development and is not yet strong or sharp. Therefore, it is called "White Wax Metal."

    During the years of Ren Wu and Gui Wei, the "Willow Wood" element is in a state of decay and is buried, making it weak and flexible, like a willow tree. Therefore, it is called "Willow Wood."

    During the years of Jia Shen and Yi You, the "Well Water" element is in its "official" state in the year of Shen and in its "imperial" state in the year of You. Therefore, it is called "Well Water."

    436

    既生旺则水由以生然方生之际力量未洪故曰井泉水也丙戌丁亥屋上土者丙丁属火戌亥为天门火既炎上则土非在下而生故曰屋上土也戊子己丑霹火者丑属土子属水水居正位而纳音乃火水中之火非龙神则无故曰霹火也庚寅辛卯松柏木者木临官寅帝旺卯木既生旺则非柔弱之比故曰松栢木也壬辰癸巳长流水者辰为水库巳为金长生之地金生则水性已存以库水而逢生金则泉源终不竭故曰长流水也甲午乙未沙中金者午为火旺之地火旺则金败未为火衰之地火衰则金冠带败而方冠带未能斫伐故曰沙中金也丙申丁酉山下火者申为地戸酉为日入之门日至此时而藏光故曰山下火

    Explanation

    436

    In the case of the well water, it is said to be generated when it is strong and flourishing, but at the moment of its generation, its power is not yet great, hence it is called "well water." For the years Bing Xu and Ding Hai, it is said to be "earth on the roof," as Bing and Ding belong to fire, and Xu and Hai are the celestial gates. When fire is already rising upward, earth is not born from below, hence it is called "earth on the roof." For the years Wu Zi and Ji Chou, it is said to be "thunderbolt fire," as Chou belongs to earth and Zi belongs to water. Water is in its proper position, and the tone is fire, which is the fire in water. Without the dragon deity, there is none, hence it is called "thunderbolt fire." For the years Geng Yin and Xin Mao, it is said to be "pine and cypress wood," as wood is in its official position in Yin and in its imperial position in Mao. When wood is already strong and flourishing, it is not comparable to weak, hence it is called "pine and cypress wood." For the years Ren Chen and Gui Si, it is said to be "long-flowing water," as Chen is the reservoir of water and Si is the place where metal is born. When metal is generated, the nature of water already exists. With the reservoir of water and the encounter of metal, the spring source will never run dry, hence it is called "long-flowing water." For the years Jia Wu and Yi Wei, it is said to be "gold in the sand," as Wu is the place where fire is strong. When fire is strong, metal is defeated. Wei is the place where fire is declining. When fire is declining, metal is crowned and adorned. Defeated and adorned, it has not yet been cut, hence it is called "gold in the sand." For the years Bing Shen and Ding You, it is said to be "fire under the mountain," as Shen is the earth gate and You is the gate of the sun's entry. When the sun reaches this time, it hides its light, hence it is called "fire under the mountain."

    437

    也戊戌己亥平地木者戌为原野亥为木生之地夫木生于原野则非一根一株之比故曰平地木也庚子辛丑壁上土者丑虽土家正位而子则水旺之地土见水多则为泥也故曰壁上土也壬寅癸卯金箔金者寅卯为木旺之地木旺则金羸又金絶于寅胎于卯金既无力故曰金箔金也甲辰乙巳覆灯火者辰为食时巳为禺中日之将中艶阳之势光于天下故曰覆灯火也丙午丁未天河水者丙丁属火午为火旺之地而纳音乃水水自火出非银汉不能有也故曰天河水也戊申己酉大驿土者申为坤坤为地酉为兑兑为泽戊巳之土加于坤泽之上非其他浮薄之土也故曰大驿土也庚戌辛亥钗钏金者金至戌而衰至亥而病

    Explanation

    437

    The translation of the provided Classical Chinese text is as follows:

    戊戌己亥平地木 refers to the year when the wood grows on flat land, with "戌" representing the wild and "亥" the place where wood grows. Thus, it is called "flat land wood."

    庚子辛丑壁上土 refers to the year when the earth is on a wall, with "丑" being the central position of earth and "子" a place where water is strong. When earth meets much water, it turns into mud, hence it is called "wall earth."

    壬寅癸卯金箔金 refers to the year when the gold is like gold leaf, with "寅" and "卯" being places where wood is strong. When wood is strong, gold becomes weak, and by the time it reaches "寅" and "卯," gold has lost its strength, thus it is called "gold leaf gold."

    甲辰乙巳覆灯火 refers to the year when the lamp is covered, with "辰" representing the time of eating and "巳" the middle of the day. When the sun is at its zenith, the light is bright and covers the world, hence it is called "covered lamp fire."

    丙午丁未天河水 refers to the year when the water is from the sky, with "丙丁" representing fire and "午" a place where fire is strong. However, the element of water comes from this fire, and such water can only be found in the Milky Way, thus it is called "heavenly river water."

    戊申己酉大驿土 refers to the year when the earth is at a large station, with "申" representing the earth (坤) and "酉" the泽 (兑). The earth and泽 are combined, making it a special kind of earth, hence it is called "large station earth."

    庚戌辛亥钗钏金 refers to the year when the gold is used for jewelry, with "戌" representing the decline of gold and "亥" the illness of gold. By the time it reaches these positions, gold has become weak, thus it is called "jewelry gold."

    438

    金既衰病则诚柔矣故曰钗钏金也壬子癸丑桑柘木者子属水丑属金水方生木金则伐之犹桑柘方生人便以餧故曰桑柘木也甲寅乙卯大溪水者寅为东北维卯为正东水流正东则其性顺而川涧池沼俱合而归故曰大溪水也丙辰丁巳沙中土者土库辰絶巳而天干丙丁之火至辰冠带巳临官土既库絶旺火复与生之故曰沙中土也戊午己未天上火者午为火旺之地未中之木又复生之火性炎上及逢生地故曰天上火也庚申辛酉石榴木者申为七月酉为八月此时木则絶矣惟石榴之木反结实故曰石榴木也壬戌癸亥大海水者水冠带戌临官亥水临官冠带则力厚矣兼亥为江非他水之比故曰大海水也
    　　化气
    　　甲己土乙庚金丁壬木丙辛水戊癸火此十干化五行真气也其法取歳首月建之干如甲己丙作首丙属火火生土故化土余仿此又一説亦通谓遇龙则化龙辰也甲巳得戊辰戊属土故化土乙庚得庚辰庚属金故化金丙辛以下皆然

    Explanation

    438

    Gold, when it declines and becomes weak, truly becomes soft, hence it is called jewelry. In the years of Renzi and GuiChou, the element of wood corresponds to mulberry and catalpa trees, as Zi belongs to water and Chou belongs to metal, water nourishes wood, and metal cuts it, just like mulberry and catalpa trees that grow in the presence of water and are cut by metal, hence they are called mulberry and catalpa trees. In the years of JiaYin and YiMao, the element of water corresponds to a large river, as Yin is the northeast and Mao is the east, water flows to the east, and its nature is smooth, hence it is called a large river. In the years of BingChen and DingSi, the element of earth corresponds to sandy soil, as Chen is the earth's storage and Si is the earth's exhaustion, and the heavenly stems Bing and Ding of fire reach Chen, which is the earth's maturity, and Si is the earth's authority, the earth having been exhausted and revived by fire, hence it is called sandy soil. In the years of WuWu and JiWei, the element of fire corresponds to heaven, as Wu is the place where fire is strong, and Wei is the wood within it, which is again revived by fire, the nature of fire being upward and meeting its place of birth, hence it is called heaven. In the years of GengShen and XinYou, the element of wood corresponds to pomegranate trees, as Shen is the seventh month and You is the eighth month, at this time wood is exhausted, but only the pomegranate trees bear fruit, hence they are called pomegranate trees. In the years of RenXu and GuiHai, the element of water corresponds to the great sea, as water is mature in Xu and authoritative in Hai, water being mature and authoritative, its strength is great, and Hai is the river, unlike other waters, hence it is called the great sea.

    Transformation of Elements

    The ten heavenly stems transform into the five elements' true energies. The method is to take the first month of the year and the corresponding element. For example, if Jia and Ji are the first, and Bing is the leader, Bing belongs to fire, which generates earth, hence it transforms into earth. The rest follow this example. Another explanation is also acceptable, which says that if one encounters the dragon, it transforms into a dragon. For example, Jia and Si get WuChen, Wu belongs to earth, hence it transforms into earth. Yi and Geng get GengChen, Geng belongs to metal, hence it transforms into metal. The rest follow this pattern.

    439

    应聘不遇
    　　胡石塘先生尝应聘入京世皇召见于便殿趋进张皇不觉笠子欹侧上问曰秀才何学对曰脩身齐家治国平天下之学上笑曰自家一笠尚不端正又能平天下耶然怜其贫特授州路儒学教授吁以先生之学行而不见遇于明君是果命矣夫
    　　皇舅墓
    　　河间路景州蓨县河浒一土阜相传为皇舅墓自国家奄混区夏即有謡云皇舅墓门闭运粮向北去水渰墓门开运粮却回来至正辛卯中原大水舟行木杪间及水退土阜崩圮墓门显露继后天下多事海道不通先是张蜕庵【翥】尝有诗云青州刺史河上坟坟不可识碑仍存维舟上读半磨灭使君乃縁戚里恩当时赐葬宜过厚阙树立湏雄尊岂知陵谷有迁变石马尽没趺蹲驿夫指我元傍岸县官恐坠移高原岸濵徃徃多古零落空余秋草根至今父老传防记野人之语那足论我疑其藏必深锢或谓已被湍流吞安得壮士塞河水万古莫令开墓门读公之诗伤今之世则防纬之説诚不可诬矣

    Explanation

    439

    Translation:

    Unsuccessful Application
    　　Mr. Hu Shitang once applied for a position in the capital and was summoned by the Emperor in an inner hall. As he hurried forward, he was so nervous that he did not notice his hat tilting to one side. The Emperor asked, "What do you study, scholar?" He replied, "The study of self-cultivation, family management, state governance, and world peace." The Emperor laughed and said, "If you cannot even manage your own hat properly, how can you manage the world?" Nevertheless, out of pity for his poverty, the Emperor specially appointed him as a professor of Confucian learning in Yangzhou. Alas, with Mr. Hu's learning and conduct, not being recognized by a wise ruler truly seems to be a matter of fate.

    　　The Tomb of the Emperor's Maternal Uncle
    　　In the county of Tiao, in the prefecture of Jingzhou, along the riverbank, there is a mound of earth. It is said to be the tomb of the Emperor's maternal uncle. Since the Yuan dynasty conquered and unified China, there has been a folk saying: "When the tomb gate is closed, grain is transported to the north; when the water submerges the tomb gate, it opens, and the grain returns." In the year 1351 of the Yuan dynasty, a great flood in Central China caused boats to float among the treetops. When the water receded, the mound of earth collapsed, and the tomb gate became visible. Afterward, with many troubles in the world and the sea route blocked, the poet Zhang Zhu (Zhu) once wrote a poem about a grave by the river near the governor's residence in Qingzhou: "The grave cannot be identified, but the stele remains. Boats stop to read the inscriptions, which are partially worn away. The governor was appointed due to his connection to the imperial family, and at the time, a generous burial was granted. Who would have thought that changes in the landscape would cause the statues of horses and turtles to be submerged or toppled? The post station attendant pointed out where it originally was by the shore, and the county official moved it to higher ground to prevent it from being submerged again. By the shore, there are often many ancient graves, with only the roots of autumn grass remaining. Until today, the elders have passed down the story. The words of common people are not worth discussing, but I suspect that it must be deeply hidden, or it has already been swallowed by the swift current. How can a strongman stop the river to prevent it from uncovering the tomb? Never let the tomb gate be opened again. Reading this poem, one is saddened by the state of the world today, and indeed, the prophecy of the changing times is not without merit.

    440

    真率防
    　　林昉田间书载防友人游山檄云人有残缣败素绘一山一水爱之若寳售之必千金至于目与眞景防则略不加喜毋乃贵僞而贱真耶求乐之真今日正在我辈春雪既霁春风亦和或坐钓于鸥边或行歌于犊外百年瞬息懽乐防何肴核杯盘随意所命毋以丰约拘也檄书驰告盍勇而前此文殊清新向予避兵云间泗濵时其地有林泉之胜而无烽燧之虞同时嘉遯者皆文人高士

    Explanation

    440

    Translation:

    True Sincerity

    In The Book of the Countryside by Lin Fang, there is a proclamation inviting friends to go on a mountain trip. It says: "There are old, tattered silks and cloths that depict a mountain and a river. One loves them as if they were treasures and would sell them for a thousand gold coins. Yet, when it comes to looking at the true scene, one shows little joy. Is it not that one values the false and despises the true? The true joy today lies with us. After the spring snow has melted and the spring breeze is gentle, we may sit by the side of the oysters and fish, or walk outside the cowshed and sing. A hundred years is but a moment, and joyful moments are fleeting. What matters is the variety of dishes and utensils, which we command as we please, without being bound by abundance or scarcity." The proclamation is sent out, and why not bravely go forward? This text is very fresh and new. At a time when I was avoiding war in the cloud region by the Si River, the place had the beauty of forests and springs, and there was no fear of beacon fires. Those who retired with honor were all scholars and high-ranking gentlemen.

    441

    因仿司马温公故事俾予作约语云百嵗光隂万物乃天地逆旅四时行乐我辈亦风月主人幸居同泗水之滨况地接九山之胜尽可傍花随柳庻防防目骋懐节序骎骎莫芒鞵竹杖杯盘草草何惭野蔌山肴虽云一饷之清懽亦是百年之嘉话敢烦同志互作遨头慨元祐之耆英衣冠逺矣集永和之少长觞咏依然订约既勤践言弗替用附于此以见真率之防不让逰山之乐也珠帘秀
    　　歌儿珠帘秀姓朱氏姿容姝丽杂剧当今独歩胡紫山宣慰极钟爱之尝拟沉醉东风小曲以赠云锦织江边翠竹绒穿海上明珠月淡时风清处都隔断落红尘土一片闲情任卷舒挂尽朝云暮雨冯海粟先生亦有鹧鸪天云十二阑干映逺眸醉香空防楚天秋鰕影薄防防见背纹轻细细浮香雾敛翠云海霞为带月为钩夜来卷尽西山雨不着人间半防愁皆咏珠帘以寓意也由是声誉益彰

    Explanation

    441

    Following the example of Master Sima Wen Gong, I was asked to write this agreement: "A hundred years of time, all things are but temporary guests in the world. Enjoying the four seasons, we too are masters of the moon and wind. Fortunately living by the banks of the Si River, and moreover, being near the beauty of the Nine Mountains, one can fully enjoy the scenery, letting the eyes wander and the mind roam. As the seasons hurry by, do not hesitate to wear straw shoes, carry a bamboo cane, and have simple meals, what is there to be ashamed of? Even a moment of clear joy can be a good story for a lifetime. I dare ask my fellow scholars to take turns as host, reflecting on the distinguished scholars of the Yuanyou era whose glory has faded, while gathering the young and old of the Yonghe era who still share in the joy of wine and poetry. Having made this agreement diligently, we should not neglect to follow through with our words, and thus I record it here to show that our sincerity is no less than the joy of traveling to the mountains."

    Pearl Curtain Beauty

    The performer Pearl Curtain Beauty, whose real name is Zhu, is graceful and beautiful. She is unrivaled in today's mixed drama. Mr. Hu Zishan, the Pacifier, deeply loves her. He once composed a short song titled "Chen Zui Dong Feng" (Drunk by the East Wind) to give to her: "Silk woven from green bamboo by the river, fine threads embroidered with pearls from the sea. When the moon is faint and the wind is clear, they all separate from the fallen red dust. A piece of idle sentiment, freely rolling and unrolling, hanging up morning clouds and evening rain." Mr. Feng Haisu also has a "Zhegu Tian" (Partridge Sky): "Twelve railings reflecting distant eyes, drunk fragrance emptying the autumn sky of Chu. The shadow of a shrimp is thin and barely visible, the turtle's back pattern is light and fine. Fragrant mist gathers, green clouds form a belt, sea霞 forms a hook. At night, it rolls up all the rain from the West Mountain, leaving no trace of sorrow in the world." All these poems use the image of the pearl curtain to convey deeper meanings. Thus, her reputation has grown even more prominent.

    442

    汉儿字圣防
    　　至元丙子秋八月宋州守臣朱焕以城降后于焕之孙道存家钦覩世祖招谕诏防其文曰上天眷命大元皇帝圣防谕淮安州安抚朱焕据陈楚客奏臣与朱安抚同年又有通家之好自戊午归顺之后不相见者十有八载今王师吊伐诸道并进数内一路领涟河清河将士攻取淮东未附州郡切恐城防之日玉石俱焚臣于故人情分不容缄黙且彼所以婴城自守者无他原其本心但未知趋向之方初无执迷抗拒之意今大江南北西至全蜀悉入版图若圣慈特发使命宣示徳音开其生路彼亦识时达变之士也宁不以数万生灵为念乎臣昧死上言伏候

    Explanation

    442

    In the third year of the Zhiyuan era (1266), during the autumn of the eighth month, Zhu Huan, the governor of Yangzhou in the Song dynasty, surrendered the city. Later, at the home of Zhu Huan's grandson Dao Cun, I respectfully observed the Imperial Edict of the Great Ancestor (Emperor Shizu) for pacification. The text of the edict reads as follows:

    "By the grace of Heaven, the Great Emperor of the Yuan dynasty, the Great Ancestor, commands Zhu Huan, the pacifier of Huai'an Prefecture: According to the report of Chen Chu Ke, I, the Great Ancestor, was born in the same year as you and we have a close family friendship. Since the year Wuwu (1274) when you surrendered, we have not seen each other for eighteen years. Now, the imperial army's campaign of punishment and retribution is advancing on all fronts.
