One of the routes, led by the soldiers of Lianhe and Qinghe, is attacking and capturing the eastern part of Huai, targeting states and counties that have not yet submitted. I fear that on the day the city is besieged, both good and bad will be destroyed. As a friend, I cannot remain silent. The reason why they are besieging the city and defending themselves is no other than their true intention, but they do not know the direction to which they should turn. They originally had no intention of stubbornly resisting. From the south and north of the Yangtze River to all of Sichuan, all have been incorporated into the territory. If Your Majesty's benevolence specially sends an envoy to proclaim Your Majesty's benevolent words and open their way of life, they are also people who recognize the times and adapt to changes. Would they not consider the lives of tens of thousands of people? I humbly present this petition, awaiting Your Majesty's command."

    443

    勑防准奏今遣使特防前去宣布大信若能识时逹变可保富贵应在城守御将帅同谋归顺者意不殊此故兹诏示想宜知悉至元十二年七月日白麻正书北方谓之汉儿字圣防此诏嵗月在城未降一载先则焕之来归必先有所期矣焕之子徳辉承父廕仕至汉阳同知道存徳辉之子也亦以父廕仕至江隂知事既而复受僞周戸部主事之职将命州被执至淮安杀之弟兄子侄客居上海又悉死于苗獠之刼掠焕之宗族所遗殆无噍夫焕既不能尽忠于前而道存又不能尽忠于后执遭戮之地适在州淮安天之报施固其宜也谩书于此以为叛逆之劝

    443

    Imperial Decree

    By imperial command, a special envoy has been dispatched to announce the great trust. If you can recognize the times and adapt to changes, you can ensure your wealth and status. All those who are in charge of defending the city and who conspire with the generals to surrender will have the same intention. Therefore, this decree is issued, and I hope you understand and are aware.

    On the seventh day of the seventh month of the twelfth year of the Zhiyuan era (1275 AD), the imperial decree is written in the official script. In the northern regions, it is called the Han script. This decree has been in effect for a year since the city has not surrendered. First, Huan returned, and there must have been some expectation beforehand. Huan's son Dehui succeeded his father's favor and served as the same official position in Hanyang, knowing Dao Cun. Dao Cun, who is Dehui's son, also succeeded his father's favor and served as the magistrate of Jiangyin. Later, he was appointed to the position of official in charge of the Ministry of Revenue of the false Zhou dynasty and was sent to Yangzhou, where he was captured and taken to Huai'an, where he was killed. His brothers, nephews, and other relatives who were living in Shanghai were all killed by the raids of the Miao and Lao tribes. Huan's clan is almost completely extinct, with very few survivors. Huan was unable to be loyal in the past, and Dao Cun was unable to be loyal in the present. The place where they were captured and killed happened to be in Yangzhou and Huai'an. It is indeed as it should be according to heaven's retribution. I write this casually to serve as a warning against rebellion.

    444

    碧澜妾
    　　吴兴赵公碧澜宋宗室也老而益贫二妾方少艾虑无以安其心因遣之去咸弗肯嫁数献肴酒致殷勤焉公于卒也覆诸水曰慎毋再见昔吾割情忍爱以去尔尔弗我忘祗搅我心耳既而各与其父母俱至泣而言曰妾家每歳请给足可养赡愿执事终身为尼以报主恩公遂复留之他日公死果如所言公有寡女复资育之四明黄伯成先生【玠】尝有诗曰感之以诚感必深应之以真应必防真情一合了弗离听我长歌碧澜

    Explanation

    444

    Bi Lan's Concubines

    • Zhao Gong Bi Lan, a member of the Song royal family from Wu Prefecture, grew older and increasingly poor. His two young and beautiful concubines were concerned that there was nothing to make them feel at ease, so he sent them away. All of them refused to remarry and often offered food and wine to show their kindness. When Zhao Gong died, he threw them into the water and said, "Do not see each other again. In the past, I cut off my emotions and endured love to send you away. If you do not forget me, you only disturb my heart." Later, they came together with their parents and cried, saying, "Our families receive enough support each year to sustain us. We wish to serve you for life as nuns to repay your kindness." Zhao Gong then kept them. Later, when Zhao Gong died, just as they had said, he had a daughter who was a widow, and he again supported and raised her. Mr. Huang Bocheng from Siming once wrote a poem saying, "Moved by sincerity, the response is deep; responded with truth, the connection is strong. True feelings once united never separate. Listen to my long song about Bi Lan."
    445

    妾碧澜亦是诸王孙世殊事异老且贫少陵尚爱燕玉暖况是当时真贵人春衣典尽春寒峭二妾朱顔正姝好忍将罗带拆同心懊恨浮生头白早珠钿翠靥幸仅存此时犹及嫁夫君十二楼头燕子去挥手不可留仙裠去妾相悲两相约既去犹烦送肴酌主君讵忍覆弃之见此翻令心绪恶一心专天天得知忍着主衣还事谁遂携衾襥与俱来后君死者当为尼碧澜堂下双溪水使客徃来岂知此不愿新懽恋旧恩千万人中两人耳
    　　箕仙咏史
    　　悬箕扶鸾召仙徃徃皆古名人高士来格所作诗文间有絶佳者意必英爽不昧之依冯精魄以阐其灵恠耳友人檇李顾元凯【舜举】亦善此术尝召一仙至大书曰独乐园主也可命题众以咏史请鸾不停留作成长篇自非熟于史学者弗能焉殊不知此等为何如也诗曰三皇之前不可传尧舜垂衣化自然夏衰商败兵革起征讨有罪非传贤苍姬种徳极深厚歴载八百何緜緜孔丘孟轲不得位唯有文字登书编春秋笔削严一字诛恶褒善持大权丘明作传详本末下迨战国何茫然秦皇并吞六王毕始废封

    Explanation

    445

    I am Bi Lan, also among the descendants of the royal family, with different times and events. I am old and poor, yet the young man still loves the warm jade of Yan, especially when I was truly a noble lady at that time. All my spring clothes have been pawned, and the spring cold is sharp. How can I bear to untie the tied ribbons, symbolizing our union? Regretting the fleeting life and growing old too soon, the pearl diadems and green cheek ornaments are still intact. At this moment, I can still marry my husband. The swallows fly away from the twelve-story tower, and no one can hold them back. When the fairy leaves, I am sad; we had agreed to part, but still, the food and wine must be offered. How could the master bear to abandon me? Seeing this, it makes his mood even worse. My heart is devoted, and heaven knows; I cannot bear to wear the master's clothes for anyone else. So I bring the quilt and bedding with me. If the master dies first, I will become a nun. Under the Bi Lan Hall, the double streams of water; the guests coming and going do not know this. I do not want new joy, but lament the old grace; I am one of the two people in a million.

    The divination by the箕 (a type of bird) and the recitation of history. Hanging the箕 and supporting the鸾 to summon the spirits, often they are ancient famous scholars who come to write poems and essays. Some are exceptionally good, suggesting that the spirits of the wise and clear-minded are not forgotten and are expressing their supernatural abilities. My friend Gu Yuanke from Cui Li (also known as Shunju) is also skilled in this art. Once, he summoned a spirit who wrote "The Master of the Unique Garden." When asked to write a poem about history, the spirit did not stop and wrote a long piece. Only those well-versed in history could do this, but we don't know what kind of spirit this is. The poem says: Before the Three Sovereigns, nothing can be passed down. Yao and Shun ruled by virtue, and everything was natural. In the decline of Xia and the fall of Shang, wars began. Punishments were not passed to the virtuous, and the Zhou dynasty's virtue was extremely deep, lasting for eight hundred years. Confucius and Mencius did not get positions, but their words were recorded in the books. The Spring and Autumn Annals are strict in recording, using one word to condemn evil and praise good, holding great power. Qiu Ming wrote the biography, detailing the beginning and end. Down to the Warring States period, everything is unclear. Qin Shi Huang unified the six kingdoms and abolished feudalism.

    446

    建迷井田功高自谓传万世仁义不施徒托仙东游弗返祖龙死赤灵火徳明中天汉朝文景称至治刑措可比成康前无端杂用黄老术是以未得称其全王莽贼臣簒汉祚赖有光武如周宣云台名将应列宿婉婉良防扶戎轩絶胜高祖彭越可比周召终天年崇儒徃谒曲阜庙典章灿灿罗星后人不省创业苦宠任阉宦皆貂蝉西园粥爵诚可耻党锢忠士灾何延一朝曹氏帝称魏铜驼荆棘生荒烟关张早死后主弱典午自帝开坤埏五方云扰乱中国五马南渡何翩翩六朝兴废有得失岂知合并归杨坚琼花城里建

    Explanation

    446

    The attempt to implement the well-field system, though grand in ambition, failed to endure and was abandoned, leaving behind only a vain hope for its legacy to last a thousand years. The Qin emperor, in his pursuit of immortality, never returned from his eastern voyage, and after his death, the red flame of the Han dynasty's mandate shone brightly in the heavens. The reigns of Emperor Wen and Emperor Jing of Han were celebrated for their good governance, their laws and punishments compared to those of the sage rulers Cheng and Kang of the Zhou dynasty. Yet, before this, there was a haphazard adoption of Huang-Lao philosophy, which prevented the Han dynasty from achieving its full potential. Wang Mang, the usurper, seized power, but it was Emperor Guangwu, akin to the virtuous Zhou Xuanyuan, who restored the Han dynasty. The famous generals of the Han, enshrined in the Cloud Platform, were like stars in the sky, their loyalty and valor unmatched even by the founders of the Han dynasty, Gaozu and Xiang Yu, and their generals, who could be compared to the wise ministers of the Zhou dynasty, Zhou Gong and Zhao Gong. The Han dynasty's reverence for Confucianism led to the veneration of Confucius at his temple in Qufu, where the brilliance of its institutions and rituals shone like the stars. Yet, later generations forgot the hardships of founding the dynasty, and the court became corrupt, with eunuchs, all adorned with the rank of "Diao Chan," holding power. The scandalous sale of official positions in the Western Garden was indeed a disgrace. The persecution of loyal officials during the Party Prohibitions brought disaster upon the state. In a single day, the Cao family ascended to the throne, establishing the Wei dynasty, and the symbols of power, the copper camels, lay overgrown with weeds in the wilderness. The early deaths of Guan Yu and Zhang Fei weakened Liu Shan, the last emperor of Shu Han. The Cao family, with their imperial mandate, opened the way for the founding of the Wei dynasty. The chaos caused by the Five Barbarians disrupted China, and the migration of the Eastern Jin court to the south, symbolized by the five horses of the imperial carriage, was a graceful yet tragic event. The rise and fall of the Six Dynasties each had their successes and failures, but who could have predicted that the reunification of China would ultimately fall to Yang Jian, the founder of the Sui dynasty, who built the "City of Precious Flowers."

    447

    宫阙汴河春水浮龙船乱离思治否复泰唐室高祖催飞骞秦王神武不可及遂承天祚传高大纲不正有惭色我尝抚卷思其渊纷纷女祸握神器扰扰藩镇横戈鋋乘舆避乱数奔窜翠华几度游西川黄巢残贼不忍説白骨山积血成泉侵凌渐使唐祚絶江海虽大犹防防朱温降将乃一贼僣号暂时得复失后唐石晋暨知逺但以功利不尚徳周家亦僣登天基独有世宗明治术我朝列圣皆深仁天下苍生得苏息史书浩浩充屋栋人主欲观宁遍及小臣纂集作通鉴治乱兴亡明似日愿言乙夜细垂观比美成王戒无逸

    Explanation

    447

    Imperial palaces and the Bian River in spring, with imperial boats floating on the water, evoke thoughts of order and peace amidst chaos and disorder. The Tang Dynasty was founded by Emperor Gaozu, who urged forward the great deeds of Li Shimin, whose divine military prowess was unmatched. Thus, the dynasty was established by divine mandate and passed down through generations, though its grand outline was not always just, causing a sense of shame. I have often pondered the depth of history, reflecting on the influence of women who wielded power, the chaos caused by the regional military governors, and the emperor's flight from the capital during times of turmoil. The imperial court retreated to the west, particularly to Sichuan, and the rebellion led by Huang Chao, which caused immense destruction, is a tragedy too terrible to speak of. The devastation was so great that mountains were piled with bones and blood flowed like springs. Constant attacks and invasions gradually led to the fall of the Tang Dynasty, though it was vast and powerful like the sea and rivers. Zhu Wen, a former Tang general, became a traitor and usurped the throne, though his reign was short-lived. The Later Tang, Later Jin, and Later Zhou dynasties followed, but they focused more on power and profit than on moral governance. The Later Zhou also usurped the throne, but Emperor Shizong of Later Zhou was known for his wise governance. The Song Dynasty, with its emperors known for their benevolence, brought relief and peace to the people. Historical records are vast and numerous, filling the halls. Rulers should read these records to learn from them, and I, a lowly official, have compiled the Zizhi Tongjian to clearly show the patterns of governance, chaos, rise, and fall, as evident as the sun. I wish to read the Zizhi Tongjian at night to gain deeper understanding, and to heed the advice from the Shijing (Book of Songs) to avoid laziness and remain vigilant.

    448

    夫妇同棺
    　　张春儿叶县军士李青之妻也年二十青疾革顾谓春曰吾殆矣汝其善事后人春截发示信誓弗再适未几青死春恸垂絶且嘱匠人曰造棺宜极大将以尽纳亡者衣服劔之属匠如其言既敛乃自经邻里就用此棺同葬之事奏上于朝旌其墓时至正戊子也呜呼春儿生长寒防不闲礼节尚知夫妇大义如此顾世之名门巨族动以衣冠自徃徃有夫骨未寒而求匹之念已萌于中者岂不为春儿万世之罪人也与
    　　宋幼主诗

    Explanation

    448

    Translation:

    Husband and Wife in the Same Coffin

    Zhang Chun'er, the wife of Li Qing, a military officer from Yexian, was twenty years old. When Li Qing was gravely ill, he looked back and said to Chun'er, "I am going to die. You must take good care of the afterlife." Chun'er cut off her hair as a sign of her vow, promising never to remarry. Soon after, Li Qing died, and Chun'er was on the brink of death. She then instructed the carpenter, "The coffin should be made very large, so that it can fully accommodate the deceased's clothes, weapons, and other belongings." The carpenter followed her instructions. After the burial, Chun'er hanged herself. The neighbors used this coffin for the joint burial. The event was reported to the court, and her grave was honored. This occurred in the year 1348 during the Yuan dynasty. Alas, Chun'er grew up in poverty and was not familiar with etiquette, yet she knew the great virtue of husband and wife. In contrast, the prominent and wealthy families often boast about their social status, and there are those who, even before the husband's bones have cooled, have already conceived the desire to remarry. Are they not sinners for all time compared to Chun'er?

    Song Dynasty Young Ruler's Poem

    449

    寄语林和靖梅花防度开黄金台下客应是不归来此宋幼主在京都所作也始终二十字含蓄无限凄戚意思读之而不兴感者几希
    　　孔掾史
    　　孔某者皇庆癸丑间为江浙省掾史身躯短小仅与堂上公案相等凡呈署牍文必用低凳阁足令高托欢丞相以其先圣子孙而且才学优长甚礼遇之时有诏许文正公从祀夫子庙庭公之子参知政事恶孔风度不雅因小过叱之退丞相曰他祖公容得参政父亲坐参政反不容他一个子孙立耶许大惭挽文教授诗
    　　至元间宋文丞相有子出为郡教授行数驿而卒人皆作诗以悼之闽人翁某一聨云地下修文同父子人间读史各君臣独为絶唱
    　　狷洁
    　　郑所南先生【思肖】福州连江人宋太学上舎应博学宏词科刚介有立志防天兵南叩阙上防犯新禁众争目之由是遂变今名曰肖曰南义不忘赵北面他姓也居吴下一室萧然坐必南向嵗时伏腊望南野哭而再拜乃返人莫识焉誓不与朔客交徃或于朋友坐上见有语音异者便引去人咸知其狷防亦弗为怪工画墨兰不妄

    Explanation

    449

    A Message to Lin Hejing: The plum blossoms are about to bloom, but the guests from the Golden Terrace should not return. This poem was written by the last emperor of the Song Dynasty in the capital. From beginning to end, twenty characters contain infinite sorrowful meaning in a subtle way. How many readers can read it without feeling moved?

    Kong, the Deputy Clerk

    Kong, during the year 1313, served as a deputy clerk in the Jiang-Zhe Province. He had a short stature, barely reaching the height of the desk in the office. Whenever he needed to submit official documents, he would use a low stool to elevate his feet so he could reach the desk. The Prime Minister, recognizing him as a descendant of Confucius and praising his excellent talent and learning, treated him with great courtesy. At one time, there was an imperial decree allowing Wen Zheng Gong (a title given to Fan Zhongyan) to be worshipped in the Temple of Confucius. Wen Zheng Gong's son, who held the position of Vice Premier, disliked Kong's demeanor as being unrefined. He reprimanded him for a minor offense and sent him away. The Prime Minister remarked, "His grandfather could tolerate the Vice Premier sitting, but the Vice Premier cannot tolerate his own grandson standing?" Xu, feeling ashamed, composed a poem to mourn the professor.

    During the year 1279, Wen Tianxiang, a famous minister of the Song Dynasty, had a son who became a county-level teacher. After traveling several post stations, he died. People all composed poems to mourn him. A man from Fujian named Wang wrote a couplet: "In the afterlife, they will study literature together as father and son; in this world, they will read history as separate sovereigns and subjects. Uniquely a masterpiece."

    Veneration of Purity

    Zheng Sizhao, a scholar and painter of the late Song Dynasty, was from Lianjiang, Fuzhou. He was a student at the Imperial Academy during the Song Dynasty and passed the examination for the broad learning and eloquence category. He was upright, principled, and determined. During the Mongol invasion of the Southern Song Dynasty, he faced new prohibitions. People all looked at him. Therefore, he changed his name to Xiang and Nan, symbolizing his loyalty to the Song Dynasty, facing north to the Zhao family, while using another surname. He lived in a simple room in Wu, always sitting facing south. During festivals, he would cry and bow twice toward the south before returning. No one recognized him. He swore never to associate with Mongol guests. If he saw someone with a different accent in a gathering, he would leave. People all knew of his purity and integrity, nor did they find it strange. He was skilled in painting ink orchids, not frivolously.

    450

    与人邑宰求之不得闻先生有田三十畆因胁以赋役取先生怒曰头可斫兰不可画尝自写一防长丈余高可五寸许天真烂漫超出物表题云纯是君子絶无小人深山之中以天为春过齐子芳之书塾云此世但除君父外不曾别受一人恩寒菊云御寒不籍水为命去国自同金铸心其忠肝义胆于此可见晚年究竟性命之学以夀终鴈书
    　　零落风高恣所如归期回首是春初上林天子援缴穷海累臣有帛书中统十五年九月一日放鴈获者勿杀国信大使郝经书于真州忠勇军营新馆右五十九字郝公书也公字伯常泽州陵川人世皇召居潜邸嵗己未扈従济江授江淮宣慰司副使中统元年拜翰林侍读学士充国信使宋馆于真州凡十有六年始得归此书当在至元十一年是时南北隔絶但知纪元为中统也先是有以鴈献命畜之鴈见公辄鼓翼引吭似有所诉者公感悟择日率从者具香案北向拜舁鴈至前手书尺帛亲系鴈足而縦之后虞人获之中以闻上恻然曰四十骑留江南曽无一人鴈比乎遂进师南伐越二年宋亡至今秘监帛书尚存

    Explanation

    450

    A Man of Principle and His Goose

    When seeking from the county magistrate, it was not obtained. Upon hearing that the master had thirty acres of land, he was threatened with taxes and corvée labor. The master became angry and said, "One's head can be cut off, but one's integrity cannot be compromised." He once painted a portrait of himself, over a zhang in length and about five cun in height, innocent and carefree, transcending the material world. He titled it "Purely a Gentleman, Devoid of Any Petty Person." In the deep mountains, he took the sky as spring, passing by the school of Qi Zifang. He said, "In this world, apart from one's sovereign and father, one has never received the grace of another." Regarding the chrysanthemum, he said, "To withstand cold without depending on water for life, leaving the country, one's heart is as solid as gold." His loyalty and righteousness are evident here. In his old age, he finally attained the study of life and death, and died peacefully. The goose letter:

    Fallen and scattered, high winds at will, the time of return is early spring. The emperor of Shanglin shoots with a bow and arrow, a message was sent to release the goose and not to kill it. On the 1st day of the 9th month of the 15th year of Zhongtong, the national envoy Hao Jing wrote these 59 characters in the new hall of the loyal and brave military camp in Zhenzhou, which is the writing of Hao Gong. His name was Bo Chang, from Lingchuan, Zezhou. The world emperor summoned him to live in the secret residence, in the year Jiwei, he followed the emperor across the river, and was appointed deputy governor of the Jianghuai Pacification Commission. In the first year of Zhongtong, he was appointed as a scholar-official of the Hanlin Academy and a national envoy, detained in Zhenzhou for 16 years before returning. This book was written in the 11th year of Zhiyuan, when the north and south were isolated, and only the Zhongtong era was known. Previously, a goose was offered and kept alive. The goose saw Hao Gong and flapped its wings and called out as if to complain. Hao Gong was moved and chose a day to lead his followers to set up an altar, facing north, and tied a piece of silk to the goose's foot before releasing it. Later, a gamekeeper captured it and reported it to the emperor, who was moved and said, "Forty riders were left in the south, but none were as loyal as a goose," and then he sent troops to conquer the south, and two years later, the Song dynasty fell. To this day, the secret archive of the silk book still exists.

    451

    碑刻印识
    　　李和钱唐人国初时尚存粥故书为业尤精于碑刻凡博古之家或有赝本求一印识毅然弗从其印文李和鉴定石刻印
    　　九姑元女课
    　　吴楚之地村巫野叟及妇人女子辈多能卜九姑课其法折草九茎屈之为十八握作一束祝而呵之两两相结止留两端已而抖开以占休咎若续成一条者名曰黄龙傥仙又穿一圏者名曰仙人上马圏不穿者名曰蟢窠落地皆吉兆也或纷错无绪不可分理则防矣又一法曰九天元女课其法折草一把不计茎数多寡茍用算筹亦可两手随意分之左手在上竪放右

    Explanation

    451

    Translation:

    Stone Inscription Authentication

    Li He, a native of Qiantang during the early Ming Dynasty, was known for his expertise in authenticating stone inscriptions. He specialized in identifying and verifying ancient texts, particularly those inscribed on stone. Li He was especially renowned for his meticulous work in this field, and he was highly respected by families and individuals who were knowledgeable about antiquities. Despite their requests, Li He firmly refused to authenticate any fake or counterfeit copies, ensuring the integrity of the historical records. His seal, inscribed with "Li He Identifies Stone Inscriptions," was a mark of his authority and expertise.

    Divination by the Nine Ancestral Deities

    In the regions of Wu and Chu, village shamans, rural elders, and women were skilled in performing the Nine Ancestral Deities divination. The method involved breaking nine stems of grass, bending them to form eighteen grasps, and tying them into a bundle. They would then invoke blessings and curses while joining the stems in pairs, leaving only the two ends. After shaking the bundle, they would interpret the outcome based on the arrangement of the stems. If the stems continued in a single line, it was called the "Yellow Dragon," a sign of good fortune. If a circle was formed, it was called "Immortal Ascending on a Horse," another auspicious sign. If no circle was formed, it was called "Spider Nest Falling," also a favorable omen. If the stems were disordered and could not be logically divided, it was considered safe. Another method, known as the Nine Heavens Ancestral Deities divination, involved breaking a bunch of grass, regardless of the number of stems, and using counting rods if necessary. The grass was then divided between the left and right hands, with the left hand placed above and the right hand below, forming a specific arrangement.

    452

    手在下横放以三除之不及者为卦一竪一横曰大阳二竪一横曰灵通二竪二横曰老君二竪三横曰太吴三竪一横曰洪石三竪三横曰祥云皆吉兆也一竪二横曰太隂一竪三横曰悬崕三竪三横曰隂中皆凶兆也愚意俗谓九姑岂即九天元女欤离骚经云索璚茅以筳篿兮命灵氛为余卜注曰璚茅灵草也筳小破竹也楚人名结草折竹以卜曰篿据此则亦有所本矣
    　　白翎雀
    　　白翎雀者国朝教坊大曲也始甚雍容和缓终则急躁繁促殊无有余不尽之意窃尝病焉后见陈云峤先生云白翎雀生于乌桓朔漠之地雌雄和鸣自得其乐世皇因命伶人硕徳闾制曲以名之曲成上曰何其末有怨怒衰婺之音乎时谱已传矣故至今卒莫能改防稽张思廉【宪】作歌以咏之曰真人一统开正朔马上鞮鞻手亲作教坊国手硕徳闾传得开基太平乐檀槽呀鳯凰腭十四银环挂冰索摩诃

    Explanation

    452

    In divination, the hand is placed horizontally below, and the number is divided by three. The remainder is used to form a hexagram. One vertical line and one horizontal line is called "Great Yang," two vertical lines and one horizontal line is called "Spiritual Communication," two vertical lines and two horizontal lines is called "Laojun," two vertical lines and three horizontal lines is called "Tai Wu," three vertical lines and one horizontal line is called "Hongshi," and three vertical lines and three horizontal lines is called "Xiangyun." All these are auspicious omens. One vertical line and two horizontal lines is called "Great Yin," one vertical line and three horizontal lines is called "Xuanji," and three vertical lines and three horizontal lines is called "Yinzhong," all of which are ominous signs. My opinion is that the common folk call "Jiu Gu" — is this the same as the "Nine Heavens' Primordial Lady"? The Li Sao states, "Seeking the jingmáo with the tīngzhān, I command Lingfen to divine for me." The note says, "Jingmáo is a magical grass, and tīng is a small broken bamboo. The people of Chu call tying grass and breaking bamboo for divination 'zhān.'" Based on this, it also has its roots.

    Bai Ling Que

    The Bai Ling Que is a grand piece of music from the imperial court of the Yuan Dynasty. It begins with a dignified and gentle melody, but ends with a hurried and abrupt rhythm, lacking a sense of completeness. I have often found this troubling. Later, I met Mr. Chen Yunqiao, who said that the Bai Ling Que originated from the Uyghur steppes, where the male and female birds harmonize and enjoy their own happiness. The Emperor, inspired by this, commissioned the musician Shuode Lü to compose a piece named after it. When the piece was completed, the Emperor remarked, "Why is there a tone of resentment and decline at the end?" By then, the score had already been disseminated, so it could not be changed. Fuzhou Zhang Siliang (Xian) composed a song to commemorate it, saying, "The True Man unifies the world, establishing the new calendar. On horseback, he personally creates the music, with the court musicians Shuode Lü transmitting the opening melody of peace. The tancang flutters, the fenghuang palate, fourteen silver rings hanging on the bing suo."

    453

    不作兠率声听奏筵前白翎雀霜皬皬风殻殻白草黄云日色薄玲珑碎玉九天来乱撒冰花洒毡幕玉翎琤珄起盘礴左旋右折入寥廓崒嵂孤高绕羊角啾啁百鸟纷叅错须臾力倦忽下跃万防寒星坠丛薄砉然一声震雷拨一十四喑一抹鴐鵞飞起暮云平鸷鸟东来海天濶黄羊之尾文豹胎玉液淋漓万寿杯九龙殿高紫帐暖歌声里懽如雷白翎雀乐极哀节妇死忠臣摧八十一年生草莱鼎湖龙去何时回
    　　天下士
    　　漫浪野録云苏子瞻泛爱天下士无贤不肖懽如也尝自言上可以陪玉皇大帝下可以陪卑田院乞儿子由晦黙少许可尝戒子瞻择交子瞻曰吾眼前见天下无一个不好人此乃一病以余言之先生天下士也此其所以泛爱天下士顾今之忌才嫉能口尧舜而心盗蹠者使先生视之乃土苴之不若矣

    Explanation

    453

    The white-winged bird, its voice not made to sing, listens to the music of the court. Before the feast, it appears, its feathers white and frosty, the wind and clouds swirling around it, the grass yellow and the sky pale. It rises from the ninth heaven, scattering ice flowers and sprinkling the felt canopy. Its jade-like wings clink and clatter, rising and falling, turning left and right, entering the vast emptiness. It soars high, circling around the horns of the wind, chirping and calling with the myriad birds, their songs intermingling. Suddenly, exhausted, it descends, the myriad stars of winter falling into the thickets. With a sudden, thunderous sound, it plunges down, as if plucking the strings of a zither, its melody fading into silence. A goose takes flight, the evening clouds level, a fierce bird comes from the east, the sea and sky vast. The tail of a yellow sheep, the fetus of a leopard, the jade-like liquid flows, the cup of longevity, the nine dragon palace high, the purple tent warm, the singing like thunder. The white-winged bird, its joy turned to sorrow, the loyal minister crushed, eighty-one years of life in the wilds, the dragon of Dinghu gone, when will it return?

    Scholars of the world. The Wild Notes recount that Su Shi loved all scholars, regardless of their virtue or lack thereof, and was always joyful. He once said that he could accompany the Jade Emperor above and the beggars at the temple below. His brother, Su Zhe, was more reserved and selective in his friendships, often advising Su Shi to choose his associates carefully. Su Shi replied, "I see no one in the world who is not good." This, he admitted, was a flaw. According to my view, Master Su is indeed a scholar of the world, which is why he loved all scholars. But today, those who envy talent and ability, who speak of Yao and Shun but have the heart of a bandit, if Master Su were to see them, he would consider them no better than chaff.

    454

    辍耕録卷二十
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷二十一　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰宫阙制度
    　　至元四年正月城京师以为天下本右拥太行左注沧海抚中原正南面枕居庸奠朔方峙万歳山浚太液池玉泉通金水萦畿带甸负山引河壮哉帝居择此天府城方六十里里二百四十歩分十一门正南曰丽正南之右曰顺承南之左曰文明北之东曰安贞北之西曰健徳正东曰崇仁东之右曰齐化东之左曰光熈正西曰和美西之右曰肃清西之左曰平则大内南临丽正门正衙曰大明殿曰延春阁宫城周囘九里三十歩东西四百八十歩南北六百十五歩高三十五尺甎甃至元八年八月十七日申时动土明年三月十五日即工分六门正南曰崇天十二间五门东西一百八十七尺深五十五尺高八十五尺左右楼二

    Explanation

    454

    In the fourth year of the Zhiyuan era, during the first month, the capital city was constructed as the foundation of the empire. It was positioned with the Taihang Mountains to the right and the Bohai Sea to the left, facing the central plains to the south, with the Juyong Pass as its pillow and the northern frontier as its base. The Wansui Mountain stood, and the Taiye Pool was dredged, with the Jinshui River flowing through and the suburbs and fields encircling. Backed by mountains and with rivers flowing through, how magnificent is the imperial residence, chosen in this heavenly land. The city was square and sixty li on each side, divided into eleven gates. The main southern gate was called Lize, the gate to the right of the southern side was called Shuncheng, and the gate to the left of the southern side was called Wenming. The gate to the east of the northern side was called Anzhen, and the gate to the west of the northern side was called Jiande. The main eastern gate was called Chongren, the gate to the right of the eastern side was called Qihua, and the gate to the left of the eastern side was called Guangxi. The main western gate was called Hemei, the gate to the right of the western side was called Sucheng, and the gate to the left of the western side was called Pingze. The inner palace was south of the Lize Gate, with the main hall called Daming Hall and the pavilion called Yanchun Pavilion. The palace city was nine li and thirty steps around, 480 steps wide and 615 steps long, and 35 chi high, built with brick. On the 17th day of the eighth month of the eighth year of the Zhiyuan era, at the shen hour, the ground was broken, and on the 15th day of the third month of the following year, the work began. It was divided into six gates, with the main southern gate called Chongtian, twelve bays wide with five gates, 187 chi wide, 55 chi deep, and 85 chi high, with two watchtowers on the sides.

    455

    楼登门两斜庑十门阙上两观皆三楼连楼东西庑各五间西楼之西有涂金铜幡竿附宫城南面有宿卫直庐凡诸宫门皆金铺朱户丹楹藻绘彤壁琉璃瓦饰檐脊崇天之左曰星拱三间一门东西五十五尺深四十五尺高五十尺崇天之右曰云从制度如星拱东曰东华七间三门东西一百十尺深四十五尺高八十尺西曰西华制度如东华北曰厚载五间一门东西八十七尺深高如西华角楼四据宫城之西隅皆三楼琉璃瓦饰檐脊直崇天门有白玉石桥三虹上分三道中为御道镌百花蟠龙星拱南有御膳亭亭东有拱辰堂盖百官防集之所东南角楼东差北有生料库库东为柴塲夹垣东北隅有羊圏西南角楼南

    Explanation

    455

    To the east of the grand gate (崇天门) is the Star Arch (星拱), a three-story gate with three doors, 55 feet wide, 45 feet deep, and 50 feet high. To the west of the grand gate is the Cloud Follow (云从), with the same specifications as the Star Arch. To the east is the Eastern Palace Gate (东华门), a seven-story gate with three doors, 110 feet wide, 45 feet deep, and 80 feet high. To the west is the Western Palace Gate (西华门), with the same specifications as the Eastern Palace Gate. To the north is the Thick Bearing Gate (厚载门), a five-story gate with one door, 87 feet wide, 45 feet deep, and 80 feet high. There are four corner towers (角楼) at the southwest corner of the palace city, each with three stories and adorned with glazed tiles on the eaves and ridges. Directly in front of the grand gate are three white marble bridges (白玉石桥), each with three arches, the middle one serving as the imperial path, carved with hundreds of flowers and coiled dragons. To the south of the Star Arch is the Imperial Dining Pavilion (御膳亭), and to the east of the pavilion is the Hall of the Celestial Arch (拱辰堂), where officials gather. To the southeast of the corner tower is a storage area for raw materials (生料库), and to the east of the storage area is a place for storing wood (柴塲). To the northeast corner of the palace city is a sheep pen (羊圏). To the southwest of the corner tower is another corner tower.

    456

    红门外留守司在焉西华南有仪鸾局西有鹰房厚载北为御苑外周垣红门十有五内苑红门五御苑红门四此两垣之内也大明门在崇天门内大明殿之正门也七间三门东西一百二十尺深四十四尺重檐日精门在大明门左月华门在大明门右皆三间一门大明殿乃登极正旦寿节会朝之正衙也十一间东西二百尺深一百二十尺高九十尺柱廊七间深二百四十尺广四十四尺高五十尺寝室五间东西夹六间后连香阁三间东西一百四十尺深五十尺高七十尺青石花础白玉石圎磶文石甃地上借重茵丹楹金饰龙绕其上四面朱锁窓藻井间金绘饰燕石重陛朱防涂金铜飞雕冐中设七寳云龙御榻白盖金缕褥并设

    Explanation

    456

    Outside the Red Gate, the Liu Shou Si (Liu Shou Office) is located. To the southwest, the Yi Luan Ju (Ceremonial Bureau) stands, and to the west, the eagle house. To the north, the imperial garden is situated, surrounded by an outer wall with fifteen red gates. The inner garden has five red gates, and the imperial garden itself has four red gates. These are within the two walls.

    The Great Bright Gate is located inside the Chong Tian Gate, serving as the main entrance to the Great Bright Hall. The hall is eleven bays wide, 200 feet long, 120 feet deep, and 90 feet high. It has a double-eaved roof. To the left of the Great Bright Gate is the Ri Jing Gate (Sun Gate), and to the right is the Yue Hua Gate (Moon Gate). Both gates are three bays wide and one door wide.

    The Great Bright Hall is the main hall for the emperor's ascension, New Year's Day, birthday celebrations, and court gatherings. It is eleven bays wide, 200 feet long, 120 feet deep, and 90 feet high. The columned corridor is seven bays wide, 240 feet long, 44 feet wide, and 50 feet high. The sleeping quarters consist of five rooms, with six rooms on either side, followed by three rooms in a row. The area is 140 feet wide, 50 feet deep, and 70 feet high.

    The ground is paved with blue stone flower bases, white marble round steps, and patterned stones. The upper part is covered with heavy brocade, the pillars are red and gold-decorated with dragons, the windows are painted red, and the ceiling is decorated with gold paintings. The steps are made of fine stone, the pillars are red-painted with gold accents, and there are bronze flying dragons. The throne is adorned with seven treasures and cloud dragons, with a white canopy and a gold-threaded cushion.

    457

    后位诸王百寮集赛官侍宴坐牀重列左右前置灯漏贮水运机小偶人当时刻捧牌而出木质银裹漆瓮一金云龙蜿绕之高一丈七尺贮酒可五十余石雕象酒卓一长八尺濶七尺二寸玉瓮一玉编磬一巨笙一玉笙玉箜篌咸备于前前悬绣縁朱帘至冬月大殿则黄猫皮壁幛黒貂褥香阁则银鼠皮壁幛黑貂暖帐凡诸宫殿乘舆所临御者皆丹楹朱锁窓间金藻绘设御榻裀褥咸备屋之檐脊皆饰琉璃瓦文思殿在大明寝殿东三间前后轩东西三十五尺深七十二尺紫檀殿在大明寝殿西制度如文思皆以紫檀香木为之缕花龙涎香间白玉饰壁草色髹緑其皮为地衣寳云殿在寝殿后五间东西五十六尺深六十三尺高三十

    Explanation

    457

    The emperor and the princes and nobles, along with a hundred officials, gathered for a grand banquet. They were seated in rows, with mechanical figures holding signs indicating the time. There was a wooden vessel coated with silver and lacquer, adorned with a golden cloud dragon, standing 17 feet tall, capable of holding over 50 stones of wine. A carved wooden table for wine, 8 feet long and 7.2 feet wide, was also present. Jade vessels, a jade drum, a large flute, and a lute were all prepared in front. A curtain with embroidered edges hung in front. In winter, the main hall had walls adorned with yellow cat fur and black sable fur, while the fragrant pavilion had walls adorned with silver mouse fur and warm tents made of black sable fur. All palaces where the emperor resided were decorated with red pillars, red locks, golden decorations, and prepared thrones and bedding. The eaves and ridges of the roof were decorated with glazed tiles. The Wen Si Palace was located to the east of the main palace, with three rooms in front and a veranda, 35 feet wide and 72 feet deep. The Zitan Palace was located to the west of the main palace, with a similar layout and made of Zitan wood, intricately carved and decorated with white jade and green lacquer. The Bao Yun Palace was located behind the main palace, with five rooms, 56 feet wide and 63 feet deep, and 30 feet high.

    458

    尺鳯仪门在东庑中三间一门东西一百尺深六十尺高如其深门之外有庖人之室稍南有酒人之室麟瑞门在西庑中制度如鳯仪门之外有内藏库二十所所为七间钟楼又名文楼在鳯仪南鼓楼又名武楼在麟瑞南皆五间高七十五尺嘉庆门在后庑寳云殿东景福门在后庑寳云殿西皆三间一门周庑一百二十间高三十五尺四隅角楼四间重檐凡诸宫周庑并用丹楹彤壁藻绘琉璃瓦饰檐脊延春门在寳云殿后延春阁之正门也五间三门东西七十七尺重檐懿范门在延春左嘉则门在延春右皆三间一门延春阁九间东西一百五十尺深九十尺高一百尺三檐重屋柱廊七间广四十五尺深一百四十尺高五十尺寝殿

    Explanation

    458

    The Gate of the Phoenix with a scale of one foot is located in the eastern wing, consisting of three rooms and one entrance. It is 100 chi wide, 60 chi deep, and 60 chi high. Outside the gate, there is a kitchen room, and slightly south of it, a room for wine. The Gate of the Unicorn and Auspiciousness is located in the western wing, with the same dimensions and structure as the Gate of the Phoenix. Outside this gate, there are twenty inner treasuries, each consisting of seven rooms. The Bell Tower, also known as the Literary Tower, is located to the south of the Gate of the Phoenix, and the Drum Tower, also known as the Martial Tower, is located to the south of the Gate of the Unicorn and Auspiciousness. Both towers are five rooms wide and 75 chi high. The Gate of Auspicious Blessings is located to the east of the Treasure Cloud Hall in the rear wing, and the Gate of Auspicious Blessings is located to the west of the Treasure Cloud Hall in the rear wing. Both gates are three rooms wide and one entrance. The surrounding wings of the palace are 120 rooms wide and 35 chi high, with four corner towers, each consisting of four rooms with double eaves. All the palaces and surrounding wings are adorned with red pillars, red walls, and colorful paintings, with glazed tiles decorating the eaves and ridges. The Gate of the Spring of Longevity is located behind the Treasure Cloud Hall and serves as the main entrance to the Spring of Longevity Pavilion. It is five rooms wide, with three entrances and a double-eaved roof. The Gate of Exemplary Conduct is located to the left of the Spring of Longevity Pavilion, and the Gate of Good Principles is located to the right. Both gates are three rooms wide and one entrance. The Spring of Longevity Pavilion is nine rooms wide, 150 chi long, 90 chi deep, and 100 chi high, with a triple-eaved roof. The main hall has seven rooms wide, 45 chi broad, 140 chi deep, and 50 chi high, serving as the private residence or sleeping quarters.

    459

    七间东西夹四间后香阁一间东西一百四十尺深七十五尺高如其深重檐文石甃地借花毳防檐帷咸备白玉石重陛朱阑铜冐楯涂金雕翔其上阁上御榻二柱廊中设小山屏牀皆楠木为之而饰以金寝殿楠木御榻东夹紫檀御榻壁皆张素画飞龙舞凤西夹事佛像香阁楠木寝床金缕褥黒貂壁幛慈福殿又曰东暖殿在寝殿东三间前后轩东西三十五尺深七十二尺明仁殿又曰西暖殿在寝殿西制度如慈福景耀门在左庑中三间一门高三十尺清灏门在右庑中制度如景耀钟楼在景耀南鼓楼在清灏南各高七十五尺周庑一百七十二间四隅角楼四间玉徳殿在清灏外七间东西一百尺深四十九尺高四十尺饰以白

    Explanation

    459

    The palace has seven main rooms, four side rooms, and a rear pavilion, with a total width of 140 feet and a depth of 75 feet. The pavilion has a double-eaved roof, its floor is paved with patterned stone, and its eaves are adorned with flowered and fur-like curtains. All these features are present. The steps are made of white marble, the railings are red, and the handrails are topped with copper and painted gold, with carved decorations soaring above. On the pavilion, there are two imperial beds, and in the columned walkway, there is a camphor wood bed with a mountain-shaped screen, all decorated with gold. The sleeping chamber has a camphor wood imperial bed on the east side, and on the west side, there are purple sandalwood beds with walls adorned with plain paintings of flying dragons and dancing phoenixes. The pavilion also has a camphor wood bed with a gold-threaded bedspread and a black sable fur wall hanging. The hall known as the Hall of Benevolent Blessings is also called the Eastern Warm Hall, located to the east of the sleeping chamber, with three rooms in front and back, a width of 35 feet, and a depth of 72 feet. The Hall of Enlightened Humanity is also called the Western Warm Hall, located to the west of the sleeping chamber, with the same dimensions and layout as the Hall of Benevolent Blessings. The Gate of Splendor is situated in the left wing, consisting of three rooms with a height of 30 feet. The Gate of Clarity is located in the right wing, with the same dimensions and layout as the Gate of Splendor. The bell tower is situated to the south of the Gate of Splendor, and the drum tower is to the south of the Gate of Clarity, each with a height of 75 feet. The surrounding corridors consist of 172 rooms, with four corner towers. The Hall of Jade Virtue is located outside the Gate of Clarity, with seven rooms, a width of 100 feet, a depth of 49 feet, and a height of 40 feet, all decorated in white.

    460

    玉甃以文石中设佛像东香殿在玉徳殿东西香殿在玉徳殿西宸庆殿在玉徳殿后九间东西一百三十尺深四十尺高如其深中设御榻帷裀褥咸备前列朱阑左右辟二红门后山字门三间东更衣殿在宸庆殿东五间高三十尺西更衣殿在宸庆殿西制度如东殿隆福殿在大内之西兴圣之前南红门三东西红门宫各一缭以砖垣南红门一东红门一后红门一光天门光天殿正门也五间三门高三十二尺重檐崇华门在光天门左膺福门在光天门右各三间一门光天殿七间东西九十八尺深五十五尺高七十尺柱廊七间深九十八尺高五十尺寝殿五间两夹四间东西一百三十尺高五十八尺五寸重檐藻井琐牕文石甃

    Explanation

    460

    The Jade-lined halls, adorned with patterned stone, housed a Buddha statue. The East Fragrance Hall stood to the east of the Jade Virtue Hall, while the West Fragrance Hall stood to the west of the Jade Virtue Hall. The Heavenly Celebration Hall, with nine bays, was located behind the Jade Virtue Hall, measuring 130 feet east-west, 40 feet deep, and 40 feet high. It contained a throne, with curtains, cushions, and bedding all prepared, and red railings at the front. Two red doors were set on either side, and a three-bay mountain-shaped gate was positioned at the back. The Eastern Changing Room, with five bays and 30 feet high, stood to the east of the Heavenly Celebration Hall. The Western Changing Room, to the west of the Heavenly Celebration Hall, had the same layout as the Eastern Changing Room. The Auspicious Fortune Hall was situated to the west of the inner palace and before the Hall of Ascending Sanctity, with three southern red gates, one red gate in the east palace, and one in the west palace. The area was surrounded by a brick wall, with one southern red gate, one eastern red gate, and one rear red gate. The Bright Sky Gate, the main gate of the palace, was a five-bay hall with a height of 32 feet and a double-eaved roof. The Exalted Splendor Gate stood to the left of the Bright Sky Gate, and the Bestowal of Blessings Gate stood to the right, each with three bays and a single door. The Bright Sky Hall, with seven bays, measured 98 feet east-west, 55 feet deep, and 70 feet high, with a double-eaved roof and a columned corridor 98 feet deep and 50 feet high. The bedroom hall, with five bays and two side rooms, measured 130 feet east-west and 58.5 inches high, with a double-eaved roof, coffered ceiling, and intricate windows, all lined with patterned stone.

    461

    地借花毳裀县朱重陛朱阑涂金雕冐楯正殿缕金云龙樟木御榻従臣坐牀重列前两傍寝殿亦设御榻裀褥咸备青阳门在左庑中明晖门在右庑中各三间一门翥鳯楼在青阳南三间高四十五尺骖龙楼在明晖南制度如翥鳯后有牧人宿衞之室夀昌殿又曰东暖殿在寝殿东三间前后轩重檐嘉禧殿又曰西暖殿在寝殿西制度如夀昌中位佛像傍设御榻针线殿在寝殿后周庑一百七十二间四隅角楼四间侍女庐五所在针线殿后又有侍女室七十二间在直庐后及左右浴室一区在宫垣东北隅文徳殿在明晖外又曰楠木殿皆楠木为之三间前后轩一间盝顶殿五间在光天殿西北角楼西后有盝顶小殿香殿在宫

    Explanation

    461

    The palace was adorned with luxurious bedding and decorations. Red curtains hung from the high, double-staircase, and red railings were gilded and intricately carved. The main hall featured a throne made of camphor wood, decorated with golden cloud and dragon patterns. Court officials were arranged in multiple rows on both sides of the throne. The sleeping hall also had a throne, with bedding and cushions ready. The Qingyang Gate was located in the left wing, and the Minghui Gate in the right wing, each with three rooms and one gate. The Phoenix Tower, located south of the Qingyang Gate, was three stories high and 45 feet tall. The Dragon Tower, situated south of the Minghui Gate, followed the same design as the Phoenix Tower and had rooms for shepherds and guards behind it. The Shouchang Hall, also known as the Dongnuan Hall, was located east of the sleeping hall, with three rooms and a front and rear veranda. The Jiaxi Hall, also known as the Xinnuan Hall, was situated west of the sleeping hall, following the same design as the Shouchang Hall. In the center, a Buddha statue was placed beside a throne, with a sewing hall behind the sleeping hall. The palace had 172 surrounding rooms and four corner towers. There were five servant quarters behind the sewing hall, 72 servant rooms behind the quarters, and baths on both sides in the northeast corner of the palace. The Nanmu Hall, also known as the Wende Hall, was located outside the Minghui Gate, made entirely of nanmu wood, with three rooms and a front and rear veranda. There were five rooms with a pyramidal roof, located west of the northwest corner tower of the Guangtian Hall, and a small pyramidal-roofed incense hall in the palace.

    462

    垣西北隅三间前轩一间前寝殿三间柱廊三间后寝殿三间东西夹各二间文宸库在宫垣西南隅酒房在宫垣东南隅内庖在酒房之北兴圣宫在大内之西北万夀山之正西周以砖垣南辟红门三东西红门各一北红门一南红门外两傍附垣有宿衞直庐凡四十间东西门外各三间南门前夹垣内有省院台百司官侍直板屋北门外有窨花室五间东夹垣外有官人之室十七间凌室六间酒房六间南北西门外棊置卫士直宿之舍二十一所所为一间外夹垣东红门三仪天殿吊桥西红门一达徽政院门内差北有盝顶房二各三间又北有屋二所各三间差南有库一所及屋三间北红门外有临街门一所三间此夹垣之北门

    Explanation

    462

    In the northwest corner of the wall, there are three rooms in front of a pavilion, followed by three rooms of the bedchamber and a three-room columned veranda, then three rooms of the rear bedchamber. On either side, there are two rooms each. The Wenchen Library is located in the southwest corner of the palace wall. The wine room is situated in the southeast corner of the palace wall, with the kitchen north of the wine room. The Xingsheng Palace is located in the northwest of the imperial palace, directly to the west of Wanshou Mountain. It is surrounded by brick walls, with three red gates opened to the south, one red gate each to the east and west, and one red gate to the north. Outside the southern red gate, on both sides of the wall, there are forty rooms for the guards on duty. There are three rooms each at the east and west gates. Inside the wall between the southern gate, there are wooden houses for officials on duty in the administrative offices. Outside the northern gate, there are five rooms for the greenhouse. Outside the eastern side of the wall, there are seventeen rooms for the officials. There are six rooms for the ice house and six rooms for the wine room. Outside the north, south, and west gates, there are twenty-one rooms arranged in a grid pattern for the guards on duty. Each room is one unit. Outside the eastern red gate, there are three rooms. Near the Huiyingtian Palace, there is a western red gate leading to the Huizheng Institute gate, slightly north of which there are two domed rooms, each with three rooms. Further north, there are two rooms, each with three rooms. Slightly south, there is a storage room and three rooms. Outside the northern red gate, there is a street gate with three rooms. This is the northern gate of the wall.

    463

    也兴圣门兴圣殿之北门也五间三门重檐东西七十四尺明华门在兴圣门左肃章门在兴圣门右各三间一门兴圣殿七间东西一百尺深九十七尺柱廊六间深九十四尺寝殿五间两夹各三间后香阁三间深七十七尺正殿四面朱县琐牕文石甃地借以毳裀中设扆屏榻张白盖帘帷皆锦绣为之诸王百寮宿卫官侍宴坐牀重列左右其柱廊寝殿亦各设御榻裀褥咸备白玉石重陛朱防涂金冐楯覆以白磁瓦碧琉璃饰其檐脊宏庆门在东庑中宣则门在西庑中各三间一门凝晖楼在宏庆南五间东西六十七尺延颢楼在宣则南制度如凝晖嘉徳殿在寝殿东三间前后轩各三间重檐寳慈殿在寝殿西制度同嘉徳山字门在

    Explanation

    463

    The Gate of the Sacred Ancestral Temple is the northern gate of the Sacred Ancestral Temple, consisting of five bays and three doors with a double-eaved roof, measuring seventy-four feet east to west. The Bright Splendor Gate is to the left of the Gate of the Sacred Ancestral Temple, and the Gate of Respectful Chapters is to the right, each with three bays and one door. The Sacred Ancestral Temple consists of seven bays, measuring one hundred feet east to west and ninety-seven feet deep, with a pillared corridor six bays wide and ninety-four feet deep. The sleeping hall is five bays wide, with two side rooms, each three bays wide, and a back incense pavilion three bays deep and seventy-seven feet wide. The main hall has red-painted windows with intricate patterns and is paved with decorative stones, with fur rugs laid on the ground. In the center, there is a screen behind the throne and a throne covered with a white canopy and curtains, all made of brocade and embroidery. The princes, officials, and guards sit on beds arranged in rows on both sides. The pillared corridor and the sleeping hall also have imperial thrones with mats and cushions, all prepared with white jade and stone steps. The pillars are red-painted and have gold-plated caps, covered with white glazed tiles and green琉璃 decorations on the eaves and ridges. The Gate of Grand Celebration is in the eastern wing, and the Gate of Declaration is in the western wing, each with three bays and one door. The Tower of Dazzling Light is south of the Gate of Grand Celebration, consisting of five bays and measuring sixty-seven feet east to west. The Tower of Extending Brightness is south of the Gate of Declaration, with the same design as the Tower of Dazzling Light. The Hall of Excellent Virtue is east of the sleeping hall, consisting of three bays with a double-eaved roof, and the Hall of Precious Mercy is west of the sleeping hall, with the same design as the Hall of Excellent Virtue. The Gate in the shape of a mountain character is located elsewhere.

    464

    兴圣宫后延华阁之正门也正一间两夹各一间重檐一门脊置金寳瓶又独脚门二周阁以红版垣延华阁五间方七十九尺二寸重阿十字脊白琉璃瓦覆青琉璃瓦饰其檐脊立金寳瓶单陛御榻从臣坐牀咸具东西殿在延华阁西左右各五间前轩一间圆亭在延华阁后芳碧亭在延华阁后圆亭东三间重檐十字脊覆以青琉璃瓦饰以緑琉璃瓦置金寳瓶徽青亭在圎亭西制度同芳碧亭浴室在延华阁东南隅东殿后傍有盝顶井亭二间又有盝顶房三间辉和尔殿在延华阁右六间傍有窨花半屋八间木香亭在辉和尔殿后东盝顶殿在延华阁东版垣外正殿五间前轩三间东西六十五尺深三十九尺柱廊二间深二十六

    Explanation

    464

    The main gate of the Yan Hua Pavilion in the rear of Xing Sheng Palace is a central room with two side rooms, each with a double-eaved roof and a single door. The ridge of the roof is adorned with a gold vase, and there are two unique single-legged doors surrounding the pavilion, with a red-painted wall. The Yan Hua Pavilion itself is five rooms wide, measuring seventy-nine feet and two inches square, with a cross-shaped ridge and a double-eaved roof. The main roof is covered with white-glazed tiles, while the eaves are decorated with green-glazed tiles, and the ridge is adorned with a gold vase. The emperor's throne is on a single-step platform, and the attendants have their seats prepared. East and west halls are located to the west of the Yan Hua Pavilion, each with five rooms, and a front veranda. A round pavilion is situated behind the Yan Hua Pavilion, with the Fang Bi Pavilion to the east of it, also with a cross-shaped ridge and a double-eaved roof, covered with green-glazed tiles and adorned with a gold vase. The Hui Qing Pavilion is to the west of the round pavilion, with a similar design to the Fang Bi Pavilion. A bathhouse is located in the southeast corner of the Yan Hua Pavilion, adjacent to the east hall, with two dome-shaped pavilions nearby, and three dome-shaped houses. The Hui He Er Hall is to the right of the Yan Hua Pavilion, with six rooms and eight half-houses with hidden flower arrangements nearby. The Mu Xiang Pavilion is behind the Hui He Er Hall, with a dome-shaped hall to the east of the Yan Hua Pavilion, outside the red-painted wall. The main hall is five rooms wide, with a front veranda of three rooms, measuring sixty-five feet wide and thirty-nine feet deep, with a columned corridor two rooms deep and twenty-six feet deep.

    465

    尺寝殿三间东西四十八尺前宛转置花朱阑八十五扇殿之傍有盝顶房三间庖室二间面阳盝顶房三间妃嫔库房一间缝纫女库房三间红门一盝顶之制三椽其顶若笥之平故名西盝顶殿在延华阁西版垣之外制度同东殿东殿之傍有庖室三间好事房二各三间独脚门二红门一妃嫔院四二在东盝顶殿后二在西盝顶殿后各正室三间东西夹四间前轩三间后有三椽半屋二间侍女室八十三间半在东妃嫔院左西向半在西妃嫔院右东向室后各有三椽半屋二十五间东盝顶殿红门外有屋三间盝顶轩一间后有盝顶房一间庖室一区在凝晖楼后正屋五间前轩一间后披屋三间又有盝顶房一间盝顶井亭一间周

    Explanation

    465

    A small sleeping chamber (尺寝殿) three rooms wide, forty-eight feet long from east to west, with flowers arranged in a curved manner (宛转置花) and eighty-five red latticed windows (朱阑八十五扇). Beside the chamber are three rooms with a flat-topped roof (盝顶房), two kitchens (庖室), three more flat-topped rooms facing the sun (面阳盝顶房), one storage room for the imperial concubines (妃嫔库房), three storage rooms for the female seamstresses (缝纫女库房), and one red gate (红门). The flat-topped roof design (盝顶之制) consists of three rafters, with the top resembling a flat box (其顶若笥之平), hence the name "Western Flat-Topped Pavilion" (西盝顶殿). It is located west of the Yanhua Pavilion (延华阁) and outside the earthen wall (版垣), with the same layout as the Eastern Pavilion (东殿). Beside the Eastern Pavilion are three kitchens (庖室), two sets of three rooms for miscellaneous purposes (好事房), two single doors (独脚门), and one red gate (红门). There are four imperial concubine quarters (妃嫔院), two behind the Eastern Flat-Topped Pavilion and two behind the Western Flat-Topped Pavilion, each with three main rooms (正室), four side rooms (东西夹), three front halls (前轩), and two back rooms with half a rafter (后有三椽半屋). There are eighty-three and a half servant rooms (侍女室), half facing west in the eastern concubine quarters and half facing east in the western concubine quarters, each with two back rooms with half a rafter (室后各有三椽半屋) in twenty-five rooms. Outside the red gate of the Eastern Flat-Topped Pavilion are three rooms (屋三间), one flat-topped pavilion (盝顶轩), and one back flat-topped room (后有盝顶房). There is also a kitchen area (庖室一区) behind the Ningsui Pavilion (凝晖楼), with five main rooms (正屋五间), one front hall (前轩), three back rooms (后披屋), and one more flat-topped room (又有盝顶房) and one flat-topped well pavilion (盝顶井亭) surrounding the area.

    466

    以土垣前辟红门酒房在宫垣东南隅庖室南正屋五间前盝顶轩三间南北房各三间西北隅盝顶房三间红门一土垣四周之学士院在阁后四盝顶殿门外之西偏三间生料库在学士院南又南为鞍辔库又南为军器库又南为庖人牧人宿卫之室藏珍库在宫垣西南隅制度并如酒室惟多盝顶半屋三间庖室三间万夀山在大内西北太液池之阳金人名琼花岛中统三年修缮之至元八年赐今名其山皆疉玲珑石为之峰峦映松桧隆郁秀若天成引金水河至其后转机运汲水至山顶出石龙口注方池伏流至仁智殿后有石刻蟠龙昻首喷水仰出然后由东西流入于太液池山前有白玉石桥长二百余尺直仪天殿后桥

    Explanation

    466

    With a dirt wall in front, a red-gated wine house was opened in the southeastern corner of the palace walls, south of the kitchen, with five main rooms and a three-room pavilion with a gable roof in front. There were also three rooms each on the north and south sides, and three gable-roofed rooms in the northwest corner. The scholar's courtyard, with four gable-roofed halls, was located behind the hall, with a three-room structure to the west of the hall. The raw materials storage was south of the scholar's courtyard, further south was the saddle and bridle storage, and further south was the weapons storage. The rooms for cooks, shepherds, and guards were also present. The treasure storage, located in the southwest corner of the palace, had a structure similar to the wine house but with more gable-roofed half-rooms. The wine house had three rooms, and the kitchen area had three rooms.

    Mount Wanshou, located in the northwest of the inner palace, on the sunny side of Taiye Pool, was known as Qionghua Island by the Jin people. It was repaired in the third year of Zhongtong and given its current name in the eighth year of Zhiyuan. The mountain was made of exquisite stone, with peaks and forests that seemed naturally beautiful. The Jinshui River was diverted to the back of the mountain, where water was drawn to the top and flowed out through a stone dragon's mouth into a square pond. The hidden stream flowed to the back of Renzhi Hall, where a carved coiled dragon spouted water upward, then flowed into Taiye Pool from east and west. In front of the mountain, there was a white marble bridge over 200 feet long, leading directly to the back of Yitian Hall.

    467

    之北有玲珑石拥木门五门皆为石色内有隙地对立日月石西有石棋枰又有石坐牀左右皆有登山之径萦纡万石中洞府出入宛转相迷至一殿一亭各擅一景之妙山之东有石桥长七十六尺濶四十一尺半为石渠以载金水而流于山后以汲于山顶也又东为灵圃竒兽珍禽在焉广寒殿在山顶七间东西一百二十尺深六十二尺高五十尺重阿藻井文石甃地四面琐窓板宻其里徧缀金红云而蟠龙矫蹇于丹楹之上中有小玉殿内设金嵌玉龙御榻左右列从臣坐牀前架黒玉酒瓮一玉有白章随其形刻为鱼兽出没于波涛之状其大可贮酒三十余石又有玉假山一峰玉响鐡一县殿之后有小石笋二内出石龙首以噀

    Explanation

    467

    To the north, there are delicate stones encircling wooden doors, five of which are made of stone. Inside, there is an open space with a pair of stones representing the sun and the moon, facing each other. To the west, there is a stone chessboard and a stone bench. On both sides, there are winding mountain-climbing paths that wind through thousands of stones, leading to a cave where the entrances and exits are winding and confusing. There is a hall and a pavilion, each with its own unique charm. To the east of the mountain, there is a stone bridge that is seventy-six feet long and forty-one and a half feet wide, with a half-stone channel to carry golden water, which flows behind the mountain and is used to draw water to the top of the mountain. To the east of this, there is a spiritual garden where rare beasts and precious birds reside. The Palace of the Cold and Distant Moon stands on the summit, seven rooms wide, one hundred and twenty feet long, sixty-two feet deep, and fifty feet high. The ceiling is intricately carved, and the floor is paved with patterned stone. The windows are small and tightly sealed, with gold and red clouds adorning the inside, and a coiled dragon on the vermilion pillars. Inside, there is a small jade pavilion with a golden dragon-embroidered imperial bed. On either side, there are seats for the attendants, and in front of the bed, there is a black jade wine vat, which has a white mark that follows its shape, carved into the form of fish and beasts emerging from the waves. It is large enough to hold over thirty stones of wine. There is also a jade artificial mountain, and a jade bell iron hanging above the palace. Behind the palace, there are two small stone pines, with stone dragon heads spouting water.

    468

    所引金水西北有厠堂一间仁智殿在山之半三间高三十尺金露亭在广寒殿东其制圎九柱高二十四尺尖顶上置琉璃珠亭后有铜幡竿玉虹亭在广寒殿西制度同金露方□亭在荷叶殿后高三十尺重屋八面重屋无梯自金露亭前复道登焉又曰线珠亭瀛洲亭在温石浴室后制度同方□玉虹亭前仍有登重屋复道亦曰线珠亭荷叶殿在方□前仁智西北三间高三十尺方顶中置琉璃珠温石浴室在瀛洲前仁智西北三间高二十三尺方顶中置涂金寳瓶圜亭又曰胭粉亭在荷叶稍西盖后妃添妆之所也八面介福殿在仁智东差北三间东西四十一尺高二十五尺延和殿在仁智西北制度如介福马湩室在介福前三

    Explanation

    468

    The area to the northwest of the Golden Water River has a toilet hall. The Hall of Benevolence and Wisdom is located in the middle of a mountain, with three rooms, each 30 feet high. The Golden Dew Pavilion is situated to the east of the Hall of Eternal Cold, with a circular base and nine columns, 24 feet high, and a pointed top adorned with a glass pearl. Behind the pavilion is a copper flagpole. The Jade Rainbow Pavilion is located to the west of the Hall of Eternal Cold, with the same structure as the Golden Dew Pavilion. Behind the square pavilion (name unclear), which is 30 feet high and has an eight-sided upper story, there is a covered walkway from the front of the Golden Dew Pavilion to reach the upper story. This pavilion is also known as the Threaded Pearl Pavilion. The Pavilion of Penglai is located behind the Warm Stone Bathhouse, with the same structure as the square pavilion. In front of the Pavilion of Penglai, there is a covered walkway to reach the upper story of the Jade Rainbow Pavilion, which is also known as the Threaded Pearl Pavilion. The Lotus Leaf Hall is located in front of the square pavilion, to the northwest of the Hall of Benevolence and Wisdom, with three rooms, each 30 feet high, and a square top with a glass pearl in the center. The Warm Stone Bathhouse is located in front of the Pavilion of Penglai, to the northwest of the Hall of Benevolence and Wisdom, with three rooms, each 23 feet high, and a square top with a gilded vase in the center. This pavilion is also known as the Blush Pavilion, located slightly to the west of the Lotus Leaf Hall, where the empresses would apply makeup. The Eight-Sided Blessing Hall is located to the east of the Hall of Benevolence and Wisdom, with three rooms, 41 feet wide, and 25 feet high. The Hall of Continuity and Harmony is located to the northwest of the Hall of Benevolence and Wisdom, with the same structure as the Eight-Sided Blessing Hall. The Milk Room is located in front of the Eight-Sided Blessing Hall.

    469

    间牧人之室在延和前三间庖室在焉湩前东浴室更衣殿在山东平地三间两夹太液池在大内西周回若干里植芙蓉仪天殿在池中圎坻上当万夀山十一楹高三十五尺围七十尺重檐圆盖顶圎台址甃以文石借以花茵中设御榻周辟琐牕东西门各一间西北厠堂一间台西向列甃塼龛以居宿卫之士东为木桥长一百廿尺濶廿二尺通大内之夹垣西为木吊桥长四百七十尺濶如东桥中阙之立柱架梁于二舟以当其空至车驾行幸上都留守官则移舟防桥以禁徃来是桥通兴圣宫前之夹垣后有白玉石桥乃万夀山之道也犀山台在仪天殿前水中上植木芍药隆福宫西御苑在隆福宫西先后妃多居焉香殿在石假

    Explanation

    469

    The shepherds' room is located in front of the Yanhe Palace, with three kitchens present. The eastern bathing and changing room is situated on flat ground in the east, with the Taiye Pool in the west, surrounded by lotus flowers. The Yitian Hall stands on a circular platform in the middle of the pool, facing the Wanshou Mountain. It has eleven bays, is thirty-five feet high, and seventy feet in circumference, with a double-eaved, circular roof. The base of the hall is paved with patterned stone and covered with flower mats. Inside, there is an imperial bed, and the hall is surrounded by small windows. There are separate east and west doors, each with a one-room toilet to the northwest. The platform faces west, with brick niches for the accommodation of the guards. To the east, there is a wooden bridge that is one hundred and twenty feet long and twenty-two feet wide, connecting to the inner wall of the palace. To the west, there is a wooden drawbridge that is four hundred and seventy feet long and as wide as the eastern bridge. The bridge spans an empty space supported by two boats, and when the emperor travels to the northern capital, the guards move the boats to block the bridge and prevent passage. This bridge connects to the inner wall of the Xingsheng Palace. Behind it, there is a white marble bridge, which is the path to the Wanshou Mountain. The Rhinoceros Mountain Platform is located in front of the Yitian Hall, on a mountain in the water, with peony trees planted on top. The Longfu Palace's western imperial garden is located to the west of the Longfu Palace, where the empresses and concubines often reside. The Fragrant Hall is situated on a stone platform.

    470

    山上三间两夹二间柱廊三间头屋三间丹楹琐牕间金藻绘玉石础琉璃瓦殿后有石台山后辟红门门外有侍女之室二所皆南向并列又后直红门并立红门三三门之外有太子鄂尔多荷叶殿二在香殿左右各三间圎殿在山前圎顶上置涂金寳珠重檐后有流杯池池东西流水圆亭二圆殿有庑以连之歇山殿在圎殿前五间柱廊二各三间东西亭二在歇山后左右十字脊东西水心亭在歇山殿池中直东西亭之南九柱重檐亭之后各有侍女房三所所为三间东房西向西房东向前辟红门三门内立石以屏内外外筑四垣以周之池引金水注焉棕毛殿在假山东偏三间后盝顶殿三间前啓红门立垣以区分之仪鸾局

    Explanation

    470

    On the mountain, there are three main halls, each flanked by two smaller halls, and a three-bay columned corridor. There are also three halls with curved roofs like turtle heads, adorned with red pillars, intricate windows, gold and silver decorations, jade stone bases, and glazed tiles. Behind the main hall, there is a stone platform, and at the back of the mountain, a red gate is opened. Outside the gate, there are two rooms for the maidservants, both facing south and arranged in parallel. Behind the red gate, three more red gates are aligned, and beyond these, there are two lotus-shaped halls for the Crown Prince, each with three bays, located on either side of the incense hall. In front of the mountain, there is a circular hall with a dome topped by a golden pearl, and behind it, a wine-flowing pool with water flowing from east to west. Two round pavilions are situated on either side of the pool. The circular hall is connected to a five-bay hall with two three-bay columned corridors. Behind the five-bay hall, there are two pavilions on either side, with a central pavilion in the pool, and a nine-columned, double-eaved pavilion south of the eastern and western pavilions. Behind each of these pavilions, there are three rooms for the maidservants, each facing west from the east room and east from the west room. Each room has three bays and a red gate facing forward, with a stone screen inside and a four-walled enclosure outside. The pool is fed by the Golden Water. On the eastern slope of the artificial hill, there is a three-bay bamboo leaf pavilion, followed by a three-bay hall with a pyramid-shaped roof, and a red gate at the front. A wall is built to separate these areas, and the Imperial Bureau of Ceremonial Rites is located here.

    471

    在三红门外西南隅正屋三间东西屋三间前开一门史官虞集曰尝观纪籍所载秦汉隋唐之宫阙其宏丽可怖也高者七八十丈广者二三十里而离宫别馆绵延聨络弥山跨谷多或至数百所嘻真木妖哉由余有言使为之则劳神矣使人为之则苦人矣余由当秦穆公之时为是俾见后世之侈何如也虽然紫宫着乎元象得无栋宇有等差之辨而茅茨之简又乌足以重威于四海乎集佐修经世大典将作所防宫阙制度为详于是知大有径庭于古也方今幅贠之广户口之伙贡税之富当倍秦汉而叅隋唐也顾力有可为而莫为则其所乐不在于斯也孔子曰禹吾无间然矣卑宫室而尽力乎沟洫重于此则轻于彼理固然矣

    Explanation

    471

    At the southwest corner of the Three Red Gates, there stands a main house with three rooms and side houses with three rooms, with a front door. The historian Yu Ji said, "I have observed the records of the palaces of the Qin, Han, Sui, and Tang dynasties, which are magnificent and terrifying. The tallest are seventy to eighty zhang, and the widest are twenty to thirty li. The palaces and pavilions extend and connect, spanning mountains and valleys, with some numbering up to several hundred. Ah, truly, they are like wooden demons! The words of You Yu say that if ghosts were to build them, it would exhaust their spirits; if humans were to build them, it would cause suffering to the people. I, during the time of Duke Mu of Qin, would have liked to see how the extravagance of later generations compares. However, the Purple Palace is established in the celestial realm; does it not have a distinction between different levels of buildings? Yet, the simplicity of thatched roofs is not enough to establish authority over the four seas? I assisted in the compilation of the Jingshi Dadian and was involved in the planning of the imperial construction bureau, which detailed the systems of palaces and pavilions. From this, I know that there is a significant difference from ancient practices. Today, the vast territory, large population, and abundant taxes should be double that of the Qin and Han and comparable to the Sui and Tang. Yet, though capable of doing so, they do not act; their pleasure does not lie in this. Confucius said, 'Yu, I have no criticism of him; he built simple dwellings and focused on irrigation. He valued one thing more and thus devalued another; this is the natural order of things.'"

    472

    大都等路都转运盐使司
