    　　内史府
    　　省架阁库
    　　左右部架阁库
    　　长信寺
    　　喝盏
    　　天子凡宴飨一人执酒觞立于右阶一人执柏板立于左阶执板者抑其声賛曰鄂宻竒执觞者如其声和之曰达卜实则执板者节一板从而王侯卿相合坐者坐合立者立于是众乐皆作然后进酒诣上前上饮毕授觞众乐皆止别奏曲以饮陪位之官谓之喝盏盖沿袭亡金旧礼至今不废诸王大臣非有赐命不敢用焉鄂托克达拜彼中方言未暇考求其义碧珠示防
    　　文宗潜邸金陵日歳当戊辰适太平兴国寺铸大钟为金数万斤方在冶上至其所取相嵌碧珠指环黙祝曰若天命在躬此当不壊即投液中钟成其欵有曰皇帝万歳珠宛然在其上若故识之而坚固完好光采明发不以灼毁万目惊覩欢叹如一及登大寳方与近侍言向时祝天之防

    Explanation

    477

    Translation:

    The Salt Transportation Office of the Capital and Its Surrounding Areas

    • The Tax Collection Office of the Capital
    • The Wine Tax Collection Office of the Capital
    • The Military Command Office for Both Northern and Southern Forces in the Capital
    • The Northern Military Command Office
    • The Inner History Office
    • The Provincial Repository for Official Documents
    • The Repositories for Documents on the Left and Right Sides of the Government
    • The Long Faith Temple
    • The Ritual of Drinking Wine (喝盏)

    During banquets hosted by the emperor, one person holds a wine cup and stands on the right side of the stairs, while another person holds a wooden board and stands on the left side of the stairs. The person holding the board raises and lowers their voice to chant, "鄂宻竒" (E Mí Qí), and the person holding the wine cup responds in harmony, saying, "达卜实" (Dá Bǔ Shí). Then, the person holding the board strikes the board once, and all those who are seated, including princes, marquises, and high officials, remain seated, while those who are standing remain standing. At this point, all the music begins, and then the wine is presented to the emperor. After the emperor finishes drinking, he hands the cup back, and all the music stops. A different piece of music is then played to drink with the officials who accompany the emperor, a ritual known as "喝盏" (hē zhǎn), which is a continuation of the old customs of the Jin Dynasty and has not been abandoned to this day. Princes and high officials may not use this ritual unless they have been granted special permission. "鄂托克达拜" (É Tuō Kè Dá Bái) is a Mongolian term that may refer to a form of greeting or respect, but its exact meaning is uncertain. "碧珠" (Bì Zhū) may refer to a type of pearl or a specific object, but its exact meaning is unclear.

    During the reign of Emperor Wenzong, when he was living in the Jinling Palace in Nanjing, in the year of the Earth Dragon (戊辰), he visited the Great Peace and National Prosperity Temple in Luoyang, Henan Province, where a large bell was being cast using tens of thousands of pounds of bronze. When the bell was in the furnace, he took a pearl ring and secretly prayed, "If the mandate of heaven is with me, this bell should not be damaged." He then threw the ring into the molten bronze. When the bell was completed, the inscription on it read, "Long Live the Emperor," and the pearl ring was still intact, as if it had recognized its place and remained unharmed, shining brightly and not scorched. All who saw it were amazed and exclaimed in unison. When he ascended the throne, he spoke to his close attendants about the vow he had made to heaven at that time.

    478

    辍耕録卷二十一
    <子部,小说家类,杂事之属,辍耕录>
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録二十二　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰圣门弟子
    　　孔门弟子姓字见诸家语论语史记等书金华张君孟兼稽考异同集为章句以便记诵即古急就之义也其文曰繄昔圣门弟子三千身通六艺七十二人徳行著称顔回子渊冉耕伯牛闵损子骞及冉雍仲为四科之先宰予子我【并鲁人】端木赐子贡【卫人】言语是称赐言多中乃多才艺仲由季路【陈人】冉求子有【鲁人】政事并着言偃子游【吴人】卜商子夏【卫人】文学著名孰可方驾曾参子舆纯孝全归父防子晳浴沂舞雩囘父无繇【并鲁人】仲由同字有公晳哀【齐】人字以季次县成子祺

    Explanation

    478

    The Record of Abandoned Plowings, Volume Twenty-One
    <Novels, Miscellaneous Category, The Record of Abandoned Plowings>
    　　Imperially Approved Complete Library in Four Branches
    　　The Record of Abandoned Plowings, Volume Twenty-Two　　　Yuan Dynasty, Tao Zongyi, Compiler of the Disciples of Confucius
    　　The names and courtesy names of the disciples of Confucius are recorded in various texts such as the Analects of Confucius, the Records of the Grand Historian, and others. Zhang Mengjian from Jinhua examined the differences and similarities, compiled them into chapters and sentences to facilitate memorization, following the meaning of the ancient text "Rapid Mastery." The text reads: In ancient times, there were three thousand disciples of Confucius, seventy-two of whom personally mastered the six arts. Among them, seventy-two were renowned for their virtue and conduct, including Ziyuan, Ziyun, Zengeng, Bo Niu, Min Sun, Ziqian, and Ran Yong, who were the foremost in the four categories. Zaiyu and Ziyi (both from the state of Lu), Duanmu Ci and Zigong (from the state of Wei) were renowned for their eloquence, and Ci often spoke with insight, possessing many talents. Zhong You and Jilu (from the state of Chen), Ran Qiu and Ziyou (from the state of Lu) were both renowned for their government affairs. Yanyan and Ziyou (from the state of Wu), Bushang and Zixia (from the state of Wei) were famous for their literature. Who can be compared to Zengyan and Ziyu, who fully achieved filial piety? Fufang and Zixi bathed in the Yi River and danced under the wind pavilion. Hui and Wu you, also known as Gongxi Ai (a person from Qi), also known as Jici, and Xiancheng and Ziqi.

    479

    左郢子行【并鲁人】乐欬【亡】顔哙同字子声其字子羽澹台灭明【并鲁人】子之是字公祖句兹【亡】其有秦非亦字子之孔忠子蔑叔仲会子期乃子旗字者粤巫马施顔之防子叔申枨子续商瞿子木【并鲁人】蘧瑗伯玉【卫人】有若子有公伯寮子周【并鲁人】其申党【一作续】者止字曰周【亡】司马犂耕【宋人】乃字子牛颛孙师子张【陈人】公冶长子长【齐人】一字子禽其陈亢子亢【陈人】名而不字唯句井疆高柴子羔【并卫人】公肩定子中【亡】有南宫适子容【鲁人】薛子

    Explanation

    479

    Zuo Yingzi Xing, a native of Lu, Le Kai (deceased), Yan Huai, courtesy name Ziseng, Dan Tai Mie Ming, a native of Lu, Zi Zhi, courtesy name Gongzu Quzi, Qin Fei, courtesy name Zizi, Kong Zhong, courtesy name Zimie, Shu Zhong Hui, courtesy name Ziqi and Ziqi, Yue Wu Ma Shi Yan, courtesy name Zhifangzi, Shu Shen Cheng, courtesy name Zixu, Shang Qu, courtesy name Zimu, a native of Lu, Qu Yuan, courtesy name Boyu, a native of Wei, You Ruo, courtesy name Ziyou, Gong Bo Liao, courtesy name Zizhou, a native of Lu, Shen Dang, also known as Zixu, Zhi, courtesy name Zhou (deceased), Si Ma Li Geng, a native of Song, courtesy name Ziniu, Zhuang Sun Shi, courtesy name Zizhang, a native of Chen, Gong Ye Chang, courtesy name Zichang, a native of Qi, courtesy name Ziqin, Chen Kang, courtesy name Zikang, a native of Chen, known only by his given name, Gou Jing Jiang Gao Chai, courtesy name Zigao, Gong Jian Ding, courtesy name Zizhong (deceased), Nan Gong Shi, courtesy name Zirong, a native of Lu, Xue, courtesy name Zizi.

    480

    从【亡】公西箴【鲁人】及公西舆如【亡】字子上同穰驷赤子徒【秦人】子洁子庸【卫人】漆雕开【鲁人】琴牢子开字同宓不齐子贱【并衞人】步叔乘子车【齐人】其漆雕哆邽巽子敛字俱【并鲁人】粤梁鳣者其字叔鱼【齐人】秦祖子南【秦】人燕伋【亡】樊湏子迟【齐人】亦有后处字以里之【亡】原宪【鲁人】同字子思郑国荣旂字子徒子祺伯防子柝公首夏子乘施之常子恒【并亡】公良孺子正【陈人】冉孺子鲁冉季子产字子栁者顔幸【并鲁人】字子象者县亶石作蜀子

    Explanation

    480

    A List of Individuals and Their Descriptions

    From Gongxi Zhen of Lu, and Gongxi Yuru, both with the courtesy name Zishang, to Rang Si Chi, a disciple of Qin; Zijie and Ziyong of Wei, Qidiao Kai of Lu, Qin Lao, a disciple of Zikai, with the courtesy name Tong; Mifu Buqi, a disciple of Zijian, all from Wei; Bushu Cheng, a disciple of Ziche, from Qi; Qidiao Duo, Guixun, and Zilian, all disciples of Qidiao, with the courtesy names Ju, all from Lu; Yue Liang Zhan, with the courtesy name Shuyu, from Qi; Qin Zu, a disciple of Zinan, from Qin; Yan Ji, a disciple of Yan, with the courtesy name Wu; Fan Xu, a disciple of Zichi, from Qi; Hou Chu, a disciple of Yili, with the courtesy name Wu; Yuan Xian of Lu, with the courtesy name Zisi; Zheng Guo Rong Qi, a disciple of Zitong, with the courtesy name Ziqi, Bo Fang, a disciple of Zituo, Gong Shou Xia, a disciple of Zicheng, Shi Zhi Chang, a disciple of Ziheng, all with the courtesy names Wu; Gongliang Ru, a disciple of Zizheng, from Chen; Ran Ru, a disciple of Zilu, Ran Ji, a disciple of Zichan, with the courtesy name Ziliu, Yan Xing, all from Lu, with the courtesy names Zi Xiang, Xian Dan, Shi Zu, and Shu Zi.

    481

    明【并亡】公孙龙子石【楚人】商泽子季奚谷箴子晳之罕父黒子索其原亢籍仍字子籍【并亡】字子丕字子丕者曰惟秦商【楚人】秦冉字开顔祖字襄【并亡】任不齐子选【楚人】曹防子循漆雕徒父字曰子文顔高【一作刻】子骄鄡单子家【并亡】顔何字冉公西赤子华【并鲁人】猗与多贤升堂入室慨举世之所传名固逾乎七十乃稽纪载尚遗其实家语史迁所録不一嗟嗟小子何敢忘逸爰重列以自识俾防士之易述其不铨次岂縁声律不韪之辠莫敢自防尚同好事之君子幸有以钉愚之失

    Explanation

    481

    A Record of Virtuous Scholars

    Among the virtuous scholars, there are Gongsun Long, a native of Chu, and Shang Zezi, Jixi, Gupin, and Xizhi, all from various regions. There is also Hanfuhei, whose origin is traced back to his father, and Kang Ji, who is known by his courtesy name Zijie. Qin Shang, another native of Chu, and Qin Ran, known by his courtesy name Kaiyan, are also mentioned. Renshi, a native of Chu, and Caofang, known by his courtesy name Xuan, are noted alongside Qidiao Tufu, known by his courtesy name Ziwén. Yan Gao, sometimes referred to as Yanke, and Zijiao, are also recorded. Xiaodan Zijia, Yan He, known by his courtesy name Ran, Gongxi Chi, and Zihua, all from the state of Lu, are celebrated for their virtue. Indeed, the number of virtuous scholars exceeds seventy. Upon examining historical records, it is evident that many of their true stories remain untold. The Analects of Confucius and the works of Sima Qian, the historian, do not always agree in their accounts. Alas, young scholars, how dare you forget the extraordinary contributions of these virtuous individuals? Therefore, I have listed them again for my own recognition, so that scholars may easily recount their deeds. If they are not arranged or classified, it is not because of the rules of poetry, but rather due to the fault of not being proper. No one dares to protect themselves from criticism. May this be shared with those who appreciate such matters, and may it help to correct any mistakes made by the foolish.

    482

    顔无繇【音遥音由】　县【音】成　公祖句【音钧】兹　句【音勾】井疆宓【音宻当作伏】不齐　邽【音圭】巽　鄡【音枭】单【音善】　漆雕哆【音侈】

    <子部,小说家类,杂事之属,辍耕录,卷二十二>
    　　潘昻霄志曰延祐乙夘春圣天子以四海万国之广轸念庶民艰虞罔控告也分使诣外郡诸道布扬徳心戚休兴替之清洿激之畿甸宻迩独不得均其泽越五月诏前翰林学士承防臣库库楚翰林侍读臣昻霄奉使宣抚京畿西道臣昻霄承命惊悸防措唯务罄竭忠赤尽恤民瘼后已阔公一日语昻霄余尝从余兄荣禄公都实抵西国穷河源耳之不觉瞿然以骇有是乎哉请毕其语公曰世祖皇帝至元十七年歳在庚辰钦承圣谕黄河之入中国夏后氏导之知自积石矣汉唐所不能悉其源今为吾地朕欲极其源之所出营一城俾畨贾互市规置航传凡物贡水行达京师古无有也朕为

    Explanation

    482

    顔无繇 (Yán Wúyáo)　县成 (Xiànchéng)　公祖句兹 (Gōngzǔ Jùzī)　句井疆 (Jùjǐngjiāng)　宓不齐 (Mì Bùqí)　邽巽 (Guīxùn)　鄡单 (Xiédān)　漆雕哆 (Qīdiāo Duō)

    In the spring of the year 1315, during the reign of the Holy Emperor, who ruled over the vast expanse of the four seas and myriad states, he was deeply concerned about the hardships and worries of the common people, who had no one to appeal to. He therefore dispatched envoys to various regions to spread his benevolent intentions and to address the clarity or corruption of the rise and fall of governance. However, the capital region and its immediate surroundings did not receive the same benefits. In the fifth month, the emperor issued an edict appointing the former Hanlin Scholar and Defense Minister, Lord Kukuchu, and the Hanlin Reader, Lord Pang Aoxiao, to go on a mission to pacify the western part of the capital region. Lord Pang Aoxiao, upon receiving the command, was filled with anxiety and took great care to fulfill his duties with utmost loyalty and diligence, ensuring the welfare of the people. Later, after a considerable time, Lord Pang Aoxiao spoke to the emperor, saying, "I once accompanied my elder brother, Lord Dushi, who was granted the title of Minister of Revenue, to the western country to trace the source of the Yellow River. When we reached the Jishui Mountains, I was suddenly startled and asked, 'Is this possible?' Please allow me to finish my story." The emperor said, "In the seventeenth year of the Zhiyuan era, during the reign of Emperor Shizu (Kublai Khan), I received the imperial decree. The Yellow River enters China, and it is said that the Yellow Emperor controlled it, knowing it originates from the Jishui Mountains. The Han and Tang dynasties could not fully trace its source. Now it belongs to us, and I wish to trace its source to its origin. I will build a city to facilitate trade between Chinese and foreign merchants and establish shipping routes so that all goods transported by water can reach the capital. There was none before."

    483

    之以永后来无穷利益盖难其人都实汝旧人且习诸国语往图汝谐授招讨使佩金虎符以行是歳四月至河州州东六十里有宁河驿驿西南五六十里山曰杀马闗林麓穹隘译言塔斯达巴啓足寖高一日程至巅西迈愈高四阅月约四五千里始抵河源冬还图城传位置以闻上悦往营之授土蕃等处都元帅仍金虎符置寮寀督工工师悉资内地造航为艘六十城传措工物完库库楚驿闻适僧格征恭噶蔵布尔力沮遂止翼歳兄都实旋都河源在土蕃朶甘斯西鄙有泉百余或泉或潦水沮洳散涣方可七八十里且泥淖溺不胜人迹逼观弗克旁履高山下眎灿若列星以故名鄂端诺尔鄂

    Explanation

    483

    To bring endless benefits to future generations, it is indeed difficult to find such a person. Dusi, you are an old acquaintance who has learned various languages. Go and plan to appoint you as the Commander-in-Chief with a Golden Tiger Talisman to proceed. In that year, in April, he arrived at Hezhou. Sixty miles east of the city is Ninghe Post. Fifty to sixty miles southwest of the post is a mountain called Shimaga Pass. The forest and foothills are vast and narrow. The translation says that Tastaba begins to rise higher. After one day's journey, he reached the summit. Moving west, it becomes even higher. After four months, traveling about four to five thousand li, he finally reached the source of the river. In winter, he returned to the city and reported the location. The emperor was pleased and went to establish it. He was appointed as the Grand Marshal of the Tibetan region and was still given the Golden Tiger Talisman. He set up an office and supervised the workers. All the craftsmen were from inland. He built sixty ships. The city and the post received the completed goods and materials. Just as Sengge was campaigning against Gongga Zangbu, the effort was halted due to discouragement. In the following year, Dusi would return to the source of the river. In the western border of the Tibetan region, there are over a hundred springs. Some are springs, some are marshes, and the water is scattered and dispersed. The area is about seventy to eighty li in size and is so muddy that it is impassable for people. Upon close inspection, it was impossible to walk around it. From a high mountain, it looks like scattered stars. Therefore, it was named E Dong Noro.

    484

    端译言星宿也羣流奔凑近五七里汇二巨泽名鄂楞诺尔自西徂东连属吞噬广轮马行一日程迤逦东骛成川号齐必勒河二三日程水西南来名伊尔齐与齐必勒河合三四日程南来名呼兰又水东南来名伊拉齐合流入齐必勒其流寖大始名黄河然水清人可渉又一二日岐裂八九股名伊逊鄂罗木译言九度通广六七里马亦可度又四五日程水浑浊土人抱革囊乘马过之民聚落紏木干象舟傅毛革以济仅容两人继是两山峡东广可一里二里或半里深叵测矣朶甘斯东北鄙有大雪山名伊拉玛博啰其山最高译言腾格哩哈达即昆仑也山腹至顶皆雪冬夏不消土人言逺年成氷

    Explanation

    484

    Beginning with the stars, numerous streams converge and flow, covering a distance of about five to seven miles, where they meet two vast lakes known as "鄂楞诺尔." These lakes stretch from west to east, merging into each other and forming an expansive area that takes a full day's journey by horse to traverse. The water then flows eastward in a winding path, eventually forming a river called "齐必勒河." After two or three days of travel, the river is joined by another stream coming from the southwest, named "伊尔齐," which merges with the Qibile River. After three or four days, a third river, coming from the south and named "呼兰," joins the Qibile River. Another river, coming from the southeast and named "伊拉齐," also flows into the Qibile River, causing its flow to gradually increase until it is known as the Yellow River. Despite its name, the water remains clear enough for people to cross. After one or two more days, the river splits into eight or nine branches, known as "伊逊鄂罗木," which are described as nine degrees wide, spanning about six to seven miles, and wide enough for horses to cross. After four or five more days, the water becomes turbid. Local people cross this section by holding leather bags and riding horses. The local population builds makeshift boats from wooden frames and animal hides to cross the river, with each boat only large enough for two people. Beyond this, the river flows through a narrow canyon between two mountains, with the width varying from one to two miles or half a mile, and the depth being impossible to measure. In the northeast of Dokang, there is a large snow-capped mountain named "伊拉玛博啰," which is the highest peak, also known as "腾格哩哈达" or the Kunlun Mountains. From the base to the peak, it is covered in snow, which does not melt in winter or summer. The local people say that the ice has formed over many years.

    485

    时六月见之自八九股水至昆仑行二十日程河行昆仑南半日程地又四五日程至地名库济克特二地相属又三日程地名哈喇伯勒齐尔四达之冲也多冦盗有官兵镇防昆仑迤西人简少多处山南山皆不穹峻水亦散漫兽有髦牛野马狼狍羱羊之其东山益高地亦渐下岸狭隘有狐可一跃越之者行五六日程有水西南来名纳琳哈喇译言细黄河也又两日程水南来名竒尔穆蘓二水合流入河河北行转西至昆仑北二日程地水过之北流少东又北流约行半月程至贵徳州地名必齐勒始有州事官府州隶河州置司土蕃等处宣慰司所辖又四五日程至积石州即禹贡积石五日程

    Explanation

    485

    In the sixth month, I saw that from the confluence of several rivers to the Kunlun Mountains, it took twenty days of travel. The river journey south of the Kunlun Mountains took half a day, followed by another four or five days of travel to a place called Kuziket. These two places are adjacent, and another three days of travel led to a place called Harabulqi'er, which is a strategic location with frequent bandit activity. There are soldiers stationed there to defend the area. To the west of the Kunlun Mountains, there are fewer people, with scattered mountains and water. The region is home to various animals such as wild cattle, wild horses, wolves, roe deer, and wild goats. To the east, the mountains are higher and gradually descend, with a narrow riverbank where a fox can leap across. After five or six days of travel, a river from the southwest is named Nalin Halah, which is translated as the "Fine Yellow River." Another two days of travel led to a river from the south named Qiermusu, which merges into the main river. The river flows north, then west to the Kunlun Mountains, where it turns slightly east before flowing north again. After about half a month of travel, the destination is Guidu Prefecture, also known as Biqile, where there are official offices under the jurisdiction of the River Prefecture's Office for Pacifying the Tibetans and Other Places. Another four or five days of travel led to Jishizhou, also known as Yu贡 Jishi, mentioned in the Book of Documents (Shujing), and it took five days to reach.

    486

    至河州安乡闗一日程至打罗坑东北行一日程洮河水南来入河又一日程至兰州其下过北卜渡至鸣沙州过应吉里州正东行至宁夏府南东行即东胜州隶西京大同路地面自发源至汉地南北涧溪细流傍贯莫知纪极山皆草山石山至积石方林木畅茂世言河九折彼地有二折盖竒尔穆蘓及贵徳州必齐勒也汉张骞使絶域覊聨拘执艰厄百罹歴大宛月氐等数国其傍大国五六皆称传闻以为穷河源乌能覩所谓河源哉史称河有两源一出于阗一出葱岭于阗水北行出葱岭河注蒲海不流洑至临洮出焉今洮水自南来非蒲明矣询之土人言于阗葱岭水其下流散之沙碛又

    Explanation

    486

    From Hezhou to Anxiang Pass is one day's journey, then another day's journey to Daluo Pit, traveling northeast. The Tao River flows south and joins the main river, and another day's journey brings one to Lanzhou. Below Lanzhou, one passes through Beibudu to Mingsha State, then through Yingjili State, traveling directly east to Ningxia Prefecture, and then south-east to Dongsheng State, which is part of the Datong Road in the Western Capital region. From its source to the Han lands, the north and south streams and small rivers run parallel, their extent unknown. The mountains are mostly grassy and rocky, but near Jishu, the forests are lush and abundant. The world says the river has nine bends, and in this place, there are two bends, which is remarkable in places like Murong and Guide Prefectures. Han Zhang Qian was sent to the distant lands, where he faced many hardships and was captured by the states of Dayuan and Yuezhi. The surrounding large states are numerous, and all are known only by hearsay. How could one see the true source of the river? Historically, it is said that the river has two sources, one from Yutian and one from the Kunlun Mountains. The Yutian water flows north and joins the Kunlun River, which empties into the Puli Sea. It does not flow directly but re-emerges at Lintao. The Tao River now comes from the south, clearly not from Puli. Asking the locals, they say that the Yutian and Kunlun waters flow down into the sandy deserts.

    487

    有言河与天河通寻源得织女支机石以归亦妄也昆仑至嵩高五万里阆风元圃积瑶华盖仙人所居又何耶唐史土蕃传河上流由河洪济梁南二千里水益狭春可渉秋夏乃胜舟其南三百里三山中高而四下曰紫山古所谓昆仑其言颇然止称河源其间云国家敞天威亘天所覆焘无间海内外冠带万国罔非臣妾视汉唐为不足讶故穷河源去万里若歩闺闼嘻盛典也不可不志因志之都实族女真富察氏统乌思藏路暨招讨都元帅凡三至吐蕃库库楚今除甘肃行省参知政事是歳八月初吉翰林侍读学士中奉大夫知制诰同修国史臣潘昻霄谨述柯九思序云河源有志自本朝始

    Explanation

    487

    It is said that the Yellow River connects to the Milky Way, and that someone once found a stone left by the Weaving Maid of the Milky Way at its source and brought it back. This is also false. From Kunlun to Songshan, it is said to be five thousand miles, with the mythical gardens of Langfeng and Yuanpu, filled with precious jade flowers, where immortals reside. How can this be? The "Tubo" chapter in the Tang dynasty history describes the upper reaches of the Yellow River, which flows from the River Hongji to the south for two thousand li, becoming narrower in spring, when it can be crossed, but only in autumn and summer can boats pass. Three hundred li to the south are three mountains, with a central peak that rises high and slopes down on all sides, called Zishan, the ancient Kunlun. The description is somewhat similar, but it only mentions the source of the Yellow River, stating that the state's imperial power extends to the heavens, covering the entire world without exception, with all nations within its borders being subjects. It views the Han and Tang dynasties as insufficiently impressive. Therefore, traveling to the source of the Yellow River, a distance of ten thousand li, is like entering one's own home. What a grand and significant event! It cannot be omitted, and it is therefore recorded. The Du Shi clan, the Jurchen Fu Cha family, governed the Uyghur and Tibetan regions and served as the Commander-in-Chief of the Pacification and Subjugation. He visited Tibet three times and is now the Deputy Chief Censor of the Gansu Province. In this year, on the first day of August, the Secretary of the Imperial Academy, the Minister of the Imperial Academy, the Chief Secretary, and the Co-Editor of the National History, Minister Pan Angxiao, respectfully recorded this. Ko Jiu-si wrote a preface saying, "The record of the source of the Yellow River began in this dynasty."

    488

    前乎此曷为未有志河源者道路辽阻所传闻异辞莫能防河之源也山经曰敦薨之水西流注于泑泽出于昆仑之东北陬实惟河源而水经载河出昆仑经十余国乃至泑泽山经又称阳纡之山河出其中凌门之山河出其中穆天子传亦云阳纡之山河曰冯夷所居是惟河宗氏释氏西域志称阿耨达大山上有大渊水即昆仑山也地里志亦称昆仑山在临羌西而汉书载河出两源或称有或称无而河源所着异同况世殊代易名地亦异终莫能有究之者我太祖皇帝二十有一年春正月征西夏夏取甘肃等城秋取西凉府遂过沙陀至黄河九渡按昆仑当九渡下流则昆仑固已归我职方氏矣

    Explanation

    488

    Before this, why has no one recorded the source of the river? The distant and difficult roads lead to varying accounts, and no one could trace the source of the river. The Shan Jing (山经) states that the Dunhong (敦薨) water flows westward into the Yaoze (泑泽), emerging from the northeast corner of Kunlun (昆仑), which is the true source of the river. However, the Shui Jing (水经) claims that the Yellow River originates from Kunlun (昆仑), passing through more than ten countries before reaching Yaoze (泑泽). The Shan Jing also mentions the Yangyu (阳纡) mountain, where the river flows, and the Lingmen (凌门) mountain, where the river also flows. The Mu Tianzi Zhuan (穆天子传) also states that the Yangyu (阳纡) mountain is where the river god Fengyi (冯夷) resides, known as the Hezong (河宗) clan. Buddhist texts on the Western Regions (Xiyu) describe Anuoda (阿耨达) as the mountain with a large body of water, equated with Kunlun (昆仑). Dili Zhi (地里志) also places Kunlun (昆仑) to the west of Lin Qiang (临羌). The Han Shu (汉书) records that the Yellow River has two sources, sometimes claiming it exists and sometimes not, leading to conflicting accounts of the river's origin. Given the differences in accounts across different eras and the changes in names and locations, no one has been able to fully investigate the matter. In the twenty-first year of the reign of our founding emperor, in the first month of spring, he launched a campaign against the Western Xia. The Western Xia captured cities like Gansu (甘肃) in the autumn and Xiliang (西凉), crossing Shatuo (沙陀) to reach the ninth crossing of the Yellow River. According to this, Kunlun (昆仑) is downstream of the ninth crossing, so Kunlun (昆仑) has already come under the jurisdiction of our empire.

    489

    宪宗皇帝二年命皇太弟实喇帅诸部军征西域凡六年辟封疆四万里于是河源及所注枝出者尽在封域之内当时在行有能记其说皆得于目击非放也逮世祖皇帝功成治定天下殷富遂命臣都实置郡河源故翰林侍读学士潘公得究其详实搜源析而作斯志乃知更昆仑行一月始穷河源于戯当四海混一之盛闻广见覈致数千载莫能究者俾后世有考而传信焉岂斯文之光实家无疆之休也公之子诩能不坠其先业増光而润色之至顺间以同知嘉定州事来吴将刋是书行于世属九思叙其说于篇端元统元年冬十有一月日南至奎章阁学士院鍳书博士文林郎柯九思序
    　　皇太子署牒
    　　国朝政事正六品以下官中书奉勑署牒以命之牒具中书官位最尊者令也署牒者自丞相以下而不敢以烦令惟皇太子立必兼中书令枢宻使皇太子既受册即中书上日独署一牒明日省臣以其名闻天子即以宣命超拜五品官其人自非素亲近有誉望取于羣臣者不得也

    Explanation

    489

    During the second year of Emperor Xianzong's reign, the crown prince Shi La was ordered to lead troops from various tribes to campaign in the Western Regions. Over the course of six years, they expanded the territory by four hundred thousand li, encompassing the source of the river and its tributaries within their borders. At the time, those who accompanied the expedition and were able to record their observations did so based on firsthand experience, not fabrication. By the time of Emperor Shizu, when the great work was accomplished and the state was stabilized, the world became prosperous. He then appointed Minister Du Shi to establish a prefecture at the source of the river. The former Hanlin Scholar and Reader, Pan Gong, was able to thoroughly investigate the details and explore the source and analyze the branches, creating this record. It was thus known that traversing the Kunlun Mountains for a month was required to finally reach the source of the river. In the great unity of the four seas, hearing widely and seeing critically, reaching back thousands of years, no one had been able to investigate it as thoroughly. This enables future generations to verify and believe, how can this be the glory of the text and the boundless blessing of the nation? His son Xu was able to maintain the achievements of his ancestors, adding luster and refining it. During the Zhishun period, he came to Wu as a co-governor of Jiading Prefecture and prepared to publish this book for the world. He entrusted Jiǔsī to write the preface at the beginning of the text. On the winter solstice in the first month of the first year of the Yuantong era, the Preface was written by Ko Jiǔsī, a scholar and official of the Qiguang Ge Academy.

    The decree issued by the crown prince: In the governance of the state, officials of the sixth rank and below are appointed by the chancellor through imperial command. The decree specifies the highest-ranking official in the chancellery as the chancellor. The decree is issued by officials below the chancellor and does not trouble the chancellor. When the crown prince is established, he must also hold the positions of chancellor and chief military commissioner. When the crown prince receives his investiture, he alone issues a decree in the chancellery. The next day, the provincial officials report his name to the emperor, and he is immediately appointed to a fifth-rank official. The person must be someone close and well-regarded by the emperor and the court officials.

    490

    禽戏
    　　余在杭州日尝见一弄百禽者蓄七枚大小凡七等置几上击鼓以使之则第一等大者先至几心伏定第二等者从而登其背直至第七等小者登第六等之背乃竪身直伸其尾向上宛如小塔状谓之乌叠塔又见蓄虾防九枚先置一小墩于席中其大者乃踞坐之余八小者左右对列大者作一声众亦作一声大者作数声众亦作数声既而小者一一至大
    者前防首作声如作礼状而退谓之虾蟆说法至松江见一全真道士寓太古庵一日取二鳅鱼一黄色一黒色小大相侔者用药涂利刃各断其腰互换接缀首尾异色投放水内浮游如故郡人卫立中以盆池养之经半月方死叠塔说法固教习之功但其质性蠢蠢非他禽鸟可比诚难矣哉若夫断而复续死而复生药欤法欤是未可知也但剧戱中似此者果亦罕见哉

    Explanation

    490

    Bird Mimicry Exercise

    During my time in Hangzhou, I once saw a performer who trained a hundred birds. He kept seven turtles of varying sizes and placed them on a turtle-shaped table. He struck a drum to command them, and the largest turtle would first crawl to the center of the table and lie down, followed by the second largest, which would climb onto its back, and so on until the smallest turtle climbed onto the sixth turtle's back, forming a tower-like structure. This formation was called the "Turtle Tower Stacking." I also saw a performer who kept nine frogs. He placed a small stool in the middle of the mat, and the largest frog would sit on it. The other eight smaller frogs would arrange themselves around it, and when the largest frog made a sound, the others would follow suit, repeating the sound several times. This formation was called the "Frog Preaching." In Songjiang, I met a Daoist practitioner of the Quanzhen School who lived in Taigu Temple. One day, he took two yellow and black eels of similar size, cut them in half, and reattached them so that their heads and tails were reversed. He then placed them in water, and they swam normally. A local man named Wei Lizhong kept them in a small pond, and after half a month, they died. The "Turtle Tower Stacking" and "Frog Preaching" formations were clearly the result of training, but the nature of the turtles and frogs is dull and unresponsive, unlike other birds. It is truly difficult to achieve such feats. As for cutting and rejoining, and bringing the dead back to life, whether it is through medicine or magic, is uncertain. However, such performances are indeed rare and uncommon.

    491

    虎祸
    　　大徳间荆南境内有九人山行值雨避于路傍旧土洞中忽有一虎来踞洞口哮咆怒视目光射人内一人素愚八人者宻议虎若不得人恶得去因绐愚者先出我辈共掩杀之愚者意未决遂各解一衣作人形掷而出之虎愈怒八人倂力排愚者于外虎即衔置洞口怒视如前湏臾土洞压塌八人皆死愚者获生夫当颠沛患难之际乃欲以八人之智而防一人之愚其用心亦险矣天道果梦梦耶
    　　河南妇死
    　　河南妇世为河南民家天兵下江南妇被虏姑与夫行求数年得之湖南妇已妻千戸某饶于财情好甚洽视夫姑若涂人防有防凡妇人被虏许银赎敢匿者死某惧罪亟遣妇妇坚不行夫姑留以俟妇闭其室弗与通遂号恸顿絶而去行未百歩青天无云而雷回视妇已震死钱唐白湛囦先生记以诗曰从军古云乐获罪祷应难母望明珠复夫求破镜完押衙逢义士公主奉春官为报河南妇天刑不可干

    Explanation

    491

    Tiger Disaster

    During the Dade era, in the region of Jingnan, nine people were traveling in the mountains and encountered rain. They took shelter in an old earthen cave by the roadside. Suddenly, a tiger came and sat at the entrance of the cave, roaring angrily and glaring, its eyes seeming to shoot beams at the people. One of them was usually foolish, and the other eight secretly discussed, saying, "If the tiger doesn't get someone, how can it leave?" They then deceived the foolish one to go out first, and they would all surround and kill it together. The foolish one's mind was not yet made up, so they each took off an outer garment and made human shapes, throwing them out. The tiger became even angrier. The eight of them used all their strength to push the foolish one outside. The tiger immediately carried him and placed him at the entrance of the cave, glaring as before. In a short while, the earth cave collapsed, and all eight of them died. The foolish one survived. In times of hardship and adversity, they wanted to use the intelligence of eight people to guard against the foolishness of one. Their intentions were also very dangerous. Is the way of heaven truly so dreamlike?

    Death of a Woman from Henan

    A woman from Henan Province, who was from a family of commoners, was captured during the Mongol invasion of the Southern Song Dynasty. Her mother-in-law and husband traveled for several years to find her in Hunan Province. Their relationship was very harmonious, but they treated her mother-in-law and husband like dirt. They guarded against her. The sky was clear, yet there was thunder. She died. Bai Zhancong, a historical figure, recorded the event in a poem, saying, "From ancient times, military service has been a source of joy, but the consequences can be severe. The mother hopes for the return of her pearl, and the husband seeks the reunion of the broken mirror. The officer meets a righteous man, and the princess receives a spring official. In response to the heavens, the punishment is inevitable."

    492

    玊堂嫁妓
    　　姚文公【燧】为翰林学士承防日玉堂设宴歌妓罗列中有一人秀丽闲雅微操闽音公使来前问其履歴初不以实对叩之再泣而诉曰妾乃建宁人氏真西山之后也父官朔方时禄薄不足以给侵贷公帑无偿遂卖入娼家流落至此公命之坐仍遣使诣丞相三寳努请为落籍丞相素敬公意公欲以侍巾栉即令教坊检籍除之公得报语一小吏【黄防后显官者】曰我以此女为汝妻女即以我为父也吏忻然从命京师之人相传以为盛事云嘉兴贝阙尝有诗曰断丝弃道边何日縁长松堕羽别炎洲不复巢梧桐昔在至元日六合车书同玉堂盛文士燕集来雍雍金刀手割鲜酒给葡萄浓坐有一枝春秀色不可双【叶】娉婷刘碧玉绰

    Explanation

    492

    The Jade Hall Marries a Courtesan

    When Yao Wen Gong, also known as Yao Sui, served as the Hanlin Scholar and Chief of the Jade Hall, the hall held a banquet with a troupe of singing and dancing girls. Among them was one woman who was exceptionally beautiful and graceful, with a slight accent from the Min region. Yao Wen Gong summoned her to the front and asked about her background. She initially did not answer truthfully. He questioned her again, and she wept as she recounted her story: "I am from Jianning, a descendant of Zhen Xishan. My father served in the northern frontier, where his salary was insufficient to support us. He borrowed from the public treasury and was unable to repay it, so he sold me into the life of a courtesan, and I have been wandering here since." Yao Wen Gong had her sit down and sent a messenger to Chancellor Sanbao Nu to request that she be removed from the register of courtesans. Chancellor Sanbao Nu, who respected Yao Wen Gong's intentions, agreed to help. Yao Wen Gong wanted to take her as his concubine, so he ordered the Music Bureau to remove her name from the register. Upon receiving the news, Yao Wen Gong told a minor official, Huang Fang, who later became a high-ranking official: "I will give this woman to you as your wife. You will consider me your father." The official happily agreed. The people of the capital spread the story as a great event. A poet from Jiaxing, Bei Que, once wrote a poem about it:

    A broken thread discarded by the roadside,
    When will it grow into a tall pine?
    Feathers falling from a warm island,
    No longer nesting in the phoenix tree.
    In the past, during the Zhizhou era,
    The world was unified under one rule and script.
    The Jade Hall was filled with scholars and writers,
    Gathering for banquets, harmonious and splendid.
    With a golden knife, fresh meat was cut,
    Wine as rich as grapes.
    Seated with a branch of spring,
    Her beauty was unparalleled.
    Graceful Liu Biyu, elegant and refined.

    This translation aims to capture the essence and cultural nuances of the original text while ensuring it reads naturally in English.

    493

    约商玲珑寳钏金雀钗已觉燕赵空或闻操南音未解歌北风上客惊且疑姓氏初未通问之惭复泣乃起陈始终妾本建宁女逺出西山翁父母生妾时谓是金母童梨花鎻院落燕子窥帘栊迢迢官朔方位卑食不充侵贷国有刑桎梏加父躬粥女以自赎白璧沦泥中秋娘教歌舞屡入明光宫永为娼家妇遂属梨园工京华多少年门外嘶青骢不如孟光丑犹得嫁梁鸿自伤妾薄命失落似秋客闻为三叹天道何懵懵遣使白宰相削籍归旧宗小史十八九勿恨相如穷配尔执箕箒今夕看乘龙鸳鸯并玉鹦鹉开金笼弃汝桃花扇红牙不复从提瓮自汲水絺绤自御冬时多困轗轲事或忻遭逢安知百尺井忽登羣玉峯借问为者谁内相姚文公

    Explanation

    493

    A Woman with Exquisite Jewelry

    During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, a woman from Jian Ning, far from the Western Mountains, was born. Her parents, living in a remote and lowly position, believed she was a child of the Golden Mother. The pear blossoms locked her courtyard, and swallows peered through the curtains. Food was often scarce, and her father was imprisoned for debts. To save her father, she sold herself, and her life was as fleeting and beautiful as autumn flowers.

    She was taught dance and music by a teacher named Mid-Autumn Lady and performed in the palace, where she became a courtesan and eventually part of the imperial music and dance troupe. Young men outside the city gates, riding their horses, were a lively and bustling scene. She compared her situation to that of Meng Guang, who married the virtuous scholar Liang Hong despite her plain appearance. She lamented her fate, feeling that her life was as fleeting and beautiful as autumn flowers. The guest was moved to sigh three times, questioning the fairness and justice of the universe.

    The narrator was sent to inform the chancellor to restore her family's status and return her to her former clan. She was advised not to be envious of Sima Xiangru's poverty and to marry the young man, taking on the role of a wife. That night, they would see the auspicious marriage, symbolized by a pair of mandarin ducks flying together and a parrot released from its golden cage. She would discard the peach blossom fan, no longer following the path of a courtesan. She would live a simple and humble life, wearing coarse clothing during the winter. Her life was full of difficulties and setbacks, but sometimes good fortune could be encountered. She wondered how she could suddenly rise to a high and noble position. She asked who was responsible for her transformation, and it was the Chancellor Yao Wen Gong.

    This translation aims to capture the essence of the original text while ensuring it reads naturally in English. It includes all the key elements and maintains the cultural and historical context as closely as possible.

    494

    数防
    　　至元甲子阿哈玛特拜中书平章领制国用使司时乐府中盛唱胡十八小令知防纬者谓其当擅重权十八年人未之信果于至元壬午伏诛越五年丁亥闰二月僧格拜中书平章立尚书省贪残忍又十倍于阿哈玛特人亦谓桑字拆而为四十八桑字后改作相字亦拆为四十八竟不知应之于夀或应之于职然自立省之日至辛夘正月败绩恰四十八月其神验如是【案僧格原文作桑哥故当时附防如此】

    494

    During the year Jiazi of the Zhiyuan era, Ahmad was appointed as the Chancellor of the Central Secretariat and head of the Department for Managing National Finances. At that time, the imperial music bureau was popularly singing the eighteen little tunes of the Hu style. Those who understood the signs of the times predicted that he would gain significant power. However, people did not believe this prediction for eighteen years, until he was executed in the year Renwu of the Zhiyuan era. Five years later, in the leap month of the second month of the Dinghai year, Sangha was appointed as the Chancellor of the Central Secretariat and established the Shangshu Province. He was even greedier and more cruel than Ahmad, and people also said that the character "桑" (sang) could be split into forty-eight "桑" (sang) characters, and after it was changed to "相" (xiang), it could also be split into forty-eight. It is unclear whether this refers to his lifespan or his position. However, from the day the Shangshu Province was established until the first month of the Xintou year, when he suffered a defeat, it was exactly forty-eight months. This is as mysterious and accurate as this. [Note: The original text referred to "Sangha" as "Sangge," and people at the time believed in these numerological interpretations.]

    495

    戎显再生
    　　大徳戊戍二月二十日张汉臣尚书赵松雪学士费北山漕侯同在杭州泛舟过西湖至毛家步上岸乘肩舆将游水乐洞行里余逢一尼寺赵公偕二公入寺访亲俄而从人来报张公之老防戎显卒死矣亟回至其所呼救不省气絶身僵忽有二道士过一老一防云不妨事老者即于死人面上吹呵防者就篱落间摘一青叶度于老者若作法书符状置死人顶上随即再生顷间失二道士所在或云恐是洞賔变现隐括其姓如此耳
    　　算命得子
    　　檇李郭宗夏尝见建徳路总管赵良臣言都下有李总管者官三品家巨富年逾五十而无子闻枢宻院东有术者设肆算命谈人休咎多竒中试往叩焉且语之曰吾之禄夀已不必言但推有子与否术者笑曰君有子矣何为绐我李曰吾实无子岂绐汝耶术者怒曰君年四十当有子今年五十六矣非绐我而何同坐者皆军官见二人争执甚讶之李沉吟良乆曰吾年四十时一婢有娠吾以职事赴上都比归则吾妻粥之矣莫知所徃若有子则此是也术者曰此子终当还

    Explanation

    495

    Translation:

    The Rebirth of Rong Xian

    On the 20th day of the second month in the 1298th year of the Great Virtue era, Zhang Hanchen, the Minister of Works, Zhao Songxue, the Scholar, and Fei Beishan, the Commissioner, were together in Hangzhou, sailing across the West Lake and landing at Maojia Step. They boarded sedan chairs to visit the Water Music Cave, traveling a few miles when they encountered a nunnery. Zhao Gong accompanied by two others entered the temple to visit relatives. Soon, a servant came to report that Zhang Gong's old servant Rong Xian had died. They hurried back to the place where Rong Xian lay, calling for help but he was unconscious, breathless, and stiff. Suddenly, two Taoist priests appeared, one old and one young. They said, "It's nothing." The older one blew on the dead man's face, and the younger one picked a green leaf from the fence and gave it to the older one, as if performing a ritual and writing talismans. They placed it on the dead man's head, and he immediately came back to life. In a moment, the two Taoist priests disappeared. Some said it might have been a transformation of a Taoist immortal, playfully twisting his surname in this way.

    Predicting the Birth of a Child

    Cui Li's Guo Zongxia once met Jiande Prefecture's Governor Zhao Liangchen, who said there was a Rui Guan, a high-ranking official of the third rank, with a wealthy family, over fifty years old without a child. He heard that to the east of the Zhongshu Province, there was a fortune-teller who set up a shop to predict people's fortunes and misfortunes, often accurately. He tried to go and consult him, saying, "My salary and lifespan are already well-known, but please tell me if I will have a son." The fortune-teller laughed and said, "You will have a son." The protagonist said, "I truly have no son, how could I deceive you?" The fortune-teller became angry and said, "You will have a son when you are forty. You are now fifty-six, how could you deceive me?" Those sitting with us were all military officers, and they were very surprised to see the two arguing. The protagonist pondered for a long time and said, "When I was forty, a maid became pregnant. I went to the capital on official business, and by the time I returned, my wife had sold her. I don't know where she went. If I have a son, this must be him." The fortune-teller said, "This child will eventually return."

    496

    君相别而出时坐中一千户邀李入茶坊告之曰十五年前吾亦无子因到都置一婢则已有孕到家时适吾妻亦有孕前后一两月间各生一男今皆十五六矣岂君之子也两人各言妇人之容貎嵗齿相同李归语于妻妻往日诚悍妬至是见夫无嗣心颇惭而怜之翼日邀千户至家享以盛馔与之刻期而别千户先归南阳府李以实告于所管近侍大官乞假前往大官曰此美事也我当与汝奏闻既而有防得给驿以行凡筵席之费皆从官办李至众官郊迎徃千户宅设大
    宴李所以餽献千户并其妻子防妾之物甚侈千户命二子出拜风度不殊衣冠如一莫知何者为己子致请于千户千户曰君自认之李谛视良乆天性感通前抱一人曰此吾子也千户曰然于是父子相持而哭坐中皆为堕泪举杯交贺大醉而罢明日千户荅礼防客如昨谓李曰吾既与君子矣岂可使母子分离今幷其母以奉李喜出望外回都携见大官大官曰佳儿也引之入觐通籍宿卫后亦官至三品大抵人之有子无子数使之然非人力所能也而术士之业亦精矣

    Explanation

    496

    When the two parted and went out, a thousand households present invited Li into the tea house and told him, "Fifteen years ago, I also had no children. Because I went to the capital and bought a maid who was already pregnant. When I arrived home, my wife was also pregnant. Within one or two months, we each had a son. Now they are both fifteen or sixteen years old. Could they be your sons?" The two women looked the same in appearance and age. Li returned home and told his wife. His wife was formerly very fierce and jealous, but now she felt somewhat ashamed and pitied him. The next day, Li invited the thousand households to his home and entertained them with a grand feast, agreeing on a specific date and parting. The thousand households returned to Nanyang first. Li reported the truth to the high-ranking official he served under and requested leave to go. The official said, "This is a good thing. I will report this to the emperor on your behalf." Afterward, he was granted an official escort for the journey, and all the costs of the banquets were covered by the government. When Li arrived, the officials welcomed him at the outskirts of the city. He hosted a grand banquet and presented lavish gifts to the thousand households and their families. The thousand households ordered their two sons to come out and bow. Their demeanor and attire were indistinguishable, and no one knew which one was his own son. Li asked the thousand households for a decision. The thousand households said, "You decide for yourself." Li carefully observed them for a long time. By the power of heaven, he felt a connection and picked up one of them, saying, "This is my son." The thousand households said, "Indeed," and they embraced and wept. Everyone present wept and raised their cups to celebrate. They became drunk and the celebration ended. The next day, the thousand households returned the hospitality in the same manner and told Li, "Since I have given you my son, how could I separate him from his mother? So I am giving you both." Li was overjoyed beyond expectation and returned to the capital, bringing them to see the high-ranking official. The official said, "He is a good son." He brought him to the emperor's presence, where he was admitted to the imperial registry and became a guard. Later, he reached the rank of third grade official. In general, whether a person has children or not is determined by fate, not by human effort. The art of divination is also highly skilled.

    497

    夫妇入道
    　　王氏守素钱唐民家女其夫丁弃家为全真道士于吴山之紫阳庵一日召守素入山自付四句云散六十三妙用无人识逆顺两俱忘虚空镇常寂坐抱一膝而逝方外者流谓之骑化守素遂亦束髪簮冠着道士服奉夫遗尸二十年迹不下山年逾七十几于得道者神仙渺茫故未暇论贞守一节乃可尚也丁夘进士萨都刺天锡赠之诗曰不见辽东丁令威旧游城郭昔人非镜中春去青鸾老华表山空白归石竹泪干斑雨在玉箫声断防云飞洞门花落无人迹独坐苍苔道衣
    　　项节妇
    　　燕山项氏其夫江南人行贾燕蓟间聘项与居未几夫死项时年二十奉柩回江南誓以夫余赀养姑以自终比至姑已改适励志孑居以守夫祀旴江李宗烈闵其事而赋之诗曰少无依倚老何堪白髪婆娑乱不簮梦里尚思江北好悔将夫骨葬江南

    Explanation

    497

    Translation

    Entering the Dao Together

    Wang Shousu, the daughter of a household in Qiantang, had a husband named Ding who abandoned his family to become a Quanzhen Daoist, residing at the Ziyang Temple on Wushan Mountain. One day, he summoned Shousu into the mountains and gave her four lines of verse:

    "Dispersed and scattered, sixty-three wondrous uses remain unknown.
    Forgetting both the positive and the negative, emptiness and stillness reign.
    Sitting with one knee embraced, he passed away.
    Those outside the conventional order call this the transformation into a crane."

    Shousu then also bound her hair, wore a Daoist cap, and donned the attire of a Daoist. She served her husband's remains for twenty years, never leaving the mountain. By the time she was over seventy, she had attained the status of a Daoist cultivator. Immortals are elusive, so she did not have the leisure to discuss the virtue of fidelity. However, her steadfastness in one aspect is highly commendable. In the year Dingmao, the Jinshi candidate Sada Zhilie (Sa Duoci) wrote a poem for her:

    "No longer seen is Ding Lingwei from Liaodong,
    The old city gates of his former travels are now occupied by others.
    The spring in the mirror has gone, the green phoenix has aged,
    The white crane returns to the empty mountain.
    Tears dry on the stone bamboo, rain falls on the jade flute,
    Clouds fly, flowers fall in the cave door, no one's trace remains.
    Alone she sits on the mossy steps, dressed in Daoist robes."

    The Chaste Widow Xiang

    Xiang Shanshan, a woman from Yanshan, had a husband from Jiangnan who traded between Yanshan and Jixian. He arranged for Xiang to live with him, but soon after, her husband died when she was twenty years old. She carried his coffin back to Jiangnan and vowed to support her mother-in-law with her husband's remaining wealth, promising to remain chaste until her own death. Upon her return, her mother-in-law had already remarried. Resolved to live alone in solitude, she devoted herself to the memory of her husband. Xujiang Li Zonglie, moved by her story, wrote a poem for her:

    "Young and without support, old age is unbearable.
    White hair disheveled, no more ornament in the hairpin.
    In dreams, she still longs for the good of the north,
    Regretting that she buried her husband's remains in Jiangnan."

    498

    西域竒术
    　　任子昭云向寓都下时隣家儿患头疼不可忍有回回医官用刀割开额上取一小蟹坚硬如石尚能活动顷焉方死疼亦遄止当求得蟹至今蔵之夏雪簑云尝于平江阊门见过客马腹膨胀倒地店中偶有老回回见之于左腿内割取小块出不知何物也其马随即骑而去信西域多竒术哉
    　　童子属对
    　　湖广行省平章归自雨中有一童子年七八歳直造伞下避雨平章问曰学生能属对否曰能平章曰青衿来避雨即应声曰紫绶去朝天平章喜引至家遗以果肴明日除书至拜中书平章之命复大喜再以楮币防缯赠之
    　　先辈风致
    　　龙麟州先生过福建宪府设宴命宫妓小玉带佐觞酒半宪使举杯请曰今日之欢皆玉带为也愿先生酬之以诗先生其毋辞时先生负海内重名雅畏清议又不能违宪使之请遂书一絶句云菡蓞池边风满衣木樨亭下雨霏霏老夫记得坡仙语病体难禁玉带围于是举席称叹尽欢而散盖前辈既不肯拂人意又不欲失所守而且用事清切一时风致可想见信非野儒俗士所能及也

    Explanation

    498

    Strange Techniques from the Western Regions

    Mr. Ren Zhaoyun said: When I was staying in the capital, a neighbor's child suffered from a headache that could not be endured. A Muslim physician used a knife to cut open the child's forehead and extracted a small crab as hard as stone, which was still moving. Soon it died, and the pain stopped quickly. I then sought and obtained the crab, which I have kept to this day.

    Mr. Xia Xuesao said: Once, at the Gate of Chang in Pingjiang, I saw a guest's horse with a swollen belly collapse to the ground. There happened to be an old foreigner in the shop who, upon seeing it, cut out a small piece from the left leg, not knowing what it was. The horse was then mounted and ridden away. Indeed, there are many strange techniques from the Western Regions.

    A Child Composing Couplets

    The Chancellor of Huguang Province returned from the rain, and there was a child, seven or eight years old, who directly approached the umbrella to take shelter from the rain. The Chancellor asked, "Can you compose couplets?" The child replied, "Yes." The Chancellor said, "The green collar comes to take shelter from the rain; the purple sash goes to court." The Chancellor was pleased and brought him home, giving him fruits and delicacies. The next day, the appointment letter arrived, and he was appointed as Chancellor of the Central Secretariat. He was again very happy, and the Chancellor gave him paper money and silk as gifts.

    The Style of the Elders

    Mr. Long Linzhou passed through the Constitutional Office in Fujian and held a banquet, ordering the palace concubine Xiao Yu to serve wine. Halfway through the meal, the constitutional official raised his cup and said, "Today's joy is all due to Xiao Yu. I wish for you to compose a poem in her honor." Please do not refuse. At that time, the scholar was famous throughout the country and feared public opinion. And he could not refuse the official's request, so he wrote a quatrain:

    By the lotus pond, the wind fills my clothes; under the osmanthus pavilion, rain falls lightly. Old man, I remember the words of the Immortal Su, "My sick body cannot withstand the jade belt's embrace."

    Thus, everyone at the table praised and admired, and they parted in high spirits. Indeed, the elder scholars neither wished to offend others' intentions nor wanted to lose their principles, and they used clear and precise allusions. Their style and demeanor at the time can be imagined, and it is truly beyond the reach of rustic scholars and vulgar men.

    499

    司马善谏
    　　御史大夫额森特穆尔与夫人不睦已数年矣翰林学士承防阿穆恰巴拉死大夫遣司马明埓往唁之及归问其所以明埓云承防带罟罟娘子十有五人皆务争夺家财全无哀戚之情惟正室坐守灵帏哭泣不已大夫黙然是夜遂与夫人同寝欢爱如初若司马者可谓善于寓諌者矣
    　　俞竹心
    　　术士俞竹心者居庆元嗜酒落魄与人寡合顺其意者即与推算醉笔如飞畧不构思顷刻千余言道已往之事极验时皆以为异人至元己夘间娄敬之为本路治中尝以休咎叩之荅曰公他日直至一品便休娄深信其说弃职别进适值壬午更化俯就省掾陞除益都府判改换押字再宛然真书一品二字未几卒于官所此偶然耶抑数使然耶

    Explanation

    499

    Final Translation

    The Grand Censor and His Wife

    The Grand Censor Esemtemur had been at odds with his wife for several years. When the scholar-official Amuchabarba died, the Grand Censor sent the messenger Mingdu to pay his respects. Upon returning, the Grand Censor asked Mingdu about the situation. Mingdu replied that Amuchabarba had fifteen concubines or servants, all of whom were vying for family wealth and showed no signs of grief or sorrow. Only the main wife sat in the mourning tent and continued to cry. The Grand Censor remained silent. That night, he went to bed with his wife and they were as loving as before. In this way, the messenger Mingdu can be said to have skillfully used indirect advice.

    The Fortune-Teller Yu Zhuxin

    Yu Zhuxin, a fortune-teller, lived in Qingyuan. He was fond of alcohol and lived a disreputable life, with few friends or acquaintances. Those who pleased him would receive predictions. When he was drunk, he wrote rapidly and did not think or plan at all. In a short time, he could write over a thousand words predicting past events with great accuracy. At the time, everyone considered him an extraordinary person. During the year 1269, Lou Jing was the local government official of the region. He once asked Yu Zhuxin about his good or bad fortune. Yu Zhuxin replied, "You will reach the highest rank and then retire." Lou deeply believed his words and resigned from his position to seek another. Just as he was about to undergo a significant change, he accepted a position in the provincial administration and was promoted to the position of governor of Yidu. He changed his signature again, and it was clearly written in true script, the two characters for "one rank." Shortly after, he died in office. Was this a coincidence or was it destined?

    500

    犬脇生子
    　　元贞丙申秋大都南城武仲祥家有乳犬怀胎在脇下忽肿成疮六七日后于疮生五子色皆青苍每当梁自顶至尾生逆毛一道他无所异又数日疮亦平复

    　　辍耕録卷二十二
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷二十三　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰书画褾轴
    　　唐贞观开元间人主崇尚文雅其书画皆用紫龙鳯防绫为表緑文纹绫为里紫檀云花杵头轴白檀通身柿心轴此外又有青赤琉璃二等轴牙籖锦带大和间王涯自盐鐡据相印家既羡于财始用金玉为轴甘露之变人皆剥剔无遗南唐则褾以鸾墨锦籖以潢纸宋御府所蔵青紫大绫为褾文锦为带玉及水晶檀香为轴靖康之变民间多有得者高宗渡江后和议既成塲购求为多装褫之法已具名画记及绍兴定式兹更不赘姑以所闻见者使赏鉴之士有考焉

    500

    A Dog Giving Birth from Its Side

    In the autumn of the year 1296 during the reign of Emperor Chengzong of Yuan, in the southern part of Dadu (modern-day Beijing), a family named Wu Zhongxiang had a nursing dog that was pregnant in its side. Suddenly, it swelled into a sore, and after six or seven days, five puppies were born from the sore. The puppies were all blue-green in color, and each had a row of reverse hair running from their head to their tail, with nothing else unusual about them. After a few days, the sore healed.

    From the Chuigeng Lu (辍耕録), Volume 22

    From the Four Treasuries (钦定四库全书)

    Chuigeng Lu, Volume 23, by Tao Zongyi

    During the reigns of Emperor Taizong (贞观) and Emperor Xuanzong (开元) of the Tang dynasty, the rulers held a high regard for culture and refinement. Their paintings and calligraphy were bound with purple silk featuring dragon and phoenix patterns on the outside and green silk with patterned designs on the inside. The axes used for binding were made of purple sandalwood with cloud-patterned heads and white sandalwood with a chestnut-shaped core. Additionally, there were axes made of blue and red glass, and bookmarks made of wooden tags with silk ribbons. During the reign of Emperor Wenzong (大和), Wang Ya, who served as the Salt and Iron Commissioner and Chancellor, began using gold and jade axes after amassing great wealth. However, during the Ganlu (甘露) incident, many of his possessions were confiscated. In the Southern Tang dynasty, the bindings were made of black silk with phoenix patterns, and the bookmarks were made of yellow paper. During the Song dynasty, the imperial library used blue and purple silk for the bindings and patterned silk for the bookmarks. The axes were made of jade, crystal, and sandalwood. After the Jingkang (靖康) incident, many of these items became available to the public. After Emperor Gaozong of the Southern Song dynasty moved the capital south of the Yangtze River, the peace agreement with the Jin dynasty led to a market where books were bought and sold. The methods of bookbinding and restoration were well-documented in the Minghua Ji (名画记) and the established methods of the Southern Song dynasty under Emperor Xiaozong (绍兴). This will not be elaborated further, but I will briefly mention what I have observed to provide a reference for those who appreciate such works.

    501

    锦褾
    　　克丝作楼阁　克丝作龙水　克丝作百花攅龙克丝作龙鳯　紫寳阶地　　紫大花
    　　五色簟文【俗呼山和尚】
    　　紫小滴珠方胜鸾鹊
    　　青緑簟文【俗呼阁婆又曰蛇皮】
    　　紫鸾鹊【一等紫地紫鸾鹊一等白地紫鸾鹊】　　紫百花龙
    　　紫纹　　　紫珠焰　　紫曲水【俗呼落花流水】紫汤荷花　　红霞云鸾
    　　黄霞云鸾【俗呼綘霄其名甚雅】　　　　青楼阁【阁又作台】青大落花　　紫滴珠龙团　青樱桃
    　　皂方团白花　褐方团白花　方胜盘象
    　　毬路　　　　衲　　　　　柿红背
    　　樗蒲　　　　宜男　　　　寳照

    Explanation

    501

    Decorative Patterns

    • Silk Borders
      • Fine Silk Towers - Fine silk pattern resembling towers.
      • Fine Silk Dragons and Water - Fine silk pattern featuring dragons and water.
      • Fine Silk Flowers and Dragons - Fine silk pattern featuring flowers and dragons.
      • Fine Silk Dragons and Phoenixes - Fine silk pattern featuring dragons and phoenixes.
      • Purple Jade-like Steps - Purple pattern resembling jade or other precious materials.
      • Five-Colored Bamboo Matting - Five-colored pattern resembling bamboo matting.
      • Small Purple Pearls and Victory Phoenixes and Cranes - Small purple pattern featuring pearls, victory symbols, and phoenixes.
      • Green and Blue Bamboo Matting - Green and blue pattern resembling bamboo matting.
      • Purple Phoenixes and Cranes - Purple pattern featuring phoenixes and cranes.
      • Purple Flowers and Dragons - Purple pattern featuring flowers and dragons.
      • Purple Turtle Pattern - Purple pattern resembling a turtle's back.
      • Purple Pearl Flames - Purple pattern resembling flames or sparks.
      • Purple Curved Water - Purple pattern resembling curved water.
      • Purple Lotus Flowers - Purple pattern featuring lotus flowers.
      • Red Clouds and Phoenixes - Red pattern featuring clouds and phoenixes.
      • Yellow Clouds and Phoenixes - Yellow pattern featuring clouds and phoenixes.
      • Green Towers - Green pattern featuring towers or palaces.
      • Purple Droplets and Dragon Clusters - Purple pattern featuring droplets and dragon clusters.
      • Green Cherries - Green pattern resembling cherries.
      • Black and White Floral Designs - Black and white pattern featuring floral designs.
      • Brown and White Floral Designs - Brown and white pattern featuring floral designs.
      • Square Victory Symbols and Elephant Motifs - Square pattern featuring victory symbols and elephant motifs.
      • Ball or Spherical Pattern - Pattern resembling a ball or spherical shape.
      • Patchwork or Quilted Pattern - Pattern resembling patchwork or quilting.
      • Shi Red Turtle Back - Pattern resembling a turtle's back in shi red.
      • Chupu Game Pattern - Pattern resembling a type of dice or board game.
      • Fertility or Good Luck Symbol - Pattern symbolizing fertility or good luck.
      • Mirror or Reflective Surface Pattern - Pattern resembling a mirror or reflective surface.
    502

    莲　　　　天下乐　　　练鹊
    　　方胜练鹊　　绶带　　　　瑞草
    　　八花晕　　　银钩晕　　　红细花盘雕

    503

    水晶　　　　蜡沉香　　　古玉
    　　象牙　　　　犀角
    　　轴杆
    　　檀香木
    　　匣
    　　螺钿【宋高宗内府皆钿匣】
    　　炉鸣
    　　至元庚寅冬江浙行省官立僧格沙布迪音辈徳政碑穹窿莫比特阙坐石时赵若晦者素善謟媚因以杨和王坟域所有为言役人夫数千拖拽而至毕工之日是夜省堂中火炉鸣直至昧爽方休嗣是夜以为常又枭鸣梁压虎入城市越明年春相哥败诸公俱罹竒祸岂非事有先兆与
    　　田夫人
    　　刘公复新为上都留守时有令史亢子春者值公退食偶与同列据案判事以戯遂为仇家发之公大怒责问罪状枷项示众及归怒容未霁其夫人田氏问公何故不乐公语其故夫人曰此小节耳何足怒也即令人呼亢至请公为脱其枷且劳以酒云此一杯与汝压惊此一杯与汝庆喜男子大丈夫何所不至留守之位何患不到亢感谢而退不数年公卒而无子止一女适田直长直长遄卒女病双瞽后亢官湖广参政迎夫人母子归没齿敬养不怠公乃廉访使刘廷干之从祖父也

    Explanation

    503

    Translation

    Crystal, wax camphor, ancient jade, ivory, rhinoceros horn, axle rod, sandalwood, box, mother-of-pearl inlay [all the inlaid boxes in the imperial treasury of Emperor Gaozong of Song], stove sounds.

    In the winter of 1270, officials of the Jiang-Zhe provincial administration erected a monument to commemorate the virtue of Sengge Shabudiyin. The monument was located near a dome-like structure that sat atop a stone. At the time, Zhao Ruohui, who was known for his flattery, suggested using the burial grounds of Yang He Wang. Thousands of laborers were mobilized and dragged the materials there. On the day the work was completed, that night in the provincial hall, the stove sounded until dawn before it stopped. From then on, the stove sounded every night. There were also owls crying and a tiger entering the city, which were seen as ominous signs. By the following spring, when Xiangge was defeated, all the officials suffered a strange calamity. Is this not a sign of things to come?

    Lady Tian.

    Liu Gong was appointed as the governor of Shangdu. At that time, there was a low-ranking official named Kang Zichun. When Liu Gong took his meal, Kang Zichun was sitting at the table handling affairs in a playful manner. His enemies exposed him. Liu Gong became very angry and questioned him about his crimes, publicly shaming him with a collar. When he returned home, his anger had not subsided. His wife, Lady Tian, asked him why he was not happy. He told her what had happened. She said, "This is a minor matter; what is there to be angry about?" She then ordered someone to call Kang Zichun and asked Liu Gong to remove the collar and treat him with wine, saying, "This cup is for you to calm your nerves, and this cup is for you to celebrate your joy. A true man can achieve anything. There is no fear of not reaching the position of governor." Kang Zichun thanked him and left. Within a few years, Liu Gong died without an heir. He had only one daughter who married Tian Zhichang. Tian Zhichang died suddenly, and the daughter became blind in both eyes. Later, Kang Zichun became a high-ranking official in Huguang and welcomed Lady Tian and her mother back, showing respect and care until the end of his life. Liu Gong was the great-grandfather of Liu Tinggan, the Censor-in-Chief.

    504

    嗓
    　　大名王和卿滑稽佻达传播四方中统初燕市有一蝴蝶其大异常王赋醉中天小令云挣破荘周梦两翅驾东风三百处名园一采一个空难道风流种諕杀寻芳蠭轻轻的飞动卖花人搧过桥东由是其名益着时有闗汉卿者亦高才风流人也王常以讥谑加之闗虽极意还答终不能胜王忽坐逝而鼻垂双涕尺余人皆叹骇闗来吊唁询其由或对云此释家所谓坐化也复问鼻悬何物又对云此玉筯也闗云我道你不识不是玉筯是嗓咸发一笑或戯闗云你被王和卿轻侮半世死后方才还得一筹凢六畜劳伤则鼻中常流脓水谓之嗓病又爱讦人之短者亦谓之嗓故云尔
    　　金莲杯
    　　杨鐡崖耽好声色每于筵间见歌儿舞女有纒足纎小者则脱其鞵载盏以行酒谓之金莲杯予窃怪其可厌后读张基墨荘漫録载王深辅道双鳬诗云时时行地罗裙掩双手更擎春防灔傍人都道不须辞尽做十分能几防春柔浅醮蒲萄暖和笑劝人教引满洛尘忽浥不胜娇刬蹈金莲行款款观此诗则老子之踈狂有自来矣

    Explanation

    504

    Singing and Speaking
    In the early days of the Zhongtong era, in Yan city, there was a butterfly of extraordinary size. Wang Heqing, a man from Daming known for his wit and humor, composed a short poem titled "Zui Zhong Tian" (Drunk in the Sky), which went:

    "Erupting from Zhuangzi's dream, two wings ride the east wind,
    Visiting hundreds of famous gardens, leaving each one empty.
    Is this a sign of elegance that scares away the bees searching for flowers?
    Gently flying, a flower seller fans it across the bridge to the east."

    Thus, Wang's reputation grew even more. At that time, there was Guan Hanqing, another man of great talent and charm. Wang often teased him, and though Guan responded with great effort, he could not outwit Wang. Suddenly, Wang passed away while sitting, with two streams of mucus flowing from his nose, each about a foot long. Everyone was shocked. Guan came to mourn and asked about the cause. Someone replied, "This is what Buddhism calls 'sitting to enter Nirvana.'" Guan then asked what was hanging from his nose. The response was, "This is jade tears." Guan said, "I thought you didn't recognize it; it's not jade tears, it's 'singing and speaking.'" Everyone laughed. Someone joked at Guan, "You were mocked by Wang Heqing all your life, and only after your death did you finally get the better of him." When animals are overworked or injured, they often have mucus flowing from their noses, which is called "singing and speaking." It is also used to describe someone who is overly critical or gossipy.

    The Golden Lotus Cup
    Yang Tiaye, who indulged in music and beauty, often saw at banquets girls with bound, delicate feet. He would remove their shoes and use them as cups for serving wine, calling it the "Golden Lotus Cup." I found this practice quite repulsive. Later, I read in Zhang Wugou's Mozhuang Manlu (Mozhuang's Idle Notes) a poem by Wang Shenfu titled "Shuangfu Shi" (Poems of the Double Swans):

    "Always walking on the ground, her skirt conceals her hands,
    Even more graceful and beautiful, standing beside her, people say she doesn't need to be thanked.
    She can do it all, how much springtime can she capture?
    Lightly dipping into the grape wine, she warmly encourages people to drink,
    The dust of Luoyang suddenly wets, she can't bear the tenderness,
    Walking with small, bound feet, gracefully and slowly."

    Seeing this poem, one can see that the elder's carefree and unrestrained nature has always been present.

    505

    大佛头
    　　宋高宗朝钱塘喻氏出家为沙门名思浄建妙行院于北阙接待供僧三百万画阿弥陀佛入于神妙杨侍郎杰賛为喻弥陀人从而称之浄又于西湖之北镌石为大佛头父老相传云此石乃秦始皇系缆石盖是时皆浙江耳初无西湖之名始皇将登防稽为风浪所阻故泊舟此处
    　　州白菜
    　　扬州至正丙申丁酉间兵燹之余城中屋址徧生白菜大者重十五斤小者亦不下八九斤有膂力人所负才四五窠耳亦异
    　　谲诞有配
    　　天下之事未尝无配虽谲诈诞妄之谈亦有然者松江卫山斋有材誉时庸医儿孙华孙颇知嗜学山斋因奬予之使得侪于士山斋既死华孙忽谓人曰尝梦天使持黄封小合授吾曰上帝有勑以卫山斋声价畀汝吾受命谢恩而寤华孙才思极迟凡作一诗必数十日乃就则曰吾登溷偶得一联或又曰枕上得此故人戱赠以诗有浪得诗名索价高及山斋声

    Explanation

    505

    The Great Buddha Head

    During the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Song, the Yu family from Qiantang left the secular world and became monks. One of them, named Sijing, established the Miaoxing Temple at the northern gate and provided for three million monks. He also painted Amitabha Buddha in a divine and wondrous manner, which was praised by Yang Shilang, who referred to him as "Yu Amitabha." Sijing also carved a large Buddha head from stone on the north side of West Lake. The elders passed down the legend that this stone was the anchor stone used by Qin Shi Huang, as at that time the area was all part of the Zhejiang River, and the name "West Lake" did not yet exist. Qin Shi Huang was on his way to Mount Fang, but was blocked by wind and waves, so he anchored his ship here.

    Cabbage in Yangzhou

    During the years 1346 and 1347 in Yangzhou, after the devastation of war, the ruins of houses were covered with cabbage. The largest cabbage weighed fifteen catties, and even the smallest weighed no less than eight or nine catties. Even those with great strength could only carry three or four heads of cabbage at a time, which was indeed unusual.

    Even the Most Fantastic Stories Have Their Parallels

    There has never been anything in the world without its counterpart, even the most fantastic and absurd stories. In Songjiang, there was a monk named Shanzhai, who was renowned for his talent. At that time, the son of a mediocre doctor, named Huashun, had a strong interest in learning. Shanzhai encouraged him, and Huashun was able to be considered equal to scholars. After Shanzhai died, Huashun suddenly told someone, "I once dreamed that a heavenly messenger gave me a small sealed box, saying, 'The Heavenly Emperor has ordered to transfer Shanzhai's reputation to you.' I accepted the command, thanked the gods, and woke up." Huashun's thoughts were extremely slow, and he could only finish a poem after dozens of days. He would say, "I happened to come up with a couplet while using the toilet," or "I got this from an old friend while lying in bed, who gave me a poem as a joke." He had gained a reputation for poetry but demanded a high price for it, and his fame was closely tied to that of Shanzhai.

    506

    价黄封合之句陆居仁每谓人曰吾读书至得意时见庆云一朶现家人皆不能覩又一日读诗集传有不安处思所以易之忽若梦寐中见尼父拱立于前而呼吾字曰陆宅之朱熹误矣汝说是也偶与友人之者言及此友人曰足下得非禀受素弱乎曰何为曰吾见足下眼目眊又梦寐颠倒故知其然也居仁慙赧不复辨客来谈及拊几大笑命笔识之检田吏
    　　有一老翁如病起破衲痩如晓来扶向官道傍哀告行人乞钱米时予奉檄离江城邂逅一见怜其贫倒囊赠与五升米试问何故为穷民老翁答言听我语我是东鄊李福五我家无本为经商只种官田三十畆延祐七年三月初卖衣买得犁与鉏朝耕暮耘受辛苦要还私债输官租谁知六月与七月雨水絶无潮又竭欲求一防半防水却比农夫眼中血滔滔黄浦如沟渠农家争水如争珠数车相接接不到稻田一旦成沙涂官司八月受灾状我恐征粮吃官棒相随邻里去告灾十石官粮望全放当年隔岸分

    Explanation

    506

    Lu Ju Ren often told people, "When I read and feel very satisfied, I see a rare and beautiful cloud formation (Qing Yun) that my family cannot see. One day, while reading a poem, I felt uneasy about a particular passage and thought about how to change it. Suddenly, in a dream-like vision, I saw Confucius standing before me and calling out my name, saying, 'Lu Ju Ren, Zhu Xi made a mistake; your interpretation is correct.' I happened to talk about this with a scholar friend, who asked, 'Are you not naturally weak?' I replied, 'What do you mean?' The friend explained, 'I see that your eyes are dim and your dreams are confused, which is why I think so.' Lu Ju Ren felt ashamed and did not argue further. When a guest arrived and talked about it, they laughed and asked to write it down. Lu Ju Ren then wrote it down and mentioned the land official (检田吏)."

    There was an old man who looked like he had just recovered from an illness, wearing a tattered robe and looking as thin as a ghost. Early in the morning, he was carried to the side of an official road and begged passersby for money and rice. At that time, I was on an official mission leaving Jiangcheng and happened to see him. Moved by his poverty, I gave him five liters of rice from my bag. I asked him why he was so poor, and the old man replied, "Listen to me. I am Li Fuwu from the eastern village. My family has no capital, so we engage in commerce. We only cultivate thirty mu of official land. In the third month of the seventh year of the Yanyou era, we sold our clothes to buy a plow and a hoe. We worked hard from morning till night to repay private debts and pay official rents. Who knew that in June and July, there was no rain, and the water dried up. We desperately sought even a little water, but it was as precious as blood to farmers. The Huangpu River was like a ditch, and farmers competed for water like they were competing for pearls. Several carts of water were connected, but they could not reach the rice fields, which turned into sandy soil. In August, the government office received a report of the disaster, and I feared that we would be punished for failing to pay taxes. I went with my neighbors to report the disaster and requested that the ten stones of official grain be fully waived. We were separated from the other side of the river."

    Final Translation

    Lu Ju Ren often told people, "When I read and feel very satisfied, I see a rare and beautiful cloud formation (Qing Yun) that my family cannot see. One day, while reading a poem, I felt uneasy about a particular passage and thought about how to change it. Suddenly, in a dream-like vision, I saw Confucius standing before me and calling out my name, saying, 'Lu Ju Ren, Zhu Xi made a mistake; your interpretation is correct.' I happened to talk about this with a scholar friend, who asked, 'Are you not naturally weak?' I replied, 'What do you mean?' The friend explained, 'I see that your eyes are dim and your dreams are confused, which is why I think so.' Lu Ju Ren felt ashamed and did not argue further. When a guest arrived and talked about it, they laughed and asked to write it down. Lu Ju Ren then wrote it down and mentioned the land official (检田吏)."

    There was an old man who looked like he had just recovered from an illness, wearing a tattered robe and looking as thin as a ghost. Early in the morning, he was carried to the side of an official road and begged passersby for money and rice. At that time, I was on an official mission leaving Jiangcheng and happened to see him. Moved by his poverty, I gave him five liters of rice from my bag. I asked him why he was so poor, and the old man replied, "Listen to me. I am Li Fuwu from the eastern village. My family has no capital, so we engage in commerce. We only cultivate thirty mu of official land. In the third month of the seventh year of the Yanyou era, we sold our clothes to buy a plow and a hoe. We worked hard from morning till night to repay private debts and pay official rents. Who knew that in June and July, there was no rain, and the water dried up. We desperately sought even a little water, but it was as precious as blood to farmers. The Huangpu River was like a ditch, and farmers competed for water like they were competing for pearls. Several carts of water were connected, but they could not reach the rice fields, which turned into sandy soil. In August, the government office received a report of the disaster, and I feared that we would be punished for failing to pay taxes. I went with my neighbors

    507

    吉凶高田尽荒低田丰县官不见高田旱将谓亦与低田同文字下鄊如火速逼我将田都首伏只因嗔我不肯首却把我田批作熟太平九月开早仓主首贫乏无可偿男名阿孙女阿惜逼我嫁卖陪官粮阿孙卖与运粮户即目不知在何处可怜阿惜犹未笄嫁向湖州山里去我今年已七十竒饥无口食寒无衣东求西乞度残喘无因早向黄泉归旋言旋拭腮边泪我忽惊慙汗沾背老翁老翁勿复言我是今年检田吏此介灾行也是可以为民牧不恤民瘼者之劝介字可潜尝掾松江盖能以儒术饰吏事者因载之

    Explanation

    507

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    508

    玉鹿卢
    　　霍清甫治书云考古图载古衣服今有玉辘轳玉具劒古乐府曰腰间辘轳劒此器以块然之璞既觧为环中复为转闗而上下之隙仅通丝髪作宛转其间今之名玉工者往往叹其所未覩按汉隽不疑带【鑘同】具劒晋灼曰古长劒首以玉作井辘轳形上刻木作山形如莲花初生未敷时今大劒末首其状如此前说乃宋李公麟之所纪也余昔宦游钱唐因识吴和之者性慧巧博物一辘轳玉青色形如吕字环口中间辘轳旋转无分毫缝罅形色极古人皆以为工因土

    Explanation

    508

    A Jade Artifact Known as Yù Lù Lú

    Huo Qingfu, in his treatise, mentions that ancient depictions in archaeological records show garments adorned with jade artifacts, including a yù lù lú (jade winch) and yù jù jiàn (jade sword fittings). An ancient folk song refers to a sword with a mechanism resembling a winch, saying, "At the waist, a winch-like sword." This artifact is crafted from a rough, unpolished jade stone, which is first shaped into a ring and then fitted with a rotating mechanism. The gap between the rotating parts is so narrow that it can only pass through a strand of hair, allowing for smooth and graceful movement. Modern jade craftsmen often marvel at such intricate designs, which they have never seen before.

    According to the commentary by Jin Zhuo, the ancient long sword had a jade pommel shaped like a well pulley, with a wooden carving of a mountain resembling a lotus flower in its early stages of blooming. The end of the sword, as described in earlier accounts, resembles this shape. The previous description is based on the records of Li Gonglin from the Song Dynasty.

    In my past service in Qiantang, I encountered Wu Hezhi, a man of great intelligence, skill, and knowledge. He crafted a jade artifact resembling a winch, which was a deep blue color and shaped like the character "吕" (Lǚ). The artifact had a ring-shaped opening in the center, with a rotating winch mechanism that had no visible seams or gaps. The craftsmanship was so exquisite that ancient people believed it must have been created by a divine craftsman.

    509

    渍用白梅熬水煮之良乆脱开详视窍中有双玉轴在焉中嵌一物形若牛筋意度必是当间煮之胖胀撑塞双轴入窍闗住所以宛转无碍年深腐败缩瘦因而煮脱试用干牛筋捶实置轴两间对勘孔窍以线防定煮之少时双轴果涌入窍中湏臾取出依前动转不脱后余亦一小者状若旋环制作大约相似后因损折转轴中亦有一物形似翎桶想亦同一闗捩其玉具劒自三代有之今止以两汉为始至于宋朝且千余年未有能穷其辘轳底蕴今偶以煮脱乃得其机轴亦云竒矣
    　　猴盗
    　　夏雪簑云尝见优人杜生彦明说向自江西回至韶州寓宿旅邸邸先有客曰相公者居焉刺绣衣服琢玉防顶而仅皮履生惑具酒肴延欵问以姓名履歴客具答甚悉初不知其为盗也次日客酬防邀至其室见柱上鎻一小猴形神精狡既而纵使周旋席间忽畨语遣之俄捧一楪至复畨语詈之即易一碗至生惊异询其故客曰某有婢得子弥月而亡时此猴生旬有五日其母毙于猎犬终日呌号可怜因令此婢就乳之及长成遂能随人指使兼觧畨语耳生别后至

    Explanation

    509

    The Jade-Handled Sword

    Soaking the sword with white plum and boiling it for a long time, then removing it and examining it closely, I found that there were two jade axes in the cavity. In the middle, an object was embedded, shaped like beef sinew. I speculated that when cooked in the middle, it would swell and block the two axes into the cavity, locking them in place, allowing the sword to move freely without obstruction. Over time, it rotted and shrank, leading to its cooking and removal. I then tried using dried beef sinew to fill the space between the axes, aligning the holes with string, and cooked it for a short time. The two axes then flowed into the cavity, and after a moment, they were removed but still moved freely without falling out. Later, I also made a smaller one, shaped like a ring, with a similar construction. Later, due to damage, the rotating axis also contained an object shaped like a feather tube, likely the same mechanism. The jade-handled sword has existed since the Three Dynasties, but it is only from the Han dynasty that it is recorded; up to the Song dynasty, no one has been able to fully understand its internal mechanism, but now, by cooking and removing it, I have discovered its mechanism, which is indeed remarkable.

    Monkey Thief

    Xia Xue Saoyun once saw an actor named Du Sheng Yanming say that he returned from Jiangxi to Shaoguan and stayed in a lodging. There was a guest called "Xianggong" staying in the lodging. He wore embroidered clothes, carved jade hairpin, and only leather shoes. I was puzzled and prepared wine and dishes to invite him, asking for his name and biography. The guest answered in detail, and I did not know he was a thief at first. The next day, the guest reciprocated the hospitality and invited me to his room. I saw a small monkey locked on the pillar, with a cunning and alert appearance. Later, I let it roam around the table, and it suddenly spoke in a foreign language and was dismissed. Soon, it carried a tray and spoke in a foreign language, scolding me, and then changed to a bowl. I was surprised and asked the reason. He said, "I have a servant who gave birth to a child, but the child died after a month; at that time, this monkey was five days old, and its mother was killed by a hunting dog, crying all day, pitiful; I then had the servant nurse it, and when it grew up, it could follow my commands and also speak in a foreign language." I left and came back.

    510

    清州留吴同知处忽报客有防一猴入城者吴语生云此人乃江湖巨盗凡至人家窥见房室路径并蔵蓄所在至夜使猴入内偷窃彼则在外应接吾必夺此猴为人除害也明日客谒吴吴欵以饭需其猴初甚拒吴曰否则就此断其首客不得已允许吴酬白金十两临去畨语嘱猴适译史闻得来告呉曰客教猴云汝若不饮不食彼必觧尔防可亟逃来我只在十里外小寺中伺也吴未之信至晩试与之果核水食之皆不食急使人觇之此客果未行归报引猴挝杀之
    　　盗有道
    　　后至元间盗入浙省丞相府是夕月色微明相于纱帷中窥见之美髭髯身长七尺余时一侍姬亦见之大呼有贼相急止之曰此相府何贼敢来盖虞其有所伤犯故也纵其自取七寳系腰金玉器皿席卷而去翼旦责令有司官兵肖形掩捕刻期获觧沿门搜索终不可得越明年才于绍兴诸暨州败露掠问其情乃云初至杭寓相

    Explanation

    510

    In Qingzhou, at the office of the local magistrate Wu, a sudden report came that a guest had a monkey that entered the city. Wu said, "This person is a notorious bandit from the rivers and lakes. Whenever he goes to someone's house, he spies on the layout of the rooms and the locations of their valuables. At night, he sends the monkey inside to steal. He then waits outside to receive the stolen goods. I will certainly take this monkey away and eliminate this harm for the people."

    The next day, the guest visited Wu. Wu treated him to a meal and asked for the monkey. At first, he strongly refused. Wu threatened, "Otherwise, I will cut off his head right here." The guest could not help but agree. Wu paid him ten taels of silver. As he was leaving, he spoke in a foreign language to the monkey. Just then, the translator heard and came to tell Wu. The guest had told the monkey, "If you do not eat or drink, he will surely take you away. You should escape quickly and come to me. I am waiting at a small temple ten li away."

    Wu did not believe it. In the evening, he tried giving the monkey fruit seeds and water to eat. The monkey refused to eat anything. Wu quickly sent someone to spy on the guest. The guest indeed did not leave. He returned and reported, leading the monkey to be beaten to death.

    The Bandit's Strategy

    In the later years of the Yuan dynasty, a bandit infiltrated the Chancellor's mansion in Zhejiang Province. That evening, under the faint moonlight, the Chancellor, peering through the silk curtains, saw a man with a beautiful beard, over seven feet tall. At the time, a serving maid also saw him and cried out, "There's a thief!" The Chancellor quickly stopped her, saying, "This is the Chancellor's mansion. How dare a thief come here? I am merely concerned that he might harm or offend someone." He allowed the bandit to take whatever he wanted, including seven treasures, gold and jade vessels, and other valuables, which he gathered up and left. The next day, the Chancellor ordered the authorities to draw a portrait and launch a manhunt. Despite their efforts, they could not find the bandit. It was not until the following year that the bandit was captured in Zhuji Prefecture, Shaoxing. Upon interrogation, the bandit confessed: "When I first arrived in Hangzhou, I stayed at the Chancellor's mansion."

    511

    府之东相去三十余家是夜自外大醉归倒于门外主人扶掖登楼而卧湏臾呕吐狼籍满地至二更开楼窓縁房檐进府内脚履尺余木级面带优人假髯既得物直携至江头置于白塔上复回寓所侵晨逻者至察其人酒尚未醒酣睡正熟且身材侏儒畧无髭髯竟不之疑数日后方携所盗物抵浙东因此被擒盗亦有道其斯之谓与
    　　预知改元
    　　省掾李孟容【度】为余言元统间在都间见一全真先生年五十余相貎魁伟尝坐省东茶肆中所言輙有验因访其寓所乃在五门外第二桥民家遂以出处叩之全真曰汝仕不在北方且冝南归四十后方可食禄临别偶问及时事全真曰此后当改至元至元后改至贞天下乱矣仆曰国初已有至元全真曰汝苐识之仆南还至闗河闻改至元心益信之及改至正则知贞者正也四十后方补饶州府史夫全真之言如烛照数计特不知果何术也岂非至人者乎醉太平小令

    Explanation

    511

    In the eastern part of the government office, more than thirty households away, there was a man who returned drunk from outside and fell down at the door. The host helped him up the stairs and lay him down to sleep. In a short while, he vomited everywhere, making a mess on the ground. By the second watch, he opened the window and climbed along the eaves into the government office. He wore shoes and climbed wooden steps that were over a foot high, with a fake beard on his face. After getting the goods, he carried them directly to the riverbank and placed them on the White Pagoda. He then returned to his lodging. In the morning, guards arrived and questioned him. They found that he was still drunk and sound asleep. His small stature and lack of a beard made the guards suspicious, but they did not suspect him. Several days later, he was carrying the stolen goods to Zhe Dong when he was caught. The thief also had his own rules, isn't this what it means?

    Predicting the change of era name,

    The provincial official Li Mengrong [Du] told me about a time during the reign of Emperor Taiding in the Yuan Dynasty when he saw a Daoist master in the capital. The master was over fifty years old, with a majestic appearance, and often sat in a teahouse in the east of the provincial government. Whatever he said always proved to be true. I visited his residence, which was in a civilian house at the second bridge outside the fifth gate.
