I asked him about his background, and he said, "You should not serve in the north; it is better to return to the south. After forty years, you can enjoy your official salary." On our parting, I asked him about current affairs, and he said, "After this, the era name will change to 'Zhiyuan.' After 'Zhiyuan,' it will change to 'Zheng,' and the world will be in chaos." I said, "At the beginning of the country, there was already 'Zhiyuan.'" The master said, "Just remember it." I returned to the south and reached the Guanhe region, where I heard that the era name had indeed changed to 'Zhiyuan,' and my heart was more convinced. When it changed to 'Zheng,' I realized that 'Zheng' means 'correct.' After forty years, I was appointed as a magistrate in Raozhou. The words of the Daoist master were like a lamp shining on numbers, but I do not know what his true method was. Could he not be a sage? Drunkard's Song of Tranquility

    512

    堂堂大元奸佞专权开河变钞祸根源惹红巾万千官法滥刑法重黎民怨人吃人钞买钞何曽见贼做官官做贼混愚贤哀哉可怜右醉太平小令一阕不知谁所造自京师以至江南人人能道之古人多取里巷之歌谣者以其有闗于世教也今此数语切中时病故録之以俟采民风者焉
    　　讥省台
    　　集庆失守行御史台移置绍兴路前御史大夫纳琳再任时浙省丞相达实特穆尔得便冝行事民间颇言其贪后又以大夫子安安判行枢宻院护台治大夫之政一听决于院判有人作诗云旧省新丞相新台旧大夫大夫听子语丞相爱金珠又有人大书于台之门曰包苴贿赂尚公行天下承平徳未能二十四官徒獬廌越王台上望金陵

    512

    The Great Yuan, with its corrupt officials wielding power, opened rivers and changed currency, which became the root of disaster, stirring up the Red Turbans in their thousands. The laws were corrupt, the criminal punishments severe, and the people's grievances deep. People ate each other, buying and selling paper money, when have we ever seen bandits become officials? Officials acted like bandits, confusing the wise with the foolish. Alas, how pitiful!

    To the right, a short lyric poem titled "Righteousness in Times of Peace," unknown who composed it, from the capital to the south, everyone could recite it. Ancient people often collected folk songs and ballads from the streets and alleys because they were related to the teachings of the world. Now, these few words accurately address the current issues, so I record them for those who collect folk customs.

    Satirizing the Provincial Government

    Jiankang fell, and the provincial office of the Censorate was moved to Shaoxing. The previous Censorate Grand Master, Nalin, was reappointed. At that time, the Chancellor of Zhejiang, Dasi Temur, took advantage of convenient circumstances to act, and the people often complained about his corruption. Later, the son of the Grand Master, An'an, was appointed as the judge of the Military Council to protect and govern the province, and all decisions were made by the judge of the Military Council. Someone wrote a poem saying, "The old province with a new Chancellor, the new provincial office with the old Grand Master. The Grand Master listened to his son's words, the Chancellor loved gold and jewels." Someone else wrote a large sign on the door of the provincial office, "Corruption and bribery still openly practiced, the world is at peace but virtue is lacking. The 24 officials of the provincial office are mere Xiezhi (mythical creatures symbolizing justice). Looking at Jinling from the Yue Wang Tower."

    513

    造物有报复
    　　防稽陈思可【睿】云至正丙申御史大夫纳琳开行台于绍兴于时庆元慈溪则有县尹陈文昭本路余姚则有同知图卜戬在城则有録事达噜噶齐穆尔古蘓皆总制团结民义者纳琳之子安安以三人为不易制思有以去之乃先绐召图卜戬至拘于寳林寺夜半率台军擒杀之从而方国珍亦执陈文昭沈之海独存穆尔古蘓一人耳人皆以图卜戬之死归罪于穆尔古
    蘓不能力救殊不知当时之执图卜戬乃所以擒穆尔古蘓也执图卜戬之谋出于潘子素子素亦为安安缢诸途执子素之谋出于辛敬所敬所间闗投张士诚客死平江僧舍及拜□克代纳琳为大夫又不能容穆尔古蘓挝杀于其私第拜□克以弟吹斯防拜中书右相诏入朝既得罪兄弟诛戮家无噍但未知安安死所耳静而思之若有尸于之中者不知造物果如何也

    Explanation

    513

    The universe has a sense of retribution.
    　　Fangqi Chen Sikě [Rui] said that in the year Bing Shen of the Zhizheng era, the Grand Censor Na Lin established a temporary administrative office in Shaoxing. At that time, in Qingyuan Cixi, there was the County Magistrate Chen Wenzhao, and in the road of Yuyao, there was the Deputy Official Tu Bu Jian. In the city, there were the Recorder Darugaqi and the Judge Muru Su, all of whom were responsible for organizing and mobilizing the populace. Na Lin's son An An found these three to be difficult to control and thought of a way to get rid of them. He first deceived and summoned Tu Bu Jian to the Bao Lin Temple, where he was detained. At midnight, An An led the troops of the temporary office to capture and kill him. Consequently, Fang Guozhen also captured Chen Wenzhao and drowned him in the sea, leaving only Muru Su alive. People blamed Muru Su for Tu Bu Jian's death, thinking he could not rescue him. However, it was not known that the capture of Tu Bu Jian was actually a means to capture Muru Su. The plan to capture Tu Bu Jian came from Pan Zisu, who was also strangled by An An on the road. The plan to capture Pan Zisu came from Xin Jingsuo, who, after a difficult journey, fled to Zhang Shicheng and died as a guest in a temple in Pingjiang. When Bai took over Na Lin's position as Grand Censor, he could not tolerate Muru Su and killed him in his private residence. Bai's brother, Chusi Fang, was appointed as the Right Chancellor of the Central Secretariat and summoned to court. They both committed crimes and were executed, leaving no survivors. But it is unknown where An An died. Upon reflection, there might be a body in a certain place, but it is unclear how the universe truly operates.

    514

    鎻鎻
    　　回纥野马川有木曰鎻鎻烧之其火经年不灭且不作灰彼处女取根制防入火不焚如火鼠布云
    　　叶氏还金
    　　叶公政字克明淮隂人行宣政院都事季实之子翰林直学士蟾心之从子也至政甲午公政以浙西幕史奉布延平章檄转饷鄂阃时丹阳富民束子章先与是役防饮于蕲志相合即以兄礼事公政未几子章起赴沔泣别公政曰弟今济大江涉重地兄言行笃信愿以赀囊相托公政辞弗获俾子章手缄而为谨蔵之越两月子章之友朱君譲率其奴来谒曰子章不幸入莲防湖遇盗死矣子章昔寄囊中亦有某物在间欲啓嚢而请之公政曰汝寓物子章未尝语我子章已矣家固无恙也义必质诸其家明以付汝君让以公政匿为已有衘之去明年既竣事还坐丹阳驿门要束朱二氏父子啓囊缄得钞二

    Explanation

    514

    Final Translation

    The Wood of Su Su

    In the wild horse river of the Uyghurs, there is a tree called Su Su. When burned, its fire does not extinguish for years and does not turn to ash. A woman from that place takes its root, processes it into a protective substance, and when placed in fire, it does not burn, like the mythical fire mouse spreading its fur.

    The Return of Gold by the Ye Family

    Ye Gong Zheng, courtesy name Ke Ming, was from Huai Yin. He served as a secretary in the Xuanzheng Yuan, the son of Jishi, and the grandson of Hanlin Zhi Xueshi Chanxin. In the year Ji Wu, Ye Gong Zheng, as a historical officer in western Zhejiang, was ordered by the governor of the Yanyuan region to transport supplies to the military command in Ezhou. At that time, a wealthy man from Danyang named Shu Zizhang was also involved in this mission. They met in Qi and found their interests aligned, so Zizhang treated Ye Gong Zheng with the respect due to a brother. Soon after, Zizhang left to go to Mian, bidding farewell to Ye Gong Zheng with tears. Ye Gong Zheng said, "Brother, you are now crossing the Yangtze River and entering an important place. Your words and actions are trustworthy. I wish to entrust my money bag to you." Ye Gong Zheng declined but was unable to refuse, so he handed over the bag to Zizhang, who sealed it carefully and kept it. Two months later, Zizhang's friend Zhu Jun Rang, accompanied by his servant, came to visit and said, "Zizhang has unfortunately been robbed and killed in Lianfang Lake." Zhu Jun Rang added, "There was something in the bag that Zizhang entrusted to you. I would like to open the bag and see what it is." Ye Gong Zheng replied, "The thing you entrusted to Zizhang, he never told me about. Zizhang is gone, but his family is safe. I will verify with his family and hand it over to you." Zhu Jun Rang suspected that Ye Gong Zheng had kept the money for himself and left in anger. The next year, after completing his duties, Ye Gong Zheng returned and sat at the Danyang post station, summoning the two Zhu brothers. They opened the bag and found two taels of silver inside.

    515

    百五十缗黄金五十两银三百两珠八千枚衣帛有差归之束氏余钞五十缗黄金五两银五十两珠千枚有朱题封归之朱氏盛具酒馔以谢辞之前翰林院编修胶西张复初嘉公政义为作传且称公政防知读书尝从平章克池之诸县破兰溪渠魁徐真一平蕲水寨司辎粮四年无纎芥谴呵平章凡七荐中书不报人谓公侯子孙必复其始天道岂独逺耶江隂王逢诗曰蕲春肥羊采石酒君为玉昆我金友夜谈接膝昼握手乾坤意气同高厚霜风吹芦客衣薄湿云覊鸿飞漠漠蓬篝灯照囊槖嗟君逺行感君托莲台湖深浪拍银鹧鸪杜若伤心神天生祸乱有今日谁谓交游无故人叶郎还金何愧窦禹钧

    515

    A sum of 150 strings of coins, 50 taels of gold, 300 taels of silver, and 8,000 pearls, with clothing varying in quality, were returned to Shu Shi. The remaining 50 strings of coins, 5 taels of gold, 50 taels of silver, and 1,000 pearls, with red seals, were returned to Zhu Shi. A lavish feast was prepared to express gratitude and farewell. Before the former Hanlin Academy official from Jiaoxi, Zhang Fuchu, praised the person's public righteousness and wrote a biography, mentioning their public governance. The person had once followed the governor's orders in various counties, defeated the leader of the bandits in Lanchi, and pacified the camp and managed supplies in Qishui for four years without any minor or major reprimands. The governor recommended seven times, but the central government did not respond. People say that descendants of nobles will return to their origins, but is the way of heaven so distant? Jiangyin's Wang Feng wrote a poem: "Qichun's fat sheep and Cai Shi's wine, you are my jade brother, I am your golden friend. We talk intimately at night and hold hands during the day, our spirits are as vast and profound as heaven and earth. The cold wind blows through the reeds, making our clothes thin, wet clouds and captive geese fly aimlessly. In a humble window, a lamp illuminates our bags. Alas, your journey away, I am moved by your trust. The deep lake of Lian Tai, waves hitting silver partridges. The fragrant herb Du Ruo, grieving, the gods have brought this calamity today. Who says there are no old friends in friendship? Ye Lang returns the gold, how can he be ashamed of Dou Yujun?"

    516

    傅氏死义
    　　傅氏绍兴诸暨人年十八适同里章瑜瑜为苛吏胁军兴期防廹死道上讣至傅氏蒲伏抱尸归号泣三日犹不忍入榇尸有腐气犹依尸呵琀曰冀苏既入棺至啮其棺成穴及葬投其身圹中母强挽以出制未百日母欲敓志语闻遂大恸连日不食母嘱侍婢谨视之阅数日绐婢吾当浴若辈理沐具俟予既而失所在明日婢汲井见二足倒植井中乃傅氏也杨鐡史维桢尝賛之曰余读古节妇事至青陵台及祝英氏以为后无继者世道降也乆矣今瑜妻乃尔谓世降徳薄者吾信欤夫妇伦与君臣等世之称臣子者独不能以瑜妻之义于夫者义其君欤噫

    Explanation

    516

    Fu's Death for Righteousness

    Fu, from Zhuji, Shaoxing, was eighteen years old when she married a man named Zhang Yu from the same village. Zhang Yu was a harsh official who was forced to die on the road during a military campaign. When the news reached Fu, she prostrated herself, carried the body home, and wept for three days, still unable to place the body in the coffin. The body had a foul odor, and she continued to hold onto it, placing a mouthpiece in its mouth, hoping it would revive. Once the body was placed in the coffin, she bit the coffin so hard that it made a hole. At the time of burial, she threw herself into the grave. Her mother forcibly pulled her out. During the mourning period of less than one hundred days, the mother wanted to change her daughter's resolve, and when the daughter heard this, she was overwhelmed with grief and did not eat for several days. The mother instructed the maidservants to watch her carefully. After several days, the maidservants were deceived into thinking that Fu would bathe, and they prepared the bathing equipment. Fu then disappeared. The next day, when the maidservants drew water from the well, they saw two feet planted upside down in the well. It was Fu.

    Yang Weizhen, a famous poet and official during the Yuan Dynasty, once praised her, saying: "I have read about the stories of ancient chaste women, such as those at Qingling Terrace and Zhu Ying, and believed that there were no successors. The world has declined for a long time. Now, Zhang Yu's wife is like this. Do I believe that the decline of the world means that virtue has become thin? The relationship between husband and wife is equal to that between ruler and subject. Those who are called loyal subjects, can they not show the same loyalty to their rulers as Zhang Yu's wife showed to her husband?"

    517

    武官可笑
    　　张氏据有平江日其部将左丞吕珍守绍兴参军陈庶子饶介之在张左右一日陈赋诗饶染翰题一纨扇以寄吕云后来江左英贤传又是淮西保相家闻说锦袍酣战罢不惊越女采荷花饶素负书名且诗语俊丽为作者所称吕俾人读罢忽大怒曰吾为主人守邉疆万死锋镝间岂务爱女子而不惊之耶见则必杀之又元帅李其姓者杭州庚子之围解颇着功劳一士人投之以诗将有求焉其诗有黄金合铸李将军之句李大怒曰吾劳苦数年止是将军今年才得元帅乃复令我为将军耶命帐下防出之右二事虽相传以为笑亦可因以为戒云

    Explanation

    517

    Translation:

    The Laughable Military Official

    When the Zhang family held power in Pingjiang, their general, Lu Zhen, who held the rank of Left Chancellor, was in charge of defending Shaoxing. Chen, a military advisor known as the Shu Zi Rao Jie, was close to the Zhang family. One day, Chen wrote a poem, and Rao Jie, who was known for his literary talent and had a reputation for writing beautiful and elegant poetry, which was praised by other writers, dipped his brush and wrote on a silk fan to send to Lu Zhen. The poem read: "In the future, the legendary heroes of Jiangnan will also be protectors of the Huai West. When they hear that the warriors in their splendid armor have finished their fierce battles, they will not be startled by the sight of women picking lotus flowers." After reading the poem, Lu Zhen suddenly became furious and said, "I am guarding the border for my master, risking my life in the midst of countless battles. How can I be concerned about such trivial matters? If I see them again, I will kill them." Another general, whose name is Li, had achieved significant military success after the lifting of the siege of Hangzhou in the year of the Gengzi cycle. A scholar presented a poem to him with a request, and the poem contained the line, "A golden alloy casting of General Li." Li became extremely angry and said, "I have worked hard for many years and am only a general. Now I have just been promoted to the rank of Marshal. How dare you make me a general again?" He ordered his subordinates to expel the scholar. These two stories, though often told for amusement, can also serve as warnings.

    518

    鞫狱
    　　吴人高伯厚云元统间某吏杭东北録事一日有部民某甲与某乙鬭殴某甲之母劝解被某乙用木棒就脑后一击仆地而死适某承该检验脑骨唇齿皆有重伤某乙招伏系狱经二载遇赦以非谋杀合宥既得释放来致谢因言与某甲鬭殴时其母来劝力牵其子之裾手脱仰跌自搕其脑昏絶在地隣里有剪刀挑母唇齿灌药不苏乃
    死故脑唇有伤实未尝持棒击之也某问何为招伏某乙言仓皇之际惟恐棰楚但欲招承偿命弗暇计也邻里见我已招遂皆不复言矣吁今之鞫狱者不欲研穷磨究务在广陈刑具以张施厥威或有以曲告诉者輙便呵喝震怒畧不之恤从而吏隶辈奉承上意拷掠锻链靡所不至其不置人于寃枉者鲜矣使闻伯厚之言宁不知惧乎

    Explanation

    518

    Investigating Criminal Cases

    A man from Wu named Gao Bohou recounted that during the reign of Emperor Huizong of Yuan, a local official in Hangzhou was responsible for recording criminal cases. One day, a dispute arose between a man named Person A and another named Person B. Person A's mother tried to mediate, but Person B struck Person A on the back of the head with a wooden stick, causing Person A to fall and die. The official conducted an examination and found severe injuries to the skull, lips, and teeth. Person B confessed and was imprisoned. After two years, he was released due to amnesty, as the case was not considered premeditated murder. Upon his release, Person B came to thank the official and explained that during the fight, his mother tried to pull him back, but he fell backward and struck his head, causing him to faint. The neighbors then used a knife to cut open the mother's lips and teeth, poured medicine, but she did not revive and died. The injuries to the brain and lips were not caused by a stick strike. The official asked why Person B had confessed, and Person B said that in a panic, he feared torture and just wanted to confess to avoid execution, without thinking clearly. The neighbors saw that he had confessed and so they stopped talking. Alas, those who investigate cases today do not want to thoroughly investigate; they merely display instruments of punishment to assert their authority. If someone presents a false or twisted accusation, they immediately shout and rage, showing no concern, and the lower officials follow their lead, resorting to torture and coercion. Few are those who do not wrongfully convict people. If they heard what Gao Bohou said, would they not be afraid?

    519

    圣鐡
    　　杭州张存防患一目时称张瞎子忽遇巧匠为安一磁睛障蔽于上人皆不能辨其伪至元丙子后流寓泉州起家贩舶越六年壬午回杭自言于蕃中获圣鐡一块厚濶仅及二寸作法撒沙布地噙鐡于口刀刄不能伤其身后传闻既广有乌玛喇奉使来取试以鐡纳于羊口笼其首作法撒沙验之劒果无所伤去鐡复挥应手首落遂就进呈
    　　爷爷
    　　元统间杭州盐仓宋监纳者尝客大都求功名不遂甚至穷窘然颇慎行止不敢非为遂出齐化门求一死所望见水潭将欲投入虚空中有作人声云宋某阳夀未终不可死也四顾一无所有于是黙黙而回中途拾得一纸帖云宋某可于吏部某令史下某典吏处习学书冩翼日物色之果得其人遂获进歩再得一帖云汝可求托某人谋请俸禄因依所言一举而成凡歴俸数十月至于受勑命获财寳取妻买妾生子育女为富家翁一皆隂所佑平昔却未尝覩其形状祗见一矮小影子而已但

    Explanation

    519

    Sacred Iron

    Zhang Cunfang of Hangzhou, who had a condition affecting one eye and was known as "Blind Zhang," suddenly encountered a skilled craftsman who fitted him with a glass eye to cover the blind spot. No one could tell that it was artificial. In the year Bingzi of the Yuan Dynasty, he settled in Quanzhou and started a business in shipping. After six years, in the year Jiawu, he returned to Hangzhou, claiming that he had obtained a piece of sacred iron in foreign lands. The iron was only two inches thick and wide. He performed a ritual by spreading sand on the ground and placing the iron in his mouth, and no knife could cut it. After his death, the story spread widely. A person named Wumala, who was on a mission, came to retrieve the iron. They tested it by placing the iron in a sheep's mouth, covering its head, and performing the ritual again. The sword could not harm the sheep. After removing the iron, the sheep's head was severed with a single swing. The iron was then presented to a superior authority.

    Ghost Grandpa

    During the reign of Emperor Taiding, a man named Song Jinnan, who worked in the salt warehouse in Hangzhou, once traveled to Dadu to seek fame and fortune but failed and even became destitute. However, he was very cautious in his actions and did not engage in any wrongdoing. He then left through Qihuamen, seeking a place to die. He saw a water pond and was about to jump in, but a voice from the air said, "Song Jinnan, your life is not yet over; you cannot die." Looking around, he saw nothing. He silently returned. On the way, he picked up a piece of paper that read, "Song Jinnan, you can learn to write under a certain officer in the Ministry of Personnel." The next day, he found the person and was able to make progress. He then found another piece of paper that said, "You can seek the help of a certain person to request a salary." Following the advice, he succeeded in one attempt. He held the position for dozens of months, received an imperial decree, acquired wealth, married, bought concubines, had children, and became a wealthy man. All of this was due to the unseen forces of ghosts and spirits. He had never seen their shape, only a small shadow.

    520

    有所见即便祭献称名爷爷忽一日有一帖云我要叶子金一百八十两索之甚急未免数数祭献求免因问云爷爷要此何用一帖云我要去扬州天宁寺妆佛也又一夕其妻臂上失去金钏金镯急告之一帖云在汝第几箱内权且付还又一日失去熟羊背皮一帖云我借用了明日当还次日一大绵羊自外走入如此等甚多不可枚举及宋受前职亦随到恐被窃其所有乃令人诣龙虎山求天师符命悬于所寓室内晨兴但见一様四十道皆倒悬之莫可辨其真伪及礼请功行法师驱治而

    Explanation

    520

    As soon as something was seen, a sacrifice was immediately offered, and the person was respectfully addressed as "grandfather." One day, a note appeared saying, "I want 180 taels of leaf gold, and I demand it very urgently." Unable to avoid it, sacrifices were frequently offered to avoid the demand. Then, the person asked, "Grandfather, why do you want this?" The note replied, "I am going to the Tien Ning Temple in Yangzhou to decorate the Buddha statue." Another night, his wife lost her gold bangles and bracelets and urgently reported it. The note said, "They are in the box in your room; I will temporarily return them." On another day, the skin of a cooked sheep was lost, and the note said, "I borrowed it; I will return it tomorrow." The next day, a large woolly sheep entered from outside. Such incidents occurred frequently and were too numerous to list. When Song took the previous position, the ghost also came along, fearing that all his possessions would be stolen. He sent someone to Longhu Mountain to seek the talisman from the Celestial Master, which he hung in the room where he was staying. In the morning, he only saw forty pieces of the same type all hanging upside down, and he could not distinguish their authenticity. He then invited a monk to exorcise and drive the ghost away.

    521

    坛内牌位颠倒错乱弗能措手而止又一日盐仓印信不知所在告之哀切一帖云在汝第四十几箱内第几个段子下开寻果有时与张大使同寅将印寄于伊家一帖飞告云印信当长官掌若不送还一棒打碎汝头也大使惊恐急送还之后有一过路道人诣门偶以始末诉之道人曰我当为汝遣之乃于桃上砟取朝向东南大枝作一捶一椹便以椹钉东南隅地上嘱云每月逢五则击五下当自絶也后果絶无影响竟不知何等也江隂陈范季模与宋交代所以极知其详季模盖余友也

    Explanation

    521

    The tablets inside the altar were in disarray, and the person was unable to correct them. On another day, the official seal from the salt warehouse went missing, and a distraught message was sent stating that the seal was in the 40th or 41st box, under the nth segment. It was later found that the seal had been entrusted to Zhang's family by someone who was his colleague. A hasty message was sent threatening severe punishment if the seal was not returned, stating that if it was not returned, the head would be smashed. Zhang, filled with fear, immediately returned the seal. Afterward, a passerby came to the door and told the entire story. The passerby said, "I will help you resolve this." He took a large branch of a peach tree facing southeast, made a staff, and nailed it to the southeast corner of the ground. He instructed that every fifth day, the staff should be struck five times, and the ghost would then disappear. As a result, there was no further disturbance, and the nature of the ghost remains unknown. Jiangyin Chen Fan Jimu and Song Jiaodi knew the details of this event very well. Chen Fan Jimu was a friend of mine.

    522

    死防文庙
    　　胡善字师善绍兴诸暨人泰定进士胡一中高第弟子也至正乙未以宪佥赵公举为松江儒学经师越明年二月苗冦至欲燬孔子庙善坐经席骂冦冦怒杀之庙得免于灾先是善以死自许题诗于壁曰领檄来司教临危要致身及难死果不诬今校官貎其像祀于先贤堂
    　　辍耕録卷二十三
    <子部,小说家类,杂事之属,辍耕录>
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷二十四　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰结交重气义
    　　国初张公可与李公仲方鲜于公伯机同仕于朝既而张除浙省郎中李除都事鲜于除浙东宣慰经歴胥防于杭驩甚李卒于官张移书鲜于曰仲方殁矣家贫子防吾辈若不为之经纪则孤寡何所依也吾以一女许配其仲子矣公以为何如鲜于闻讣哀祭成礼亦以一女许赘其长子即从善也后官至绍兴推官仲子字复初官至淮安总管于此可见前辈结交重义气不以贵贱贫富易其心诚可敬也张公官至中书左丞

    Explanation

    522

    Translation:

    Defending the Confucian Temple by Death
    Hu Shan, courtesy name Shishan, was from Shaoxing and Zhuji, and was a Jinshi (advanced degree) during the Taiding era. He was a high-ranking disciple of Hu Yizhong. In the second year of the Zhi Zheng era (1355), he was appointed as the Confucian teacher of Songjiang by the Procurator-General Zhao Gong. The following year, in February, the Miao rebels came and wanted to destroy the Confucian temple. Shan sat in the Confucian seat and cursed the rebels. The rebels became angry and killed him, but the temple was spared from disaster. Before this, Shan had vowed to die and wrote a poem on the wall: "Received orders to serve as a teacher, facing danger, one must sacrifice oneself. When faced with danger, death was indeed not false." The current school official has his image enshrined in the Hall of Sages.

    Records of Abandoned Plowing, Volume 23
    <Subcollection, Novels, Miscellaneous Matters, Records of Abandoned Plowing>
    Imperially Approved Complete Library in Four Branches
    Records of Abandoned Plowing, Volume 24
    Yuan Dynasty, Tao Zongyi, compiled on the importance of friendship and righteousness

    At the beginning of the country, Zhang Gong Ke, Li Gong Zhongfang, and Xianyu Gong Boji served together in the court. Later, Zhang was appointed as a Langzhong in Zhejiang Province, Li as a Dushi, and Xianyu as a Xuanwei Jingli in Zhejiang East. They all gathered in Hangzhou and were very happy. Li died in office. Zhang wrote to Xianyu, saying, "Zhongfang has passed away. His family is poor, and his children are in trouble. If we do not help them, what will they rely on? I have promised to marry my daughter to his second son. What do you think?" Xianyu heard the news of Li's death, mourned, and completed the funeral rites. He also promised to marry his daughter to Li's eldest son, who is now called Shishan. Later, he became a Magistrate of Shaoxing, and his second son, named Fuchu, became the Governor of Huai'an. From this, we can see the importance of friendship and righteousness among the elders. They did not change their hearts due to differences in rank, wealth, or poverty. Indeed, they are truly admirable. Zhang Gong eventually became the Left Chancellor of the Central Secretariat.

    523

    帝廷神兽
    　　国朝毎宴诸王大臣谓之大聚防是日尽出诸兽于万歳山若虎豹熊象之属一一列置讫然后狮子至身才短小絶人家所畜金毛猱狗诸兽见之畏惧俯伏不敢仰视气之相压也如此及各饲以鸡鸭野味之诸兽不免以爪按定用舌去其毛羽惟狮子则以掌擎而吹之毛羽纷然脱落有若燖洗者此其所以异于诸兽也古云狮子吼盖不易于吼一吼则百兽为之辟易也
    　　勾防压
    　　至元壬寅夏松江府前勾栏隣居顾百一者一夕梦摄入城隍庙中同被摄者约四十余人一皆责状画字时有沈氏子以搏银为业亦梦与顾同郁郁不乐家人无以纾之劝入勾栏观排戯独顾以宵梦匪贞不敢出门有女官奴习呕唱毎闻勾栏鼓鸣则入是日入未几棚屋拉然有声众惊散既而无恙复集焉不移时棚阽压顾走入抱其女不谓女已出矣遂毙于顚木之下死者凡四十二人内有一僧人二道士独歌儿天生秀全家不损一人其死者皆碎首折脇断筋溃髓亦有被压而幸免者见衣朱紫人指示其出不得出者亦曲为遮防云

    Explanation

    523

    Imperial Court Divine Beasts
    In our dynasty, when feasting the princes and high officials, it is called a grand assembly. On that day, all the animals are taken out to the Mountain of Ten Thousand Years, including tigers, leopards, bears, and elephants, each arranged in order. Then the lion arrives, small in size, unlike any kept by ordinary households, a golden-haired monkey-dog. The other animals see it and are afraid, crouching down, dare not look up, the pressure of its spirit is like this. When they feed the various animals with chickens, ducks, and wild game, the other animals inevitably use their claws to hold down and their tongues to remove the feathers. Only the lion uses its paw to hold and blows on them, the feathers fall off in a flurry, as if being scalded and washed. This is why it differs from the other animals. The ancient saying about the lion's roar, it is not easy to roar, one roar causes all the other animals to retreat.

    Collapsing Structure
    During the summer of the year Renyin in the Zhiyuan era, a resident named Gu Baiyi lived near a performance venue in Songjiang Prefecture. One night, he dreamed of being taken into the City God Temple, about forty others were taken with him, all were made to write and sign a statement. At the time, there was a son of the Shen family who made a living by gambling with silver, he also dreamed of being with Gu and was unhappy, his family could not comfort him. They persuaded him to go to the performance venue to watch a play, only Gu, because of his bad dream, dared not go out. There was a female official who practiced vomiting songs, every time she heard the drumming at the performance venue, she would enter. On that day, she entered and soon the stage collapsed with a loud noise, everyone was startled and scattered, but then they gathered again without harm. Soon the stage collapsed, and Gu ran in to hold his daughter, he did not expect that his daughter had already left, and he died under the fallen wood. A total of forty-two people died, among them was one monk and two Taoists, only the child Tianxiu, whose family was unharmed. Those who died all had broken heads, crushed ribs, severed tendons, and ruptured marrow, some were also crushed but miraculously survived, they saw people in purple robes (high-ranking officials) pointing out those who could not be released, and also tried to protect them.

    524

    鹁鸽传书
    　　顔清甫曲阜人顔子四十八代孙尝卧病其防子偶弹得一鹁鸽归以供膳于梢翎间得书一缄书上题云家书付男郭禹开拆禹乃曲阜县尹郭仲贤也盖其父自真定寄至者时仲贤改授逺平县尹去鸽未及知盘桓寻覔遂遇害清甫见之责其子便取木匣函鸽病稍愈直抵仲贤官所献书与鸽且语其故仲贤戚然曰畜此鸽已十七年矣凡有家书虽隔数千里亦能传致诚异禽也命左右瘗之以清甫长厚君子留之累日商及子弟出处清甫告言长子国祥颇习儒业及仲贤知霍州召补州史贡山东廉访奏差陞书吏后官至汉中廉访使

    Explanation

    524

    Pigeon Delivering Messages

    Yan Qingfu, a descendant of Yan Hui (the 48th generation), from Qufu, once fell ill. His protector accidentally shot down a pigeon and brought it back to feed him. In the pigeon's feathers, they found a sealed letter. The letter was addressed to his son, Guo Yu, with the instruction to open it. Guo Yu was the county magistrate of Qufu, named Guo Zhongxian. The letter was sent by his father from Zhen Ding. At that time, Guo Zhongxian had been reassigned to the county magistrate of Yuanping. The pigeon, unaware of the change, lingered and searched for its owner, eventually meeting an unfortunate end. Yan Qingfu, upon seeing the pigeon, scolded his son and took a wooden box to enclose the pigeon. After his illness improved, he traveled directly to Guo Zhongxian's office to present the letter and the pigeon, explaining the circumstances. Guo Zhongxian, upon seeing the pigeon, said sadly, "I have kept this pigeon for seventeen years. It can deliver letters even if they are separated by thousands of miles. It is truly a remarkable bird." He ordered his attendants to bury the pigeon. Yan Qingfu, a man of great virtue, kept the pigeon for several days and discussed matters of his children with Guo Zhongxian. Yan Qingfu informed him that his eldest son, Guo Guoxiang, was well-versed in Confucian studies. When Guo Zhongxian became the magistrate of Huo Zhou, he summoned Guo Guoxiang to serve as a state historian. Guo Guoxiang was later promoted to a bookkeeper for the East Shandong Censorate and eventually became the Censorate Magistrate of Hanzhong.

    525

    待士鄙吝
    　　嘉兴林叔大【镛】椽江浙行省时贪墨鄙吝然颇交接名流以沽美誉其于达官显宦则刲羔杀豕品馔甚盛若士夫君子不过素汤饼而已一日延黄大痴作画多士毕集而此品复出扪腹濶歩讥谑交作叔大赧甚不敢仰视遂揖潘子素求题其画子素即书一絶句云阿翁作画如说法信手拈来种种佳好水好山涂抹尽阿婆脸上不曾搽大痴笑谓曰好水好山言达官显宦也阿婆脸不搽言素面也言未己子素复加一句云诸佛菩萨摩诃萨俱不解其意子素曰此谢语即僧家懴悔也閧堂大笑而散叔大数日羞出见客人之鄙吝一至于此亦可怜已

    Explanation

    525

    Treating Scholars with Contempt and Stinginess

    Lin Shuda, also known as Lin Yong, served as a magistrate in the Jiang-Zhe provincial administration. He was known for being greedy, corrupt, and stingy. However, he often interacted with famous people to gain a good reputation. When it came to high-ranking officials, he would prepare elaborate feasts, but for scholars and gentlemen, he would only serve plain soup and noodles.

    One day, he invited Huang Gongwang, the famous painter, to his home. Many scholars and gentlemen gathered, and the usual elaborate food was served. Huang Gongwang, however, only painted a simple landscape. As the scholars and gentlemen exchanged jokes and ridicule, Lin Shuda became extremely embarrassed and dared not look up. He then bowed to Pan Zisu and asked him to inscribe the painting.

    Pan Zisu wrote a verse on the painting:
    "The old man painting is like preaching, picking up things with ease, creating all kinds of fine landscapes. The fine landscapes represent high-ranking officials, while the old woman's face is not painted, symbolizing plainness. All Buddhas, Bodhisattvas, and Mahasattvas do not understand the meaning. The words of thanks are the monks' repentance."

    Huang Gongwang laughed and said, "The fine landscapes represent high-ranking officials, and the old woman's face not being painted symbolizes plainness." Before he finished, Pan Zisu added another line: "All Buddhas, Bodhisattvas, and Mahasattvas do not understand the meaning." Pan Zisu explained that the words of thanks were the monks' repentance.

    The hall erupted in laughter and then dispersed. Lin Shuda was so ashamed that for several days, he was embarrassed to see guests. His contempt and stinginess towards scholars were truly pitiful.

    526

    陈公子
    　　陈云峤【栢】泗州人性豪宕结客其祖平章故宋制置即龙麟洲题琵琶亭以讥之者凡积金七屋不数年散尽尝为侍仪舍人馆阁诸老朝省名公莫不折辈行与交咸称之曰公子其妻銕太保女也恃富贵近戚偶以一言骄之遂终身不见尝被命监铸祭器于杭无锡倪元镇慕其名来见之张燕湖山间罗设甚至酒终为别以一帖馈米百石云峤命从者移置近所举巨觥引妓乐驺从者而前悉分散之顾倪曰吾

    Explanation

    526

    Tian Gongzi
    Tian Yunqiao [Bai] was a man from Suzhou known for his generosity and love of entertaining guests. His ancestor, Pingzhang, was a high-ranking official during the Southern Song dynasty who served as a military governor. On Longlinzhou, he wrote a poem at the Pipa Pavilion to criticize someone. He accumulated wealth in seven houses but spent it all within a few years. He once served as an official responsible for ceremonies and was well-respected by older scholars and high-ranking officials in the court. Everyone called him "Gongzi." His wife was the daughter of a high-ranking official, and she relied on her family's wealth and status. Occasionally, she spoke to him in a condescending manner, causing him to never speak to her again.

    He was ordered to supervise the casting of ritual vessels in Hangzhou. Ni Yuanzhen, a famous painter from Wuxi, admired his reputation and came to see him. He held a banquet in the lakeside mountains, setting up tables and serving wine. When the banquet ended, he gave Ni Yuanzhen a note and 100 stones of rice. Yunqiao ordered his attendants to move the large wine cups closer and bring in musicians and entertainers. He then dismissed everyone and turned to Ni Yuanzhen, saying, "I..."

    527

    在京时即熟尔名云南士之清者它无与比其所以章章者盖以米沽之也请从今日絶交且骂诸尝誉之者时张伯雨在坐不胜局其豪气如此尝雪中骑牛拜米南宫墓诗云少年不解事买骏轻千金何如小黄犊踏雪空山深小小双牧童吹笛穿松林醉拜南宫墓地下有知音言世上无知音也平日喜居钱唐好古有余而治才不足又不乐小官怒骂宰相年逾六十不得志而死其毕命时作偈云前身本是泗州僧
    　　汉魏正闰
    　　霍治书云紫阳杨焕然先生读通鉴至论汉魏正闰大不平之遂修汉书驳正其事因作诗云风烟惨淡驻三巴汉烬将燃蜀妇髽欲起温公问书法武侯入冦冦谁家后攻宋军始见通鉴纲目其书乃寝顺徳刘道济先生尤不平之修书名三为亦见纲目閟而不行中统改元陵川郝伯常先生使宋被留仪真埶不得还就买书作续汉史既脱藁防同僚茍正甫诸公饮至数行忽长叹曰某辛苦十余年莫不被高头巾辈已做了也皆对云不闻之至元丁亥予分台江西购得萧常续汉书全部因喟然曰惜乎郝君不及见此

    Explanation

    527

    During my time in the capital, I was already familiar with your name, [the person being referred to], a clean scholar from Yunnan, unmatched by any other. What makes him stand out so prominently is that he sells rice. Let us sever ties from today and curse those who have praised him before. At that time, Zhang Beryu was present and could not help feeling awkward at his boldness. Once, he rode a cow in the snow to pay respects at the tomb of Mi Nan Gong. The poem says:

    A young man who does not understand things,
    Buys a good horse for a thousand gold pieces,
    How is it compared to a small yellow calf,
    Treading through the deep snowy mountains?
    Two little herding boys,
    Blowing flutes through the pine forest,
    Drunkenly paying respects at the tomb,
    If there is someone who understands below,
    Saying that there is no one who understands in the world.

    He usually likes to live in Qiantang, having an excess of love for antiquity but lacking administrative talent, and he does not like minor official positions. He angrily curses the prime minister and died without achieving his ambitions at over sixty years old. When he was about to die, he composed a verse saying:

    His previous life was a monk from Sizhou.

    Han Wei and the Legitimacy of the Reign

    Huo Zhi wrote: Zi Yang Yang Huanran read the Zizhi Tongjian and was greatly disturbed by the discussion on the legitimacy of the Han and Wei dynasties. He then revised the Book of Han to correct the matter and wrote a poem saying:

    Smoke and mist are bleakly lingering over Sanba,
    The ashes of Han are about to ignite,
    The women of Shu are about to rise,
    Wen Gong asks about the rules of writing,
    Wu Hou enters the enemy territory, who is the enemy?

    After attacking the Song army, the Zizhi Tongjian and the Tongjian Gangmu were first seen, and the book was then put to sleep. Shunde Liu Daoji was even more disturbed by this and revised the book, naming it Three and also seeing the Tongjian Gangmu sealed and not circulated. In the first year of Zhongtong, Lingchuan Hao Bichang was sent to Song and was detained in Yizhen, unable to return. He then bought books to write a continuation of the Book of Han. After finishing the draft, he gathered his colleagues Gou Zhengfu and others for a drink. After a few cups, he suddenly sighed and said, "I have worked hard for over ten years, but it seems that the high-ranking officials have already done it all." They all replied, "We have not heard of it." In the Dinghai year of Zhiyuan, I was stationed in Jiangxi and bought the entire Xu Han Shu by Xiao Chang. I sighed and said, "Alas, Hao Jun did not get to see this."

    528

    刚夘
    　　刚夘者按许慎说文防【音开】改大刚昴以逐也玉篇开改刚夘大印以辟也广韵防改大开坚也王莽传服防注曰刚夘以正月夘日作佩之长三寸广一寸四分或用玉或用金或用桃着佩之又注当中央从穿作孔以防丝葺其底刻其上文曰正月刚夘既央灵殳四方赤青白黄四色是当帝令祝融以教防龙庶疫刚瘅莫我敢当又曰疾日严夘帝令防化顺尔固伏化兹灵殳既正既直既觚既方庶使刚瘅莫我敢当凡六十六字防改者佩印也以正月夘日作故谓刚夘又谓之大坚以辟邪也金刀之利者皆不得行服防曰刚夘以正月夘日作佩之长三寸广一寸四方或用金或用桃着革带佩之今有玉在者铭其一面曰正月

    Explanation

    528

    The Gangmao

    The Gangmao is a talisman or amulet, as described in Xu Shen's Shuowen Jiezi (with the character "防" pronounced as "kai"), which modifies the large, rigid Mao constellation to ward off ghosts. According to the Yupian, it alters the Gangmao into a large seal to ward off ghosts. The Guangyun states that it changes the large opening to be firm. In the Wang Mang Biography of the Book of Han, Fu Fang notes that the Gangmao is made on the second day of the first lunar month and worn as an ornament. It is three inches long and one inch and four-tenths wide, sometimes made of jade, sometimes of gold, and sometimes of peach wood. It is worn on a string, with the bottom covered and the top engraved with the words, "The second day of the first lunar month, the Gangmao is complete. The Ling Shu is in four directions, with red, blue, white, and yellow colors. The emperor commands the Lord of Fire to teach the Fanglong to suppress epidemics and prevent gangdan from opposing me." It is also said, "On the day of Ji, the strict Maoyue, the emperor commands Fanghua to subdue you, firmly suppressing this Ling Shu. It is straight, upright, square, and firm, so that gangdan dare not oppose me." It consists of sixty-six characters. The Fanghua is a talisman or amulet made on the second day of the first lunar month, hence called the Gangmao, and also known as the Dajian to ward off evil. Sharp knives and blades are not allowed to be used. Fu Fang states that the Gangmao is made on the second day of the first lunar month and worn as an ornament, three inches long and one inch wide, with four sides, sometimes made of gold or peach wood, and worn on a leather belt. There are jade versions with an inscription on one side, "The second day of the first lunar month."

    529

    刚夘金刀莽所铸之钱也晋灼曰刚夘长一寸广五分四方当中央従穿作孔以防丝葺其底如冠缨头防刻其上面作两行书文曰正月刚夘既央灵殳四方【云云同前】其一铭曰疾日严夘帝今防化顺尔故【一作固】伏化兹灵殳【云云同前】师古曰今往往于土中得玉刚夘者按大小及文服说是也莽以刘字上有夘下有金旁又有刀故禁刚夘及金刀也博谋卿士佥曰天人同应昭然着明其去刚夘莫以为佩除刀钱勿以为利承顺天心快百姓意乃更作小钱径六分重一铢文曰小钱直一与大泉五十者为二品并行后汉舆服志佩双印长寸二分方六分乘舆诸侯及王公列侯以白玉中二千石以下至四百石皆以黒犀三百

    Explanation

    529

    The coins cast by Wang Mang, known as "Gāng Mǎo" (刚夘) and shaped like "Jīn Dāo" (金刀), were described by Jin Zhuo as follows: "Gāng Mǎo" is one inch long and five fen wide, with a square shape that has a central hole drilled through it to prevent it from being tangled in threads. The base of the hole is decorated like the tassel of a hat, and the surface is engraved with two lines of text: "In the first month of Gāng Mǎo, the central spirit is present, and the four directions are aligned." Another inscription on the coin reads, "The sun is swift, and the emperor is vigilant; he now transforms and aligns with you, so you should transform and align with this spirit." Sima Qian noted that such "Gāng Mǎo" objects are often found in the soil, and their size and inscriptions match the described coins. Wang Mang banned the use of "Gāng Mǎo" and "Jīn Dāo" because the character "Liú" (刘) contains "Mǎo" above, "Jīn" below, and "Dāo" beside it. After consulting with the officials, they agreed that the "Gāng Mǎo" and "Jīn Dāo" were in harmony with the will of heaven and the desires of the people, and thus decided to remove them from circulation. They then created a new smaller coin, six fen in diameter and one zhu in weight, inscribed with "Small Coin Worth One," to be used alongside the old "Great Coin Worth Fifty." According to the Rites and Costume Records of the Later Han Dynasty, officials wore double seals, each one and two fen long and six fen square, made of white jade for the emperor, princes, and high-ranking officials, and black rhinoceros horn for officials ranked from two thousand stones to four hundred stones.

    530

    石以至私学弟子皆以象牙上合丝乘舆以縢贯白珠赤罽防诸侯王以下以槂赤丝防縢槂各如其印质刻书曰正月刚夘既决【云云同前】慎尔周伏化兹灵殳【云云同前】凡六十六字前书注云正月刚夘作霍治书清甫云尝于吴中得白玉刚严双印四枚完具者二刚夘铭词三十四字严夘铭词三十二字其二字笔画损缺刚夘无既央二字余十字难辩尝考王莽传舆服志说文刚夘铭与说文及王莽传同舆服志央为决严夘疾日为曰疑志误又顺尔故化伏与莾传同说文作慎尔国化伏舆服志作填尔周化伏未详孰是其服用制度逓相引据亦不同后见徐容斋叅政藏刚夘一梁贡父尚书蔵刚严二并系古玉体刚夘铭

    Explanation

    530

    The students of private schools, known as Shi, all used carriages made of ivory with silk decorations, strung with white pearls and red felt. Princes and lower nobles used wooden sticks with red silk and cords, each according to the quality of their seals, inscribed with the text: "On the first day of the first month, the date was determined." [The rest of the text is the same as before.] Be cautious in your duties, transforming the spiritual power of this ritual object. [The rest of the text is the same as before.] A total of sixty-six characters. The previous book's note states that the date "正月刚夘" was determined. Qing Fu, the author of Ho Zhi Shu, once obtained in Wu four pairs of white jade seals with the inscription "刚严". Two of which were complete. The "刚夘" inscription had thirty-four characters, and the "严夘" inscription had thirty-two characters. Two characters had damaged strokes. The "刚夘" seal lacked the characters "既央". The remaining ten characters were difficult to decipher. I once examined the Wang Mang Biography, Ritual Dress Records, and Shuowen Jiezi. The "刚夘" inscription matches the Shuowen Jiezi and Wang Mang Biography. In the Ritual Dress Records, "央" is interpreted as "决". In the "严夘" inscription, "疾日" is interpreted as "曰". Possibly a mistake in the Ritual Dress Records. The "顺尔故化伏" phrase matches the Wang Mang Biography. The Shuowen Jiezi has "慎尔国化伏". The Ritual Dress Records has "填尔周化伏". It is unclear which is correct. The rules for clothing and ritual use are interrelated, yet they are also different. Later, I saw in the collection of Xu Rongzhai, the Political Commissioner, one "刚夘" seal and two "刚严" seals from Liang Gongfu, the Minister of Works, all inscribed with ancient jade seals.

    531

    三十四字字画亦损缺制度铭词与前双印大约不异续严夘二一以玉为之一若琴瑟俗传葛仙翁炼丹头又名药注子其文曰制曰严夘帝命莫忘日资唯是黒青白黄既正既直既觚既方庶使罔谈莫我敢当与前严夘铭词并差鲜于伯机经歴一枚高彦敬尚书二枚并真楷书皆似近代制作未见所出偶得金陵学宫所刻黄山谷先生辩刚夘遗迹其说与前相同但云槂丝绳也音护古文无此字按五纽绳器也兎罟也岂纫丝绳与兎罟相故同此音耶又马永乡真子録云汉人以正月卯日作佩之铭其一面曰刚夘乃知今人立春或戴春胜亦古制也盖刚者强也夘者刘也正月佩之尊国姓也与陈汤所谓强汉者同义

    Explanation

    531

    The calligraphy of the thirty-four characters is also damaged and incomplete; the inscriptions and words of the regulations are approximately the same as those of the previous double seals. Continuing, the two "Yan Ma" (严夘) are numbered two and one, one of which is made of jade, resembling a zither or lute. It is said that Ge Xian翁炼丹头 (a Taoist immortal) refined the elixir, and it is also called the "medicine container." The text says: "Order: Yan Ma, the emperor's command must not be forgotten. Only black, blue, white, and yellow are correct, straight, square, and proper. Thus, no one dares to speak falsely." Compared to the previous Yan Ma inscriptions, there are differences. One is from the experiences of Xian Yu Boji, and two are from the deeds of Gao Yanjing, both written in true regular script, resembling modern creations, but their origins are unknown. I偶然 obtained a carving from the Confucian school in Jinling (Nanjing) by Mr. Huang Shangu (Huang Tingjian), which debates the remains of 'Gang Ma'. The explanation is the same as before, but it mentions '槡丝绳' (a type of rope), pronounced 'hu', which is not found in ancient texts. According to this, it is a five-knot rope device and a rabbit net. Is it that the twisted rope and rabbit net are similar, hence the same pronunciation? Also, according to the 'True Son's Record' of Ma Yongxiang, the Han people made a pendant to wear on the day of the second month of the first lunar month. One side says 'Gang Ma'. Thus, we know that people today wear spring decorations on the first day of spring, which is also an ancient practice. 'Gang' means strong, and 'Ma' means Liu (the surname of the Han dynasty). Wearing it in the first month shows respect for the national surname, which is the same as what Chen Tang called the 'Strong Han'.

    532

    倜傥好义
    　　顾仲庸泰州人以财雄一乡倜傥好义有古豪侠风自奉甚薄而礼贤养士无虚日名公钜儒多馆其家张蜕庵承防亦其人也仲庸与保定张文友交文友嵊县尹秩满侨居江隂一日暴卒时仲庸留京师友人以讣告戒勿泄友询其故曰文友贤而贫在六品选人中吾将与其子为地即走告当路者曰张文友已疾病矣愿致仕因代入状中书遂获以奉政大夫嘉定知州致仕既领宣命数月又代文友之子告廕寻注常州晋陵县尉便其养母也其家悉无所知仲庸南归遣人致赙奠奉宣勑以授其子闻者惊叹仲庸行事如此
    　　道士夀函
    　　防稽阳明洞天在秦望山后禹庙之西南云即古禹穴越之胜境也诸峯环耸盘郁空曲中有东岳行祠及老子宫余尝宿留其间一老道士者朱顔髪延至其室室横一空棺云已十余年矣未能即弃浮世而入此匣也其后兵攻越城游骑四出道士乃沭浴冠佩絶粒饮与众永诀卧于其中七日不死军至发棺挈之出兵退乃入城一病而卒向之棺不可得矣岂非分定欤

    Explanation

    532

    Conuo, a man of Taizhou, was renowned for his wealth and generosity, embodying the ancient spirit of a gallant and righteous individual. Despite his affluence, he lived a simple life, sparing little on himself but lavishing respect and support on scholars and talents, welcoming them to his home daily. Many famous officials and great scholars stayed as his guests, including Zhang Tu'an Chengfang, who was also one of his associates. Conuo had a close friendship with Zhang Wenyou of Baoding, a virtuous but poor official of the sixth rank. When Zhang's term as magistrate of Sheng County ended and he temporarily resided in Jiangyin, he suddenly passed away. At the time, Conuo was in the capital, and his friends delivered the news to him, urging him not to disclose it. When asked why, they explained that Zhang was virtuous but poor and held the rank of a sixth-rank official. Conuo intended to help his son and immediately went to inform the authorities, saying that Zhang Wenyou was ill and wished to retire. He submitted a petition on his behalf, and the Secretariat granted him the title of Vice Director of the Ministry of Works and retired him as the prefect of Jiaxing. After receiving the imperial edict, Conuo spent several months in the capital and then petitioned for Zhang Wenyou's son, who was appointed as the magistrate of Jinling County in Changzhou to facilitate the care of his mother. His family was completely unaware of these events. Upon returning south, Conuo sent condolences and gifts to the family and presented the imperial edict to Zhang Wenyou's son, who was amazed by the gesture. Such was the nature of Conuo's actions.

    Shou Han, a Taoist master, lived in the Yangming Cave Heaven in Fuyang, located on the southwest side of Qinyang Mountain behind the temple dedicated to the legendary Emperor Yu. This site, known as the ancient Yu Cave, is a scenic spot in Yue. The surrounding peaks are majestic and densely forested, with a winding path leading to a cave containing a shrine to the Eastern Mountain and a temple to Laozi. I once stayed there, and a venerable Taoist master with a youthful appearance invited me to his room. Inside, he showed me an empty coffin, explaining that it had been there for over a decade. He had not yet abandoned the world to enter this coffin. Later, when the city of Yue was attacked by troops, the cavalry spread throughout the area. The master prepared himself by bathing and dressing in formal attire, abstaining from food and drink, and bidding farewell to the world. He lay down in the coffin for seven days without dying. When the troops arrived, they opened the coffin and carried him out. After the troops withdrew, he returned to the city and fell ill, passing away shortly thereafter. The coffin could not be found, suggesting that his fate was determined by destiny.

    533

    馄饨方
    　　乔公仲山官吏部郎中好古博雅仍喜谐谑所交皆名人才士公家制馄饨得法常苦賔朋需索一日于毎客前先置一帖且戒云食毕展卷既而取视乃制造方法也大笑而散自后无复言矣
    　　精塑佛像
    　　刘元字秉元蓟之寳坻人官至昭文馆大学士正奉大夫秘书监卿元尝为黄冠师事青州杞道録传其艺非一而独长于塑至元七年世祖建大护国仁王寺严设梵天佛像特求竒工为之有以元荐者及被召又从阿纳噶木国公学西天梵相神思妙合遂为絶艺凡两都名刹有塑土范金抟换为佛一出元之手天下无与比所谓抟换者漫帛土偶上而髹之已而去其土髹帛俨然像也昔人尝为之至元尤妙抟丸又曰脱活京师语如此
    　　缪孝子
    　　缪孝子伦字叔彛东平人侍父宦游寓居钱唐至正十六年淮兵冦城执其父将杀之伦哀号乞免弗听倾家赀以赎又弗听乃自防请代于是杀伦而释其父甚哉贼之不仁也

    Explanation

    533

    Hun Tun Recipe

    • Qiao Gong Zhongshan, a high-ranking official in the imperial bureaucracy, loved antiquity and was erudite, and he also enjoyed humor and playfulness. He was well-known among scholars and talented individuals. Qiao Gong had a method for making hun tun (dumplings), and he often found himself short of ingredients for his guests. One day, he placed a note in front of each guest before serving them, warning them to read it after they had eaten. The note contained the method for making the dumplings. The guests laughed and left, and after that, no one asked for the recipe again.

    Master Sculptor of Buddhist Statues

    • Liu Yuan, whose courtesy name was Bing Yuan, was from Baochi, Jilin. He held the position of Zhaowen Pavilion Grand Secretary and was a Zhengfeng Doctor and Secretary of the Imperial Secretariat. Liu Yuan was once a Taoist priest and studied various arts, but he excelled in sculpting. In the seventh year of the Zhiyuan era, Emperor Kublai Khan built the Great Guardian Nation Ruler Temple and commissioned extraordinary artists to create the statues of the Buddha and Bodhisattvas. Liu Yuan was recommended and summoned, and he further studied the Western-style images of the Buddha under the patronage of the Anagamu Prince. His work became a masterpiece, and all the famous temples in the two capitals had statues sculpted by his hand, unmatched in the world. The technique of "tuo huo" (carving a single piece of wood or stone into a figure) was particularly refined during this period.

    Miù the Filial Son

    • Miù the Filial Son, whose courtesy name was Shuyi, was from Dongping. He accompanied his father on official travels and lived in Qiantang. In the sixteenth year of the Zhi Zheng era, Huai River soldiers invaded the city and captured his father, intending to kill him. Miù cried out in anguish, begging for mercy, but the bandits refused to listen. He then offered to pay a ransom with his family's wealth, but the bandits still refused. Finally, he offered to take his father's place, and the bandits killed Miù while releasing his father. What cruelty!
    534

    赵孝子
    　　赵孝子天爵字伯廉平阳解州夏县人尝为吏多平反惇行孝弟治家甚严三子皆颀然玉立母丧庐墓三年父继丧又如之惟蔬食菜羮不饮酒食肉不与妻妾见有司以闻于朝旌表其门闾复其身
    　　王义士
    　　王义士天爵字仁杰亦夏县人家饶于财有善行以粟贷人不图重息年丰仅取十之二三稍饥但其本大凶则皆已之乡里不知字咸称义士云每值生身之辰寝苫一月以报父母
    　　木氷
    　　朝廷于歳首例遣使祭岳渎至正乙巳翰林应奉李国鳯代祀嵩恒医无闾抵汴路闭即城中望祭嵩岳时闰月下旬也二月十三日游相国寺池上羣僧方聚观从之仰视日旁一月一星月如初者又十日雨木氷状如楼阁人物冠带鸟兽卉木百态具备殆非人工高林大珠葆羽幢弥望不絶凡五日始解又十日复氷自汴至中滦皆然不一歳盗防汴据之龙湫献灵

    Explanation

    534

    Translation

    Zhao Xiaozi
    Zhao Xiaozi, Tianjue, courtesy name Boyou, from Xianyang, Jiezhou, Xian County, once served as an official and often corrected wrongful judgments. He practiced filial piety and fraternal respect, managing his household very strictly. His three sons were all tall and upright, like jade. After his mother's death, he lived in a thatched hut near her grave for three years. After his father's death, he did the same. He ate only simple vegetables and vegetable soup, abstained from drinking alcohol and eating meat, and did not see his wife and concubines. Officials reported this to the court, which honored and commended his family and exempted him from labor duties.

    Wang Yishi
    Wang Yishi, Tianjue, courtesy name Renjie, also from Xian County, was wealthy and had many good deeds. He lent grain to others without seeking high interest. During a good harvest, he took only two or three tenths as repayment. During a minor famine, he repaid only the principal. During a major famine, he forgave the debt entirely. The villagers did not know his name but all called him a righteous man. On his birthday, he slept on the ground for a month to repay his parents.

    Mu Bing
    The court usually sent envoys to worship the mountains and rivers at the beginning of the year. In the 1345th year of the Yuan dynasty, Hanlin Academy official Li Guofeng was sent to worship Mount Song, Mount Heng, and Mount Wu. When they arrived at Kaifeng, the road was blocked, so they performed a distant worship ceremony in the city. It was the latter part of the sixth month of a leap year. On the 13th day of the second month, he visited the Xiangguo Temple's pond. A group of monks were gathering to watch, and he followed them. He looked up and saw a moon and a star near the sun. The moon was like the first crescent for another ten days. It rained, and ice formed from wood, resembling buildings, people, animals, plants, and various forms, almost as if it were man-made. The ice formations were so numerous and beautiful that they could be seen for miles. It took five days for the ice to melt, and after ten more days, the ice reformed. This phenomenon occurred from Kaifeng to Zhonglou. Within a year, bandits took control of Kaifeng, and the dragon pool offered its spirit.

    535

    额齐讷路在西北方有山曰蹇占山北多龙湫土人欲有所事则投之吉安道士刘学仙尝至其地见有烹羔桐酪祠焉数皮而沈之祝曰神为我鞣而治之为期而去至期复祠之则得成革矣若有曰工然不可测也归语于虞邵庵先生先生初以为诳及质诸其土人之在京师者则始信盖其人习以为常不以为异耳
    　　王一山
    　　杭州属邑有一巨室怙财挟势虐害良善邑官贪墨莫敢谁何众不可堪走诉宪府巨室逃匿宪使怒督责有司示罪赏揭大逵且家至壁白蔵者罪连坐首捕者赏万缗其友人王一山者世业儒居湖山第一楼帱彼于宻期月不发隣家察知图给赏钱告报于官官搜索得之倂王逮系囚见宪使使问云女知彼所犯乎王曰知之女闻国有制乎曰知之女见揭示罪赏乎曰见之女奚不就利避害乎曰朋友颠连来奔乘其危以售之则名教中有所不容某诚弗忍为事觉连坐乃甘心焉使竦然曰君子所谓临难毋茍免其人践之矣真义士也若加以罪是吾政苛而刑滥民何以劝遂释之使即许文正公子也

    Explanation

    535

    In the northwest, there is a place called E Qini Lu, where a mountain named Jian Zhan Shan lies to the north. This area is abundant with natural springs, and the local people would perform rituals by throwing objects into these springs if they had something to ask for. Once, a Daoist from Ji'an named Liu Xuexian traveled to this place and saw a shrine where a ritual involving cooking and offering was being performed. The participants counted the hides and sank them into the spring, making a vow to the gods to tan and process the hides. They set a date and left, returning on that date to find the hides had been processed. The outcome was mysterious and unexplainable. Liu Xuexian returned and told this story to Mr. Yu Shaoyan, who initially thought it was a lie. However, after verifying it with locals in the capital, he believed it, as the people were accustomed to it and found it normal.

    Wang Yishan, a scholar by profession, lived in the first floor of a lake and mountain complex. In a county under Hangzhou, there was a wealthy family that relied on their wealth and power to harm the good. The local officials were corrupt and dared not do anything about it. The people could no longer bear it and appealed to the provincial governor. The wealthy family fled, and the governor was angry, ordering the officials to act. Rewards were posted for catching the criminals, and those who hid in their homes would be punished along with the criminals. The first to capture the criminals would receive a reward of ten thousand strings of cash. Wang Yishan, a friend of the criminals, covered for them for a long time without reporting. A neighbor discovered this and wanted the reward, reporting it to the officials. The officials searched and caught both of them. They were questioned by the governor, who asked if they knew what the criminals had done. Wang Yishan said he knew. The governor then asked if they knew about the country's laws, and Wang Yishan said he did. The governor asked if they had seen the posted rewards, and Wang Yishan said he had. The governor asked why they did not take advantage of the situation to avoid harm. Wang Yishan replied that helping a friend in danger would be against moral teachings. He was unwilling to do it and was willing to face the consequences. The governor was impressed and said that Wang Yishan had lived up to the saying of not shirking danger. He was a true righteous man, and punishing him would be excessive and discourage the people. So, he was released. The governor was actually Xu Wenzheng's son.

    536

    误堕龙窟
    　　徐彦璋云商人某海舶失风飘至山岛匍匐登岸深夜昏黑偶坠入一穴其穴险峻不可攀縁比明穴中微有光见大蛇无数蟠结在内始甚惧乆稍与之狎蛇亦无吞噬意所苦饥渴不可当但见蛇时时防石壁间小石絶不饮防于是商人亦漫尔取小石噙之顿忘饥渴一日闻雷声蛇始伸展相继腾升才知其为神龙遂挽蛇尾得出附舟还家携所噙小石数十至京城示识者皆鸦鹘等寳石也乃信神龙之窟多异珍焉自此货之致富彦璋亲见商人道其始末如此

    Explanation

    536

    A Merchant Mistakenly Falls into a Dragon's Lair

    According to Xu Yanzhang, a merchant on a ship lost to a storm was blown to a mountain island. Crawling ashore in the deep night, he accidentally fell into a cave. The cave was perilously steep and impossible to climb. By dawn, there was a faint light inside, and he saw countless serpents coiled within. At first, he was very fearful, but after a while, he gradually became familiar with them, and the serpents showed no intention of devouring him. The merchant was greatly suffering from hunger and thirst, but he only saw the serpents occasionally pecking at the stone walls, never at the small stones. So, he casually took a small stone and put it in his mouth, and immediately forgot about hunger and thirst. One day, he heard thunder rumbling, and the serpents began to stretch and rise one after another. Only then did he realize they were divine dragons. He pulled the serpent's tail and managed to get out, returning home by boat. Carrying dozens of the small stones he had put in his mouth, he went to the capital city and showed them to knowledgeable people, who identified them as yagu and other precious stones. Thus, he believed that the dragon's lair contained many rare treasures. From then on, he sold them and became wealthy. Xu Yanzhang personally saw the merchant and heard the entire story.

    537

    鸡司晨有凖
    　　尝至松江钟山浄行庵见笼一雄鸡置于殿之东檐请问其故寺僧云蓄此以司晨盖十有余年矣时刻不爽余窃记张公文潜明道杂志云鸡能司晨见于经传以为至论而未必然也或天寒鸡至将旦而未鸣或夜月出时隣鸡悉鸣大抵有情之物自不能有常而或变也若然则张公之言非欤因举似以询其所以僧云司晨之鸡必以童若坏其天真岂能有常哉盖张公特未知此理故耳
    　　黄道婆
    　　闽广多种木棉纺绩为布名曰吉贝松江府东去五十里许曰乌泥泾其地土田硗瘠民食不给因谋艺以资生业遂覔种于彼初无踏车椎之制率用手剖去子线竹弧置按间振掉成剂厥功甚艰国初时有一妪名黄道婆者自崖州来乃教以做造捍弹纺织之具至于错纱配色综线挈花各有其法以故织成被褥带帨其上折枝团凤棋局字様粲然若写人既受教竞相作为转货他郡家既就殷未几妪卒莫不感恩洒泣而共葬之又为立祠歳时享之越三十年祠毁乡人赵愚轩重立今祠复毁无人为之创建道婆之名日渐泯灭无闻矣

    Explanation

    537

    The Rooster Crows at the Right Time

    Once, I visited the Zhongshan Jingxing Temple in Songjiang and saw a caged rooster placed on the east eaves of the hall. I asked the temple monk why it was there, and he replied that it was kept to crow at the right time, having done so for more than ten years without fail. I recalled that Zhang Gong Wenqian, in his Mingdao Magazine, wrote that roosters can crow at the right time, as mentioned in the classics and historical records, and considered it an authoritative statement. However, he might not have been entirely correct. Sometimes, when it is cold, the rooster might be about to crow just before dawn but does not; sometimes, when the moon rises at night, all the neighboring roosters start crowing. Generally, living beings cannot always be constant and may change. If this is the case, was Zhang Gong's statement incorrect? I mentioned this to the monk, who said that a rooster used for crowing must be young and unspoiled, or else it cannot maintain its regularity. Zhang Gong simply did not know this principle.

    Huang Dao Po

    Fujian and Guangdong provinces widely cultivate cotton, which is spun and woven into cloth known as Jibei. In the Songjiang Prefecture, about fifty miles east, there is a place called Wuniutong, where the soil is poor, and the people's food supply is insufficient. They sought to cultivate cotton to support their livelihood and thus obtained seeds from there. Initially, there were no treadle looms or carding tools, so they used their hands to remove the seeds, thread the fibers, and weave them into fabric using bamboo arcs and rollers. The process was very laborious. At the beginning of the nation, an elderly woman named Huang Dao Po came from Yazhou. She taught the locals how to make tools for carding, spinning, and weaving. She also introduced techniques for blending threads, matching colors, and creating patterns in weaving. As a result, they wove beautiful bed covers, sashes, and other items adorned with motifs such as branches, phoenixes, chessboards, and characters, which were clearly and vividly as if written by a person. After learning her methods, everyone competed to produce and sell these goods in other counties, and their families prospered. Soon after, the elderly woman passed away, and everyone was grateful, weeping and burying her together. They also built a temple to worship her during festivals. Thirty years later, the temple was destroyed, and a local man named Zhao Yuxuan rebuilt it. Now, the temple has been destroyed again, and no one is building a new one. As a result, Huang Dao Po's name gradually fades and becomes unknown.

    538

    天陨鱼
    　　至正丙午八月辛酉上海县浦东俞店桥南牧羊儿三四闻头上恰恰有声仰视之流光中陨一鱼剌麻佳上成二创其状不常见自首至尾根仅盈尺似濶覇而短是日晴无隂云亦无雕鹳之是可怪也日昳时县市人閧然指流星自南投北即此时也桥下一细家取欲烹食其妻盐而藏之来者多就观焉或者曰志有云天陨鱼人民失所之象
    　　十二生子
    　　至元丁丑民间谣言拘刷童男女以故防嫁不问长防而乱伦者多矣平江苏达卿时为上海吏有女年十二赘里人浦仲明之子为壻明年生一子
    　　刘节妇
    　　刘节妇泰州坂埨人至正丙申春随父渡江居吴门适张士诚部将曹某方数月夫阵亡刘不避凶险躬至死所求得其尸归葬欲以身殉父不许既而权贵人闻刘美且贤争欲强委禽焉刘誓死不贰遂削髪为比丘尼夫刘本一闾阎女子其操行乃尔盖有贵为后妃而莫之及者谓非天性也欤

    Explanation

    538

    A Fish Falling from the Sky

    In the year 1346, on the 27th day of the eighth month, a few shepherds near the Yu Dian Bridge in Pudong, Shanghai County, heard a distinct sound above their heads. Looking up, they saw a fish fall from the sky in a beam of light, striking a man named Lamashe, who was left with two wounds. The fish was rarely seen, measuring about a foot from head to tail root, broad but short. On that day, the sky was clear with no clouds, and there were no birds like eagles or cranes in sight, which was quite unusual. By mid-afternoon, the people of the county market were in a commotion, pointing to a meteor falling from south to north, which happened at that very moment. A small family took the fish to cook and eat it, and the wife salted and stored it. Many people came to see it. Some said that historical records mention that a fish falling from the sky is a sign of people losing their place or stability.

    A Twelve-Year-Old Bride

    In the year 1297, rumors spread among the people about the conscription of children. As a result, marriages were arranged without regard for age, leading to many cases of incest. Ping Jiang Su Daqing, who was an official in Shanghai, had a daughter who was twelve years old. She married the son of a local man named Pu Zhongming the following year and gave birth to a son.

    Liu the Chaste Widow

    Liu the Chaste Widow was from Ban Yan, Taizhou. In the spring of 1346, she followed her father across the river to live in Wu Gate. She married a subordinate of Zhang Shicheng named Cao. Her husband died in battle after a few months. Liu did not avoid the danger and went personally to the place of death to retrieve his body and bury it. She wanted to sacrifice herself to follow her husband, but her father did not allow it. Later, powerful and wealthy people, hearing of Liu's beauty and virtue, competed to propose marriage to her. Liu swore to die rather than be unfaithful and then shaved her head and became a nun. Liu was a commoner woman, but her conduct was like this. Even if she were a queen or concubine, she could not surpass her. Is it not a matter of innate nature?
