    542

    金
    　　成无已　　何公务　　刘守真　　侯徳和张子和　　马守素　　杨从政　　李道源张元素【防古老人】景安

    　　辍耕録卷二十四
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷二十五　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰论秦蜀
    　　秦皇坑儒武侯相汉未有置异议于其间者偶读宋萧森希通録及俞文豹吹劒録而得其说可采森曰李斯曰非博士官所职天下敢有藏诗书百家语者皆诣守尉杂烧之则是天下之书虽焚而博士官犹有存者惜乎入关收图籍而不及此竟为楚人一炬耳前辈尝论之但坑儒一事未有究极之者仆按史书所坑特侯生卢生四百六十余人非能尽坑天下儒者为其所坑又非儒者何以知之始皇三十二年使卢生求羡门刻碣石门壊城郭决通隄防又卢生入海还因奏録图书曰亡秦者胡也始皇乃遣恬发兵三十万人北伐匈奴起临洮筑辽水又卢生说始皇曰日方中人主时为微行以辟恶恶辟

    Explanation

    542

    Final Translation

    Jin
    　　Cheng Wuyi　　He Gongwu　　Liu Shouzhen　　Hou Dehe Zhang Ziheng　　Ma Shousu　　Yang Congzheng　　Li Daoyuan Zhang Yuansu [Fanggu Laoren] Jing'an

    　　Zhuo Geng Lu Volume Twenty-Four
    　　Qinding Siku Quanshu
    　　Zhuo Geng Lu Volume Twenty-Five　　　　Yuan Dynasty　Tao Zongyi　Author Discusses Qin and Shu
    　　Emperor Qin burned the scholars, and Zhuge Liang served as chancellor of Han. There has been no disagreement about this. I recently read Song Dynasty's Xiao Sen's Xitong Lu and Yu Wenbiao's Chuijian Lu, and found their explanations worth noting. Xiao Sen said: Li Si said that the duty of the doctoral officials was not to collect and preserve books, and anyone in the world who dared to hide the Shijing (Book of Poetry), Shujing (Book of Documents), or writings from the hundred schools of thought should be brought to the local magistrate and the books burned. Thus, although the books in the world were burned, the doctoral officials still had some books preserved. It is regrettable that when entering the pass and collecting the books, they did not include these, and they were ultimately burned by the Chu people. My predecessors have often discussed this, but the matter of burning the scholars has not been fully explored. I have examined the historical records and found that only 460 people, such as Hou Sheng and Lu Sheng, were actually burned. It was not possible to burn all the scholars in the world, and those who were burned were not necessarily scholars. How do we know this? In the 32nd year of Emperor Qin's reign, he sent Lu Sheng to seek the Xianmen (Immortal Gate), carving the Jieshi Stone Gate, destroying city walls, and breaking through the embankments. Later, Lu Sheng returned from the sea and submitted a record of the books, saying, "The fall of Qin will come from the Hu people." Emperor Qin then sent Meng Tian to lead 300,000 troops north to attack the Xiongnu, building the Great Wall from Lintao to Liaodong. Lu Sheng also advised Emperor Qin, saying, "When the sun is at its zenith, the emperor should occasionally travel in secret to avoid evil spirits." This was to ward off evil spirits.

    543

    真人至愿上所居宫毋令人知然后不死之药始可得也其后建阿房宫千间万落必自此言发之观其二事皆卢生稔其恶又纵臾之特方伎之流耳岂所谓儒者哉始皇因封禅之议谤口纷纷已懐杀意及其一怒而坑之或者天理之不容方其求药海上也则挟童男童女以行皆取于民间夺其无告之孤肆厥不轨之状如今所谓妖教窃其中死无辜者多矣此一罪也因亡胡之防兴北伐之师筑长城断地脉南北生灵因是役而死者不可胜算骸积如山血流成川调发频仍剥及闾左原始要终谁生厉阶此二罪也献辟之术觊真人之来咸阳宫观二百七十道相连有言其所幸之处者罪死梁山之上其语一

    Explanation

    543

    The ultimate wish of a true person is that their residence should not be known, only then can the elixir of immortality be obtained. After this, the construction of the A-Fang Palace, which was vast and extensive, must have been initiated by these words. Considering these two events, it is clear that Lu Sheng harbored evil and encouraged it. He is merely a practitioner of various arts and techniques, not a true Confucian scholar. The emperor, due to the controversy over the Fengshan ritual, harbored numerous slanderous words, and his murderous intent became evident. When he executed the scholars, it was perhaps not tolerated by heavenly justice. When he sought the elixir of immortality at sea, he took young boys and girls from the people, taking orphans with no one to protect them, acting in an unrestrained and lawless manner. This is similar to what is now called a false teaching, and many innocent people have died as a result. When he launched a northern campaign, he built the Great Wall, severing the earth's veins and disrupting the natural flow of the land. The people living in the north and south, as a result of this labor, cannot be counted. Bones piled up like mountains, and blood flowed like rivers. Frequent mobilization and conscription stripped the people of the left side of the street. Tracing the origin and concluding the end, who created the cause of trouble? Offering exorcism techniques, hoping for the arrival of a true person, the palace in Xianyang has 270 paths connected. Anyone who speaks of the places he favors will be punished and die on Liang Mountain. The statement is consistent.

    544

    泄时在旁者尽杀之自是莫知行之所在此三罪也有一于此罪不容于死况兼有之以四百六十余人之坑偿万人之命良不为过天网恢恢防而不漏真可畏哉始皇曰卢生等吾尊赐之甚厚今乃诽谤我诸生在咸阳者吾使廉问或为妖言以乱黔首于是使御史按问诸生传相告引仆亦信卢生非吾儒中人况始皇自谓尊赐甚厚岂非如前三者方术图防之类有以中其欲故尊赐之初不闻其诵孔子之言以进古今相承皆曰坑儒盖惑于扶苏之谏扶苏曰诸子皆诵法孔子皇上皆重法绳之臣恐天下不安呜呼至若卢生者何尝诵法孔子自扶苏言之误使儒者不韪之名自我一洗亦万世之快也不然如两

    Explanation

    544

    All those present at the time were killed; from then on, no one knew where he was. These are the three crimes. If there is one of these, the crime cannot be forgiven by death, especially when combined. To compensate for the lives of ten thousand people with the burial of four hundred and sixty plus individuals is not excessive. The net of heaven is vast and prevents nothing from escaping, truly to be feared. Qin Shi Huang said, "Lu Sheng and others were greatly honored by me, but now they slander me. The scholars in Xianyang, I have instructed the officials to investigate them, some speak of strange words to confuse the common people. Thus I have ordered the Censor to question the scholars, they pass on information and incriminate each other. I also believe that Lu Sheng is not among our Confucian scholars. Qin Shi Huang, who claimed to have greatly honored them, was not it like the previous three, the methods of Daoist alchemy and divination, which satisfied his desires, hence the initial honor. I did not hear them recite the words of Confucius to advance. Throughout history, it has been said that the scholars were buried alive, which was misled by Fu Su's advice. Fu Su said, 'All the scholars recite the teachings of Confucius, the emperor values the law and restrains them. I fear the world will not be at peace.' Alas, as for Lu Sheng, he never recited the teachings of Confucius. From the perspective of Fu Su, his words mistakenly led to the scholars being blamed. To cleanse this from myself is also a great joy for all time. Otherwise, like two...

    545

    生四皓伏生之流鸿飞冥冥弋人何慕肯揺唇鼓吻自投于防穽哉仆故曰卢生四百六十余人皆方伎之士也天下之大所谓儒者固不止此其坑之者此而已矣有道之士秦不能坑火德一炎两生以讲礼闻四皓以羽翼之功闻伏生以口授古书闻岂非天夀其脉留此数公以见吾儒不可磨灭而朋奸党恶小人终不能为长乆计商君以变法祸秦竟遭车裂卢生以方伎祸秦坑于咸阳其罪等也天其或者假手于秦欤商君裂矣卢生坑矣而秦以不祀抑亦自相挤防之明报而祸淫之道为不偏矣仆恶夫坑儒之名故论其颠末如此文豹曰古今论孔明者莫不以忠义许之然余兄文龙尝考其颠末以为孔明之才

    Explanation

    545

    Here is the final translation of the source text:

    Those who were born as the Four Sages and Fu Sheng, like the great birds flying high into the vast sky, what does a hunter desire to pursue? Would they speak or argue at length and throw themselves into a trap? I therefore say that over 400 individuals associated with Lu Sheng were all skilled in various arts and techniques. The vastness of the world means that so-called scholars are certainly not limited to this; those who were buried alive are only these. Scholars of virtue could not be buried alive, for the fire element of the Qin dynasty was one of destruction. The two scholars were known for their expertise in ritual, the Four Sages for their support of the royal family, and Fu Sheng for orally transmitting ancient texts. Is it not heaven that preserved their lineage, leaving these few scholars to demonstrate that our Confucianism is indestructible? Those who ally with villains and evil people cannot have a long-term plan. Shang Yang brought disaster to the Qin through his legal reforms and was eventually subjected to the punishment of being torn apart. Lu Sheng brought disaster to the Qin through his skills and was buried alive in Xianyang. Their sins are equal. Perhaps heaven used the Qin as a tool. Shang Yang was torn apart, and Lu Sheng was buried alive, and the Qin dynasty ceased to exist. Or perhaps it was a clear retribution for their mutual destruction and the path of disaster for those who indulge in vice. I dislike the name "burying scholars alive," therefore, I discuss the details as such. Wen Bao says: throughout history, those who discuss Zhuge Liang have always praised him for his loyalty and righteousness. However, my brother Wen Long once examined the details and believed that Zhuge Liang's talent...

    546

    谓之识时务则可谓之明大义则未也谓之忠于刘备则可谓之忠于汉室则未也其说有四一者备虽称为中山靖王之后然其服属疏逺世数难攷温公谓犹宋高祖自称楚元王后故通鉴不敢以绍汉统况备又非人望之所归周瑜以枭雄目之刘巴以谁人视之司马懿以诈力鄙之孙权以猾虏呼之亮独何见而委身焉借使以为刘氏族属然献帝在上犹当如光武之事更始东征西伐一切听命焉可也二者备之枉驾草庐也始谋不过曰主上尘孤不度德量力欲伸大义于天下其辞甚正其志甚伟自亮开之以跨荆益成覇业之利而备之志向始移无复以献帝为念由建安举兵以来二十四年天子或都许

    Explanation

    546

    Those who claim to understand the ways of the world cannot be said to understand great principles. Those who claim to be loyal to Liu Bei cannot be said to be loyal to the Han dynasty. There are four reasons for this:

    Firstly, although Liu Bei claims to be a descendant of King Jing of Zhongshan, his lineage is distant and obscure, making it difficult to verify. Sima Guang compared him to Emperor Song Gaozu, who claimed to be a descendant of King Yuan of Chu, and thus the Zizhi Tongjian does not regard him as a legitimate successor to the Han dynasty. Moreover, Liu Bei was not the one that the people looked up to. Zhou Yu regarded him as a fierce and ambitious man, Liu Ba saw him as an insignificant person, Sima Yi scorned him for his deceit and force, and Sun Quan called him a cunning barbarian. What insight did Zhuge Liang have that led him to dedicate himself to Liu Bei? Even if we assume that Liu Bei was a member of the Liu clan, Emperor Xian was still on the throne, and one should still act like Emperor Guangwu did with Emperor Gengshi, campaigning in the east and west, and following orders completely.

    Secondly, Liu Bei's visit to Zhuge Liang's thatched cottage. His initial plan was nothing more than saying that the emperor was in distress, and without considering his virtue and strength, he wanted to uphold great righteousness in the world. His words were very righteous, and his aspirations were very grand. It was from Zhuge Liang's opening that he gained the benefits of controlling Jing and Yi provinces and establishing hegemony. However, Liu Bei's ambitions began to shift, and he no longer thought of Emperor Xian. Since the Jian'an uprising, twenty-four years have passed, and the emperor has sometimes resided in Xu.

    547

    或居长安或幸洛阳宫室煨烬越在篱间备未尝使一介行李诣行在所今年合众万余明年合众三万未尝一言禀命朝廷而亮亦未尝一谈及焉盖其帝蜀之心已定于草庐一见之时矣三者曹操欲顺流东下求救于吴无一言及献帝而独说以鼎足之说夫鼎足之说始于蒯通然通之说韩信以此犹有汉之一足当三国时而为是说则献帝无复染指之望矣頼周瑜汉贼之骂足以激怒孙权故能成赤壁之胜若亮若备何以厉将士之气服曹操之心哉荆楚之士从之如云非从备也乃从汉也四者备之称王汉中则建安二十四年也献帝在上而敢于自王及称帝武担则闻献帝之遇害也亮不能如董公说高

    Explanation

    547

    Sometimes residing in Chang'an or being favored in Luoyang, the imperial palaces were reduced to ashes, now only a few walls standing. The lord never sent even a single messenger to the emperor's temporary residence. This year, he gathered over ten thousand followers; next year, he gathered thirty thousand. He never once sought permission from the court, and Zhuge Liang never once mentioned it. His heart to establish a kingdom in Shu was already set at the first meeting in the thatched cottage. The third point is that Cao Cao, wanting to advance eastward down the river and seek help from Wu, did not mention Emperor Xian but instead spoke of the strategy of a tripod. The strategy of a tripod began with Kuai Tong. However, when Kuai Tong advised Han Xin, it still left one leg of the tripod for Han. But during the Three Kingdoms period, if such a strategy were used, Emperor Xian would have no chance of reclaiming power. Thanks to Zhou Yu's scolding of Cao Cao as a traitor, which sufficiently enraged Sun Quan, thus enabling the victory at the Battle of Red Cliffs. If Zhuge Liang and Liu Bei had done so, how could they have inspired the soldiers' morale and convinced Cao Cao? The people of Jing and Chu followed him like clouds, not following Liu Bei but following Han. The fourth point is that Liu Bei declared himself king of Hanzhong in the twenty-fourth year of Jian'an, despite Emperor Xian being still on the throne, and later declared himself emperor upon hearing of Emperor Xian's death. Zhuge Liang could not be like Dong Gong in persuading Gao.

    548

    祖率三军为义帝缟素仗大义连孙吴声罪讨贼乃遽乘此即帝位而反锋攻吴晋文公有言父死之谓何又因以为利故费诗以为大敌未克便先自立恐人心疑惑而谏以高祖不敢王秦之事亮反怒而黜之夫以操之奸雄其王其公犹必待天子之命荀彧且以此愤死以丕之簒逆亦必待献帝之禅杨彪且不肯臣之备虽称宗室而亦臣也何所禀命而自王自帝固方哓哓以兴复汉室为辞不知兴复汉室为献帝耶为刘备耶亮即有心于帝备矣万一果能兴复将置献帝于何地出师一表虽忠诚恳恳特忠于所事耳其于大义实有所未明也管仲乐毅之事君子所羞道者以其但知有燕齐而不知有王室也亮乃

    Explanation

    548

    Liu Bei led the three armies in mourning attire for the Righteous Emperor, upholding great righteousness by allying with Sun and Wu to declare crimes and punish the traitors. Yet, he hastily seized the imperial throne and then turned the army against Wu. Duke Wen of Jin once said, "What does it mean when a father dies? How can it be used for personal gain?" Thus, Fei Shi believed that before defeating the major enemy, one should not hastily proclaim oneself king, fearing that it would confuse the people, and he advised against the precedent set by Liu Bang. Zhuge Liang became angry and demoted Fei Shi. Even a cunning and powerful figure like Cao Cao would still need the emperor's command to become a king or a duke. Xun You died of grief over this matter. Cao Pi's usurpation would also require the abdication of Emperor Xian, and Yang Biao refused to be a subject of him. Liu Bei, though claiming to be of the imperial lineage, was still a subject, and thus had no authority to proclaim himself king or emperor. He insisted on the pretext of restoring the Han dynasty, but it is unclear whether restoring the Han dynasty refers to Emperor Xian or Liu Bei. If Liu Bei truly restores the Han dynasty, what will happen to Emperor Xian? The "Expedition Manifesto" is filled with sincere and earnest words, but it only shows loyalty to one's cause and lacks clarity on great righteousness. The actions of Guan Zhong and Yue Yi are considered shameful by gentlemen because they only knew about Yan and Qi but not about the royal house. Zhuge Liang did so.

    549

    以管乐自许宜其志虑之所图囘功业之所成就止于区区一蜀耳或者但为备刘氏宗也备帝蜀则汉祚存矣亮忠于备即忠于汉矣吁无献帝则可有献帝在而君臣自相推戴则赤眉之立盆子亦有辞于世矣春秋之末诸侯争强周室微弱孔子无一日不以尊王为心若如亮之见则鲁同姓也亦可奉之为王矣天下后世惟持此见故于亮之事无敢置异议于其间文中子曰通也敢忘大皇昭烈之懿识孔明公瑾之盛心噫汉之君既称献帝魏之君又称武帝吴之君又称大皇帝蜀之君又称昭烈皇帝天无二日民无二王一天下而四帝并立可乎通之见如此宜其为续书之僣也余兄尝以是说取解于同文馆

    Explanation

    549

    Considering oneself as capable as Guan Zhong and Yue Yi, it is appropriate for the thoughts and plans one devises, and the accomplishments and achievements one attains, to be limited to a small place like Shu. Perhaps it was just to serve as a guardian of the Liu family. If Liu Bei were to rule Shu, the Han dynasty would continue. Zhuge Liang's loyalty to Liu Bei is loyalty to the Han dynasty. Alas, if there were no Emperor Xian, then it would be acceptable. But if Emperor Xian were still alive and the rulers and ministers crowned each other, then the Red Eyebrows' establishment of Liu Pengze (Liu Yan) also had a justification in the world. At the end of the Spring and Autumn period, the feudal lords competed for power while the Zhou royal house grew weak. Confucius never ceased to have the heart of honoring the king. If it were as Zhuge Liang saw it, even if the state of Lu were a branch of the royal family, it could also be made a king. The world and future generations only hold this view, and there was no one who dared to raise objections to Zhuge Liang's actions. The Middle Sage (Wang Tong) said, "How can I forget the noble virtues of the great emperor Liu Bei and the great minds of Zhuge Liang and Zhou Yu?" The Han emperor was called Emperor Xian, the Wei emperor was called Emperor Wu, the Wu emperor was called Great Emperor, and the Shu emperor was called Emperor Liu Bei. There cannot be two suns in the sky or two kings for the people. Can the world be unified while four emperors coexist? Given Wang Tong's view, it is appropriate that he is considered a heretic in the continuation of the classics. My brother once used this argument to explain it at the Tongwen Guan.

    550

    院本名目
    　　唐有传竒宋有戯曲唱诨词说金有院本杂剧诸公调院本杂剧其实一也国朝院本杂剧始厘而二之院本则五人一曰副净古谓之参军一曰副末古谓之苍鹘鹘能击禽鸟末可打副净故云一曰引戯一曰未泥一曰孤装又谓之五花防弄或曰宋徽宗见防国人来朝衣装鞵履巾裹傅粉墨举动如此使优人效之以为戯又有焰叚亦院本之意但差简耳取其如火焰易明而易灭也其间副净有散说有道念有筋斗有科泛教坊色长魏武刘三人鼎新编辑魏长于念诵武长于筋斗刘长于科泛至今乐人皆宗之偶得院本名目载于此以资博识者之一览

    Explanation

    550

    Translation:

    Names of Yuanben (Court Theater)

    In the Tang dynasty, there were Chuanqi (legendary plays); in the Song dynasty, Xiqu (operas), Changhun Cisuo (singing, humor, and spoken dialogue); in the Jin dynasty, Yuanben (court theater) and Zaju (miscellaneous drama). Yuanben and Zaju are essentially one and the same. In our dynasty, Yuanben and Zaju were first distinguished into two separate forms. Yuanben consists of five roles: the first is Fujing, an ancient term for Canjun (a clown or comic character); the second is Fumò, an ancient term for Canggu (a type of bird, metaphorically used to describe the role of Fumò). The Canggu can strike birds, and Fumò can strike Fujing, hence the name. The third is Yinxi (the leader or director); the fourth is Weinì (an assistant role); and the fifth is Guzhuang (costumes or appearance), also known as Wuhua Fangnong (five-flower defense play). Some say that during the reign of Emperor Huizong of Song, he saw foreign envoys who came to pay homage, dressed in shoes, hats, and makeup, moving in a certain way. He ordered court jesters to imitate them for entertainment. There is also Yan Duan (a simplified version of Yuanben), which is similar in meaning but simpler, as it is like a flame—easy to light but also easy to extinguish. Among the Fujing roles, there are those who perform Sanshuo (spoken dialogue), Daonian (chanting), Jindou (acrobatic movements), and Kefan (rules or techniques). The head of the imperial music and dance troupe, along with Hui, Wu, and Liu, edited and innovated. Hui excelled in chanting, Wu in acrobatic movements, and Liu in rules and techniques. To this day, performers all follow their methods. By chance, I obtained the names of Yuanben and recorded them here to provide a reference for those with broad knowledge.

    551

    和曲院本
    　　月明法曲　郓王法曲　烧香法曲　送香法曲上坟伊州　浇花新水　熈州骆驼　列良嬴府

    　　四酸逍遥乐　　　　　贺贴万年欢
    　　廪降黄龙　　　　　列女降黄龙
    　　上皇院本
    　　壶春堂　　太湖石　　金明池　　恋鳌山六变妆　　万岁山　　打草阵　　赏花灯错入内　　问相思　　探花街　　断上皇打毬防　　春从天上来

    Explanation

    551

    Translation:

    The Harmonious Court Collection

    • Moonlit Ritual Music

    • Duke of Yüan's Ritual Music

    • Incense Offering Ritual Music

    • Incense Offering Ritual Music for Tomb Sweeping in Yizhou

    • Watering Flowers with Fresh Water

    • Camels of Xizhou

    • The Court of Liezi

    • Sick Zheng's Leisurely Music

    • The Four Worthies' Leisurely Music

    • The Four Tastes' Leisurely Music

    • The Descent of the Yellow Dragon

    • The Descent of the Yellow Dragon by the Virtuous Women

    The Imperial Court Collection

    • Taihu Stones
    • Jinming Pool
    • Loving Ao Mountain
    • Six Transformations of Makeup

    • Viewing Lanterns

    • Asking for Longing
    • Exploring the Flower Street

    552

    题目院本
    　　栁絮风　　红索冷　　墙外道　　共粉泪杨栁枝　　蔡消闲　　方偷眼　　呆太守画堂前　　梦周公　　梅花底　　三笑图窄布衫　　呆秀才　　隔年期　　贺方囘王安石　　断三行　　竞寻芳　打梨花院
    　　覇王院本
    　　悲怨覇王　范增覇王　草马覇王　散楚覇王三官覇王　补塑覇王
    　　诸杂大小院本
    　　乔记孤　　旦判孤　　计算孤　　判孤百戯孤　　哨孤　　烧枣孤　　孝经孤菜园孤　　货郎孤　　合房酸　　麻皮酸花酒酸　　狗皮酸　　还魂酸　　别离酸王纒酸　　谒食酸　　三揲酸　　哭贫酸揷拨酸　　酸孤旦　　毛诗旦　　老孤遣旦纒三旦　　禾哨旦　　哮卖旦　　贫富旦书柜儿　　纸襴儿　　蔡奴儿　　刴毛儿喜牌儿　　卦册儿　　绣箧儿　　粥碗儿似娘儿　　卦铺儿　　师婆儿　　教学儿鸡鸭儿　　黄丸儿　　棱角儿　　田牛儿小丸儿　　丑奴儿　　病襄王

    Explanation

    552

    Final Translation

    Title Plays

    • Willow Down Wind - The light, fluffy seeds of the willow tree, often used metaphorically to describe something delicate or fleeting.
    • Red Cord Cold - A poetic term referring to something that is both beautiful and cold or harsh.
    • Outside the Wall Road - A path or route outside a walled area, or metaphorically to something external or peripheral.
    • Sharing Tears with Willow Branch - A poetic phrase that could mean "sharing the tears of the willow branch with powdered makeup," possibly referring to a scene of sorrow or beauty.
    • Pleasant Leisure - A reference to a person or character named Cai who enjoys leisure.
    • Just Peeking - A moment of observation or curiosity.
    • Dazed Prefect Before the Painted Hall - A scene involving a prefect and a painted hall.
    • Dreaming of Duke Zhou - A reference to dreaming of the Duke of Zhou, a symbol of wisdom and virtue in Chinese culture.
    • Under the Plum Blossoms - A scene under a plum tree.
    • Three Smiles and Narrow Cloth Robe - A reference to a painting or scene involving three smiles and a narrow cloth robe.
    • Dazed Scholar - A scholar who is absent-minded or dreamy.
    • Year-Long Separation - A year-long separation or waiting period.
    • He Fanghui and Wang Anshi - References to two historical figures, He Fanghui and Wang Anshi, both of whom were prominent figures in Chinese history.
    • Cutting Three Lines - A poetic form or a specific action.
    • Competing to Seek Fragrance - A scene of competition or pursuit of beauty.
    • Plays of the Overlord
      • Sorrowful Overlord - A tragic story or character.
      • Fan Zeng and the Overlord - A reference to Fan Zeng, a historical figure associated with the Overlord.
      • Grass Horse of the Overlord - A metaphorical or symbolic figure.
      • Scattered Overlord of Chu - A fragmented or dispersed figure.
      • Three Officials Overlord - A group of officials or a specific role.
      • Patched-Up Sculpted Overlord - A repaired or restored figure.
    • Various Large and Small Plays
      • Tall and Thin Orphan - A character in a play.
      • Female Judge of the Orphan - A female character who judges or evaluates the orphan.
      • Calculating the Orphan - A scene involving calculation or evaluation of the orphan.
      • Hundred Plays Orphan - A collection of plays involving the orphan.
      • Flute-Playing Orphan - A character who plays the flute.
      • Burning Jujube Orphan - A scene involving burning jujubes.
      • Orphan of the Book of Filial Piety - A character associated with filial piety.
      • Orphan of the Vegetable Garden - A character associated with a vegetable garden.
      • Orphan of the Peddler - A character associated with a peddler.
      • Sour One Who Combines Rooms - A character who combines or unites rooms.
      • Sour One with Rough Skin - A character with rough skin.
      • Sour One of Flowers and Wine - A character associated with flowers and wine.
      • Sour One with Dog Skin - A character with dog skin.
      • Sour One Who Returns to Life - A character who returns to life.
      • Sour One of Parting - A character associated with parting.
      • Sour One of Wang's Entanglement - A character associated with Wang's entanglement.
      • Sour One of Visiting and Eating - A character associated with visiting and eating.
      • Sour One of Three Shuffling - A character associated with three shuffling.
      • Sour One Who Cries for Poverty - A character who cries for poverty.
      • Sour One of Inserting and Plucking - A character associated with inserting and plucking.
      • Sour Orphan Female - A female character who is sour.
      • Female of the Book of Songs - A female character associated with the Book of Songs.
      • Old Orphan Sent Female - An old female character who is sent.
      • Entangled Three Females - Three female characters who are entangled.
      • Female Who Plays the Reed Pipe - A female character who plays the reed pipe.
      • Female Who Barks and Sells - A female character who barks and sells.
      • Female of Poverty and Wealth - A female character associated
    553

    马明王閙学堂　　閙浴堂　　寛布衫　　泥布衫赶汤瓶　　纸汤瓶　　閙旗亭　　芙蓉亭壊食店　　閙酒店　　壊粥店　　荘周梦花酒梦　　蝴蝶梦　　三出舍　　三入舍瑶池防　　八仙防　　蟠桃防　　洗儿防藏防　　打五脏　　兰昌宫　　广寒宫閙结亲　　倦成亲　　强风情　　大论情三园子　　红娘子　　太平还乡　衣锦还乡四论艺　　殿前四艺　竞敲门　　都子撞门呆大郎　　四酸擂　　问前程　　十様锦长庆馆　　癞将军　　两相同　　竞花枝五变妆　　洪福无疆　白

    Explanation

    553

    A Noisy Schoolhouse, A Noisy Bathhouse, A Loose Cotton Shirt, A Cotton Shirt Chasing a Teapot, A Paper Teapot, A Noisy Flag Pavilion, The Hibiscus Pavilion, A Ruined Food Shop, A Noisy Hotel, A Ruined Porridge Shop, Zhuang Zhou's Dream of Wine and Flowers, The Butterfly Dream, Three Times Leaving a House, Three Times Entering a House, The Defense of the Jade Pool, The Defense of the Eight Immortals, The Defense of the Peach of Immortality, The Defense of a Child's Bath, The Defense of Hidden Treasures, Playing with the Five Organs, The Lan Chang Palace, The Palace of the Cold and Far, A Noisy Wedding, A Weary Wedding, Forced Romance, A Grand Discussion of Love, Three Gardens, The Red-Named Woman, A Peaceful Return to One's Hometown, Returning Home in Glory, A Discussion of Four Arts, Four Arts Presented Before a Palace, Competing to Knock on a Door, A Group of People Crashing into a Door, A Slow or Dull Person Named Da Lang, A Competition Involving Four Sour Elements, Asking About One's Future Prospects, Ten Different Types of Brocade, The Chang Qing Guesthouse, A General with Leprosy, Two Things Being the Same, Competing to Reach a Flower Branch, Five Changes of Makeup, Great Blessings Without Limit, White.

    554

    牡丹　　赤壁鏖兵穷相思　　金坛谒宿　调渐　　官吏不和閙巡铺　　判不由己　大勘刀　　同官不睦閙平康　　赶门不上　卖花容　　同官贺授无论　　四酸讳偌　閙防阑　　药盘街閙文林　　四国来朝　双捉壻　　酒色财气医作媒　　风流药院　监法童　　渔樵问话鬬鹌鹑　　杜甫游春　鸳鸯简　　四酸提猴满朝欢　　月夜闻筝　鼓角将　　閙芙蓉城鬬医　　张生煑海　賖馒头　　文房四寳谢神天　　陈桥兵变　揭榜　　蒙哑质库福神　　院公狗儿　告和来　　佛印

    Explanation

    554

    Translation:

    Peony
    The Battle of Red Cliffs, a tumultuous longing
    Visiting Jin坛 for the night, a gradual tune
    Officials in disarray, chaos in the patrol
    Judgment not by oneself, a major legal or military action
    Officials at odds, disorder in the city
    Chasing but not catching, selling flowers or something of beauty
    Officials congratulating each other on a promotion, no formal ceremony
    Four sour things, a taboo
    Disorder in defense, a lively scene in the market or literary circle
    Four countries coming to pay homage, two husbands caught
    Alcohol, beauty, wealth, and power as mediators
    A place of pleasure or indulgence, a young monitor of rules
    Fishermen and woodcutters conversing, fighting quails
    Du Fu enjoying a spring outing, a document related to love or partnership
    Four sour things, a monkey causing merriment in the court
    Listening to the zither under the moonlight, a military leader
    A chaotic or lively scene in a city or a competition between doctors
    Zhang Sheng boiling the sea, buying steamed buns on credit
    The four treasures of the study offered to heaven
    The Chenqiao mutiny, a list or announcement made public
    A deity or a place of fortune or luck, a servant or attendant named "Dog"
    Coming to report or inform, a Buddhist monk or a person with a seal or mark of a monk

    烧猪酸卖徕　　琴劒书箱　花前饮　　五听琴白云庵　　迓鼓二郎　壊道塲　　独脚五郎卖花声　　进奉伊州　错上坟　　医五方打五铺　　拷梅香　　四道姑　　隔听硬行蔡　　义养娘　　师姨　　论秋蝉刘盼盼　　墙头马上　刺董卓　　锯周朴四柏板　　大论谈　　撁龙舟　　击梧桐渰蓝桥　　入桃园　　防送　　海棠春香药车　　四方和　　九头顶　　閙元宵赶村禾　　眼药孤　　两同心　　更漏子隂阳孤　　提头巾　　三索债　　防送哨偌卖旦　　是耶酸　　怕水酸

    560

    拴搐艶叚
    　　襄阳防　　驴轴不了　　　　　　鞭敲金镫
    　　门儿　　天长地乆　　　　　　衙府则例金含楞　　天下太平　归塞北　　春夏秋冬鬬百草　　呌子盖头　大刘备　　石榴花诗哑汉书　　说古棒　　唱拄杖　　日月山河胡饼大　　觜揾地　　屋里藏　　骂吕布张天觉　　打论语　　十果顽　　十般乞还故里　　刘金带　　四草虫　　四厨子四妃艶　　望长安　　长安住　　骂江南风花雪月　错寄书　　睡起教柱　打婆束三文两扑　大对景　　小防乡　　少年游打青提　　千字文　　酒家诗　　三拖旦睡

    Explanation

    560

    A Fragment of Classical Chinese Text

    The defense of Xiangyang, an unfinished wheel, the sound of a whip striking a golden stirrup,
    A door child, heaven and earth enduring, government rules and regulations with golden elements, peace throughout the world, returning to the northern border, the competition among plants in the four seasons,
    Calling out to cover one's head, Liu Bei, poetry of pomegranate flowers and the mute's book, talking about ancient times, singing while holding a walking stick,
    The sun, moon, mountains, and rivers with large trampled flatbreads, a bird wiping the ground, keeping something hidden,
    Cursing Lu Bu and Zhang Tianjue, beating the Analects, ten stubborn fruits, ten ways to beg for a return to the homeland, Liu Jin, four grass insects,
    Four kitchen boys and four beautiful consorts, looking toward Chang'an, living in Chang'an, cursing the beauty of Jiangnan, mistakenly sending a letter,
    Rising from sleep and teaching a pillar, beating the old woman and three coins, a grand scene, a small village, young people playing with green melons,
    The Thousand Character Classic, wine shop poetry, three days of dawn sleep.

    561

    马杓　　四生厉　　乔唱诨　　桃李子麦屯儿　　大菜园　　乔打圣　　杏汤来谢天地　　十脚　　请生打纳　建成食　　　毬棒艶　　破巢艶　　开封艶鞍子艶　　打虎艶　　四王艶　　蝗虫艶撅子艶　　七捉艶　　修行艶　　般调艶儿艶　　蛮子艶　　快乐艶　　慈乌艶眼里乔　　访戴　　　众半　　　陈蔡范蠡　　　扯休书　　鞭寨　　　锨扒扫竹感吾智　　诸宫调　　金铃　　　雕出板来套靴　　　舌智　　　俯饭　　　钗发多襄阳府　　仙哥儿
