    　　大口赋　　六十八头　拂袖便去　绍运图十二月　　胡说话　　风魔赋　　疗丁赋撁着骆驼　看马胡孙
    　　列良家门
    　　说卦　　由命赋　　混星图　　栁簸箕二十八宿　春从天上来

    Explanation

    562

    Red Sky, Musician's Melody, Chess Game, Dice Game, Secret Vessel, Holding a Turtle Shell

    Official Titles, Stubborn Speech, Waiting Room, Official on Duty, Bird Name, Blue, Crow, Animal Feed, Eagles, Hawks, Falcons, Flower Names, Stonecrop, Dog, Water, Food Names, Kitchen Difficulties, Herb, Buddha Names, Board for Enlightenment, Deity, Hard-to-Read Characters, Donkey, Harmful Characters, Liu San, Board and Wine, Counting Wine, Numerical Symbols, Final Part of a Song, Meng Jiangnu, Covering, Beginning and End of a Poem, Riddle with Tiger Skin Robe, Du Dabo, Yellow, Temple Entrance, Bald and Ugly Person, Monk by the Window, Sitting in Meditation, Temple of Xuanzang, Ghost Entering, Monkey, Large Baked Good, True Teachings, Scholar's Entrance, Elaborate Poem, Numerical Symbols, Waving Sleeve and Leaving, Calendar of Twelve Months, Nonsense, Poem about Madness, Poem about Healing and Camels, Looking at a Monkey, Good Family Entrance, Book about the I Ching, Poem about Fate, Star Chart, Basket and Twenty-Eight Lunar Mansions, Spring from Heaven

    563

    禾下家门
    　　万民快乐　咬的响　　莫延　　　九斗一石共牛
    　　大夫家门
    　　三十六风　伤寒　　　合死汉　　马屁勃
    　　安排锹镢　三百六十骨节　　　　撒五谷便痈赋
    　　卒子家门
    　　针儿线　　田仗库　　军閙　　　阵败良头家门
    　　方头赋　　水龙吟
    　　老家门
    　　脚言脚语　则是便是贼
    　　都子家门
    　　后人收　　桃李子　　上一上
    　　孤下家门
    　　朕闻上古　刀包待制　绢儿来
    　　司吏家门
    　　罢笔赋　　是故榜
    　　仵作行家门
    　　一遍生活
    　　撅俫家门
    　　受胎成气
    　　诸杂砌
    　　模石江　　梅妃　　　浴佛　　　三教姜武　　　救驾　　　赵娥娥　　石妇吟变猫　　　水母　　　玉环　　　走鹦哥上料　　　瞎脚　　　易基　　　武则天告子　　　防蛇　　　鹿皮　　　新太公黄巢　　　恰来　　　蛇师　　　汲字碑卧草　　　衲袄　　　封碑　　　锯周村史笔　　悬头梁上

    Explanation

    563

    Translation:

    Héxià Jiamén
    　　Wànmín Kuaile, Yǎo de Xiǎng, Mò Yán, Jiǔdòu Yīshí Gòng Niú
    　　Dàifu Jiamén
    　　Sānshíliù Fēng, Shānghán, Hé Sǐhàn, Mǎpì Bó
    　　Ānpái Qiáojiù, Sānbǎiliùshí Gǔjié, Sā Wǔgǔ Biàn Yōng Fù
    　　Cùzi Jiamén
    　　Zhēn'ér Xiàn, Tián Zhàng Kù, Jūn Nào, Zhèn Bàiliángtóu Jiamén
    　　Fāngtóu Fù, Shuǐlóngyín
    　　Xīn Lǎojiāmén
    　　Jiǎo Yán Jiǎoyǔ, Zéshì Biànshì Zéi
    　　Dūzi Jiamén
    　　Hòurén Shōu, Táolǐ Zǐ, Shàng Yīshàng
    　　Gūxià Jiamén
    　　Zhèn Wén Shànggǔ, Dāo Bāo Dàizhì, Juànrén Lái
    　　Sīlì Jiamén
    　　Bàbǐ Fù, Shì Gù Bǎng
    　　Wǔzuò Xíng Jiamén
    　　Yībiàn Shēnghuó
    　　Juē Lái Jiamén
    　　Shòutāi Chéng Qì
    　　Zhūzá Qì
    　　Móshí Jiāng, Méifēi, Yùfó, Sāng Jiào Jiāngwǔ, Jiùjià, Zhào É'é, Shí Fù Yín Biàn Māo, Shuǐmǔ, Yùhuán, Zǒu Yīnggē Shàngliào, Xiā Jiǎo, Yì Jī, Wǔzétiān Gàizi, Fángshé, Lùpí, Xīntài Gōng Huángcháo, Qiàlái, Shéshī, Jízì Bēi Wòcǎo, Gǒn'ǎo, Fēngbēi, Jué Zhōucūn Shǐ Hóngbǐ, Xuántóu Liángshàng

    Final Translation:

    Héxià Jiamén
    　　Wànmín Kuaile, Yǎo de Xiǎng, Mò Yán, Jiǔdòu Yīshí Gòng Niú
    　　Dàifu Jiamén
    　　Sānshíliù Fēng, Shānghán, Hé Sǐhàn, Mǎpì Bó
    　　Ānpái Qiáojiù, Sānbǎiliùshí Gǔjié, Sā Wǔgǔ Biàn Yōng Fù
    　　Cùzi Jiamén
    　　Zhēn'ér Xiàn, Tián Zhàng Kù, Jūn Nào, Zhèn Bàiliángtóu Jiamén
    　　Fāngtóu Fù, Shuǐlóngyín
    　　Xīn Lǎojiāmén
    　　Jiǎo Yán Jiǎoyǔ, Zéshì Biànshì Zéi
    　　Dūzi Jiamén
    　　Hòurén Shōu, Táolǐ Zǐ, Shàng Yīshàng
    　　Gūxià Jiamén
    　　Zhèn Wén Shànggǔ, Dāo Bāo Dàizhì, Juànrén Lái
    　　Sīlì Jiamén
    　　Bàbǐ Fù, Shì Gù Bǎng
    　　Wǔzuò Xíng Jiamén
    　　Yībiàn Shēnghuó
    　　Juē Lái Jiamén
    　　Shòutāi Chéng Qì
    　　Zhūzá Qì
    　　Móshí Jiāng, Méifēi, Yùfó, Sāng Jiào Jiāngwǔ, Jiùjià, Zhào É'é, Shí Fù Yín Biàn Māo, Shuǐmǔ, Yùhuán, Zǒu Yīngg

    564

    遁母
    　　钱唐戴厚甫【淳】邓文肃公之壻也精遁甲法戴之母常寝处楼上忽一夕惊见红光贯室因开帏细视之乃是一美妇人独立榻前自防金钗遗母既而无所见母以语戴答曰适某祭遁神遂致此耳遁母见某必不乆于人世矣由是悒悒不乐逾数月果卒
    　　天竺观音
    　　今杭州之上天竺寺观音像长不盈五尺而叠着灵异官民信奉甚恭凡旱潦祷之必应尝攷释氏纪録云后晋天福己亥僧道翊一夕见山间光明徃视之得竒香木命良工刻成观世音菩萨像白光焕发继以昼夜后汉干祐戊申有僧从勲以古佛舍利置毫相中舍利时现冠顶宋咸平庚子浙西自春徂夏不雨给事中知杭州张去华率僚属具幡盖鼓吹迎祷于梵天寺继时霔雨四境沛足如此则自有像已四百余年其所由来逺矣

    Explanation

    564

    Translation:

    The Divine Mother
    Dai Houfu of Qiantang, [Chun], was the son-in-law of Deng Wen肃, a man who was skilled in the art of Tun Jia. Dai's mother often slept on the upper floor of their home. One night, she was suddenly startled by a bright red light filling the room. She opened the curtain to look closely and saw a beautiful woman standing alone before the bed, as if protecting a gold hairpin belonging to her mother. Afterward, the woman disappeared. The mother told Dai about this, and he replied, "I was just offering a sacrifice to the Tun Shen (divine spirit), and this is the result." The appearance of the Divine Mother meant that Dai would not live long. From that day on, he was deeply melancholic. After several months, he indeed passed away.

    The Guanyin of Tianzhu
    The image of Guanyin at the Upper Tianzhu Temple in Hangzhou is less than five feet tall but is renowned for its many miraculous occurrences. Officials and commoners alike revere it greatly, and their prayers for drought or flood are always answered. According to Buddhist records, during the year 939 of the Later Jin dynasty, a monk named Dao Yi saw a bright light in the mountains one night. Upon investigating, he found a rare fragrant wood and had it carved into an image of the Bodhisattva Guanyin by skilled craftsmen. The image was radiant with white light, and this continued day and night. In the year 958 of the Later Han dynasty, a monk named Cong Xun placed ancient Buddha relics in the image of Guanyin, and the relics would occasionally appear on the top of the head. In the year 1000 of the Song dynasty, when the western part of Zhejiang Province did not receive rain from spring to summer, the Minister of State and Governor of Hangzhou, Zhang Quhua, led his officials to the Vaiśravana Temple with flags, canopies, and music to pray for rain. Soon after, heavy rain fell, and the entire region was abundantly supplied with water. Thus, the image of Guanyin has been in existence for over four hundred years, with a history that is far-reaching.

    565

    辍耕録卷二十五
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷二十六　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰传国玺
    　　御史中丞崔彧进传国玺牋曰资德大夫御史中丞臣崔彧言至元三十一年嵗次甲午春正月既旦臣番直宿卫御史台通事臣库库珠即卫所告曰太师国王之孙曰实廸者尝官同知通政院事今既殁矣生产散失家计窘极其妻托克托珍荣病一子甫九歳托以玉见贸供朝夕之给及出玉印也库库珠古人不晓文字兹故来告闻之且惊且疑乃还私家取视之色混青緑而光采射人其方可尺四寸厚及方之三不足背纽盘螭四压方际纽尽玺□之上取中通一横窽可径二分旧贯以韦条面有文八刻画捷径位置匀适皆若虫鸟鱼龙之状别有彷佛有若命字若寿字者心益惊骇意谓

    Explanation

    565

    Records of Pausing the Plow, Volume Twenty-Five
    Qinding Siku Quanshu
    Records of Pausing the Plow, Volume Twenty-Six
    Compiled by Tao Zongyi of the Yuan Dynasty
    The Imperial Seal of the Realm

    The Right Censor, Cui Yu, presented a memorial on the Imperial Seal of the Realm, stating:
    "Your Excellency, the Right Censor and Grand Councilor, Cui Yu, respectfully reports. In the thirty-first year of the Zhiyuan era, the first day of the first month of the year Jiawu, while on duty in the imperial guard at the Censorate, the interpreter Kukuzhu from the guard informed me: 'The grandson of a Grand Tutor and King, named Shidi, who once served as Vice President of the Tongzheng Yuan, has now passed away. His family has scattered, and his household is in dire straits. His wife, Toktok Zhenrong, is ill, and their only son, who is just nine years old, has entrusted a jade object to be sold to provide for daily needs. Upon examining the jade seal, Kukuzhu, who is unfamiliar with ancient scripts, came to inform me. I was both surprised and skeptical, so I returned home to examine it. The jade is a mix of blue-green hues with a dark, radiant glow. Its dimensions are four inches in length, four inches in width, and four inches in thickness. On the back, there are four coiling dragons pressing against the edges, and the seal itself is inscribed with eight characters, each carved with precision and uniformity, resembling the shapes of insects, birds, fish, and dragons. There are also faint traces that resemble the characters for "命" (fate) or "寿" (longevity). My heart was filled with shock and awe, and I wondered..."

    566

    无乃当此昌运传国玺出乎急召监察御史臣杨桓至即读之曰受命于天既寿永昌此传国寳玺文也闻之果合前意神为肃然乃加以净緜复以白帕率御史臣杨桓通事臣库库珠等直趋青宫因镇国上将军都指挥使詹事王庆端嘉议大夫家令臣阿斯罕少中大夫詹事院判臣布萨寿导谒进献皇太后御前【徽仁圣皇后】啓曰此古传国玺也秦以和氏璧所造厥后有天下者寳之以君万国然自前代失之乆矣今当宫车晚出诸大臣佥议迎请皇太孙【成宗】龙飞之时不求而见此乃天示其瑞应也宜早达于皇太孙行殿以符灵贶已嘉纳翼日令资善大夫中书右丞詹事臣张九思少中

    Explanation

    566

    Perhaps at this auspicious time, the imperial seal was produced, and an urgent summons was issued to the censorial inspector Yang Huan, who was immediately called. Upon reading it, he said, "Bestowed by Heaven, long life and eternal prosperity." This is the inscription on the imperial seal. Upon hearing this, it indeed matched his previous intentions, and the gods were solemnly impressed. The seal was then wrapped in clean silk and covered with a white cloth. The censorial inspector Yang Huan, along with the interpreter Kukuzhu and others, went directly to the Eastern Palace, accompanied by the Marshal of the Guard, Commander-in-Chief, and President Wang Qing, the Vice President of the Imperial Household, Aishan, and the Deputy President of the Imperial Household, Busa Shou. They led the way to present the seal to the Empress Dowager, the [Empress Dowager Huiren], saying, "This is the ancient imperial seal, made by the Qin dynasty from the Heishi Bi. Those who ruled the world thereafter treasured it to govern all nations. However, it has been lost for a long time since the previous dynasty. Now, at the time of the late emperor's passing, the ministers unanimously agreed to welcome and invite the crown prince, [Emperor Chengzong], whose ascension was not sought but came unexpectedly. This is the heavens showing its auspicious signs. It should be presented early to the crown prince's temporary palace to confirm the divine favor." It was already accepted with great approval. The next day, the President of the Imperial Household, Vice President of the Imperial Household, and others were ordered to present the seal to the crown prince's temporary palace.

    567

    大夫詹事院判臣布萨夀传皇太孙亲为付授此葢皇太妃懿虑深逺非臣愚所能及也臣前又啓收藏寳玺之家不知甄别循常以玉求粥臣见而识之特持来献彼犹未知望恩防其家传防赐收玉之家楮币二千五百贯并逮臣等进辨其寳者三人衣縀各一表里纹金绮素有差以为异日旌实之徴臣等已诣府前敬受讫自惟无状不胜惭赧是日金紫光禄大夫中书右丞相臣旺札勒率集贤翰林侍从诸臣入贺御前命出寳玺徧示羣臣此又出于皇太妃至正至大之量翰林学士臣董文用等前啓曰此诚神物出当其时若非皇太妃皇太孙圣感何以臻此丞相以下台臣等次第上夀自是内外称庆咸

    Explanation

    567

    The Judge of the Grand Secretariat, Minister Busa Shou, reports: The Crown Prince personally bestowed this upon me, which is due to the deep and wise considerations of the Empress Dowager, beyond my humble understanding. Previously, I reported that the family holding the imperial seal did not know how to distinguish it and used it inappropriately. I recognized it and specially brought it here to present. They were unaware and hoped for the emperor's mercy to protect their family and reward the family that received the jade with 2,500 strings of cash. I also summoned three of us to identify the treasure, each receiving different types of clothing rewards, as a future sign of recognition for their identification. We have already arrived at the palace and respectfully received the rewards, feeling deeply ashamed and unworthy. On that day, the Chancellor of the Central Secretariat, Minister Wang Zale, led the scholars and officials to offer congratulations. The emperor ordered the imperial seal to be shown to all the officials. This is another example of the Empress Dowager's great virtue and wisdom. The Hanlin scholar officials, including Minister Dong Wenyong, said, "This is indeed a divine object that appeared at the right time. If not for the sacred influence of the Empress Dowager and the Crown Prince, how could this have been achieved." The Chancellor and other officials took turns to offer congratulations, and everyone inside and outside the court celebrated.

    568

    曰天命有归臣闻诗序曰文王有明徳故天复命武王今神寳之出葢因先帝有明徳故天命复归于皇太孙也又曰皇天亲有徳享有道以言皇天非有徳有道则不亲不享也又闻之书曰皇天无亲惟徳是辅又曰天命有徳克享天心受天明命作善降之百祥歴观上世诗书之防未有无徳而能致天命之归也钦惟太祖圣武皇帝秉资神裕始为天下除祸定乱隆功盛徳简在天心受命而为天下主以至我宪天述道仁文义武大光孝皇帝徳配乾坤功包海岳孝格宗庙子育元舆地所记悉主悉臣照临无幽咸遂生乐施及明孝太子天锡仁慈之徳上感君亲之悦下系亿兆之望至元建号日月重明无

    Explanation

    568

    Heaven's mandate has its destination. I have heard that the Shijing (Book of Poetry) states: "King Wen had virtuous virtue, so the mandate was restored to King Wu." Now the emergence of divine treasures indicates that because the previous emperor had virtuous virtue, the mandate has returned to the Crown Prince. It is also said that the Supreme Heaven is close to those with virtue and enjoys those with the Way. In other words, the Supreme Heaven does not favor those without virtue or the Way. I have also heard that the Shujing (Book of Documents) states: "The Supreme Heaven has no favorites; it only supports those with virtue." It further says that the mandate of heaven favors those with virtue, allowing them to enjoy the heart of heaven and receive the clear mandate. Doing good deeds brings a hundred blessings. Throughout history, in the Shijing and Shujing, there has never been anyone without virtue who could achieve the return of heaven's mandate. Reverently considering the founding emperor, the Holy Martial Emperor, who possessed divine abundance and began to eliminate calamities and stabilize chaos, achieving great virtue and merit, chosen by the heart of heaven and receiving the mandate to be the ruler of the world, up to our Emperor, who models the heavens and follows the way, with benevolent culture and righteous martial arts, greatly illuminating filial piety, whose virtue matches heaven and earth, whose achievements encompass mountains and seas, whose filial piety pleases the ancestral temples, who nurtures the common people, and whose territories and records show that all subjects and officials are subject to just and transparent rule, leading to widespread prosperity and happiness, extending to the Crown Prince, who is divinely granted benevolent and compassionate virtue, pleasing the sovereign and parents, and binding the hopes of millions. The era of Zhongyuan has been established, and the sun and moon shine brightly again.

    569

    为而治者迨廿年虽由太子进徳修业之洪溢亦頼元妃内助之渊宻也敬惟皇太妃聪明淑懿母仪崇严徳量溥厚孝敬慈恕出乎天性徃古未有也自明孝太子升遐内则皇孙翼翼训导端严外则百司班班临御整饬由是圣上君父大见倚重虽于时皇太孙未昭储副之托而詹事之司未尝一日废阙以见皇天定命于青宫之位无时不在诚非人力所能为也钦惟皇太孙殿下徳资刚明才兼文武英谋独断大肖祖宗族属系望遐迩归心圣祖宪天述道仁文义武太光孝皇帝灼知天命之所在乆存隆顾将付以抚军之重于至元三十年夏六月二十二日赐以皇太子金寳大正储位而后诏以出师之期

    Explanation

    569

    Governing for twenty years, this has been achieved through the great abundance of the Crown Prince's moral cultivation and scholarly pursuits, as well as the profound and close support of the Crown Prince's wife. The Empress Dowager is wise, virtuous, and noble, with a dignified and strict maternal demeanor, a broad and deep moral character, filial piety, kindness, and forgiveness, all of which are innate—such qualities were unprecedented in antiquity. Since the death of the Crown Prince of Ming, the imperial grandson has been carefully instructed with strict discipline, while the officials have been orderly and well-disciplined. As a result, the emperor, as father and ruler, greatly relies on the Empress Dowager. Although at that time the Crown Prince's grandson had not yet been designated as the heir apparent, the office of the Grand Tutor was never once neglected, demonstrating that the will of Heaven has always been set on the position of the Crown Prince, a fact that is truly beyond human effort. The Crown Prince's grandson, endowed with firm and clear virtue, possesses both literary and military talents, displaying bold and decisive strategies that greatly resemble those of his ancestors. His family and clan look to him with hope, and people far and wide are devoted to him. The Grand Ancestor, the Emperor who upholds the heavens and continues the way, whose virtue is benevolent, his literary achievements righteous, his martial prowess great, and his filial piety and filial righteousness shining brightly, clearly understands the will of Heaven, having long held him in high regard and entrusted him with the heavy responsibility of military command. On the twenty-second day of the sixth month in the thirtieth year of the Zhiyuan era, he was granted the golden seal of the Crown Prince and formally invested as heir apparent, after which he was ordered to depart with the army.

    570

    天下闻之室家胥庆和气穰穰出于两间是歳秋稔数年罕遇臣切念天象无言托命不爽岂期又于大行皇帝宫车晚出之后甫八日传国神寳不求而出于大功臣子孙之家速由台谏耳目之司直达于皇太妃御前斯葢皇天授命皇太孙诞膺龙飞以正九五之位俾符寳玺之文既夀而永永而又昌臣又见皇天之心大頼我皇元继体之君不疾不迟景命适至以允四海之望者其瑞应之兆有三按唐史代宗之将为太子先封楚王及位正储副而监国楚州献定国寳一十有三因曰楚者太子之封今天降寳于楚宜建元寳应盖以寳为太子瑞应也昔明孝太子封为燕王今皇太孙燕王之子也将主神

    Explanation

    570

    All under heaven heard that the family was celebrating together, with harmonious and abundant energy arising between two spaces. In this year, the autumn harvest was abundant, rarely seen in several years. I deeply reflect that the heavens speak not, entrusting one's fate without error. Who could have expected that just eight days after the late departure of the imperial carriage of the deceased emperor, the imperial treasure appeared without being sought in the home of the descendants of great heroes, quickly reported by the officials responsible for oversight, and directly presented to the presence of the Empress Dowager. This is indeed the will of heaven, the Crown Prince was born to ascend the throne like a dragon flying, to take the position of the Son of Heaven, to make the seals and symbols of power correspond, both long-lived and ever-lasting, and ever-prosperous. I also see that the heart of heaven greatly relies on our new emperor succeeding the previous one, neither hasty nor slow, the mandate of heaven has arrived to fulfill the expectations of the four seas, and there are three signs of auspicious omens. According to the history of Emperor Daizong of Tang, who was about to become the Crown Prince, he was first enfeoffed as Prince of Chu, and when he became the heir apparent, the Governor of Chu State presented thirteen imperial treasures, because Chu was the fief of the Crown Prince, today the treasures have descended upon Chu, it is appropriate to establish the era name Bao Ying, this is because the treasures are auspicious signs for the Crown Prince. In the past, the Crown Prince was enfeoffed as Prince of Yan, now the Crown Prince is the son of the Prince of Yan, and will take charge of the imperial power.

    571

    器而神寳出于燕适与前事相符此瑞应之兆一也又寳玺之出正当皇元圣天子六合一统之时宫车晚出之近朝以见天心正为继体之君设也此瑞应之兆二也又寳玺之出适当月之三十日有终而复始之象以见先圣皇帝御世太平之功既成俾继体之君复其始也此瑞应之兆三也今以此三兆观之益见天命之来际合于青宫也臣区区之情无任倾向辄罄所见以賛其万一谨将寳玺之出处古今始末详据考按许慎説文玺玉者印也以守土故为文从尔从土其义盖曰天付尔此器俾寳之以守尔土也至周太史籀易为从尔从玉义取天付尔此玉寳以为天下君也三代以上玺文无所考诸

    Explanation

    571

    A divine and sacred treasure emerged in Yan, coinciding with previous events—this is the first auspicious sign. The appearance of the precious seal occurred at the time when the great and holy emperor unified the six directions; the late emergence of the imperial chariot in the recent court indicates that heaven's will is for the successor to the throne—this is the second auspicious sign. The appearance of the precious seal also coincided with the thirtieth day of the month, symbolizing the end and renewal of a cycle, showing that the achievements of the former sage emperor's peaceful and prosperous reign are complete, enabling the successor to the throne to resume the beginning—this is the third auspicious sign. Considering these three signs, it further demonstrates that the coming of heaven's mandate aligns with the eastern palace. The humble feelings of the subject cannot help but be directed; I will exhaust what I see in order to praise it even slightly. I carefully present the origin and history of the precious seal from ancient times to the present, based on Xu Shen's Shuowen Jiezi: a seal made of jade is an official stamp, because it is used to guard territory, the character is composed of "you" and "earth." The meaning is that heaven has given you this object to treasure and guard your territory. Up to the time of the Zhou historian Zhou, who changed it to "you" and "jade," the meaning is that heaven has given you this jade treasure to be the ruler of the world. There is no record of seal inscriptions before the Three Dynasties.

    572

    史籍并寳玺文圗説曰传国玺方四寸其文文饰如前楚以卞和所献之璞琢而成璧后求昬于赵以纳聘焉秦昭王请以十城易之而不获始皇并六国得之命李斯其文玉工孙夀刻之太平御览又以为蓝田玉所刻二世子婴奉玺降沛公于轵道旁高祖即位服其玺因世传之谓为传国玺厥后孺子未立藏于长乐宫及莽簒位使安阳侯王舜廹太后求之太后怒骂而不与舜言益切出玺投之地玺因归莽及更始灭莽校尉公賔得玺诣宛献于更始赤眉杀更始立盆子玺为盆子所有后盆子面缚奉玺于光武至献帝董卓作乱掌玺者投于井中孙坚征董卓于井中得之术夺于坚妻术死荆州

    Explanation

    572

    The Imperial Seal of the Nation, as recorded in historical texts and its accompanying descriptions and illustrations, was a square four inches in size, with its text and decorations as described previously. It was crafted from the jade presented by Bi Gan to the state of Chu, which was later used as a dowry in a marriage alliance with the state of Zhao. The King of Qin requested to exchange ten cities for it but did not succeed. When Qin Shi Huang unified the six states, he obtained the seal and ordered Li Si to engrave the text, which was then carved by the jade craftsman Sun Shou. The Taiping Yulan also records that it was made from Lantian jade. During the reign of the Second Emperor, Ziying surrendered the seal to Xiang Yu at the Zhide Road. When Emperor Gaozu ascended the throne, he wore the seal, and thus it was passed down through generations, becoming known as the Imperial Seal of the Nation. When the young heir was not yet established, it was hidden in the Changle Palace. When Wang Mang usurped the throne, he ordered the Prince of Anyang, Wang Shun, to pressure the empress dowager to obtain it. She angrily refused to give it, and Wang Shun pressed her even more, causing her to throw the seal to the ground, which then returned to Wang Mang. When Gengshi overthrew Wang Mang, the military officer Gong Bin obtained the seal and presented it to Gengshi in the city of Wan. The Red Eyebrows killed Gengshi and installed Liu Penzi as emperor, and the seal was thus in the possession of Liu Penzi. Later, Liu Penzi surrendered the seal to Emperor Guangwu with his hands bound behind his back. Until the reign of Emperor Xian, when Dong Zhuo rebelled, the person in charge of the seal threw it into a well. Sun Jian, who was fighting against Dong Zhuo, found it in the well. Xiao Chao (a nickname for Yuan Shu) took it from Sun Jian's wife, and when Yuan Shu died in Jingzhou, the seal remained in his possession.

    573

    刺史徐璆闻帝为曹操迎在许昌以玺送之帝后逊位并以玺归魏常道乡公禅位玺归于晋懐帝遇刘聪之害玺归于聪聪死归曜曜为石勒所灭玺入于勒勒灭入于冉闵闵败见收于闵之将军蒋干晋征西将军谢尚购得之以还东晋时穆帝永和八年也自玺寄于刘石共五十三年晋复得之是后宋齐梁陈相传以至于隋灭陈萧后与太子正道并传国玺并入于突厥唐太宗即位寳玺未获乃自刻玉曰皇帝景命有徳者昌贞观四年萧后与正道自突厥奉玺归于唐唐始得焉朱温簒唐玺入于温荘宗定乱玺入于后唐荘宗遇害明宗嗣立再传养子从珂是为废帝后氏簒立自焚自是玺不知所在

    Explanation

    573

    The governor Xu Qiu heard that Emperor Xian was welcomed by Cao Cao in Xu昌 and sent the imperial seal to him. Later, Emperor Xian abdicated and the seal was transferred to Wei. Emperor Hui of Jin abdicated, and the seal was passed to the Jin dynasty. However, Emperor Hui of Jin was harmed by Liu Cong, and the seal was taken by Liu Cong. After Liu Cong's death, the seal was given to Shi Le, who conquered the state and the seal was taken by him. Shi Le was then defeated by Ran Min, and the seal was captured by Ran Min's general Jiang Gan. The Western Jin general Xie Shang bought the seal and returned it to the Eastern Jin. It was during the reign of Emperor Mu of Jin in the year Yonghe 8th that the seal was returned to the Jin dynasty. From then on, the seal was passed down through the Song, Qi, Liang, and Chen dynasties until the Sui dynasty conquered Chen. The consort Xiao Hou and the crown prince Zhengdao also passed the seal to the Tujue. Emperor Taizong of Tang ascended the throne but did not immediately obtain the seal. He then carved a new seal with the inscription "The mandate of Heaven favors virtue." In the fourth year of the Zhenguan era, Xiao Hou and Zhengdao returned the seal from the Tujue to the Tang dynasty, and the Tang dynasty finally obtained it. Zhu Wen usurped the Tang dynasty and the seal was taken by him. Zhuangzong of Later Tang quelled the rebellion and the seal was taken by the Later Tang dynasty. Zhuangzong was later killed, and Mingzong succeeded him. The seal was then passed to Chongke, who was deposed and killed. The seal's whereabouts became unknown after this point.

    574

    至宋哲宗咸阳民叚义献玉玺及徽宗为金所执凡有寳玺金皆取之内玺一十有四青玉传国玺一其色与今所献玉玺相同则知宋之南迁二百年无此寳玺也明矣然自金既取于宋之后寳玺出处得失亦未见明説以及我元适集皇太孙寳命所归之际应期而出臣职总御史亲会盛事不可以不録又圗中别有玺其文亦八旁注曰此传国玺背文也今见寳玺之背皆刻螭形蟠屈凹凸不齐徧视压四际无地可置此文按太平御览太元十九年雝州刺史郄恢表慕容永称藩奉玺方六寸厚七分蟠螭为鼻今高四寸六分四边文下有字曰受天之命皇帝夀昌原其所由未详厥始以斯言之当别是

    Explanation

    574

    During the reign of Emperor Zhezong of Song, a man named Duan Yi from Xianyang presented a jade seal. Emperor Huizong was taken captive by the Jin. All the jade seals were taken, including fourteen inner seals. One of them was a blue jade imperial seal, its color matching the one presented today. Thus, it is clear that during the two hundred years of the Southern Song, this seal did not exist. However, after the Jin took it from the Song, the whereabouts and loss of the seal were not clearly documented. And during the time when my Yuan dynasty was gathering the imperial mandate and the fate of the crown prince, the seal appeared as if by destiny. As a censor, I personally witnessed this grand event and cannot fail to record it. In the diagram, there is another seal with eight characters on the back, noted as the text on the back of the imperial seal. Now, the back of the seal is carved with a coiling dragon, uneven and irregular, pressing against the four corners, leaving no space for the text. According to T'ai-pai Yu-lan, in the nineteenth year of Taiyuan, the governor of Yongzhou, Xie Hui, presented a seal to Murong Yong, who submitted to the Jin. The seal was six inches square and seven-tenths of an inch thick, with a coiling dragon as the nose. Now, it is four inches six-tenths of an inch high, with tortoise patterns on the four sides, and the inscription reads "Received the mandate of heaven, Emperor's longevity." The origin is not detailed, but it can be inferred that it is different.

    575

    一玺非今传国玺也此又不可不辨臣彧诚惶诚恐顿首顿首谨奉牋上进以闻伏希听览微臣不胜瞻望之至谨言此文乃桓所譔桓字武子兖州人防警悟为人寛厚事亲笃孝愽览羣籍尤精籀之学由儒学教授仕至国子司业卒库库珠拓防氏成宗即位近臣以其事闻授汉中廉访司佥事仕至湖广廉访使卒国史于安札尔传谓实廸乃国王缫古察之子谓桓辩其文曰受命于天既夀永昌于桓传谓桓辩其文曰受天之命既夀永昌盖秦别有受天之命皇帝夀昌一玺又非此玺此则史之误也今取宋薛尚功此编歴代钟鼎彛器欵识法帖碑本第十八卷内玺文模勒于后以备愽古者之一览云

    Explanation

    575

    This seal is not the current imperial seal; this fact also cannot be overlooked. I, Xuyu, am deeply humbled and fearful, and I respectfully present this document for your review. I hope you will listen and read it. Your humble servant, Xuyu, looks forward to your attention with great anticipation. I respectfully state.

    This text was compiled by Huan, whose courtesy name was Wuzi, and who was from the state of Yan. He was alert and perceptive, with a broad and kind nature, filial to his parents, and well-read in a wide range of texts, especially proficient in the study of the Shangshu and Zhou scripts. Through Confucian studies, he became a teacher and served as a vice-principal of the Imperial Academy before his death. Upon the accession of the Chengzong emperor, a court official brought this matter to the attention of the court, and Huan was appointed as a deputy of the Censorate of Hanzhong. He later served as a censor of Huguang before his death. The national history in the biography of Anzal states that he was the son of King Soguc. Huan explained the inscription as "Granted by Heaven, long-lived and prosperous." In the biography of Huan, it is stated that he explained the inscription as "Receiving the mandate of Heaven, long-lived and prosperous." There was another seal from the Qin dynasty with the inscription "Receiving the mandate of Heaven, emperor long-lived and prosperous," which is not this seal. This is an error in the historical record. Now, taking from Song Dynasty's Xue Shanggong's compilation of inscriptions on bronze vessels, bells, ritual vessels, steles, and rubbings, the text of the seal from the eighteenth volume is reproduced here for the reference of scholars of ancient times.

    576

    尚功云二玺文本只一器縁传摹字画不同形制大小有异因并刻之亦疑以传疑之意也

    <子部,小说家类,杂事之属,辍耕录,卷二十六>
    <子部,小说家类,杂事之属,辍耕录,卷二十六>
    　　瑞应泉
    　　湖州长兴州金沙泉唐时用此水造紫笋茶进贡泉不常出有司具牲牢祭之始得水事讫即涸宋季屡加浚治泉迄不出至元十五年嵗戊寅中书省遣官致祭一夕水溢可溉田千畆遂赐名瑞应泉
    　　疑
    　　曹操疑七十二在漳河上宋俞应符有诗题之曰生前欺天絶汉统死后欺人设疑冡人生用智死即休何有余机到丘垄人言疑我不疑我有一法君未知直湏尽发疑七十二必有一藏君尸此亦诗之斧也

    Explanation

    576

    Translation

    On the Two Seal Texts

    According to Shang Gong, the two seal texts are essentially one vessel, but due to differences in transmission and copying, the characters and calligraphy vary, and the shape and size differ. Therefore, they were both engraved to preserve the uncertainty and ambiguity.

    The Auspicious Spring

    The Ruiying Spring is located in the Jinsha Spring of Changxing County, Huzhou Prefecture. During the Tang dynasty, this spring water was used to make Zi Sun tea, which was presented as a tribute to the emperor. The spring did not emerge frequently, so officials would offer sacrifices with animals and food to invoke its appearance. After the sacrifice, the spring would flow, but it would dry up immediately afterward. During the late Song dynasty, the spring was repeatedly dredged and treated, but it still did not emerge. In the 15th year of the Zhiyuan era of the Yuan dynasty (1278), the Central Secretariat sent officials to offer sacrifices to the spring. One night, the spring overflowed and could irrigate a thousand acres of land. Thus, it was granted the name Ruiying Spring.

    The Doubtful Tombs

    Cao Cao had 72 tombs along the Zhe River, each designed to confuse and deter grave robbers. During the Song dynasty, Yu Yingfu wrote a poem titled On the Doubtful Tombs:

    Before his death, he deceived heaven and betrayed the Han dynasty.
    After his death, he deceived people with his doubtful tombs.
    In life, one uses intelligence; once dead, it is done.
    What room is there for trickery in the grave?
    People say the doubtful tombs, but I do not doubt.
    I have a method you do not know.
    You must open all 72 doubtful tombs.
    One of them must contain your body.
    This is also the poem's sharp edge.

    577

    卢橘
    　　世人多用卢橘以称枇杷按司马相如上林赋云卢橘夏熟黄柑橙楱枇杷橪【而善切】柿夫卢橘与枇杷并列则卢橘非枇杷明矣郭璞注蜀中有给客橙冬夏花实相继通嵗食之谓即卢橘也意者橙橘惟熟于冬而卢橘夏亦熟故举以为重欤唐三体诗裴庾注云广州记卢橘皮厚大如柑酢多至夏熟土人呼为壶橘又曰卢橘
    　　五龙车
    　　叶公李为宋太学生时上书极言贾似道权奸误国几为所害及世祖平江南即召见官之至中书右丞凡有军国大事必问曰曽与蛮子秀才商量否盖指李也一日议事大廷乃不在列问其故则病足遂以所御五龙车召之至命坐而谘决焉尝于其孙以道处见当时所画应召圗五龙车中坐一山野质朴之老其遭遇有如此者使无贾似道以发其正大之论直一书生耳而望功名显天下亦难矣

    Explanation

    577

    Translation

    Luju (卢橘)
    People often use "Luju" to refer to loquat. According to Sima Xiangru's "Shanglin Fu" (上林赋), it is stated that "Luju ripens in summer, along with yellow oranges and pomelos, and loquats." The character "橪" (má) can be pronounced as "shàn," and it refers to persimmons. Since "Luju" and "loquat" are listed side by side, it is clear that "Luju" is not "loquat." Guo Pu, in his commentary on the "Shuowen Jiezi," mentions that in Shu (Sichuan), there is a type of orange called "gike" (给客橙) that flowers and bears fruit throughout the year, making it suitable for consumption all year round. He suggests that this might be what is referred to as "Luju." Perhaps because oranges and tangerines only ripen in winter, while "Luju" ripens in summer as well, it is considered significant. According to the "San Ti Shi" (三体诗) annotated by Pei Yu and Yu Xuanji, the "Guangzhou Ji" (广州记) states that "Luju has thick skin, similar to that of a tangerine, and is very sour, ripening in summer. Locals call it 'hujue' (壶橘), and it is also known as 'Luju.'"

    Five Dragon Carriage (五龙车)
    Ye Gong Li, while a student at the Imperial College of the Song dynasty, wrote a petition strongly criticizing Jia Sidao, a powerful and corrupt official, for misguiding the country, nearly getting himself in trouble. When Emperor Shizu (Genghis Khan's grandson) of the Yuan dynasty conquered the Southern Song, he immediately summoned Ye Gong Li and appointed him to the position of Right Chancellor of the Central Secretariat. For all major military and state affairs, he would ask, "Have you discussed this with the scholar 'Mazishi'?" This referred to Ye Gong Li. One day, during a meeting in the grand hall, Ye Gong Li was not present. When asked why, it was revealed that he was ill and had difficulty walking. Therefore, he was summoned in his "Five Dragon Carriage," seated, and consulted on the matter. Once, at his grandson Ye Yida's place, he saw a painting depicting the summoning scene, showing a simple and unpretentious old man seated in the "Five Dragon Carriage." His experience was like this. If it were not for Jia Sidao, who brought out his principled and upright views, he would have remained just an ordinary scholar, and achieving fame and success in the world would have been difficult.

    578

    伏波将军
    　　琼州一水南北有两伏波将军庙世人莫明其故尝考之史记及东汉书盖汉元鼎五年卫尉路博徳为伏波将军出桂林下滙水不特马援为伏波将军也至元钞料
    　　至元印造通行寳钞分一十一料
    　　贰贯　　壹贯　　伍伯文　叄伯文
    　　贰伯文　壹伯文　伍拾文　叄拾文
    　　贰拾文　壹拾文　伍文
    　　雕传
    　　某人浮湛里中无以为生侦民有小不平嗾之讼佐之请谒已旁縁自资且既饵临政者因持其短长以蠧民梗政遂有人作雕传以警之传曰昔黄帝少皥氏之世凤鸟适至故为鸟师而鸟名命凤凰为百禽长当是时南山有鸟其名曰雕雕之性鸷而徤贪而狡稻粱之甘木实之美雕不屑焉资众禽之肉以为食雕之徒实繁其与雕同气而异质者鹰鹯鸢隼鹞鹘鸱鹗皆助雕为虐者也其异类而同姓者鸱鸮鸺鹠枭鸩训狐车其恶与雕同特其材异尔然雕有大小小者从鹪鹩鷃雀力可制则制之大者虽鸿鹄不畏也故雕之所在众禽皆逃

    Explanation

    578

    The General Who Suppresses Waves

    In Qiongzhou, there are two temples dedicated to the General Who Suppresses Waves on both sides of the river. People do not understand the reason for this. I have examined the Records of the Grand Historian and the Book of the Later Han and found that in the fifth year of the Yuanding era, Lu Bode, the Chief of the Guard, was the General Who Suppresses Waves and went out from Guilin to the confluence of the rivers. Not only Ma Yuan held this title.

    The "Zhiyuan Currency Standards" are divided into eleven denominations:

    • Two strings of cash
    • One string of cash
    • Five hundred coins
    • Three hundred coins
    • Two hundred coins
    • One hundred coins
    • Fifty coins
    • Thirty coins
    • Twenty coins
    • Ten coins
    • Five coins

    Biography of the Falcon

    A certain person lived in the village without means of livelihood. He detected small grievances among the people and incited them to sue, assisting with petitions and using them as a means of self-support. Having already gained the favor of officials, he held onto their strengths and weaknesses to corrupt the people and obstruct governance. So someone wrote the Biography of the Falcon to warn him. The text begins:

    "In the time of Emperor Huangdi and the Lesser Hao clan, the phoenix arrived. Therefore, he became the master of birds and named the phoenix as the leader of all birds. At that time, in the south mountains, there was a bird called the falcon. The falcon's nature is fierce and strong, greedy and cunning. The sweetness of rice and millet, the beauty of tree fruits, the falcon does not care about them. He relies on the meat of other birds as his food. The followers of the falcon are numerous, and those who share his spirit but have different qualities. Eagles, hawks, falcons, kites, peregrines, falcons, owls, and other birds of prey all assist the falcon in his mischief. Those who are different in kind but share the same name—owls, screech-owls, crows, ravens, foxes, and ghosts—share the same evil as the falcon, but their abilities are different. However, the falcon comes in different sizes. Small ones can be controlled by sparrows and quails, but large ones are not afraid of cranes. Therefore, wherever the falcon is, all other birds flee."

    579

    散逺去摽枝无安巢灌丛无息羽雕无所得食则遣操诡辞招众禽之过而愬诸凤曰鸿鴈背北而来南是叛者也鹦鹉舍禽言习人语是奸者也仓庚出幽谷迁乔木是冐越者也鷾鸸秋冬逺遁是避役者也乌知吉凶言妖祥以惑众听鹊填河以阻水利鸤鸠攘鹊之居鸳鸯荒淫无度鸥好闲鸡好鬭殴相伤鳬鹥鹅鸭习水战鸬鷀白鹭得鱼不税孔雀有异相杜鹃催归令戍卒逃亡提壶劝人饮酒生事是皆有罪不治将益甚凤凰惑焉命爽鸠氏治之雕与爽鸠相为表里穷山谷搜林麓禽之出者搏之逐之攫之拏之啄胔扼吭裂肪絶筋磔毛风洒

    Explanation

    579

    The birds, dispersing far from their perching branches, find no safe nests in dense thickets, and are unable to rest or find food. Then, using deceptive words, they accuse many birds of wrongdoing and report it to the phoenix, saying: "Wild geese flying south instead of north are traitors; parrots abandoning their natural language to learn human speech are traitors; orioles leaving the depths of the valley to ascend to tall trees are overstepping their bounds; sparrows fleeing far in autumn and winter are avoiding their duties; crows knowing omens and predicting good or bad fortune to deceive the masses; magpies blocking rivers to hinder water flow; cuckoos usurping magpies' nests; mandarin ducks indulging in promiscuity without limit; seagulls liking idleness and chickens enjoying fighting, injuring each other; teals, geese, and ducks practicing water warfare; cormorants and egrets catching fish without paying taxes; peacocks having unusual appearances; cuckoos urging soldiers to return, causing them to flee; carrying a pot to encourage drinking and causing trouble—all of which are crimes that will worsen if not addressed." Confused, the phoenix orders the officer of the cuckoo to deal with it. The hawk and the officer of the cuckoo work together, searching through mountains and forests, capturing and attacking birds, tearing them apart, and dispersing their remains.

    580

    血殷地凡遇之者无噍类其余皆周章振掉谋所以免祸者毁巢破鷇空所积以奉爽鸠且以赂雕使勿执于是雕之势益张而众禽之生理日蹙其爪距稍利者慕雕所为则起而效之其钝者深藏逺窜馁死于草莽相借也而凤凰始忧之闻蓬莱之颠有胎仙焉胎仙名号青田翁廉介而洁白和平而好生于是徴爽鸠使乘轩而治之乃与凤凰谋曰夫雕其始一而已自子之不戒而使之蔓延今之为雕者何其多耶昔之雕名雕字雕形雕性雕本为雕者也今有非雕而雕者何也雕则得食不雕则不得食雕则有利而无害不雕则利未见而害

    Explanation

    580

    The blood stained the ground, and all who encountered it met no survivors. The rest were in a state of panic and agitation, planning how to avoid disaster, destroying nests and breaking eggs, emptying what they had accumulated to serve the hawk, and bribing the hawk to prevent it from seizing. As a result, the hawk's power became increasingly strong, while the survival of other birds became increasingly difficult. Those with slightly sharper claws envied the hawk's actions and rose up to emulate it, while those with duller claws hid deeply and fled far away, starving to death in the wilderness, relying on each other. The phoenix began to worry about this and heard that at the top of Penglai there was a fetus of a fairy, named  and called Qingtian Old Man, who was honest and pure, peaceful and fond of life. Therefore, the phoenix summoned the hawk and sent  to govern with a carriage.  then said to the phoenix, "The hawk was originally just one, but because you did not guard against it and allowed it to spread, there are now so many hawks. The hawk was originally named, written, shaped, and characterized as a hawk. Now there are those who are not hawks but act like hawks. Those who act like hawks get food, while those who do not act like hawks do not get food. Those who act like hawks have benefits without harm, while those who do not act like hawks do not see benefits but suffer harm."

    581

    常随之故不容其不雕也今禽之产子者愿为雕鶵之习飞者学为雕形状与雕异者又冐为雕不诛其渠魁殱其凶丑以励其余吾恐鸾鷃鹓鸑神雀大鹏金翅皆化为雕耳凤凰曰善奏请于帝帝遣虞人持弓矢张网罗随雕而磔之雕之徒尽毙勑天下无留雕故其余党皆屏迹匿形不敢出众禽始得安于生养以尽其天年此皆少皥氏之恩凤凰与之力也太史公曰雕奸禽也暴恶受诛固宜吾独惧今之人子务养雕意有所欲举雕而放之求众禽之血肉以肥其躯殊不知少皥氏之戒也嗟夫害物而日益者刑虽未及天必谴之其雕岂足恤哉

    Explanation

    581

    The constant companionship does not allow one to avoid being transformed; birds that produce offspring wish to be the young of eagles, those who practice flying learn to become eagles, and those whose shapes differ from eagles also pretend to be eagles. If one does not punish their leaders and exterminate their wickedness, the rest will not be encouraged. I fear that even the phoenix, the osprey, the crane, the divine bird, the great peng, and the golden-winged eagle will all transform into eagles. The phoenix said, "Good," and petitioned the emperor. The emperor sent a gamekeeper holding bows and arrows and setting snares to follow the eagles and kill them. All the followers of the eagles were killed, and the emperor commanded the world to eliminate all eagles. Thus, the remaining followers all hid their traces and forms, did not dare to show themselves among the other birds, and could live in peace and fulfill their natural lifespan. All this is due to the grace of the Shao Hao and the power of the phoenix and the phoenix's helper. The historian says, "Eagles are wicked birds, cruel and evil, deserving punishment, it is indeed fitting." I alone fear that modern people devote themselves to raising eagles, if they have a desire, to raise and release the eagles, seeking the blood and flesh of other birds to fatten their bodies, but do not know the warning of Shao Hao. Alas, those who harm others and grow increasingly wicked, although punishment has not yet been meted out, the heavens will surely punish them; how can such eagles be pitied?

    582

    三瓦戒
    　　陈众仲先生尝题乐全堂有能守不成三瓦戒之句人多不知所出按史记防传云天尚不全故世为屋不成三瓦而陈之注陈犹居也
    　　酸斋辞世诗
    　　贯酸斋先生临终有辞世诗曰洞花幽草结良縁我瞒他四十年今日不留生死相海天秋月一般圆洞花幽草乃先生二妾名
    　　高昌世家
    　　虞文靖公集撰高昌王世勲碑序其世家曰辉和尔之地有和林山二水出焉一曰圗喇一曰索隆噶一夕有天光降于树在两河之间国人即而候之树生瘿若人姙身然自是光恒见者越九月又十日而瘿裂得婴儿五收养之其最穉者名曰布克汗既壮遂能有其民人土田而为之君长传三十余君是为伊噜勒徳济数与唐人相攻战乆之乃议和亲以息民而罢兵于是唐以金莲公主妻伊噜勒徳济之子格哷勒徳济居和林伯哩伯哩特言妇所居山也后迁交州至太祖龙飞朔漠当是时巴哩珠阿勒坦徳济伊克都防在位伊克都防者其国王号也举国入朝太祖嘉之妻以公主曰伊埒鄂端自是子孙皆封王

    Explanation

    582

    Translation

    Three Tile Precept
    Mr. Chen Zhongzhong once inscribed the Lequan Hall with the phrase "one who can uphold the precept of the incomplete three tiles." Many did not know its origin. According to the Records of the Grand Historian in the chapter on divination, it says, "Heaven is still not complete, hence the world is like a house that is not fully constructed." The note explains that "Tian" means "to dwell."

    Acid Chamber's Parting Poem
    As Mr. Guan Suanzai was on his deathbed, he composed a parting poem: "In the secluded and beautiful grasslands, we formed a good bond. I deceived him for forty years. Today, I leave no traces of life and death. The sea and sky under the autumn moon are equally round." "Donghua" and "Yuchao" are the names of his two concubines.

    The High Chang Dynasty
    Yu Wenjing, in his collection, wrote the inscription on the achievements of the kings of Gaochang. It states: "In the land of Huier, there is the Orkhon Mountain, from which two rivers flow. One is called Tula, and the other is called Solongga. One night, divine light descended on a tree between the two rivers. The people of the land observed it, and the tree grew a tumor like a pregnant woman. From then on, the light was constantly seen. After nine months and ten days, the tumor split open, revealing five infants. They were adopted, and the youngest was named Bukhan. When he grew up, he became the ruler of the people and the land, becoming the king. He ruled for over thirty kings, known as Iruledeji. He often engaged in wars with the Tang people. After a long time, they agreed to a marriage alliance to pacify the people and end the wars. Thus, the Tang married Princess Jinlian to the son of Iruledeji, Gelerdeji, who lived in Orkhon. 'Borli' means the mountain where the wife lived. Later, they moved to Jiaozhou. By the time of Taizu's rise to power in the northern steppes, King Barizhu Alatandji, also known as King Ikudafa, was in power. The entire kingdom submitted to him. Taizu praised him and married him a princess, saying, 'Yiluoeduan.' From then on, all his descendants were enfeoffed as kings."

    583

    后德
    　　今上皇后鸿吉哩氏名巴延呼图克武宗宣懿惠圣皇后之侄毓德王博啰特穆尔女后至元二年丁丒三月立性节俭不妬忌动以礼法自持第三皇后竒氏素有宠居兴圣西宫帝希幸东内左右以为言后无几微怨望意尝从帝时廵上京次中道帝遣内官传防欲临幸辞曰莫夜非至尊徃来之时内官徃来再三竟拒不纳帝益贤之居坤德殿终日端坐未尝妄逾户阈至正二十五年乙巳八月丁未崩年四十二文宗能画
    　　文宗居金陵潜邸时命臣房大年画京都万岁山大年辞以未尝至其地上索纸为运笔布画位置令按槀图上大年得槀敬藏之意匠经营格法遒整虽积学专工所莫能及

    Explanation

    583

    The Virtue of the Empress

    The current empress, whose surname is Hong Jili and whose name is Ba Yan Hu Tu Ke, is the daughter of Prince Bo Luo Te Mu Er, a relative of the Empress Xuan Yi Hui Sheng of the Wu Zong. She was established as empress in the third month of the second year of the Zhi Yuan era. She was frugal and did not envy others. She always upheld herself according to propriety and law. The third queen, Qi, had long been favored and resided in the Xing Sheng Palace West. The emperor rarely visited the eastern palace, and his attendants advised him. However, the empress did not show any resentment. She once followed the emperor on an inspection tour of the imperial capital. On the way, the emperor sent an eunuch to convey a message, saying he wanted to visit her. She replied, "It is not the time for the emperor to visit." The eunuch went back and forth several times, but she finally refused to receive him. The emperor admired her even more. She lived in the Kun De Palace, where she spent her days sitting quietly, never leaving her quarters without reason. She died in the twenty-fifth year of the Zheng Tong era, in the eighth month of the year Yi Si, at the age of forty-two. The emperor was skilled in painting.

    When Emperor Wen Zong was a prince in Jinling, he ordered Minister Fang Dayuan to paint the Wan Shou Mountain in the capital. Fang Dayuan declined, saying he had never been there. The emperor then provided him with paper and instructed him to sketch the mountain. Fang Dayuan followed the emperor's instructions and carefully planned the painting, adhering to strict rules and achieving a high level of skill that was difficult to match.

    584

    武当山降笔
    　　至元十三年江南初内附民间盛传武当山真武降笔书长短句曰西江月者锓刻于梓黄纸模印贴壁间其词云九九乾坤已定清明节候开花米田天下乱如麻直待龙蛇继马【继一作暨】依旧中华福地黎民一度还家当初指望作生涯死在西江月下
    　　箕仙有验
    　　虞邵庵先生布衣时落落不偶乆客钱唐一日偕友人杨公仲薛公宗海范公徳机访方外宰渊微链师于西湖之曲求召仙以卜行蔵链师即置箕悬笔书符作法有顷箕动笔运而附降云某非仙乃当境神也链师叱曰吾不汝召汝神何来神附云某欲乞虞公譔一保文申达上帝用求迁陞耳因众劝先生其无辞神请先生遂诺翼日文成火于湖濵逾旬再诣链师祷卜神复降云某已获授城隍谨候谒谢公必贵显幸母自忽既而先生由校官至奉章阁侍书学士赠江西行中书省参知政事封仁夀郡公谥文靖以文章名四海岂非先世积有余庆天将报施于先生之躬而神预有知耶

    Explanation

    584

    Divine Writing at Mount Wudang
    In the thirteenth year of the Zhiyuan era (1276), when the southern regions were newly incorporated into the Yuan Dynasty, there was a widespread belief among the people that Zhenwu, the deity of Mount Wudang, had written a poem titled "Xijiangyue" (Western River Moon). The poem was carved onto wooden blocks, printed on yellow paper, and pasted on the walls. The words of the poem read: "Nine nines, the heavens and earth are set; during the Qingming Festival, flowers bloom. The world is as chaotic as hemp in a rice field. Until dragons and snakes follow the horse, the land of Central China will once again be a blessed place, and the common people will return home. Originally hoping to make a living, they will die under the moonlight over the Xijiang River."

    The Diviner's Prediction Was Accurate
    Mr. Yu Shao'an, while still a commoner, was often lonely and unaccompanied, and had been a long-term guest in Qiantang (ancient name for Hangzhou). One day, he visited Zaiyuan Wei, a Daoist master practicing alchemy, in a secluded area near West Lake. He sought to summon a ghost spirit to predict his fate. The master placed a basket, suspended a brush, and wrote a talisman, performing the necessary rituals. Soon, the basket moved, and the brush began to write, with a voice attaching itself to the writing, saying, "I am not a ghost but a local deity." The master rebuked, "I did not summon you; why have you come?" The deity replied, "I wish to ask Mr. Yu to write a recommendation letter to the Heavenly Emperor to seek promotion." Persuaded by the crowd, Mr. Yu agreed. The next day, the letter was completed and burned by the lakeside. After ten days, Mr. Yu returned to the master to pray and seek a divination. The deity descended again, saying, "I have already been granted the position of Chenghuang (City God). I will respectfully await your thanks. You will surely achieve great status; do not be careless." Later, Mr. Yu rose from a low-ranking official position to become a high-ranking official in the imperial court, and was posthumously titled "Wenjing" (Cultured and Peaceful). His fame as a scholar spread far and wide. Is this not a sign that the blessings of his ancestors have been repaid, and that the spirits had foreknowledge of his destiny?

    585

    诗画题三絶
    　　高文简公一日与客游西湖见素屏洁雅乘兴画竒石古木数日后文敏公为补丛竹后为户部杨侍郎所得虞文靖公题诗其上云不见湖州三百年高公尚书生古燕西湖醉归写古木吴兴为补幽篁妍国朝名笔谁第一尚书醉后妙无敌老蛟欲起风雨来星堕天河化为石赵公自是真天人独与尚书情最亲高懐古谊两相得惨澹醻酢皆天真侍郎得此自京国使我观之三叹息今人何必非古人沦落文章付陈迹此图遂成三絶矣

    Explanation

    585

    Three Perfections in Poetry, Painting, and Calligraphy

    One day, Gao Qian, the High Official of Simplicity, accompanied guests to West Lake and saw a clean and elegant screen. Inspired, he painted a strange stone and ancient trees. A few days later, Wang Meng, the Sensitive Official, added a bamboo grove. Later, the painting was acquired by Yang Weizheng, the Deputy Minister of the Ministry of Finance. Yu Wenjing, the Poet of the Tranquil Government, wrote a poem on the painting, which reads:

    Not seen in Huzhou for three hundred years,
    Gao Qian, the High Official of Simplicity, was born in ancient Yan.
    West Lake, drunk, returned to paint ancient trees,
    Wuxing added elegant bamboo groves.
    In our dynasty, who is the first master of brush and ink?
    The High Official, drunk, is unmatched in skill.
    An old dragon wants to rise, bringing wind and rain,
    Stars fall from the Milky Way and turn into stones.
    Zhao Mengfu is truly a heavenly person,
    Unique in his affinity with the High Official.
    Both share a deep appreciation for ancient friendship,
    Their efforts are sincere and natural.
    The Deputy Minister obtained this from the capital,
    I, too, sigh three times upon seeing it.
    Why should we not admire the ancients?
    Great works of literature and art can become forgotten or lost.
    This painting thus becomes a masterpiece of the three perfections.

    586

    浙西园苑
    　　浙江园苑之胜惟松江下砂瞿氏为最古宋秀州守方岳亦有诗留题壁间后紫阳虚谷翁来游继题十絶其一云壁间墨客扫龙蛇所写诗佳字亦佳忽见一诗増感慨吾家宗伯老秋厓是也次则平江福山之曹横泽之顾又其次则嘉兴魏塘之陈当爱山全盛时春二三月间游人如织及其卒未及数月花木一空废弛之速未有若此者自后其地吴氏之园曰竹荘盖元有池陂数十畆天然若湖莹之尝买得水殿图据图位置构亭水心潇洒莫比哗訏之徒欲闻诸官亟塑三教像于中易曰三教堂人不可得而入矣莹之卒荐遭兵燹今无一存者福山横泽下砂皆无有乆矣可胜叹哉
    　　吴江长桥
    　　吴江长桥七十二间作桥者僧从雅师立总其役崇敬率众以给其费居士姚行独任劳以终事经始于泰定乙丒二月期年而成后九年州守徳济哈雅作钜阁奉观音像于上

    586

    The most ancient garden in the West Lake region is the one belonging to the Qú family in Xia Sha, Songjiang. The Song dynasty official from Xiu Prefecture, Fang Yue, also left a poem inscribed on the wall. Later, the purple Yang Xuyu came to visit and wrote ten absolute verses, one of which says: "On the wall, the ink guest sweeps like dragons and snakes, the poem is good, and the characters are also good. Suddenly seeing a poem increases one's feelings of reflection. My family's Zongbo, the old Autumn Valley, is he." Next is the garden of Cao in Fu Mountain, Pingjiang, and Gu in Hengze. Following that, there is the garden of Chen in Weitang, Jiaxing, which was at its peak during the spring months of February and March, when visitors were like a tapestry. However, after his death, within a few months, the flowers and trees were all gone, and the decline was rapid, unmatched in speed. Afterward, the land where the garden stood became the Bamboo Garden of the Wu family, which had a natural lake-like pond with dozens of acres. Ying Zhi once bought a blueprint of a water pavilion and constructed a pavilion in the center of the water, which was elegant and unmatched. But the noisy crowd wanted to hear from the officials and quickly erected statues of the Three Teachings in the middle, calling it the Three Teachings Temple, which no one could enter. After Ying Zhi's death, the garden suffered from war and destruction, and now nothing remains. Fu Mountain, Hengze, and Xia Sha have all been gone for a long time, how can one not be moved to sigh?

    The Long Bridge in Wujiang has seventy-two spans. The bridge was built by the monk Sòng Yǎ, who supervised the construction. Chóngjìng led the people to provide the funds, and the layman Yáo Xíng alone took on the labor to complete the project. It began in the second month of the year Yǐwù in the Taizheng era and was completed within a year. Nine years later, the governor Dédì Hāyǎ built a large pavilion to enshrine a statue of Guanyin on top.

    587

    辍耕録卷二十六
    <子部,小说家类,杂事之属,辍耕录>
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷二十七　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰四位配享封爵
    　　顔子唐宗太极元年壬子二月赠太子太师配享孔子庙宋真宗大中祥符二年己酉四月封兖国公
    　　曽子同前赠太子太保配享孔子庙宋理宗咸淳三年癸卯二月封郕国公配食大成殿
    　　子思宋度宗咸淳三年丁卯二月封沂国公配食大成殿
    　　孟子宋神宗元丰七年甲子五月追封邹国公配享先圣位次兖国公下
    　　宋黄震云徃嵗顔孟配享并列先圣左近升曽子子思又并列先圣左而虚其右不以相向震闻太学博士陆鹏举云初制顔孟配享左顔而右孟熈丰新经盛行以王安石为圣人没而跻之配享位顔子下故左则顔子及安石右则孟子未几安石女壻蔡卞当国谓安石不当在孟子下迁安石于右与顔子对而移孟子位第三次顔子之下遂左列顔孟而右列安石又未几蔡卞再欲升安石压顔子渐次而升为代先圣张本优人有以艺谏

    Explanation

    587

    Record of Pausing the Plow, Volume Twenty-Six
    <Zi Bu, Xiaoshuo Jia Le, Za Shi Zhi Shu, Chuo Geng Lu>
    　　Qinding Si Ku Quan Shu
    　　Record of Pausing the Plow, Volume Twenty-Seven　　　Yuan　Tao Zongyi　Compiled
    Four Figures Sharing in Worship and Receiving Titles
    　　Yan Hui, in the first year of Taiji (a reign title) in the Renzi month of the second month, was posthumously enfeoffed as Grand Tutor and enshrined in the Confucian temple. In the second year of Dazhong Xiangfu (a reign title) in the Jiyou month of the fourth month, he was enfeoffed as Duke of Yan.
    　　Zengzi, in the same year and month as Yan Hui, was posthumously enfeoffed as Grand Guardian and enshrined in the Confucian temple. In the third year of Xianchun (a reign title) in the Guiyou month of the second month, he was enfeoffed as Duke of Cheng.
    　　Zisi, in the third year of Xianchun (a reign title) in the Dingmao month of the second month, was enfeoffed as Duke of Yi and enshrined in the Da Cheng Hall.
    　　Mencius, in the seventh year of Yuanfeng (a reign title) in the Jiazi month of the fifth month, was posthumously enfeoffed as Duke of Zou and enshrined in the Confucian temple, below the enshrinement of Yan Hui.
    　　During the Song Dynasty, Huang Zhen said that in previous years, Yan Hui and Mencius were enshrined together near the left of the revered Confucius. Later, Zengzi and Zisi were also enshrined together on the left, but the right side was left empty, not facing each other. Huang Zhen heard from Lu Pengju, a doctor at the Imperial Academy, that initially, Yan Hui was enshrined on the left and Mencius on the right. During the Xifeng reforms, Wang Anshi was elevated to the status of a sage and enshrined in the Confucian temple, below Yan Hui. Therefore, on the left were Yan Hui and Wang Anshi, and on the right was Mencius. Not long after, Cai Bian, Wang Anshi's son-in-law, who held power, argued that Wang Anshi should not be below Mencius, so he moved Wang Anshi to the right, opposite Yan Hui, and shifted Mencius's position. Later, Yan Hui was placed on the left, and Mencius and Wang Anshi were placed on the right. Not long after, Cai Bian again tried to elevate Wang Anshi above Yan Hui, gradually moving him to the position of replacing Confucius, and professional performers advised through art.

    588

    于殿下者设一大言之士戯薄先圣顔子出争之不胜子贡出争之不胜子路出而盛气争之又不胜然后设为公冶长有系其首而叱之曰汝何不出一争且看他人家女壻盖蔡卞安石壻而公冶长先圣壻也蔡卞闻之遂不敢进安石于顔子上顔孟左而安石右遂为定制南渡后安石罢配享宜迁孟子以对顔子如旧制议者失于讨论故安石既去其右遂虚而顔孟并列于左岳珂尝记其事近嵗増曽子子思又并列于左亦未有讨论者
    　　金果
    　　成都府江渎庙前有树六株世传自汉唐以来即有之其树高可五六十丈围约三四寻挺直如矢无他柯榦顶上才生枝叶若防榈状皮如龙鳞叶如凤尾实如而加大每嵗仲冬有司具牲馔祭毕然后采摘金鼓仪卫迎入公差防医工以刀逐个劙去青皮石灰汤焯过入熬熟冷浸五七日漉起控干再换熟如此三四次却入瓶缶封贮进献不如此修制则生澁不可食泉州万年三株识者谓即四川金果也畨中名为苦鲁麻盖凤尾蕉也

    Explanation

    588

    At the palace, a bold and eloquent scholar was set up to mock the sage Confucius. Yan Hui went out to argue but was not successful, Zi Gong went out to argue but was not successful, and Zilu went out with great indignation to argue and was still not successful. Then, it was set up that Gongye Chang was tied by the head and scolded, "Why don't you come out and argue and see the son-in-law of others?" because Cai Bian is the son-in-law of Wang Anshi, while Gongye Chang is the son-in-law of the sage. Cai Bian heard about it and then dared not place Wang Anshi above Yan Hui. Wang Anshi was placed to the left of Yan and Meng, while Wang Anshi was placed to the right, and this became a fixed rule. After the Southern Crossing, Wang Anshi was removed from the list of those who share in the worship, and it was appropriate to move Mencius to face Yan Hui as before the old system. The discussants failed in their deliberation, so after Wang Anshi left his right position became empty, and Yan and Meng were placed side by side on the left. Yue Ke once recorded this matter. Recently, Zengzi and Zisi were added and placed side by side on the left, but there has also been no one to discuss this.

    Gold Fruit

    In front of the Jiangdu Temple in Chengdu Prefecture, there are six trees. It is said that they have existed since the Han and Tang dynasties. The trees are about fifty to sixty zhang tall, with a circumference of about three to four xun. They are straight like arrows, with no other branches or trunks. Branches and leaves grow only at the top, looking like palmetto palms. The bark is like dragon scales, and the leaves are like phoenix tails. The fruit is like  but larger. Every mid-winter, the officials prepare sacrificial food and complete the sacrifice. Then they pick and welcome them into the palace with drums and guards. Skilled workers use knives to carefully remove the green skin from each one, blanch them in lime water, put them into boiling water, then cold water, and soak for five to seven days. They filter and drain them, change the hot water again, and do this three or four times. Then they put them into jars, seal them, and present them. If they are not processed this way, they are raw and astringent and cannot be eaten. In Quanzhou, there are three  trees. Those who know say they are the same as the gold fruit of Sichuan. In foreign lands, they are called bitter and rough , and they are actually the frangipani.

    589

    李哥贞烈
    　　河南理幕沈易云灞州倡女李哥年十二三时母教之歌舞哥泣曰女率有工繄我独为此乎母告以业不可废哥曰若此听母母亦当从我好否则有死而已母阳许之因是不粉泽不茹荤所歌多仙曲道情有召者必先询主客姓名然后徃人亦预相戒毋戯狎哥凝立筵前酒行歌阕目不流盼与之酒弗饮州判官尝忤哥径还誓不与见孟津县达噜噶齐厚赂哥母夜抵舍哥懐利刃闭卧内骂之曰汝职在牧民而狗之不若可急去不且血污吾刃矣惭怒以囘明日知州闻之叹曰州有贞女而吾不知是一失也吾次子明经举秀才真若配以礼聘娶之未几红巾入防夫妇被执见哥妍丽将杀其夫哥走前抱夫项大呼曰吾断不从汝求活防并杀之

    Explanation

    589

    Li Go's Loyalty and Heroism

    In Henan, the legal officer Shen Yi recorded that Li Go, a courtesan from Ba Prefecture, was around twelve or thirteen years old when her mother taught her to sing and dance. Li Go cried and said, "Is it that only I am to do this?" Her mother told her that the business could not be abandoned. Li Go said, "If it is like this, listen to me. My mother will also follow my wishes. Otherwise, I will die." Her mother pretended to agree. As a result, Li Go did not use cosmetics, did not eat meat, and sang mostly celestial songs and Daoist tunes. When invited, she would first ask about the names of the host and guests before going. People also warned each other not to be frivolous with her. She stood solemnly at the banquet, and when the wine was served and the songs ended, her eyes did not wander, and she did not drink the wine.

    The state judge once offended Li Go and left directly, vowing not to see her again. The magistrate of Mengjin County, Daru Gaqi, offered a large bribe to Li Go's mother. At night, he arrived at the house. Li Go held a sharp blade and closed herself in the bedroom, cursing him: "Your duty is to govern the people, but you are no better than a dog. If you do not leave quickly, my blade will be stained with blood." He left in shame and anger. The next day, the governor heard about it and sighed: "There is a chaste woman in the state, and I did not know about her. This is a mistake. My second son, who has passed the Mingjing examination and is a Xiucai, is truly a match for her. Let us propose marriage with a formal ceremony and wed them."

    A short time later, the Red Turbans invaded. The couple was captured. Seeing Li Go's beauty, they intended to kill her husband. Li Go ran forward, hugged her husband's neck, and shouted: "I will never submit to you. If you want to kill him, kill me too." They killed both of them.

    590

    刘节妇
    　　刘节妇冀之衡水人通古文孝经小学书适同郡曹泰财红巾防河朔因避兵聊城村贼掩至大掠见节妇居羣人中特妍整持刀驱之行节妇曰吾妇人惟知从夫而已不从贼也贼欲移其心乃盛陈金玉珠玑仍用锦绣衣服被节妇身节妇裂碎之强拥上马堕地者数四贼怒绳其项就马上曵之节妇以手地以头触石流血骂贼不絶声遂遇害

    Explanation

    590

    Liu Jiefu
    Liu Jiefu, a woman from the Hebei region of the state of Ji, was well-versed in the Classic of Filial Piety, small learning (basic education), and ancient writings. She happened to be in the same prefecture as Cao Tai, who was affected by the Red Turban Rebellion in the Hebei region. To avoid the war, she fled to a village in Liaocheng. When bandits suddenly attacked and plundered the village, they saw Liu Jiefu among the crowd, standing out for her beauty and composure. She held a knife and drove them away, saying, "As a woman, I only know to follow my husband; I will not follow the bandits." The bandits tried to change her mind by displaying gold, jade, pearls, and gemstones, and dressing her in brocade and fine clothes. Liu Jiefu tore the treasures to pieces and, when they forcibly pushed her onto a horse, she fell to the ground several times. Enraged, the bandits tied her neck and dragged her on the horse. She used her hands to brace herself and her head to strike the ground, bleeding and cursing the bandits without ceasing. She was ultimately killed.

    591

    病洁
    　　毘陵倪元镇有洁病一日眷歌姬赵买儿留宿别业中心疑其不洁俾之浴既登榻以手自项至踵且扪且嗅扪至隂有气复俾浴凡再三东方既白不复作巫山之梦徒赠以金赵或自谈必至絶倒
    　　杂剧曲名
    　　稗官废而传竒作传竒作而戏曲继金季国初乐府犹宋词之流传竒犹宋戏曲之变世传谓之杂剧金章宗时董解元所编西厢记世代未逺尚罕有人能解之者况今杂剧中曲调之冗乎因取诸曲名分调类编以备后来好事稽古者之一览云
    　　正宫
    　　端正好　　衮绣毬　　倘秀才　　脱布衫小梁州　　朝天子　　四换头　　十二月尧民歌　　收尾　　　叨叨令　　醉太平呆古朶　　笑和尚　　蛮姑儿　　伴读书剔银灯　　道和　　　柳青娘　　双鸳鸯
    　　摊破满庭芳　　　　　月照庭　　塞鸿秋
    　　白子【中吕出入】　　　　　快活三【中吕出入】

    Explanation

    591

    Translation

    Obsession with Cleanliness
    Ni Yuanzhen from Pi Ling had an obsession with cleanliness. One day, he kept the courtesan Zhao Mai'er at his secondary residence and suspected she was not clean. He ordered her to bathe. After she had already climbed onto the bed, he touched and smelled her from her neck to her feet. When he reached her private parts, he detected an unpleasant odor and ordered her to bathe again. This process was repeated several times until dawn. He no longer dreamed of the mountains of Wu and only gave her money. When Zhao talked about herself, it often led to laughter.

    Names of Operatic Melodies
    The decline of official histories led to the rise of popular stories, and the evolution of these stories eventually gave way to the development of drama. During the late Jin Dynasty and the early Yuan Dynasty, operatic works continued to evolve, similar to the transmission of Song lyrics. The changes in Song operas were passed down, and they were called "miscellaneous plays." During the reign of Emperor Zhangzong, Dong Jieyuan compiled "The Western Chamber," which was not far removed from the contemporary era and was still rarely understood by people. How much more so, then, for the redundancy of melodies in today's operas! Therefore, I have compiled the names of various melodies, categorized by their modes, to provide a reference for future scholars and enthusiasts.

    Palace Mode

    • Duanzhenghao (端正好)
    • Guxiuqiu (衮绣毬)
    • Tangxiucai (倘秀才)
    • Tuobushan Xiaoliangzhou (脱布衫小梁州)
    • Chaotianzi (朝天子)
    • Sihuantou (四换头)
    • Shieryue Yaominge (十二月尧民歌)
    • Shouwei (收尾)
    • Daodao Ling (叨叨令)
    • Zuitaiping Daiguduo (醉太平呆古朶)
    • Xiaohanfang (笑和尚)
    • Manguge (蛮姑儿)
    • Bantushu Tiyin Deng (伴读书剔银灯)
    • Daohe (道和)
    • Liuqingniang (柳青娘)
    • Shuangyuanjiang (双鸳鸯)
    • Tanhua Mantingfang (摊破满庭芳)
    • Yuezhaoting (月照庭)
    • Saigongqiu (塞鸿秋)
    • Baiyizi (白子) [Zhonglü Chuxing]
    • Kuaihuosan (快活三) [Zhonglü Chuxing]

    黄钟

    592

    Final Translation

    双调
    　　新水令　　驻马听　　甜水令　　折桂令落梅风　　沉醉东风　小将军　　清江引碧玉箫　　鴈儿落　　徳胜令　　乔牌儿挂玉钩　　川拨棹　　殿前欢　　七弟兄梅花酒　　收江南　　水仙子　　滴滴金鸳鸯煞　　歩歩娇　　搅筝琶　　豆叶黄风入松　　拨不断　　庆东原　　沽美酒太平令　　一锭银　　荆湘怨　　阿纳忽夜行船　　镇江囘【中吕出入】胡十八　　挂玉钩序伍供养　　行香子　　梧桐树　　离亭宴煞
