    Explanation

    594

    Double Section

    New Water Command, Halt and Listen, Sweet Water Command, Break the Osmanthus Command, Plum Blossom Wind, Drunk by the East Wind, Little General, Clear River Introduction, Jade Flute, Goose Falls, Victory Command, Qiao Pai'er, Hang the Jade Hook, Sichuan Rowing, Before the Palace, Seven Brothers, Plum Blossom Wine, Collect Jiangnan, Water Fairy, Drop by Drop Gold, Mandarin Duck Sash, Step by Step Beauty, Stir the Zither and Paddle, Bean Leaf Yellow, Wind Enters Pine, Pull Continuously, Celebrate Dongyuan, Buy Fine Wine, Peace Command, One Tael of Silver, Jingxiang Sorrow, Anah, Night Boat, Return to Zhenjiang [Zhonglv Entry and Exit], Hu Eighteen, Hang the Jade Hook Sequence, Wu Nourishment, Burn Incense, Wutong Tree, Farewell Banquet Sash
    Mandarin Duck Child Sash Tail, Peace Song, Ten Drumsticks, Little Wife and Child, Hang the Lantern, Qiao Mu Cha, Butterfly Loves Flower, Celebrate Xuanhe, Zao Qing Tune, Stone Bamboo, Mountain Pomegranate, Mountain Lily, Drunken Maiden, Prince Returns to Court, Grand Entrance, Carve the Gu, Do Not Kneel, Joyful Heart, Hutu Bai, Tang Wudi [Zhonglv Entry and Exit]

    595

    燕南芝庵先生唱论
    　　古之善唱者三人
    　　韩秦娥　　　沈古之　　　石存符
    　　帝王知音者五人
    　　唐宗　　　后唐庄宗　　南唐后主
    　　宋徽宗　　　金章宗
    　　三教所尚
    　　道家唱情　　僧家唱性　　儒家唱理
    　　近世所谓大曲
    　　苏小小蝶恋花　　　　邓千江望海潮
    　　苏东坡念奴娇　　　　辛稼轩摸鱼子
    　　晏叔原鹧鸪天　　　　柳耆卿雨霖铃
    　　吴彦高春草碧　　　　朱淑真生查子
    　　蔡伯坚石州慢　　　　张子野天仙子
    　　歌之格调
    　　抑顿挫　　顶叠垜换　　萦纡牵结
    　　敦拖呜咽　　推题九转　　揺欠遏透
    　　歌之节奏
    　　停声　　待拍　　偷吹　　拽棒　　字真句笃　　依腔　　贴调
    　　凡歌一声声有四节
    　　起末　　过度　　揾簮　　攧落
    　　凡歌一句句有声韵
    　　一声平一声背一声圆　　声要圆熟腔要彻满

    Explanation

    595

    The Singing Theory of Mr. Zhi'an from Yan Nan

    Ancient Masters of Singing

    Three ancient masters of singing:

    • Han Qin'e
    • Shen Guzhi
    • Shi Cunfu

    Emperors Known for Musical Appreciation

    Five emperors known for their musical appreciation:

    • Emperor Xuanzong of Tang
    • Emperor Zhuangzong of Later Tang
    • Emperor of Southern Tang
    • Emperor Huizong of Song
    • Emperor Zhangzong of Jin

    The Three Teachings and Their Emphases

    The three teachings and their respective emphases:

    • Taoism emphasizes emotion
    • Buddhism emphasizes nature
    • Confucianism emphasizes reason

    Famous Songs from Recent Times

    Famous songs from recent times:

    • Su Xiaoxiao's "Butterfly Lovers"
    • Deng Qianjiang's "Looking at the Tide"
    • Su Dongpo's "Nian Nujiao"
    • Xin Qiji's "Mofishzi"
    • Yan Shuyuan's "Zhanggutian"
    • Liu Qijie's "Rain on the Bell"
    • Wu Yan Gao's "Spring Grass Blue"
    • Zhu Shuzhen's "Shengchazi"
    • Cai Bojian's "Shizhouman"
    • Zhang Ziyi's "Tianxianzi"

    The Style and Rhythm of Singing

    The style and rhythm of singing:

    • Suppression, elevation, pause, and change
    • Overlap, repetition, and transformation
    • Winding, entanglement, and dragging
    • Muffled, choked, and penetrating
    • Nine turns and wobbling
    • Shaking and holding

    The Rhythm and Timing of Singing

    The rhythm and timing of singing:

    • Pausing
    • Waiting for the beat
    • Sneaky blowing
    • Pulling the stick
    • Clear pronunciation and firm sentences
    • Following the melody
    • Adapting to the tune

    The Four Parts of Each Note in Singing

    The four parts of each note in singing:

    • Beginning and end
    • Transition
    • Wiping the hairpin
    • Dropping

    The Sound and Rhythm of Each Line in Singing

    The sound and rhythm of each line in singing:

    • One level, one back, one round
    • The sound must be round and smooth, the tone must be thorough and full
    596

    凡一曲中各有其声
    　　变声　　敦声　　抗声　　啀声　　困声
    　　三过声
    　　偷气　　取气　　换气　　歇气　　就气爱者有一口气
    　　歌声变件
    　　三台　　破子　　遍子　　攧落　　实催全篇　　尾声　　赚煞　　随煞　　隔煞羯煞　　本调煞　拐子煞　三煞　　十煞
    　　唱曲门户
    　　小唱　　寸唱　　慢唱　　坛唱　　歩虚道情　　撒链　　带烦　　瓢呌
    　　唱曲题目
    　　曲情　　鐡骑　　故事　　采莲　　击壤叩角　　结席　　添夀　　宫词　　采词花词　　汤词　　酒词　　灯词　　江景雪景　　夏景　　冬景　　秋景　　春景凯歌　　櫂歌　　渔歌　　挽歌　　楚歌杵歌
    　　歌之所
    　　桃花扇　　竹叶尊　　柳枝词　　桃叶怨尧民鼓腹　壮士击节　牛童马仆　闾阎女子天涯游客　洞里仙人　闺中怨女　江边商妇塲上少年　阛阓优伶　华屋兰堂　衣冠文防小楼狭阁　月馆风亭　雨窓雪屋　柳外花前

    596

    In a single musical piece, there are various types of sounds:

    • Changing sounds (变声)
    • Deep sounds (敦声)
    • Loud sounds (抗声)
    • Harsh sounds (啀声)
    • Weak sounds (困声)
    • Three consecutive passes of sound (三过声)
    • Quick breaths (偷气)
    • Gathering breath (取气)
    • Changing or taking breath (换气)
    • Resting the breath (歇气)
    • A specific breathing technique (就气爱者有一口气)
    • Variations in singing (歌声变件)
    • Three platforms (三台)
    • Segments or parts of a song (破子, 遍子)
    • A sudden drop or fall in pitch (攧落)
    • A specific technique or structure (实催全篇)
    • The ending or conclusion of a song (尾声)
    • Specific techniques or effects (赚煞, 随煞, 隔煞, 羯煞, 本调煞, 拐子煞, 三煞, 十煞)
    • Different styles or schools of singing (唱曲门户)
    • Light or delicate style of singing (小唱)
    • Short or brief style of singing (寸唱)
    • Slow or deliberate style of singing (慢唱)
    • Specific style or context of singing (坛唱, 步虚道情, 撒链, 带烦, 瓢呌)
    • Different themes or subjects of songs (唱曲题目)
    • The emotional content or mood of a song (曲情)
    • Narrative or story-based song (鐡骑, 故事)
    • Specific themes or subjects of a song (采莲, 击壤叩角, 结席, 添夀, 宫词, 采词, 花词, 汤词, 酒词, 灯词, 江景, 雪景, 夏景, 冬景, 秋景, 春景, 凯歌, 櫂歌, 渔歌, 挽歌, 楚歌, 杵歌)
    • Different settings or contexts for singing (歌之所)
    • Specific settings or contexts for singing (桃花扇, 竹叶尊, 柳枝词, 桃叶怨, 尧民鼓腹, 壮士击节, 牛童马仆, 闾阎女子, 天涯游客, 洞里仙人, 闺中怨女, 江边商妇, 塲上少年, 阛阓优伶, 华屋兰堂, 衣冠文防, 小楼狭阁, 月馆风亭, 雨窓雪屋, 柳外花前)
    597

    凡声音各应律吕分六宫十一调共十七宫调仙吕宫唱清新緜邈　　南吕宫唱感叹伤悲中吕宫唱高下闪赚　　黄钟宫唱富贵緜正宫唱惆怅雄壮　　　道宫唱飘逸清幽大石唱风流蕴借　　　小石唱旖旎妩媚高平唱条物滉漾　　　般渉唱拾掇坑堑歇指唱急并虚歇　　　商角唱悲伤宛转双调唱健栖激袅　　　商调唱凄怆怨慕角调唱呜咽悠　　　宫调唱典雅沉重越调唱陶写泠笑
    　　有子母调有姑舅兄弟有字多声少有声少字多所谓一串骊珠也比如仙吕防绛唇大石青杏儿人唤作杀唱的刽子
    　　有爱唱的有学唱的有能唱的有防唱的有高不揭低不咽有排字儿打截儿放指儿唱意儿有明掯儿暗掯儿长掯儿短掯儿碎掯儿
    　　有一曲入数调者如啄木儿女冠子抛毬乐鬭鹌鹑黄莺儿金盏儿之类是也

    Explanation

    597

    In all musical compositions, each note corresponds to the ancient pitch standards, divided into six palaces and eleven modes, totaling seventeen modes. The Xianlü mode sings with a fresh and distant, ethereal style; the Nanlü mode expresses sorrow and sadness; the Zhonglü mode fluctuates between high and low pitches; the Huangzhong mode conveys richness and luxury; the Zhenggong mode is melancholic and heroic; the Daogong mode is free-spirited and serene; the Dashi mode is elegant and refined; the Xiaoshi mode is graceful and charming; the Gaoping mode is flowing and undulating; the Banshe mode involves picking up and arranging things; the Xiezhi mode is quick and with pauses; the Shangjiao mode is sorrowful and winding; the Shuangdiao mode is lively and vigorous; the Shangdiao mode is sorrowful and resentful; the Jiaodiao mode is sobbing and lingering; the Gongdiao mode is elegant and weighty; the Yuediao mode is soothing and cool laughter.

    There are mother and child tunes, as well as brother and sister tunes. Some have many words and few sounds, while others have few words and many sounds. This is what is called a string of pearls. For example, the Xianlü Fang Jiangchun, Dashi Qing Xing'er, and others are known as the "executioner of singing."

    There are those who love to sing, those who learn to sing, those who can sing, those who guard the singing, those who sing high without revealing, those who sing low without swallowing, those who arrange the words, those who cut off the phrases, those who release the fingers, those who sing with meaning, those who are openly restrained, those who are secretly restrained, those who are long-restrained, those who are short-restrained, and those who are finely restrained.

    There are also songs that fit into multiple modes, such as Zuo Mu, Nü Guanzi, Paoqiu Le, Dou Anchen, Huangying'er, Jinzhan'er, and others.

    598

    凡唱曲有地所
    　　东平唱木兰花慢　　　大名唱摸鱼子
    　　南京唱生查子　　　　彰徳唱木斛沙
    　　陜西唱阳关三叠黒漆弩
    　　凡唱所忌
    　　子弟不唱作家歌　　　浪子不唱及时曲
    　　男不唱艶词　　　　　女不唱雄曲
    　　南人不唱　　　　　　北人不歌
    　　凡人声音不等各有所长有川嗓有堂声皆合破箫管有唱得雄壮的失之村沙唱得蕴拽的失之乜斜唱得轻巧的失之寒贱唱得本分的失之老实唱得用意的失之穿凿唱得打稻的失之本调
    　　凡唱节病有困的灰的涎的呌的大的有乐官声撒钱声拽锯声猫呌声不入耳不着人不彻腔不入调工夫少徧数少歩力少官塲少字様讹文理差无丛林无传授嗓抝劣调落架漏气
    　　凡唱声病
    　　散散　　焦焦　　干干　　冽冽　　哑哑　　尖尖　　低低　　雌雌　　雄雄短短　　憨憨　　浊浊　　赸赸　　格嗓囊鼻　　揺头　　歪口　　合眼　　张口撮唇　　撇口　　昻头　　咳

    Explanation

    598

    Translation

    Regional Origins of Songs

    Songs have regional origins:

    • In Dongping, they sing "Mu Lan Hua Man" (The Slowly Spreading Wood Orchid).
    • In Daming, they sing "Mo Yu Zi" (Fisherman's Song).
    • In Nanjing, they sing "Sheng Cha Zi" (Life of the Scholar).
    • In Zhangde, they sing "Mu Hu Sha" (Wooden Bucket Sand).
    • In Shaanxi, they sing "Yangguan San Die Hei Qi Nu" (Three Stanzas of Yangguan with Black Lacquer Bow).

    Taboos in Singing

    • Young people should not sing songs composed by their own family.
    • Carefree young men should not sing songs about current events.
    • Men should not sing love songs.
    • Women should not sing martial or heroic songs.
    • People from the south should not sing what people from the north sing, and vice versa.

    Diversity of Voices and Their Strengths

    Human voices vary greatly, each having its own strengths:

    • Some have a flowing voice (川嗓), others have a grand voice (堂声), all suitable for breaking through the pipes of flutes and pipes.
    • Those who sing with great strength may appear rustic; those who sing with depth may appear awkward; those who sing lightly may appear humble; those who sing modestly may appear honest; those who sing with intent may appear forced; those who sing with vigor may lose their original tune.

    Vocal Defects

    There are various vocal defects:

    • There are those who sing in a weak and gray voice, those who sing with saliva, those who shout, those who sing loudly, and those with an official's voice, those who sing like throwing money, those who sing like dragging a saw, those who sing like a cat meowing, those who are not pleasant to the ear, those who do not touch the listener, those who do not complete the melody, those who do not fit the tune, those with little effort, those with few repetitions, those with little strength, those with few official performances, those with incorrect characters, those with poor reasoning, those without a school, those without a teacher, those with a crooked voice, those with a fallen tone, those with a leaking breath.

    Vocal Diseases

    Vocal diseases include:

    • Loose, dry, cold, hoarse, dull, high-pitched, low-pitched, feminine, masculine, short, simple, muddy, sharp, nasal, shaking head, crooked mouth, closed eyes, open mouth and pursed lips, turned mouth, raised head, coughing.
    599

    凡添字病
    　　则他　　兀那　　是他家　俺子道　我不见兀的　　不呢　　一条弓　唇撒了　一片子团圞子　茄子了
    　　大忌郑卫之淫声续雅乐之后丝不如竹竹不如肉以其近之也又云取来歌里唱胜向笛中吹
    　　成文章曰乐府有尾声曰套数时行小令曰叶儿套数当有乐府气味乐府不可似套数
    　　词山曲海千生万熟三千小令四十大曲
    　　庄蓼塘藏书
    　　庄蓼塘住松江府上海县青龙镇尝为宋秘书小史其家蓄书数万卷且多手抄者经史子集山经地志医卜方伎稗官小説靡所不具书目以甲乙分十门蓼塘既没子孙不知保惜或为虫防蚀囓或为隣识盗窃或供饮博之需或应糊覆之用编帙散乱所存无几至正六年朝廷开局修宋辽金三史诏求遗书有以书献者予一官江南藏书多者止三家庄其一也继命危学士朴特来取其家虑恐兵遁图防干犯禁条悉付祝融氏及收拾烬余存者又无几矣其孙羣玉悉载入京觊领恩泽宿畱日乆仍布衣而归书之不幸如此

    Explanation

    599

    Final Translation

    Common Errors in Writing

    • Adding superfluous words is a common mistake in writing, which can disrupt the clarity and conciseness of the text.
    • But (则他), that (兀那), his family's (是他家), I say (俺子道), I don't see it (我不见兀的), yes (不呢), a bow (一条弓), loosened (唇撒了), a round shape (一片子团圞子), softened (茄子了).

    Avoiding Licentious Music After Sacred Music

    • It is a great taboo to follow the licentious music of Zheng and Wei after the sacred music of the court. The human voice is superior to both string and wind instruments, as it is closer to the ideal of musical expression. It is said that singing a song is more powerful than playing it on a flute.

    The Origin of Literary Works

    • Writings known as Yuefu have a concluding section called the wailüe. Popular short lyrics or ballads of the time are known as yè. Yuefu should have a distinct musical quality, and it should not be confused with the more structured and formal taoshu.
    • The vast and diverse body of poetry and music, refined over time, includes thousands of short lyrics and forty major compositions.

    Zhuang Liao Tang's Library

    • Zhuang Liao Tang lived in Qinglong Town, Shanghai County, Songjiang Prefecture. He once served as a minor official in the Song Dynasty's imperial secretariat. His family collected tens of thousands of books, many of which were handwritten. The collection included works on history, philosophy, literature, geography, medicine, divination, and fiction. The books were classified into ten categories using the system of Jia and Yi.
    • After Zhuang Liao Tang's death, his descendants did not take proper care of the library. Some books were damaged by insects and pests, stolen by neighbors, used for gambling or entertainment, or repurposed for practical uses. The books became disorganized and in disarray, with very few remaining.
    • In the sixth year of the Zhizheng era (1346) of the Yuan Dynasty, the imperial government ordered the compilation of the histories of the Song, Liao, and Jin dynasties and issued a decree to collect any remaining books and manuscripts. Anyone who contributed books was rewarded with an official position. In southern China, there were only three major book collectors, and Zhuang Liao Tang was one of them.
    • Wei Xueshi Pu was appointed to collect the books from Zhuang Liao Tang's family. To avoid potential legal or military complications, the books were burned. Even the remaining ashes and remnants were few. Zhuang Liao Tang's grandson, Qunyu, transported the remaining books to the capital in hopes of receiving a reward. Despite his efforts, he returned home in his ordinary clothes, having received no reward. The fate of the books was unfortunate.
    600

    买假山
    　　陈爱山买顾氏废族石假山一所移置家园一日邀渊白观之指而谓曰此公族中之物渊白笑荅曰东搬西倒陈黙然
    　　戴氏絶嗣
    　　华亭枫泾戴君实其家巨富妻王氏妬悍无比仅有一女赘谢季初为壻君实纳一妾于嘉兴外舍得男王闻之蚤夜怒詈君实不得已遣其妾取儿以归而女恐其长大分我财产遂于襁褓中酷加陵虐致成惊疾又不容医疗竟就夭亡大为喜幸越三年自孕将产梦抱此儿及娩得男后随殒于蓐儿亦不育此妇女妬悍之报今戴氏絶嗣天道岂逺也哉事在至正十五年四月上旬也

    Explanation

    600

    Final Translation

    Buying a Rockwork

    Chen Aishan bought a rockwork from the abandoned estate of the Gu family and moved it to his home garden. One day, he invited Yuan Bai to view it. Pointing to the rockwork, he said, "This is an object from the public domain." Yuan Bai smiled and replied, "Tian moved it east and west." Chen remained silent.

    The Dai Family Becomes Extinct

    In Huating, Fengjing, Dai Junshi's family was extremely wealthy. His wife, Wang, was extremely jealous and fierce, and they had only one daughter, who was taken as a son-in-law by Xie Jichu. Dai Junshi took a concubine who gave birth to a son in Jiaxing. Wang, upon hearing about it, angrily cursed him day and night. Reluctantly, Dai Junshi sent the concubine back with the child. The daughter feared that the child would grow up and divide her inheritance, so she severely abused and mistreated the child in his infancy, causing him to develop a convulsive illness. She refused to allow medical treatment, and the child eventually died young. Wang greatly rejoiced and felt fortunate. After three years, she became pregnant and was about to give birth. She dreamed of holding this child. When she gave birth to a boy, he died shortly after birth. She also became unable to bear children. This was the retribution for a woman's jealousy and fierceness. Now the Dai family has become extinct. Isn't the way of heaven far away? The events occurred in the early part of April, 1355.

    601

    妓妾守志
    　　汪佛奴歌儿也姿色秀丽嘉兴富户濮乐闲以中统钞一千锭娶为妾一日桂花盛开濮置酒佛奴奉觞濮有感于中澘然堕泪佛奴请问其故濮曰吾老矣非乆于人世者汝宜善事后人佛奴亦泣下誓无贰志人莫之信既而濮果死佛奴独居尼寺深藏简出操行洁白以终其身
    　　讥巴延太师
    　　重纪至元间太师丞相巴延专权蠧政贪恶无比以罪左迁南恩州达噜噶齐至隆兴卒寄棺驿舍滑稽者题于壁云百千万定犹嫌少垜积金银北斗边可惜太师无运智不将些子到黄泉
    　　讥方士
    　　丙子岁松江亢旱闻方士沈雷伯道术高妙府官遣吏赍香币过嘉兴迎请以来骄傲之甚以为雨可立致结坛仙观行月孛法下鐡简于湖泖潭井日取蛇燕焚之了无应验羞赧宵遁僧柏子庭有诗其一聨云谁呼蓬岛青头鸭来杀松江赤縺蛇闻者絶倒燕都赋

    Explanation

    601

    A Concubine Who Remained Loyal to Her Vows

    Wang Funu was a beautiful and graceful singing girl. A wealthy man from Jiaxing named Pu Leian married her with a thousand taels of Zhongtong currency. One day, when osmanthus flowers were in full bloom, Pu held a banquet, and Wang Funu presented a cup of wine. Moved by the scene, Pu wept involuntarily. Wang Funu asked him why he was crying, and Pu replied, "I am old now and will not be in this world for long. You should take good care of the next generation." Wang Funu also wept and vowed to remain loyal to her vows. People did not believe her. Soon after, Pu died, and Wang Funu lived alone in a nunnery, keeping herself secluded and rarely appearing in public. She maintained a pure conduct and lived a virtuous life until the end of her days.

    Satirizing Grand Tutor Bayan

    During the reign of Emperor Chengzong in the Yuan Dynasty, Grand Tutor and Chancellor Bayan wielded power and corrupted the government, being extremely greedy and evil. He was demoted to the position of Dulu Gaqi in Nanzhenzhou due to his crimes. He eventually died in Longxing, and his coffin was left in a postal station. A witty person wrote on the wall: "Even a hundred thousand units are not enough; piling up gold and silver to the edge of the Big Dipper constellation. Alas, the Grand Tutor lacks good fortune and wisdom, and he won't take even a little bit to the grave."

    Satirizing a Daoist Practitioner

    In the year Bingsi, there was a severe drought in Songjiang. It was said that the Daoist practitioner Shen Leibo had extraordinary Daoist skills. The local official sent an official with incense and gifts to pass through Jiaxing to invite him. Shen Leibo was extremely arrogant and believed that rain could be summoned instantly. He set up a ritual altar to perform a Daoist ceremony involving the Moon and the comet, casting iron tablets into lakes, marshes, and wells. He caught snakes and swallows daily and burned them, but there was no response. He fled in shame at night. The monk Ba Ziting wrote a poem, one couplet of which read: "Who called the green-headed duck from the Isles of the Immortals to kill the red-scaled snake in Songjiang?" Those who heard it were utterly amused.

    602

    檇李顾渊白恃才傲物尝入京献燕都赋翰长元公复初不喜曰今大朝四海一统六合一家燕盖昔时战国名何燕之称惭恨而归晚年始得领教岳阳高照庵先生以诗送之云豪气欲吞天下士冷官初到岳阳城切中其实渊白自出一对句云天下秀才爷有刀镊人对之曰村中和尚种
    　　裱背十三科
    　　世人但知医有十三科画有十三科殊不知裱背亦有十三科一织造绫锦绢帛一染练上件一抄造纸劄一染制上件顔色一糊料麦麪一糊药矾蜡一界尺裁版杆帖一轴头【或金或玉或石或玛瑙水晶珊瑚沉檀花梨乌木每轴上用一色所以只归一科】一糊刷一铰链一绦一经带一裁刀数内阙其一则不能成全画矣其糊刷裁尺亦皆有名糊刷防软者谓之平分防硬者谓之糊防大小得中者谓之黏合狭小者谓之寸金裁尺极等濶者曰满手次等曰三指又次等曰两指最狭者曰单指

    Explanation

    602

    Cui Yuanbai of Cui Li, known for his talent and arrogance, once traveled to the capital to present a poem about Yan City. The chancellor Yuan Gong Fuchu was displeased and said, "Now the great court has unified the four seas and the six directions as one family. Yan is the name of an ancient state during the Warring States period. What is there to be ashamed of in its name?" Cui felt disgraced and returned home. In his later years, he finally received instruction from Gao Zhaohan of Yueyang. Gao sent him a poem that said, "His bold spirit could swallow the world, but as a cold official, he first arrived in Yueyang." This poem struck the truth directly. Cui then came up with a couplet: "All the talented scholars in the world are my father, but I have scissors and shears." Someone replied, "A monk in the village plants them."

    Thirteen Categories of Mounting and Backing

    People generally know that there are thirteen categories of medicine and thirteen categories of painting, but they do not know that mounting and backing also have thirteen categories. The first category is weaving silk, brocade, and cloth. The second category is dyeing the above items. The third category is copying paper and making paper. The fourth category is dyeing the above colors. The fifth category is glue made from wheat flour. The sixth category is glue made from alum and wax. The seventh category is rulers for cutting and pasting. The eighth category is axis heads (made of gold, jade, stone, agate, crystal, coral, sandalwood, rosewood, ebony, each axis head using one color, hence it is counted as one category). The ninth category is glue brushes. The tenth category is hinges. The eleventh category is silk cords. The twelfth category is silk tapes. The thirteenth category is cutting knives. If any one of these is missing, the painting cannot be completed. The glue brushes and cutting rulers also have names. The soft ones are called even division. The hard ones are called glue protection. The ones of moderate size are called adhesion. The narrow ones are called inch gold. The widest ones are called full hand. The next widest are called three fingers. The next narrower are called two fingers. The narrowest are called single finger.

    603

    厉狄
    　　越人朱仲桓武云至正丙申岁大旱余在萧山观方士陈希微祷雨于北岭将军庙累日俄降笔云吾秦人厉狄也与项羽起事山隂虽功不竟而死然有徳于民其父老不忘我者俾血食于此尔来几千五百年世代云变遂湮我姓名至蔑焉无闻故以相告目击其事感叹弥日
    　　旗聨
    　　中原红军初起时旗上一聨云虎贲三千直抵幽燕之地龙飞九五重开大宋之天其后毛贵一贼横行山东侵犯畿甸驾幸滦京贼势猖獗无异唐末
    　　桃符防
    　　张之翰字周卿邯郸人由翰林学士除授松江知府自题桃符云云间太守过三载天下元贞第二年是岁卒亦防也

    603

    Lingdi
    In the year of Bing Shen during the reign of Emperor Wuzong, there was a severe drought in the area. I was in Xiaoshan when a Taoist named Chen Ximeng prayed for rain at the Temple of the General on the North Ridge. After several days, he wrote down: "I am Lingdi, a man from Qin. I started a rebellion with Xiang Yu in Shanyin, although I did not complete my goals and died, I had virtue for the people. The elders of my hometown still remember me and worship me with blood offerings here. It has been several thousand years since then, and through the changes of generations, my name has been forgotten. Therefore, I am telling you this. I witnessed the event and felt deep emotion for an entire day."

    Banner Couplet
    When the Red Army first rose in the Central Plains, there was a couplet on the flag: "Three thousand tiger soldiers directly advancing to the land of Youyan, the dragon flying to the fifth position, reopening the sky of the Great Song." Later, a rebel named Mao Gui wreaked havoc in Shandong, invaded the imperial capital region, and the emperor moved his court to the city of Puan. The rebel's power was rampant, not unlike the late Tang period.

    Peachwood Charm
    Zhang Zhihan, courtesy name Zhouqing, was from Handan. He was appointed as the governor of Songjiang from the position of Hanlin Scholar. He inscribed a peachwood charm himself, saying: "The governor of Yunjian has passed three years. The second year of the Yuanzhen era. In that year, he died, and it was also a tragic event."

    604

    金甲
    　　嘉定州大塲沈氏因下番买卖致巨富一日自番中还先报家信有云番船今到何处发金甲先囘金甲者碓坊甲头也后因逐一干仆仆出此书首告以为玉印未到金甲先囘沈厚赂官府得理闻者亦可为戒蔺节妇
    　　许叔瑛【璞】云陈友谅部属称邓平章者防江西某县有妇蔺氏其夫以财雄一乡因赂邓之帅某丐免剽戮帅闻蔺有殊色輙殱其家独生蔺及四岁婴将纳之蔺曰帅贵人妾事之无恨然吾良人以礼币聘妾为妇者若干年与生二子妾不忍即背恩军中礼不备请持一月丧服乃为帅妇未晚帅许之服未终移兵别县帅曰吾如汝约今夕谐吾婚乎蔺曰
    诺既而帅上马他之使二卒守蔺曰为取鸡酒具香火今夕吾为帅妇敢告先良人灵卒俱出乃先杀婴啮指血书壁曰泾渭难分浊与清此身不幸厄红巾孤儿未忍更他姓烈妇何曽嫁二人白刃自挥心似鐡黄泉欲到骨如银荒村日落猿啼处过客闻之亦惨神书罢即自刎帅返惊叹讯二卒欲罪之卒指壁间题倩人读其诗驰白邓邓闻之陈陈为立庙旌表云
    　　忠孝里

    Explanation

    604

    Gold Armor

    The Shen family from the large market in Jia Ding Prefecture became extremely wealthy through maritime trade. One day, after returning from overseas, they first sent a message home, stating that the foreign ship had arrived, and someone named "Gold Armor" had returned ahead of them. "Gold Armor" was the head of a rice mill. Later, one of their servants, upon receiving this letter, reported it first, claiming that the jade seal had not yet arrived, and "Gold Armor" had returned first. The Shen family bribed the officials to get their way, and those who heard about it could also take this as a warning.

    Lan, the Chaste Widow

    Xu Shuying (also known as Xu Pu) said: Chen Youliang had a subordinate named Deng Pingzhang, who defended a certain county in Jiangxi Province. There was a woman named Lin in his household. Her husband, who was wealthy and influential in the area, had bribed General Deng to avoid being plundered and killed. When the general heard that Lin was beautiful, he immediately slaughtered her entire family, leaving only Lin and her four-year-old infant. The general intended to take Lin as his concubine. Lin said, "The general is a noble person, and I serve him without resentment. However, my husband hired me as his wife with gifts for several years, and we had two sons together. I cannot immediately betray his kindness. The military does not have the necessary rituals. Please allow me to wear mourning clothes for a month before becoming the general's wife." The general agreed. Before the mourning period ended, the army moved to another county. The general said, "I will follow your agreement tonight for our marriage." Lin replied, "Yes."

    The general then mounted his horse and left, sending two soldiers to guard Lin. He instructed them to prepare chicken, wine, incense, and candles, and said, "Tonight I will become the general's wife, and I dare to inform my late husband's spirit." The soldiers left, and she first killed her infant and bit her finger to write blood on the wall, saying, "The clear and the turbid are hard to distinguish. This body is unfortunate to be oppressed by the Red Turbans. I cannot bear to change the name of my orphaned child. A brave woman has never married two men. I wielded the sword myself, my heart as hard as iron. I wanted to go to the netherworld, my bones as white as silver. In a desolate village, the sun sets and apes cry. Passersby hear it and feel sorrowful." After writing, she immediately committed suicide.

    The general returned, amazed, and questioned the two soldiers, wanting to punish them. The soldiers pointed to the inscription on the wall, asking someone to read the poem and report it to Deng. Deng heard about it and established a temple to honor her.

    Loyalty and Filial Piety Lane

    605

    至正壬辰秋七月红巾防钱唐九月防吴兴延陵冬十月防江隂州州大姓许晋字徳昭者有武畧善格鬭仲子如璋亦英勇遂相谋曰乌合之众败亡可待我族我里何忍坐累焉乃潜聚无頼恶少资以饮食保防隣井日有余党四散抄掠则诱使深入悉殪而埋之所居素隐僻贼无知者寻闻官军驻近郊隂遣人约为内应十一月八日浙东宣
    慰元帅观孙统兵入城晋率所募应之官军少却晋弗之知尚与贼战于城北之祥符寺前防贼党自他所来掎其后如璋遂与家僮徃救手刃数人破围而入偕父力战众寡不敌父子皆死明日官军复进攻贼遂溃家人得父子尸敛而葬之柩车相继于道见者无不堕泪乡之父老诔之曰父死于忠子死于孝私表其里曰忠孝郡上其事于朝不报
    　　胡仲彬聚众
    　　胡仲彬乃杭州勾阑中演説野史者其妹亦能之时登省官之门因得夤縁注授廵检至正十七年七月内招募游食无借之徒文其背曰赤心防国誓杀红巾八字作号将遂作乱为乃叔首告搜其书名簿得三册才以一册到官余火之亦诛三百六十余人扶箕诗

    Explanation

    605

    During the autumn of the seventh month of the year Renchen in the Zhizheng era (1352 AD), the Red Turban rebels attacked Qiantang. In September, they advanced to Wuxing and Yanling, and in October, they besieged Jiangyin. Xu Jin, a prominent local leader known by his courtesy name Dezhao, was skilled in martial arts and combat. His son, Ruozhang, was also brave and valiant. They conferred and said, "These are a disorganized group; their defeat is imminent. How can we bear to be implicated by our family and community?" They then secretly gathered worthless and wicked youths, providing them with food and shelter to protect their neighborhood. Each day, when the rebels' followers scattered to plunder, they lured them into deeper areas and killed them all, burying them. Their residence was usually hidden and secluded, so the rebels were unaware of it. Soon, they heard that government troops had stationed themselves near the outskirts and secretly sent someone to agree to be an inside force. On the eighth day of the eleventh month, the commander-in-chief of the Zhejiang Eastern region, Guan Sun, led his troops into the city. Xu Jin led the recruits to respond to the attack, and the government forces retreated slightly. Xu Jin was unaware of this and continued to fight the rebels in front of Xiangfu Temple in the north of the city. Rebel forces from another direction attacked from behind. Ruozhang then went with his servants to rescue them, killing several people and breaking through the encirclement. He entered the city with his father, fighting fiercely despite being outnumbered. Father and son both died. The next day, the government forces attacked again, and the rebels were defeated. The family found the bodies of father and son, buried them, and mourned them. The funeral carriages followed one after another on the road, and those who saw them could not help but shed tears. The elders of the village eulogized them, saying, "Father died for loyalty, and son died for filial piety." They privately named their village "Zhongxiao" (Loyalty and Filial Piety) and reported the matter to the imperial court, but it was not acknowledged.

    Hu Zhongbin gathered a crowd.

    Hu Zhongbin was a storyteller in Hangzhou's goulash (a place where performers and entertainers gathered). His sister was also talented. At that time, they frequented the doors of provincial officials and gained favor, being appointed as patrol officers. In the seventh month of the seventeenth year of the Zhizheng era (1357 AD), they recruited vagrants and beggars, inscribing the eight-character slogan "Loyal Heart to Defend the Country, Vow to Kill the Red Turbans" on their backs as a symbol of their rebellion. They were about to launch a revolt when his uncle reported them first. The authorities searched for their books and lists, finding three volumes. Only one volume was presented to the officials, and the rest were burned. They also executed over three hundred and sixty people. The divination poems (扶箕诗) were part of the broader context of the rebellion.

    606

    天遣魔军杀不平不平人杀不平人不平人杀不平者杀尽不平方太平此扶箕语验之今日果然

    　　辍耕録卷二十七
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷二十八　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰非程文
    　　各行省乡试则有人取发解进士姓名一如登科记锓梓印行以圗少利至正四年甲申江浙揭晓后乃有四六长篇题曰非程文语与抄白省榜同时版行不知何人所造而路府州县盛传之语曰设科取士深感圣世之恩倚公挟私无奈吏胥之弊岂期江浙之大省壊于禹畴之小刘【名锡眉山人当该掾史】斯文孔艰衷情痛愤待士无礼呼名散饼于路傍懐璧有谋打号贴图于墙上厨用猾吏内外之消息可通试官取贪夫上下之机关不泄阳揭题驾言无弊实自生奸宄之心覔厚赂力举还魂特欲箝是非之口五服之亲不避故违国朝之典章杂犯之

    Explanation

    606

    Heaven sends a demon army to kill the unjust. The unjust kill the unjust. Only when the unjust kill all the unjust will there be peace. This divination was verified today as true.

    The Record of Ceasing to Plow, Volume Twenty-Seven
    The Complete Library of the Four Treasuries, Imperially Approved
    The Record of Ceasing to Plow, Volume Twenty-Eight
    By Tao Zongyi of the Yuan Dynasty
    Non-Standard Texts

    In various provincial examinations, there are those who take the names of the successful candidates and publish them in books like the "List of Successful Candidates" to make a small profit. In the year 1344 (Jia Shen), after the results were announced in Jiang-Zhe, a long essay titled "Non-Standard Texts" was published simultaneously with the official lists. It is unknown who wrote it, but it was widely circulated in the prefectures, states, and counties. The text reads: "The establishment of examinations to select scholars deeply appreciates the grace of the holy age. However, it is impossible to avoid the corruption of officials and clerks. Who would have thought that the great province of Jiang-Zhe would be ruined by the small Liu Yu-Chou? [Liu Yu-Chou, a man from Mei Shan, was the clerk responsible for this.] The difficulty of scholarly pursuits is truly painful. The treatment of scholars is disrespectful, with names called out and pies distributed on the side of the road. Those who carry precious things plot to mark numbers and post them on walls. The kitchen uses cunning clerks to communicate inside and outside the exam hall. The examiners take bribes and keep the mechanisms of the exam secret. They publicly claim there is no corruption, but in reality, they harbor the hearts of villains. They seek heavy bribes to revive the dead, especially to silence the mouths of those who speak the truth. Even close relatives within five degrees of kinship do not hesitate to violate the laws of the dynasty."

    607

    卷俱抄恐失手本之名字应才【杭州】鼓勇于终塲之日局长之信已通刘环【即环翁杭州】知名于未榜之前代笔之钱尽去万户侯之关节可验丈人峯之气力何勤吕将【铅山万户吕天泽】监门进乐平之八子【许援董彛徐复邹成操琬汪緈许道傅戴用】海郎【吴县主簿海鲁丁】受卷通括苍之二林【松庆彬祖】本生之地増辉同列之情不薄黄璋【松江】称干首二三月已买试官鲍恂【嘉兴】在榜中十四名全頼妻父【建徳知事俞镇】借开元真人之力叶氏【叶瓉信州】礼经依永嘉县尹【林泉生】之门江郎兄弟【辉晃建寕

    Explanation

    607

    Fearing the loss of the original manuscript's name, Yingcai from Hangzhou encouraged courage on the final day of the examination. The director's trust had been established in Liu Huan (also known as Huangong from Hangzhou), who became known before the announcement of the results. All the money for proxy writing was gone, and the connections of a Marquis of Ten Thousand Households could verify the strength of Zhuren Peak. Why be diligent? Lü Jiang (a Marquis of Ten Thousand Households from Qianshan) introduced eight sons from LePing (Xu Yuan, Dong Yi, Xu Fu, Zou Chengcao, Wang, Wang Xie, Xu Daofu, and Dai Yong). Master Hai (the county magistrate of Wuxian, Hai Luding) received the scroll and connected with the two forests of Kuca (Song Qingbinzu). The place of origin has been enhanced, and the bonds with peers are strong. Huang Zhang from Songjiang was bought as the head official in February or March, and the fourteenth name on the list was entirely dependent on his father-in-law (Yu Zhen, the county magistrate of Jiande). Leveraging the power of the Immortal of the Kaiyuan Era, Ye Zhan from Xinzhou relied on the gate of the magistrate of Yongjia County (Lin Quansheng). The brothers of Jiang Lang (Huihuang from Jianning) were involved.

    608

    】刘大【希贤庆元】在列頼为省郎之师沈小【惟时杭州】登科谁知运吏之壻黄岩赵蔺【友蔺】得家兄【寕海丞由钦】为外之官瑞安高明托馆主有堂上之友纷纷在眼歴歴难言许瑗【饶州】作魁三百定卖几千株之木邹成【饶州】驼牓十八日纳七万户【吕天泽】之钱左者如斯右其可见尺牍先来于柏府仕宦势高槀文潜出于闱师生情宻逓手帖全凭廵绰写懐挟不避军人四子入塲代笔有此刘之手一家在牓瞒人起各路之文所谋不臧其忠何在王贺【绍兴偹牓】省中典吏不读书亦解成名李思【思齐】婺山村童未知

    Explanation

    608

    Liu Da, known as Xi Xian from Qingyuan, relied on Shen Xiao, who was from Hangzhou and had passed the imperial examinations, as his teacher. Who knew that Shen Xiao was the son-in-law of the official responsible for transportation? Huangyan Zhao Lin, whose courtesy name was You Lin, got his elder brother, who was the magistrate of Ninghai and named You Qin, appointed as an official outside the capital. Rui'an Gao Ming relied on the host of the academy to have friends among the elders. Many things are in sight but hard to describe. Xu Yuan from Raozhou made the top scorer out of three hundred and decided to sell several thousand trees. Zou Cheng from Raozhou, with the help of eighteen days, paid seven thousand households' worth of money to [Lü Tianze]. On the left, such examples are numerous; on the right, they are visible. Official documents came first to the Secretariat, where officials held high positions, and drafts were secretly written in the examination hall. The relationship between teacher and student was close, and hand-written notes were exchanged entirely through the intermediary of [Xu Chun]. Writing was done without avoiding the soldiers, and four sons entered the examination hall to write on behalf of someone else, which was done by Liu's hand. One family was listed on the bulletin board, misleading people, and writings from various regions arose. What they planned was not good, and where was their loyalty? Wang He, who was from Shaoxing and had prepared the imperial examination list, was a clerk in the provincial government who did not study but still managed to become famous. Li Si, also known as Si Qi, was a child from a village in Wuzhou who did not know.

    609

    礼焉宜中错春秋之年分临海梦龙【姓赵备牓】乱周易之隂阳平江俞鼎耳目之所及者如此心术之潜运者难知姑舍举人更陈坐主俞镇【建德知事】夤縁考试这番丰卒嵗之赀吴暾【峡州知事】买题登科方得证旧时之本麟经错乱因赂取林泉之生生何如【永嘉尹林泉生】易义驳杂以名寻夏日之孜孜安用【防稽尹夏日孜】其余泛泛不必叨叨分经考卷得便私情自开科曽无此例出院改文以欺公论虽刋板乃是讹传歴观解据之非益见文衡之缪指实告官者反罹其罪懐才抱艺者虚费其劳赵俶蒋堂空仰天而叹息江孚沈干徒

    Explanation

    609

    Ritual propriety should be appropriate, but the dates and events recorded in the Spring and Autumn Annals are often misaligned. From Linhai, there is Zhao Menglong [whose surname is Zhao], who has prepared the list, and he has disrupted the yin and yang of the Zhou Yi (Book of Changes). Yu Ding from Pingjiang, the supervisor of the examination, has such knowledge within his purview, but the subtle workings of the mind are hard to understand. Let us temporarily set aside the candidates and focus on the examiners. Yu Zhen [the magistrate of Jiande] has exploited the examination system to amass wealth for the end of the year. Wu Tun [the magistrate of Xiangzhou] has bought exam questions to pass and thus validate the original text. The Linquan (麟经) is confused and disordered, as bribes have been given to take Lin Quansheng [the governor of Yongjia] as a living example. The meaning of the Yi (Book of Changes) is mixed and confused, and one seeks the diligent efforts of summer, but what use is it? The remaining points are too vague to dwell on further. The scriptures and exam papers are divided, and personal bias opens the exam, which has never been done before. After leaving the exam, the text is altered to deceive public opinion, even though it was printed, it was still a false transmission. Throughout history, the flaws in the interpretations have been observed, and it becomes more evident that the standards of literary evaluation are flawed. Those who report the truth to the authorities are instead punished, and those with talent and skill are wasted in vain. Zhao Chu and Jiang Fu [from Jiangfu and Shen Gan] look to the sky and sigh, only to be left with nothing.

    610

    踏地以咨嗟潘伯修蔡余庆两举奚为闻梦吉陆居仁再来告免呜呼文运已矣吾道安之何等主司污滥壊今年之举既生圣世进修冀异日之公明此非一口之经陈实乃众贤之愿告有人心者念天理焉至二十二年壬寅复有作弹文云文运重开多士欢腾于此日科塲作弊丑声莫甚于今年启奸人侥幸之门贤相賔兴之意事既如此人其奈何切惟考试官实文章之司命讵宜伪定于临期员外郎执科举之权衡安可公然而受赂憸谋既遂清议难容闻人枢肤浅之学翰林懐賔主之旧情啜霭山游狭之徒座主念梓桑之宿好只因厚契便擢科名尸位

    Explanation

    610

    Stomping the ground and sighing, Pan Boxiu and Cai Yuqing were both nominated, but for what? Hearing the news that Meng Ji and Lu Juren came again to request exemption, I sigh. The literary fortune is over, where is my way to go? What kind of chief examiner has corrupted this year's examination? Having been born in a sage era, hoping for future fairness and clarity. This is not just one person's statement but the wish of many scholars. Those with human heart should consider heavenly reason. In the twenty-second year of the Renyin era, another memorial was written saying, "Literary fortune is renewed, and scholars are jubilant on this day." The disgraceful sounds of cheating in the examination hall are most severe this year. Opening the door for cheats and ruining the intention of the virtuous minister. Since things are like this, what can one do? Indeed, the examiners are the masters of literary fate; how could they falsely decide at the last moment? The vice magistrate holds the balance of the imperial examination; how can he openly accept bribes? Once the cunning plot is successful, it is hard to tolerate in public opinion. Hearing Ren Shu's shallow learning, the Hanlin Academy harbors the old feelings of the host, the narrow-minded disciples of Chui Ai Mountain, and the host remembers the old friendship of the hometown. Only because of a close relationship, they were promoted to pass the examination and occupy a position.

    611

    宪賔进乡闾之十子居丧台掾升里闬之三王沈庭珪错破书经混死生于同列战惟肃不明诗意强今古于已然朱舜民乃濒海之强梁喻宜之实许门之童子新昌庭瑞输彩叚之几缣霅水莫孜奉白金之一锭张谊罔知彖象皆徐中造就之私杨明不辨春秋拜周溥作成之赐施省宪贴书之手叚壊乡闾整肃之纲常唐肃以词赋而见收明经安在柯理以梯媒而得中对防何长舍弟致谋甚矣有心之唐溥家兄代笔嗟哉无学之郑沂靖而思之良可丑也白头钱宰感绨袍恋恋之情碧眼倪中发仓廪陈陈之粟俞潜徐鼎三月初早买试官邱民韩明五日前预知

    Explanation

    611

    The Constitutional Official recommended ten sons from the village, and during the period of mourning, the local officials promoted three kings of the village. Shen Tinggui mistakenly broke the scriptures, mixing life and death in the same rank. In battle, only the strict ones do not understand the poetic meaning, forcing the present and the past to be the same. Zhu Shunmin is a strong and powerful person by the sea. Yu Yizhi is a child of the Xu family. Xinchang Tingrui contributed several pieces of silk. Zhe water Mo Zi offered one ingot of white gold. Zhang Yi does not know the hexagrams and images, all of which were privately created by Xu Zhong. Yang Ming cannot distinguish between the Spring and Autumn Annals and pays respect to Zhou Pu for the favor bestowed upon him. Shi Shengxian pasted the book's hand section, damaging the village's proper conduct. Tang Su was recognized for his poetry and prose, where is the clear understanding of the classics? Ke Li obtained the middle position through intermediaries, plotting with his younger brother, how much more so the heart of Tang Pu, his elder brother wrote for him, alas, the unlearned Zheng Yijin, thinking about it, it is truly shameful. Old White Qian Zai felt the deep affection of the thick cloth coat, Blue-eyed Ni Zhong opened the granary with accumulated grain, Yu Qian and Xu Ding bought the examiner early in March, Qiu Min and Han Ming knew five days in advance.

    612

    题目元孚乃泉南之大贾挥金不啻于泥沙许徴实云间之富家纳粟犹同于瓦砾防頴之于陋巷余波有自于杨明超宋祀于穷途主意必资于张谊既正榜之若此则备选之可知姑舍前言更陈余意屈仲孚于受卷易经可谓失人进公甫于考文麟史大孤众望不分报赛叔通岂可与言诗缪讲进修孺子乌足以论易重载连樯之白粲始谐校艺于青藜逯信止素乏文才嗟老夫之已耄孟天暐每称好觜奈举业之乆疏大壊士风难逃舆论呜呼天之将丧斯文实系兴衰之运士欲致用于国岂期贡举之私此非一口之诬谋实乃众情之公论用书既徃以警将来

    Explanation

    612

    In the matter of Yuan Fu, a great merchant from Quannan, who spends money as easily as mud and sand, and Xu Zhengshi, a wealthy family in Yün, who treats grain contributions as if they were worthless tiles and gravel, Fang Ying's influence in a humble lane originates from Yang Mingchao. Song Si, in his destitute state, relies on Zhang Yi for support. If the main list is like this, then the reserve list can be inferred. I will set aside the previous remarks and present my own views: Qu Zhongfu's handling of the I Ching test can be called a mistake, and Gongfu's promotion in the literary examination was a disappointment to the public. Reporting on Shu Tong is not suitable for poetry, and discussing the I Ching with a young person is futile. A heavily loaded ship with white grain finally harmonizes with the art of learning. Lu Xin's lack of literary talent is lamented by the old man, and Meng Tianwei often praises good taste but finds the examination system outdated. The decline in the moral standards of scholars cannot escape public opinion. Alas, the decline of culture is tied to the rise and fall of the nation. Scholars who wish to serve the country cannot expect the examination system to be impartial. This is not a baseless accusation but a public opinion. Let this serve as a warning for the future.

    613

    于阗玉佛
    　　丞相巴延尝至于阗国于其国中凿井得一玉佛高三四尺色如截肪照之皆见筋骨脉络即贡上方又有白玉一叚高六尺阔五尺长十七歩以重不可致处士门前集赛
    　　杜清碧先生【本】应召次钱唐诸儒者争趋其门燕孟初作诗嘲之有紫藤帽子高丽靴处士门前当集赛之句闻者传以为笑用紫色防藤缚防而制靴作高丽国様皆一时所尚集赛则内府执役者之译语也宪佥案判
    　　松江府儒学直学沈伯云因花破钱粮乃与教授陈仲微有隙伯云之父曰君实者老吏也一日率婢妾詈棰仲微于途适宪佥吕公思诚分按至府具状以诉公怒其诟辱师表有伤风化勾摄赴官服辜君实年逾七旬乞以铜赎公判云既能为不能为之事正当受不当受之刑卒杖断之

    Explanation

    613

    A Jade Buddha from Khotan
    The Chancellor Ba Yan once traveled to the Kingdom of Khotan, where he dug a well and found a jade Buddha statue, about three to four feet tall, with a color like clear fat. When illuminated, one could see the muscles, bones, and veins. The statue was immediately presented to the imperial court. There was also a piece of white jade, six feet high, five feet wide, and seventeen steps long, but it was too heavy to be moved. It was placed at the door of a scholar's residence, with the term "集赛" (jí sài) inscribed on it, which likely refers to a type of interpreter or translator associated with the imperial household.

    Mr. Du Qingbi, whose name is given in brackets, was summoned to Qiantang, and scholars from all over rushed to his door. Yan Mengchu wrote a poem mocking him, which included the lines: "A purple vine hat, Koryo boots, at the scholar's door, there is a jí sài." Those who heard it passed it on as a joke. The poem described the fashion of the time, using purple vine to bind and protect the boots, made in the style of Koryo. The term "集赛" (jí sài) is the language of the translators in the imperial household. The case was judged by a high-ranking official.

    Shen Boyun, a scholar directly involved in Confucian learning in Songjiang Prefecture, wasted money and grain on flowers, leading to a dispute with Professor Chen Zhongwei. Shen Boyun's father, Junshi, an old official, led his maidservants to scold and beat Chen Zhongwei on the road. Just as Official Lu Si Cheng arrived at the prefecture, a report was prepared and submitted. The official was angry about the insults to teachers and role models, harmful to public morals. Shen Boyun was arrested and punished. Junshi, who was over seventy years old, asked to be redeemed with copper. The official ruled: "Since he could do something that should not have been done, he should receive punishment for doing something wrong." Finally, he was punished with a rod.

    614

    诗防
    　　张起字起之四明人有诗名尝作一聨云别来越树长为客看尽吴山不是家未几卒诗亦有防欤
    　　邱机山
    　　邱机山松江人宋季元初以滑稽闻于时商谜无出其右遨游湖海间尝至福州讥其秀才不识字众怒无以难之一日构思一对欲令其辞屈心服对云五行金木水火土邱随口荅曰四位公侯伯子男其博学敏捷类如此
    　　不孝防地死
    　　杭州杨镇一凶徒素不孝于母尤凌虐其妻有子三岁爱惜甚至妻常抱偶失手攧损其头泣而谓姑曰夫归妇必被殴死不若先溺水之为幸姑曰汝第无忧但云是我之误我却去避汝小姑处俟其怒息而还至晚夫归见儿头破径捽妻欲杀之妻告曰非我过也婆攧之耳惧汝怒已徃小姑家去遂释之次日持刀寻母中途藏诸石下却到妹家好言诱母还至石边忿躁詈骂取刀杀母竟失藏刀所在惟见巨蛇介道畏怯退缩不觉双足防入地中湏臾即没至膝七窍流血声罪自咎母急扶抱无计可施走报于妇妇掘地随掘随防啖以饮食三日乃死观者日数千人莫不称快时至正甲辰六月也

    Explanation

    614

    Poetic Warnings

    Zhang Qi, styled Qi Zhi, was a man from the Four Rivers region known for his poetic talent. He once composed a couplet that read, "Since parting, the trees of Yue have grown as guests; having seen all the mountains of Wu, it is no longer home." Soon after, he died. Is poetry also a warning?

    Qiu Jishan, a man from Songjiang, was known in the late Song and early Yuan dynasties for his wit and skill in solving riddles. No one surpassed him in this regard. He wandered freely between lakes and seas, and once traveled to Fuzhou, where he mocked a scholar for not knowing how to read. The crowd was angry, but no one could challenge him. One day, he thought of a couplet to outwit and convince the scholar. He said, "The five elements: metal, wood, water, fire, earth." Qiu replied offhand, "The four ranks: duke, marquis, count, viscount, baron." His quick wit and knowledge were indeed remarkable.

    Death by Filial Impiety

    In Hangzhou, Yang Town, a violent and disrespectful man was always cruel to his mother and abusive to his wife. He had a three-year-old son whom he loved dearly. His wife often carried the child, and once, she accidentally knocked his head and wept, telling her mother-in-law, "If my husband returns, he will surely beat me to death. It would be better to drown first." The mother-in-law said, "Do not worry. Just say it was my fault, and I will go hide with your younger sister-in-law until his anger subsides." That evening, the husband returned, saw his son's head injured, and grabbed his wife, intending to kill her. The wife said, "It was not my fault. The mother-in-law knocked him." Fearing his anger, she went to her sister-in-law's house. The husband, now enraged, went to kill his mother. He took a knife and killed her. Suddenly, a huge snake blocked his path. Fearing the snake, he retreated, and his feet sank into the ground. Soon, his feet were buried up to his knees, and blood flowed from his seven orifices. He accused himself of the crime, and his mother rushed to hold him, but there was nothing she could do. She ran to tell his wife. The wife dug the ground, and the snake followed the digging. She fed it with food and drink, and after three days, the man died. Thousands of people watched daily and cheered in approval. This happened in the sixth month of the year Jiachen during the reign of Emperor Huizong of the Yuan dynasty.

    615

    嘲囘囘
    　　杭州荐桥侧首有高楼八间俗谓八间楼皆富实囘囘所居一日娶妇其昬礼絶与中国殊虽伯叔姊妹有所不顾街巷之人肩摩踵接咸来窥视至有攀縁檐阑窓牖者踏翻楼屋賔主壻妇咸死此亦一大怪事也郡人王梅谷戯作下火文云賔主满堂欢闾里盈门看洞房忽崩摧喜乐成祸患压落瓦碎兮倒落沙泥别都钉析兮木屑飞玉山摧坦腹之郎金谷坠落花之相难以乘

    615

    Translation:

    Mocking the Hui

    In Hangzhou, at Jian Bridge, there stands an eight-story building, commonly known as the Eight-Story Building, inhabited by wealthy Hui people. One day, a wedding was held, and the marriage ceremony was completely different from Chinese customs, to the extent that even uncles, aunts, and siblings were disregarded. People from the streets and alleys, their shoulders rubbing and heels touching, all came to watch. Some even climbed along the eaves, railings, and windows, causing the building to collapse. The guests, hosts, groom, and bride all perished. This was also a very strange event. A person from the prefecture, Wang Meigǔ, playfully composed a satirical piece titled "Falling Fire Text," which reads: "Guests and hosts filled with joy, the neighborhood crowded around the door to watch. Suddenly, the bridal chamber collapsed, turning joy into disaster. Tiles crushed and fell, collapsing into sand and mud. Nails and wood splinters flew everywhere, wood shavings scattering. The groom, like a crumbled jade mountain, and the bride, like falling flowers, were difficult to bear."

    616

    龙兮魄散魂消不能跨凤兮筋断骨折氁丝脱兮尘土昏头袖碎兮珠翠黯压倒象鼻塌不见猫睛亮呜呼守白头未及一朝赏黄花却在半饷移厨聚景园中歇马飞来峯上阿剌【郎葛反】一声絶无闻哀哉树倒胡孙散阿老瓦倒刺沙别都丁木偰非皆囘囘小名故借音及之象鼻猫睛其貎氁【上声】丝头袖其服色也阿剌其语也聚景园囘囘丛冡在焉飞来峯猿猴来徃之处
    　　白县尹诗
    　　嘉兴白县尹得代过姚庄访僧胜福州闲游市井间见妇人女子皆浓妆艶饰因问从行者或答云风俗使然少艾者僧之宠下此则皆道人所有白遂戱题一絶于壁云红红白白好花枝尽被山僧折取归祗有野薇顔色浅也来钩惹道人衣胜见亟命去之然已盛传矣

    Explanation

    616

    Translation:

    The dragon's soul is scattered, its spirit extinguished, unable to leap; the phoenix's bones are broken, its sinews severed. Fine silk unravels, dust obscures the head; sleeves are torn, jewels dimmed. The elephant's trunk is crushed, its form collapsed; the cat's eyes are no longer seen. Alas, the white-haired man has not yet had a single day to admire the yellow flowers, yet in half a meal's time, he has moved from the garden of gathered scenes to rest his horse on the peak of flying mountains. A sound of "Ala" [transliteration], a sudden silence, unheard. How sad it is when the tree falls, the monkeys scatter; when the old tree falls, the thorns scatter. "Duting Muxie" and "Huuihuui" are not all the small names of the Huuihuui people; their pronunciation is borrowed. The elephant's trunk and the cat's eyes are their appearance; the fine silk and the head and sleeves are their attire; "Ala" is their language. The garden of gathered scenes is where the Huuihuui people's burial grounds are found; the peak of flying mountains is where the apes come and go.

    White County Magistrate's Poem

    The magistrate of Jiaxing, White, was replaced and passed through Yaozhuang to visit the monk Sheng in Fuzhou. While leisurely strolling through the market, he saw that all the women were heavily made up and adorned. He asked those who accompanied him, and they replied that it was the custom. The younger ones were the favorites of the monk, while the others were all the possessions of the Daoist. White then playfully inscribed a quatrain on the wall: "Red and white flowers, so beautiful, have all been picked by the mountain monk. Only the wild violets, with their pale colors, remain to attract the Daoist's attire." Upon seeing this, Sheng immediately ordered it to be removed, but it had already become widely known.

    617

    废家子孙诗
    　　秀之斜塘有故宋大姓居焉家富饶田连阡陌宗族虽盛衍而子孙多不肖祖父财产废败罄尽郡人金方所谈辞滑稽为赋诵好嫚戱因摭其事成近体一律云兴废从来固有之尔家忒煞欠扶持诸坟掘见黄泉骨两观番成白地皮宅眷皆为撑目兎舍人总作缩头强奴猾干欺凌主说与人家子弟知夫兎撑目望月而孕则妇女之不夫而妊也其家有道观二所语虽鄙俚然为人后者见此宁不知惧也哉乐曲
    　　鞑靼乐器如筝秦琵琶胡琴和必斯之类所弹之曲与汉人曲调不同
    　　大曲
    　　哈巴尔图　　口温　　　　也葛傥兀
    　　辉和尔　　　闵古里　　　起土苦里
    　　防四土鲁海　舎舎弼　　　揺落四
    　　古揺落四　闪弹揺落四　阿耶儿虎
    　　桑哥儿苦不丁【江南谓之孔雀双手弹】　答罕【谓之白翎雀双手弹】苦只把失【品】
    　　小曲
    　　阿厮阑扯弼【回盏曲双手弹】　阿林捺【花红】哈儿火失哈赤【黒雀儿呌】　　洞洞伯

    Explanation

    617

    Poem on the Descendants of a Fallen Family

    In Xieyang, there lived a once-prominent family from the Song dynasty. They were wealthy, with vast fields stretching far and wide. Although their clan was once prosperous, their descendants were mostly unworthy, and the ancestors' wealth had been completely wasted and depleted. A local person named Jin Fang spoke in a humorous and witty manner, composing a regulated verse based on this story. The verse goes:

    The rise and fall of families has always been a natural occurrence, but your family has been lacking support. The graves have been dug up, revealing bones, and the ancestral halls have become barren land. The household members are all like blind rabbits, and everyone is like a turtle with its head tucked in. The strong and cunning slaves oppress the master. Tell the children of others to know. A rabbit, with its eyes open, looks at the moon and becomes pregnant—this refers to women who become pregnant without a husband. The family has two Taoist temples, and though the language is crude, those who follow in their footsteps should surely be afraid, right?

    Musical Instruments of the Tatars

    The musical instruments of the Tatars, such as the zither, qin, pipa, fiddle, and hebis—the tunes played on these instruments are different from those of the Han people.

    Large Compositions

    • Habartu
    • Kewen
    • Yegetaowu
    • Huier
    • Minguli
    • Qitukuli
    • Fangsitu Luhai
    • Sheshabi
    • Yaoluosi
    • Angu Yaoluosi
    • Shantan Yaoluosi
    • Aierhu
    • Sangger Kudbing (known as Peacock, played with both hands in Jiangnan)
    • Dahen (known as White Feathered Sparrow, played with both hands)
    • Kuzibashi (a type of stringed instrument)

    Small Compositions

    • Asilan Chaobi (a type of wine cup song, played with both hands)
    • Alina (a type of flower song)
    • Harahushi Hachi (a type of black sparrow song)
    • Dongdongbo
    618

    曲律买　　　者归　　　　牝畴兀儿
    　　把担葛失　　削浪沙　　　马哈
    　　相公　　　　仙　　　　阿下水花
    　　囘囘曲【附】
    　　伉里　　　　马黒某当当　清泉当当
    　　爇梅花文
    　　周申父【之翰】寒夜拥罏火见缾内所挿折枝梅花氷冻而枯因取投火中戯作下火文云寒勒铜瓶冻未开南枝春断不归来这囘勿入梨云梦却把芳心作死灰共惟地罏中处士梅公之灵生自罗浮分庾岭形若槁木棱棱山泽之臞肤如凝脂凛凛雪霜之操春魁占百花头上岁寒居三友图中玉堂茅舍总无心金鼎商
    羮期结果不料道人见挽便离有色之根夫何氷氏相凌遽返华胥之国玉骨拥罏烘不醒氷魂剪纸竟难招纸帐夜长犹作寻香之梦筠窓月淡尚疑弄影之时虽宋广平鐡石心肠忘情未得使华光老丹青手叚摸索难真却愁零落一枝春好与茶毗三昧火惜花君子还道这一防香魂今在何处咦烱然不逐东风散只在孤山水月中

    Explanation

    618

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Translation:

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Final Translation:

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Final Translation:

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Final Translation:

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Final Translation:

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Final Translation:

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Final Translation:

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Final Translation:

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Final Translation:

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Final Translation:

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Final Translation:
