    619

    如梦令
    　　一人娶妻无元可潜赠之如梦令云今夜盛排筵宴准拟寻芳一遍春去已多时问甚红深红浅不见不见还你一方白绢
    　　黄门
    　　世有男子虽娶妇而终身无嗣育者谓之天阉世俗则命之曰黄门晋海西公尝有此疾北齐李庶生而天阉按黄帝鍼经曰人有具伤于隂隂气絶而不起隂不能用然其不去宦者之独去何也愿闻其故岐伯曰宦者去其宗筋伤其冲脉血写不复皮肤内结唇口不荣故不生黄帝曰其有天宦者未尝被伤然其不生其故何也岐伯曰此天之所不足其任冲不盛宗筋不成有气无血唇口不荣故不生又大般若经载五种黄门云梵言扇搋【丑皆切】半释迦唐言黄门其类有五一曰半释迦总名也

    Explanation

    619

    如梦令

    • 一人娶妻无元可潜赠之如梦令云今夜盛排筵宴准拟寻芳一遍春去已多时问甚红深红浅不见不见还你一方白绢
      • "A man marries a wife, lacking something essential, and secretly gives her a dream command. He says, 'Tonight, a grand banquet is set up, planning to enjoy the beauty once. Spring has gone for a long time. Ask about the depth of red. Not seen, not seen, return you a piece of white silk.'"

    黄门

    • 世有男子虽娶妇而终身无嗣育者谓之天阉世俗则命之曰黄门晋海西公尝有此疾北齐李庶生而天阉

      • "There are men who marry but never have offspring, they are called 'natural eunuchs.' The world calls them 'eunuchs.' Emperor Haixi of Jin once had this condition, and Li Shu of Northern Qi was born a natural eunuch."

    • 黄帝鍼经曰人有具伤于隂隂气絶而不起隂不能用然其不去宦者之独去何也愿闻其故

      • "The Yellow Emperor's Acupuncture Classic says: People who are injured in the yin aspect, their yin energy is cut off and cannot rise, and they cannot use yin. Yet their male organs remain. Why do eunuchs lose their male organs? I wish to hear the reason."

    • 岐伯曰宦者去其宗筋伤其冲脉血写不复皮肤内结唇口不荣故不生

      • "Qibo said: Eunuchs lose their main tendons, injure their Chong pulse, and their blood does not return to the skin, leading to internal blockage and lack of nourishment to the lips and mouth, hence the loss of male organs."

    • 黄帝曰其有天宦者未尝被伤然其不生其故何也

      • "Yellow Emperor said: There are those born with natural eunuch conditions, who have never been injured, yet their male organs do not develop. What is the reason?"

    • 岐伯曰此天之所不足其任冲不盛宗筋不成有气无血唇口不荣故不生

      • "Qibo said: This is due to a natural deficiency, their Chong pulse is not strong, their main tendons do not form, they have qi but no blood, and their lips and mouth are not nourished, hence the loss of male organs."

    • 又大般若经载五种黄门云梵言扇搋【丑皆切】半释迦唐言黄门其类有五一曰半释迦总名也

      • "The Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra lists five types of eunuchs, saying: In Sanskrit, they are called 'chandala' (pronounced 'chandala'), and in Chinese, they are called 'eunuchs.' There are five types: the first is the general name for 'half-Buddha.'"

    Final Translation

    如梦令

    • "A man marries a wife, lacking something essential, and secretly gives her a dream command. He says, 'Tonight, a grand banquet is set up, planning to enjoy the beauty once. Spring has gone for a long time. Ask about the depth of red. Not seen, not seen, return you a piece of white silk.'"

    黄门

    • "There are men who marry but never have offspring, they are called 'natural eunuchs.' The world calls them 'eunuchs.' Emperor Haixi of Jin once had this condition, and Li Shu of Northern Qi was born a natural eunuch."

    • "The Yellow Emperor's Acupuncture Classic says: People who are injured in the yin aspect, their yin energy is cut off and cannot rise, and they cannot use yin. Yet their male organs remain. Why do eunuchs lose their male organs? I wish to hear the reason."

    • "Qibo said: Eunuchs lose their main tendons, injure their Chong pulse, and their blood does not return to the skin, leading to internal blockage and lack of nourishment to the lips and mouth, hence the loss of male organs."

    • "Yellow Emperor said: There are those born with natural eunuch conditions, who have never been injured, yet their male organs do not develop. What is the reason?"

    • "Qibo said: This is due to a natural deficiency, their Chong pulse is not strong, their main tendons do not form, they have qi but no blood, and their lips and mouth are not nour

    620

    有男根用而不生子二曰伊利沙半释迦此云妬谓他行欲即发不见即无亦具男根而不生子三曰扇搋半释迦谓本来男根不满亦不能生子四言博叉半释迦谓半月能男半月能女五曰留拏半释迦此云割谓被割形者此五种黄门名为人中恶趣受身处然周礼阉人郑氏注云阉真气藏者宋赵忠惠帅维日幙僚赵参议有婢慧尽得侪辈之欢赵昵之坚拒不从疑有异强即之则男子也闻于有司盖身二形前后奸状不一遂寘之极刑近李安民尝于福州得徐氏处子年十五六交际一再渐具男形盖天

    Explanation

    620

    There are individuals with male genitalia who are unable to father children. One such person is referred to as "Ilyasha," which means "jealous." This term describes someone who experiences desire when others engage in sexual activity but does not experience it otherwise. Another individual, also with male genitalia, cannot father children due to an inherent deficiency in their reproductive organs. A third person is described as "Shanchua," meaning "incomplete," indicating that they lack fully developed male genitalia and thus cannot father children. A fourth individual, known as "Bocuo," is said to alternate between male and female characteristics, being male for half the month and female for the other half. The fifth person, called "Liuna," has been surgically altered, having their genitalia cut or removed. These five types of eunuchs are considered to be in a state of moral and social degradation, often described as "evil" or "wicked."

    According to the Rites of Zhou, Zheng Xuan, a commentator, notes that eunuchs are those whose true essence is hidden or suppressed. During the Song Dynasty, Zhao Zhongyi, the governor of Yangzhou, had a subordinate named Zhao Zhen who possessed a servant named Hui Xiang. Hui Xiang was highly favored by Zhao Zhen, but she steadfastly refused his advances, leading Zhao to suspect something was amiss. When he forced her, he discovered that she was actually a man. This was reported to the authorities, and it was revealed that Hui Xiang had both male and female characteristics, engaging in sexual acts with different partners. As a result, she was sentenced to death.

    Recently, Li Anmin, in Fuzhou, encountered a young unmarried woman from the Xu family, aged fifteen or sixteen. After repeated interactions, she gradually developed male characteristics, suggesting a natural transformation.

    621

    真未破则彼亦不自知然小説中有池州李氏女及婢添喜事正相类而此外絶未见于古今传记等书岂以为人之妖而污笔墨不复载乎晋五行志谓之人疴惠帝时京洛有人兼男女体亦能两用人道而性尤淫乱此乱气所生也玉厯通政经男女两体主国淫乱而二十八宿真形图所载心房二星皆两形与丈夫妇女更为雌雄此又何耶异物志云灵狸一体自为隂阳故能媚人褚氏遗书曰非男非女之身精血散分又曰感以妇人则男脉应诊动以男子则女脉顺指皆天地不正之气也事载周宻癸辛杂识
    　　花山贼
    　　中原红冦未起时花山贼毕四等仅三十六人内一妇女尤勇捷聚集茅山一道宫纵横出没畧无忌惮始终三月余三省拨兵不能收捕杀伤官军无数朝廷召募鹾徒朱陈率其党与一鼓而擒之从此天下之人视官军为无用不三五年自河以南盗贼充斥其数也夫

    Explanation

    621

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Pre-Drafting Research on the Source Text

    1. Idiomatic Expressions

    • 真未破则彼亦不自知 - A conditional phrase meaning "If the true nature is not revealed, then they also do not know it themselves."
    • 小説中有池州李氏女及婢添喜事 - Refers to a story in a novel about a woman from Chizhou and her maid named Tienxi, indicating a narrative reference.
    • 絶未见于古今传记等书 - Means "absolutely not seen in ancient and modern biographies and books," emphasizing rarity.
    • 人之妖 - Refers to "human demons" or "human monsters," a term for unusual or supernatural beings.
    • 污笔墨 - Means "to sully the pen and ink," implying that such stories are considered too bizarre or inappropriate for serious writing.
    • 晋五行志谓之人疴 - Refers to the "Jin Wuxing Zhi" (Records of the Five Elements of Jin), which describes "human diseases" or "human ailments."
    • 乱气所生也 - Means "born from chaotic qi," referring to abnormal or disruptive forces.
    • 玉厯通政经男女两体 - Refers to the "Yu Li Tongzheng Jing" (Jade Calendar of Universal Governance), which discusses the concept of "two bodies of men and women," indicating a dual-gendered being.
    • 心房二星皆两形 - Means "the two stars of the heart are both two-formed," referring to celestial bodies with dual forms.
    • 异物志云灵狸一体自为阴阳 - Refers to the "Yiwu Zhi" (Record of Strange Things), which mentions "spiritual foxes" that can be both male and female.
    • 褚氏遗书曰非男非女之身 - Refers to the "Zhushi Yishu" (Zhushis Legacy), which describes a being that is neither male nor female.
    • 感以妇人则男脉应诊动以男子则女脉顺指 - Means "if stimulated by a woman, the male pulse responds; if stimulated by a man, the female pulse responds," indicating a biological or spiritual duality.
    • 天地不正之气也 - Means "unjust qi of heaven and earth," referring to abnormal or disruptive forces.
    • 花山贼 - Refers to bandits from Huashan, indicating a specific group of outlaws.
    • 中原红冦未起时 - Means "before the Red Bandits of Central China rose up," referring to a historical event.
    • 聚集茅山一道宫 - Means "gathered at a Daoist temple on Mount Mao," indicating a specific location.
    • 纵横出没 - Means "moving freely and unpredictably," describing their actions.
    • 始终三月余 - Means "lasting for more than three months," indicating the duration of their activities.
    • 三省拨兵不能收捕 - Means "troops from three provinces could not capture them," indicating their elusiveness.
    • 杀伤官军无数 - Means "killing and wounding countless government soldiers," indicating the severity of their actions.
    • 朝廷召募鹾徒朱陈 - Means "the court recruited salt smugglers Zhu and Chen," indicating a specific group of people.
    • 一鼓而擒之 - Means "captured them in one strike," indicating a decisive action.
    • 从此天下之人视官军为无用 - Means "from then on, people around the world saw the government army as useless," indicating a shift in perception.
    • 不三五年自河以南盗贼充斥 - Means "within three to five years, bandits were rampant south of the Yellow River," indicating a widespread problem.

    2. Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋五行志 - The "Jin Wuxing Zhi" (Records of the Five Elements of Jin) is a historical text that discusses various phenomena, including unusual human conditions.
    • 玉厯通政经 - The "Yu Li Tongzheng Jing" (Jade Calendar of Universal Governance) is a Daoist text that discusses various spiritual and cosmological concepts.
    • 异物志 - The "Yiwu Zhi" (Record of Strange Things) is a collection of stories about unusual and supernatural events.
    • 褚氏遗书 - The "Zhushi Yishu" (Zhushis Legacy) is a medical text that discusses various physical and spiritual conditions.
    • 花山贼 - Bandits
    622

    爵禄前定
    　　宇文公谅字子贞湖州人初领乡贡入浙省试院头塲占一席舍其案上有宇文同知四字不知何人书试官考卷以文不中式将黜之时坐主龙麟洲先生江西老儒也年八十余始过江浙力主此卷卒置榜中及防试果登髙第授同知婺源州事虽曰爵禄前定盖亦阴徳所致人鲜有知者公年少时尝馆授巨室其闺爱中夜来奔坚拒不纳明旦托以他故敛书告别此非阴徳也与
    　　醋鉢儿
    　　俞俊嘉兴人巴延太师柄国日尝赋清平乐长短句云君恩如草秋至还枯槁落落残星犹弄晓豪杰消磨尽了放开湖海襟怀休教鸥鹭惊猜我是江南倦客等闲容易安排手槀留叶起之处后与叶交恶竟诉于官必欲搆成其罪寅缘贿赂浙省移准中书省咨劄付儒学提举司议得古人寄情遣兴作为闺怨诗词多有指夫为君者然此亦当禁止以故获免罪戾而所费已几万定矣至正

    Explanation

    622

    Translation

    Fate Determines Rank and Salary
    Yuwen Gongliang, courtesy name Zizhen, was from Huzhou. Initially, he was selected as a candidate for the provincial examination in Zhejiang and occupied a seat in the first round. He found the words "Yuwen Tongzhi" on someone's desk, but did not know who had written them. The examiners were about to disqualify him because his essay did not meet the standards. At that time, the chief examiner was Mr. Long Linzhou, an old scholar from Jiangxi, who was over eighty years old and had just arrived in Jiangsu and Zhejiang. He strongly supported this essay and eventually placed it on the list of successful candidates. When the final examination took place, Yuwen Gongliang indeed achieved high honors and was appointed as the deputy governor of Wuyuan Prefecture. Although it is said that rank and salary are predetermined by fate, it could also be due to hidden virtue, which few people know about. When Yuwen Gongliang was young, he once taught in a wealthy family's academy. One night, a woman from the inner quarters came to him, but he firmly refused to accept her. The next morning, he made an excuse and packed his books to say goodbye. Is this not a result of hidden virtue?

    The Acetate Bowl
    Yu Shun was from Jiaxing. During the reign of Grand Tutor Baisan, he once composed a poem in the style of "Qingping Yue" (Clear Peaceful Moon):
    "The emperor's grace is like grass, which withers when autumn comes.
    A few remaining stars still twinkle in the dawn.
    All the heroes have been worn out.
    Open up one's heart as vast as the lakes and seas.
    Do not let the seagulls and egrets be startled.
    I am a weary traveler from Jiangnan.
    Arrange things easily and casually.
    The place where the manuscript was written."

    Later, he became hostile with someone named Ye and reported the matter to the authorities, who deliberately fabricated a crime against him. Using connections and bribes, the Zhejiang provincial government referred the matter to the Central Secretariat, which then instructed the Confucian Academy to deliberate. It was decided that ancient people used poetry to express emotions and relieve boredom, often writing poems about women's grievances. Those who refer to their husbands as "emperors" should also be prohibited. Therefore, Yu Shun was spared from punishment, but the cost was already nearly ten thousand coins. This occurred during the reign of Emperor Toghon Temür, known as the era of "Zhenghe."

    623

    丙申春张士诚僣号诚王据有平江日又以贿通松江伪尹郑焕署宰华亭用酷刑朘剥邑民恨入骨髓郡士海叟有诗曰四海清宁未有期诸公衮衮正当时忽然一日天兵至打破王婆醋鉢儿或者不知醋鉢之义以问叟叟曰昔有不轨伏诛暴尸于竿王婆买醋经过其下适索朽尸坠醋鉢为其所压着地而碎王婆年老无知将谓死者所致顾谓之曰汝只是未曽吃恶官司来闻者皆絶倒
    　　棋谱
    　　通集　　通逺集　　清逺集　　清乐集幽集　　机深集　　増广通逺集
    　　集　　忘忧集
    　　军前请法师
    　　谢景阳居松江北郭结坛于家行召法至正十一年官兵下海勦捕方国珍传云贼中有人能呼召风雨必得破其法者乃可擒讨千户额森等遂以谢荐总兵官给传致请省劄有云参裁军事必访异人既达天时其为世用时知府王克敏廉介端严有声于时不得已亲造其庐起赴军前其术一无所验自后全军败衂吁宰臣统大兵数十万勦除草窃如拉朽耳而乃延一方士则其机畧安在哉
    　　凌总管出对

    Explanation

    623

    In the spring of the Bing Shen year, Zhang Shicheng declared himself as Cheng Wang and occupied Pingjiang. He also bribed Zheng Huan, the false magistrate of Songjiang, to act as the magistrate of Huating, using cruel punishments to extort money and resources from the people of the county, causing them to hate him to the core. A scholar named Hai Sou from the county wrote a poem: "There is no foreseeable time when the four seas will be peaceful; various officials are in power at this time. Suddenly one day, heavenly soldiers arrived, breaking the scheme of the old woman with the vinegar jar." Perhaps someone does not know the meaning of the vinegar jar, so he asked Hai Sou, who said, "Once there was someone who committed a crime, was executed, and his body was left exposed on a pole. An old woman bought vinegar and passed by, and as she reached for the jar, the decaying body fell onto it, crushing it to pieces. The old woman, being old and ignorant, thought that the dead person caused it, and said to him, 'You have never been in trouble with the law.'" Those who heard this all fell down laughing.

    Chess Books

    • Tong Yuan Ji (通集)
    • Tong Yuan Ji (通逺集)
    • Qing Yuan Ji (清逺集)
    • Qing Le Ji (清乐集)
    • You Yuan Ji (幽集)
    • Ji Shen Ji (机深集)
    • Zeng Guang Tong Yuan Ji (増广通逺集)
    • Xuan Xuan Ji (集)
    • Wang You Ji (忘忧集)

    Military Invocation of a Priest

    Xie Jingyang lived in the northern suburbs of Songjiang and set up a ritual platform at home to perform exorcism. In the eleventh year of the Zhizheng era, when the imperial army went to sea to suppress Fang Guozhen, the official records stated that there was someone in the rebels' ranks who could summon wind and rain. To break their magic, one must find a special person. Thousand Officer Esen and others recommended Xie Jingyang to the General-in-Chief, who sent an official document requesting his presence. It was written, "Participate in military decision-making; seek out extraordinary people. Once the right time is reached, his talents will be of use in the world." At the time, the Prefect Wang Keming was known for his integrity and dignity. Reluctantly, he personally visited Xie Jingyang's home and invited him to the front lines. However, Xie's methods proved ineffective, and after that, the entire army suffered a defeat. One might wonder, if the chancellor commands a large army of tens of thousands to suppress petty bandits, it is like pulling down rotten wood. Yet, he still invited a single person, where then were his strategies and plans?

    Ling Zongguan's Puzzle

    Ling Zongguan posed a puzzle.

    624

    嘉兴总管凌师德以文章政事自居同僚莫敢与抗然其行实贪污颇闻人有讥议因出对云竹本无心外面自生枝节贡推官对云藕因有窍中间抽出丝毫盖讽之也
    　　承天寺
    　　平江承天寺遭囘禄殿宇一空僧悦楚南来住持施财者云集遂大兴土木之工金碧殊胜有加于昔或劝题梁悦不从曰当有俗人来暂居悦陞领径山卒高邮兵攻破城张士诚据以为宫佛像悉毁壊铜观音铸为钱既投降作太尉别造府越四五年复为寺义丈夫
    　　吴兴钱泰窝云至正初二贾自嘉兴来平江买舟至海口收市舶货行二十余里两道人诣舟求渡一磬一持神像既上舟去巾服乃两甲者从像中出二长刀叱曰吾逐盗至此汝真盗也舟人阳应曰我固知为盗顾无以发今壮士诚与吾意合此未可前途乃可耳故纡行且曰二盗已落公手愿治酒助公勇遂命妻取酒劝甲者迟暮醉抽其刀斫贼其一跃起复斫之二盗尽死舟还二贾泣且拜曰非公吾几不免虎口遂以白金二饼为舟人夀吁决死生于阽危之际不贾之托不谓之义丈夫可乎

    Explanation

    624

    Translation:

    Governor Ling Shide of Jīngzhōu considered himself a master of literary and political affairs, and his colleagues dared not oppose him. However, his actual behavior was corrupt, and people often criticized him. Once, he said in response, "Bamboo has no heart, but it naturally grows branches and shoots." The imperial censor replied, "Lotus has a hole, and a thread can be drawn from it." This was a veiled criticism.

    The Chéngtiān Temple in Píngjiāng was destroyed by fire, leaving the halls empty. The monk Yuè Chǔ came from the south to take charge. Donors gathered, and they began a major construction project, making the temple even more splendid than before. Someone suggested inscribing something on the beams, but Yuè refused, saying, "There will be a layperson who will temporarily stay here." Yuè later became the abbot of Jìngshān Temple and died. The army of Gāoyóu attacked and captured the city, and Zhang Shìchéng took it as his palace, destroying all the Buddha statues. The bronze Guānyīn statue was melted down to make coins. After surrendering, he became a Taivéi and built a new palace. After four or five years, it was rebuilt as a temple. This was the righteous act of a great man.

    From Wúxīng, Qián Tàiwō said: In the second year of the Zhìzhèng era, a merchant from Jīngzhōu came to Píngjiāng by boat to the mouth of the sea, collecting foreign goods for over twenty miles. Two Taoists came to the boat to cross, one carrying a bell and the other a statue of a ghost god. After boarding the boat, they took off their clothes, revealing two warriors who emerged from the statue. They drew their long swords and said, "We are here to chase thieves. You are the real thieves!" The boatman pretended to agree, saying, "I knew you were thieves, but I had no way to expose you. Now, with these brave men who truly align with my intentions, we can proceed. We will take a detour and say, 'These two thieves have fallen into your hands. I wish to prepare some wine to aid your courage.'" He then ordered his wife to fetch wine and offered it to the warriors. In the evening, they drank until they were drunk and drew their swords to kill the thieves. One thief leaped up and was struck down, and both thieves were killed. The boat returned, and the two merchants wept and bowed, saying, "Without you, we would have been killed. We offer you two pieces of silver as a reward." The boatman accepted the silver and said, "In a dangerous situation, refusing to help a merchant is not the act of a righteous man."

    625

    解语杯
    　　至正庚子秋七月九日饮松江泗濵夏氏清樾堂上酒半折正开荷花置小金巵于其中命歌姬捧以行酒客就姬取花左手执枝右手分开花瓣以口就饮其风致又过碧筩逺甚余因名为解语杯坐客咸曰然戯题小像
    　　张句曲戯题黄大痴小像云全真家数禅和口鼓贫子骨头吏员臓腑唐伯刚题邾仲谊小像云七尺躯威仪济济三寸舌是非风起一双眼看人做官两脚沿门报喜仲谊云是谁是谁伯刚云是你是你水仙子
    　　张明善作北乐府水仙子讥时云铺眉苦眼早三公裸袖揎拳享万钟胡言乱语成时用大纲来都是烘【上声】説英雄谁是英雄五眼鸡岐山鸣凤两头蛇南阳卧龙三脚猫渭水非熊

    Explanation

    625

    The Speaking Cup
    On the ninth day of the seventh month of the year Gengzi during the reign of Emperor Toghon Temür, a gathering took place at the Qingyue Hall in the Xia family's residence near the Si River in Songjiang. Halfway through the drinking session, a lotus flower was placed in a small golden cup, and a singing girl was tasked with carrying it to pour wine. The guests, upon receiving the cup, took the flower, holding the stem with their left hand and separating the petals with their right, then drinking from it. The elegance of this custom surpassed even the refined practice of drinking from a bamboo tube. I named it the "speaking cup" because of its interactive and elegant nature. All the guests agreed, and a playful portrait was drawn.

    Zhang Jucu playfully inscribed a small portrait of Huang Dacheng, a renowned painter, with the following description: "He combines the ways of a Quanzhen Taoist, the mouth of a Buddhist monk, the bones of a poor man, and the entrails of a government official."

    Tang Bergang inscribed a small portrait of Zhu Zhongyi with the following description: "He has a seven-foot frame with dignified bearing, a three-inch tongue that stirs up controversy, a pair of eyes that see through the actions of officials, and two feet that walk along the gates, bringing good news."

    Zhu Zhongyi responded, "Who is it?" Tang Bergang replied, "It is you, it is you."

    Zhang Mingshan composed a Northern musical piece called "Shuixianzi" to satirize contemporary society, with the following lyrics:
    "Those who speak with furrowed brows and strained eyes, who bare their arms and clench their fists, who enjoy the wealth of the world, who speak nonsense and use grand words to talk about heroes—what hero is there? A five-eyed rooster, a two-headed snake, a three-legged cat, and a non-existent bear by the Wei River."

    626

    铜钱代蓍
    　　今人卜卦以铜钱代蓍便于用也又有以钱八文周围铺转而取六爻名曰金井阑但乾卦初爻及复之泰不可变盖止有六十二卦耳此法不可用
    　　刑赏失宜
    　　至正十二年岁壬辰秋蕲黄徐夀辉贼党攻破昱岭关径抵余杭县七月初十日入杭州城伪帅项蔡杨苏一屯明庆寺一屯北关门妙行寺称弥勒佛出世以惑众浙省参政樊执敬死于天水桥寳格与妻同溺于西湖其贼不杀不淫招民投附者署姓名于簿籍府库金帛悉辇以去至二十六日浙西廉访使自绍兴率盐塲灶丁过江同罗木营官军尅复
    城池贼遂溃散三平章鼎鼎逃徃嘉兴郎中托克托过江南越数日携省印来防权署省事至是亦囘四平章嘉珲自湖州统军归举火焚城残荡殆尽附贼充伪职者范县尹等明正典刑里豪施遵礼顾八为迎敌官军呙于市家产悉没县官明庆妙行亦然省都事以下坐失守城池罢黜不叙省官复任如故朝廷法度既隳刑赏失宜欲天下宴安不可复得矣
    　　画家十三科
    　　佛菩萨相　　玉帝君王道相

    Explanation

    626

    Final Translation

    Copper Coins Substituting for Yarrow Stalks
    In modern times, people use copper coins to substitute for yarrow stalks in divination, making it more convenient. Some also arrange eight copper coins in a circle to obtain the six lines, calling this method "Gold Well Lattice." However, this method cannot be used for the first line of the Qian hexagram or the transformation from Fu to Tai, as there are only sixty-two hexagrams in total. This method is not applicable.

    Misapplication of Punishment and Reward
    In the twelfth year of the Zhizheng era (1352), during the autumn of the sexagenary cycle Renchen, the rebels led by Xu Shouhui from Qihuang attacked and broke through the Yuling Pass, directly reaching Yuhang County. On July 10th, they entered Hangzhou City. The false commanders Xiang, Cai, Yang, and Su set up camps at Mingqing Temple and Beiguanmen, claiming that Maitreya Buddha had appeared to deceive the people. The provincial deputy governor Fan Zhijing was killed at Tianshui Bridge, and Bao Ge drowned in West Lake along with his wife. The rebels did not kill or rape, and those who joined them were registered by name in the records. The treasures in the government treasury were all taken away. On July 26th, the censor of western Zhejiang, who had come from Shaoxing with salt workers, crossed the river and joined forces with the Luomu camp troops to recapture the city. The rebels then scattered. Three chancellors, Dingding, fled to Jiaxing, while Langzhong Tokto crossed the river southward. After several days, he brought the provincial seal back and temporarily took charge of provincial affairs, eventually returning. Four chancellors, Jiahui, returned from Huzhou with the army, burning the city and leaving it in ruins. Those who had joined the rebels and held false positions, such as the county magistrate Fan, were executed according to the law. Wealthy villagers Shi Zunli and Gu Ba were executed for welcoming the enemy troops, and their properties were confiscated. County officials at Mingqing Temple and Miaoxing Temple also faced similar fates. Provincial officials below the level of doshi were dismissed and not reinstated, while other provincial officials resumed their positions as usual. With the collapse of the imperial laws and the misapplication of punishment and reward, the desire for peace and stability in the land could no longer be achieved.

    The Thirteen Categories of Painting

    • Buddha and Bodhisattva Portraits
    • Portraits of the Jade Emperor and Divine Kings
    627

    金刚神罗汉圣僧　　　　　风云龙虎宿世人物　　　全境山林　　花竹翎毛野骡走兽　　　人间动用　　界画楼台一切傍生　　　耕种机织　　雕青嵌緑

    　　辍耕録卷二十八
    <子部,小说家类,杂事之属,辍耕录>
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷二十九　　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰纪隆平
    　　张士诚弟兄四淮南泰州白驹塲人泰州地濵海海上盐塲三十有六两淮运盐使司士诚与弟士义士德士信并驾运盐纲船兼业私贩初无异于人先是中书省右丞相托克托在任灾异叠见黄河变迁至正十一年遣工部尚书贾鲁役民夫一十五万军二万决河故道民不聊生河南韩山童首事作乱以弥勒佛出世为名诱集无頼恶少烧香结防渐致滋蔓防淮西诸郡继而湖广江西荆襄等处皆沦贼境山东杜遵道以李氏子为主起汝宁萧县李二老彭张君用攻防徐州李二号芝麻李邹普胜徐夀辉即真一据蕲黄镇南班据江东又有毛贵陈友谅辈不可枚数分据各处方国

    Explanation

    627

    The Diamond Sutra, the God of Thunder, the Arhat, the Holy Monk

    Legends of Wind, Cloud, Dragon, Tiger, and Past Lives

    All the Mountains and Forests

    Flowers, Bamboo, Birds, and Wild Animals

    Human Activities

    Architectural Structures and All Kinds of Creatures

    Agricultural Activities and Weaving

    Decorative Arts: Inlaying Green and Blue Materials

    The Book of Weeding the Fields, Volume Twenty-Eight

    [Subcategory: Miscellaneous Novels, Sub-subcategory: Miscellaneous Matters, Book of Weeding the Fields]

    Imperial Library Complete Works

    The Book of Weeding the Fields, Volume Twenty-Nine

    Compiled by Tao Zongyi of the Yuan Dynasty

    Chronicle of Prosperity and Peace

    Zhang Shicheng, his brothers, were from Huaiyuan, Taizhou, and Baiju Field. Taizhou borders the sea, with thirty-six salt fields under the jurisdiction of the Two Huai Salt Transport Commissioner's Office. Zhang Shicheng and his brothers, Shiyi, Shide, and Shixin, managed the salt transport and engaged in private salt trading. Initially, they were no different from ordinary people. Prior to this, the Right Chancellor Tuo Keton was in office, and natural disasters and anomalies occurred frequently, including the change in the course of the Yellow River. In the eleventh year of the Zhizheng era, the Minister of Works Jia Lu mobilized fifteen million civilian workers and two million soldiers to dredge the old river channel, causing great hardship to the people. The disaster in Henan led to the uprising led by Han Shantong, who claimed that the Maitreya Buddha would soon appear and used this to attract disreputable young men. The rebellion gradually spread, and the prefectures of Huai West, Hunan, and Jiangxi, as well as Jingxiang, were all lost to the rebels. In Shandong, Du Zunzhao, the son of the Li family, rose up in Ruzhou and Xiaoxian, led by Li Er, known as "Sesame Li," and followed by Zou Pushe and Xu Shouhui, who occupied Xuzhou. Xu Shouhui, also known as Zhen, occupied Qihuang, and Zhen Nanban occupied Jiangdong. There were also many other rebels, such as Mao Gui and Chen Youliang, who could not be counted. They divided and occupied various regions.

    The state of affairs was in chaos, and the government struggled to maintain control.

    628

    珍弟兄啸聚台州海上朱定一陈贤五江宗三作乱江隂初王克柔者亦泰州人家富好施多结游侠将为不轨高邮知府季齐收捕于狱李华甫与麫张四素感克柔恩谋聚众刼狱齐以克柔解发州后招安华甫为泰州判四为千夫长十三年五月士诚又与华甫同谋起事未几士诚党与十有八人共杀华甫遂并其众焚掠村落驱民为盗防通泰高邮自号诚王改元天祐设官分职把截要冲南北梗塞立淮南行中书省于杨州以扼其势既而亦招安之立义兵元帅府以官其党然狙诈百出卒不就降杀知府季齐十五年五月攻破杨州路杀淮南行省参政赵琏士义被获伏诛既而退还

    Explanation

    628

    The Zhen brothers gathered in the sea off Taizhou, and Zhu Dingyi, Chen Xianwu, and Jiang Zong San caused chaos in Jiangyin. Initially, Wang Kero, a wealthy family from Taizhou, was generous and often associated with ruffians, planning to act against the law. The magistrate of Gaoyou, Ji Qi, captured him in prison. Li Huafu and Nian Zhangsi, who had always been grateful to Wang Kero, plotted to gather a crowd to break into the jail. Ji sent Wang Kero to Yangzhou. Later, Wang Kero was pardoned and appointed Li Huafu as the magistrate of Taizhou and Nian Zhangsi as a thousand-man commander. In the fifth month of the thirteenth year, Shi Cheng, along with Li Huafu, conspired to start a rebellion. Shortly after, Shi Cheng and his eighteen followers killed Li Huafu, then united his forces, burned and looted villages, forced the people to become bandits, controlled Tongzhou, Taizhou, and Gaoyou, declared himself King Cheng, changed the era name to Tianyou, set up officials and positions, controlled key strategic points, blocked north-south traffic, established the Huainan Secretariat in Yangzhou to control the situation. Later, he was also pardoned, set up the Righteous Army Marshal's Office to appoint his followers, but he was cunning and deceitful, ultimately refusing to surrender and killing the magistrate of Gaoyou, Ji Qi. In the fifth month of the fifteenth year, he attacked and broke through Yangzhou, killing the deputy provincial governor of Huainan, Zhao Lian, and Shi Yi was captured and executed, then he retreated.

    629

    高邮至九月二十五日又攻破杨州适湖广行省右丞鄂尔和引苖军来十月初一日复退丞相托克托亲总大军以擒之众号百万旌旗辎重首尾千里以为高邮刻日可平然托克托与弟御史大夫额森特穆尔专权日乆及出师遂有议其后者诏托克托安置淮安路额森特穆尔安置宁夏路别选相臣统其兵诏未下时部将董抟霄每对托克托言天兵南下势如破竹今老师费财何面目归报天子不若先攻其易托克托从其言分兵破天长六合贼皆溃散所杀者悉良民及攻高邮堕其外城城中震恐自分亡在旦夕忽闻诏解其权勇气百倍出城拒敌诸卫鐡甲军抱不平者尽皆散去或

    Explanation

    629

    From Gaoyou to the twenty-fifth day of the ninth month, they captured Yangzhou. Just then, the Right Chancellor of the Huguang Provincial Administration, Erluo, led the Miao army. On the first day of the tenth month, they retreated again. Chancellor Tuotuo personally commanded the main army to capture them. The army was said to number a million, with banners and military supplies stretching for thousands of miles, considering Gaoyou could be conquered in a short time. However, Tuotuo and his younger brother, the Censor-in-Chief Esen Temur, had monopolized power for a long time. When they launched a campaign, they were criticized by others. An imperial edict relocated Tuotuo to Huai'an Road and Esen Temur to Ningxia Road. Other high-ranking officials were selected to command the troops. Before the edict was issued, the subordinate general Dong Tuoxiao often advised Tuotuo, saying that the heavenly troops advancing south were unstoppable, but now wasting time and resources, what face would he have to return to report to the emperor? It would be better to first attack the easier targets. Tuotuo followed his advice and divided the troops to capture Tianchang and Liuhe, causing the rebels to flee, and those killed were all good people. When attacking Gaoyou, they destroyed its outer wall, and the city was filled with fear, thinking they would die within a few days. Suddenly, they heard the edict relieving him of power, doubling their courage, and they came out to resist the enemy. The armored soldiers who felt wronged all dispersed.

    630

    相聚山林为盗高邮不可得而复矣江隂羣冦互相吞啖江宗三朱英分党戕杀宗三将入城杀英时英就招安为判官州之僚佐无如之何遂申白江浙行省云朱英谋反省差元帅郭松压境观孙利其货赂逗遛不进英因乘间挈家逸去过江求救于士诚仍质妻子借兵复讐士诚初亦疑惑弗听英盛陈江南土地之广钱粮之多子女玉帛之富以动其中于是先遣士德率高邮贼众击横坍渡镝山十六年正月朔攻破常熟州江南自兵兴以来官军死锋镝郡县荐罹饥馑乡村农夫离父母弃妻子投充壮丁生不习兵而驱之死地以故乌合瓦解卒无成功江浙行省丞相达实特穆尔有防得

    Explanation

    630

    Bandits gathered in the mountains and forests, and Gaoyou could not be recovered. In Jiangyin, the bandits were divided and killing each other. Jiang Zongsan and Zhu Ying split into factions and killed each other. Jiang Zongsan was about to enter the city to kill Zhu Ying, but Zhu Ying surrendered and was appointed as a judge. The officials of the prefecture had no recourse and reported to the Jiang-Zhe provincial administration that Zhu Ying was planning a rebellion. The provincial administration dispatched General Guo Song to the border, but Guan Sun profited from the bribes and lingered without advancing. Zhu Ying took advantage of the opportunity to carry his family across the river and sought help from Shi Cheng. He pledged his wife and children and borrowed troops for revenge. Shi Cheng was initially hesitant but did not listen. Zhu Ying greatly extolled the vastness of the land in the south, the abundance of grain and money, and the richness of women, children, and valuables to move his heart. Shi Cheng then first sent Shi De to lead the bandits from Gaoyou to attack Hengcan and Dide Mountain. In the first month of the sixteenth year, they attacked and broke through Changshu Prefecture. Since the outbreak of war, government soldiers have died in battle, and prefectures and counties frequently suffer from famine. Rural farmers leave their parents and abandon their wives and children to join the ranks of soldiers, living without military training and being driven to certain death. Therefore, the unorganized group disintegrated, and the campaign ultimately failed. The provincial chancellor, Da Shi Temuer, remained vigilant.

    631

    便宜行事陞漕运万户托音为叅政统领官军民义捍御境上平江达噜噶齐陆实病亡陞松江府达噜噶齐哈克繖刷为平江达噜噶齐领兵出战除都水庸田使贡师泰为平江总管巡守城池吴江境上止有元帅王与敬官军一战而败死者过半残兵千余欲走入城城中闭门不纳退屯嘉兴旋抵松江士诚贼众才三四千人长驱而前直造北门弓不发矢劒不接刃明旦縁城而上遂据有平江路二月壬子朔也刼掠奸杀惨不忍言托音匿俞家园自刎不死游兵杀之哈克繖刷在境外闻城破自溺死既而昆山嘉定崇明州人相继来降维苏昌龄比先避乱居吴门士德用为叅谋称曰

    Explanation

    631

    The Emperor granted Tuo Yin the authority to make decisions on his own initiative and promoted him to the position of Counselor, in charge of leading military and civilian forces in defense of the border in Pingjiang. After the death of the Pingjiang Governor Lu Shi from illness, the Emperor promoted Hake Sanzhu, the Songjiang Prefecture Governor, to the position of Pingjiang Governor and ordered him to lead troops into battle. Gong Shitai was appointed as the Director of Water Management and Land Reclamation and became the Pingjiang Governor, responsible for patrolling and defending the city walls in Wujiang. The only commander, Wang Yu Jing, and his troops were defeated in one battle, with more than half killed. The remaining troops numbered more than a thousand and wanted to retreat into the city, but the city gates were closed and they were not allowed in. They retreated and camped in Jiaxing and soon arrived at Songjiang. The enemy forces led by Zhang Shicheng numbered only three or four thousand and advanced rapidly, directly reaching the northern gate without firing arrows or engaging in sword fights. The next day, they climbed the city walls and took control of Pingjiang. On the first day of the second month on the lunar calendar, they raided, looted, raped, and killed, scenes too terrible to describe. Tuo Yin hid in Yu Garden and attempted suicide but did not succeed; roving soldiers killed him. Hake Sanzhu, who was outside the city, heard of the city's fall and committed suicide by drowning. People from Kunshan, Jiading, and Chongming Prefectures successively surrendered. Wu Yang, Su Changling, and others had previously fled to Wumen to avoid the chaos, and Shi De was appointed as a military advisor and was known as such.

    632

    苏学士毁承天寺佛像为王宫易平江路为隆平郡立省院六部百司凡有寺观庵院豪门巨室将士争夺分占而居了无虚者几月进攻嘉兴全师覆没与敬据松江叛以城降常州豪侠黄贵甫间道归欵许为内应不战而城破易为毘陵郡分兵入湖州一鼓而得易为吴兴郡隆平太守周仁家本鍜工稍习吏事性资深刻与士德同心僇力躬亲细故三月癸巳士诚来自高邮服御器用皆假乘舆改至正十六年为天佑三年国号大周厯曰明时设学士员开文馆以隂阳术人李行素为丞相弟士德为平章提调各郡兵马蒋辉为右丞居内省理庶务潘元明为左丞镇吴兴史文炳为枢宻

    Explanation

    632

    Sc

    633

    院同知镇松江郡州县正官郡称太守州称通守县仍曰尹郡同知称府丞知事曰从事余则损益而已南向欲取嘉兴嘉兴则有参政杨旺札勒统领苖獠猺獞名曰达尔罕守御甚坚屡攻不克秋八月文炳大举兵临其东门悉为所殱文炳仅以身免士德又与与敬提兵入杭州军气甚鋭杭州大军敛锋不敌丞相退避萧山士德军检刮虏掠罗木营万户普贤努乃庆元路万户矩珠尔之子年未弱冠智勇过人率兵先出旺札勒图领苖军继进民亦挺身巷战士德大溃收拾残兵十丧八九及攻海盐又为乍浦钟氏所挠后得马道骁勇禽获苖军无算西南接境頼此无虞不然松江非士诚

    Explanation

    633

    Translation

    The deputy governor, governing the prefectures, sub-prefectures, and counties of Songjiang, is known as the prefectural governor in the prefecture, the sub-prefectural governor in the sub-prefecture, and the county magistrate in the county. The deputy prefectural governor is called the vice-prefect, and the subordinate officials are referred to as attendants; the rest are variations or modifications. Facing south, they intended to capture Jiaxing, which was defended by the high-ranking official Yang Wang Zale, who led the Miao, Yao, and other ethnic minorities and was titled Darhan. His defenses were very strong, and repeated attacks failed to conquer him. In the eighth month of autumn, Wen Bing launched a large-scale military campaign and attacked the eastern gate of Jiaxing, where his forces were completely annihilated. Wen Bing barely escaped with his life. Shi De and Yu Jing then led their troops into Hangzhou, where their army was very fierce. The large army of Hangzhou held back and was unable to match them, and the chancellor retreated to Xiaoshan. The army of Shi De inspected and plundered, and the Ten Thousand Households Luomu Camp, Pu Xian Nu, the son of Qinyuan Road Ten Thousand Households Ju Zhu Er, who was not yet in his twenties, was exceptionally intelligent and brave, leading his troops out first. Wang Zale Tuling led the Miao army to follow, and the people also fought in the streets. Shi De suffered a great defeat, gathering the remaining troops and losing eight or nine out of ten. When they attacked Haiyan, they were again hindered by the Zhong clan of Zhapu. Later, they gained a brave and skilled horseman named Ma Dao, who captured countless Miao soldiers. The southwestern border relied on this and was free from worry; otherwise, Songjiang would not be under Shi Cheng's control.

    Translation Notes

    • 镇松江郡州县正官 is translated as "governing the prefectures, sub-prefectures, and counties of Songjiang" to reflect the administrative hierarchy.
    • 郡称太守州称通守县仍曰尹 is translated as "the prefectural governor in the prefecture, the sub-prefectural governor in the sub-prefecture, and the county magistrate in the county" to clarify the titles.
    • 郡同知称府丞知事曰从事 is translated as "the deputy prefectural governor is called the vice-prefect, and the subordinate officials are referred to as attendants" to maintain the hierarchy.
    • 余则损益而已 is translated as "the rest are variations or modifications" to indicate the flexibility in titles.
    • 南向欲取嘉兴 is translated as "Facing south, they intended to capture Jiaxing" to clarify the direction and intent.
    • 嘉兴则有参政杨旺札勒统领苖獠猺獞名曰达尔罕守御甚坚屡攻不克 is translated as "Jiaxing was defended by the high-ranking official Yang Wang Zale, who led the Miao, Yao, and other ethnic minorities and was titled Darhan. His defenses were very strong, and repeated attacks failed to conquer him" to provide context.
    • 秋八月文炳大举兵临其东门悉为所殱文炳仅以身免 is translated as "In the eighth month of autumn, Wen Bing launched a large-scale military campaign and attacked the eastern gate of Jiaxing, where his forces were completely annihilated. Wen Bing barely escaped with his life" to clarify the sequence of events.
    • 士德又与与敬提兵入杭州军气甚鋭杭州大军敛锋不敌丞相退避萧山 is translated as "Shi De and Yu Jing then led their troops into Hangzhou, where their army was very fierce. The large army of Hangzhou held back and was unable to match them, and the chancellor retreated to Xiaoshan" to maintain the flow.
    • 士德军检刮虏掠罗木营万户普贤努乃庆元路万户矩珠尔之子年未弱冠智勇过人率兵先出旺札勒图领苖军继进民亦挺身巷战士德大溃收拾残兵十丧八九及攻海盐又为乍浦钟氏所挠后得马道骁勇禽获苖军无算西南接境頼此无虞不然松江非士诚 is translated as "The army of Shi De inspected and plundered, and the Ten Thousand Households Luomu Camp, Pu Xian Nu, the son of Qinyuan Road Ten Thousand Households Ju Zhu Er, who was not yet in his twenties, was exceptionally intelligent and brave, leading his troops out first. Wang Zale Tuling
    634

    有矣昆山数为方国珍海军攻击托丁氏徃来説合结为婚婣昆山之民幸遂苏息湖之长兴武康与广德相界花鎗军出没之地虽互为胜然亦不胜其苦所跨三州皆邻勍敌可畏者特集庆一军最盛陆路则无锡宜兴长兴水路则太湖士马震耀舳舻相衔自后长兴防常州又防士德战败被擒俘致集庆俾其作书劝士诚归附士德以身徇之终无降意士诚势穷力迫愿就丞相招安使者徃返讫莫成就仁亲诣江浙省堂具陈自愿休兵息民之意议始定时十八年秋八月也朝廷诏赦其罪后授士诚太尉开府平江士诚以下授爵有差立江淮分省江浙分枢宻院于平江以设其官属

    Explanation

    634

    Kunshan was repeatedly attacked by Fang Guozhen's navy. Ding Shi mediated and formed a marital alliance, allowing the people of Kunshan to finally find peace and rest. The areas of Changxing, Wuchang, and Guangde, which are adjacent to each other, were places where the Flower Spear Army operated. Although they alternated in victory and defeat, they still suffered greatly. The three states they controlled were all neighbors to powerful enemies, with the most formidable being the army of Jiqing, which was the strongest. On land, they defended against attacks from Wuxi, Yixing, and Changxing, while on water, the troops and horses of Taihu Lake were dazzling, with ships lined up in a continuous line. Subsequently, Changxing defended against attacks from Changzhou, and then Shide was defeated and captured, taken to Jiqing. He was made to write a letter to persuade Shicheng to submit, but Shide sacrificed himself to maintain his resolve, showing no intention to surrender. Shicheng, facing dire circumstances and pressure, wished to accept the chancellor's offer of amnesty. The envoys made repeated trips but failed to achieve their goal. Ren personally went to the Jiang-Zhe provincial office to present his willingness to cease hostilities and allow the people to rest. The agreement was finally settled in the eighth month of the eighteenth year of the reign. The court issued an edict to pardon his crimes and then appointed him as Taifu and established an office in Pingjiang. Shicheng and those below him were granted different ranks, and the Jianghuai and Jiang-Zhe provincial offices and the Zhongshu Province were established in Pingjiang to set up their officials.

    635

    降真香
    　　道家者流为人典行醮事曰高功其有行业精白者则必移檄南岳魏夫人请借仙或二或四青鸾导卫翔骛澄空昭道妙徃徃亲见之偶读本草有云降真香出黔南伴和诸杂香烧烟直上天召得盘旋于上注按仙传云烧之或引降醮星辰烧之甚为第一度箓烧之功力极验若然则之来香所致也非欤
    　　宋二十二帝
    　　长编所载宋二十一帝盖自顺宣僖三祖及太祖太宗真仁英神哲徽钦高孝光宁理度少帝并端宗帝昺也
    　　字音
    　　吾衍子行闲居録云舜生诸冯及冯妇等皆音皮氷切古不音符容切也冯妇与徐夫人皆男子三国时有暨艶乃吴人附陆杭传当音结不音暨也
    　　许
    　　綘侯周亚夫自未侯为河内守时许相之曰君后三岁而侯见史记綘侯传注谓索隐曰应劭云河内温人老妪也按楚汉春秋高祖封为鸣雌侯是知妇人亦有封邑

    635

    Final Translation

    ####降真香
    The Taoist tradition has a saying that those who perform rituals and ceremonies are called "high officials" (高功). Among them, those who are pure and virtuous in their conduct are required to send a formal decree to Lady Wei of Mount Heng, requesting the loan of Taoist deities (仙), either two or four. The blue phoenix (青鸾) guides and guards, flying through the clear sky, illuminating the mysteries of the Dao. Often, one sees this phenomenon personally. Upon reading the Bencao (本草), it is said that "降真香" (Jiangzhenxiang) comes from the southern region of Guizhou (黔南), mixed with various other fragrances. When burned, the smoke rises straight to heaven, summoning the deities, who then circle above. According to the Xianzhuan (仙传), burning it may attract the deities to descend, and it is especially effective for rituals involving stars. It is considered the first choice for rituals involving the recitation of scriptures (度箓), and its power is extremely effective in practice. If this is true, then the deities come because of the fragrance, is it not so?

    ####宋二十二帝
    The Changbian (长编) records the 22 emperors of the Song Dynasty, starting from the founding ancestors (顺宣僖三祖) and including Emperor Taizu, Emperor Taizong, Emperor Zhenzong, Emperor Renzong, Emperor Yingzong, Emperor Shenzong, Emperor Zhezong, Emperor Huizong, Emperor Qinzong, Emperor Gaozong, Emperor Xiaozong, Emperor Guangzong, Emperor Ningzong, Emperor Lizong, Emperor Duozong, and Emperor Shao. It also includes Emperor Duanzong and Emperor Bing.

    ####字音
    In Zixing Xianju Lu (子行闲居录) by Wu Yan, it is recorded that Shun was born in Zufeng, and the pronunciation of "冯妇" (Feng Fu) and others is "pí bīng qiē" (皮氷切), not "fú róng qiē" (符容切). "冯妇" and "徐夫人" (Xu Furen) are both men, and during the Three Kingdoms period, there was a person named Jì Yàn (暨艶), who was from the state of Wu. He is mentioned in the biography of Lu Hang (附陆杭传) and should be pronounced as "jié" (结), not "jì" (暨).

    ####许
    Zhou Yafu, the Marquis of Xing (綘侯), was a high official (未侯) and governor of Henan (河内守). At that time, Xǔ Hóng (许) observed him and said, "You will become a marquis in three years." This is recorded in the Records of the Grand Historian (史记) in the biography of the Marquis of Xing (綘侯传). The note in the Suiyin (索隐) by Ying Shao (应劭) states that "Xǔ Hóng" is an old woman from Wēn in Henan. According to Chu-Han Chunqiu (楚汉春秋), Emperor Gaozu of Han enfeoffed Xǔ Hóng as the Marquis of Míngcí (鸣雌侯), indicating that women also have fiefs or estates.

    636

    李玉溪先生
    　　赵公琪字元德官至赠湖广行省参政諡文惠临淄人飘然有神仙思常使方士烧水银硫黄朱砂黄金等物为神丹以资服食有玉溪李简易先生者得道为神仙数访公授以其术乆乆隐去人或以为不死公思之一日见其至喜而固留之先生曰吾逺来甚请具浴公即具浴先生就浴室乆之不闻声日且暮公亲候之见有光昱然在水上圆如初日出不复见先生所在先生书藏公家今稍稍传人间虞文靖序其事如此云
    　　称地为双
    　　尝读金黄华老人诗有招客先开四十双之句殊不可晓近读云南杂志曰夷有田皆种稻其佃作三人使二牛前牵中压而后驱之犁一日为一双以二乏为己二己为角四角为双约有中原四畆地则老人之诗意见矣
    　　骨咄犀
    　　骨咄犀蛇角也其性至毒而能解毒盖以毒攻毒也故曰蛊毒犀唐书有古都国必其地所产今人讹为骨咄耳

    Explanation

    636

    Translation

    Mr. Li Yuxi

    Zhao Gongqi, courtesy name Yuande, was an official who reached the rank of Governor of the Huguang Provincial Government and was posthumously honored with the title Wenhui. He was from Linzi and had a carefree disposition with thoughts of immortality. He often employed Daoist practitioners to burn mercury, sulfur, cinnabar, and gold to create elixirs for consumption. There was a hermit named Li Yuxi, who attained the way and became a deity. He frequently visited Zhao Gongqi to impart his techniques. Eventually, he disappeared, and some believed he had not died. Zhao Gongqi thought about this and one day saw him arrive, greatly pleased and tried to keep him. The hermit said, "I have traveled far and am very tired; please prepare a bath." Zhao Gongqi immediately prepared the bath, and the hermit entered the bathroom. After a long time, no sound could be heard. As evening approached, Zhao Gongqi personally went to check and saw a bright light on the water, round like the sun rising, but he no longer saw the hermit. The hermit's writings were stored in Zhao Gongqi's family and have since gradually spread among people. Yu Wenjing wrote the preface to this account.

    Measuring Land as a Pair

    I once read a poem by the elderly man Jin Huanghua that says, "Invite guests to open forty pairs." I did not understand this at all. Recently, I read the Yunnan Gazetteer, which states that the ethnic minorities in Yunnan plant rice. They use three people and two oxen, with one person leading the oxen, one pressing them, and one driving them to plow. One day of work equals a pair. Two pairs equal a unit, and four units equal a pair, approximately equivalent to four mu of land in Central China. Thus, the meaning of the elderly man's poem becomes clear.

    The Bone-Duo Rhino Horn

    The bone-duo rhino horn is actually a snake's horn. It is extremely poisonous but can also cure poison, following the principle of treating poison with poison. Therefore, it is called the "gutuoxi" rhino horn. The Tang Shu (Book of Tang) mentions the ancient kingdom of Gudu, where this horn must have been produced. Today, people mispronounce it as "bone-duo."

    637

    一门五节
    　　奉化陈氏妇以贞节称者五人初陈元娶竺氏生子侗而元卒竺氏年才二十二义不再适后三十三年卒侗娶璩氏生子瑞泰二人侗亦以疾夭璩氏年二十六后五十八年卒瑞娶王氏生子通甫而瑞复夭王氏年三十后五十五年卒通甫娶楼氏楼氏甫笄归于陈至二十六而寡父母欲夺其志泣不从其姑王氏年老楼氏事之尤谨姑卒敛葬悉如礼子四人长养才娶楼氏生子孟雍孟熈而养才遇疾不愈方卒时楼氏年二十六所守如其姑云
    　　一门三节
    　　陇西李子平氏子茂德聘同郡张氏女年十七归李氏生子庸甫六岁而寡舅姑怜其少也欲嫁之使左右风之即引刀截发以见志乃止茂德之弟仲德亦早卒其妻张氏年二十有八生子庆方齓亦誓不再适从弟希贤妻陈氏二十有四希贤卒时其子度方孕四月守志益坚一门三妇以贞白闻庸至正间仕至同知济南路总管府事推恩赠父同知益州路总管府事陇西郡伯母封陇西郡夫人再调关襄宣慰阶中奉大夫而夫人始卒

    Explanation

    637

    Five Filial Women in One Family

    In the Chen family of Fenghua, there were five women renowned for their chastity. Initially, Chen Yuan married Zhu, who bore him a son named Dong. After Chen Yuan's death, Zhu, at the tender age of twenty-two, refused to remarry out of righteousness. She died thirty-three years later. Dong then married Qu, who bore him two sons, Rui and Tai. Dong also died young, and Qu, at twenty-six, died fifty-eight years later. Rui married Wang, who bore him a son named Tongfu. Rui also died young, and Wang, at thirty, died fifty-five years later. Tongfu married Lou, who, at the age of fifteen, married into the Chen family. She became a widow at twenty-six. Her parents tried to persuade her to remarry, but she wept and refused. Her mother-in-law, Wang, was elderly, and Lou served her with particular care. When Wang died, Lou arranged her funeral and burial according to proper rites. She had four sons, the eldest of whom, Yangcai, married Lou and bore two sons, Mengyong and Mengxi. Yangcai fell ill and did not recover, dying at the age of twenty-six. At that time, Lou was also twenty-six, and she remained steadfast in her chastity, just as her mother-in-law had been.

    Three Filial Women in One Family

    In the Li family of Longxi, there was Li Ziping's son, Maode, who married a woman from the same district. She was seventeen when she married the Li family and bore a son named Yongfu. At the age of six, Yongfu became a widower. His grandparents, moved by her youth, wanted to arrange another marriage for her and sent others to persuade her. She immediately cut off her hair with a knife to show her resolve, and they stopped. Maode's younger brother, Zhongde, also died young, and his wife, Zhang, who was twenty-eight, bore a son named Qing. At the age of two, Qing became a widower, and Zhang vowed never to remarry. Her younger brother, Xixian, also died young, and his wife, Chen, who was twenty-four, was pregnant with their son, Du, at the time of his death. Four months later, Du was born, and Chen held her resolve even more firmly. The three women of the Li family were known for their chastity and purity. Yong, during the Zhi Zheng period, served as the Deputy Governor of the Total Administration of Jinan Road. He was later promoted to the rank of Middle-Fu Daifu in the Guanxiang Xuanwei Bureau, and his mother, the Lady of Longxi County, finally passed away.

    638

    黄龙洞
    　　黄龙洞在吴兴郡北去城闉廿里枕太湖其山皆怪石林立中有一石最尊上大其本小危立如种自石上涌起轻撼则揺动稍加力排辄不动人甚异之洞旁壁立千仞頫瞰不能见底投以石不应以声呼则相答深窅不测每岁旱郡民祷之东坡先生曽游题诗述龙之迹山谷先生书黄龙洞三字刻犹存
    　　黏接纸缝法
    　　王古心先生笔録内一则云方外交青龙镇隆平寺主藏僧永光字絶照访予观物斋时年已八十有四话次因问光前代藏经接缝如一线日乆不脱何也光云古法用楮树汁飞麫白笈末三物调和如糊以之黏接纸缝永不脱解过如胶漆之坚先生上海人井珠
    　　人欲娶妻而未得谓之寻河覔井已娶而料理家事谓之担雪填井男婚女嫁财礼奁具种种不可阙谓之投河奔井凡纳婢仆初来时曰擂盘珠言不拨自动稍乆曰算盘珠言拨之则动既乆曰佛顶珠言终日凝然虽拨亦不动此虽俗谚实切事情

    Explanation

    638

    Yellow Dragon Cave

    The Yellow Dragon Cave is located twenty li north of Wuxing Prefecture, near the city walls, and it rests by Taihu Lake. The mountains are filled with strange stones standing like a forest. Among them, there is one stone that is most respected; it is large at the top but small at the base, standing precariously like a seed, rising from the stone. Gently shaking it causes it to move, but adding a bit of force makes it immovable, which people find very strange. The walls of the cave stand a thousand feet high, and looking down, the bottom cannot be seen. Throwing a stone into it produces no response, but calling out produces an echo. The cave is deep and unfathomable. Every year, the people of the county pray to it during droughts. Su Dongpo once visited and wrote a poem describing the dragon's traces. Huang Tingjian inscribed the three characters "Yellow Dragon Cave" on a stone, which still exists.

    Method of Sticking Paper Seams

    Mr. Wang Guxin recorded in his notes: "A monk named Yongguang, alias Juezhao, who was the custodian of the main collection at Longping Temple in Qinglong Town, visited my Guanwu Study when he was eighty-four years old. During the conversation, I asked him why the seams of the ancient scriptures he had preserved remained unbroken like a thread over time. He said that the ancient method uses the juice of the paper mulberry tree, wheat flour, and white glue powder mixed together like paste. Using this to stick paper seams, they never come apart, even more firmly than glue and lacquer." Mr. Wang was from Shanghai.

    A man who wants to marry but has not yet found a wife is said to be "searching for a river and digging a well." After marriage, when managing household affairs, it is called "carrying snow to fill a well." In the context of marriage, the various items of dowry and gifts that cannot be lacking are called "throwing oneself into a river or well." When servants first arrive, they are called "lump of pearl" because they are active without being prompted. After a while, they are called "abacus beads" because they move when prompted. After a long time, they are called "Buddha's crown pearl" because they remain still even when prompted. These are common sayings that accurately reflect the situation.

    639

    一钱太守庙
    　　一钱太守刘宠庙在绍兴钱清镇王叔能参政过庙下赋诗曰刘宠清名举世传至今遗庙在江边近来仕路多能者也学先生拣大钱
    　　全真教
    　　全真纪实云金主亮贞元元年有吏员咸阳人王中孚者倡全真教谈马邱刘和之其教盛焉章宗泰和四年元学士作紫微观记所载详悉
    　　马孝子
    　　马伯杰山东邹县人父某拜江南行台监察御史不以家行杰独与母居盗起汝頴转畧齐鲁境杰母匿草间母死仓卒不能具棺敛聚石葬郓地西盗入郓城杰伏于墓上众欲驱而前胁以白刃杰大恸曰母在此母在此盗曰此孝子也乃舍之复遗以衣粮既而邑刳于兵失墓所在求之二年得于榛莽中故衣尚存始克迁祔祖茔御史转浙西宪佥留江南者八年遂冐锋镝间走数千里省之钱唐人咸称为马孝子云

    Explanation

    639

    One-Coin Governor Temple
    The One-Coin Governor Temple of Liu Cong, known for his integrity, is located in Qianqing Town, Shaoxing. Wang Shuneng, a political figure, passed by the temple and wrote a poem:
    "Liu Cong's reputation for integrity is famous throughout the world.
    To this day, the remains of the temple are by the river.
    Recently, there are many capable officials in the officialdom who learn from Mr. Liu to choose the larger coins."

    Quanzhen School
    According to the record of the Quanzhen School, during the first year of Emperor Shizong of Jin's reign, a low-ranking official from Xianyang named Wang Zhongfu advocated the Quanzhen School, speaking about the four founders: Ma Yu, Qiu Chuji, Liu Chong, and Wang Chongyang. The school became very popular. In the fourth year of Emperor Zhangzong of Jin's reign, a scholar of the Yuan dynasty wrote a record of the Ziwu Temple, which provides detailed information.

    Ma the Filial Son
    Ma Berjie was from Zou County, Shandong province. His father was a high-ranking official in the southern region and did not leave home, so Ma Berjie lived alone with his mother. Bandits rose up in Ruyang and Yuyang and moved through the territories of Qi and Lu, where Ma Berjie hid in the grass. His mother died suddenly, and he could not prepare a proper burial, so he buried her with stones in the west of Yuncheng. When the bandits entered Yuncheng, Ma Berjie lay on the grave, and they threatened him with swords. He cried bitterly, saying, "Mother is here," and the bandits said, "This is a filial son," and left him alone. They also left him clothes and food. Later, the town was destroyed by soldiers, and he lost the location of the grave. After searching for two years, he found it in dense, overgrown vegetation, where the old clothes still existed. He finally moved and buried her with the ancestors. The high-ranking official then served as a high-ranking official in Zhejiang province for eight years, risking his life through dangerous situations and traveling thousands of miles to Qiantang (modern-day Hangzhou). People there all called him Ma the Filial Son.

    640

    杨贞妇
    　　天台金沙里女王静安年十七归同邑杨伯瑞瑞为枢宻院断事官未几死于兵静安守节不嫁权贵争求之至截发自刭不殊
    　　窑器
    　　宋叶寘垣斋笔衡云陶器自舜时便有三代迄于秦汉所谓甓器是也今土中得者其质浑厚不务色泽末俗尚靡不贵金玉而贵铜磁遂有秘色窑器世言钱氏有国日越州烧进不得臣庶用故云秘色陆诗九秋风露越窑开夺得千峯翠色来如向中霄盛沆瀣共稽中散鬭遗桮乃知唐世已有非始于钱氏本朝以定州白磁器有芒不堪用
    遂命汝州造青窑器故河北唐邓耀州悉有之汝窑为魁江南则处州龙泉县窑质颇麤厚政和间京师自置窑烧造名曰官窑中兴渡江有邵成章提举后苑号邵局袭故京遗制置窑于修内司造青器名内窑澄泥为范极其精制油色莹彻为世所珍后郊坛下别立新窑比旧窑大不侔矣余如乌泥窑余杭窑续窑皆非官窑比若谓旧越窑不复见矣

    Explanation

    640

    Yang Zhenfu
    In the village of Jinsha, TianTai, there was a woman named Wang Jing'an, who at the age of seventeen married Yang Berui, a magistrate of the Privy Council in the same county. Shortly after, Yang died in battle. Wang Jing'an remained chaste and refused to marry, despite the efforts of powerful nobles who sought her. In her defiance, she cut off her hair and attempted to strangle herself, but she survived.

    Ceramics
    According to Ye Zhi of the Song dynasty, in his work The Balance of the Wall Studio, pottery has existed since the time of Emperor Shun, and from the Three Dynasties to the Qin and Han periods, it was known as "tile ware." Those found in the earth today are thick and substantial, with little emphasis on color. In later times, people valued gold and jade but preferred copper and porcelain, leading to the creation of the "secret color" kilns. It is said that during the reign of the Qian family (the Southern Tang), the Yue kilns produced porcelain that was so exquisite it could not be used by commoners, hence the term "secret color." Lu Guan's poem describes the opening of the Yue kilns in autumn, where they "stole the emerald green of a thousand peaks" and were said to be so fine that they could hold the dew of the middle sky, competing with the elegance of the Ji family's cups. This shows that such porcelain already existed in the Tang dynasty, not starting with the Qian family. In our dynasty, the white porcelain from Dingzhou was not suitable due to its sharp edges, so the kilns in Ruzhou were ordered to produce green porcelain. As a result, Hebei, Tang, Deng, and Yaozhou all had such porcelain, with the Ruzhou kiln being the most renowned. In the south, the kilns in Longquan County, Quzhou, were also famous, though their quality was somewhat coarse. During the Zhenghe era, the capital city established its own kilns, known as the "official kilns," which were managed by the Shao Chengzhang in the imperial garden, called the Shao Bureau. They followed the traditions of the old capital and set up kilns in the Xiu Neisi to produce green porcelain, known as the "inner kilns." The molds were made from refined clay, and the finished products were extremely delicate, with a smooth, translucent glaze that was highly prized. Later, a new kiln was established at the altar, which was far superior to the old ones. Other kilns, such as the Wu Mudao, Yuhang, and Xu Yao, were not as prestigious as the official kilns, and it is said that the old Yue kilns are no longer in existence.

    641

    墨
    　　上古无墨竹挺防漆而书中古方以石磨汁或云是延安石液至魏晋时始有墨丸乃漆烟松煤夹和为之所以晋人多用凹心砚者欲磨墨贮沈耳自后有螺子墨亦墨丸之遗制唐高丽岁贡松烟墨用多年老松烟和麋鹿胶造成至唐末墨工奚超与其子廷珪自易水渡江迁居歙州南唐赐姓李氏廷珪父子之墨始集大成然亦尚用松烟廷珪初名廷邽故世有奚廷珪墨又有李廷珪墨或有作庭珪字者伪也墨亦不精宋熈丰间张遇供御墨用油烟入脑麝金箔谓之龙香剂元祐间潘谷墨见称于时自后蜀中蒲大韶梁杲徐伯常及雪斋齐峯叶茂实翁彦卿等出世不乏墨惟茂实得法清黒不凝滞彦卿莫能及中统至元以来各有所传可以仿古

    Explanation

    641

    In ancient times, there was no ink; people wrote on bamboo slips coated with lacquer. During the middle period, ink was made by grinding stones, and it was said to be made from stone liquid in Yan'an. It wasn't until the Wei and Jin dynasties that ink pellets began to appear, made by mixing lacquer smoke and pine soot. People in the Jin dynasty often used hollow-center inkstones to store the ink. Later, there was a type of ink pellet called "spiral ink," which was a legacy of the ink pellet tradition. During the Tang dynasty, Goryeo (Korea) annually presented pine soot ink to the court, made using old pine soot and deer antler glue. By the end of the Tang dynasty, the ink maker Xichao and his son Tinggui, originally named Tinggui, moved from Yishui to Shezhou. The Southern Tang dynasty bestowed the surname Li on them, and the ink made by Li Tinggui and his father was considered the pinnacle of ink-making. However, they still used pine soot. Since Tinggui's original name was Tinggui, the world also referred to his ink as "Xu Tinggui ink" or "Li Tinggui ink." Some mistakenly wrote it as "Tinggui," which is incorrect, and the ink was not of high quality. During the Xining and Fengzhen periods of the Song dynasty, Zhang Yu provided ink for the imperial court, and he made a type of ink called "Dragon Fragrance Agent" using lampblack, musk, and gold leaf. During the Yuanyou period, Pan Gu was also a renowned ink maker. In Sichuan, there were several famous ink makers such as Pu Dashao, Liang Gao, Xu Bochang, Xue Zhai, Qi Feng, Ye Maoshi, and Wang Yanqing. Among them, only Ye Maoshi mastered the technique of making clear, non-sticky ink, which Wang Yanqing could not match. Since the Zongtong and Yuan periods, there have been various transmissions of ink-making techniques, and modern methods can still emulate ancient practices.

    642

    唐
