    645

    混沌材　万壑松　雪夜冰　玉涧鸣泉
    　　石上清泉　　　　秋塘寒玉
    　　九霄环佩
    　　戯语
    　　至正丙申高邮兵累攻嘉兴不克或人撰戯语云史帅一日下令行兵叅谋掌史进言自古行师必先祭旗史曰王元帅破松江时曽祭否答曰不祭史曰王元帅不祭我也不祭盖祭济字音同传以为笑又有一説红军与苖军战不胜禀主帅曰彼中军前有十丈大旗旗上字大元统兵官五字帅曰我此间亦效之旗成军吏禀所写何字帅曰八分书写赵王令既而写赵字未成才写得走字传报苖军到走走走二说皆可捧腹
    　　日家安命法
    　　日家者流以日月五星及计罗炁孛四余气度过宫迟留伏逆推人之生年日月时可以知休咎定夀夭其书曰百中经经首有安命法曰周天宿度十二宫安命例凡十叶有术士以其例节为一叶简明易见其法但看本生日太阳所何度便以本生时加在上向下逐宫虚数如下面已尽则又于此行自上而下见夘住即是此度安命真防径也

    Explanation

    645

    Formless materials, a thousand valleys of pines, snowy nights with ice, the ringing spring in the jade stream.
    Clear spring on the stone, cold jade in the autumn pond.
    Ringing jade from the nine heavens.
    Playful words.
    During the reign of Emperor Ling in the year 1356, the forces of Gao You repeatedly attacked Jiaxing but failed to capture it. Someone wrote a playful story: One day, the commander Shi ordered an advance, and the chief counselor Shi advised that in ancient times, one must first sacrifice to the flag before setting out. Shi asked, "Did the General Wang sacrifice when he broke through Songjiang?" The answer was, "He did not." Shi replied, "If General Wang did not sacrifice, I will not either." The reason was that the character "济" (jì) sounds the same as "祭" (jì), which means "to sacrifice." This was passed on and became a joke. Another story goes that the Red Army was defeated by the Miao Army. The chief counselor reported to the commander, "Their central army has a ten-zhang tall flag with the characters '大元统兵官五字' (Da Yuan Tongbing Guan Wu Zi) on it." The commander said, "I will also have one made." When the flag was completed, the military clerk asked what characters should be written. The commander said, "Write the Zhao Wang's command in eight-point script." They started writing the character "赵" (Zhao), but before it was finished, they wrote the character "走" (zou) instead. The messenger reported to the Miao Army, "They are running, running, running." Both stories are laughable.
    The method of determining one's fate by the Sun.
    The method of determining one's fate by the Sun involves using the positions of the sun, moon, five planets, and the four additional stars (Jupiter, Saturn, Mars, and Venus) to calculate the degree of the sun at the time of one's birth. By adding the hour of birth to the degree of the sun, one can determine the position of the sun in the twelve palaces. If the bottom row is exhausted, one continues from the top. The method is simple and easy to understand. The true path to determining one's fate lies in seeing where the sun stops in the degree.

    646

    子丑寅卯辰巳午未申酉戍亥

    　　淮涡神
    　　泗州塔下相传泗州大圣鎻水母处谬也按地志云水神在临淮县山之下形若猕猴缩鼻高额青躯白首金目雪牙颈伸百尺力逾九象禹获之鎻其颈于山之足淮水乃安流注海迩来渔者知鎻所在古岳渎经云禹治水三至桐栢山获淮涡水神曰无支祈乃命庚辰制之鎻于山之足淮水乃安唐永泰初楚州有渔人夜钓山下其钩为物所掣沈水视之见大鐡鎻绕山足一兽形如青猿兀若昏醉
    涎沫腥不可近又东坡濠州涂山诗川鎻支祈水尚浑注程演曰异闻集载古岳渎经禹治水至桐栢山获淮涡水神名曰巫支祈善应对辨淮之浅深源之逺近而神曰庚辰者鎻于山之足淮乃安流唐时有渔者钓得一古鎻牵出其末有如猕猴者盖此物也国史补曰楚州渔人于淮中钓得古鐡鎻刺史李阳大集人力引之鎻穷有青猕猴跃出水而逝山海经水兽好为云雨禹鎻于军山之下其名无支祈

    Explanation

    646

    The Twelve Earthly Branches

    1. Zi (子)
    2. Chou (丑)
    3. Yin (寅)
    4. Mao (卯)
    5. Chen (辰)
    6. Si (巳)
    7. Wu (午)
    8. Wei (未)
    9. Shen (申)
    10. You (酉)
    11. Xu (戌)
    12. Hai (亥)

    The Huai River Water Deity

    Beneath the Pagoda of Sizhou, it is said that the revered Sizhou Great Saint restrains the Water Mother. However, this is a misconception. According to local records, the water deity resides beneath Gui Mountain in Linhuai County. It is shaped like a macaque, with a small nose, high forehead, blue body, white head, golden eyes, and snow-white teeth. Its neck stretches a hundred feet, and its strength exceeds that of nine elephants. Emperor Yu obtained it and locked its neck at the base of Gui Mountain, after which the Huai River flowed peacefully into the sea. Since then, fishermen have known the location of the lock.

    The Yue Du Jing (Classic of Mountains and Seas) recounts that Emperor Yu tamed the waters three times, reaching Tongbi Mountain, where he captured the Huai River water deity, Wuzhiqi. He then ordered Gengchen to restrain it, locking it at the base of Gui Mountain, after which the Huai River flowed peacefully. In the early years of the Yongtai era in the Tang dynasty, a fisherman from Chuzhou was fishing at the foot of the mountain at night. His hook was pulled by something and sank into the water. When he looked, he saw a large iron lock around the base of the mountain, with a creature shaped like a green monkey, sitting dazed and drunk. Its drool was foul and unbearable to approach.

    In Su Shi's poem Chuan Suo Zhi Qi (Locking Wuzhiqi) from Huizhou, the water was still turbid. The Yue Du Jing records that Emperor Yu tamed the waters three times, reaching Tongbi Mountain, where he captured the Huai River water deity, Wuzhiqi, who was skilled in responding to questions and discerning the depth and source of the Huai River. The deity Gengchen was said to have restrained it at the base of Gui Mountain, after which the Huai River flowed peacefully. In the Tang dynasty, a fisherman caught an ancient iron lock in the Huai River. The governor Li Yang gathered people to pull it, and when the lock was exhausted, a green monkey jumped out of the water and disappeared.

    The Shan Hai Jing (Classic of Mountains and Seas) describes water creatures that often create clouds and rain. Emperor Yu locked Wuzhiqi under Jun Mountain, and its name is Wuzhiqi.

    647

    寄衣
    　　洞庭刘氏有夫叶正甫乆客都门因寄衣侑以诗云情同牛女隔天河又喜秋来得一过岁岁寄郎身上服丝丝是妾手中梭剪声自觉和肠断线脚那能抵泪多长短只依先去様不知肥瘦近如何【先去亦作旧时】

    　　辍耕録卷二十九
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　辍耕録卷三十　　　　　元　陶宗仪　撰印章制度
    　　周礼玺节郑氏注云玺节者今之印章也按许慎説文云印执政所持信也徐锴曰从爪手以持信也卫宏曰秦以前民皆以金玉为印龙虎钮惟其所好然则秦以来天子独以印称玺独又以玉羣臣莫敢用也七雄之时臣下玺始称曰印汉制诸侯王金玺玺之言信也古者印玺通名汉旧仪云诸侯王黄金玺槖佗钮又曰玺谓刻曰某王之玺列侯黄金印钮文曰某侯之章丞相太尉与三公前后左右将军黄金印钮文曰章中二千石银印钮文曰章千石六百石四百石至二百石以上皆铜印鼻钮文曰印建武元年诏诸侯王金印綟绶公侯金印紫绶中二千

    Explanation

    647

    Final Translation

    Sending Clothes
    A woman from Dingtong named Liu had a husband named Ye Zhengfu, who had been away in the capital for a long time. She sent him clothes along with a poem that read: "Our love is like that of the Cowherd and the Weaving Maid, separated by the Milky Way. I am also glad that autumn has come, allowing me to visit once more. Year after year, I send you clothes to wear. Every thread is woven by my hands. The sound of cutting makes me feel sorrow naturally. The seams cannot match the tears I shed. The length and fit are only according to the style from before. I wonder how your figure and weight are these days." [Before can also be read as in the past.]

    Cutting the Land
    The Book of Rites commentary by Zheng Xuan states that "玺节" refers to today's seals. According to Xu Shen's Shuowen Jiezi, a seal is a token held by those in power. Xu Kai said it is composed of the character for hand holding the character for faith. Wei Hong said that before the Qin dynasty, common people used seals made of gold and jade with dragon and tiger knobs, depending on their preferences. Thus, since the Qin dynasty, only the emperor used the term "玺" for his seal, which was made of jade, and no minister dared to use it. During the Warring States period, ministers began to call their seals "印." In the Han dynasty, the system was as follows: kings were given golden seals with a knob shaped like a "tuo" (a type of knob), inscribed with "某王之玺." Marquises received golden seals with a turtle knob, inscribed with "某侯之章." Chancellors, Grand Commanders, and the Three Dukes (Grand Tutor, Grand Guardian, and Grand Instructor) received golden seals with a turtle knob, inscribed with "章." Officials of the rank of 2,000 stones received silver seals with a turtle knob, inscribed with "章." Officials of the ranks of 1,000 stones, 600 stones, 400 stones, and 200 stones or above received copper seals with a nose knob, inscribed with "印." In the first year of Jianwu, an edict was issued that kings would receive golden seals with a purple ribbon, and marquises would receive golden seals with a purple ribbon. Officials of the rank of 2,000 stones would receive silver seals with a purple ribbon.

    648

    石以上银印青绶千石至四百石以下铜印黒绶及黄绶陈制金章或钮钮兽钮豹钮银章或钮熊钮罴钮羔钮鹿钮银印或珪钮兎钮铜印率环钮吾衍云汉有摹印其法只是方正篆法与隶相通后人不识古印妄意盘屈且以为法大可笑也多见古家藏得汉印字皆方正近乎隶书此即摹印也玉球啸堂集古録所载古印正与相合凡屈曲盘囘唐篆始如此今碑刻有鲁公官诰尚书省印可考其説汉晋印章皆用白文大不过寸许朝爵印文皆铸盖择日封拜可缓者也军中印文多凿盖急于行令不可缓者也古无押字以印章为官职信令故如此耳唐

    Explanation

    648

    In ancient China, officials of a certain rank and above were granted silver seals with blue ribbons, while those ranked from a thousand shi down to four hundred shi were given copper seals with black or yellow ribbons. The imperial system also included gold seals with various animal-shaped knobs, such as turtles, tigers, and beasts, as well as silver seals with knobs resembling bears, pines, lambs, and deer. Silver seals could also have knobs shaped like jade discs or rabbits, while copper seals typically had circular knobs. Wu Yan, a scholar, noted that during the Han dynasty, the practice of copying ancient seals was prevalent, but the method was simple and straightforward, combining the regular and clerical scripts. Later generations, however, misunderstood these seals, interpreting their square and regular style as complex and twisted, which he found laughable. He observed that many ancient seals in private collections from the Han dynasty were indeed simple and close to the style of clerical script, confirming his point. The Yuqiu Xiaotang Ji Gu Lu (Record of Ancient Seals from the Yuqiu Xiaotang), a historical document, also supports this view, showing that the complexity and twisting of seals only began during the Tang dynasty. For example, the seals found in the Lu Gong Guan Gao (Edict of Duke Lu) and the Shangshu Sheng Yin (Seal of the Ministry of Works) from the Tang dynasty are examples of this style. In contrast, seals from the Han and Jin dynasties were all in the simple and regular white text style, typically no larger than an inch in diameter. Official seals for titles and ranks were usually cast and sealed on a specific day, allowing for a more deliberate process. However, military seals were often carved on the spot, as they needed to be issued quickly to carry out urgent commands. In ancient times, seals were used as a form of official signature and command, a practice that became more common during the Tang dynasty.

    649

    用朱文古法渐废至宋南渡絶无知此者故后宋印文皆大谬白文印皆用汉平正方直字不可圆纵有斜笔亦当取巧写过三字印右一边一字左一边两字者以两字处与一字处相等不可两字中断又不可十分相接四字印若前二字交界有空后二字无空湏当空一画别之字有有脚无脚故言及此不然一邉见分一邉不分非法度也轩斋等印古无此式唯唐相李泌有耑居堂白文玉印或可照例终是白文非古法不若只从朱文朱文印或用杂体篆不可太怪择其近人情免费辞说白文印用崔子玉写张平子碑上字及汉器上并碑盖印章等字最为第一凡

    Explanation

    649

    The ancient method of using red seal characters gradually fell into disuse by the time of the Southern Song Dynasty, and there was no one who knew this anymore. Therefore, the seal texts of the later Song Dynasty were all greatly erroneous. White seal texts all use Han-style flat, square, and upright characters, and cannot be round or vertical. Even if there are slanted strokes, they should be written with a touch of ingenuity. For three-character seals with one character on the right and two characters on the left, the space for two characters should be equal to the space for one character, and the two characters should not be interrupted or too closely connected. For four-character seals, if there is a gap at the junction of the first two characters, the last two characters should not have a gap, and a stroke should be left to distinguish them. Characters have those with feet and those without feet, so I mention this. Otherwise, if one side is divided and the other is not, it is not in accordance with the rules. Seals for study rooms, etc., did not exist in ancient times, but Li Bi of the Tang Dynasty had a white seal text called "Zhuangju Tang" which can be followed as an example. However, white seal texts are not in the ancient style, so it is better to follow the red seal method. Red seal texts may use mixed-style seal script, but it should not be too bizarre, and one should choose those that are close to human feelings and free from empty rhetoric. White seal texts should use the characters written by Cui Ziyu on the stele of Zhang Pingzi, as well as the characters on Han artifacts and the seals on stele covers, which are the best.

    650

    姓名表字古有法式不可用杂篆及朱文白文印必逼于邉不可有空空便不古朱文印不可逼邉湏当以字中空白得中处为相去庶免印出与邉相倚无意思耳字宜细四旁有出笔皆带邉邉湏细于字邉若一体印出时四邉虚纸皆昻起未免邉肥于字也非见印多不能晓此黏邉朱文建业文房之法多有人依欵识字式作印此大不可盖汉时印法不曽如此三代时却又无印学者慎此周礼虽有玺节及职金掌其媺恶揭而玺之之説注曰印其实手执之印也正面刻字如秦氏玺而不可印印则字皆反矣古人以之表信不问字反淳朴如此若战国时苏秦六印制

    Explanation

    650

    As for names and courtesy names, there were ancient rules: one cannot use mixed seal script or red and white seal script. The seal must not be too close to the edge; there should be no empty space, as this would not be ancient. Red seal script must not be too close to the edge; it should be spaced according to the middle of the blank space in the characters, to avoid the seal appearing to lean against the edge. The characters should be fine, and any strokes extending from the sides should touch the edge. The border should be finer than the character edges. If printed as one piece, the four edges of the paper will lift up, which will inevitably make the border thicker than the characters. One must see many seals to understand this. The method of Jianye writing room, which involves red seal script close to the edge, is followed by many people who follow the pattern of the inscription style to make seals. This is not advisable, as the seal methods of the Han dynasty were not like this. During the Three Dynasties period, there were no seals. Scholars should be cautious about this. The Rites of Zhou (周礼) mentions seals and tokens, and the management of good and bad things. The note says that the seal is actually a hand-held seal. The front is carved with characters like the Qin dynasty seal but cannot be used for printing; if used for printing, the characters will be reversed. Ancients used it to express trust, regardless of the characters being reversed, it remains simple and rustic. If during the Warring States period, the six seals of Su Qin were like this.

    651

    度未闻淮南子人间训曰鲁君召子贡授以大将军印刘安寓言而先辞耳道号唐人虽有不曽有印故不可以道号作印用也三字屋扁唐印有法凡印文中有一二字忽有自然空缺不可映带者听其自空古印多如此凡印仆有古人印式二册一为官印一为私印具列所以实为甚详不若啸堂集古録所载只具音释也凡名印不可妄写或姓名相合或加印章等字或兼用印章字曰姓某印章不若只用印字最为正也二名者可囘文写姓下着印字在右二名在左是也单名者曰姓某之印却不可囘文写名印内不得着氏字表德可加氏字宜审之表字印只用二字

    Explanation

    651

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    652

    此为正式近人或并姓氏于其上曰某氏某若作姓某父古虽有此称系他人美已却不可入印人多好古不论其原不为俗乱可也汉人三字印非姓及无印字者皆非名印盖字印不当用印字以乱名汉张安字防君有印曰张防君【右一字左二字】唐吕温字化光有印曰吕化光此亦三字表德式诸印下有空处悬之最佳不可妄意伸开或加屈曲务欲填满若写得有道理自然不觉空也字多无空不必问此李阳冰曰摹印之法有四功侔造化防受神谓之神笔画之外得微妙法谓之竒艺精于一规矩方圆谓之工緐简相参布置不紊谓之巧赵彦卫云古印

    Explanation

    652

    This is the proper way for recent people to address themselves, sometimes adding their surname before their given name, called "Mr. Such-and-Such." If they were to address themselves as "Mr. Such-and-Such's father," an ancient honorific form, it should not be used on seals. Most people prefer the ancient ways, regardless of their origins, and do not let modern customs disrupt them, which is acceptable. In the Han dynasty, seals with three characters, unless they include the full name or the word 'seal,' are not name seals. Name seals should not use the word 'seal' to confuse with the name. For example, Han dynasty official Zhang An, whose courtesy name was Fangjun, had a seal inscribed as "Zhang Fangjun" (one character on the right and two on the left). Tang dynasty official Lü Wen, whose courtesy name was Huaguang, had a seal inscribed as "Lü Huaguang." This is also a three-character seal for expressing virtue or honor. It is best to leave some empty space below the seal for hanging. Do not arbitrarily extend or curve the characters to fill up the space. If the writing is done well, the empty space will not be noticed. If there are many characters, there will naturally be no empty space, and it is not something to worry about. Li Yangbing said that the methods of seal engraving have four levels: the first, which is so skilled that it seems like divine intervention, is called "divine"; the second, which involves subtle techniques beyond the strokes, is called "wonderful"; the third, which is about precision within the rules of round and square shapes, is called "skilled"; the fourth, which balances complexity and simplicity without disorder, is called "ingenious." Zhao Yanwei commented on ancient seals.

    653

    文作白文盖用以印泥紫泥封诏是也今之米印及仓敖印近之矣自有纸始用朱字间有为白字者通典云北齐有木印长一尺广二寸五分背上为鼻钮长九寸厚一寸广七分腹下隐起文曰督摄万几惟以印籍缝今龊合缝条印盖原于此秦有八体书三曰刻符即古所谓缪五曰摹印萧子良以刻符摹印合为一体徐锴谓符者竹而中刻之字形半分理应别为一体摹印屈曲填宻则秦玺文也子良误合之宣和谱四卷杨克一圗书谱一卷【又名集古印格】王厚之复斋印谱顔叔夏古印谱二卷姜防集古印谱一卷吾衍古印文二卷赵孟頫印史二卷银工

    Explanation

    653

    White text is used for sealing official documents with purple clay, which is the practice of the purple clay seal. Modern seals used for rice and granaries are similar to this practice. Since the advent of paper, red characters have been used, with occasional use of white characters. According to the Tongdian, during the Northern Qi dynasty, there was a wooden seal one foot long and two and a half inches wide, with a nose-shaped knob on its back, nine inches long, one inch thick, and seven inches wide. On its underside, there were hidden inscriptions that read, "To oversee myriad affairs, only the seal can record the seams." This is the origin of the modern practice of sealing seams with a seal. The Qin dynasty had eight script styles, the third being engraved symbols, which is the ancient term for miuqi. The fifth was seal carving. Xiao Ziliang combined engraved symbols and seal carving into one category, but Xu Ke argued that engraved symbols were carved on bamboo with half-character shapes and should be a separate category. Seal carving, with its intricate and dense lines, is the style of the Qin seals. Xiao Ziliang mistakenly combined them. The Xuanhe Spectrum has four volumes, Yang Ke's Book Spectrum has one volume, Wang Houzhi's Repeated Studio Seal Spectrum has one volume, Yan Shuxia's Ancient Seal Spectrum has two volumes, Jiang Fang's Ancient Seal Spectrum has one volume, Wu Yan's Ancient Seal Texts has two volumes, and Zhao Mengfu's Seal History has two volumes. Silver workers are also involved in this craft.

    654

    浙西银工之精于手艺表表有声者屈指不多数也朱碧山【嘉兴魏塘】　谢君余【平江】　谢君和【同上】　唐俊卿【松江】祖孝子
    　　祖孝子浩然字养吾建宁浦城人世儒家至元中盗黄华起政和朝廷命将帅师徃讨未至盗已就缚囘军经浦城焚其庐舍孝子母全氏遭掠而北是时孝子年六岁母子相失独与父居不闻问者二十又八年至大三年福建阃府檄为三山书院山长将之任或告之曰而母在河南而不能名其处孝子欣然弃职辞父为河南行既渡江抵河南每舍逆旅行道途间闻操南音者必就与语庶防有所遇也当时从军之人犹有存者或曰此有赵副使故为军校归自军中得妇人全氏非而母也耶赵死而家替全氏归一古氏挈之而南当在汝邓间耳孝子知母

    Explanation

    654

    Among the silver craftsmen in Zhe West, those renowned for their exceptional skills are so few that they can be counted on one's fingers: Zhu Bishan of Weitang, Jiaxing; Xie Junyu of Pingjiang; Xie Junhe of the same place; and Tang Junqing of Songjiang. There was also Zu Xiaozǐ, whose courtesy name was Yang Wu, from Pucheng, Jianning. He was from a scholarly family. During the reign of Emperor Shizu of the Yuan Dynasty, a bandit named Huang Hua rose in Zhenghe. The court ordered generals and troops to go and suppress them, but before they arrived, the bandits were already captured. On their return, the troops passed through Pucheng and burned his house. The mother of the filial son, Ms. Quan, was taken captive and taken north. At that time, the filial son was six years old, and he lost his mother. He lived alone with his father, and there was no news of his mother for twenty-eight years. In the third year of the Zhida era, the governor of Fujian issued an edict appointing him as the principal of the Sannshan Academy, and he was about to take up his post. Someone told him, "Your mother is in Henan, but you do not know her exact location." The filial son happily resigned his position, bid farewell to his father, and set out for Henan. After crossing the river and arriving in Henan, he stayed at inns along the way. Whenever he heard someone speaking in a southern accent, he would approach and talk to them, hoping to meet his mother. At that time, some of the soldiers who had served in the army were still alive. Someone said, "There is a Deputy Magistrate Zhao, who was formerly a military officer. He returned from the army with a woman named Quan. Is she your mother?" After Zhao died, his family declined, and Ms. Quan returned to the custody of Ms. Gu, who took her south. She was probably in the area of Ru and Deng. The filial son knew his mother.

    655

    定在惊喜遂囘汝州抵鵶路山不遇行八百里至牛蹄白石不遇又行七百余里至阳崔桥又不遇然自离汝州行路既逺知母所乡停车道傍投宿旅舍举其状以问人颇有相酬答可物色或指唐州以告曰彼有别盖山可寻讨也孝子梦神人顾而言有月圆再圆之语既寤言犹在耳益喜忭自崔桥三百余里至别盖访其母在焉既见相与抱持涕泣七月之望也神人之言于是有徴矣留别盖半月舟具奉母南归当时闻其事者自朝廷达官以至湖海名胜莫不歌诗以美之多至数十百篇徃徃举朱夀昌事以为比防稽韩庄节先生【性】作孝子传行于世

    Explanation

    655

    In a moment of surprise, he returned to Ruzhou, arriving at Yalu Mountain without finding what he sought. He then traveled eight hundred li to Niutiji Baishi, still without success. Continuing for another seven hundred li, he reached Fuyang Cuiqiao, yet still found nothing. Despite the long journey, he knew the direction of his mother and stopped by the roadside to stay at an inn. He described his situation to the locals, who gave him some responses, with some suggesting to look in Tangzhou, where there is a different mountain to search. The filial son dreamed of a divine figure who spoke of the moon being full again. Upon waking, the words still rang in his ears, increasing his joy. From Cuiqiao, he traveled another three hundred li to Biegai, where he found his mother. They embraced and wept together on the seventh day of the seventh month, and the words of the divine figure were thus proven true. He stayed at Biegai for half a month, preparing boats, and took his mother back south. At the time, many people, from court officials to scholars, wrote poems praising his story, with many dozens or even hundreds of poems. Often, the story of Zhu Shouchang was cited as a comparison, and Han Zhuangjie Mr. Xing wrote a biography of the filial son that circulated in the world.

    656

    白日圜文
    　　周易痴处馆讲授賔主不合遂作白日圜文飘然而去其文曰听之不闻视之不见子以我为隐乎用之则行舍之则藏吾亦从此逝矣未得青云路且坐白日圜饭疏食乐亦在中素贫贱不愿乎外兹承贤主人不取通士乃求拘儒匪我求童取其交以道餽以礼择师教子弟盖亦据于德依于仁圜土而居重门以待当尔耳不听淫目不视恶将以塞其兊闭其聪然而口不絶吟手不停披安能存其心养其性黄芽若就白发已空常念流地上之白水真人且作鎻洞门之清溪道士子其子亲其亲固宜造次必于是颠沛必于是尔为尔我为我安肯哀矜而辟焉敖惰而辟焉不越文字之间自行束修以上受风魔贬为自在囚口出雌

    Explanation

    656

    White Day Round Text

    During the time of the I Ching (Book of Changes), while teaching at the Cui Residence Hall, the host and guest did not get along, so he composed the White Day Round Text and left abruptly. The text reads: "One hears it but does not hear it, one sees it but does not see it. Do you think I am hiding? When used, it moves forward; when abandoned, it hides. I too shall depart from here. Not yet having found the path to high status, I shall sit in the White Day Round, eat simple food, and find joy within. Having been poor and lowly, I do not desire external things. Now, I accept the wise host who does not seek a well-rounded person but instead seeks a narrow-minded scholar. It is not I who seek the young student; I accept him as a friend and teach him through the way of virtue and propriety. I choose a teacher to educate my children, based on virtue and relying on benevolence. I live in a round earth and have multiple doors to guard against, as one should. I do not listen to the eyes that are corrupted, nor do I look at evil, in order to block the ears and close the mind. However, the mouth does not stop reciting, and the hand does not stop reading, how can one preserve the heart and cultivate the nature? If the yellow sprout is about to grow, the white hair has already been lost. I often think of the pure water flowing on the ground, the true person, and the secluded clear stream, the Taoist priest. Treat others' children as your own and treat others' parents as your own, it is indeed appropriate to be this way in haste and distress. You are you and I am I, how can one avoid compassion and laziness? Not exceeding the bounds of words, those who bring gifts of respect, condemned to a free prisoner, the mouth speaks softly."

    657

    黄用狙翁朝四暮三之术目生虚白披羲皇天一地二之圗有朋自逺方来与进也与退也使君从此中入或止之或尼之俾我行其庭不见其人而子过我门不入我室望而未见招之不来所不与同心指苍天而为证亦欲从游耳曰黄昏以为期不以我为贫知有时为养所愿诸生人十已千以一识百师也过商也不及尚得夫子之中庸参也鲁囘也如愚竟传圣人之道统而某诗书无祟笔墨有灵蝉蜕污浊之中凤翔尘埃之表排云呌阊阖吐三千丈豪气之沈埋乘风归蓬莱诉百万亿颠厓之辛苦藩篱既剖门闼洞开纵意所如从吾所好口説五千言乘牛出函谷愿与关门令尹游乎腰纒十万贯骑上州皆曰闭户先生来也

    Explanation

    657

    A translation of the source text is as follows:

    The Yellow Emperor used the monkey trainer's morning four peaches and evening three peaches strategy, giving birth to an empty white eye and unfolding the Fuxi diagram of heaven and earth. Friends come from afar, both advancing and retreating. Let the gentleman enter from here, sometimes stopping them, sometimes hindering them, so that when I walk in the courtyard, I do not see anyone. Looking but not seeing, not invited, not coming, what I do not agree with, pointing to the sky as evidence. Also wanting to follow and learn. Take the evening as the time, not considering me poor. Knowing that sometimes it is for nurturing, wishing that out of ten people, a thousand can be cultivated. The teacher is excessive, Yan Hui falls short, but both can be considered as the mean of Confucius. Zengzi is slow, Yan Hui seems foolish, but they ultimately transmit the way of the sage. The Book of Songs and Book of Documents are not harmful, the brush and ink are alive. Shedding the shell from the mire, soaring like a phoenix above the dust. Breaking through the clouds, calling to the gates of heaven, exhaling the buried three-thousand-zhang heroic spirit. Riding the wind back to Penglai, recounting the hardships of a million cliffs. The fence is broken, the gate is wide open, allowing free will to follow my desires. Speaking five thousand words, riding a buffalo out of the Hangu Pass, wishing to travel with the gatekeeper of the pass. Wearing a waistband of ten thousand strings of coins, riding a horse through Yangzhou, all say the recluse who closes his door has come.

    658

    金灵马
    　　凡宫车晏驾棺用香楠木中分为二刳肖人形其广狭长短仅足容身而已殓用貂皮袄皮防其鞾韤系腰盒鉢俱用白粉皮为之殉以金壶瓶二盏一碗碟匙筯各一殓讫用黄金为篐四条以束之舆车用白氊青縁纳克实为覆棺亦以纳克实为之前行用古巫媪一人衣新衣骑马牵马一疋以黄金饰鞍辔笼以纳克实谓之金灵马
    　　髹器
    　　黒光　凢造碗碟盘盂之属其胎骨则梓人以脆松劈成薄片于旋床上胶黏而成名曰卷素髹工买来刀刳胶缝干净平正夏月无胶泛之患却牛皮胶和生漆微嵌缝中名曰梢当【去声】然后胶漆布之方加麤灰灰乃砖瓦捣屑筛过分麤中细是也胶漆调和令稀稠得所如髹工自家造卖低歹之物不用胶漆止用猪血厚糊之类而以麻筯代布所以易壊也麤灰过停令日乆坚实砂皮擦磨却加中灰再加细灰并如前又停日乆砖石车磨去灰浆洁净一二日候干燥方漆之谓之糙漆再

    Explanation

    658

    Gold Spirit Horse
    Whenever an emperor or high-ranking official passes away, the coffin is made from fragrant sandalwood, divided into two halves and carved to resemble the human form, with its width, narrowness, length, and shortness only enough to contain the body. The body is wrapped in a sable fur coat and accompanied by two golden vases, one cup, one bowl, a plate, a spoon, and chopsticks. The body is bound with four golden bands. The funeral carriage is covered with white felt edged in blue and filled with nacre. The coffin is also covered with nacre. A female shaman dressed in new clothes rides a horse and leads another horse, which is decorated with gold on its bridle and covered with nacre, called the "Golden Spirit Horse."

    Lacquerware
    Black Luster All lacquerware, such as bowls, plates, and jars, has a core made by a carpenter splitting brittle pine into thin slices and gluing them together on a lathe. This is called "rolled lacquer." The lacquerware is then purchased by artisans who carefully cut and clean the seams, ensuring they are smooth and even. In summer, there is no risk of the glue swelling. The artisans use cowhide glue mixed with raw lacquer, which is slightly embedded in the seams, known as "sanding." Afterward, the glue and lacquer are applied, followed by coarse ash, which is made from crushed bricks and tiles, sifted to separate coarse, medium, and fine particles. The glue and lacquer are mixed to the right consistency. If the lacquerware is of low quality, only pig blood and thick paste are used, and hemp fibers are used instead of cloth, making it prone to breaking. The coarse ash is left to dry for a long time until it becomes solid, then sanded and polished. Medium ash and fine ash are added in the same manner, and the process is repeated over time. The lacquerware is then ground with stones to remove the ash and ash paste, and left to dry for one or two days before being painted. This initial layer is called "rough lacquer."

    659

    停数月车磨糙漆绢帛挑【上声】去浆迹才用黒光黒光者用漆斤两若干煎成膏再用漆如上一半加鸡子清打匀入在内日中晒翻三五度如栗殻色入前项所煎漆中和匀试简看慢若再晒若慢加生漆多入触药触药即鐡浆沫用隔年米醋煎此物干为末入漆中名曰黒光用刷蘸漆漆器物上不要见刷痕停三五日待漆内外俱干置隂处防之然后用揩光石磨去漆中纇【雷上声】揩光石鸡肝石也出杭州上柏三桥埠牛头岭再用篛帉次用布帉次用菜油傅却用出光粉揩方明亮

    Explanation

    659

    The carriage was halted for several months to grind the rough lacquer into a smoother form, removing any adhesive residues from the silk and cloth. Only then was the glossy black lacquer used, which required a specific amount of lacquer boiled into a paste. Half of this paste was mixed with beaten egg whites and stirred thoroughly, then left to dry in the sun, turning it a chestnut shell color. This mixture was then combined with the previously boiled lacquer and tested. If it was too thick, it was dried further; if it was too thin, more raw lacquer was added, along with a substance called "touch medicine," which was an iron-based adhesive. This mixture was then processed with aged vinegar for a year, dried, and ground into a powder, which was added to the lacquer and named "black light." A brush was dipped into the lacquer to apply it to the object, ensuring no brush marks were visible. The object was left to dry for several days, both inside and out, in a shaded place. Once fully dry, a polishing stone called "liver stone," which is a type of stone from Hangzhou's Shangbai Sanqiao Pu Niutouling, was used to remove any imperfections from the lacquer. This was followed by polishing with fine reed cloth, then fine cloth, and finally vegetable oil, before using a polishing powder to achieve a bright finish.

    660

    朱红　修治布灰一一如前不用糙漆却用贉朱桐叶色然后用银朱以漆煎成膏子调朱如朱一两则膏子亦一两生漆少许看四时天气试简加减冬多加生漆顔色闇春秋色居中夏四五月秋七月此三月顔色正且红亮
    　　鳗水　好桐油煎沸以水试之看躁也方入黄丹腻粉无名异煎一滚以水试如之状令冷油水各等分杖棒搅匀却取砖灰一分石灰一分细麫一分和匀以前项油水搅和调黏灰器物上再加细灰然后用漆并如黒光法或用油亦可
    　　济逊宴服

    Explanation

    660

    Red Pigment (朱红)
    Prepare a mixture of lime and ash as before, but do not use coarse paint. Instead, use a specific type of cinnabar (朱砂) mixed with tallow (桐叶色). Then, make a paste by boiling silver cinnabar (银朱) with lacquer. The ratio of cinnabar to the paste should be one to one. Add a small amount of raw lacquer. Adjust the recipe based on seasonal weather conditions. In winter, add more raw lacquer to achieve a darker color. In spring and autumn, the color should be moderate. In late spring, summer, and early autumn (fourth and fifth months of summer, and seventh month of autumn), the color should be bright and red.

    Lacquer (鳗水)
    Boil good tung oil until it boils. Test the consistency with water and add yellow lead (黄丹), talcum powder (腻粉), and other ingredients. Stir until it cools and the oil and water are evenly mixed. Take one part brick dust, one part lime, and one part fine flour, and mix them with the previous oil and water mixture. Apply it to objects and add more fine ash. Then, use lacquer or oil to achieve a black glossy finish.

    Formal Attire (济逊宴服)
    Refers to a type of formal clothing or garment.

    661

    济逊宴服者贵臣见飨于天子则服之今所赐綘衣是也贯大珠以饰其肩背间膺首服亦如之

    <子部,小说家类,杂事之属,辍耕录,卷三十>
    　　银锭字号
    　　银锭上字号州元寳乃至元十三年大兵平宋囘至州丞相巴延号令搜捡将士行李所得撒花银子销铸作锭每重五十两归朝献纳世祖大防皇子王孙驸马国戚从而颁赐或用货卖所以民间有此锭也后朝廷亦自铸至元十四年者重四十九两十五年重四十八两辽阳元寳乃至元二十三年二十四年征辽东所得银子而铸者
    　　学宫讲説
    　　凡学宫朔望讲説乃礼之常所属上司官或省宪官至自教授学官暨学賔斋谕等皆讲説一书然儒生未逹时宜徃徃迕意多矣泰定甲子开吴淞江省台宪僚咸集时治书刘公泺源北方学者首谒先圣先师其年值闰詹肖岩讲书尧典三百有六旬有六日以闰月定四时成嵗大咈其意以为学校讲説虽贱夫皂隶执鞭坠镫之人皆令通晓今乃稽算度数何为肖岩由是悒怏而卒至元己卯冬分宪老老公检踏灾伤以

    Explanation

    661

    Final Translation:

    The ceremonial attire worn by noble ministers when they are entertained by the emperor is now the red robe that has been bestowed upon them. Large pearls are strung to adorn the shoulders and back, and the headwear is similarly decorated.

    Additional Translations:

    Silver Ingot Inscriptions

    The inscriptions on silver ingots, such as "Yangzhou Yuanbao," were cast during the thirteenth year of the Zhiyuan era (1276) when the great army returned to Yangzhou after subduing the Song dynasty. The chancellor Bayan issued orders to search and collect the silver items obtained from the soldiers' belongings, which were then melted down and cast into ingots, each weighing fifty taels. These were presented to the imperial court and contributed to the treasury of the founding emperor. They were subsequently distributed to princes, princesses, and nobles, or sold in the market, which is why such ingots are found in civilian hands. Later, the court also began to cast its own ingots. Those from the fourteenth year of the Zhiyuan era weighed forty-nine taels, and those from the fifteenth year weighed forty-eight taels. The "Liaoyang Yuanbao" ingots were cast during the twenty-third and twenty-fourth years of the Zhiyuan era, using silver obtained from the conquest of Liaodong.

    Lectures at the Academy

    Lectures at the academy on the first and fifteenth days of the lunar month are a customary ritual. When officials from higher ranks, such as provincial or constitutional officials, visit, the professors, scholars, and attendants all deliver lectures on a single text. However, scholars often fail to understand the proper context, leading to many misunderstandings. In the year Taoding Jiazi (1324), during the opening of the Wu Song River, officials from the provincial court gathered. At the time, the magistrate Liu Gong, known as Luoyuan, was the first to pay homage to the Sage and his disciples. That year was a leap year, and when Zhan Xiaoyan delivered a lecture on the Yao典 (The Classic of Yao), he mentioned that "three hundred and sixty-six days, with six extra days, are adjusted by a leap month to establish the four seasons and complete the year." This greatly offended him, as he believed that even the lowest-ranking officials should understand such matters, and now they were discussing calculations and measurements. Xiaoyan died from depression shortly after. In the year Zhizhi Jiyou (1339) winter, the provincial inspector, an elderly man, inspected the disaster and damage.

    662

    复熟粮为急陆宅之讲省刑罚薄税敛一章公变色而作至正辛巳知府杨侯锐意浚河以兴利除害为己任时宪佥某谒学宫王玉岩讲禹别九州随山濬川结意皆归美于知府宪佥不悦而罢丁酉岁张士诚据有姑苏日遣苏守周仁来王可权讲易泰卦盖君子道长小人道消之义周以为讥讪累及诸职事皆停月廪惟钱先生伯全父作训导时行刑官至讲钦哉钦哉惟刑之恤哉讲毕称赏不已前数君子亦可为后人戒也
    　　松江之变
    　　王与敬字可权淮西安丰人由浙省典吏充宣使后于董搏霄部下立功擢松江府判未任转省都镇抚陞元帅至正丙申二月朔伪诚王张士诚红军破平江时与敬兵败径趍嘉兴又与苖军参政杨旺扎勒不恊乃投松江名曰守御实欲恋倡妇董赛儿故也达噜噶齐巴图特穆尔知府崔思诚皆无制变之术激成其祸盖其至也不郊迎之已自不悦越二日浙省又命元帅特穆尔喇实等提兵而来镇守城池二帅抗衡不相上下帖防两仓脚夫散

    Explanation

    662

    In preparation for the river's management, the urgency of preparing food was emphasized. Governor Yang, in the year Xinsi of the Zhizheng era, was resolute in dredging the river to bring benefits and eliminate harm. At that time, a certain official visited the academy, and Wang Yuyan lectured on Yu the Great's division of the nine provinces and his efforts to follow the mountains and dredge the rivers. The intention was to praise the governor and the official, but they were unhappy and dismissed. In the year Dingyou, Zhang Shicheng occupied Suzhou, and daily sent Suzhou governor Zhou Ren to come. Wang Kequan lectured on the I Ching (Book of Changes) and the Tai hexagram, indicating the meaning that the way of the gentleman grows and the way of the petty person diminishes. Zhou thought it was sarcastic, affecting all officials, their monthly stipends were stopped. Only Mr. Qian, the father of Mr. Boquan, served as a teacher. When the execution official arrived and lectured on the importance of mercy in punishment, he praised it without end. The previous several gentlemen can also serve as a warning for future generations.

    Songjiang's Turmoil

    Wang Yu Jing, courtesy name Kequan, from Huai'an and Anfeng, served as a messenger after being a clerk in Zhejiang Province. He established merit under Dong Bo Xiao's command and was promoted to judge of Songjiang Prefecture but did not take up the post. Transferred to provincial commandant and promoted to general, in the year Bingshen of the Zhizheng era, on the first day of the second month, the rebel king Zhang Shicheng's Red Army broke through Pingjiang. At that time, Wang Yu Jing's troops were defeated and rushed directly to Jiaxing. Another incident occurred with the Miao army and Yang Wang Zale not agreeing. He then threw himself at Songjiang, claiming to defend it, but actually wanting to be with the courtesan Dong Sai'er. Dalu Gazi Batu Temur and Prefect Cui Sicheng both lacked the ability to control the situation, causing the disaster. Because they did not welcome him to the outskirts, he was already displeased. Two days later, Zhejiang Province again ordered General Temur La Shi to bring troops to garrison the city. The two generals were evenly matched, and the guards of the two granaries were scattered.

    663

    口粮给器械发号施令盖意在逐与敬行十八日帖宴军民官无一人至者至夜与敬下万户戴列孙等率引军卒自西门放火鼓噪而叛官僚溃散寺观民房悉化焦土捡刮金银财物塞满舟船自与敬以下人口辎重皆出西门二十四日旺札勒下元帅萧亮员成等率苖军突至兵不与敌遂北出通波塘而去投降士诚子女玉帛悉为苖军所有民亦持挺相逐列孙孔镇抚等死者过半苖军恣肆检刮截人耳鼻城中女妇多为淫污房舍间有存者皆为
    焚燬靡有孑遗居民两遭锋镝死者填街塞巷水为不流四月初十日士诚下元帅史文炳一部兵马自湖泖入古浦塘舳舻相衔旗帜蔽日苖军一矢不交竟溃散而去南村野史曰天下本无事庸人自扰之卓哉斯言也初王与敬之戾止茍得一守土官能以智虑处之则不致若是况松江尚侈靡习淫风者乆矣余尝扼腕而叹必有后日之患终为一贱倡祸及数万家非小变也与敬逆贼之名遗臭万年戴氏逞匹夫之勇卒丧其生皆自取之也悲夫

    Explanation

    663

    Rations were provided, weapons distributed, and orders issued, with the intention of following the plan set by Wuying on the eighteenth day to hold a banquet for military and civilian officials. However, no officials attended the banquet. That night, under Wuying's command, the military officers Dai Liesun and others led the soldiers to set fire and create a commotion from the western gate, leading to a rebellion. Officials fled, temples and civilian houses were all turned to ashes, and valuables were looted and filled with boats. From Wuying down to the common people, everyone and their belongings left through the western gate. On the twenty-fourth day, under the command of General Wang Zale, the Miao army led by Generals Xiao Liang and Yuan Cheng suddenly arrived, but without engaging in battle, they retreated northward through Tongbotang. The surrendered people, including the children and wealth of Shicheng, were all taken by the Miao army. Civilians armed with spears pursued and killed a significant number of people, including Liu Sun and Kong Zhenfu, and many others died. The Miao army acted recklessly, mutilating people and leaving the city with no survivors. The residents suffered a double tragedy, with dead bodies filling the streets and waterways. On the tenth day of the fourth month, under the command of General Shi Wenbing, a large army entered from Lake Luhao into the ancient canal, with ships and flags blocking the sun. The Miao army did not fight and dispersed. The historical commentator from Nan Village said, "There was originally no trouble, but it was created by ordinary people." Indeed, this is a profound statement. Initially, Wuying's actions led to chaos, but if a wise local official had handled the situation with wisdom, it would not have come to this. Especially in Songjiang, where luxury and debauchery had long been prevalent, I often lamented the impending troubles, fearing that a minor incident would lead to widespread disaster. Wuying's name will be forever tainted as a traitor, and Dai's reckless courage ultimately led to his own demise. What a tragedy!

    664

    果典坐
    　　嘉兴天宁寺有老僧曰果典坐平生不蓄积得钱輙买酒饮长老念空海每岁遗衣叚至正癸巳正月一日无疾而卒年一百二岁
    　　诗防
    　　潮逢谷水难兴浪月到云间便不明松江古有此语谷水云间皆松江别名也近代来作官者始则赫然有声终则阘茸贪滥始终廉洁者鲜两句竟成诗防书画楼
    　　松江自来无大火灾至正丙戌闰十月廿九日夜普照寺西业制防民姚不谨于火延燎三千余家重门馆灵宫梵宇悉为煨烬而夏爱闲氏收藏古法书名画楼岿然独存岂有神物防之也耶抑亦数耶物必遇主
    　　松江普照寺门首刀镊胡忽见街上有小片荷叶舒卷不已一人拾置懐中去胡叩之曰汝得何物但欲见之以决所疑及出示乃至元钞三十文又同郡夏氏仆尝见小花蛇盘旋道左行人捉藏诸袖仆颇讶问其所以则至元钞贰拾文右二事絶相类吁三十文二十文直微末耳尚必待主今之积金蓄谷倍息计赢孳孳以利为念者于此宁不可鉴哉

    Explanation

    664

    Translation:

    Master Fruit Seat

    At Tianning Temple in Jiaxing, there was an elderly monk named Master Fruit Seat. Throughout his life, he never accumulated wealth and would always buy wine to drink. The senior monk Niankonghai would give him clothing each year. On the first day of the first month of the year Zhizheng Gui Si (1364 AD), he passed away peacefully at the age of one hundred and two.

    Poetic Reflection

    When the tide meets the river, waves cannot rise; when the moon reaches the clouds, it becomes dim. The phrase "When the tide meets the river, waves cannot rise; when the moon reaches the clouds, it becomes dim" is an old saying from Songjiang. Both "river" and "clouds" are alternative names for Songjiang. In modern times, officials often start their careers with great promise but end up corrupt and greedy. Those who remain clean and upright throughout are few. These two lines have become a poetic reflection on the impermanence of human affairs, much like the painting and calligraphy tower.

    Songjiang has never experienced a major fire until the night of the twenty-ninth day of the leap tenth month of the year Zhizheng Bing Xu (1366 AD). A fire started near the West Industry District at Pu Zhao Temple due to negligence by a person named Yao. The flames spread, destroying over three thousand households, including grand halls, temples, and sacred buildings. However, the collection of ancient calligraphy and paintings by the Xia Aixian family remained standing, seemingly protected by some divine force. Was it a matter of fate or destiny? It seems that objects must find their rightful owners.

    At the entrance of Pu Zhao Temple, a man named Hu, who sold knives and scissors, suddenly saw a small lotus leaf continuously unfolding and curling on the street. Someone picked it up and put it in their pocket. Hu asked, "What did you find?" He wanted to see it to resolve his doubts. Upon showing it, it turned out to be thirty coins of the Zhiyuan currency. Another servant of the Xia family from the same prefecture once saw a small snake coiling on the side of the road. A passerby caught it and hid it in his sleeve. The servant was surprised and asked why. It turned out to be twenty coins of the Zhiyuan currency. These two stories are remarkably similar. Thirty coins and twenty coins are trivial amounts, yet they still require a rightful owner. How much more so for those who accumulate gold and store grain, calculating double interest and profits, constantly thinking about their gains? Surely, they should take heed from this!

    665

    鎗金银法
    　　嘉兴斜塘杨滙髹工鎗【去声】金鎗银法凡器用什物先用黒漆为地以针刻画或山水树石或花竹翎毛或亭台屋宇或人物故事一一完整然后用新罗漆若鎗金则调雌黄若鎗银则调韶粉日晒后角挑挑嵌所刻缝罅以金薄或银薄依银匠所用纸糊笼罩置金银薄在内遂旋细切取铺已施漆上新绵揩拭牢实但着漆者自然黏住其余金银都在绵上于熨斗中烧灰甘锅内镕鍜浑不走失
    　　磨兠坚箴
    　　磨兠鞬已见第九卷昔李侍郎都哩尝揭磨兠坚三字于座隅磨兠坚者古之慎言人也其善于自防者哉金华宋濂为着箴曰磨兠坚慎勿言口为祸门昔人之云磨兠坚人各有心山高海深磨兠坚高不知极深不知测磨兠坚言出诸口祸随其后磨兠坚钟鼓之声因叩而鸣磨兠坚不叩而鸣必骇众听磨兠坚惟口之则守之以黙是曰德磨兠坚磨兠坚慎勿言

    Explanation

    665

    Translation of the Source Text

    Method of Inlaying Gold and Silver

    In the city of Jiaxing, in the Xie Tang area, there was a lacquerworker named Yang Hui who specialized in the method of inlaying gold and silver. For any utensils or objects, the first step was to apply a base layer of black lacquer. Then, using needles, intricate designs were carved into the lacquer surface, such as landscapes with mountains and rocks, flowers, bamboo, bird feathers, pavilions, towers, buildings, or figures and stories. Once the designs were complete, a type of lacquer from Silla (an ancient Korean kingdom) was used. If inlaying gold, orophthalme (a yellow pigment) was mixed; if inlaying silver, shao powder (a white pigment) was mixed. After drying, the lacquer was exposed to sunlight. Then, using a tool similar to a pick, thin sheets of gold or silver were carefully inserted into the carved lines. These metal sheets were placed according to the paper frame used by silversmiths, and the sheets were then cut and precisely positioned. After applying the lacquer, new cotton was used to rub and smooth the surface, ensuring that the lacquer adhered firmly. Only the parts that were lacquered would naturally stick, while the rest of the gold and silver remained on the cotton. The metal was then melted and shaped in a pot over a fire, ensuring that none of it was lost.

    Admonition on Caution in Speech

    The method of caution in speech has been discussed in the ninth chapter. In the past, Li Shi, a high-ranking official, once inscribed the three characters "Mó Dòu Jiān" (Caution in Speech) on the side of his seat. "Mó Dòu Jiān" refers to ancient people who were cautious in their speech, and it is a virtue of self-protection. The famous Ming dynasty writer Song Lian wrote an admonition on this topic, saying: "Be cautious in speech, for the mouth is the gate to disaster. As the ancients said, 'Caution in speech'—each person has their own heart, as deep as the sea and as high as the mountains. 'Caution in speech'—its height is unknown, its depth is unknown. 'Caution in speech'—words spoken will bring trouble afterward. 'Caution in speech'—the sound of bells and drums is produced by striking, but if they ring without being struck, they will surely alarm the listeners. 'Caution in speech'—only by keeping silent can one guard one's mouth. This is called profound virtue. 'Caution in speech'—be cautious in speech."

    666

    三笑图
    　　杨鐡厓云坡翁防石恪所画以为三人皆大笑至衣服冠屦皆有笑态其后之童子亦罔知而大笑永叔书室图三笑于壁想见石恪所作与此无异然坡翁所防三笑不言为谁山谷特实以逺公陶陆事陈贤良舜俞庐山记亦谓举世信之有赵彦通者作庐岳独笑一篇谓逺公不与脩静同时楼攻媿亦言脩静元嘉末始来庐山时逺公亡已三十余年渊明亡亦二十余年其不同时信哉后世传讹徃徃如此使坡翁见之亦当絶倒也
    　　官制字讹
    　　按古官制取义皆有所主非徒名也后世或讹其音者有矣音虽讹而义则不讹也如仆射秦官仆主也古者重武事每官必有主射以督课之射音神夜反关中人讹为寅诈反韵书不取其义于神夜反中却收在寅诈反下尚书亦秦官秦世少府遣吏四人在殿中主发书故谓之尚书尚犹主也如尚方尚食尚医尚衣尚冠尚浴尚席之尚并音时亮反后世乃以尚书之尚讹

    Explanation

    666

    Translation of the Source Text

    Three Laughs

    Yang Tieya said that Po Wang preserved a painting by Shike, depicting three figures all laughing, to the extent that their clothes, hats, and shoes all have a laughing expression. The child behind them also laughs, unaware of why. Yongshu drew a picture of three laughs in his study, imagining that Shike's work is similar to this. However, Po Wang did not specify who the three laughing figures are. Shan Gu specifically mentioned the story of Far Gong, Tao Yuanming, and Lu Ji. Chen Xianliang, in his Records of Mount Lu, also said that everyone believes this story. Zhao Yantong wrote an article titled "The Unique Laugh of Mount Lu," stating that Far Gong did not live at the same time as Xiu Jing. Lou Gongkui also said that when Xiu Jing first came to Mount Lu, Far Gong had been dead for over thirty years. Tao Yuanming had also died over twenty years before that. Indeed, they did not live at the same time. Such errors in later transmissions are often like this. If Po Wang saw this, he would also be utterly surprised.

    Errors in Official Titles

    According to ancient official systems, the meanings of titles all have a basis and are not just names. Later generations sometimes mispronounced them. Although the pronunciation might be misinterpreted, the meaning remains unchanged. For example, "Pushe" was a Qin official title, where "Pu" means "servant" and "She" means "master." Anciently, military affairs were valued, and each official must have a chief archer to supervise and evaluate them. "She" is pronounced as "shén yè," but people from Guanzhong mispronounced it as "yín zhà." Rhyming dictionaries do not take its meaning from the correct pronunciation but include it under the mispronunciation. "Shangshu" is also a Qin official title. During the Qin dynasty, the Minister of the Imperial Household sent four officials to the palace to issue documents. "Shang" still means "chief." Titles like "Imperial Workshops," "Imperial Kitchen," "Imperial Medical Office," "Imperial Wardrobe," "Imperial Crown Office," "Imperial Bathing Office," and "Imperial Seating Office" all have "Shang" pronounced as "shí liàng." Later generations misinterpreted the pronunciation of "Shang" in "Shangshu."

    667

    为辰羊反陆德明亦音平声韵书遂两收之洗马前汉志太子太傅少傅属官有先马张晏曰先马员十六人秩比谒者如淳曰前驱也国语载勾践亲为夫差先马先先之也从先见反今韵书作苏典反字作洗愚意此类并当从其正义不当从其讹音今人但见读仆射之射作神夜反尚书之尚作时亮反洗马之洗作先见反便非哂之不究其义故也此类甚多今姑举其显者
    　　巾帻考
    　　巾帻释名巾谨也当自谨于四教仪礼二十成人士冠【如字】庶人巾说文发有巾曰帻帻即巾也又方言覆髻谓之帻汉书卑贱执事不冠者所服或谓之承露按仪礼士冠庶人巾则古者士以上有冠无巾帻惟庶人戴之秦谓民为黔首汉为仆隶为苍头汉书谓卑贱者所服此其证也后世上下通用之谓之燕巾蔡邕独防曰汉元帝额有壮发不欲人见故加巾帻以包之也然则巾自巾帻自帻不独卑贱者所服虽尊者亦服之矣至王莽冠内加巾故诗人云王

    Explanation

    667

    Final Translation

    The pronunciation "chén yáng" (辰羊) is also read as "píng shēng" (平声), and the rhyme dictionary includes both readings. In the "前汉志" (Records of the Former Han Dynasty), the position of "洗马" (xiǎn mǎ) is described as a subordinate official under the Grand Tutor and Junior Tutor, with sixteen members ranked similarly to "谒者" (谒者) and interpreted as a "前驱" (qian qu), meaning a vanguard or forerunner. The "国语" (Discourses of the States) records that King Goujian personally served as a vanguard for King Fuchai. Scholars Zhang Yan and Ru Chun explain that the modern rhyme dictionary reads "苏典" (sū diǎn) and the character is written as "洗" (xiǎn). The author believes these readings are incorrect and should follow the correct pronunciation. Modern readers often mispronounce "仆射" (pú yè), "尚书" (shàng shū), and "洗马" (xiǎn mǎ) without understanding their correct meanings.

    Examination of Headgear

    The term "巾" (jīn) is explained as a head covering, symbolizing caution and self-discipline in the context of the Four Teachings and the "仪礼" (Yili, Rituals of Rites). The "说文" (Shuowen Jiezi) describes "帻" (zé) as a head covering for hair, which is another term for "巾" (jīn). The "汉书" (History of the Han Dynasty) mentions that "卑贱执事" (bēi jiàn zhí shì) wore "帻" (zé) instead of a hat, and it is also called "承露" (chéng lù). According to the "仪礼" (Yili), scholars and above wore hats, while commoners wore "巾" (jīn) or "帻" (zé). In the Qin Dynasty, commoners were called "黔首" (qián shǒu), and in the Han Dynasty, servants were called "苍头" (cāng tóu), as confirmed by the "汉书". In later times, "燕巾" (yàn jīn) was used universally by both high and low-ranking people. Cai Yong explains that Emperor Yuan of Han wore "巾帻" (jīn zé) to cover his prominent forehead. Therefore, "巾" (jīn) and "帻" (zé) were not exclusive to commoners but were also worn by the elite. Wang Mang wore "巾" (jīn) inside his hat, as mentioned in poetry.

    668

    莽秃帻施屋又光武岸帻见马援又按魏志注太祖以天下凶荒资财乏匮拟皮弁裁缣帛为帢【或作幅乞洽反】合乎简易随时之议以色别其贵贱本施军饰非为国容韵书弁缺四隅谓之帢【前时军人弓手所戴小白防是也】一曰按头使下故曰帢増韵埤苍皆曰防也晋舆服志哀帝立秋御读令改用素白幍汉末王公名士多委王服以幅巾为雅魏武始制幍成帝制使尚书入坐丞郎门下二省侍官乘车白幍低帏出入掖门又二宫直宫着乌纱幍徃徃士人
    宴居皆着幍矣幍虽冠弁遗制去古益逺用巾帻为近之一说秦加武将首餙为绛帕后稍稍作顔题汉兴续其顔却摞【音罗】之施巾连题却覆之即丧帻也名之曰帻至孝文帝乃高顔题续之以耳崇其巾为屋合后施巾上下文者长耳武者短耳古者冠制皆硬殻自额上至于顶如今礼冠者然后世乃作小冠厪以束发冠下施帻冠帻之上又总施巾皆效汉元帝所服之制也夫歴代损益随其所宜茍不害于义从俗可也孔子居宋衣缝掖居鲁冠章甫亦从俗也

    Explanation

    668

    A Hat with a Bald Crown and Roof Structure

    Mang Tu wore a hat with a bald crown and a roof-like structure, and Emperor Guangwu met Ma Yuan wearing a headband. According to the Records of Wei (魏志), Cao Cao, due to the famine and scarcity of resources, imitated the skin cap (皮弁) by cutting silk and hemp into a hat (帢). This design was in line with the simple and easy-to-follow principles of the time, distinguishing between nobility and commoners by color. Originally, it was designed for military decoration, not for national appearance. According to the rhyme book, a cap (弁) with four missing corners is called a hat (帢). Pressing the head down, hence it is called a hat (帢). The increased rhyme and the thin blue both call it a hat (防). In the Jin dynasty's record of carriages and clothing, Emperor Ai ordered the use of white and plain headbands during the autumn. At the end of the Han dynasty, many kings, princes, and famous scholars wore plain headbands as a sign of elegance. Cao Cao (魏武) first established the use of headbands (幍). Emperor Cheng ordered the secretaries to enter and sit in the offices of the two ministries, and the officials who served there wore white headbands and low veils when entering and leaving the palace gates. In the two palaces, the guards wore black gauze headbands. Often, scholars and officials wore headbands during banquets and leisure time. Although the headband is a relic of ancient caps, it has become more distant from ancient times and closer to the use of headbands and headwraps. Another account says that during the Qin dynasty, military generals wore red headbands as decorations. Later, it gradually became a headband with a forehead band. When the Han dynasty began, the forehead band was continued and the headwrap was attached to cover it, which is the mourning headband. It was called a headwrap (帻). Until Emperor Wen of Han raised the forehead band and added ears to the headwrap, making it like a roof. After combining them, the headwrap for scholars had long ears, and for soldiers, short ears. In ancient times, the crown was a hard shell from the forehead to the top, like the ceremonial crown used today. Later, small crowns were made to bind the hair. A headwrap was placed under the crown. On top of the crown and headwrap, a headband was added, all following the style worn by Emperor Yuan of Han. Throughout history, gains and losses have been adjusted according to what is suitable, as long as it does not harm the principles. Confucius, when living in Song, wore a patched robe; when living in Lu, he wore a ceremonial cap. He followed the customs of the time.

    This translation aims to be faithful to the original text while maintaining natural English phrasing. Key terms and cultural references are explained where necessary, and the structure of the text is preserved to ensure clarity and coherence.

    669

    屦舄履考
    　　屦舄履屦人注襌下曰屦复下曰舄【说文无舄字舄本鹊字今借为履舄字也陆佃云舄通为舄履之舄古人居欲如燕行不欲如鹊故借为舄字所以为行戒也然借鹊为舄作思积反者盖舄履也古今注以木置屦下干腊不畏泥湿故曰舄以是知舄屦之下必再用木矣】士丧礼夏葛屦冬皮屦屦人注又谓凡屦青絇【劬】繶【亿】纯【准】素帻白屦缁絇繶纯爵弁纁屦黒絇繶纯是也絇说文纑绳絇也玉藻注履颈饰也【□防状如刀衣鼻在屦头言拘者取自拘持使低目不暇顾视一曰用缯一寸屈为之头着屦头以受穿贯】繶屦人注缝中紃也绦也纯屦人注縁也言繶

    Explanation

    669

    Shoes, Boots, and Sandals Examined

    The Ju Ren (Shoe Maker) section of the Liji (Book of Rites) states that a single-layered shoe is called jù (sandals), and a double-layered shoe is called xì (boots). The Shuowen Jiezi (Explanatory Dictionary) does not include the character xì. The character xì originally referred to the bird que (magpie), but it is now borrowed to mean xì (boots). According to Lu Dian, xì is used interchangeably with xì (boots). Ancient people wanted to walk like swallows but did not want to walk like magpies, so they borrowed the character xì as a warning to walk properly. However, when que is used to mean xì, it is pronounced sī jī (a variant pronunciation of xì), which refers to xì (boots). The Jin Gu Zhi (Ancient and Modern Notes) states that xì (boots) have wooden soles that are dried and preserved, making them resistant to mud and moisture. Therefore, it is known that xì (boots) must have a wooden sole.

    In the Shi Sang Li (Rites for the Death of a Scholar), it is recorded that in summer, straw sandals (gě jù) are worn, and in winter, leather shoes (pí jù) are worn. The Ju Ren section further notes that all shoes, whether they have green gu (decorative tassels), xian (decorative borders), and chun (decorative edges), or a plain headband (sù zé), white shoes with black gu, xian, and chun, or red shoes for the jue bian (a type of ceremonial cap) with black gu, xian, and chun, are all examples of such footwear. The Shuowen Jiezi defines the gu as a rope made of hemp, while the Yu Zao (Notes on the Jade) explains that the gu is a decoration on the neck of the shoe. The Ju Ren section further explains that the xian is a thread or ribbon in the middle of the shoe, and the chun is the edge of the shoe, also called xian. Dried and preserved, xì (boots) are not afraid of mud and moisture. All have predecessors who can serve as teachers and models. Seek knowledge broadly, and nothing is unhelpful in matters.

    670

    必有絇繶纯矣凡絇繶纯皆一色又按屦人注舄有三等赤舄白舄黒舄也赤舄为上冕服之舄诗曰王锡韩侯衮赤舄则诸侯与王同矣所谓舄青舄王后祭服之舄也凡屦之饰如绣次也黄屦白饰白屦黒饰黒屦青饰天子诸侯吉事皆写其余服冕着舄耳士爵弁纁屦黒絇繶纯尊祭服之屦饰从缋也至若履者说文足所依也从尸从文从舟象履形毛氏曰舟能载物屦能载人又草曰屝【芳末反】麻曰屦【凡布皆可谓之麻】皮曰履按履无别制说文屦履也从履省娄声又鞮也徐曰鞮革履也舄韵防履也古今注以木置履下干腊不畏泥湿故曰舄以是知屦舄履之异名也但

    Explanation

    670

    A shoe must have a gù (絇), biàn (繶), and chún (纯). All of these components must be of one color. According to the commentary on the Jù Rén (屦人), there are three types of xì (舄): red, white, and black. The red xì is the highest grade for ceremonial attire. The Shī (诗) says, "The king bestowed the Duke of Han with a ceremonial robe and red shoes," indicating that feudal lords are the same as the king in this regard. The so-called black and blue/green xì are for the queen's ceremonial attire for sacrifice. The decoration of shoes is like embroidery in order: yellow shoes with white decoration, white shoes with black decoration, and black shoes with blue/green decoration. The emperor and feudal lords wear ceremonial attire for auspicious events, and the rest of the attire is worn with shoes. Scholar-officials wear a ceremonial hat and reddish-brown shoes with black gù, biàn, and chún. The decoration of shoes for ceremonial attire for sacrifice follows embroidery. As for shoes, the Shuō Wén (说文) says that shoes are what the foot relies on. The character for "履" (lǚ) is structured to resemble a shoe, with components that follow the corpse, culture, and boat. Mao Qiling said that boats can carry objects and shoes can carry people. Shoes made of grass are called "屝" (fèi), and those made of hemp are called "屦" (jù). All cloth can be called hemp. Shoes made of leather are called "履" (lǚ). According to the Shuō Wén, shoes are called "屦" and "履." The character for "履" is structured with a reduced "履" and a "娄" (lóu) sound. Shoes are also called "鞮" (dī). Xu (徐) said that "鞮" refers to a type of leather shoe. The Yùn Fáng (韵防) says that "舄" (xì) refers to shoes, and the Gǔ Jīn Zhù (古今注) explains that "舄" refers to shoes with a wooden base that is not afraid of mud and moisture. Therefore, we know that "屦," "舄," and "履" are different names for shoes.

    671

    有襌下复下用木之异耳古人舄屦履至阶必脱唯着韤而入礼户外有二屦是脱屦而入者也汉赐劒履上殿是不赐则不敢着履上殿明矣谏不行则纳履而去纳结也言纳履则在外明矣是脱履而入者也王乔入朝双舄化鳬先至是脱舄而入者也古者堂上皆有席所以着韤为宜况韤又从韦乎又按乡饮酒云说屦揖让如初升堂防云凡堂上揖行礼不脱屦坐则脱屦理固然也由是观之凡宗庙堂室之间行礼亦必不脱屦矣夫降而脱屦然后升坐礼也其后賔与主人酬酢之时皆在两阶之间又湏降而着屦复升于阶酬酢之礼毕又降而脱屦复升于坐也古人礼繁如此今何畧也

    　　辍耕録卷三十

    Explanation

    671

    In the past, there were differences in the use of wooden materials for various types of footwear. Ancient people would remove their shoes or sandals when they reached the threshold of a house, wearing only socks or stockings inside. According to ritual practices, there were two pairs of shoes kept outside the house, one for indoor use and one for outdoor use. During the Han dynasty, certain officials were granted the privilege of wearing shoes and carrying swords into the imperial hall, but if they were not granted this privilege, they would not dare to do so. If advice was not heeded, the advisor would put on their shoes and leave. The act of putting on shoes indicates being outside. Thus, it is clear that shoes were removed before entering. The story of Wang Qiao, who transformed his shoes into cranes and arrived at court ahead of others, also illustrates the practice of removing shoes before entering. In ancient times, mats or rugs were placed on the floor of rooms, and it was appropriate to wear socks or stockings on these mats, especially since socks were often made from leather. According to the Rites of Zhou, guests would exchange shoes and bow before entering the hall, and they would remove their shoes when sitting down. Therefore, in all formal settings, including temples and halls, shoes were not removed during rituals. The ritual of removing shoes before ascending to sit was also practiced. Later, during drinking ceremonies, guests and hosts would exchange shoes and ascend the steps, and after the ceremony, they would remove their shoes again before sitting down. Ancient rituals were more elaborate compared to modern practices.
